\mathbf {T} = \mathbf {T} _ {\mathrm {f l u i d}} + \mathbf {T} _ {\mathrm {h e a t}} = \left[ (\mathbf {P} + \rho) \mathbf {u} \otimes \mathbf {u} + \mathbf {P g} \right] + \left[ \mathbf {q} \otimes \mathbf {u} + \mathbf {u} \otimes \mathbf {q} \right].
$$

We compute the equation expressing energy conservation along the fluid flow:

$$
0 = (\nabla \cdot \mathbf {T}) \cdot \mathbf {u} = (\nabla \cdot \mathbf {T} _ {\text {f l u i d}}) \cdot \mathbf {u} + (\nabla \cdot \mathbf {T} _ {\text {h e a t}}) \cdot \mathbf {u}. \tag {1}
$$

By a short calculation (see Solution 5.20) the first term is seen to be $-\mathrm{d}\rho /\mathrm{d}\tau +(d\mathfrak{n} / d\tau)(\mathbb{P} + \rho) / \mathfrak{n}$ which by the $1^{\mathrm{st}}$ law of thermodynamics equals $-\mathbf{nTds} / \mathbf{dr}$ (see Problem 5.19). The second term is

$$
(\nabla \cdot \mathbf {T} _ {\mathrm {h e a t}}) \cdot \mathbf {u} = (\mathbf {q} ^ {\mu} \mathbf {u} ^ {\nu} + \mathbf {u} ^ {\mu} \mathbf {q} ^ {\nu}) _ {; \nu} \mathbf {u} _ {\mu}.
$$

Since $\mathbf{q}^{\alpha}\mathbf{u}_{\alpha} = 0$ (heat flux is spacelike in comoving frame) and $\mathbf{u}^{\alpha}\mathbf{u}_{\alpha} = -1$ , which imply

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = \left(q ^ {\mu} u _ {\mu}\right) _ {; \nu} = q ^ {\mu} _ {; \nu} u _ {\mu} + q ^ {\mu} u _ {\mu ; \nu} \\ 0 = \left(\mathrm {u} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {u} _ {\mu}\right) _ {; \nu} = 2 \mathrm {u} _ {; \nu} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {u} _ {\mu}, \\ \end{array}
$$

the second term reduces to $-\mathbf{q} \cdot \mathbf{a} - \nabla \cdot \mathbf{q}$ . Thus Equation (1) becomes

$$
0 = - n T d s / d \tau - q \cdot a - \nabla \cdot q.
$$

Finally using the definition $\mathbf{S} = \mathbf{nsu} + \mathbf{q} / \mathbf{T}$ , we compute

$$
\begin{array}{l} \nabla \cdot \mathbf {S} = \mathbf {s} \nabla \cdot (\mathbf {n u}) + \mathbf {n} (\nabla \mathbf {s} \cdot \mathbf {u}) + \frac {1}{T} \nabla \cdot \mathbf {q} - \frac {\nabla T}{T ^ {2}} \cdot \mathbf {q} \\ = n \frac {d s}{d r} + \frac {1}{T} \nabla \cdot q - \frac {\nabla T \cdot q}{T ^ {2}} \\ = - \frac {\mathbf {q} \cdot \mathbf {a}}{\mathbf {T}} - \frac {\nabla \mathbf {T}}{\mathbf {T} ^ {2}} \cdot \mathbf {q}. \\ \end{array}
$$

The second term says entropy is generated by heat flow thru a temperature gradient, as in nonrelativistic thermodynamics. The first term says that it is generated by flow along an acceleration, this is a "redshift" effect arising from the fact that in a accelerated system, constant temperature is not an equilibrium state: photons from the "forward" side are blueshifted in going backward, thus carrying a net heat flux.

Solution 5.29. An equilibrium state obtains when there is no entropy generation, so according to the results of Problem 5.28

$$
- \mathbf {q} \cdot \mathbf {a} = \frac {\nabla \mathrm {T}}{\mathrm {T}} \cdot \mathbf {q}
$$

must hold for all heat flows $\mathbf{q}$ . Thus we must have $\mathbf{a} = -\nabla \ell \mathbf{n} \mathbf{T}$ . The solution to this equation is $\ell \mathbf{n} \mathbf{T} = -\underline{\mathbf{a}} \cdot \underline{\mathbf{x}} + \text{constant}$ , or

$$
\mathrm {T} = \mathrm {T} _ {0} \exp (- \underline {{\underline {{a}}}} \cdot \underline {{\underline {{x}}}}).
$$

Solution 5.30. Since $\mathbf{T}^{\alpha \beta}\mathbf{u}_a = -\rho \mathbf{u}^\beta$ there are no energy flow terms, hence $\mathbf{q} = 0$ and $\mathbf{s} = \mathbf{nsu}$ . The rate of entropy production is thus

$$
S _ {; \alpha} ^ {\alpha} = (\mathrm {n u} ^ {\alpha}) _ {; \alpha} \mathrm {s} + \mathrm {n} \frac {\mathrm {d s}}{\mathrm {d} \tau} = \mathrm {n} \frac {\mathrm {d s}}{\mathrm {d} \tau}
$$

where the second equality follows from baryon number conservation $\nabla \cdot (\mathbf{nu}) = 0$ .

With the first law of thermodynamics

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \rho}{\mathrm {d} \tau} = \frac {\rho + \mathrm {p}}{\mathrm {n}} \frac {\mathrm {d n}}{\mathrm {d} \tau} + \mathrm {n T} \frac {\mathrm {d s}}{\mathrm {d} \tau}
$$

and the law of baryon conservation

$$
\left(\mathrm {n u} ^ {\alpha}\right) _ {; \alpha} = \frac {\mathrm {d n}}{\mathrm {d} r} + \mathrm {n} \theta
$$

the rate of entropy production can be written as

$$
\mathsf {S} _ {; a} ^ {\alpha} = \frac {1}{\mathsf {T}} \left[ \frac {\mathrm {d} \rho}{\mathrm {d} \tau} + \theta (\rho + \mathsf {p}) \right].
$$

To express the right hand side in terms of the viscosity coefficients take the divergence of the vector $\mathbf{T}^{a\beta}\mathbf{u}_a$ :

$$
- (\rho \mathbf {u} ^ {\beta}) _ {; \beta} = - \frac {\mathrm {d} \rho}{\mathrm {d} \tau} - \rho \theta = (\mathbf {T} ^ {\alpha \beta} \mathbf {u} _ {\alpha}) _ {; \beta} = \mathbf {T} ^ {\alpha \beta} \mathbf {u} _ {\alpha ; \beta}.
$$

The last term on the right can be calculated using the stress-energy for the viscous fluid and the expansion for $\mathfrak{u}_{\alpha;\beta}$ given in Problem 5.18. The $\mathbf{T}^{\alpha \beta} \omega_{\alpha \beta}$ term vanishes by symmetry; the $\mathbf{T}^{\alpha \beta} \mathfrak{a}_{\alpha} \mathfrak{u}_{\beta}$ term vanishes since $\mathbf{T}^{\alpha \beta} \mathfrak{u}_{\beta} \propto \mathfrak{u}^{\alpha}$ and we are left with

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {T} ^ {\alpha \beta} \mathrm {u} _ {\alpha ; \beta} = [ \rho \mathrm {u} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {u} ^ {\beta} + (\mathrm {p} - \zeta \theta) \mathrm {P} ^ {\alpha \beta} - 2 \eta \sigma^ {\alpha \beta} ] [ \sigma_ {\alpha \beta} + \frac {1}{3} \theta \mathrm {P} _ {\alpha \beta} ] \\ = - 2 \eta \sigma_ {\alpha \beta} \sigma^ {\alpha \beta} + \theta (\mathfrak {p} - \zeta \theta). \\ \end{array}
$$

[Here we used the easily verified relations

$$
\mathbf {u} ^ {\alpha} \sigma_ {\alpha \beta} = 0, \quad \mathbf {P} _ {\alpha \beta} \sigma^ {\alpha \beta} = 0, \quad \mathbf {P} _ {\alpha \beta} \mathbf {P} ^ {\alpha \beta} = 3. ]
$$

The result then is

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \rho}{\mathrm {d} \tau} + \theta (\rho + \mathrm {p}) = 2 \eta \sigma_ {a \beta} \sigma^ {a \beta} + \zeta \theta^ {2}.
$$

When this is substituted in our previous formula for $S_{;a}^{\alpha}$ the desired equation results.

Solution 5.31. We know that projecting $\mathbf{T}^{\alpha \beta}_{,\beta}$ along $\mathfrak{u}_\alpha$ gives an equation of local energy conservation; to get an equation of motion for the fluid, we project perpendicular to $\mathfrak{u}_\alpha$ .

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = \mathrm {P} _ {\alpha} ^ {\gamma} \mathrm {T} ^ {\alpha \beta}, \beta = \mathrm {P} _ {\alpha} ^ {\gamma} (\rho , \beta \mathrm {u} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {u} ^ {\beta} + \rho \mathrm {u} ^ {\alpha}, \beta \mathrm {u} ^ {\beta} + \rho \mathrm {u} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {u} ^ {\beta}, \beta + \mathrm {p}, \beta \mathrm {P} ^ {\alpha \beta} \\ + \mathrm {p P} ^ {\alpha \beta}, \beta - 2 \eta , \beta^ {\sigma^ {\alpha \beta}} - 2 \eta^ {\sigma^ {\alpha \beta}}, \beta - \zeta , \beta^ {\theta \mathrm {P} ^ {\alpha \beta}} - \zeta \theta , \beta^ {\mathrm {P} ^ {\alpha \beta}} - \zeta \theta \mathrm {P} ^ {\alpha \beta}, \beta) . \\ \end{array}
$$

We now use the following identities: $\mathbf{P}_{\alpha}^{\gamma}\mathfrak{u}^{\alpha} = 0$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {P} _ {\alpha} ^ {\gamma} \mathrm {u} _ {, \beta} ^ {\alpha} = \mathrm {u} _ {, \beta} ^ {\gamma} + \mathrm {u} _ {\alpha} ^ {\gamma} \mathrm {u} _ {, \beta} ^ {\alpha} = \mathrm {u} _ {, \beta} ^ {\gamma} \\ \mathbf {P} _ {a} ^ {\gamma} \mathbf {P} ^ {a \beta} = \mathbf {P} ^ {\gamma \beta} \\ \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {P} _ {\alpha} ^ {\gamma} \mathrm {P} _ {, \beta} ^ {\alpha \beta} = \mathrm {P} _ {\alpha} ^ {\gamma} (\mathrm {u} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {u} ^ {\beta}), \beta = \mathrm {u} ^ {\gamma}, \beta \mathrm {u} ^ {\beta} \\ \mathrm {P} _ {\alpha} ^ {\gamma} \sigma^ {\alpha \beta} = \sigma^ {\gamma \beta} \\ \end{array}
$$

$$
\mathbf {P} _ {\alpha} ^ {\gamma} \sigma^ {\alpha \beta}, \beta = \sigma^ {\gamma \beta}, \beta + \mathfrak {u} ^ {\gamma} \mathfrak {u} _ {\alpha} \sigma^ {\alpha \beta}, \beta = \sigma^ {\gamma \beta}, \beta - \mathfrak {u} ^ {\gamma} \mathfrak {u} _ {\alpha}, \beta \sigma^ {\alpha \beta} = \sigma^ {\gamma \beta}, \beta - \mathfrak {u} ^ {\gamma} \sigma_ {\alpha} \beta \sigma^ {\alpha \beta}.
$$

With these identities the projected equations of motion become:

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = (\rho + p) u ^ {\gamma}, \beta u ^ {\beta} + p, \beta P ^ {\alpha \beta} - 2 (\eta \sigma^ {\gamma \beta} + \zeta \theta P ^ {\gamma \beta}), \beta \\ + 2 \eta \mathrm {u} ^ {\gamma} \sigma_ {\alpha \beta} \sigma^ {\alpha \beta} + \zeta \theta^ {2} \mathrm {u} ^ {\gamma}. \tag {1} \\ \end{array}
$$

In the nonrelativistic limit,

$$
u ^ {t} \approx 1, \quad u ^ {j} \approx v ^ {j}, \quad p = \mathcal {O} (v ^ {2}), \quad \rho = \mathcal {O} (1).
$$

Taking the j-component of Equation (1), and working to $\mathcal{O}(\mathbf{v}^2)$ , we get

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = \rho (\mathrm {v} _ {, t} ^ {\mathrm {j}} + \mathrm {v} _ {, k} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \mathrm {v} ^ {\mathrm {k}}) + \mathrm {p} _ {, j} \\ - \left[ \eta \left(\mathrm {v} _ {\mathrm {j}, \mathrm {k}} + \mathrm {v} _ {\mathrm {k}, \mathrm {j}} - \frac {2}{3} \delta_ {\mathrm {j k}} \mathrm {v} ^ {\mathrm {m}}, \mathrm {m}\right) \right], _ {\mathrm {k}} + \left(\zeta \mathrm {v} ^ {\mathrm {m}}, \mathrm {m}\right), _ {\mathrm {j}} \\ \end{array}
$$

which is the Navier-Stokes equation.

Solution 5.32. For a perfect Maxwell-Boltzmann gas,

$$
p = n k T \tag {1}
$$

$$
\rho / \mathfrak {n} = \mathrm {U} (\mathrm {T}) \tag {2}
$$

Equation (2) says that the energy per particle is a function of temperature alone. From the first law of relativistic thermodynamics,

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {T d s} = \mathrm {d} (\rho / \mathrm {n}) + \mathrm {p d} (1 / \mathrm {n}) \\ = \frac {\mathrm {d} U}{\mathrm {d} T} \mathrm {d} T + \mathrm {p d} \left(\frac {1}{n}\right) \tag {3} \\ \end{array}
$$

we see that

$$
c _ {v} = \frac {d U}{d T}. \tag {4}
$$

Equation (1) implies that

$$
\mathrm {p d} \left(\frac {1}{\mathrm {n}}\right) + \frac {1}{\mathrm {n}} \mathrm {d p} = \mathrm {k d T} \tag {5}
$$

so Equation (3) becomes

$$
\mathrm {T d s} = \left(\frac {\mathrm {d U}}{\mathrm {d T}} + \mathrm {k}\right) \mathrm {d T} - \frac {1}{\mathrm {n}} \mathrm {d p} \tag {6}
$$

and thus

$$
c _ {p} = \frac {d U}{d T} + k = c _ {v} + k. \tag {7}
$$

From Equation (1)

$$
\Gamma_ {1} = \left. \frac {\partial \log p}{\partial \log n} \right| _ {s} = 1 + \frac {n}{T} \frac {d T}{d n} \Bigg | _ {s} \tag {8}
$$

But Equation (3) implies that, when $\mathbf{s} =$ constant,

$$
c _ {v} \frac {d T}{d n} = \frac {p}{n ^ {2}} = k \frac {T}{n} = \left(c _ {p} - c _ {v}\right) \frac {T}{n}. \tag {9}
$$

Substituting Equation (9) in Equation (8) gives us

$$
\Gamma_ {1} = 1 + \frac {\left(\mathrm {c} _ {\mathrm {p}} - \mathrm {c} _ {\mathrm {v}}\right)}{\mathrm {c} _ {\mathrm {v}}} = \gamma .
$$

Solution 5.33. From Problem 5.32,

$$
\gamma = \Gamma_ {1} = \left. \frac {\partial \log p}{\partial \log n} \right| _ {s}
$$

so if $\gamma =$ constant,

$$
\mathbf {p} = \mathbf {K n} ^ {\gamma}.
$$

For adiabatic changes the first law of thermodynamics is

$$
\mathrm {d} \rho = \frac {\rho + \mathrm {p}}{\mathrm {n}} \mathrm {d n} = \left(\frac {\rho}{\mathrm {n}} + \mathrm {K n} ^ {\gamma - 1}\right) \mathrm {d n},
$$

or

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} n} \left(\frac {\rho}{n}\right) = K n ^ {\gamma - 2}
$$

so that the solution is

$$
\frac {\rho}{n} = \frac {K n ^ {\gamma - 1}}{\gamma - 1} + c o n s t a n t.
$$

But when $\mathfrak{n} \to 0$ , then $\rho / \mathfrak{n} \to \mathfrak{m}$ , so

$$
\rho = \operatorname {m n} + \frac {\operatorname {K n} ^ {\gamma}}{\gamma - 1}.
$$

Solution 5.34. We obtain scalar integrals by dotting various combinations of $\mathbf{u}^{\alpha}$ into the vector and tensor integrals for $\mathbf{J}^{\mu}$ and $\mathbf{T}^{\mu \nu}$ . Thus

$$
\mathrm {n} = - J ^ {\mu} \mathrm {u} _ {\mu} = \int \mathcal {N} \mathrm {d} ^ {3} \mathrm {P} = \frac {\mathrm {g}}{\mathrm {h} ^ {3}} \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} \frac {4 \pi \mathrm {P} ^ {2} \mathrm {d P}}{\exp \left[ (\mathrm {P} ^ {2} + \mathrm {m} ^ {2}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} / \mathrm {k T} - \theta \right] - \varepsilon},
$$

where $\mathbf{g} = 2\mathbf{J} + \mathbf{1}$ . Make the substitution $\mathbf{P} = \mathfrak{m}\sinh \chi$ .

Then

$$
n = \frac {4 \pi g m ^ {3}}{h ^ {3}} \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} \frac {\sinh^ {2} \chi \cosh \chi d \chi}{\exp (\beta \cosh \chi - \theta) - \varepsilon}, \tag {1}
$$

where $\beta = \mathbf{m} / \mathbf{kT}$ . Similarly, we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {p} = \frac {1}{3} \left(\mathrm {u} _ {\mu} \mathrm {u} _ {\nu} + \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \nu}\right) \mathrm {T} ^ {\mu \nu} = \frac {1}{3} \int \mathfrak {N} \frac {\mathrm {P} ^ {2}}{\left(\mathrm {P} ^ {2} + \mathrm {m} ^ {2}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \mathrm {d} ^ {3} \mathrm {P} \\ = \frac {4 \pi \mathrm {g m} ^ {4}}{3 \mathrm {h} ^ {3}} \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} \frac {\sinh^ {4} \chi \mathrm {d} \chi}{\exp (\beta \cosh \chi - \theta) - \varepsilon} \tag {2} \\ \end{array}
$$

and finally,

$$
\begin{array}{l} \rho - 3 \mathrm {p} = - \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \nu} \mathrm {T} ^ {\mu \nu} = \mathrm {m} ^ {2} \int \mathfrak {N} \frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {3} \mathrm {P}}{(\mathrm {P} ^ {2} + \mathrm {m} ^ {2}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \\ = \frac {4 \pi \mathrm {g m} ^ {4}}{\mathrm {h} ^ {3}} \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} \frac {\sinh^ {2} \chi \mathrm {d} \chi}{\exp (\beta \cosh \chi - \theta) - \varepsilon}. \tag {3} \\ \end{array}
$$

(b) From Equation (2),

$$
\mathrm {d p} = \frac {4 \pi \mathrm {g m} ^ {4}}{3 \mathrm {h} ^ {3}} \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} \frac {\sinh^ {4} \chi \mathrm {d} \chi (\beta \cosh \chi \mathrm {d T} / \mathrm {T} + \mathrm {d} \theta) \exp (\beta \cosh \chi - \theta)}{\left[ \exp (\beta \cosh \chi - \theta) - \varepsilon \right] ^ {2}}.
$$

Now integrate by parts, differentiating the terms $\sinh^3\chi \cosh \chi$ and $\sinh^3\chi$ to get

$$
\mathrm {d p} = \frac {4 \pi \mathrm {g m} ^ {4}}{3 \mathrm {h} ^ {3}} \frac {1}{\beta} \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} \frac {\mathrm {d} \chi [ (3 \sinh^ {2} \chi + 4 \sinh^ {4} \chi) \beta \mathrm {d T / T} + 3 \sinh^ {2} \chi \cosh \chi \mathrm {d} \theta ]}{\exp (\beta \cosh \chi - \theta) - \varepsilon}.
$$

Substitute expressions (1), (2), and (3) for the integrals, and find

$$
\mathrm {d p} = (\rho + \mathrm {p}) \mathrm {d T} / \mathrm {T} + \mathrm {n k T} \mathrm {d} \theta . \tag {4}
$$

(c) From the definition of $\mu$ we have

$$
\mathrm {d} \mu = \mathrm {d} \rho / \mathrm {n} + \mathrm {d p} / \mathrm {n} - (\rho + \mathrm {p}) \mathrm {d n} / \mathrm {n} ^ {2} - \mathrm {s d T} - \mathrm {T d s},
$$

but

$$
\mathrm {d} \rho = (\rho + \mathrm {p}) \mathrm {d n} / \mathrm {n} + \mathrm {n T d s},
$$

therefore

$$
\mathrm {d} \mu = \mathrm {d p} / \mathrm {n} - \mathrm {s d T} = \mathrm {d p} / \mathrm {n} - (\rho + \mathrm {p}) \mathrm {d T} / (\mathrm {n T}) + \mu \mathrm {d T} / \mathrm {T}.
$$

Here we have substituted for $s$ in terms of $\mu$ . Comparing this with Equation (4), we identify

$$
\mu = k T \theta .
$$

(d) For $\varepsilon = 0$

$$
\frac {\mathsf {p}}{\mathsf {n}} = \frac {\frac {\mathsf {m}}{3} \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} \sinh^ {4} \chi \mathsf {e} ^ {- \beta \cosh \chi} \mathsf {d} \chi}{\int_ {0} ^ {\infty} \sinh^ {2} \chi \cosh \chi \mathsf {e} ^ {- \beta \cosh \chi} \mathsf {d} \chi},
$$

but

$$
\begin{array}{l} \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} \sinh^ {4} \chi \exp (- \beta \cosh \chi) d \chi = - \frac {1}{\beta} \sinh^ {3} \chi \exp (- \beta \cosh \chi) | _ {0} ^ {\infty} \\ + \frac {3}{\beta} \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} \sinh^ {3} \chi \cosh \chi \exp (- \beta \cosh \chi) d \chi . \\ \end{array}
$$

The first term on the right vanishes, so

$$
\mathbf {p} = \mathbf {n k T}.
$$

(e) For $\varepsilon = 0$ , the integrals (1), (2), and (3) can be expressed in terms of modified Hankel functions, since

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {K} _ {\mathfrak {n}} (\beta) = \frac {\beta^ {\mathfrak {n}}}{(2 \mathfrak {n} - 1) ! !} \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} \mathrm {d} \chi \sinh^ {2 \mathfrak {n}} \chi \exp \left(- \beta \cosh \chi\right) \\ = \frac {\beta^ {n - 1}}{(2 n - 3) ! !} \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} d x \sinh^ {2 n - 2} x \quad \cosh x e ^ {- \beta \cosh x}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Thus for $\mathbf{a} = 4\pi \mathbf{g}\mathbf{m}^3\mathbf{e}^{\theta}\mathbf{h}^{-3}$ we have

$$
n = a K _ {2} (\beta) / \beta
$$

$$
p = a m K _ {2} (\beta) / \beta^ {2}
$$

$$
\rho - 3 p = a m K _ {1} (\beta) / \beta^ {2}.
$$

The exact expression for $\rho / n$ is

$$
\rho / n = m \left[ K _ {1} (\beta) / K _ {2} (\beta) + 3 / \beta \right].
$$

For $\mathbf{kT} <   \mathbf{m},\beta \rightarrow \infty$ .In this limit

$$
\mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {n}} (\beta) \rightarrow \left(\frac {\pi}{2 \beta}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {e} ^ {- \beta} \left[ 1 + \frac {4 \mathrm {n} ^ {2} - 1}{8 \beta} + \dots \right].
$$

so

$$
\rho / \mathfrak {n} \rightarrow \mathfrak {m} \left[ \frac {1 + 3 / (8 \beta)}{1 + 1 5 / (8 \beta)} + \frac {3}{\beta} \right] = \mathfrak {m} \left(1 + \frac {3}{2} \frac {\mathrm {k T}}{\mathrm {m}}\right)
$$

For kT >> m, $\beta \rightarrow 0$ , $\mathrm{K}_1(\beta) / \mathrm{K}_2(\beta) \rightarrow 0$ , so $\rho / n \rightarrow 3kT$ . Whew!

Solution 5.35. From Problem 5.32,

$$
\gamma = \left(\mathrm {c} _ {\mathrm {v}} + \mathrm {k}\right) / \mathrm {c} _ {\mathrm {v}} = 1 + \frac {\mathrm {k}}{\mathrm {d U} / \mathrm {d T}}
$$

where, from Problem 5.34

$$
\mathrm {U} (\mathrm {T}) = \mathrm {m} \left\{\mathrm {K} _ {1} (\beta) / \mathrm {K} _ {2} (\beta) + 3 / \beta \right\}, \quad \beta = \mathrm {m} / \mathrm {k T}.
$$

This gives the formal answer. Note that for $\mathrm{kT} \ll \mathrm{m}$ , $\mathbf{U} = \mathbf{m} + 3\mathrm{kT} / 2$ , $\gamma = 5 / 3$ , and that for $\mathrm{kT} \gg \mathrm{m}$ , $\mathbf{U} = 3\mathrm{kT}$ and $\gamma = 4 / 3$ .

# CHAPTER 6: SOLUTIONS

# Solution 6.1.

(a) The analogy with polar coordinates suggests

$$
\mathbf {x} = \mathbf {v} \cosh \mathbf {u} \quad \mathbf {x} ^ {2} - t ^ {2} = \mathbf {v} ^ {2}
$$

$$
t = v \sinh u \quad x / t = \coth u.
$$

Then

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {2} = (\mathrm {d v} \cosh u + \mathrm {d u v} \sinh u) ^ {2} \\ \mathrm {d t} ^ {2} = (\mathrm {d v} \sinh u + \mathrm {d u v c o s h u}) ^ {2} \tag {1} \\ \mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {2} - \mathrm {d} t ^ {2} = \mathrm {d} \mathbf {v} ^ {2} - \mathbf {v} ^ {2} \mathrm {d} \mathbf {u} ^ {2}. \\ \end{array}
$$

(b) Solving Equations (1) for du and dv gives

$$
\mathrm {d v} = \mathrm {d x} \cosh u - \mathrm {d t} \sinh u
$$

$$
\mathrm {d} u = v ^ {- 1} (\mathrm {d} t \cosh u - d x \sinh u)
$$

and thus for a particle of unit mass,

$$
\mathbf {P} _ {\mathrm {u}} = \mathbf {g} _ {\mathrm {u u}} \mathbf {P} ^ {\mathrm {u}} = - \mathbf {v} ^ {2} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {u}}{\mathrm {d} \tau} = - \mathbf {v} \cosh \mathbf {u} \frac {\mathrm {d} t}{\mathrm {d} \tau} + \mathbf {v} \sinh \mathbf {u} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x}}{\mathrm {d} \tau} = - \mathbf {x} \frac {\mathrm {d} t}{\mathrm {d} \tau} + \mathbf {t} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x}}{\mathrm {d} \tau}.
$$

For an unaccelerated particle $\mathbf{x} = \text{const.} + \frac{\mathrm{d}\mathbf{x}}{\mathrm{d}t} t$ , and $\mathrm{dt} / \mathrm{d}\tau$ , $\mathrm{dx} / \mathrm{d}\tau$ are also both constant, so

$$
\mathrm {P _ {u}} = \text {c o n s t .} - \mathrm {t} \frac {\mathrm {d x}}{\mathrm {d t}} \frac {\mathrm {d t}}{\mathrm {d} r} + \mathrm {t} \frac {\mathrm {d x}}{\mathrm {d} r} = \text {c o n s t a n t .}
$$

Now for $\mathbf{P}_{\mathbf{v}}$ , use

$$
- \mathrm {\bf m} ^ {2} = \bf P \cdot \bf P = g ^ {v v} (P _ {v}) ^ {2} + g ^ {u u} (P _ {u}) ^ {2} = (P _ {v}) ^ {2} - (P _ {u}) ^ {2} / v ^ {2}.
$$

So, $\mathbf{P}_{\mathbf{v}}^{2} = \mathbf{P}_{\mathbf{u}}^{2} / \mathbf{v}^{2} - \mathbf{m}^{2}$ , which is not constant since in general $\mathbf{v}$ varies along the particle's trajectory. It turns out that whenever the metric coefficient does not depend on some coordinate (in this case $\mathbf{u}$ ), then

the covariant momentum of that coordinate (here $\mathbf{P_u}$ ) is conserved. (See Problem 7.13 or MTW, p. 651.)

Solution 6.2. The metric in Euclidean 4-space is

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = \mathrm {d x} _ {1} ^ {2} + \mathrm {d x} _ {2} ^ {2} + \mathrm {d x} _ {3} ^ {2} + \mathrm {d x} _ {4} ^ {2}. \tag {1}
$$

The equation of a hypersphere of radius $\mathbb{R}$ is

$$
\mathbf {x} _ {1} ^ {2} + \mathbf {x} _ {2} ^ {2} + \mathbf {x} _ {3} ^ {2} + \mathbf {x} _ {4} ^ {2} = R ^ {2}. \tag {2}
$$

By analogy with the 3-dimensional case, introduce coordinates on the hypersphere:

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {x} _ {4} = \textsf {R c o s} \alpha \\ \mathbf {x} _ {3} = \mathbf {R} \sin a \cos \theta \tag {3} \\ \mathbf {x} _ {2} = \mathrm {R} \sin a \sin \theta \cos \phi \\ \mathbf {x} _ {1} = \mathrm {R} \sin \alpha \sin \theta \sin \phi . \\ \end{array}
$$

Then Equation (2) is automatically satisfied; taking the differential of Equation (3) with $R$ constant, and substituting in Equation (1) yields the required metric of the hypersphere.

Solution 6.3.

(a) Introduce $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{y}$ coordinates on the cylinder by

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {x} = \phi \\ y = a \tan \lambda . \\ \end{array}
$$

The metric of the sphere in these coordinates is

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = \frac {\mathrm {a} ^ {4} \mathrm {d y} ^ {2}}{\left(\mathrm {a} ^ {2} + \mathrm {y} ^ {2}\right) ^ {2}} + \frac {\mathrm {a} ^ {4} \mathrm {d x} ^ {2}}{\mathrm {a} ^ {2} + \mathrm {y} ^ {2}} \tag {1}
$$

Comparing this with $\mathrm{ds}^2 = \mathrm{dx}^2 +\mathrm{dy}^2$ , we see there is the least distortion near $y = 0$ , i.e. near the equator.

![](images/700b06fe828b8a00580300663f51d09aebb6c486f6d01d3d504cfdd1f13ce2e0.jpg)

(b) For the stereographic projection, it is slightly easier to use the usual polar angle $\theta = 90^{\circ} - \lambda$ . Let $(\theta, \phi)$ be the coordinates of a point on the sphere, and $(\theta_{0}, \phi_{0})$ the spherical polar coordinates of the projected point. Clearly $\phi_{0} = \phi$ . The distance of the projected point from the axis is $\rho = 2a \tan(\theta / 2)$ , so we introduce coordinates

![](images/7305e7542c6b95d7fc175fd2800ac570a00340526e130e9ee3658dd1784149e1.jpg)

$$
\mathbf {x} = \rho \cos \phi = 2 a \tan \left(\frac {1}{2} \theta\right) \cos \phi
$$

$$
y = \rho \sin \phi = 2 a \tan \left(\frac {1}{2} \theta\right) \sin \phi
$$

and it is easily verified that

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = \mathrm {a} ^ {2} (\mathrm {d} \theta^ {2} + \sin^ {2} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2}) = \cos^ {4} \left(\frac {1}{2} \theta\right) (\mathrm {d x} ^ {2} + \mathrm {d y} ^ {2}).
$$

Here the distortion is least near $\theta = 0$ , the north pole. This projection is said to be conformal, because $(\mathrm{ds}^2)_{\mathrm{sphere}} = \mathbf{g}(\mathrm{ds}^2)_{\mathrm{map}}$ , where $\mathbf{g}$ is some function, in this case equal to $\cos^4 (\theta /2)$ . A conformal projection preserves angles (see Problem 6.7).

# Solution 6.4.

(a) Imagine traveling along some curve $\phi = \phi(\theta)$ . The compass bearing $\psi$ is given by

$$
\tan \psi = \sin \theta \frac {\mathrm {d} \phi}{\mathrm {d} \theta}. \tag {1}
$$

(Note that $\psi$ is measured clockwise from the $y$ -axis.) On the map we have

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {y}}{\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x}} = \tan \left(\psi + \frac {\pi}{2}\right) = - \frac {1}{\tan \psi}. \tag {2}
$$

Now we combine this with Equation (1):

$$
\sin \theta \frac {\mathrm {d} \phi}{\mathrm {d} \theta} = - \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} / \mathrm {d} \theta}{\mathrm {d} \mathbf {y} / \mathrm {d} \theta} = - \frac {\frac {\partial \mathbf {x}}{\partial \theta} + \frac {\partial \mathbf {x}}{\partial \phi} \frac {\mathrm {d} \phi}{\mathrm {d} \theta}}{\frac {\partial \mathbf {y}}{\partial \theta} + \frac {\partial \mathbf {y}}{\partial \phi} \frac {\mathrm {d} \phi}{\mathrm {d} \theta}}
$$

$$
\left(\frac {\partial \mathbf {y}}{\partial \theta} + \frac {\partial \mathbf {y}}{\partial \phi} \frac {\mathrm {d} \phi}{\mathrm {d} \theta}\right) \frac {\mathrm {d} \phi}{\mathrm {d} \theta} \sin \theta = - \frac {\partial \mathbf {x}}{\partial \theta} - \frac {\partial \mathbf {x}}{\partial \phi} \frac {\mathrm {d} \phi}{\mathrm {d} \theta}. \tag {3}
$$

Since Equation (3) must hold for arbitrary $\mathrm{d}\phi/\mathrm{d}\theta$ at the point we are considering, we can equate coefficients of powers of $\mathrm{d}\phi/\mathrm{d}\theta$ on the two sides of Equation (3). This gives us

$$
\partial \mathbf {y} / \partial \phi = 0, \quad \mathbf {y} = \mathbf {y} (\theta) \tag {4}
$$

$$
\partial \mathbf {x} / \partial \theta = 0, \quad \mathbf {x} = \mathbf {x} (\phi) \tag {5}
$$

$$
- \sin \theta \partial y / \partial \theta = \partial x / \partial \phi . \tag {6}
$$

Equations (4) and (5) imply that the left-hand side of Equation (6) is a function of $\theta$ only, while the right-hand side is a function of $\phi$ only, and so each side must be a constant which we can choose to be 1. Thus the map is given by

$$
\mathbf {x} = \phi , \quad \mathbf {y} = - \int \frac {\mathrm {d} \theta}{\sin \theta} = \log \cot \frac {1}{2} \theta . \tag {7}
$$

(b) Take the radius to be 1 for convenience. Then

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = \mathrm {d} \theta^ {2} + \sin^ {2} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2} \\ = \sin^ {2} \theta (\mathrm {d x} ^ {2} + \mathrm {d y} ^ {2}) \\ = \operatorname {s e c h} ^ {2} y \left(\mathrm {d} x ^ {2} + \mathrm {d} y ^ {2}\right). \tag {8} \\ \end{array}
$$

(c) The great circles are the geodesics of the 2-sphere. The geodesic equations are easy to solve, since we have two first integrals. Let a dot denote $\mathrm{d} / \mathrm{ds}$ ; then Equation (8) gives

$$
\operatorname {s e c h} ^ {2} \mathcal {Y} (\dot {\mathbf {x}} ^ {2} + \dot {\mathbf {y}} ^ {2}) = 1. \tag {9}
$$

Since $\mathbf{x}$ is an ignorable coordinate in the metric (8), $\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}}\dot{\mathbf{x}}$ is constant (see Solution 7.13), i.e.

$$
\left(\operatorname {s e c h} ^ {2} \mathrm {y}\right) \dot {\mathrm {x}} = \gamma . \tag {10}
$$

When neither $\dot{\mathbf{x}}$ nor $\dot{\mathbf{y}}$ vanishes, we can eliminate $s$ from Equations (9) and (10):

$$
\left(\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathcal {Y}}{\mathrm {d} x}\right) ^ {2} = \frac {\dot {\mathcal {Y}} ^ {2}}{\dot {x} ^ {2}} = \frac {\lambda^ {2} - \cosh^ {2} \mathcal {Y}}{\cosh^ {2} \mathcal {Y}} \tag {11}
$$

where $\lambda = 1 / \gamma$ . Equation (11) is easily integrated by putting $z = \sinh y$ . The result is

$$
\sinh y = \alpha \sin (x + \beta)
$$

where $\alpha = (\lambda^2 - 1)^{\frac{1}{2}}$ and $\beta$ is another constant of integration.

Solution 6.5. We can decide if the space is 3-dimensional or not by evaluating the 3-volume spanned by $\mathrm{d}x\mathrm{d}y\mathrm{d}z$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {d V} = \mathrm {g} ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d x d y d z} \\ = \left| \begin{array}{l l l} 1 - \left(\frac {3}{1 3}\right) ^ {2} & - \left(\frac {3}{1 3}\right) \left(\frac {4}{1 3}\right) & - \left(\frac {1 2}{1 3}\right) \left(\frac {3}{1 3}\right) \\ - \left(\frac {3}{1 3}\right) \left(\frac {4}{1 3}\right) & 1 - \left(\frac {4}{1 3}\right) ^ {2} & - \left(\frac {4}{1 3}\right) \left(\frac {1 2}{1 3}\right) \\ - \left(\frac {1 2}{1 3}\right) \left(\frac {3}{1 3}\right) & - \left(\frac {4}{1 3}\right) \left(\frac {1 2}{1 3}\right) & 1 - \left(\frac {1 2}{1 3}\right) ^ {2} \end{array} \right| d x d y d z = 0. \\ \end{array}
$$

Since this is identically zero for all $x, y, z$ , the 3 coordinates are always linearly dependent. The space is thus either two dimensional or one dimensional. We can now throw away one coordinate, say $z$ , by taking $z =$ constant; this is allowed because it is an "ignorable coordinate," i.e. the coefficients of the metric do not depend on $z$ . The metric left is

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = \mathrm {d x} ^ {2} + \mathrm {d y} ^ {2} - \left(\frac {3}{1 3} \mathrm {d x} + \frac {4}{1 3} \mathrm {d y}\right) ^ {2}.
$$

It is in fact 2-dimensional, not 1-dimensional, because

$$
\mathbf {g} = \det  \left( \begin{array}{c c} 1 - \left(\frac {3}{1 3}\right) ^ {2} & - \left(\frac {3}{1 3}\right) \left(\frac {4}{1 3}\right) \\ \left(\frac {3}{1 3}\right) \left(\frac {4}{1 3}\right) & 1 - \left(\frac {4}{1 3}\right) ^ {2} \end{array} \right) \neq 0.
$$

It is not difficult to find the coordinate transformation (e.g. by Gram-Schmidt orthogonalization)

$$
\begin{array}{l} \xi = \frac {1 2}{5} \left(\frac {3}{1 3} x + \frac {4}{1 3} y\right) \\ \eta = \frac {1 3}{5} \left(- \frac {4}{1 3} x + \frac {3}{1 3} y\right) \\ \end{array}
$$

which gives $\mathrm{ds}^2 = \mathrm{d}\xi^2 +\mathrm{d}\eta^2$ , obviously the simplest form of the metric. The more sophisticated reader will notice that the original metric was of the form

$$
\mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \beta} = \delta_ {\alpha \beta} - \mathbf {V} _ {\alpha} \mathbf {V} _ {\beta},
$$

where $\mathbf{V}_{\alpha}$ is the Euclidean unit vector $\left(\frac{3}{13}, \frac{4}{13}, \frac{12}{13}\right)$ . This metric just projects a 3-dimensional Euclidean space into a 2-dimensional space perpendicular to $\mathbf{V}$ , so it is obvious that there had to exist $\xi$ and $\eta$ coordinates which make it a flat-space 2-dimensional metric.

Solution 6.6. Let $\mathbf{A}$ be an arbitrary vector. We want to see if $\mathbf{P} \cdot \mathbf{A}$ is orthogonal to $\mathbf{u}$ , i.e. if $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{P} \cdot \mathbf{A} = 0$ :

$$
\mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {P} \cdot \mathbf {A} = \mathbf {u} ^ {\alpha} (\mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \beta} + \mathrm {u} _ {\alpha} \mathrm {u} _ {\beta}) \mathbf {A} ^ {\beta} = \mathbf {u} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {A} _ {\alpha} + (\mathrm {u} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {u} _ {\alpha}) \mathrm {u} _ {\beta} \mathbf {A} ^ {\beta} = \mathrm {u} _ {\alpha} \mathbf {A} ^ {\alpha} - \mathrm {u} _ {\beta} \mathbf {A} ^ {\beta} = 0.
$$

Also it is easy to see that if $\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{u} = 0$ , then $\mathbf{P} \cdot \mathbf{A} = \mathbf{A}$ , so $\mathbf{A}$ is unaffected by projection. If $\mathbf{n}$ is a unit spacelike vector, we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {n} \cdot \mathbf {P} \cdot \mathbf {A} = \mathfrak {n} ^ {\alpha} \left(\mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \beta} - \mathrm {n} _ {\alpha} \mathrm {n} _ {\beta}\right) \mathbf {A} ^ {\beta} \\ = \mathrm {n} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {A} _ {\alpha} - \left(\mathrm {n} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {n} _ {\alpha}\right) \mathrm {n} _ {\beta} \mathrm {A} ^ {\beta} \\ = \mathrm {n} _ {\alpha} \mathrm {A} ^ {\alpha} - \mathrm {n} _ {\beta} \mathrm {A} ^ {\beta} = 0. \\ \end{array}
$$

Likewise a vector already orthogonal to $\mathbf{n}$ is unaffected by projection. Suppose now that $\mathbf{P}$ is a projection operator orthogonal to a null vector $\mathbf{k}$ , so that $\mathbf{k} \cdot \mathbf{P} \cdot \mathbf{A} = 0$ for all $\mathbf{A}$ . It is easy to see that $\mathbf{P} + \text{constant} \times \mathbf{k} \otimes \mathbf{k}$ is also a projection operator since $\mathbf{k} \cdot \mathbf{k} = 0$ ; so $\mathbf{P}$ is not unique. (The set of null projection operators is not empty since it is easy to

check that for any 4-vector $\mathbf{w}$ , one is $\mathbf{P} = \mathbf{g} - \mathbf{w} \otimes \mathbf{k} / \mathbf{k} \cdot \mathbf{w}$ . However, there are no symmetric null projection operators at all.)

Solution 6.7. Let $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{B}$ be two vectors in the metric space. Obviously we want the angle to be related to the dot product $\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B}$ . We also want it to be unaffected by scaling $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{B}$ , so the natural choice is

$\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{B} / (|\mathbf{A}| |\mathbf{B}|)$ , which is $\cos \theta$ in the familiar Euclidean case. Now under $\mathbf{g}_{\alpha \beta} \rightarrow \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{x}^{\mu}) \mathbf{g}_{\alpha \beta}$ we have

$$
\frac {\mathbf {A} \cdot \mathbf {B}}{(| \mathbf {A} | | \mathbf {B} |)} = \frac {\mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \beta} \mathbf {A} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {B} ^ {\beta}}{\left(\mathbf {g} _ {\mu \nu} \mathbf {A} ^ {\mu} \mathbf {A} ^ {\nu} \mathbf {g} _ {\rho \sigma} \mathbf {B} ^ {\rho} \mathbf {B} ^ {\sigma}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \to \frac {\mathbf {f} (\mathbf {x} ^ {\gamma}) \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \beta} \mathbf {A} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {B} ^ {\beta}}{\left[ \mathbf {f} (\mathbf {x} ^ {\gamma}) \mathbf {g} _ {\mu \nu} \mathbf {A} ^ {\mu} \mathbf {A} ^ {\nu} \mathbf {f} (\mathbf {x} ^ {\gamma}) \mathbf {g} _ {\rho \sigma} \mathbf {B} ^ {\rho} \mathbf {B} ^ {\sigma} \right] ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}
$$

but the f's cancel, giving the same value as before. Null curves remain null curves because the square of their tangent vector remains zero

$$
0 = \boldsymbol {\ell} \cdot \boldsymbol {\ell} = \mathbf {g} _ {\mu \nu} \ell^ {\mu} \ell^ {\nu} \rightarrow \mathrm {f} (\mathbf {x} ^ {\gamma}) \mathbf {g} _ {\mu \nu} \ell^ {\mu} \ell^ {\nu} = 0.
$$

Solution 6.8. The relative velocity $\mathrm{ds}$ between two velocities $\underline{\mathbf{v}}$ and $\underline{\mathbf{v}} + \mathrm{dy}$ is (by Problem 1.3) given by

$$
d s ^ {2} = \frac {(d v) ^ {2} - (v \times d v) ^ {2}}{(1 - v ^ {2}) ^ {2}},
$$

and

$$
\left(\underline {{v}} \times d \underline {{v}}\right) ^ {2} = v ^ {2} \left(d \underline {{v}}\right) ^ {2} - \left(\underline {{v}} \cdot d \underline {{v}}\right) ^ {2}.
$$

Let $\theta$ and $\phi$ be polar and azimuthal angles about the direction of $\underline{\mathbf{v}}$ (i.e. $\mathbf{v}_{\mathbf{z}} = \mathbf{v}\cos \theta, \mathbf{v}_{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{v}\sin \theta\cos \phi, \mathbf{v}_{\mathbf{y}} = \mathbf{v}\sin \theta\sin \phi$ ). Then

$$
\begin{array}{l} \left(\mathrm {d} \underline {{v}}\right) ^ {2} = (\mathrm {d} v) ^ {2} + v ^ {2} \left(\mathrm {d} \theta^ {2} + \sin^ {2} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2}\right) \\ \underline {{\mathbf {v}}} \cdot d \underline {{\mathbf {v}}} = \frac {1}{2} d (\underline {{\mathbf {v}}} \cdot \underline {{\mathbf {v}}}) = v d \mathbf {v} \\ \end{array}
$$

so

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = \frac {\mathrm {d v} ^ {2}}{\left(1 - \mathrm {v} ^ {2}\right) ^ {2}} + \frac {\mathrm {v} ^ {2}}{1 - \mathrm {v} ^ {2}} \left(\mathrm {d} \theta^ {2} + \sin^ {2} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2}\right).
$$

Introduce the "rapidity parameter" $\mathbf{v} = \tanh \chi$ . Then

$$
\mathrm {d} \mathbf {s} ^ {2} = \mathrm {d} \chi^ {2} + \sinh^ {2} \chi (\mathrm {d} \theta^ {2} + \sin^ {2} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2}).
$$

Note that for small $\mathbf{v}$ , $\sinh \chi \sim \chi \sim \mathbf{v}$ and velocity space is flat, as we expect it to be in the Newtonian limit.

Solution 6.9. If we had $\sin^2\theta$ instead of $(\theta - \theta^3)$ the metric would just be that of the Euclidean 2-sphere. As given, it is obviously some sort of axially symmetric "warped" 2-sphere with singularities possible at $\theta = 0, \pm 1$ . From the given value of $\theta = \frac{1}{2}$ , it is obvious that the range of $\theta$ can be extended to $0 < \theta < 1$ . We must examine the cases $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = 1$ with some care to decide what range $\chi$ can take. We know that $\chi$ must be periodic because, as the metric shows, the coordinates $(\theta = 0, \text{all } \chi)$ and $(\theta = 1, \text{all } \chi)$ each represent just one point.

For $\theta \approx 0$ , we have

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} \approx \mathrm {d} \theta^ {2} + \theta^ {2} \mathrm {d} \chi^ {2}.
$$

If $\chi$ is periodic with period $P$ , then the proper circumference of a small circle of radius $\Delta \theta$ is $\int (\Delta \theta) \, \mathrm{d}\chi = (\Delta \theta) P$ . If we are to avoid a conical singularity this must equal $2\pi \Delta \theta$ , so $P = 2\pi$ . But now consider $\theta \approx 1$ : The metric is

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} \approx \mathrm {d} \theta^ {2} + (\theta - 1) ^ {2} (2) ^ {2} \mathrm {d} \chi^ {2}
$$

so the condition avoiding a conical singularity, is

$$
\int_ {0} ^ {P} 2 (\Delta \theta) d \chi = 2 \pi (\Delta \theta),
$$

implies that $\mathbf{P} = \pi$ . Thus we can either take $\mathbf{P} = 2\pi$ and have a conical singularity at $\theta = 1$ , or $\mathbf{P} = \pi$ and have it at $\theta = 0$ . These are two different global extensions of the manifold. (This problem is due to Gilbert Miller.)

Solution 6.10. A geometrical object exhibits a particular group symmetry if its functional change (i.e. its change in functional form) under the action

of the group vanishes. If $\xi$ is an infinitesimal displacement, then under the infinitesimal coordinate transformation $\mathbf{x}'^{\mu} = \mathbf{x}^{\mu} + \xi^{\mu}$ , one can easily show that the functional change of the metric, $\overline{\delta}\mathbf{g}_{\mu \nu}$ , is

$$
\overline {{{\delta}}} \mathbf {g} _ {\mu \nu} \equiv \mathbf {g} _ {\mu \nu} ^ {\prime} (\mathbf {x}) - \mathbf {g} _ {\mu \nu} (\mathbf {x}) = - \mathbf {g} _ {\mu \rho} \xi_ {, \nu} ^ {\rho} - \mathbf {g} _ {\rho \nu} \xi_ {, \mu} ^ {\beta} - \mathbf {g} _ {\mu \nu , \rho} \xi^ {\rho}. \qquad (1)
$$

(See Problem 13.12.) For spherical symmetry, $\overline{\delta}\mathbf{g}_{\mu \nu}$ must vanish under the rotation group, a particular realization of which is obtained from the generators

$$
\xi^ {0} = 0, \quad \xi^ {\mathrm {i}} = \varepsilon^ {\mathrm {i j}} \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {j}}, \tag {2}
$$

where $\varepsilon^{\mathrm{ij}} = -\varepsilon^{\mathrm{ji}}$ are three arbitrary infinitesimal constants. Substituting Equation (2) for the generators $\xi^{\mu}$ in Equation (1) and setting the left side to zero, one gets:

a. $\mu = \nu = 0$

$$
\mathbf {g} _ {0 0, \mathrm {i}} \varepsilon^ {\mathrm {i j}} \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {j}} = 0 \quad \text {o r} \quad \mathbf {g} _ {0 0, \mathrm {i}} \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {j}} = \mathbf {g} _ {0 0, \mathrm {j}} \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {i}}
$$

and hence

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {g} _ {0 0} = \mathbf {g} _ {0 0} (\mathbf {x} ^ {0}, \mathbf {r} ^ {2}) \\ r ^ {2} \equiv (x ^ {1}) ^ {2} + (x ^ {2}) ^ {2} + (x ^ {3}) ^ {2}. \tag {3a} \\ \end{array}
$$

b. $\mu = 0, \nu \neq 0$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {g} _ {0 \mathrm {i}} \varepsilon^ {\mathrm {i j}} + \mathbf {g} _ {0 \mathrm {j}, \mathrm {i}} \varepsilon^ {\mathrm {i k}} \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {k}} = 0 \\ \mathrm {g} _ {0 \mathrm {j}} = \Gamma_ {1} \left(\mathrm {r} ^ {2}, \mathrm {x} ^ {0}\right) \mathrm {x} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \tag {3b} \\ \end{array}
$$

where $\Gamma_{1}$ is an arbitrary function.

c. $\mu \neq 0$ ， $\nu \neq 0$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {g} _ {\mathbf {i k}} \varepsilon^ {\mathbf {k j}} + \mathbf {g} _ {\mathbf {\ell j}} \varepsilon^ {\mathbf {\ell i}} + \mathbf {g} _ {\mathbf {i j}, \ell} \varepsilon^ {\mathbf {\ell k}} \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathbf {k}} = 0 \\ \mathbf {g} _ {\mathbf {i j}} = \Gamma_ {2} \left(\mathrm {r} ^ {2}, \mathrm {x} ^ {0}\right) \delta_ {\mathbf {i j}} + \Gamma_ {3} \left(\mathrm {r} ^ {2}, \mathrm {x} ^ {0}\right) \mathrm {x} ^ {\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {x} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \tag {3c} \\ \end{array}
$$

where $\Gamma_{2}$ and $\Gamma_{3}$ are again arbitrary functions.

From Equation (2) one can see that the above coordinate system $(\mathbf{x}^0, \mathbf{x}^1, \mathbf{x}^2, \mathbf{x}^3)$ is “cartesian-like.” The group theoretically defined property of spherical symmetry is coordinate independent, however, and to find $\mathbf{g}_{\mu \nu}$ in any other coordinate system one merely transforms the metric given in Equations (3) in the usual way.

# CHAPTER 7: SOLUTIONS

Solution 7.1. Let the transformation be given by $\mathbf{e}_{\mu^{\prime}} = \mathbf{L}_{\mu^{\prime}}^{\sigma}\mathbf{e}_{\sigma}$ , then

$$
\begin{array}{l} \nabla_ {\mathbf {e} _ {\beta} ^ {\prime}} \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha} ^ {\prime} = \Gamma_ {\alpha^ {\prime}} ^ {\tau^ {\prime}} \mathbf {e} _ {\tau^ {\prime}} = \nabla_ {(\mathrm {L} _ {\beta^ {\prime}} ^ {\lambda} \mathbf {e} _ {\lambda})} (\mathrm {L} _ {\alpha^ {\prime}} ^ {\mu} \mathbf {e} _ {\mu}) = \mathrm {L} _ {\beta^ {\prime}} ^ {\lambda} \nabla_ {\mathbf {e} _ {\lambda}} (\mathrm {L} _ {\alpha^ {\prime}} ^ {\mu} \mathbf {e} _ {\mu}) \\ = \mathrm {L} ^ {\lambda} _ {\beta^ {\prime}} \left(\mathrm {L} _ {\alpha^ {\prime}, \lambda} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {e} _ {\mu} + \mathrm {L} _ {\alpha^ {\prime}} ^ {\mu} \Gamma_ {\mu \lambda} ^ {\tau} \mathrm {e} _ {\tau}\right) \\ = L ^ {\lambda} \beta^ {\prime} \left(L ^ {\mu} _ {\alpha^ {\prime}, \lambda} L ^ {\tau^ {\prime}} _ {\mu} e _ {\tau^ {\prime}} + L ^ {\mu} _ {\alpha^ {\prime}} L ^ {\tau^ {\prime}} _ {\gamma} \Gamma^ {\gamma} _ {\mu \lambda} e _ {\tau^ {\prime}}\right), \\ \end{array}
$$

and therefore

$$
\Gamma_ {\alpha} ^ {\tau^ {'}} \beta^ {'} = \mathrm {L} _ {\beta^ {'}} ^ {\lambda} \mathrm {L} _ {\alpha^ {'}} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {L} _ {\gamma} ^ {\tau^ {'}} \Gamma_ {\mu \lambda} ^ {\gamma} + \mathrm {L} _ {\beta^ {'}} ^ {\lambda} \mathrm {L} _ {\mu} ^ {\tau^ {'}} \mathrm {L} _ {\alpha^ {'}, \lambda} ^ {\mu}.
$$

The second term in this equation would not be present in a tensor transformation.

Solution 7.2.

(a) First consider the line $\theta = 0$ , $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{s}$ , where $\mathbf{s}$ is the affine parameter (length). The geodesic equation $\mathrm{d}^2\mathbf{x}^\mu/\mathrm{d}\mathbf{r}^2 + \Gamma_{\mathbf{rr}}^\mu = 0$ , gives

$$
\Gamma_ {\mathbf {r r}} ^ {\mathbf {r}} = \Gamma_ {\mathbf {r r}} ^ {\theta} = 0.
$$

Next, for nonradial lines, reparameterize the geodesic equation using $\theta$ as a non-affine parameter:

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathbf {x} ^ {\mu}}{\mathrm {d} \theta^ {2}} + \frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \theta / \mathrm {d s} ^ {2}}{(\mathrm {d} \theta / \mathrm {d s}) ^ {2}} \frac {\mathrm {d x} ^ {\mu}}{\mathrm {d} \theta} + \frac {\mathrm {d x} ^ {\alpha}}{\mathrm {d} \theta} \frac {\mathrm {d x} ^ {\beta}}{\mathrm {d} \theta} \Gamma_ {\alpha \beta} ^ {\mu} = 0. \tag {1}
$$

The general straight line is

$$
\operatorname {r} \cos (\theta - a) = \mathrm {R} _ {0} \tag {2}
$$

where $\alpha$ and $\mathbf{R}_0$ are arbitrary constants. This equation, and $\mathrm{ds}^2 = \mathrm{dr}^2 +\mathrm{r}^2\mathrm{d}\theta^2$ , give $\mathrm{ds} / \mathrm{d}\theta = \mathbf{R}_0 / \cos^2\Psi$ , where $\Psi \equiv \theta -\alpha$ , and this in turn yields

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \theta}{\mathrm {d s} ^ {2}} \bigg / \left(\frac {\mathrm {d} \theta}{\mathrm {d s}}\right) ^ {2} = - 2 \tan \Psi .
$$

The geodesic equation now reads

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathbf {x} ^ {\mu}}{\mathrm {d} \theta^ {2}} - 2 \tan \Psi \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {\mu}}{\mathrm {d} \theta} + \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {\alpha}}{\mathrm {d} \theta} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {\beta}}{\mathrm {d} \theta} \Gamma_ {\alpha \beta} ^ {\mu} = 0. \tag {3}
$$

Consider the point $\theta = a$ (i.e. $\Psi = 0$ ) and $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{R}_0$ on the line. At this point (3) becomes

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathbf {x} ^ {\mu}}{\mathrm {d} \theta^ {2}} + \Gamma_ {\theta \theta} ^ {\mu} = 0,
$$

which gives $\Gamma_{\theta \theta}^{\theta} = 0$ , and $\Gamma_{\theta \theta}^{\mathbf{r}} = -\mathbf{r}$ (where Equation (2) has been used). Since $a$ and $\mathbb{R}_0$ are arbitrary these expressions are true in general.

Finally, consider an arbitrary point on the line and write out the geodesic equation in full glory, putting in the $\Gamma$ 's known so far. The $\mu = r$ component of the result gives $\Gamma_{r\theta}^{r} = \frac{1}{r}$ .

(b) From $r^2 = x^2 + y^2$ , and $\cot \theta = x / y$ we get a transformation matrix

$$
\mu = \mathbf {x} \quad \mu = \mathbf {y}
$$

$$
L _ {\mu} ^ {\alpha^ {\prime}} = \left[ \begin{array}{c c} \cos \theta & \sin \theta \\ - \frac {\sin \theta}{r} & \frac {\cos \theta}{r} \end{array} \right]
$$

and its inverse

$$
\beta^ {\prime} = \mathbf {r} \quad \beta^ {\prime} = \theta
$$

$$
L ^ {\mu} _ {\beta^ {\prime}} = \left[ \begin{array}{c c} \cos \theta & - r \sin \theta \\ \sin \theta & r \cos \theta \end{array} \right]
$$

(Example: $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{r}\cos \theta \Rightarrow \mathrm{d}\mathbf{x} = \mathrm{d}\mathbf{r}\cos \theta -\mathbf{r}\sin \theta \mathrm{d}\theta \Rightarrow$ first row above.)

The transformation law for the $\Gamma$ 's (Problem 7.1) is

$$
\Gamma_ {\beta^ {\prime} \gamma^ {\prime}} ^ {a ^ {\prime}} = \mathrm {L} _ {\rho} ^ {a ^ {\prime}} \mathrm {L} _ {\beta} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {L} _ {\gamma^ {\prime}} ^ {\nu} \Gamma_ {\mu \nu} ^ {\rho} + \mathrm {L} _ {\mu} ^ {a ^ {\prime}} \mathrm {L} _ {\beta^ {\prime}, \gamma^ {\prime}} ^ {\mu}.
$$

Since $\Gamma_{\mu \nu}^{\rho} = 0$ in cartesian coordinates, only the second term contributes, and straightforward differentiation and matrix multiplication gives the desired results.

(c) We now use

$$
\Gamma_ {\beta \gamma} ^ {a} = \frac {1}{2} \left. \mathbf {g} ^ {a \mu} \left(\mathbf {g} _ {\mu \beta , \gamma} + \mathbf {g} _ {\mu \gamma , \beta} - \mathbf {g} _ {\beta \gamma , \mu}\right) \right..
$$

For the metric $\mathrm{ds}^2 = \mathrm{dr}^2 +\mathrm{r}^2\mathrm{d}\theta^2$ the only nonvanishing derivative is $\mathbf{g}_{\theta \theta ,\mathbf{r}} = 2\mathbf{r}$ . Thus $\Gamma_{\beta \gamma}^{a} = 0$ unless 2 indices are $\theta$ 's and one is an $\mathbf{r}$ :

$$
\begin{array}{l} \Gamma_ {\theta \theta} ^ {r} = g ^ {r r} \left(- \frac {1}{2} g _ {\theta \theta , r}\right) = - r \\ \Gamma_ {\mathrm {r} \theta} ^ {\theta} = \mathbf {g} ^ {\theta \theta} \left(\frac {1}{2} \mathbf {g} _ {\theta \theta , \mathrm {r}}\right) = \frac {1}{\mathrm {r}}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Solution 7.3.

(a) The geodesic equation is

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathbf {r}}{\mathrm {d s} ^ {2}} + \Gamma^ {\mathbf {r}} _ {\theta} \theta \theta \left(\frac {\mathrm {d} \theta}{\mathrm {d s}}\right) ^ {2} = 0 \qquad \frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \theta}{\mathrm {d s} ^ {2}} + 2 \Gamma^ {\theta} _ {\mathbf {r} \theta} \left(\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {r}}{\mathrm {d s}}\right) \left(\frac {\mathrm {d} \theta}{\mathrm {d s}}\right) = 0 .
$$

Using the results of Problem 7.2, we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {d ^ {2} r}{d s ^ {2}} = r \left(\frac {d \theta}{d s}\right) ^ {2} \\ \frac {d ^ {2} \theta}{d s ^ {2}} + \frac {2}{r} \left(\frac {d r}{d s}\right) \left(\frac {d \theta}{d s}\right) = 0. \\ \end{array}
$$

Now $(\mathrm{dr} / \mathrm{ds})^2 +\mathbf{r}^2 (\mathrm{d}\theta /\mathrm{ds})^2 = 1$ follows from the metric so

$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {\mathrm {d} R _ {0}}{\mathrm {d} s} = \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} s} \left[ r ^ {2} \frac {\mathrm {d} \theta}{\mathrm {d} s} \right] = r ^ {2} \frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \theta}{\mathrm {d} s ^ {2}} + 2 r \left(\frac {\mathrm {d} r}{\mathrm {d} s}\right) \left(\frac {\mathrm {d} \theta}{\mathrm {d} s}\right) \\ = \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \left[ \frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \theta}{\mathrm {d s} ^ {2}} + \frac {2}{\mathrm {r}} \left(\frac {\mathrm {d r}}{\mathrm {d s}}\right) \left(\frac {\mathrm {d} \theta}{\mathrm {d s}}\right) \right] = 0, \\ \end{array}
$$

and therefore

$$
R _ {0} \equiv r ^ {2} \frac {d \theta}{d s} = c o n s t a n t.
$$

(b) From one of the first integrals above:

$$
\left(\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {r}}{\mathrm {d} \theta} \frac {\mathrm {d} \theta}{\mathrm {d} \mathbf {s}}\right) ^ {2} + \mathbf {r} ^ {2} \left(\frac {\mathrm {d} \theta}{\mathrm {d} \mathbf {s}}\right) ^ {2} = 1.
$$

Now substitute $\mathrm{d}\theta /\mathrm{d}s = R_0 / \mathrm{r}^2$ ( $R_0 = \text{some constant}$ ):

$$
\left(\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {r}}{\mathrm {d} \theta}\right) ^ {2} + \mathbf {r} ^ {2} = \mathbf {r} ^ {4} / \mathbf {R} _ {0} ^ {4}.
$$

(c) The equation for a straight line is

$$
r = L / \cos (\theta - a)
$$

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {r}}{\mathrm {d} \theta} = \frac {\sin (\theta - \alpha)}{\cos^ {2} (\theta - \alpha)} L
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \left(\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {r}}{\mathrm {d} \theta}\right) ^ {2} + \mathbf {r} ^ {2} = \mathbf {L} ^ {2} \left[ \frac {\sin^ {2} (\theta - \alpha)}{\cos^ {4} (\theta - \alpha)} \right. \\ \left. + \frac {1}{\cos^ {2} (\theta - a)} \right] = \frac {\mathrm {L} ^ {2}}{\cos^ {4} (\theta - a)} = \frac {\mathrm {r} ^ {4}}{\mathrm {L} ^ {2}} . \\ \end{array}
$$

![](images/2525a464fd73533cb37f597f50a12126f10460852a35d3cac9c91517607e5134.jpg)

Thus, all straight lines satisfy the geodesic equation.

Solution 7.4. The nonvanishing derivatives of the metric tensor are $\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x},t} = -\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{tt},t} = -2 / t^3$ . Thus the nonvanishing connection coefficients are

$$
\Gamma_ {t t t} = 1 / t ^ {3}, \quad \Gamma_ {\mathbf {x x t}} = \Gamma_ {\mathbf {x t x}} = - \Gamma_ {\mathbf {t x x}} = - 1 / t ^ {3}.
$$

To find the geodesics it is easiest to proceed directly from their definition as curves of extreme length. Let a geodesic be $\mathbf{x}(t)$ . Then (dot represents $d/dt$ )

$$
0 = \delta \int (\mathrm {d} s ^ {2}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} = \delta \int (1 - \dot {x} ^ {2}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \frac {\mathrm {d} t}{t}.
$$

The Euler-Lagrange equation for this extremization is

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} t} \left[ \frac {\dot {\mathbf {x}}}{t \left(1 - \dot {\mathbf {x}} ^ {2}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \right] = 0.
$$

This is easily solved by letting $\tanh \theta \equiv \dot{\mathbf{x}}$ :

$$
\frac {\sinh \theta}{t} = \text {c o n s t a n t},
$$

which integrates to

$$
\left(\mathbf {x} - \mathbf {x} _ {0}\right) ^ {2} = t ^ {2} + \mathsf {a} ^ {2}.
$$

Thus the geodesics are hyperbolas asymptotic to the light cones:

![](images/f491641d3ba013c5b558b4bf93de370989935a1a5226f91188aa53e0d1f785c2.jpg)

Solution 7.5. The calculation of $\mathbf{g}_{\alpha \beta ;\gamma}$ is straightforward in a coordinate basis:

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \beta ; \gamma} = \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \beta , \gamma} - \mathbf {g} _ {\sigma \beta} \Gamma_ {a \gamma} ^ {\sigma} - \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \sigma} \Gamma_ {\beta \gamma} ^ {\sigma} \\ = \mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \beta , \gamma} - 2 \Gamma_ {(\beta \alpha) \gamma} \\ = \mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \beta , \gamma} - \mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \beta , \gamma} = 0. \\ \end{array}
$$

Solution 7.6. In a coordinate frame:

$$
\Gamma_ {\nu \lambda} ^ {\mu} = \frac {1}{2} \left(\mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \nu , \lambda} + \mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \lambda , \nu} - \mathrm {g} _ {\nu \lambda , a}\right) \mathrm {g} ^ {\mu a}. \tag {1}
$$

(a) If the metric is diagonal, $\alpha$ must be equal to $\mu$ . But since $\mu \neq \nu \neq \lambda$ , all of the terms in parentheses in Equation (1) obviously vanish.

(b) $\Gamma_{\lambda \lambda}^{\mu} = \frac{1}{2}\left(\mathbf{g}_{\alpha \lambda ,\lambda} + \mathbf{g}_{\alpha \lambda ,\lambda} - \mathbf{g}_{\lambda \lambda ,\alpha}\right)\mathbf{g}^{\mu \alpha},$ setting $\nu = \lambda$ in Equation (1).

$$
\Gamma_ {\lambda \lambda} ^ {\mu} = - \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {g} _ {\lambda \lambda , a} \mathbf {g} ^ {\mu a} = - \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {g} _ {\lambda \lambda , a} (\mathbf {g} _ {\mu a}) ^ {- 1} = - \frac {1}{2} (\mathbf {g} _ {\mu \mu}) ^ {- 1} \mathbf {g} _ {\lambda \lambda , \mu}.
$$

Here we have repeatedly used diagonality of the metric [e.g. $\mathbf{g}^{\mu \alpha} = (\mathbf{g}_{\mu \alpha})^{-1}]$ .

$$
\begin{array}{l} \Gamma_ {\mu \lambda} ^ {\mu} = \frac {1}{2} \left(\mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \mu , \lambda} + \mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \lambda , \mu} - \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \lambda , \alpha}\right) \left(\mathrm {g} _ {\mu \alpha}\right) ^ {- 1} \tag {c} \\ = \frac {1}{2} \left(\mathrm {g} _ {\mu \mu}\right) ^ {- 1} \left(\mathrm {g} _ {\mu \mu , \lambda}\right) = \frac {\partial}{\partial \mathrm {x} ^ {\lambda}} \left(\log \left(\left| \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \mu} \right| ^ {\frac {1}{2}}\right)\right). \\ \end{array}
$$

(d) Setting $\lambda = \mu$ in (c) gives

$$
\Gamma_ {\mu \mu} ^ {\mu} = \frac {\partial}{\partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\mu}} \left(\log \left(| \mathbf {g} _ {\mu \mu} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}\right)\right) .
$$

Solution 7.7.

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \beta , \gamma} = \nabla_ {\gamma} \left(\mathbf {e} _ {\alpha} \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {\beta}\right) = \left(\nabla_ {\gamma} \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha}\right) \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {\beta} + \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha} \cdot \left(\nabla_ {\gamma} \mathbf {e} _ {\beta}\right) \\ = \Gamma_ {\alpha \gamma} ^ {\mu} \mathbf {e} _ {\mu} \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {\beta} + \Gamma_ {\beta \gamma} ^ {\mu} \mathbf {e} _ {\mu} \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha} \\ = \Gamma_ {\beta a \gamma} + \Gamma_ {a \beta \gamma}. \\ \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \mu} \mathbf {g} ^ {\mu \beta} = \delta_ {\alpha} ^ {\beta} \tag {b} \\ \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \mu , \gamma} \mathbf {g} ^ {\mu \beta} + \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \mu} \mathbf {g} ^ {\mu \beta}, _ {\gamma} = 0 \\ \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \mu} \mathbf {g} ^ {\mu \beta} _ {, \gamma} = - \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \mu , \gamma} \mathbf {g} ^ {\mu \beta}. \\ \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {(c)} \quad \mathrm {g} ^ {\alpha \beta} _ {, \gamma} = - \mathrm {g} _ {\lambda \mu , \gamma} \mathrm {g} ^ {\mu \beta} \mathrm {g} ^ {\lambda \alpha} = - (\Gamma_ {\lambda \mu \gamma} + \Gamma_ {\mu \lambda \gamma}) \mathrm {g} ^ {\mu \beta} \mathrm {g} ^ {\lambda \alpha} \\ = - \Gamma_ {\mu \gamma} ^ {\alpha} \mathsf {g} ^ {\mu \beta} - \Gamma_ {\lambda \gamma} ^ {\beta} \mathsf {g} ^ {\lambda a} . \\ \end{array}
$$

(Here we have used the result in (a).)

(d) For any matrix $\| \mathbf{g}_{\alpha \beta}\|$

$$
\left(\log \det  \left\| \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \beta} \right\|\right) _ {, a} = \operatorname {T r} \left\| \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \beta} \right\| ^ {- 1} \left\| \mathbf {g} _ {\mu \nu , a} \right\|,
$$

therefore

$$
(\log \mathrm {g}), a = \mathrm {g} ^ {\mu \nu} \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \nu}, a
$$

and

$$
\frac {\mathrm {g} _ {, a}}{\mathrm {g}} = \mathrm {g} ^ {\mu \nu} \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \nu , a}.
$$

And so, by (b)

$$
\mathrm {g} _ {, a} = \mathrm {g g} ^ {\mu \nu} \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \nu , a} = - \mathrm {g g} _ {\mu \nu} \mathrm {g} ^ {\mu \nu}, a
$$

(e) In a coordinate frame, $\Gamma_{\alpha \beta}^{\mu} = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{g}^{\mu \nu}(\mathbf{g}_{\nu \alpha ,\beta} + \mathbf{g}_{\nu \beta ,\alpha} - \mathbf{g}_{\alpha \beta ,\nu})$ and since the last two terms cancel $\Gamma_{\alpha \beta}^{\alpha} = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{g}^{\alpha \nu}\mathbf{g}_{\nu \alpha ,\beta}$ . Thus, by (d)

$$
\Gamma_ {a \beta} ^ {a} = \frac {1}{2} g, \beta / g = \frac {1}{2} (\log | g |), \beta = (\log | g | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}), \beta .
$$

(f) $\mathbf{g}^{\mu \nu}\Gamma_{\mu \nu}^{\alpha} = -\mathbf{g}^{\alpha \beta},\beta -\Gamma^{\beta}_{\lambda \beta}\mathbf{g}^{\lambda \alpha}$ (putting $\beta = \gamma$ in (c) and summing)

$$
\begin{array}{l} = - \mathrm {g} ^ {\alpha \beta}, \beta - (\log | \mathrm {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}), \lambda \mathrm {g} ^ {\lambda \alpha} \quad \text {b y (e)} \\ = - \mathrm {g} _ {, \nu} ^ {a \nu} - \left(\log \left| \mathrm {g} \right| ^ {\frac {1}{2}}\right) _ {, \nu} \mathrm {g} ^ {a \nu} \quad \text {c h a n g i n g d u m m y i n d i c e s} \\ = - \mathrm {g} ^ {a \nu} _ {, \nu} - | \mathrm {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} _ {, \nu} \mathrm {g} ^ {a \nu} | \mathrm {g} | ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \\ = - \frac {1}{| \mathrm {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \left(\mathrm {g} ^ {\alpha \nu} | \mathrm {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}\right) _ {, \nu}. \\ \end{array}
$$

(g) $\mathbf{A}_{;a}^{\alpha} = \mathbf{A}_{,a}^{\alpha} + \Gamma_{\beta a}^{\alpha}\mathbf{A}^{\beta} = \mathbf{A}_{,a}^{\alpha} + \frac{1}{|\mathbf{g}|^{\frac{1}{2}}}(|\mathbf{g}|^{\frac{1}{2}}),\beta \mathbf{A}^{\beta}$ by (e)

$$
= \frac {1}{| g | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \left(| g | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} A ^ {a}\right), a
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {(h)} \quad \mathrm {A} _ {\alpha} ^ {\beta}; \beta = \mathrm {A} _ {\alpha} ^ {\beta}, \beta + \Gamma_ {\mu \beta} ^ {\beta} \mathrm {A} _ {\alpha} ^ {\mu} - \Gamma_ {\alpha \beta} ^ {\lambda} \mathrm {A} _ {\lambda} ^ {\beta} \\ = \mathrm {A} _ {\alpha} ^ {\beta}, \beta + \frac {1}{| \mathrm {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} (| \mathrm {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}), \mu \mathrm {A} _ {\alpha} ^ {\mu} - \Gamma_ {\alpha \mu} ^ {\lambda} \mathrm {A} _ {\lambda} ^ {\mu} \\ = \frac {1}{| \mathbf {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \left(| \mathbf {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {A} _ {\alpha} ^ {\beta}\right), \beta - \Gamma_ {\alpha \mu} ^ {\lambda} \mathbf {A} _ {\lambda} ^ {\mu} \\ \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {A} ^ {\alpha \beta}; \beta = \mathbf {A} ^ {\alpha \beta}, \beta + \Gamma_ {\mu \beta} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {A} ^ {\mu \beta} + \Gamma_ {\mu \beta} ^ {\beta} \mathbf {A} ^ {\alpha \mu} \tag {i} \\ = \mathrm {A} ^ {\alpha \beta}, \beta + \Gamma_ {\mu \beta} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {A} ^ {\mu \beta} + \frac {1}{| \mathrm {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} (| \mathrm {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}), \mu \mathrm {A} ^ {\alpha \mu} \\ = \frac {1}{| \mathrm {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \left(\mathrm {A} ^ {\alpha \beta} | \mathrm {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}\right), \beta + \Gamma_ {\mu \beta} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {A} ^ {\mu \beta}. \\ \end{array}
$$

But $\Gamma_{\mu \beta}^{\alpha} = \Gamma_{(\mu \beta)}^{\alpha}$ in a coordinate frame, so if $\mathbf{A}^{\mu \beta} = \mathbf{A}^{[\mu \beta]}$ , the last term vanishes, and

$$
\mathbf {A} ^ {\alpha \beta}; \beta = \frac {1}{| \mathbf {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} (| \mathbf {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {A} ^ {\alpha \beta}), \beta .
$$

$$
\square S = \left(S, _ {\alpha} g ^ {\alpha \beta}\right); \beta = \frac {1}{\left| g \right| ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \left(\left| g \right| ^ {\frac {1}{2}} S, _ {\alpha} g ^ {\alpha \beta}\right), \beta . \tag {j}
$$

(Here (g) has been used.)

Solution 7.8. Under a coordinate transformation,

$$
\mathbf {A} _ {\overline {{\mu}} \overline {{\nu}}} = \frac {\partial_ {\mathbf {x}} \overline {{\mu}}}{\partial_ {\mathbf {x}} \overline {{\mu}}} \frac {\partial_ {\mathbf {x}} \overline {{\nu}}}{\partial_ {\mathbf {x}} \overline {{\nu}}} \mathbf {A} _ {\mu \nu}
$$

implies that

$$
\overline {{{\mathbf {A}}}} = \det  \left(\frac {\partial_ {\mathbf {x}} ^ {\mu}}{\partial_ {\mathbf {x}} \overline {{{\boldsymbol {\mu}}}}}\right) \det  \left(\mathbf {A} _ {\mu \nu}\right) \det  \left(\frac {\partial_ {\mathbf {x}} ^ {\nu}}{\partial_ {\mathbf {x}} \overline {{{\boldsymbol {\nu}}}}}\right) ^ {\mathbf {T}} = \mathbf {J} ^ {2} \mathbf {A}
$$

where $J$ is the Jacobian, $\det(\partial_{\mathbf{x}}^{\mu} / \partial_{\mathbf{x}}^{\overline{\mu}})$ , and where the superscript $T$ denotes matrix transpose. We want $A_{;a}$ to transform according to the vector analog of the transformation law for $A$ . (A is called a "density of weight 2".) Since $A_{;a}$ must be linear in $A$ , set

$$
\mathrm {A} _ {; a} = \mathrm {A} _ {, a} + \mathrm {K} _ {a} \mathrm {A}
$$

where $\mathbf{K}_a$ must be determined. This can be done by demanding that $\mathbf{g}$ , the determinant of the metric tensor, have vanishing covariant derivative:

$$
0 = \mathbf {g} _ {; \alpha} = \mathbf {g} _ {, \alpha} + \mathbf {K} _ {\alpha} \mathbf {g} = 2 \mathbf {g} \Gamma^ {\beta} _ {\beta \alpha} + \mathbf {K} _ {\alpha} \mathbf {g}
$$

by Problem 7.7. Thus

$$
\mathbf {K} _ {\alpha} = - \mathbf {\nabla} 2 \Gamma^ {\beta} \mathbf {\nabla} _ {\beta \alpha} \mathbf {\nabla}.
$$

The generalization to densities of weight $W$ (i.e. they transform with a $J^W$ ) can be done by considering powers of $g$ . The result is that

$$
\mathbf {K} _ {\alpha} = - \mathbf {\nabla} \mathbf {W} \Gamma^ {\beta} \boldsymbol {\beta} _ {\alpha} \mathbf {\nabla}.
$$

Solution 7.9. A geodesic with tangent vector $\mathbf{u}$ is spacelike, timelike, or null according to whether $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{u}$ is $>0$ , $=0$ , $<0$ . But $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{u}$ is conserved along the geodesic, since

$$
\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} (\mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {u}) = 2 \mathbf {u} \cdot \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {u} = 0,
$$

since the geodesic equation is $\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{u} = 0$

Solution 7.10. The integral for length is

$$
\int \mathrm {d} \mathbf {s} = \int \left(- \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \beta} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {\alpha}}{\mathrm {d} s} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {\beta}}{\mathrm {d} s}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d} \mathbf {s}.
$$

To extremize length take $\delta \int \mathrm{d}s = 0$ . According to the Euler-Lagrange equations

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} \mathrm {s}} \left(\mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \beta} \mathrm {u} ^ {\alpha}\right) = \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \gamma , \beta} \mathrm {u} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {u} ^ {\gamma} \tag {1}
$$

where $\left(-\mathbf{g}_{\alpha \beta}\frac{\mathrm{d}\mathbf{x}^{\alpha}}{\mathrm{d}s}\frac{\mathrm{d}\mathbf{x}^{\beta}}{\mathrm{d}s}\right) = 1$ and $\mathbf{u}^{\alpha}\equiv \frac{\mathrm{d}\mathbf{x}^{\alpha}}{\mathrm{d}s}$ have been used. Now since

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} s} \left(\mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \beta} \mathbf {u} ^ {\alpha}\right) = \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \beta} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {u} ^ {\alpha}}{\mathrm {d} s} + \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {\gamma}}{\mathrm {d} s} \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \beta , \gamma} \mathbf {u} ^ {\alpha} = \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \beta} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {u} ^ {\alpha}}{\mathrm {d} s} + \mathbf {u} ^ {\gamma} \mathbf {u} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \beta , \gamma}
$$

Equation (1) becomes

$$
\mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \beta} \frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathrm {x} ^ {\alpha}}{\mathrm {d s} ^ {2}} + \mathrm {u} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {u} ^ {\gamma} \left(\mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \beta , \gamma} - \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \gamma , \beta}\right) = 0. \tag {2}
$$

Now, use

$$
\mathsf {u} ^ {\alpha} \mathsf {u} ^ {\gamma} \mathsf {g} _ {\alpha \beta , \gamma} = \mathsf {u} ^ {\alpha} \mathsf {u} ^ {\gamma} \cdot \frac {1}{2} \left(\mathsf {g} _ {\alpha \beta , \gamma} + \mathsf {g} _ {\gamma \beta , a}\right)
$$

and multiply Equation (2) by $g^{\beta \tau}$ to obtain

$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathbf {x} ^ {\tau}}{\mathrm {d s} ^ {2}} + \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {g} ^ {\beta \tau} \left(\mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \beta , \gamma} + \mathrm {g} _ {\gamma \beta , \alpha} - \mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \gamma , \beta}\right) \mathrm {u} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {u} ^ {\gamma} \tag {3} \\ = \frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathbf {x} ^ {\tau}}{\mathrm {d s} ^ {2}} + \Gamma_ {\alpha \gamma} ^ {\tau} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {\alpha}}{\mathrm {d s}} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {\gamma}}{\mathrm {d s}} = 0. \\ \end{array}
$$

Solution 7.11. Define a new curve parametrization by the functional relationship $\mathbf{s} = \mathbf{f}(\lambda)$ . Then derivatives are related by

$$
\frac {d}{d \lambda} = f ^ {\prime} \frac {d}{d s} \quad \frac {d ^ {2}}{d \lambda^ {2}} = f ^ {\prime \prime} \frac {d}{d s} + f ^ {\prime 2} \frac {d ^ {2}}{d s ^ {2}}, \tag {1}
$$

where $\mathbf{f}' \equiv \frac{\mathrm{df}}{\mathrm{d}\lambda}$ . With the new parametrization then the geodesic equation becomes

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathbf {x} ^ {\alpha}}{\mathrm {d s} ^ {2}} + \frac {\mathrm {f} ^ {\prime \prime}}{\mathrm {f} ^ {\prime 2}} \frac {\mathrm {d x} ^ {\alpha}}{\mathrm {d s}} + \Gamma^ {\alpha} _ {\beta \gamma} \frac {\mathrm {d x} ^ {\beta}}{\mathrm {d s}} \frac {\mathrm {d x} ^ {\gamma}}{\mathrm {d s}} = 0. \tag {2}
$$

Equation (2) is in the "standard form" (s an affine parametrization) if the second term vanishes. For s to be an affine parameter, f" must vanish i.e. s and $\lambda$ must be linearly related.

Solution 7.12. The components of $\nabla_{\mathbf{p}}\mathbf{p}$ are

$$
(\nabla_ {\mathbf {p}} \mathbf {p}) ^ {\beta} = \operatorname {m u} ^ {\alpha} \left(\mathbf {p} ^ {\beta} _ {, a} + \Gamma^ {\beta} _ {\sigma a} \mathbf {p} ^ {\sigma}\right) = \operatorname {m} \left(\frac {\mathrm {d p} ^ {\beta}}{\mathrm {d} \tau} + \mathrm {u} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {p} ^ {\sigma} \Gamma^ {\beta} _ {\sigma a}\right). \tag {1}
$$

In flat space time, one can always find a global coordinate system (Minkowski coordinates) in which all of the Christoffel symbols vanish. In that coordinate system, conservation of 4-momentum, $\frac{\mathrm{d}\mathbf{p}}{\mathrm{d}\tau} = 0$ , can be written as

$$
\nabla_ {\mathbf {p}} \mathbf {p} = 0 \tag {2}
$$

by Equation (1), but Equation (2) is a tensor equation so it must be the correct expression for momentum conservation in any frame. The momentum vector is proportional to the 4-velocity of particles with mass; for these particles $\mathbf{p} \cdot \mathbf{p} = -\mathfrak{m}^2$ , so the geodesics are timelike.

Solution 7.13. Let $\lambda$ be an affine parameter such that $p^{\alpha} = \mathrm{d}x^{\alpha} / \mathrm{d}\lambda$ ; then for geodesic motion of the particle

$$
0 = (\nabla_ {\mathbf {p}} \mathbf {p}) \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {1} = \mathbf {p} _ {1; \alpha} \mathbf {p} ^ {\alpha} = \frac {\mathrm {d p _ {1}}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} - \mathbf {p} _ {\sigma} \Gamma_ {\alpha 1} ^ {\sigma} \mathbf {p} ^ {\alpha},
$$

and therefore

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {p} _ {1}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} = \mathbf {p} ^ {\sigma} \mathbf {p} ^ {\alpha} \Gamma_ {(\sigma \alpha) 1} = \mathbf {p} ^ {\sigma} \mathbf {p} ^ {\alpha} \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {g} _ {\sigma \alpha , 1} = 0.
$$

Solution 7.14. Start with the geodesic equation

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathbf {x} ^ {a}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda^ {2}} + \Gamma_ {\beta \gamma} ^ {a} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {\beta}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {\gamma}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} = 0 .
$$

Change variables from affine parameter $\lambda$ to coordinate time $t$ and use

$$
0 = d t ^ {2} + \left(g _ {i j} / g _ {0 0}\right) d x ^ {i} d x ^ {k} \tag {1}
$$

to get

$$
\mathbf {g} _ {\mathrm {j k}} \frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathbf {k}}}{\mathrm {d} t ^ {2}} + \Gamma_ {\mathrm {j k l}} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathbf {k}}}{\mathrm {d} t} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {\ell}}{\mathrm {d} t} - \Gamma_ {\mathrm {j 0 0}} \frac {\mathbf {g} _ {\mathrm {k l}}}{\mathbf {g} _ {0 0}} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathbf {k}}}{\mathrm {d} t} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {\ell}}{\mathrm {d} t} + \frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathrm {t / d} \lambda^ {2}}{(\mathrm {d} t / \mathrm {d} \lambda) ^ {2}} \mathbf {g} _ {\mathrm {j k}} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathbf {k}}}{\mathrm {d} t} = 0 .
$$

Combine this with the time part of the geodesic equation

$$
\frac {\left(\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathrm {t} / \mathrm {d} \lambda^ {2}\right)}{\left(\mathrm {d t} / \mathrm {d} \lambda\right) ^ {2}} = - 2 \Gamma_ {0 \mathrm {k} 0} \frac {\mathrm {d x} ^ {\mathrm {k}} / \mathrm {d t}}{\mathrm {g} _ {0 0}}
$$

and use the expression for the $\Gamma$ 's in terms of the metric to get

$$
\gamma_ {j k} \frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} x ^ {k}}{\mathrm {d} t ^ {2}} + \frac {1}{2} \left(\gamma_ {j k, l} + \gamma_ {j l, k} - \gamma_ {k l, j}\right) \frac {\mathrm {d} x ^ {k}}{\mathrm {d} t} \frac {\mathrm {d} x ^ {\ell}}{\mathrm {d} t} = 0, \tag {2}
$$

where $\gamma_{jk} \equiv -g_{jk} / g_{00}$ . Notice that this is just a geodesic equation with affine parameter $t$ in the 3-dimensional manifold with metric $\gamma_{jk}$ . From Equation (1) it is clear then that the solution of Equation (2) extremizes $\int dt$ ; Fermat's principle holds. [See also C. Moller, The Theory of Relativity (Oxford University Press, 2nd ed., 1972), p. 308.]

# Solution 7.15.

(a) The geodesic is the path between two velocities which minimizes the arc-length between them; but arc-length in the velocity space is just the magnitude of a small change of velocity. Since a rocket expends fuel monotonically for the boost it requires, the geodesics of velocity space are paths of minimum fuel use.

(b) We need to find the velocity geodesic connecting $\underline{\mathbf{V}}_1$ and $\underline{\mathbf{V}}_2$ . In principle, we could solve the geodesic equation for the metric in Problem 6.8, but this is very tedious. An easier way is to notice that, by symmetry, a geodesic passing through the origin of the velocity space coordinates is $\theta = \phi = \text{constant}$ , $\chi = s$ (where $s$ is an affine parameter). To get a more general geodesic let us view this geodesic from a moving frame. Since $\chi = \tanh^{-1}\overline{\mathbf{V}}$ is a monotonic function of $\rho = \gamma \overline{\mathbf{V}}$ [where $\gamma \equiv (1 - \overline{\mathbf{V}}^2)^{-\frac{1}{2}}$ ], we can write the geodesics in terms of the non-affine parameter $\rho(s)$ . In terms of this parameter $\gamma = (1 + \rho^2)^{\frac{1}{2}}$ and, if we choose the geodesic to be in the $x$ -direction,

$$
u = \left[ \left(1 + \rho^ {2}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}, \rho , 0, 0 \right] \quad - \infty <   \rho <   + \infty .
$$

A boost in the $x$ -direction just takes this trajectory onto itself so we need only find its transformation under a perpendicular boost, say in the $y$ -direction. Under a boost by $\beta$ in the $y$ -direction $u$ becomes:

$$
\mathbf {u} ^ {\prime} = \left[ \gamma^ {\prime} \left(1 + \rho^ {2}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}, \rho , \left(1 + \rho^ {2}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \gamma^ {\prime} \beta , 0 \right] \tag {1}
$$

where $\gamma' = (1 - \beta^2)^{-\frac{1}{2}}$ .

The 4-velocity in Equation (1) may be represented now in terms of the corresponding 3-velocity

$$
\underline {{\mathbf {V}}} / (1 - \mathbf {V} ^ {2}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} = \rho \underline {{\mathfrak {n}}} + (1 + \rho^ {2}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \gamma^ {\prime} \beta \underline {{\mathfrak {m}}} \tag {2}
$$

where $\underline{\mathfrak{n}}$ and $\underline{\mathfrak{m}}$ are perpendicular unit vectors, and may be manipulated as if they lived in a cartesian space. We can solve for $\mathbf{V}^2$ and write Equation (2) as

$$
\underline {{\mathbf {V}}} = \left[ \frac {1 - \beta^ {2}}{1 + \rho^ {2}} \right] ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \rho \underline {{\mathbf {n}}} + \beta \underline {{\mathbf {m}}}. \tag {3}
$$

This equation gives us the general geodesic of velocity space. One picks a $\beta (|\beta | < 1)$ , two perpendicular vectors $\mathfrak{m}$ , $\mathfrak{n}$ and gets a geodesic parameterized by $\rho (-\infty < \rho < +\infty)$ . From Equation (3) it is clear that the general geodesic is a "straight line." (This does not mean the space is flat!)

For our problem there are two cases. In case (i), in which either angle $\mathrm{OV}_1\mathrm{V}_2$ or $\mathrm{OV}_2\mathrm{V}_1$ is obtuse, the distance of closest approach to the origin $\mathrm{O}$ of the geodesic (straight line) connecting $\underline{\mathbf{V}}_1$ and $\underline{\mathbf{V}}_2$ is simply $|\underline{\mathbf{V}}_1|$ or $|\underline{\mathbf{V}}_2|$ . In case (ii) in which both $\mathrm{OV}_1\mathrm{V}_2$ and $\mathrm{OV}_2\mathrm{V}_1$ are acute, the distance of closest approach $\beta$ is (by simple geometric arguments)

$$
\beta = \frac {\left| \underset {\sim} {V} _ {1} \times \underset {\sim} {V} _ {2} \right|}{\left| \underset {\sim} {V} _ {1} - \underset {\sim} {V} _ {2} \right|}.
$$

![](images/60a250f525f38f8a133fca4225c84a922eb564f105b4b8d198e2475bd05d5291.jpg)  
(i)

![](images/d1fff9baa876d953b3fc5f5f8d003c1a1bb3a7477ed365f2f4c9e249a12b3896.jpg)  
(ii)

Solution 7.16. A is parallel transported along the $\phi$ -coordinate line (i.e., $\theta = \text{constant}$ ), so

$$
0 = \mathrm {A} _ {; \phi} ^ {\alpha} = \mathrm {A} _ {, \phi} ^ {\alpha} + \Gamma_ {\beta \phi} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {A} ^ {\beta}. \tag {1}
$$

The only nonzero Christoffel symbols are $\Gamma_{\phi \phi}^{\theta} = -\sin \theta \cos \theta$ and $\Gamma_{\theta \phi}^{\phi} = \cot \theta$ , so Equation (1) gives

$$
\mathbf {A} ^ {\theta}, \phi - \sin \theta \cos \theta \mathbf {A} ^ {\phi} = 0 \tag {2}
$$

$$
\mathbf {A} _ {, \phi} ^ {\phi} + \cot \theta \mathbf {A} ^ {\theta} = 0. \tag {3}
$$

Throughout this problem, $\theta$ is kept constant at $\theta_0$ . Equations (2) and (3) are easily solved, e.g., by differentiating Equation (2) with respect to $\phi$ :

$$
\mathbf {A} _ {, \phi \phi} ^ {\theta} = \sin \theta \cos \theta \mathbf {A} _ {, \phi} ^ {\phi} = - \cos^ {2} \theta \mathbf {A} ^ {\theta}.
$$

The solution then is

$$
\mathbf {A} ^ {\theta} = a \cos (\phi \cos \theta) + \beta \sin (\phi \cos \theta)
$$

where $\alpha, \beta$ are constants. Equation (2) now gives

$$
\mathbf {A} ^ {\phi} = - a \sin (\phi \cos \theta) / \sin \theta + \beta \cos (\phi \cos \theta) / \sin \theta .
$$

At $\phi = 0$ , $\mathbf{A} = \mathbf{e}_{\theta}$ , i.e. $\mathbf{A}^{\theta} = \mathbf{1}$ , $\mathbf{A}^{\phi} = 0$ . Thus $a = 1$ , $\beta = 0$ , and

$$
\mathbf {A} ^ {\theta} = \cos (\phi \cos \theta)
$$

$$
\mathbf {A} ^ {\phi} = - \sin (\phi \cos \theta) / \sin \theta .
$$

At $\phi = 2\pi$ , after transport around the circle, the vector is

$$
\mathbf {A} = \cos (2 \pi \cos \theta) \mathbf {e} _ {\theta} - \sin (2 \pi \cos \theta) / \sin \theta \mathbf {e} _ {\phi} \neq \mathbf {e} _ {\theta},
$$

but the magnitude is unchanged

$$
(\mathbf {A} \cdot \mathbf {A}) _ {2 \pi} = \cos^ {2} (2 \pi \cos \theta) \mathbf {e} _ {\theta} \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {\theta} + \sin^ {2} (2 \pi \cos \theta) / \sin^ {2} \theta \mathbf {e} _ {\phi} \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {\phi} = 1 = (\mathbf {A} \cdot \mathbf {A}) _ {0}.
$$

Solution 7.17.

$$
\begin{array}{l} \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \left(\eta_ {\alpha} \beta\right) = \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \left(\mathbf {e} _ {\alpha} \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {\beta}\right) = \left(\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha}\right) \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {\beta} + \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha} \cdot \left(\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {e} _ {\beta}\right) \tag {i} \\ = \mathbf {A} _ {\alpha} ^ {\gamma} \mathbf {e} _ {\gamma} \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {\beta} + \mathbf {A} _ {\beta} ^ {\gamma} \mathbf {e} _ {\gamma} \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha} \\ = \mathrm {A} _ {\alpha \beta} + \mathrm {A} _ {\beta \alpha}. \\ \end{array}
$$

The tensor A must be antisymmetric.

It is conventional to write the transport law in this case as

$$
\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha} = \mathbf {A} _ {\alpha} ^ {\beta} \mathbf {e} _ {\beta} = (\mathbf {A} ^ {\gamma \beta} \mathbf {e} _ {\beta} \otimes \mathbf {e} _ {\gamma}) \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha} \equiv - \boldsymbol {\Omega} \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha} \tag {1}
$$

where $\Omega^{\beta \gamma} = \mathbf{A}^{\beta \gamma}$ is the 4-dimensional version of a 3-dimensional antisymmetric rotation matrix.

(ii) Since $\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{e}_0$ is singled out, decompose $\Omega$ along $\mathbf{u}$ and orthogonal to $\mathbf{u}$ :

$$
\Omega^ {\alpha \beta} = v ^ {\alpha} u ^ {\beta} - u ^ {\alpha} v ^ {\beta} + \omega^ {\alpha \beta} \tag {2}
$$

where $\omega^{\alpha \beta} = -\omega^{\beta \alpha}$ , $\omega^{\alpha \beta} u_{\beta} = 0$ and $v^{a}$ is as yet unspecified; without loss of generality we can take $v \cdot u = 0$ . Now Equation

(1) gives $\Omega \cdot \mathbf{u} = -\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{u} = -\mathbf{a}$

where $\mathbf{a}$ is the 4-acceleration of the observer. But Equation

(2) gives $\Omega \cdot \mathbf{u} = -\mathbf{v}$

so $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{a}$ and hence

$$
\boldsymbol {\Omega} = \mathbf {a} \otimes \mathbf {u} - \mathbf {u} \otimes \mathbf {a} + \boldsymbol {\omega}.
$$

(iii) The tensor $\omega^{\alpha \beta}$ has only three independent components and is spatial since $\boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot \mathbf{u} = 0$ . It therefore represents a purely spatial rotation of the basis vectors and vanishes if the spatial vectors are non-rotating.

One often uses an angular velocity vector $\omega$ , with $\omega \cdot \mathbf{u} = 0$ , to represent the three degrees of freedom in $\omega^{\alpha \beta}$ . The relation between $\omega$ and $\omega^{\alpha \beta}$ is

$$
\omega^ {\alpha \beta} = \varepsilon^ {\alpha \beta \lambda \sigma} \mathsf {u} _ {\lambda} \omega_ {\sigma}, \quad \omega^ {\alpha} = - \frac {1}{2} \varepsilon^ {\alpha \mu \lambda \sigma} \mathsf {u} _ {\mu} \omega_ {\lambda} \sigma .
$$

Note that a spatially non-rotating frame $(\omega = 0)$ satisfies

$$
\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha} = (\mathbf {u} \otimes \mathbf {a} - \mathbf {a} \otimes \mathbf {u}) \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha}.
$$

Any vector $\mathbf{e}_{\alpha}$ satisfying an equation of this form is said to be "Fermi-Walker" transported.

Solution 7.18. Let the two vectors be $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{y}$ . Then the Fermi-Walker transport law reads

$$
\begin{array}{l} \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {x} = (\mathbf {u} \otimes \mathbf {a} - \mathbf {a} \otimes \mathbf {u}) \cdot \mathbf {x} \\ \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {y} = (\mathbf {u} \otimes \mathbf {a} - \mathbf {a} \otimes \mathbf {u}) \cdot \mathbf {y}, \\ \end{array}
$$

where $\mathbf{u}$ is the tangent vector to the curve $\mathcal{C}$ and $\mathbf{a} = \nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{u}$ . Using the product rule we evaluate the change in the dot product along the curve:

$$
\begin{array}{l} \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} (\mathbf {x} \cdot \mathbf {y}) = (\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {x}) \cdot \mathbf {y} + \mathbf {x} \cdot (\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {y}) \\ = (\mathbf {a} \cdot \mathbf {x}) (\mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {y}) - (\mathbf {a} \cdot \mathbf {y}) (\mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {x}) + (\mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {x}) (\mathbf {a} \cdot \mathbf {y}) - (\mathbf {a} \cdot \mathbf {x}) (\mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {y}) = 0. \\ \end{array}
$$

The scalar product is unaltered.

Solution 7.19. Fermi-Walker transport has the differential equation

$$
\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {x} = (\mathbf {u} \otimes \mathbf {a} - \mathbf {a} \otimes \mathbf {u}) \cdot \mathbf {x},
$$

where $\mathbf{u}$ is the tangent vector to the curve and $\mathbf{a} = \nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{u} \equiv \mathrm{D}\mathbf{u} / \mathrm{d}\tau$ . If the curve is a geodesic, it satisfies the geodesic equation $\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{u} = 0$ , so the Fermi-Walker transport law becomes

$$
\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {x} = 0,
$$

which is just the parallel transport equation.

Solution 7.20.

$$
\mathrm {U} _ {\alpha ; \beta} \mathrm {U} ^ {\beta} \mathrm {U} ^ {\alpha} = \mathrm {U} _ {\alpha ; \beta} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {U} ^ {\beta} \mathrm {U} _ {\alpha} = (\nabla_ {\mathbf {U}} \mathbf {U}) \cdot \mathbf {U} \tag {a}
$$

$$
\mathbf {V} _ {; \beta} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {U} ^ {\beta} - \mathbf {U} _ {; \beta} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {V} ^ {\beta} = \nabla_ {\mathbf {U}} \mathbf {V} - \nabla_ {\mathbf {V}} \mathbf {U} \equiv [ \mathbf {U}, \mathbf {V} ] \tag {b}
$$

$$
\mathrm {T} _ {\alpha \beta ; \gamma} \mathbf {V} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {W} ^ {\beta} \mathbf {U} ^ {\gamma} = \mathbf {V} \cdot (\nabla_ {\mathbf {U}} \mathbf {T}) \cdot \mathbf {W} \tag {c}
$$

$$
\mathbf {W} ^ {\alpha ; \beta} \mathbf {V} _ {\beta ; \gamma} \mathbf {U} ^ {\gamma} = \mathbf {W} _ {; \beta} ^ {\alpha} (\mathbf {V} ^ {\beta}; \gamma) \mathbf {U} ^ {\gamma}) = \nabla_ {(\nabla_ {\mathbf {U}} \mathbf {V})} \mathbf {W}
$$

(e) Combine the first two terms of the given expression,

$$
(\mathsf {W} _ {; \gamma} ^ {\alpha} \mathsf {U} ^ {\gamma}) _ {; \beta} \mathbf {U} ^ {\beta} - \mathbf {U} _ {; \beta} ^ {\alpha} (\mathsf {W} _ {; \gamma} ^ {\beta} \mathsf {U} ^ {\gamma}) = \nabla_ {\mathbf {U}} (\nabla_ {\mathbf {U}} \mathbf {\bar {W}}) - \nabla_ {(\nabla_ {\mathbf {U}} \mathbf {\bar {W}})} \mathbf {U} = [ \mathbf {U}, \nabla_ {\mathbf {U}} \mathbf {\bar {W}} ] .
$$

Solution 7.21. The paths of light rays can be obtained from the geometrical optics limit of Maxwell's equations $\mathbf{F}^{\alpha \beta}$ ; $\beta = 0$ . With the identity in Problem 7.7(i), these equations can be written as

$$
\left[ \mathbf {g} ^ {\alpha \tau} \mathbf {g} ^ {\beta \mu} \mathbf {F} _ {\tau \mu} (\dot {-} \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \right], \beta = 0.
$$

Using the spatially isotropic, diagonal form of the metric and noting that $\mathbf{E}_{\mathbf{i}} = \mathbf{F}_{0\mathbf{i}}, \mathbf{B}_{\mathbf{k}} = \varepsilon^{\mathbf{k}\mathbf{j}\ell} \mathbf{F}_{\mathbf{j}\ell}$ , one sees that the above equations have the form

$$
\nabla \cdot (\epsilon \underline {{\mathbf {E}}}) = 0
$$

$$
\nabla \times (\mu^ {- 1} \underline {{\mathbf {B}}}) = \partial (\epsilon \underline {{\mathbf {E}}}) / \partial \mathfrak {t}
$$
