where $\epsilon = \mu = (\mathbf{f} / \mathbf{g}_{00})^{\frac{1}{2}}$ . Thus light moves as if in a medium with an effective index of refraction

$$
n = (\epsilon \mu) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} = (g _ {0 0} / f) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}
$$

Solution 7.22. For clarity first consider the Newtonian case. Let $\mathbf{P}(\underline{\mathbf{v}},\underline{\mathbf{n}})$ be the probability of the velocity being $\underline{\mathbf{v}}$ after $\mathbf{n}$ boosts. We want to find a differential equation for $\mathbf{P}$ . To accomplish this note that if the velocity is $\underline{\mathbf{v}}$ at step $\mathbf{n}$ then at step $\mathbf{n} - 1$ the velocity must have been somewhere in velocity space at a distance $\Delta \mathbf{v}$ from $\underline{\mathbf{v}}$ . By symmetry $\mathbf{P}(\underline{\mathbf{v}},\underline{\mathbf{n}})$ has equal contributions from points on the sphere of radius

$\Delta \mathbf{v}$ about $\underline{\mathbf{v}}$ so we can equate $\mathbb{P}(\underline{\mathbf{v}},\mathbf{n})$ with the isotropic average of $\mathbb{P}$ , at $\mathbf{n} - 1$ , over that sphere:

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {P} (\underline {{v}}, n) = <   \mathrm {P} (\underline {{v}} + \Delta v, n - 1) > _ {\text {s p h e r e}} \\ = (1 / 6) \left[ P \left(\underline {{v}} + \Delta v \underline {{e}} _ {x}, n - 1\right) + P \left(\underline {{v}} + \Delta v \underline {{e}} _ {y}, n - 1\right) + P \left(\underline {{v}} + \Delta v \underline {{e}} _ {z}, n - 1\right) \right. \\ + \mathrm {P} (\underline {{\mathrm {v}}} - \Delta \mathrm {v} \underline {{\mathrm {e}}} _ {\mathrm {x}}, n - 1) + \mathrm {P} (\underline {{\mathrm {v}}} - \Delta \mathrm {v} \underline {{\mathrm {e}}} _ {\mathrm {y}}, n - 1) + \mathrm {P} (\underline {{\mathrm {v}}} - \Delta \mathrm {v} \underline {{\mathrm {e}}} _ {z}, n - 1) ] \\ \approx \mathrm {P} (\underline {{v}}, n - 1) + (\Delta v) ^ {2} \nabla^ {2} \mathrm {P} / 6. \tag {1} \\ \end{array}
$$

The differential equation then, is

$$
\frac {\partial \mathbf {P}}{\partial \mathrm {n}} = \frac {(\Delta \mathbf {v}) ^ {2}}{6} \nabla^ {2} \mathbf {P}, \tag {2}
$$

the standard diffusion equation. Its solution, with $\mathrm{P}(\underline{\mathrm{v}},0) = \delta^3 (\underline{\mathrm{v}})$ is

$$
P (v, n) = (4 \pi) ^ {- \frac {3}{2}} (n \Delta v ^ {2} / 6) ^ {- \frac {3}{2}} \exp \left(\frac {- 3 v ^ {2}}{2 n \Delta v ^ {2}}\right).
$$

In the relativistic problem the same arguments hold except that velocities do not add linearly; we must use the relativistic velocity addition formulas. Since $\Delta v \ll c$ , however, the proper velocity $\Delta v$ does add linearly in a frame momentarily comoving with the rocket ship. Thus, the diffusion Equation (2) is locally valid in velocity space; we only need take into account that the global metric of relativistic velocity space is different. This is done by solving Equation (2) with $\nabla^2$ representing the Laplacian in a curved-metric velocity space, with metric (see Problem 6.8).

$$
\mathrm {d v} _ {\text {p r o p e r}} ^ {2} = \mathrm {d} \Psi^ {2} + \sinh^ {2} \Psi \left(\mathrm {d v} _ {\theta} ^ {2} + \sin^ {2} \theta \mathrm {d v} _ {\phi} ^ {2}\right) \tag {4}
$$

where $\Psi$ is the rapidity parameter, that is $\tanh \Psi \equiv |\underline{\mathbf{v}}|$ . Using the relation [Problem 7.7 (j)]

$$
P _ {; a} ^ {; a} = g ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \left(g ^ {\frac {1}{2}} g ^ {\alpha \beta} P, \beta\right), a \tag {5}
$$

and the fact that by spherical symmetry $\frac{\partial\mathbf{P}}{\partial\mathbf{v}_{\phi}} = \frac{\partial\mathbf{P}}{\partial\mathbf{v}_{\theta}} = 0$ we get

$$
\frac {\partial \mathrm {P}}{\partial \mathrm {n}} = \frac {\Delta \mathrm {v} ^ {2}}{6} \left[ \frac {1}{\sinh^ {2} \Psi} \frac {\partial}{\partial \Psi} \left(\sinh^ {2} \Psi \frac {\partial \mathrm {P}}{\partial \Psi}\right) \right]. \tag {6}
$$

It is not difficult to check that this has the solution

$$
\mathrm {P} (\underset {\sim} {\mathrm {v}}, \mathrm {n}) = (4 \pi) ^ {- \frac {3}{2}} \mathrm {e} ^ {- \mathrm {t}} \mathrm {t} ^ {- \frac {3}{2}} \frac {\Psi}{\sinh \Psi} \mathrm {e} ^ {- \left(\Psi^ {2} / 4 \mathrm {t}\right)}, \tag {7}
$$

where $t \equiv n\Delta v^2 / 6$ . This agrees with the Newtonian solution Equation (3) when $t \ll 1$ , $\Psi \ll 1$ , (no restriction on $\Psi^2 / 4t$ ).

The probability of $\Psi$ between $\Psi$ and $\Psi + \mathrm{d}\Psi$ is (using the velocity-space metric) $4\pi \sinh^2\Psi \mathrm{Pd}\Psi$ which for $t >> 1$ , $\Psi >> 1$ has the asymptotic behavior

$$
\frac {d P}{d \Psi} \approx (4 \pi) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \frac {\Psi}{t ^ {\frac {3}{2}}} e ^ {- \Psi^ {2} / 4 t - t + \Psi} = (4 \pi) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \frac {\Psi}{t ^ {\frac {3}{2}}} e ^ {- t (1 - \frac {\Psi}{2 t}) ^ {2}}.
$$

Only the exponential is of significance here, and this tells us that $\Psi$ has a mean value $<\Psi> \approx 2t$ , and a standard deviation $\Delta\Psi \sim \sqrt{2t}$ , so $\Delta\Psi/<\Psi> \to 0$ as $t \to \infty$ . The mean rapidity $<\Psi>$ increases linearly with number of steps, but the average boost per step is $\Delta v^2/3$ . A sober pilot would increase his rapidity by $\Delta v$ each step, $3c/\Delta v$ times as much. Nevertheless it is amazing that the drunk astronaut does as well as he does; in the Newtonian case velocity or rapidity increased only as $n^{\frac{1}{2}}$ , not as $n$ . The reason here has to do with the Lorentz transformation: if an observer moving away from you chooses a random direction to fire a bullet, the directions look biased outward from you (headlight effect).

# Solution 7.23.

(a) If the hypersurfaces are given by $f =$ constant, then

$$
\mathrm {k} \propto \nabla \mathrm {f} \quad \mathrm {k} _ {\mu} = \mathrm {h f} _ {\mu}
$$

and so

$$
\mathrm {k} _ {\mu ; \nu} = \mathrm {h}, \nu^ {\mathrm {f}}, \mu + \mathrm {h f}, \mu ; \nu \tag {1}
$$

$$
\mathrm {k} _ {[ \mu ; \nu} \mathrm {k} _ {\lambda ]} = \mathrm {h}, [ \nu^ {\mathrm {f}}, \mu^ {\mathrm {h}} \mathrm {f}, \lambda ] + \mathrm {h} ^ {2} \mathrm {f}, [ \mu ; \nu^ {\mathrm {f}}, \lambda ] . \tag {2}
$$

The first term obviously vanishes and the second term vanishes because $\mathbf{f}_{\mu; \nu} = \mathbf{f}_{\nu; \mu}$ . The converse of this, that $\mathbf{k}_{[\mu; \nu]k_{\lambda}}$ implies that $\mathbf{k}$ is hypersurface-orthogonal, is called Frobenius' theorem.

(b) From Equation (1) the extra condition $\mathbf{k}[\mu ;\nu ] = 0$ is equivalent to $\mathbf{k} = \nabla \mathbf{f}$ for some f.

Solution 7.24. Let $\mathbf{k}$ be the tangent vector to the congruence of null curves. Since $\mathbf{k}$ is hypersurface orthogonal,

$$
\mathbf {k} _ {\alpha} = \mathbf {h f} _ {, \alpha}
$$

for some scalar functions $f$ and $h$ (see Problem 7.23). Because $k$ is null,

$$
\mathbf {f} _ {, a} \mathbf {f} ^ {, a} = 0.
$$

From these two equations we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} k _ {\alpha ; \beta} k ^ {\beta} = (h, \beta^ {f}, a + h f, \alpha ; \beta) h f ^ {\prime} \beta \\ f _ {, \alpha ; \beta} f ^ {, \beta} = f _ {, \beta ; \alpha} f ^ {, \beta} = \frac {1}{2} (f _ {, \beta} f ^ {, \beta}); _ {\alpha} = 0 \\ \end{array}
$$

and thus

$$
\mathbf {k} _ {\alpha ; \beta} \mathbf {k} ^ {\beta} = (\mathrm {h}, \beta^ {\mathrm {f}}, \beta) \mathbf {k} _ {\alpha}
$$

so the geodesic equation $\nabla_{\mathbf{k}}\mathbf{k} \propto \mathbf{k}$ is satisfied. If $\mathbf{k}^{\alpha} = \mathrm{d}\mathbf{x}^{\alpha} / \mathrm{d}\lambda$ , we can introduce an affine parameter $\lambda' = \lambda'(\lambda)$ to write the equation in the standard form $\nabla_{\overline{\mathbf{k}}} \overline{\mathbf{k}} = 0$ where $\overline{\mathbf{k}}^{\alpha} = \mathrm{d}\mathbf{x}^{\alpha} / \mathrm{d}\lambda'$ .

Solution 7.25. The general case gives an Euler-Lagrange equation

$$
0 = \frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathbf {F}}{\mathrm {d y} ^ {2}} \left. \right. \frac {\mathrm {d y}}{\mathrm {d s}} \frac {\partial \mathbf {y}}{\partial \bar {\mathbf {x}}} + \frac {\mathrm {d F}}{\mathrm {d y}} \left[ \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d s}} \left(\frac {\partial \mathbf {y}}{\partial \bar {\mathbf {x}}} - \frac {\partial \mathbf {y}}{\partial \bar {\mathbf {x}}}\right)\right].
$$

Since $\mathrm{dy} / \mathrm{ds} = 0$ (for $s$ an affine parameter) and since $\mathrm{dF} / \mathrm{dy} \neq 0$ , this is the same as the Euler-Lagrange equation obtained from $\delta \int y \, \mathrm{d}s = 0$ .

# CHAPTER 8: SOLUTIONS

# Solution 8.1.

(a) The key relations are $< \widetilde{\mathbf{dx}}^a, \partial/\partial \mathbf{x}^\beta > \equiv \widetilde{\mathbf{dx}}^a \cdot \partial/\partial \mathbf{x}^\beta = \delta_\beta^a, (\partial/\partial \mathbf{x}^\alpha) \cdot (\partial/\partial \mathbf{x}^\beta) = \mathbf{g}_\alpha \beta,$ and $\widetilde{\mathbf{dx}}^a \cdot \widetilde{\mathbf{dx}}^\beta = \mathbf{g}^a \beta$ . The answers are:

$$
\begin{array}{c c c c c} 1, & 0, & \mathbf {g} _ {0 1}, & \mathbf {g} ^ {0 1}, & \mathbf {g} ^ {0 0}  . \end{array}
$$

(b) The vector $\mathbf{g}^{1a}\partial/\partial\mathbf{x}^a \equiv \mathbf{g}^{1a}\mathbf{e}_a$ corresponds to $\widetilde{\mathrm{d}}\mathbf{x}^1$ since, for any vector $\mathbf{v}$

$$
\mathbf {v} \cdot \mathbf {g} ^ {1 \alpha} \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha} = \mathbf {v} ^ {\mu} \mathbf {e} _ {\mu} \cdot \mathbf {g} ^ {1 \alpha} \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha} = \mathbf {v} ^ {\mu} \mathbf {g} _ {\mu \alpha} \mathbf {g} ^ {1 \alpha} = \mathbf {v} ^ {\mu} \delta_ {\mu} ^ {1} = \mathbf {v} ^ {1} = <   \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}} ^ {1}, \mathbf {v} >.
$$

Solution 8.2. It is easy to see that $\mathbf{f}$ is just the coordinate $\mathbf{r}$ , that is $\widetilde{\mathrm{df}} = \widetilde{\mathrm{dr}}$ , since

$$
\begin{array}{l} <   \widetilde {\mathrm {d r}}, \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} > = <   \widetilde {\mathrm {d r}}, \mathbf {e} _ {\mathbf {r}} > = 1 \\ <   \widetilde {\mathrm {d r}}, \mathbf {e} _ {\widehat {\theta}} > = \mathrm {r} ^ {- 1} <   \widetilde {\mathrm {d r}}, \mathbf {e} _ {\theta} > = 0. \\ \end{array}
$$

Now suppose there exists a g such that $\widetilde{\mathrm{dg}} = \tilde{\omega}^{\hat{\theta}}$ then

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = \langle \widetilde {\mathrm {d g}}, \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} \rangle = \langle \widetilde {\mathrm {d g}}, \mathbf {e} _ {\mathbf {r}} \rangle = \partial \mathbf {g} / \partial \mathbf {r} \\ 1 = <   \widetilde {\mathrm {d g}}, \mathrm {e} _ {\hat {\theta}} > = \mathrm {r} ^ {- 1} <   \widetilde {\mathrm {d g}}, \mathrm {e} _ {\theta} > = \mathrm {r} ^ {- 1} \partial \mathrm {g} / \partial \theta . \\ \end{array}
$$

Clearly the conditions $\partial \mathbf{g} / \partial \mathbf{r} = 0$ and $\partial \mathbf{g} / \partial \theta = \mathbf{r}$ are incompatible.

Solution 8.3. In a cartesian coordinate basis the condition is that $\sigma_{i,j} = \sigma_{j,i}$ . This is equivalent to the basis-free requirement that the curl of $\pmb{\sigma}$ (the vector equivalent of $\tilde{\pmb{\sigma}}$ ) vanish.

Solution 8.4. Choose a basis $\tilde{\omega}_1 \cdots \tilde{\omega}_N$ . Any p-form can be written as a sum $\sum_{i,j} A_{ij} \cdots (\tilde{\omega}_i \wedge \tilde{\omega}_j \cdots)$ in which there are exactly $p$ elements in each term in parentheses. Since the distributive law holds for wedge multiplication and addition, it will be sufficient to prove the identity for monomial $p$ and $q$ forms and to evaluate e.g.

$$
\left(\tilde {\omega} _ {1} \wedge \tilde {\omega} _ {2} \wedge \dots \wedge \tilde {\omega} _ {\mathrm {p}}\right) \wedge \left(\tilde {\omega} _ {1 ^ {\prime}} \wedge \tilde {\omega} _ {2 ^ {\prime}} \wedge \dots \wedge \tilde {\omega} _ {\mathrm {q} ^ {\prime}}\right).
$$

Wedge multiplication is associative, so the parentheses are not important. First move $\tilde{\omega}_{1}$ , to the extreme left by interchanging it repeatedly with its left neighbor. Each interchange brings in a minus sign, a total of $(-1)^{p}$ . Now move $\tilde{\omega}_{2}$ , left to the second position. This brings in $p$ more minus signs. And continue... By the time we are done with $\tilde{\omega}_{q}$ , the sign will have been changed $pq$ times, hence the desired identity.

Solution 8.5. We show that the two definitions are equivalent by showing that $\mathbf{d}\Omega$ has the same components in an arbitrary coordinate basis when calculated from either definition. Start with the definition in terms of the covariant derivative. Let the components of $\Omega$ be $\Omega_{\alpha \beta \dots \gamma} = \Omega_{[\alpha \beta \dots \gamma]}$ ( $\mathfrak{p}$ indices). Then the components of $\mathbf{d}\Omega$ are

$$
\begin{array}{l} \Omega_ {[ \alpha \beta \dots \gamma ; \delta ]} = \Omega_ {[ \alpha \beta \dots \gamma , \delta ]} - \Gamma_ {\epsilon} [ \alpha \delta \Omega_ {\beta} ^ {\epsilon} \dots \gamma ] \tag {1} \\ - \Gamma_ {\epsilon} [ \beta \delta \Omega_ {a} ^ {\epsilon} \dots \gamma - \dots - \Gamma_ {\epsilon} [ \gamma \delta \Omega_ {a} \beta \dots \epsilon ] = \Omega_ {[ a} \beta \dots \gamma , \delta ] \\ \end{array}
$$

(The $\Gamma$ terms vanish since $\Gamma_{\mu[\nu \sigma]} = 0$ in a coordinate basis.) Now start with

$$
\Omega = \Omega_ {\alpha} \beta \dots \gamma \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\alpha} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\beta} \wedge \dots \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\gamma}.
$$

By property (ii), $\mathbf{d}$ does not disturb the summation convention, so

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {d} \boldsymbol {\Omega} = (\mathrm {d} \Omega_ {\alpha \beta \dots \gamma}) \wedge (\widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\alpha} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\beta} \wedge \dots \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\gamma}) \tag {2} \\ + \Omega_ {\alpha \beta \dots \gamma} \mathrm {d} \left(\widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\alpha} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\beta} \wedge \dots \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\gamma}\right), \\ \end{array}
$$

where we have used property (iv) with $\mathfrak{p} = 0$ since $\Omega_{\alpha \beta \dots \gamma}$ is simply a function (zero form). Now from property (i) we know that

$$
\widetilde {\mathrm {d f}} = \mathrm {f} _ {, a} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {a}
$$

for any zero form $f$ . From property (iii), $<\widetilde{\mathrm{df}}, \partial/\partial x^{\beta}> = <f_{,a} \widetilde{\mathrm{dx}}^{a}, \partial/\partial x^{\beta}> = f_{,b}$ ; thus $\widetilde{\mathrm{d}\Omega}_{\alpha\beta\dots\gamma} = \Omega_{\alpha\beta\dots\gamma,\delta} \widetilde{\mathrm{dx}}^{\delta}$ . The second term in Equation (2) is zero: According to property (iv) it can be reduced to a sum of terms each of which contains a ddx, which vanishes by property (v). Thus Equation (2) becomes

$$
\mathbf {d} \boldsymbol {\Omega} = \Omega_ {\alpha \beta \dots \gamma , \delta} \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}} ^ {\delta} \wedge \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}} ^ {\alpha} \wedge \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}} ^ {\beta} \dots \wedge \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}} ^ {\gamma}.
$$

Since $\mathbf{d}\Omega$ is a $(p + 1)$ form (property (i)), and the wedge product is antisymmetric, the components of $\mathbf{d}\Omega$ are $\Omega_{[a\beta \dots \gamma ,\delta ]}$ as in Equation (1).

Solution 8.6. Define

$$
\mathbf {g} \equiv \left[ x ^ {1} \int_ {0} ^ {1} f \left(\xi x ^ {1}, x ^ {2}, \dots x ^ {n}\right) d \xi \right],
$$

so that $\tilde{\pmb{\beta}} = \mathbf{g}\widetilde{\mathbf{dx}}^2$ and

$$
\mathrm {d} \widetilde {\boldsymbol {\beta}} = \mathrm {g} _ {, i} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {i} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {2} = \mathrm {g} _ {, 1} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {1} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {2} + \mathrm {g} _ {, 3} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {3} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {2} + \dots .
$$

Now

$$
\mathbf {g}, _ {1} = \left[ \int_ {0} ^ {1} f \left(\xi \mathbf {x} ^ {1}, \mathbf {x} ^ {2}, \dots \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {n}}\right) d \xi \right] + \left[ \mathbf {x} ^ {1} \int_ {0} ^ {1} \xi f ^ {\prime} \left(\xi \mathbf {x} ^ {1}, \mathbf {x} ^ {2}, \dots \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {n}}\right) d \xi \right]
$$

where $f'$ indicates a derivative with respect to the first argument. The second term above can be written as

$$
\begin{array}{l} \int_ {0} ^ {1} \mathrm {x} ^ {1} \xi \mathrm {f} ^ {\prime} (\xi \mathrm {x} ^ {1}, \mathrm {x} ^ {2} \dots) \mathrm {d} \xi = \int_ {0} ^ {1} \xi \frac {\partial}{\partial \xi} \mathrm {f} (\xi \mathrm {x} ^ {1}, \mathrm {x} ^ {2}, \dots) \mathrm {d} \xi \\ = \left. \xi \mathrm {f} (\xi \mathrm {x} ^ {1}, \mathrm {x} ^ {2}, \dots) \right| _ {0} ^ {1} - \int_ {0} ^ {1} \mathrm {f} (\xi \mathrm {x} ^ {1}, \mathrm {x} ^ {2}, \dots) \mathrm {d} \xi , \\ \end{array}
$$

where we have integrated by parts; we have then

$$
g _ {, 1} = \xi f (\xi x ^ {1}, x ^ {2}, \dots x ^ {n}) | _ {0} ^ {1} = f (x ^ {1}, x ^ {2}, \dots x ^ {n}).
$$

Next, $\mathbf{d}\alpha = 0$ tells us that

$$
\mathbf {d} \alpha = f _ {, 3} \mathbf {d x} ^ {3} \wedge \mathbf {d x} ^ {1} \wedge \mathbf {d x} ^ {2} + f _ {, 4} \mathbf {d x} ^ {4} \wedge \mathbf {d x} ^ {1} \wedge \mathbf {d x} ^ {2} + \dots = 0 ,
$$

which can only be true if $f_{,3} = f_{,4} = f_{,5} = \dots f_{,n} = 0$ , and hence

$$
\mathbf {g} _ {, 3} = \left[ \mathbf {x} ^ {1} \int_ {0} ^ {1} \mathrm {f} _ {, 3} \left(\xi \mathbf {x} ^ {1}, \mathbf {x} ^ {2}, \dots , \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {n}}\right) \mathrm {d} \xi \right] = 0.
$$

Similarly $\mathbf{g}_{,4} = \mathbf{g}_{,5} = \dots \mathbf{g}_{,n} = 0$ , so that finally

$$
\mathrm {d} \widetilde {\boldsymbol {\beta}} = \mathrm {g}, 1 \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {1} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {2} = \mathrm {f} \left(\mathrm {x} ^ {1}, \mathrm {x} ^ {2}, \dots \mathrm {x} ^ {\mathrm {n}}\right) \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {1} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {2} = \alpha .
$$

Solution 8.7. The 2-form is $\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{F}_{\mu \nu}\widetilde{\mathbf{dx}}^{\mu}\wedge \widetilde{\mathbf{dx}}^{\nu}$ so that

$$
\mathbf {0} = \mathrm {d} \mathbf {F} = \mathbf {F} _ {\mu \nu , \lambda} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\lambda} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\mu} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\nu}
$$

gives us the Maxwell's equations $\mathbf{F}_{[\mu \nu ;\lambda]} = 0$ . Similarly $\mathbf{d}*\mathbf{F} = \mathbf{0}$ gives $*\mathbf{F}_{[\lambda \sigma ;\nu]} = 0$ , but this is equivalent to (see Problem 3.25)

$$
0 = \varepsilon^ {\mu \nu \lambda \sigma} * F _ {\lambda \sigma ; \nu} = 2 * * F ^ {\mu \nu} _ {; \nu} = - 2 F ^ {\mu \nu} _ {; \nu},
$$

and so we have the remaining Maxwell's equations $\mathbf{F}^{\mu \nu}_{;\nu} = 0$

Solution 8.8. At any point on the surface choose a local orthonormal tetrad $\mathbf{e}_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}}, \mathbf{e}_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}}, \mathbf{e}_{\hat{\mathbf{y}}}, \mathbf{e}_{\hat{\mathbf{z}}}$ . If the surface is spacelike, we can choose the tetrad so that $\mathbf{e}_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}}$ is the unit normal. The 3 orthogonal, linearly independent vectors lying in the surface are then spacelike $(\mathbf{e}_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}}, \mathbf{e}_{\hat{\mathbf{y}}}, \mathbf{e}_{\hat{\mathbf{z}}})$ . Now any other orthonormal tetrad we might have chosen is related to the first one by a Lorentz transformation, which does not affect whether a vector is spacelike or not. Hence the three vectors are always spacelike when the surface is spacelike.

In the null case, choose $\mathbf{e}_{\hat{\mathbf{t}}} + \mathbf{e}_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}}$ to be normal to the surface. A null vector is orthogonal to itself, so the 3 orthogonal vectors in the surface are $\mathbf{e}_{\hat{\mathbf{y}}}$ and $\mathbf{e}_{\hat{\mathbf{z}}}$ (spacelike) and $\mathbf{e}_{\hat{\mathbf{t}}} + \mathbf{e}_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}}$ (null). In the timelike case, choose $\mathbf{e}_{\hat{\mathbf{x}}}$ to be normal. Then in the surface, $\mathbf{e}_{\hat{\mathbf{t}}}$ is timelike; $\mathbf{e}_{\hat{\mathbf{y}}}$ and $\mathbf{e}_{\hat{\mathbf{z}}}$ are spacelike. Again these results are unchanged by Lorentz transformations.

Solution 8.9. The volume element is

$$
\mathsf {d} ^ {3} \Sigma_ {\mu} (\mathsf {a}, \mathsf {b}, \mathsf {c}) = \frac {1}{3 !} \varepsilon_ {\mu \nu} \beta \sigma \frac {\partial (\mathbf {x} ^ {\nu} , \mathbf {x} ^ {\beta} , \mathbf {x} ^ {\sigma})}{\partial (\mathsf {a} , \mathsf {b} , \mathsf {c})} \mathsf {d a} \mathsf {d b} \mathsf {d c}.
$$

If we change parameterization from $a, b, c$ to $\alpha, \beta, \gamma$ then

$$
\mathrm {d} a \mathrm {d} b \mathrm {d} c \rightarrow \left| \frac {\partial (a , b , c)}{\partial (\alpha , \beta , \gamma)} \right| d \alpha \mathrm {d} \beta \mathrm {d} \gamma .
$$

Provided $(a, \beta, \gamma)$ has the same orientation as $(a, b, c)$ , the Jacobian is positive (definition of same orientation!), so we can drop the absolute value signs, and

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {d} ^ {3} \Sigma_ {\mu} (\mathrm {a}, \mathrm {b}, \mathrm {c}) \rightarrow \frac {1}{3 !} \varepsilon_ {\mu \nu \rho \sigma} \frac {\partial \left(\mathrm {x} ^ {\nu} , \mathrm {x} ^ {\rho} , \mathrm {x} ^ {\sigma}\right)}{\partial (\mathrm {a} , \mathrm {b} , \mathrm {c})} \frac {\partial (\mathrm {a} , \mathrm {b} , \mathrm {c})}{\partial (\alpha , \beta , \gamma)} \mathrm {d} \alpha \mathrm {d} \beta \mathrm {d} \gamma \\ = \frac {1}{3 !} \varepsilon_ {\mu \nu \rho \sigma} \frac {\partial \left(\mathbf {x} ^ {\nu} , \mathbf {x} ^ {\rho} , \mathbf {x} ^ {\sigma}\right)}{\partial (\alpha , \beta , \gamma)} d \alpha d \beta d \gamma \\ = \mathrm {d} ^ {3} \Sigma_ {\mu} (\alpha , \beta , \gamma) \\ \end{array}
$$

which completes the proof. With differential forms, the invariance is obvious since $\mathbf{d}^3 \Sigma_{\mu}$ can be viewed as a 3-form

$$
\mathrm {d} ^ {3} \Sigma_ {\mu} = \frac {1}{3 !} \varepsilon_ {\mu \nu \rho \sigma} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\nu} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\rho} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\sigma}.
$$

Solution 8.10.

(a) The 2-form $\pmb{\theta}$ and its exterior derivative are

$$
\theta = f ^ {k} d ^ {2} S _ {k} = f ^ {k} \frac {1}{2} \varepsilon_ {k 1 m} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {1} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {m}
$$

$$
\mathrm {d} \theta = f _ {, n} ^ {k} \frac {1}{2} \varepsilon_ {k 1 m} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {n} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {1} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {m} + f ^ {k} \frac {1}{2} \left(\mathrm {d} \varepsilon_ {k 1 m}\right) \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {1} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {m}.
$$

Define the symbol $\left[\mathbf{k}\mathbf{l}\mathbf{m}\right]$ to be $+1(-1)$ if $\mathbf{k},\mathbf{l},\mathbf{m}$ is an even (odd) permutation of 1,2,3, and zero otherwise. We then have

$$
\mathbf {d} \varepsilon_ {\mathbf {k} 1 \mathrm {m}} = \mathbf {d} (| \mathbf {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} [ \mathbf {k} 1 \mathrm {m} ]) = [ \mathbf {k} 1 \mathrm {m} ] \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {g} _ {, \mathbf {n}} \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}} ^ {\mathbf {n}} / | \mathbf {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} = \varepsilon_ {\mathbf {k} 1 \mathrm {m}} \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {g} _ {, \mathbf {n}} \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}} ^ {\mathbf {n}} / \mathbf {g}.
$$

Thus we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {d} \boldsymbol {\theta} = \frac {1}{2} \left(\mathrm {f} _ {, n} ^ {k} + \frac {1}{2} g _ {, n} / g\right) \varepsilon_ {k 1 m} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {n} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {1} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {m} \\ = \frac {1}{2} | \mathbf {g} | ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} (| \mathbf {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {f} ^ {\mathbf {k}}) _ {, \mathbf {n}} \varepsilon_ {\mathbf {k} 1 \mathbf {m}} \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}} ^ {\mathbf {n}} \wedge \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}} ^ {\mathbf {1}} \wedge \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}} ^ {\mathbf {m}}. \\ \end{array}
$$

But in three dimensions, $\widetilde{\mathbf{dx}}^{\mathfrak{n}}\wedge \widetilde{\mathbf{dx}}^{1}\wedge \widetilde{\mathbf{dx}}^{\mathfrak{m}} = |\mathfrak{g}|^{\frac{1}{2}}\varepsilon^{\mathfrak{n}1\mathfrak{m}}\widetilde{\mathbf{dx}}^{\mathfrak{l}}\wedge \widetilde{\mathbf{dx}}^{2}\wedge \widetilde{\mathbf{dx}}^{3}$ and $\varepsilon_{\mathrm{klm}}\varepsilon^{\mathrm{n1m}} = 2\delta_{\mathrm{k}}^{\mathrm{n}},$ so

$$
\mathbf {d} \boldsymbol {\theta} = \mathbf {f} _ {; k} ^ {k} | \mathbf {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}} ^ {1} \wedge \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}} ^ {2} \wedge \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}} ^ {3}.
$$

Thus, in ordinary vector notation, the theorem becomes

$$
\int_ {V} \nabla \cdot \underline {{\mathbf {f}}} d V = \int_ {\partial V} \underline {{\mathbf {f}}} \cdot \underline {{\mathbf {d}}} S.
$$

(b) $\pmb{\theta} = \mathbf{f}^{\mu}\mathbf{d}^{3}\pmb{\Sigma}_{\mu} = \mathbf{f}^{\mu}\frac{1}{3!}\varepsilon_{\mu \alpha \beta \gamma}\widetilde{\mathbf{dx}}^{\alpha}\wedge \widetilde{\mathbf{dx}}^{\beta}\wedge \widetilde{\mathbf{dx}}^{\gamma}$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {d} \boldsymbol {\theta} = \frac {1}{3 !} \left(\mathrm {f} ^ {\mu}, \lambda \varepsilon_ {\mu \alpha \beta \gamma} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\lambda} + \mathrm {f} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {d} \varepsilon_ {\mu \alpha \beta \gamma}\right) \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\alpha} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\beta} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\gamma} \\ = \frac {1}{3 !} \left(\mathrm {f} ^ {\mu}, _ {\lambda} \varepsilon_ {\mu \alpha \beta \gamma} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\lambda} + \mathrm {f} ^ {\mu} \frac {1}{2} \frac {\mathrm {g} , _ {\lambda}}{\mathrm {g}} \varepsilon_ {\mu \alpha \beta \gamma} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\lambda}\right) \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\alpha} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\beta} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\gamma} \\ = \frac {1}{3 !} | \mathrm {g} | ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} (| \mathrm {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} f ^ {\mu}), \lambda^ {\varepsilon} _ {\mu \alpha \beta \gamma} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\lambda} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\alpha} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\beta} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\gamma} \\ = \frac {- 1}{3 ! \left| \mathrm {g} \right| ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \left(\left| \mathrm {g} \right| ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {f} ^ {\mu}\right) _ {, \lambda} \varepsilon_ {\mu a \beta \gamma} \varepsilon^ {\lambda a \beta \gamma} | \mathrm {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {0} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {1} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {2} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {3} \\ = \frac {1}{3 ! \left| \mathbf {g} \right| ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \left(\left| \mathbf {g} \right| ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {f} ^ {\mu}\right) _ {, \lambda} \delta_ {\mu} ^ {\lambda} 3! \left| \mathbf {g} \right| ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}} ^ {0} \wedge \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}} ^ {1} \wedge \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}} ^ {2} \wedge \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}} ^ {3} \\ = \mathrm {f} _ {; \mu} ^ {\mu} \left| \mathrm {g} \right| ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {0} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {1} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {2} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {3} . \\ \end{array}
$$

The theorem then reduces to

$$
\int_ {\Omega} \nabla \cdot \mathbf {f} \left| \mathbf {g} \right| ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d} ^ {4} \mathbf {x} = \int_ {\partial \Omega} \mathbf {f} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {d} ^ {3} \Sigma_ {\mu}.
$$

$$
\boldsymbol {\theta} = \mathbf {F} ^ {\mu \nu} \mathrm {d} ^ {2} \boldsymbol {\Sigma} _ {\mu \nu} = \mathbf {F} ^ {\mu \nu} \frac {1}{2} \varepsilon_ {\mu \nu \alpha \beta} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\alpha} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\beta} \tag {c}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {d} \boldsymbol {\theta} = \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {d} \left(\mathbf {F} ^ {\mu \nu} \varepsilon_ {\mu \nu \alpha \beta}\right) \wedge \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}} ^ {\alpha} \wedge \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}} ^ {\beta} \\ = \frac {1}{2} | \mathbf {g} | ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} (| \mathbf {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {F} ^ {\mu \nu}) _ {, \lambda} \varepsilon_ {\mu \nu \alpha \beta} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\lambda} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\alpha} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\beta} \\ \end{array}
$$

where we have taken $\mathbf{d}\varepsilon_{\mu \nu \alpha \beta}$ as in part b. Now

$$
\begin{array}{l} \varepsilon_ {\mu \nu a \beta} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\lambda} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\alpha} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\beta} = \varepsilon_ {\mu \nu a \beta} (1 / 6) \delta_ {\kappa \sigma \tau} ^ {\lambda a \beta} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\kappa} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\sigma} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\tau} \\ = - (1 / 6) \varepsilon_ {\mu \nu \alpha \beta} \varepsilon^ {\gamma \lambda \alpha \beta} \varepsilon_ {\gamma \kappa \sigma \tau} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\kappa} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\sigma} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\tau} \\ = - \varepsilon_ {\mu \nu a \beta} \varepsilon^ {\gamma \lambda a \beta} \mathrm {d} ^ {3} \boldsymbol {\Sigma} _ {\gamma} \\ = 2 \delta_ {\mu \nu} ^ {\gamma \lambda} \mathrm {d} ^ {3} \boldsymbol {\Sigma} _ {\gamma}, \\ \end{array}
$$

and by Problem 7.7(i) we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {d} \boldsymbol {\theta} = | \mathbf {g} | ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} (| \mathbf {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {F} ^ {\mu \nu}), _ {\lambda} \delta_ {\mu \nu} ^ {\gamma \lambda} \mathbf {d} ^ {3} \boldsymbol {\Sigma} _ {\gamma} \\ = 2 F ^ {\mu \nu} _ {; \nu} d ^ {3} \Sigma_ {\mu}, \\ \end{array}
$$

and finally

$$
\int_ {\partial \Omega} \mathbf {F} ^ {\mu \nu} \mathrm {d} ^ {2} \pmb {\Sigma} _ {\mu \nu} = 2 \int_ {\Omega} \mathbf {F} ^ {\mu \nu} _ {; \nu} \mathrm {d} ^ {3} \pmb {\Sigma} _ {\mu} .
$$

(d) Let $\pmb{\theta} = \mathbf{A}_{\mathbf{k}} \mathrm{d}\mathbf{x}^{\mathbf{k}} = \underline{\mathbf{A}} \cdot \underline{\mathbf{d}}\ell$ , and let $\Omega$ be 2-dimensional; then the exterior derivative is

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {d} \boldsymbol {\theta} = \mathrm {A} _ {\mathrm {k}, \mathrm {j}} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\mathrm {k}} \\ = A _ {k, j} \delta_ {m} ^ {[ k} \delta_ {n} ^ {j ]} \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}} ^ {m} \wedge \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}} ^ {n} \\ = \frac {1}{2} A _ {k, j} \varepsilon^ {r k j} \varepsilon_ {r m n} \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}} ^ {m} \wedge \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}} ^ {n} \\ = (\nabla \times \underline {{\mathbf {A}}}) ^ {\mathrm {r}} \mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathbf {S} _ {\mathrm {r}}, \\ \end{array}
$$

which completes the derivation.

Solution 8.11. Suppose there were such a tensor. What would be its components at some arbitrary point in a freely falling local orthonormal frame? In this frame all the $\mathbf{g}_{\alpha \beta, \mu}$ vanish (because all the $\Gamma_{\alpha \beta \mu}$ vanish), and $\mathbf{g}_{\alpha \beta} = \eta_{\alpha \beta}$ . Thus the tensor must be some algebraic combination of $\eta_{\alpha \beta}$ , "zero," and possibly $\delta^{\alpha}{}_{\beta}$ and $\varepsilon^{a\beta\gamma\delta}$ . You can very quickly convince yourself that the only nontrivial one is $\eta \otimes \eta \otimes \dots \otimes \eta$ (the $\delta$ only renames indices, e.g. $\delta^{\alpha}{}_{\beta}\eta_{\alpha\gamma} = \eta_{\beta\gamma}$ and the $\varepsilon^{a\beta\gamma\delta}$ gives zero by its antisymmetries, or else just gets some of its indices lowered). Since we chose an arbitrary point, the conclusion holds at all points.

Solution 8.12.

(a) The general definition of the $\Gamma$ 's is

$$
\Gamma_ {\mu a \beta} = \mathbf {e} _ {\mu} \cdot \nabla_ {\beta} \mathbf {e} _ {a}.
$$

In a coordinate frame

$$
\begin{array}{l} \Gamma_ {\mu \alpha \beta} - \Gamma_ {\mu \beta \alpha} = \mathbf {e} _ {\mu} \cdot (\nabla_ {\beta} \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha} - \nabla_ {\alpha} \mathbf {e} _ {\beta}) \\ = \mathbf {e} _ {\mu} \cdot [ \mathbf {e} _ {\beta}, \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha} ] \\ = 0, \\ \end{array}
$$

since in a coordinate frame basis vectors commute.

(b) In an orthonormal frame

$$
\begin{array}{l} \Gamma_ {\mu \alpha \beta} + \Gamma_ {\alpha \mu \beta} = \mathbf {e} _ {\mu} \cdot \nabla_ {\beta} \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha} + \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha} \cdot \nabla_ {\beta} \mathbf {e} _ {\mu} \\ = \nabla_ {\beta} \left(\mathbf {e} _ {\mu} \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha}\right) = \nabla_ {\beta} \left(\eta_ {\mu a}\right) = 0. \\ \end{array}
$$

Solution 8.13.

(a) We use the fact ("usual rule for a derivative operator") that for any vector field $\mathbf{v}$

$$
\mathscr {L} _ {\mathbf {X}} <   \tilde {\mathbf {A}}, \mathbf {v} > = <   \mathscr {L} _ {\mathbf {X}} \tilde {\mathbf {A}}, \mathbf {v} > + <   \tilde {\mathbf {A}}, \mathscr {L} _ {\mathbf {X}} \mathbf {v} >. \tag {1}
$$

Thus for the components of the 1-form $\mathcal{L}_{\mathbf{x}}\tilde{\mathbf{A}}$ we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} \left(\mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {x}} \tilde {\mathbf {A}}\right) _ {\alpha} \equiv \mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {x}} \mathbf {A} _ {\alpha} = <   \mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {x}} \tilde {\mathbf {A}}, \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha} > \\ = \mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {x}} <   \tilde {\mathbf {A}}, \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha} > - <   \tilde {\mathbf {A}}, \mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {x}} \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha} > (1) \\ = \mathrm {A} _ {\alpha , \beta} \mathrm {x} ^ {\beta} - <   \tilde {\mathrm {A}}, [ \mathrm {x}, \mathrm {e} _ {\alpha} ] > \\ - \mathrm {A} _ {\alpha , \beta} \mathrm {x} ^ {\beta} - \mathrm {A} _ {\gamma} <   \tilde {\omega} ^ {\gamma}, [ \mathrm {x} ^ {\beta} \mathrm {e} _ {\beta}, \mathrm {e} _ {\alpha} ] > \\ = \mathrm {A} _ {\alpha , \beta} \mathrm {x} ^ {\beta} - \mathrm {A} _ {\gamma} <   \tilde {\omega} ^ {\gamma}, (\mathrm {x} ^ {\beta} \mathrm {c} _ {\beta \alpha} ^ {\delta} \mathrm {e} _ {\delta} - \mathrm {x} ^ {\beta}, _ {\alpha} \mathrm {e} _ {\beta}) > \\ = \mathrm {A} _ {\alpha , \beta} \mathrm {x} ^ {\beta} + \mathrm {x} ^ {\beta}, _ {\alpha} \mathrm {A} _ {\beta} - \mathrm {A} _ {\gamma} \mathrm {x} ^ {\beta} \mathrm {c} _ {\beta a} ^ {\gamma}. (2) \\ \end{array}
$$

The last term vanishes in a coordinate frame. An alternative form is, from Equation (1),

$$
\begin{array}{l} (\mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {x}} \tilde {\mathbf {A}}) _ {a} = \nabla_ {\mathbf {x}} <   \tilde {\mathbf {A}}, \mathbf {e} _ {a} > - <   \tilde {\mathbf {A}}, (\nabla_ {\mathbf {x}} \mathbf {e} _ {a} - \nabla_ {\mathbf {e} _ {a}} \mathbf {x}) > \\ = <   \nabla_ {\mathbf {x}} \tilde {\mathbf {A}}, \mathbf {e} _ {a} > + <   \tilde {\mathbf {A}}, \nabla_ {\mathbf {e} _ {a}} \mathbf {x} > \\ = \mathrm {A} _ {\alpha ; \beta} \mathrm {x} ^ {\beta} + \mathrm {x} ^ {\beta}; \alpha \mathrm {A} _ {\beta}. \tag {3} \\ \end{array}
$$

The connection coefficients do not really enter into the definition of the Lie derivative; they cancel out of Equation (3) to leave Equation (2).

(b) For simplicity, work in a coordinate basis:

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {X}} \mathbf {T} = \mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {X}} (\mathbf {T} ^ {a} \mathbf {\beta} \mathbf {e} _ {a} \otimes \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\boldsymbol {\beta}}) \\ = \left(\mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {x}} \mathrm {T} ^ {\alpha} \beta\right) \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha} \otimes \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\beta} + \mathrm {T} ^ {\alpha} \beta \left(\mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {x}} \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha}\right) \otimes \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\beta} + \mathrm {T} ^ {\alpha} \beta \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha} \otimes \left(\mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {x}} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\beta}\right) \\ = \mathrm {T} _ {\beta , \gamma} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {x} ^ {\gamma} \mathrm {e} _ {\alpha} \otimes \tilde {\omega} ^ {\beta} - \mathrm {T} _ {\beta} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {x} ^ {\gamma}, _ {\alpha} \mathrm {e} _ {\gamma} \otimes \tilde {\omega} ^ {\beta} + \mathrm {T} _ {\beta} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {x} ^ {\beta}, _ {\gamma} \mathrm {e} _ {\alpha} \otimes \tilde {\omega} ^ {\gamma} \\ = \left(\mathrm {T} _ {\beta , \gamma} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {x} ^ {\gamma} - \mathrm {T} _ {\beta , \mu} ^ {\mu} \mathbf {x} _ {, \mu} ^ {\alpha} + \mathrm {T} _ {\mu} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {x} _ {, \beta} ^ {\mu}\right) \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha} \otimes \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\beta}. \\ \end{array}
$$

This is often written

$$
\mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {x}} \mathbf {T} ^ {\alpha} _ {\beta} = \mathbf {T} _ {\beta , \gamma} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {x} ^ {\gamma} - \mathbf {T} _ {\beta , \mu} ^ {\mu} \mathbf {x} _ {, \mu} ^ {\alpha} + \mathbf {T} _ {\mu} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {x} _ {, \beta} ^ {\mu}
$$

and the commas can be replaced by semicolons as in part (a).

Solution 8.14.

![](images/7c8f21261efc33744042bc400d815ff14261c28fe92d8ad76dc680dbcd709f7f.jpg)

At the points $\mathbf{P}_0$ and $\mathbf{P}_1$ consider two tangent vectors $\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{P}_0)$ and $\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{P}_1)$ each extending from $\lambda = 0$ to $\lambda = \Delta \lambda = \mathcal{O}(\epsilon)$ . (Note: In the figure $\Delta \lambda$ is taken to be unity so that the $\mathbf{A}$ vectors are large and the "difference vector" $\mathbf{K}$ is exaggerated for clarity.) Suppose that at point $\mathbf{P}_1$ the vector $\mathbf{B}(\mathbf{P}_1)$ connects $\mathbf{P}_1$ and $\mathbf{P}_0$ . Let us compute the amount by which the vector field $\mathbf{B}$ fails to connect points of equal $\lambda$ along the congruence $\mathbf{A}$ , i.e. let us compute the vector $\mathbf{K}$ :

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {K} = \left[ \mathbf {B} \left(\mathbf {P} _ {1}\right) + \mathbf {A} \left(\mathbf {P} _ {0}\right) \right] - \left[ \mathbf {A} \left(\mathbf {P} _ {1}\right) + \mathbf {B} \left(\mathbf {P} _ {3}\right) \right] \\ = \left[ \mathbf {B} \left(\mathbf {P} _ {1}\right) - \mathbf {B} \left(\mathbf {P} _ {3}\right) \right] - \left[ \mathbf {A} \left(\mathbf {P} _ {1}\right) - \mathbf {A} \left(\mathbf {P} _ {0}\right) \right] \\ = \mathrm {A} ^ {\tau} \mathrm {B} ^ {\gamma}, _ {\tau} \mathbf {e} _ {\gamma} + \mathcal {O} (\mathrm {A} ^ {2} \mathrm {B}) - \mathrm {B} ^ {\tau} \mathrm {A} ^ {\gamma}, _ {\tau} \mathbf {e} _ {\gamma} + \mathcal {O} (\mathrm {B} ^ {2} \mathrm {A}) \\ = [ \mathbf {A}, \mathbf {B} ] + \mathcal {O} (\epsilon^ {3}) \text {i f} | \mathbf {A} | \sim \mathcal {O} (\epsilon) \sim | \mathbf {B} |. \\ \end{array}
$$

Thus in the limit of infinitesimal vectors A and B,

$$
\mathbf {K} = \left[ \begin{array}{c c} \mathbf {A}, \mathbf {B} \end{array} \right] = \mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {A}} \mathbf {B}.
$$

Thus $\mathbf{K}$ vanishes if $\mathcal{L}_{\mathbf{A}}\mathbf{B} = 0$ , i.e. if $\mathbf{B}$ is “Lie-dragged” along $\mathbf{A}$ .

Solution 8.15. Contraction may be thought of as multiplication by the constant Kronecker-delta tensor $\delta_{\nu}^{\mu}$ . But the Lie derivative of the tensor $\delta_{\nu}^{\mu}$ is zero, as we can easily prove in a coordinate frame,

$$
\mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {x}} (\delta_ {\nu} ^ {\mu}) = \delta_ {\nu , \lambda} ^ {\mu} \mathbf {x} ^ {\lambda} + \delta_ {\lambda} ^ {\mu} \mathbf {x} _ {, \nu} ^ {\lambda} - \delta_ {\nu} ^ {\lambda} \mathbf {x} _ {, \lambda} ^ {\mu} = 0 + \mathbf {x} _ {, \nu} ^ {\mu} - \mathbf {x} _ {, \nu} ^ {\mu} = 0 ,
$$

and therefore the contraction operator $\delta_{\nu}^{\mu}$ can go on either side of $\mathfrak{L}_{\mathbf{x}}$ .

Solution 8.16. If the operator identity holds for scalar functions and for vector fields, it will hold for arbitrary tensors. This is because the action of the Lie derivative on arbitrary tensors is determined by its action on scalars and vectors (see Problem 8.13). Let $f$ be a scalar function, then

$$
\mathcal {L} _ {\bf u} \mathcal {L} _ {\bf v} f - \mathcal {L} _ {\bf v} \mathcal {L} _ {\bf u} f = \mathcal {L} _ {\bf u} \nabla_ {\bf v} f - \mathcal {L} _ {\bf v} \nabla_ {\bf u} f = [ {\bf u}, {\bf v} ] f = \mathcal {L} _ {[ {\bf u}, {\bf v} ]} f.
$$

Now let $w$ be a vector field, then

$$
\mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {u}} \mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {v}} w - \mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {v}} \mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {u},} w - \mathcal {L} _ {[ \mathbf {u}, \mathbf {v} ]} w = [ \mathbf {u}, [ \mathbf {v}, w ] ] - [ \mathbf {v}, [ \mathbf {u}, w ] ] - [ [ \mathbf {u}, \mathbf {v} ], w ] = 0 .
$$

The vanishing of this last expression (the Jacobi identity for commutators) can easily be checked e.g. by writing out all terms. This completes the proof for scalars and for vectors, and hence in general.

Solution 8.17.

(i) First the scalar case:

$$
\begin{array}{l} \Phi (\mathrm {P} _ {\mathrm {N}}) = \Phi (\mathrm {P} _ {0}) + [ \mathrm {x} ^ {\mu} (\mathrm {P} _ {\mathrm {N}}) - \mathrm {x} ^ {\mu} (\mathrm {P} _ {0}) ] \Phi_ {, \mu} (\mathrm {P} _ {0}) + \dots \\ \mathbf {\Phi} = \Phi (\mathbf {P} _ {0}) - \xi^ {\mu} \Phi_ {, \mu}, \\ \end{array}
$$

and $\overline{\Phi} (\mathbf{P}_{\mathbf{N}}) = \Phi (\mathbf{P}_{\mathbf{N}})$ since $\Phi$ is a scalar, hence

$$
\mathcal {L} _ {\xi} \Phi = \Phi_ {, \mu} \xi^ {\mu}.
$$

(ii) Since $\overline{\mathbf{x}}^{\mu}(\mathbf{P}_{\mathbf{N}}) = \mathbf{x}^{\mu}(\mathbf{P}_{0}) = \mathbf{x}^{\mu}(\mathbf{P}_{\mathbf{N}}) + \boldsymbol{\xi}^{\mu}$ , we have for vector fields

$$
\overline {{{\mathsf {A}}}} _ {\mu} (\mathbf {P _ {N}}) = (\partial \mathbf {x} ^ {a} / \partial \overline {{{\mathbf {x}}}} ^ {\mu}) \mathsf {A} _ {\mu} (\mathbf {P _ {N}}) = (\delta_ {\mu} ^ {a} - \xi_ {, \mu} ^ {a}) \mathsf {A} _ {\mu} (\mathbf {P _ {N}}).
$$

Now we combine this with

$$
\mathbf {A} _ {\mu} (\mathbf {P _ {N}}) = \mathbf {A} _ {\mu} (\mathbf {P _ {0}}) - \mathbf {A} _ {\mu , \beta} \xi^ {\beta}
$$

to get

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathcal {L} _ {\boldsymbol {\xi}} \mathrm {A} _ {\mu} = \mathrm {A} _ {\mu} \left(\mathrm {P} _ {0}\right) - \overline {{\mathrm {A}}} _ {\mu} \left(\mathrm {P} _ {\mathrm {N}}\right) \\ = \mathrm {A} _ {\mu} (\mathrm {P} _ {0}) - \left(\delta_ {\mu} ^ {\alpha} - \xi_ {, \mu} ^ {\alpha}\right) \left(\mathrm {A} _ {\alpha} (\mathrm {P} _ {0}) - \mathrm {A} _ {\alpha , \beta} \xi^ {\beta}\right) \\ = \mathrm {A} _ {\mu , \beta} \xi^ {\beta} + \mathrm {A} _ {\alpha} \xi^ {\alpha}, \mu . \\ \end{array}
$$

(iii) The tensor case is handled similarly:

$$
\begin{array}{l} \bar {\mathrm {T}} _ {\nu} ^ {\mu} \left(\mathrm {P} _ {\mathrm {N}}\right) = \left(\partial \bar {\mathrm {x}} ^ {\mu} / \partial \mathrm {x} ^ {a}\right) \left(\partial_ {\mathrm {x}} ^ {\beta} / \partial \bar {\mathrm {x}} ^ {\nu}\right) \mathrm {T} _ {\beta} ^ {a} \left(\mathrm {P} _ {\mathrm {N}}\right) \\ = \left(\delta_ {\alpha} ^ {\mu} + \xi_ {, \alpha} ^ {\mu}\right) \left(\delta_ {\nu} ^ {\beta} - \xi_ {, \nu} ^ {\beta}\right) \left[ T _ {\beta} ^ {\alpha} (P _ {0}) - T _ {\beta , \gamma} ^ {\alpha} \xi^ {\gamma} \right] \\ = \mathrm {T} _ {\nu} ^ {\mu} (\mathrm {P} _ {0}) - \mathrm {T} _ {\nu , \gamma} ^ {\mu} \xi^ {\gamma} - \mathrm {T} _ {\beta} ^ {\mu} \xi^ {\beta}, _ {\nu} + \mathrm {T} _ {\nu} ^ {a} \xi^ {\mu}, _ {a} \\ \mathcal {L} _ {\boldsymbol {\xi}} \mathrm {T} _ {\nu} ^ {\mu} = \mathrm {T} _ {\nu , \gamma} ^ {\mu} \xi^ {\gamma} + \mathrm {T} _ {\beta} ^ {\mu} \xi^ {\beta}, _ {\nu} - \mathrm {T} _ {\nu} ^ {a} \xi^ {\mu}, _ {a}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Solution 8.18. The parallel transport law $\mathfrak{u}^{\alpha_{\mathbf{v}}\beta}_{;a} = 0$ requires only the affine connection $\Gamma^{\alpha}_{\beta \gamma}$ . The Fermi-Walker transport law

$$
\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {v} = \mathbf {u} (\mathbf {a} \cdot \mathbf {v}) - \mathbf {a} (\mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {v}) \quad \mathbf {a} \equiv \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {u}
$$

requires, in addition, a metric for the dot products. The Lie transport equation $\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{v} - \nabla_{\mathbf{v}}\mathbf{u} = 0$ requires $\mathbf{u}$ to be a vector field defined off the curve, not just a tangent vector along the curve, because the gradient of $\mathbf{u}$ in the v-direction must be defined, and $\mathbf{v}$ is not in general along the curve. Although the formula above includes $\nabla$ 's, a connection is actually not needed, because the $\Gamma$ 's cancel out due to the antisymmetry of the equation. Obviously no metric is required for Lie differentiation.

Solution 8.19. If $\underline{\mathbf{u}}$ can be returned to A unchanged, then in principle it can be extended into a vector field which is both parallel transported and Lie transported from A to B. Let $\underline{\mathbf{u}} = (\mathbf{u}^{\mathbf{x}},\mathbf{u}^{\mathbf{y}},\mathbf{u}^{\mathbf{z}})$ , and consider the identity

$$
\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {v} - \nabla_ {\mathbf {v}} \mathbf {u} = [ \underline {{\mathbf {u}}}, \underline {{\mathbf {v}}} ] = \mathscr {L} _ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {v} = - \mathscr {L} _ {\mathbf {v}} \mathbf {u}. \tag {1}
$$

Setting the appropriate terms to zero in Equation (1), i.e. $\nabla_{\mathbf{v}}\mathbf{u} = \mathcal{L}_{\mathbf{v}}\mathbf{u} = 0$ and noting that the Christoffel symbols vanish, we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {v} = 0 = \mathbf {u} ^ {\mathrm {i}} \mathbf {v} _ {, i} ^ {\mathrm {k}} = 0 \tag {2} \\ - u ^ {y} = u ^ {x} = a u ^ {z} z ^ {a - 1} = 0. \\ \end{array}
$$

So $\underline{\mathbf{u}}$ can be nonzero (and have $\mathfrak{u}^z\neq 0$ ) only if

$$
\alpha = 0. \tag {3}
$$

Solution 8.20. Let the vector field be $\mathbf{u}(\mathfrak{p})$ . If it is parallel propagated along itself, we have $\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{u} = 0$ . This is just the geodesic equation, so the field must be a field of tangent vectors to a space-filling congruence of geodesics.

If the field is F-W transported along itself, we have

$$
\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {u} = (\mathbf {u} \otimes \mathbf {a} - \mathbf {a} \otimes \mathbf {u}) \cdot \mathbf {u} \tag {1}
$$

where $\mathbf{a} = \nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{u}$ . Thus

$$
\mathbf {a} = \mathbf {u} (\mathbf {a} \cdot \mathbf {u}) - \mathbf {a} (\mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {u}). \tag {2}
$$

Now dot $\mathbf{u}$ into this and get $\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{u} = \mathbf{u}^2 (\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{u}) - (\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{u}) \mathbf{u}^2 = 0$ . From (2) we have that either $\mathbf{u}^2 = -1$ or $\mathbf{a} = 0$ . The first possibility means that any field of properly normalized 4-velocities is F-W transported along itself. The second says that the tangent vectors of any congruence of geodesics are also, even if they are not normalized to $-1$ .

The Lie transport law is

$$
\mathbf {0} = \mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {u} \equiv [ \mathbf {u}, \mathbf {u} ]
$$

which is satisfied by any vector field.

Solution 8.21. Consider a 1-form $\tilde{\mathbf{U}} = \mathbf{U}_{\alpha}\widetilde{\mathrm{d}\mathbf{x}}^{\alpha}$ . First calculate $\mathrm{d}\mathcal{L}_{\mathbf{x}}^{\varrho}\tilde{\mathbf{U}}$

$$
\mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {x}} \tilde {\mathbf {U}} = (\mathbf {x} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {U} _ {\beta , a} + \mathbf {U} _ {a} \mathbf {x} ^ {\alpha}, \beta) \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\beta}
$$

$$
\mathbf {d} \left(\mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {x}} \tilde {\mathbf {U}}\right) = \left(\mathrm {x} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {U} _ {\beta , \alpha} + \mathrm {U} _ {\alpha} \mathrm {x} ^ {\alpha}, \beta\right), \gamma \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\gamma} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\beta} \tag {1}
$$

Now calculate $\mathfrak{L}_{\mathbf{x}}(\tilde{\mathbf{d}\mathbf{U}})$

$$
\mathrm {d} \widetilde {\mathbf {U}} = \mathrm {U} _ {\alpha , \gamma} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\gamma} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\alpha}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {x}} (\mathrm {d} \tilde {\mathbf {U}}) = \mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {x}} \left(\mathrm {U} _ {a, \gamma}\right) \widetilde {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x}} ^ {\gamma} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x}} ^ {a} + \mathrm {U} _ {a, \gamma} \mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {x}} \left(\widetilde {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x}} ^ {\gamma}\right) \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x}} ^ {a} \\ + \mathrm {U} _ {\alpha , \gamma} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\gamma} \wedge \mathscr {L} _ {\mathrm {x}} (\widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\alpha}) \\ = \mathrm {x} ^ {\tau} \mathrm {U} _ {a, \gamma \tau} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\gamma} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {a} + \mathrm {U} _ {a, \gamma} \mathrm {x} ^ {\gamma}, _ {\tau} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\tau} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {a} + \mathrm {U} _ {a, \gamma} \mathrm {x} ^ {a}, _ {\tau} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\gamma} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\tau} \\ = \left[ \left(\mathbf {x} ^ {\tau} \mathrm {U} _ {\beta , \tau} + \mathrm {U} _ {\alpha} \mathbf {x} ^ {\alpha}, \beta\right), \gamma - \mathrm {U} _ {\alpha} \mathbf {x} ^ {\alpha}, \beta \gamma \right] \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\gamma} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\beta}. \tag {2} \\ \end{array}
$$

Equation (2) is obtained merely by relabeling indices. By symmetry of partial derivatives and antisymmetry of wedge product, the last term in brackets on the right hand side of Equation (2) vanishes. Comparing Equations (1) and (2), we have for 1-forms:

$$
\mathbf {d} \left(\mathscr {L} _ {\mathbf {X}} \tilde {\mathbf {U}}\right) = \mathscr {L} _ {\mathbf {X}} (\mathbf {d} \tilde {\mathbf {U}}).
$$

The generalization of this method from 1-form to p-form is straightforward.

Solution 8.22.

$$
(\nabla \mathbf {S}) ^ {\widehat {\mathbf {i}}} = \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\widehat {\mathbf {i}}} \cdot \nabla \mathbf {S} = \mathrm {h} _ {\mathbf {i}} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {\mathbf {i}} \cdot \nabla \mathbf {S} = \mathrm {h} _ {\mathbf {i}} \partial \mathbf {S} / \partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathbf {i}}. \tag {i}
$$

(ii) The general expression for the curl must be

$$
(\nabla \times \mathbf {V}) ^ {\mathbf {i}} = | \mathbf {g} | ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \varepsilon^ {\mathbf {i j k}} \mathbf {V} _ {\mathbf {k}; \mathbf {j}} = | \mathbf {g} | ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \varepsilon^ {\mathbf {i j k}} \mathbf {V} _ {\mathbf {k}, \mathbf {j}}
$$

since it is a covariant relation and it is true in a locally flat (cartesian) coordinate basis. (See Problems 3.20 and 3.21 for the relevant properties of the antisymmetric tensor $\varepsilon^{ijk}$ .) In the orthonormal basis

$$
(\nabla \times \mathbf {V}) ^ {\hat {\mathbf {i}}} = \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {i}}} \cdot (\nabla \times \mathbf {V}) = h _ {\mathbf {i}} (\nabla \times \mathbf {V}) ^ {\mathbf {i}} = h _ {\mathbf {i}} (h _ {\mathbf {1}} h _ {\mathbf {2}} h _ {\mathbf {3}}) ^ {- 1} \varepsilon^ {\mathbf {i j k}} \partial (h _ {\mathbf {k}} V _ {\hat {\mathbf {k}}}) / \partial x ^ {\mathbf {j}}.
$$

(iii) Using the result in Problem 7.7(g), we have

$$
\nabla \cdot \mathbf {V} = | \mathbf {g} | ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \left[ | \mathbf {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {V} ^ {\hat {\imath}} \right] _ {, i} = (\mathsf {h} _ {1} \mathsf {h} _ {2} \mathsf {h} _ {3}) ^ {- 1} \left[ (\mathsf {h} _ {1} \mathsf {h} _ {2} \mathsf {h} _ {3}) \mathsf {h} _ {\hat {\imath}} ^ {- 1} \mathsf {V} _ {\hat {\imath}} \right] _ {, i}.
$$

(iv) With the expression in Problem 7.7(j), $\nabla^2 S$ becomes

$$
\nabla^ {2} \mathsf {S} = | \mathsf {g} | ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} (| \mathsf {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathsf {g} ^ {\alpha \beta} \mathsf {S}, \beta), _ {\alpha} = (\mathsf {h} _ {1} \mathsf {h} _ {2} \mathsf {h} _ {3}) ^ {- 1} [ \mathsf {h} _ {1} \mathsf {h} _ {2} \mathsf {h} _ {3} \mathsf {h} _ {\mathsf {i}} ^ {- 2} \mathsf {S}, _ {\mathsf {i}} ], _ {\mathsf {i}}.
$$

Solution 8.23. We use results (ii) and (iii) of Problem 8.22 with $\mathbf{x}^1 = \mathbf{r}$ , $\mathbf{x}^2 = \theta$ , $\mathbf{x}^3 = \phi$ and with $\mathbf{h}_1 = \mathbf{l}$ , $\mathbf{h}_2 = \mathbf{r}$ , $\mathbf{h}_3 = \mathbf{r}$ sin $\theta$ . (For simplicity we follow the usual notation here and omit the caret's indicating physical components.)

$$
\begin{array}{l} \nabla \cdot \underline {{\mathbf {A}}} = (\mathrm {r} ^ {2} \sin \theta) ^ {- 1} [ \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \sin \theta \mathrm {h} _ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {- 1} \mathrm {A} _ {\mathrm {i}} ] _ {, \mathrm {i}} \\ = \left(\mathrm {r} ^ {2} \sin \theta\right) ^ {- 1} \left[ \left(\mathrm {r} ^ {2} \sin \theta \mathrm {A} _ {\mathrm {r}}\right), \mathrm {r} + \left(\mathrm {r} \sin \theta \mathrm {A} _ {\theta}\right), \theta + \mathrm {r A} _ {\phi}, \phi \right] \\ \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} (\nabla \times \mathbf {A}) ^ {\mathbf {r}} = (\mathbf {r} \sin \theta) ^ {- 1} [ (\sin \theta \mathbf {A} _ {\phi}), \theta - \mathbf {A} _ {\theta , \phi} ] \\ (\nabla \times \mathbf {A}) ^ {\theta} = (\mathbf {r} \sin \theta) ^ {- 1} [ \mathbf {A} _ {\mathbf {r}, \phi} - (\mathbf {r} \sin \theta \mathbf {A} _ {\phi}), _ {\mathbf {r}} ] \\ (\nabla \times \mathbf {A}) ^ {\phi} = \mathbf {r} ^ {- 1} \left[ (\mathbf {r A} _ {\theta}) _ {, \mathbf {r}} - \mathbf {A} _ {\mathbf {r}, \theta} \right]. \\ \end{array}
$$

Solution 8.24. If forms and exterior differentiation are used, this problem is trivial. The first equation gives $\mathbf{F} = \mathrm{d}\tilde{\mathbf{A}}$ so, $\mathrm{d}\mathbf{F} = \mathrm{d}\mathbf{d}\tilde{\mathbf{A}} = 0$ , since $\mathrm{d}\mathbf{d}$ is always zero. $\mathbf{F}_{[\mu \nu ;\lambda ]}$ is exactly the components of $\mathrm{d}\mathbf{F}$ , so we are done. The trick is that the total antisymmetrization implicit in the $\mathbf{d}$ 's keeps any connection coefficient from entering the problem. If components are used, the proof is straightforward but more tedious.

Solution 8.25. In general, $\mathrm{ds}^2 = \mathbf{g}_{\alpha \beta}\mathrm{dx}^\alpha \mathrm{dx}^\beta$ . Since $\mathbf{x}^{\mathrm{i}} = \mathrm{constant}$ along any of the geodesics, $\mathrm{ds}^2 = \mathbf{g}_{00}\mathrm{dt}^2$ along any of the geodesics. But along the geodesics $\mathrm{ds}^2 = -\mathrm{d}\tau^2$ , so $\mathbf{g}_{00} = -1$ everywhere. Let $\mathbf{e}_\alpha$ be

the coordinate basis vectors, and let $\mathbf{u} = \mathrm{d} / \mathrm{d}\tau$ be the tangent vector field to the geodesics (i.e. $\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{e}_0$ ). Since $\mathbf{u}$ is orthogonal to $\mathbf{S}_{\mathbf{I}}$ , then $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{e}_i = \mathbf{e}_0 \cdot \mathbf{e}_i = \mathbf{g}_{0\mathbf{i}} = 0$ at $r = 0$ , and

$$
d \left(\mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {i}\right) / d \tau = \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \left(\mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {i}\right) = 0 + \mathbf {u} \cdot \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {e} _ {i} = \mathbf {u} \cdot \nabla_ {\mathbf {e} _ {i}} \mathbf {u}
$$

since the curves are geodesics $(\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{u} = 0)$ and since $\mathbf{e}_{\mathbf{i}}$ and $\mathbf{u}$ form a coordinate basis $([\mathbf{e}_{\mathbf{i}},\mathbf{u}] = 0)$ . This last result vanishes

$$
\mathbf {u} \cdot \nabla_ {\mathbf {e} _ {\mathbf {i}}} \mathbf {u} = \frac {1}{2} \nabla_ {\mathbf {e} _ {\mathbf {i}}} (\mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {u}) = 0
$$

and therefore $\mathbf{u}\cdot \mathbf{e}_{\mathbf{i}} = \mathbf{g}_{0\mathbf{i}} = 0$ everywhere.

# Solution 8.26.

(a) From the point of view of modern differential geometry, $\overline{\Gamma}_{\mu \nu}^{\lambda}$ and $\Gamma_{\mu \nu}^{\lambda}$ are the component description of "machines" which map two vectors and a form into a number; i.e.

$$
(\text {n u m b e r}) = \Gamma_ {\mu \nu} ^ {\lambda} \sigma_ {\lambda} \mathrm {u} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {v} ^ {\nu} \equiv <   \tilde {\boldsymbol {\sigma}}, \nabla_ {\mathbf {v}} \mathbf {u} >
$$

where the covariant derivative is taken using the metric corresponding to whichever $\Gamma$ is used. A tensor is a "machine" which is linear on all "slots" (i.e. in all its arguments). By itself $\Gamma$ or $\bar{\Gamma}$ is not a tensor since

$$
f <   \tilde {\sigma}, \nabla_ {\mathbf {v}} \mathbf {u} > \neq <   \tilde {\sigma}, \nabla_ {\mathbf {v}} (\mathbf {f u}) > = f <   \tilde {\sigma}, \nabla_ {\mathbf {v}} \mathbf {u} > + <   \tilde {\sigma}, \mathbf {u} > \nabla_ {\mathbf {v}} f
$$

and likewise with "bars" over the $\nabla$ 's for $\overline{\mathbf{g}}$ . If however we subtract the two equations, the linearity-spoiling $<\tilde{\sigma}, \mathfrak{u}>\left(\nabla_{\mathbf{v}} - \overline{\nabla}_{\mathbf{v}}\right)$ term is identically zero since all covariant derivatives acting on a scalar function give the same result, i.e. are just directional derivatives. Thus $\mathsf{S}_{\mu \nu}^{\lambda}$ is a tensor.

(b) We cannot compare the geodesic equations of $\mathbf{g}$ and $\overline{\mathbf{g}}$ directly since they would involve different affine parameters $\tau$ and $\overline{\tau}$ . If $\mathfrak{t}$ is an arbitrary function of $\tau$ , a change of variable makes the geodesic equation

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathbf {x} ^ {\lambda}}{\mathrm {d t} ^ {2}} + \Gamma_ {\mu \nu} ^ {\lambda} \frac {\mathrm {d x} ^ {\mu}}{\mathrm {d t}} \frac {\mathrm {d x} ^ {\nu}}{\mathrm {d t}} - \frac {\mathrm {d x} ^ {\lambda}}{\mathrm {d t}} \frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \tau}{\mathrm {d t} ^ {2}} \frac {\mathrm {d t}}{\mathrm {d} \tau} = 0 .
$$

To eliminate the $\mathrm{d}r / \mathrm{d}t$ term, multiply by $\mathrm{dx}^{\alpha} / \mathrm{dt}$ and antisymmetrize on $\alpha$ and $\lambda$ to get

$$
\mathfrak {u} ^ {\alpha} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathfrak {u} ^ {\lambda}}{\mathrm {d} t} - \mathfrak {u} ^ {\lambda} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathfrak {u} ^ {\alpha}}{\mathrm {d} t} + (\Gamma_ {\mu \nu} ^ {\lambda} \mathfrak {u} ^ {\alpha} - \Gamma_ {\mu \nu} ^ {\alpha} \mathfrak {u} ^ {\lambda}) \mathfrak {u} ^ {\mu} \mathfrak {u} ^ {\nu} = 0 ,
$$

where $\mathbf{u}^{\alpha}\equiv \mathrm{d}\mathbf{x}^{\alpha} / \mathrm{d}t$ . If the world lines $\mathbf{u}^{\alpha}(\mathfrak{t})$ are geodesics for both $\mathbf{g}$ and $\overline{\mathbf{g}}$ then the above equation must also hold with $\Gamma$ replaced by $\overline{\Gamma}$ . Subtracting these two equations gives

$$
(\delta_ {\sigma} ^ {a} \mathsf {S} _ {\mu \nu} ^ {\lambda} - \delta_ {\sigma} ^ {\lambda} \mathsf {S} _ {\mu \nu} ^ {a}) \mathfrak {u} ^ {\sigma} \mathfrak {u} ^ {\mu} \mathfrak {u} ^ {\nu} = 0 ,
$$

which implies that

$$
\delta_ {(\sigma} ^ {\alpha} \mathsf {S} _ {\mu) \nu} ^ {\lambda} - \delta_ {(\sigma} ^ {\lambda} \mathsf {S} _ {\mu) \nu} ^ {\alpha} = 0 .
$$

When this equation is contracted on $\alpha$ and $\nu$ the result is

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathsf {S} _ {\sigma \mu} ^ {\lambda} = \delta_ {\sigma} ^ {\lambda} \Phi_ {\mu} + \delta_ {\mu} ^ {\lambda} \Phi_ {\sigma} \\ \Phi_ {\alpha} \equiv \frac {1}{5} S _ {\alpha \mu} ^ {\mu}. \\ \end{array}
$$

[See also L. P. Eisenhart, Riemannian Geometry (Princeton University Press, 1962), Section 40.]

Solution 8.27. The difficult way to do this problem is to compute connection coefficients in a coordinate frame. Here we will use the method of "rotation 1-forms." (See MTW, Section 14.5 for details.) Choose an orthonormal frame as follows:

$$
\begin{array}{l} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} = \mathbf {e} ^ {\alpha} \tilde {\mathbf {d t}} \\ \tilde {\mathbf {w}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} = \mathrm {e} ^ {\beta \tilde {\mathbf {d r}}} \\ \tilde {\mathbf {w}} ^ {\hat {\theta}} = \mathbf {e} ^ {\gamma} \tilde {\mathbf {d} \theta} \\ \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\phi}} = e ^ {\gamma} \sin \theta \widetilde {d \phi}. \\ \end{array}
$$

The key formulas are the definition of $\tilde{\omega}^{\hat{a}}\hat{\beta}$

$$
\mathbf {d} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\alpha}} = - \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\alpha}} \hat {\boldsymbol {\beta}} \wedge \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\beta}},
$$

and the relation of the forms to the Christoffel symbols

$$
\Gamma_ {\hat {\mu} \hat {\lambda}} ^ {\hat {\nu}} \tilde {\pmb {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\lambda}} = \tilde {\pmb {\omega}} _ {\hat {\mu}} ^ {\hat {\nu}}.
$$

We now use these formulas for the given metric; let dot denote $\partial/\partial t$ and prime denote $\partial/\partial r$ :

$$
\mathrm {d} \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\mathrm {t}}} = \mathrm {e} ^ {\alpha_ {\alpha^ {\prime}} \widetilde {\mathrm {d r}} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d t}}} = \alpha^ {\prime} \mathrm {e} ^ {- \beta \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\mathrm {r}}} \wedge \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\mathrm {t}}}} \tag {1}
$$

$$
\mathrm {d} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} = \mathrm {e} ^ {\beta} \dot {\boldsymbol {\beta}} \widetilde {\mathrm {d t}} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d r}} = \mathrm {e} ^ {- \alpha} \dot {\boldsymbol {\beta}} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} \wedge \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} \tag {2}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {d} \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\theta}} = \mathrm {e} ^ {\gamma} \dot {\gamma} \widetilde {\mathrm {d t}} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d} \theta} + \mathrm {e} ^ {\gamma} \gamma^ {\prime} \widetilde {\mathrm {d r}} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d} \theta} \\ = e ^ {- \alpha} \dot {\gamma} \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {t}} \wedge \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\theta}} + e ^ {- \beta} \gamma^ {\prime} \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {r}} \wedge \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\theta}} \tag {3} \\ \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {d} \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\phi}} = \mathrm {e} ^ {\gamma} \dot {\gamma} \sin \theta \widetilde {\mathrm {d t}} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d}} \phi + \mathrm {e} ^ {\gamma} \gamma^ {\prime} \sin \theta \widetilde {\mathrm {d r}} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d}} \phi + \mathrm {e} ^ {\gamma} \cos \theta \widetilde {\mathrm {d}} \theta \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d}} \phi \\ = e ^ {- \alpha} \dot {\gamma} \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {t}} \wedge \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\phi}} + e ^ {- \beta} \gamma^ {\prime} \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {r}} \wedge \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\phi}} + \cot \theta e ^ {- \gamma} \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\theta}} \wedge \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\phi}} \tag {4} \\ \end{array}
$$

But we also know that $\mathbf{d}\tilde{\pmb{\omega}}^{\hat{\alpha}} = -\tilde{\pmb{\omega}}^{\hat{\alpha}}\hat{\beta} \wedge \tilde{\pmb{\omega}}^{\hat{\beta}}$ and $\tilde{\pmb{\omega}}_{\hat{\alpha}}\hat{\beta} = -\tilde{\pmb{\omega}}_{\hat{\beta}}\hat{\alpha}$ , where indices are raised and lowered with $\eta_{\hat{\alpha}}\hat{\beta}$ . For $\hat{\alpha} = \hat{t}$ we have

$$
\mathbf {d} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} = - \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} \wedge \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} - \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta}}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} \wedge \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta}}} - \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\phi}}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} \wedge \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\phi}}} .
$$

Comparing this with Equation (1), we guess that

$$
\tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\hat {\mathrm {r}}} ^ {\hat {\mathrm {t}}} = \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\hat {\mathrm {t}}} ^ {\hat {\mathrm {r}}} = \alpha^ {\prime} \mathrm {e} ^ {- \beta} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\mathrm {t}}} + \mathrm {K} _ {1} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\mathrm {r}}} \tag {5}
$$

$$
\tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\mathrm {t}} _ {\hat {\theta}} = \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\theta}} _ {\hat {\mathrm {t}}} =. \mathrm {K} _ {2} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\theta}} \tag {6}
$$

$$
\tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\hat {\phi}} ^ {\hat {\mathrm {t}}} = \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\hat {\mathrm {t}}} ^ {\hat {\phi}} = \mathrm {K} _ {3} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\phi}} \tag {7}
$$

where $\mathbf{K}_1, \mathbf{K}_2, \mathbf{K}_3$ are functions we must find. Similarly, Equation (2) implies that

$$
\tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} = \mathbf {e} ^ {- \alpha} \dot {\boldsymbol {\beta}} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} + \mathrm {K} _ {4} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} \tag {8}
$$

$$
\tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} _ {\hat {\theta}} = - \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\theta}} _ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} = \mathrm {K} _ {5} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\theta}} \tag {9}
$$

$$
\tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} \hat {\phi} = - \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\phi}} _ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} = \mathrm {K} _ {6} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\phi}}. \tag {10}
$$

Equation (3) implies

$$
\tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\hat {\mathrm {t}}} ^ {\hat {\theta}} = \mathrm {e} ^ {- a} \dot {\gamma} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\theta}} \tag {11}
$$

$$
\tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} ^ {\hat {\theta}} = \mathrm {e} ^ {- \beta} \gamma^ {\prime} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\theta}} \tag {12}
$$

$$
\tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\theta}} _ {\hat {\phi}} = - \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\phi}} _ {\hat {\theta}} = \mathrm {K} _ {7} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\phi}}, \tag {13}
$$

and Equation (4) gives

$$
\tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} \hat {\phi} _ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} = e ^ {- a} \dot {\gamma} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} \hat {\phi} \tag {14}
$$

$$
\tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\phi}} _ {\hat {\mathrm {r}}} = \mathrm {e} ^ {- \beta} \gamma^ {\prime} \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\phi}} \tag {15}
$$

$$
\tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\phi}} _ {\hat {\theta}} = \cot \theta \mathrm {e} ^ {- \gamma} \dot {\tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}}} ^ {\hat {\phi}}. \tag {16}
$$

Comparing Equations (5)-(16), we find

$$
\tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} = \alpha^ {\prime} \mathrm {e} ^ {- \beta} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} + \dot {\beta} \mathrm {e} ^ {- \alpha} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}}
$$

$$
\tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\mathrm {t}}} \hat {\theta} = \mathrm {e} ^ {- a} \dot {\gamma} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\theta}}
$$

$$
\tilde {\pmb {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\mathrm {t}}} \hat {\pmb {\phi}} = \mathbf {e} ^ {- a} \dot {\gamma} \tilde {\pmb {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\phi}}
$$

$$
\tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} _ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta}}} = - \mathrm {e} ^ {- \beta} \gamma^ {\prime} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta}}}
$$

$$
\tilde {\pmb {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} \hat {\phi} = - \mathrm {e} ^ {- \beta} \gamma^ {\prime} \tilde {\pmb {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\phi}}
$$

$$
\tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta}}} _ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\phi}}} = - \cot \theta \mathrm {e} ^ {- \gamma} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\phi}}} .
$$

3ut $\Gamma_{\hat{\mu}\hat{\lambda}}^{\hat{\nu}}\tilde{\omega}^{\hat{\lambda}} = \tilde{\omega}_{\hat{\mu}}^{\hat{\nu}}$ so the nonvanishing connection coefficients are

$$
\begin{array}{r l} \Gamma_ {\widehat {r t}} ^ {\widehat {t}} & = \Gamma_ {\widehat {t t}} ^ {\widehat {r}} = a ^ {\prime} e ^ {- \beta} \end{array}
$$

$$
\Gamma^ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} _ {\mathbf {\hat {r r}}} = \Gamma^ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} _ {\mathbf {\hat {t r}}} = \dot {\beta} \mathrm {e} ^ {- a}
$$

$$
\Gamma^ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} _ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta}}} \hat {\boldsymbol {\theta}} = \Gamma_ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} ^ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta}}} _ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta}}} = \Gamma_ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\phi}}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} _ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\phi}}} = \Gamma_ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} ^ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\phi}}} _ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} = \dot {\gamma} \mathbf {e} ^ {- \alpha}
$$

$$
\Gamma^ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} _ {\hat {\theta}} \hat {\theta} = - \Gamma_ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} ^ {\hat {\theta}} _ {\hat {\theta}} = \Gamma^ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} _ {\hat {\phi}} \hat {\phi} = - \Gamma^ {\hat {\phi}} _ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} \hat {\phi} = - \gamma^ {\prime} \mathrm {e} ^ {- \beta}
$$

$$
\Gamma^ {\hat {\theta}} _ {\hat {\phi}} \hat {\phi} = - \Gamma^ {\hat {\phi}} _ {\hat {\theta}} \hat {\phi} = - \cot \theta e ^ {- \gamma}.
$$

Solution 8.28. Start with $1 + z = \Delta \tau_{\mathbf{A}} / \Delta \tau_{\mathbf{B}}$ . To show that this is equivalent to the other definition, recall that $\mathbf{p}$ is proportional to $\mathbf{k}$ , the wave vector, which is the gradient of the surfaces of constant phase:

$$
\mathbf {k} = \nabla \theta .
$$

Since $\mathbf{k}$ is a null vector,

$$
0 = \mathbf {k} \cdot \mathbf {k} = \mathbf {k} \cdot \nabla \theta = \nabla_ {\mathbf {k}} \theta
$$

i.e. $\theta =$ constant along the rays. Consider two rays differing in phase by $\Delta \theta$ . Observer A computes $\Delta \theta$ as

$$
\Delta \theta = \theta (\tau + \Delta \tau_ {\mathbf {A}}) - \theta (\tau) = (\Delta \tau_ {\mathbf {A}}) \nabla_ {\mathbf {u} _ {\mathbf {A}}} \theta = (\Delta \tau_ {\mathbf {A}}) \mathbf {u} _ {\mathbf {A}} \cdot \mathbf {k}.
$$

![](images/19df07a68aa581e736e84e46b0dc2a8c946215ea8a02c952d4dd69a14863f30a.jpg)

Similarly observer B finds

$$
\Delta \theta = (\Delta \tau_ {\mathbf {B}}) \mathbf {u} _ {\mathbf {B}} \cdot \mathbf {k}
$$

where $\mathbf{k}$ is now evaluated at B. Since $\Delta \theta$ is the same for A and B, we have

$$
\frac {\Delta \tau_ {\mathbf {A}}}{\Delta \tau_ {\mathbf {B}}} = \frac {(\mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {k}) _ {\mathbf {B}}}{(\mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {k}) _ {\mathbf {A}}} = \frac {(\mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {p}) _ {\mathbf {B}}}{(\mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {p}) _ {\mathbf {A}}}
$$

so the two definitions are equivalent.

# CHAPTER 9: SOLUTIONS

Solution 9.1. If we construct a coordinate patch from geodesics we can then bisect that coordinate box with a geodesic diagonal, forming two geodesic triangles. The angular excess of a triangle made from great circles is $\pi[\text{Area}/\text{a}^2]$ . Since the sum of the interior angles of the coordinate box will be the sum of the interior angles of the two triangles, the angular excess of the coordinate box is $\pi[\text{Area}/\text{a}^2]$ .

The best coordinate patch constructed of lines of longitude and latitude is that near the equator. Clearly there is no angular excess, and

$$
\overline {{\mathbf {A C}}} = \overline {{\mathbf {B D}}}, \quad \text {b u t}
$$

![](images/77360ee1f6a95cec792f8590c73c67190578f5932365ea1abaf9fde2dbbe7870.jpg)

$$
\begin{array}{l} \overline {{C D}} - \overline {{A B}} = a \Delta \phi - a \Delta \phi \sin \left(\frac {\pi}{2} - \Delta \theta\right) \\ \approx \mathbf {a} \Delta \phi \frac {1}{2} (\Delta \theta) ^ {2}, \\ \end{array}
$$

so

$$
\frac {\overline {{\mathrm {C D}}} - \overline {{\mathrm {A B}}}}{\overline {{\mathrm {A C}}}} \approx \frac {1}{2} \Delta \phi \Delta \theta \approx \frac {1}{2} \frac {\text {A r e a}}{a ^ {2}}
$$

In both these examples, the departure from flatness is measured by $1 / a^2$ , the curvature of the sphere.

Solution 9.2. The antisymmetry of $(\alpha \beta)$ and $(\gamma \delta)$ in $\mathbf{R}_{\alpha \beta \gamma \delta}$ means that there are $\mathbf{M} = \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{n}(\mathbf{n} - 1)$ ways of choosing nontrivial pairs $(\alpha \beta)$ and similarly $\mathbf{M}$ ways of nontrivially choosing $(\gamma \delta)$ . Since the tensor is symmetric with respect to interchange of the pairs $(\alpha \beta)$ and $(\gamma \delta)$ there are $\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{M} + 1)$ independent ways of choosing $\alpha \beta \gamma \delta$ when the pair symmetries are considered.

The Riemann tensor also possesses the cyclic symmetry

$$
\mathrm {A} _ {\alpha \beta \gamma \delta} = \mathrm {R} _ {\alpha \beta \gamma \delta} + \mathrm {R} _ {\alpha \delta \beta \gamma} + \mathrm {R} _ {\alpha \gamma \delta \beta} = 0.
$$

The pair symmetries guarantee that $\mathbf{A}_{\alpha \beta \gamma \delta}$ is totally antisymmetric so that the constraint $\mathbf{A}_{\alpha \beta \gamma \delta} = 0$ is trivial unless $a, \beta, \gamma, \delta$ are all distinct. The number of added constraints is then the number of combinations of 4 objects which can be taken from $\mathfrak{n}$ objects $= \mathfrak{n}! / (\mathfrak{n} - 4)! 4!$ (This formula gives zero, and is therefore valid, if $\mathfrak{n} < 4$ in which case the permutation symmetry gives no additional constraints.) The number of independent components is then

$$
\frac {1}{2} \mathrm {M} (\mathrm {M} + 1) - \mathrm {n}! / (\mathrm {n} - 4)! 4! = \mathrm {n} ^ {2} (\mathrm {n} ^ {2} - 1) / 1 2.
$$

Solution 9.3. The problem requires ordering 21 independent components in some canonical way and assigning a number $\mathbf{N} \leq 21$ to each nonequivalent quadruple $\mathbf{I}, \mathbf{J}, \mathbf{K}, \mathbf{L}$ . The following program accomplishes this and reads the value of $\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{I}, \mathbf{J}, \mathbf{K}, \mathbf{L})$ from $\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{N})$ , $\mathbf{1} \leq \mathbf{N} \leq 21$ .

READ I, J, K, L

SET SIGN = 1

IF $\mathbf{I} = \mathbf{J}$ OR $\mathbf{K} = \mathbf{L}$ SET RIEMANN $= 0$ AND RETURN

IF I > J EXCHANGE I, J AND SET SIGN = -1

IF $\mathbf{K} > \mathbf{L}$ EXCHANGE K, L AND SET SIGN $= -$ SIGN

SET $\mathbf{N1} = (5*\mathbf{I} - \mathbf{I}*\mathbf{I}) / 2 + \mathbf{J}$

SET $\mathbf{N}2 = (5*\mathbf{K} - \mathbf{K}*\mathbf{K}) / 2 + \mathbf{L}$

IF N1 > N2 EXCHANGE N1, N2

SET $\mathbf{N} = (13*\mathrm{N1} - \mathrm{N1}*\mathrm{N1} - 12) / 2 + \mathrm{N2}$

SET RIEMANN = R(N)*SIGN

RETURN.

Solution 9.4. According to the discussion in Problem 9.2, there is only one independent component. We can choose it to be

$$
\mathsf {R} _ {\theta \phi \theta \phi} = \mathsf {g} _ {\theta \theta} \mathsf {R} _ {\phi \theta \phi} ^ {\theta} = \mathsf {g} _ {\theta \theta} \left\{\Gamma_ {\phi \phi , \theta} ^ {\theta} - \Gamma_ {\theta \phi , \phi} ^ {\theta} + \Gamma_ {\theta a} ^ {\theta} \Gamma_ {\phi \phi} ^ {a} - \Gamma_ {\phi a} ^ {\theta} \Gamma_ {\phi \theta} ^ {a} \right\}.
$$

From the metric $\mathbf{g}_{\theta \theta} = \mathbf{r}^2$ , $\mathbf{g}_{\phi \phi} = \mathbf{r}^2\sin^2\theta$ we find that the only nonvanishing Christoffel symbols are

$$
\Gamma_ {\phi \phi} ^ {\theta} = - \sin \theta \cos \theta , \quad \Gamma_ {\theta \phi} ^ {\phi} = \cot \theta ,
$$

and, therefore,

$$
R _ {\theta \phi \theta \phi} = r ^ {2} \sin^ {2} \theta = R _ {\phi \theta \phi \theta} = - R _ {\theta \phi \phi \theta} = - R _ {\phi \theta \theta \phi}.
$$

Solution 9.5. The only nonvanishing Christoffel symbols are $\Gamma_{\mathbf{uu}}^{\mathbf{v}} = \mathbf{v}$ and $\Gamma_{\mathbf{uv}}^{\mathbf{u}} = \Gamma_{\mathbf{vu}}^{\mathbf{u}} = \mathbf{v}^{-1}$ , so that

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathsf {R} _ {\mathsf {v u v u}} = \mathsf {R} _ {\mathsf {u v u}} ^ {\mathsf {v}} = \Gamma_ {\mathsf {u u}, \mathsf {v}} ^ {\mathsf {v}} - \Gamma_ {\mathsf {v u}, \mathsf {u}} ^ {\mathsf {v}} + \Gamma_ {\mathsf {v} \alpha} ^ {\mathsf {v}} \Gamma_ {\mathsf {u u}} ^ {\alpha} - \Gamma_ {\mathsf {u} \alpha} ^ {\mathsf {v}} \Gamma_ {\mathsf {u v}} ^ {\alpha} \\ = 1 - 0 + 0 - 1 = 0. \\ \end{array}
$$

Since there is only one independent Riemann component in two dimensions we conclude that $\mathbf{R}_{\alpha \beta \gamma \delta} = 0$ , and the spacetime is flat. (This was also shown by a coordinate transformation in Problem 6.1.)

Solution 9.6. From the diagram, two orthogonal infinitesimal displacements have length $\mathrm{ad}\phi$ and $(\mathbf{b} + \mathbf{a}\sin \phi)\mathrm{d}\theta$ so $\mathrm{ds}^2 = \mathbf{a}^2\mathrm{d}\phi^2 +$

$(\mathbf{b} + \mathbf{a}\sin \phi)^2\mathrm{d}\theta^2$ or $\mathbf{g}_{\theta \theta} = (\mathbf{b} + \mathbf{a}\sin \phi)^2$ $\mathbf{g}_{\phi \phi} = \mathbf{a}^2$ $\mathbf{g}_{\theta \phi} = \mathbf{g}_{\phi \theta} = 0$ . The formula for the Christoffel symbols now gives the nonvanishing $\Gamma$ 's

directly

$$
\Gamma_ {\theta \theta} ^ {\phi} = - a ^ {- 1} (b + a \sin \phi) \cos \phi
$$

$$
\Gamma_ {\theta \phi} ^ {\theta} = \Gamma_ {\phi \theta} ^ {\theta} = a \cos \phi (b + a \sin \phi) ^ {- 1}.
$$

![](images/676f10c7ed81633b56d68b980dfc5915d9f8b6a0ac296e4acc85b928efbe5b75.jpg)

![](images/a67afcc6a191ddbce0aba94b949cd380a227f47b8c5de22a42321784b58db37d.jpg)

In 2 dimensions the Riemann tensor has only one independent component. Take it to be $\mathbf{R}_{\phi \theta \phi \theta}$ , then the standard formula gives

$$
R _ {\phi \theta \phi \theta} = a \sin \phi (b + a \sin \phi).
$$

# Solution 9.7.

(a) In one dimension there can only be one component $\mathbf{R}_{111}$ , and by the symmetries of the Riemann tensor, this component must vanish.   
(b) In two dimensions the relations, due to symmetries, among the components of the Riemann tensor may be summarized in any coordinate system as

$$
R _ {\alpha \beta \gamma \delta} = (g _ {\alpha \gamma} g _ {\beta \delta} - g _ {\alpha \delta} g _ {\beta \gamma}) f
$$

but

$$
\mathbf {R} = \mathbf {R} _ {\alpha \beta} ^ {\alpha \beta} = (\mathbf {g} _ {\alpha} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {g} _ {\beta} ^ {\beta} - \mathbf {g} _ {\beta} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha} ^ {\beta}) \mathbf {f} = (4 - 2) \mathbf {f} = 2 \mathbf {f}.
$$

Thus, in any coordinate system

$$
\mathsf {R} _ {a \beta \gamma \delta} = \frac {1}{2} \left(\mathsf {g} _ {a \gamma} \mathsf {g} _ {\beta \delta} - \mathsf {g} _ {a \delta} \mathsf {g} _ {\beta \gamma}\right) \mathsf {R} .
$$

(c) In three dimensions there are six independent components of the Riemann tensor. Since the independent components of the Ricci tensor represent six independent linear combinations of the Riemann components, we should be able to invert such a relation to get the independent Riemann components in terms of the Ricci components.

To have the correct symmetries, we must have

$$
\begin{array}{l} R _ {\mu \nu \lambda \sigma} = a \left(g _ {\mu \lambda} R _ {\nu \sigma} - g _ {\nu \lambda} R _ {\mu \sigma} - g _ {\mu \sigma} R _ {\nu \lambda} + g _ {\nu \sigma} R _ {\mu \lambda}\right) \\ + b \left(g _ {\mu \lambda} g _ {\nu \sigma} - g _ {\mu \sigma} g _ {\nu \lambda}\right) R. \\ \end{array}
$$

Now we find a and b by contracting:

$$
R _ {\nu \sigma} = a \left(3 R _ {\nu \sigma} - R _ {\nu \sigma} - R _ {\nu \sigma} + g _ {\nu \sigma} R\right) + b \left(3 g _ {\nu \sigma} - g _ {\nu \sigma}\right) R.
$$

So we must have

$$
a = 1 \quad b = - \frac {1}{2} .
$$

Contract again to check:

$$
R = a (R + 3 R) + b \cdot 6 R = (4 - 3) R.
$$

Solution 9.8. We give the proof for a tensor of arbitrary rank. Choose locally flat coordinates so that all Christoffel symbols (but not their derivatives!) vanish at a point. At that point

$$
\mathsf {R} _ {\gamma \nu \kappa} ^ {\lambda} = \Gamma_ {\gamma \kappa , \nu} ^ {\lambda} - \Gamma_ {\gamma \nu , \kappa} ^ {\lambda} = - 2 \Gamma_ {\gamma [ \nu , \kappa ]} ^ {\lambda}
$$

and

$$
\begin{array}{l} \left(\mathrm {T} _ {a \dots} ^ {\beta \dots}\right) _ {, \nu \kappa} = \left(\mathrm {T} _ {a \dots} ^ {\beta \dots} _ {, \nu} + \mathrm {T} _ {a \dots} ^ {\sigma \dots} \Gamma_ {\sigma \nu} ^ {\beta} + \dots - \mathrm {T} _ {\sigma \dots} ^ {\beta \dots} \Gamma_ {a \nu} ^ {\sigma} - \dots\right) _ {, \kappa} \\ = \mathrm {T} _ {\alpha \dots} ^ {\beta \dots} _ {, \nu \kappa} + \mathrm {T} _ {\alpha \dots} ^ {\sigma \dots} \Gamma_ {\sigma \nu , \kappa} ^ {\beta} + \dots - \mathrm {T} _ {\sigma \dots} ^ {\beta \dots} \Gamma_ {\alpha \nu , \kappa} ^ {\sigma} - \dots . \\ \end{array}
$$

It follows that

$$
2 (\mathrm {T} _ {a} \dots^ {\beta \dots}); _ {[ \nu \kappa ]} = - \mathrm {T} _ {a} \dots^ {\sigma \dots} \mathrm {R} _ {\sigma \nu \kappa} ^ {\beta} - \dots + \mathrm {T} _ {\sigma} \dots^ {\beta \dots} \mathrm {R} _ {a \nu \kappa} ^ {\sigma} + \dots .
$$

Since this relation is covariant it is true in any coordinate system.

Solution 9.9. The second derivates of a scalar are

$$
\mathbf {S} _ {; a \beta} = (\mathbf {S} _ {, a}) _ {; \beta} = \mathbf {S} _ {, a \beta} - \mathbf {S} _ {, \sigma} \Gamma_ {a \beta} ^ {\sigma}.
$$

This expression is obviously symmetric in $\alpha$ and $\beta$ .

To investigate third derivatives use local flat coordinates as in Problem 9.8. We have then

$$
\mathsf {S} _ {; a \beta \gamma} = (\mathsf {S} _ {, a \beta} - \mathsf {S} _ {\sigma} \Gamma_ {a \beta} ^ {\sigma}) _ {, \gamma} = \mathsf {S} _ {, a \beta \gamma} - \mathsf {S} _ {\sigma} \mathsf {I} _ {a \beta , \gamma} ^ {\sigma} = \mathsf {S} _ {; (a \beta) \gamma}.
$$

From this expression and from the result of Problem 9.8 we also have

$$
\mathsf {S} _ {; \alpha [ \beta \gamma ]} = \frac {1}{2} \mathsf {S} _ {; \sigma} \mathsf {R} _ {\alpha \beta \gamma} ^ {\sigma}.
$$
