Solution 9.10. Form the tensor

$$
\mathbf {C} _ {\mu \nu} ^ {\alpha \beta} \equiv \mathbf {A} _ {; \mu \nu} ^ {\alpha \beta} - \mathbf {A} _ {; \nu \mu} ^ {\alpha \beta}.
$$

From Problem 9.8 we have

$$
\mathbf {C} _ {\mu \nu} ^ {\alpha \beta} = - \mathbf {A} _ {\sigma \mu \nu} ^ {\sigma \beta} \mathbf {R} _ {\sigma \mu \nu} ^ {\alpha} - \mathbf {A} _ {\sigma \mu \nu} ^ {\alpha \sigma} \mathbf {R} _ {\sigma \mu \nu} ^ {\beta}
$$

so that

$$
\mathbf {C} _ {\mu \nu} ^ {\mu \nu} = - \mathrm {A} _ {\sigma \mu \nu} ^ {\sigma \nu} \mathbf {R} _ {\sigma \mu \nu} ^ {\mu} - \mathrm {A} _ {\sigma \mu \nu} ^ {\mu \sigma} \mathbf {R} _ {\sigma \mu \nu} ^ {\nu} = - \mathrm {A} _ {\sigma \nu} ^ {\sigma \nu} \mathbf {R} _ {\sigma \nu} + \mathrm {A} _ {\sigma \mu} ^ {\mu \sigma} \mathbf {R} _ {\sigma \mu} = 0.
$$

Solution 9.11. Choose locally flat coordinates at the starting point $\mathbf{P}$ . Thus $\Gamma_{\beta \gamma}^{\alpha} = 0$ at $\mathbf{P}$ but $\Gamma_{\beta \gamma}^{\alpha} = \Gamma_{\beta \gamma, \delta}^{\alpha} \mathbf{u}^{\delta}$ after displacement $\mathbf{u}$ . When we displace $\mathbf{A}$ from $\mathbf{P}$ by $\mathbf{u}$ we find that its components become

$$
\begin{array}{l} [ \mathbf {A} (\text {d i s p l a c e d b y} \mathbf {u}) ] ^ {\alpha} = \mathbf {A} ^ {\alpha} + \mathbf {A} _ {, \beta} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {u} ^ {\beta} \\ \mathbf {\Gamma} = \mathbf {A} ^ {\alpha} + \mathbf {A} _ {; \beta} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {u} ^ {\beta} - \Gamma_ {\gamma \beta} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {A} ^ {\gamma} \mathbf {u} ^ {\beta} = \mathbf {A} ^ {\alpha}. \\ \end{array}
$$

When we displace, again, by $\mathbf{v}$ the new components are

$$
\begin{array}{l} [ \mathbf {A} (\text {d i s p l a c e d b y u t h e n v}) ] ^ {\alpha} = \mathbf {A} ^ {\alpha} + \mathbf {A} _ {; \beta} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {v} ^ {\beta} - \Gamma_ {\gamma \beta} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {A} ^ {\gamma} \mathbf {v} ^ {\beta} \\ = \mathrm {A} ^ {\alpha} - \Gamma_ {\gamma \beta} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {A} ^ {\gamma_ {\mathrm {v}} \beta} = \mathrm {A} ^ {\alpha} - \Gamma_ {\gamma \beta , \delta} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {A} ^ {\gamma_ {\mathrm {v}} \beta_ {\mathrm {u}} \delta}. \\ \end{array}
$$

We could have reached this same point by travelling around the other side of the parallelogram to find, similarly

$$
[ \mathbf {A} (\text {d i s p l a c e d b y} - \mathbf {v} \text {t h e n} - \mathbf {u}) ] ^ {a} = \mathbf {A} ^ {a} - \Gamma_ {\gamma \beta , \delta} ^ {a} \mathbf {A} ^ {\gamma} \mathbf {u} ^ {\beta} _ {\mathbf {v}} ^ {\delta}.
$$

The change in A in making the complete circuit is our first result minus our second

$$
\delta \mathbf {A} ^ {\alpha} = - \Gamma_ {\gamma \beta , \delta} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {A} ^ {\gamma_ {\mathbf {v}} \beta_ {\mathbf {u}} \delta} + \Gamma_ {\gamma \beta , \delta} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {A} ^ {\gamma_ {\mathbf {u}} \beta_ {\mathbf {v}} \delta} = - \mathbf {R} _ {\gamma \delta \beta} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {A} ^ {\gamma_ {\mathbf {v}} \beta_ {\mathbf {u}} \delta}.
$$

![](images/1509e0799e2b17fe9652f6ce268f5d15f03f0a0b70acac884f111bcb1fa2b20a.jpg)

Solution 9.12.

(a) First consider the linearity in A. It is clear that

$$
\mathbf {R} \left(\mathbf {A} _ {1} + \mathbf {A} _ {2}, \mathbf {B}\right) \mathbf {C} = \mathbf {R} \left(\mathbf {A} _ {1}, \mathbf {B}\right) \mathbf {C} + \mathbf {R} \left(\mathbf {A} _ {2}, \mathbf {B}\right) \mathbf {C}.
$$

Furthermore,

$$
\begin{array}{l} \nabla_ {\mathbf {f A}} \nabla_ {\mathbf {B}} - \nabla_ {\mathbf {B}} \nabla_ {\mathbf {f A}} - \nabla_ {[ \mathbf {f A}, \mathbf {B} ]} \\ = \mathrm {f} \nabla_ {\mathbf {A}} \nabla_ {\mathbf {B}} - \mathrm {f} \nabla_ {\mathbf {B}} \nabla_ {\mathbf {A}} - (\nabla_ {\mathbf {B}} \mathrm {f}) \nabla_ {\mathbf {A}} - \nabla_ {\mathbf {f}} [ \mathbf {A}, \mathbf {B} ] - \mathbf {A} \nabla_ {\mathbf {B}} \mathrm {f}. \tag {1} \\ \end{array}
$$

The two “extra” terms cancel and hence

$$
\mathbf {R} (\mathrm {f A}, \mathbf {B}) \mathbf {C} = \mathbf {f R} (\mathbf {A}, \mathbf {B}) \mathbf {C}. \tag {2}
$$

It is easy now to prove that the value of $\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{A},\mathbf{B})\mathbf{C}$ at a point $\mathbf{P}$ depends only on the value of $\mathbf{A}$ at $\mathbf{P}$ , not on the way in which $\mathbf{A}$ varies: Consider $f(P) = 0$ ; then Equation (2) tells us that $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{0}$ at $\mathbf{P}$ whenever the first argument vanishes at $\mathbf{P}$ . Now let $\mathbf{A}_1$ and $\mathbf{A}_2$ be two vector fields which are the same at $\mathbf{P}$ but different elsewhere. From (1) we know

$$
\mathrm {R} (\mathrm {A} _ {1}, \mathrm {B}) \mathrm {C} - \mathrm {R} (\mathrm {A} _ {2}, \mathrm {B}) \mathrm {C} = \mathrm {R} (\mathrm {A} _ {1} - \mathrm {A} _ {2}, \mathrm {B}) \mathrm {C} = 0.
$$

This completes the proof for A and, by symmetry, for B. The proof for C follows similarly.

(b) Since only the values of $\mathbf{A},\mathbf{B},\mathbf{C}$ at point $\mathbf{P}$ are important, we can arbitrarily choose all three to be covariantly constant in a neighborhood of $\mathbf{P}$ ; then we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} (\mathbf {R} (\mathbf {A}, \mathbf {B}) \mathbf {C}) ^ {\alpha} = (\nabla_ {\mathbf {A}} \nabla_ {\mathbf {B}} \mathbf {C} - \nabla_ {\mathbf {B}} \nabla_ {\mathbf {A}} \mathbf {C}) ^ {\alpha} \\ = 2 \mathrm {A} ^ {\left[ \sigma \left(\mathrm {B} ^ {\lambda} \right] \mathrm {C} _ {; \lambda} ^ {\alpha}\right)}; \sigma = 2 \mathrm {A} ^ {\sigma} \mathrm {B} ^ {\lambda} \mathrm {C} _ {; [ \lambda ; \sigma ]} ^ {\alpha}. \\ \end{array}
$$

According to Problem 9.8, this is equal to

$$
\mathbb {R} _ {\mu \lambda \sigma} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {C} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {A} ^ {\lambda} \mathrm {B} ^ {\sigma}
$$

and the proof is complete.

Solution 9.13. Let $\mathbf{x}^{\mu} = \mathbf{x}^{\mu}(\lambda, \mathfrak{n})$ be a family of geodesics with affine parameter $\lambda$ . The parameter $\mathfrak{n}$ labels the individual geodesics. The tangent to the geodesics is $\mathfrak{u}^a \equiv \partial \mathbf{x}^a / \partial \lambda$ and the differential vector which connects points of equal affine parameter on adjacent curves is

$$
\mathbf {n} ^ {\alpha} = \partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\alpha} / \partial \mathbf {n}.
$$

The solution to this problem is most transparent in abstract notation:

Write

$$
\mathbf {u} = \partial / \partial \lambda , \quad \mathbf {n} = \partial / \partial \mathbf {n}.
$$

Note that $[\mathbf{u},\mathbf{n}] = 0$ since the partial derivatives commute. (The vanishing of this commutator in fact is the definition of $\mathbf{n}$ as the connecting vector, according to Problem 8.14.) Now use the Riemann operator (see Problem 9.12):

$$
\mathbf {R} (\mathbf {u}, \mathbf {n}) \mathbf {u} \equiv \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \nabla_ {\mathbf {n}} \mathbf {u} - \nabla_ {\mathbf {n}} \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {u} - \nabla_ {[ \mathbf {u}, \mathbf {n} ]} \mathbf {u} = \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \nabla_ {\mathbf {n}} \mathbf {u} = \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {n} ,
$$

where the last equality follows from the vanishing of $[\mathbf{u},\mathbf{n}]$ . In components our result, according to Problem 9.12(b), reads

$$
\frac {\mathrm {D} ^ {2} \mathbf {\hat {n}} ^ {\alpha}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda^ {2}} = (\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {\hat {n}}) ^ {\alpha} = - \mathrm {R} _ {\beta \gamma \delta} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {u} ^ {\beta} \mathbf {\hat {n}} ^ {\gamma} \mathbf {u} ^ {\delta} .
$$

![](images/1942d7c92c5241335a222e9c4fa2776ace3fab929404a2c35aa1ee07ce10b4e6.jpg)

Solution 9.14. It is convenient to transform the spatial coordinates to spherical polars and to write the metric as

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - (1 - 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r}) \mathrm {d t} ^ {2} + (1 + 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r}) (\mathrm {d r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} [ \mathrm {d} \theta^ {2} + \sin^ {2} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2} ]).
$$

For a circular equatorial orbit, $\mathbf{u}^{\mathrm{r}} = \mathbf{u}^{\theta} = 0$ so

$$
\mathrm {D u ^ {r} / d r = 0 = u ^ {\alpha} \Gamma_ {\alpha \beta} ^ {r} u ^ {\beta} = (u ^ {0}) ^ {2} \Gamma_ {0 0} ^ {r} + (u ^ {\phi}) ^ {2} \Gamma_ {\phi \phi} ^ {r}}
$$

and therefore

$$
(\mathrm {d} \phi / \mathrm {d t}) ^ {2} = \omega^ {2} = - \Gamma_ {0 0} ^ {\mathrm {r}} / \Gamma_ {\phi \phi} ^ {\mathrm {r}}.
$$

The Christoffel symbols are easily calculated and we find

$$
\omega^ {2} = (2 \pi / \text {P e r i o d}) ^ {2} = \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r} ^ {3}.
$$

This is the same as the Newtonian result. In Problem 9.13 the equation of motion is derived for $\mathfrak{n}$ , the vector joining points of equal proper time on two geodesics. We are required in this problem to deal with $\xi$ , the separation of points on the two geodesics at a given coordinate time. From the diagram, however, it is clear that the fractional difference between $\mathfrak{n}$ and $\xi$ is proportional to the relative velocity of garbage and the Skylab, and hence can be ignored to lowest order.

Since all the Christoffel symbols in the $t, x, y, z$ system are of order $\mathbf{M} / \mathbf{r}^2$ , and $d / d\tau$ is of order $\omega \sim (\mathbf{M} / \mathbf{r})^{\frac{1}{2}}\mathbf{r}^{-1}$ , we can approximate

$$
\frac {\mathrm {D}}{\mathrm {d} r} = \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} r} + \Gamma u \approx \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} r}.
$$

Furthermore

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} \tau} = \mathrm {u} ^ {0} \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} t} = \left[ 1 + \mathcal {O} \left(\frac {\mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) \right] \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} t},
$$

so we can approximate $\mathbf{D}^2 /\mathrm{d}\tau^2\approx \mathbf{d}^2 /\mathrm{d}t^2$ . Using these approximations and the equation of geodesic deviation we have

$$
\frac {d ^ {2} \xi^ {i}}{d t ^ {2}} + R _ {0 j 0} ^ {i} (u ^ {0}) ^ {2} \xi^ {j} = 0 \quad i = x, y, z.
$$

![](images/4036cec75568616f24885209f2709d3aa3f8b16bfe4b0136453654feefc019b2.jpg)

![](images/449c27331708ffcd1fa1da1d5a4d6b2c279b2395d0a7ab0b6be99dfb1ebceadf.jpg)

To lowest order the Riemann components are

$$
R _ {0 j 0} ^ {i} \approx \Gamma_ {0 0, j} ^ {i} - \Gamma_ {0 j, 0} ^ {i} = - \frac {1}{2} g _ {0 0, i j} = \frac {M}{r ^ {3}} \left(\delta_ {i j} - \frac {3 x ^ {i} x ^ {j}}{r ^ {2}}\right).
$$

If we choose to describe the Skylab orbit as

$$
\mathbf {x} = \mathbf {r} \cos \omega t, \quad \mathbf {y} = \mathbf {r} \sin \omega t,
$$

then the equations of motion for $\xi$ become

$$
\ddot {\xi} ^ {z} + \omega^ {2} \xi^ {z} = 0
$$

$$
\ddot {\xi} ^ {x} + \omega^ {2} \xi^ {x} = 3 \omega^ {2} \cos \omega t (\cos \omega t \xi^ {x} + \sin \omega t \xi^ {y})
$$

$$
\ddot {\xi} ^ {y} + \omega^ {2} \xi^ {y} = 3 \omega^ {2} \sin \omega t (\cos \omega t \xi^ {x} + \sin \omega t \xi^ {y}).
$$

The relative motion in the $z$ -direction is obviously

$$
\zeta^ {z} \propto \sin \omega t
$$

if the garbage was jettisoned at $t = 0$ .

To find the relative motion in the xy plane introduce new variables $\eta^1, \eta^2$ defined by

$$
\xi^ {\mathrm {x}} = \eta^ {1} \cos \omega t + \eta^ {2} \sin \omega t, \quad \xi^ {\mathrm {y}} = \eta^ {1} \sin \omega t - \eta^ {2} \cos \omega t
$$

and the equations of motion become:

$$
\begin{array}{l} \ddot {\eta} ^ {2} - 2 \omega \dot {\eta} ^ {1} = 0 \\ \ddot {\eta} ^ {1} + 2 \omega \dot {\eta} ^ {2} - 3 \omega^ {2} \eta^ {1} = 0. \\ \end{array}
$$

These can be solved quite easily to find the four independent solutions:

$$
[ \eta^ {1}, \eta^ {2} ] = [ 1, \frac {3}{2} \omega t ], [ 0, 1 ], [ \cos \omega t, 2 \sin \omega t ], [ \sin \omega t, - 2 \cos \omega t ].
$$

The linear combination of solutions which correspond to the garbage jettisoned at $t = 0$ (i.e. $\xi^{\mathbf{x}} = \xi^{\mathbf{y}} = 0$ at $t = 0$ ) is

$$
\begin{array}{l} \xi^ {x} = A [ \cos 2 \omega t - 3 + 2 \cos \omega t + 3 \omega t \sin \omega t ] + B [ 4 \sin \omega t - \sin 2 \omega t ] \\ \xi^ {y} = A [ \sin 2 \omega t + 2 \sin \omega t - 3 \omega t \cos \omega t ] + B [ \cos 2 \omega t + 3 - 4 \cos \omega t ]. \\ \end{array}
$$

The constants A and B depend on the x and y components of the velocity with which the garbage was jettisoned. The nonperiodic terms in the solution correspond to the fact that the two orbits are of a slightly different period, so the relative distance will exhibit a secular growth in time.

Solution 9.15. Choose locally flat coordinates so that

$$
\mathbf {R} _ {\beta \gamma \delta} ^ {a} = 2 \Gamma_ {\beta [ \delta , \gamma ]} ^ {a}.
$$

In these coordinates then

$$
\mathsf {R} ^ {a} _ {[ \beta \gamma \delta ]} = 2 \Gamma^ {a} _ {[ \beta [ \delta , \gamma ] ]} = 2 \Gamma^ {a} _ {[ \beta \delta , \gamma ]}.
$$

Since the Christoffel symbols are symmetric on their lower indices the right side must vanish, and by lowering the first index we have

$\mathbf{R}_{\alpha}[\beta \gamma \delta] = 0$ . The Riemann tensor is antisymmetric in its last two indices, so this is equivalent to the cyclic identity.

From Problem 9.8 we have for any $\mathbf{U}^{\alpha}$ that

$$
2 \mathrm {U} _ {; [ \beta \gamma ]} ^ {\alpha} = - \mathrm {U} _ {\sigma \beta \gamma} ^ {\sigma} .
$$

Take $\mathbf{U}^{\alpha}$ to be fixed at some point and parallel transported along geodesics to all nearby points in some neighborhood, then at the point $\mathbf{U}^{\alpha};\beta = 0$ for all $\beta$ , and hence

$$
2 \mathrm {U} _ {; [ \beta \gamma ] \delta} ^ {\alpha} = - \mathrm {U} _ {\sigma \beta \gamma ; \delta} ^ {\sigma}
$$

and

$$
2 \mathrm {U} _ {; [ [ \beta \gamma ] \delta ]} ^ {\alpha} = 2 \mathrm {U} _ {; [ \beta \gamma \delta ]} ^ {\alpha} = - \mathrm {U} _ {\sigma} ^ {\sigma} \mathrm {R} _ {\sigma} ^ {\alpha} [ \beta \gamma ; \delta ] .
$$

From Problem 9.8 we have for the tensor $\mathbf{U}_{; \beta}^{\alpha}$ that

$$
2 \mathrm {U} _ {; \beta} ^ {\alpha} = - \mathrm {U} _ {; \beta} ^ {\sigma} \mathrm {R} _ {\sigma \gamma \delta} ^ {\alpha} + \mathrm {U} _ {; \sigma} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {R} _ {\beta \gamma \delta} ^ {\sigma}.
$$

Since $\mathbf{U}^{\alpha};\beta = 0$ at the point we have $\mathbf{U}^{\alpha};\beta [\gamma \delta ] = 0$ and hence $\mathbf{U}^{\alpha};[\beta \gamma \delta ] = 0$ We could have chosen the direction of $\mathbf{U}^{\alpha}$ at the point in any way so it follows that

$$
\mathbf {R} _ {\sigma} ^ {a} [ \beta \gamma ; \delta ] = 0.
$$

Since the Riemann tensor is antisymmetric on its last two indices, this is equivalent to the Bianchi identities. (The Bianchi identities can also, of course, be derived by the straightforward but tedious process of differentiating and manipulating the formula for Riemann components in terms of Christoffel symbols. This is best done in locally flat coordinates.)

Solution 9.16. By contracting the Bianchi identities,

$$
\mathsf {R} ^ {\alpha} _ {\beta \gamma \delta ; \epsilon} + \mathsf {R} ^ {\alpha} _ {\beta \epsilon \gamma ; \delta} + \mathsf {R} ^ {\alpha} _ {\beta \delta \epsilon ; \gamma} = 0 ,
$$

first on $\alpha$ and $\gamma$ then on $\beta$ and $\delta$ we get

$$
\begin{array}{l} R _ {\beta \delta ; \epsilon} + R _ {\beta \epsilon ; \delta} + R _ {\beta \delta \epsilon ; a} ^ {a} = 0 \\ R _ {; \epsilon} - R ^ {\beta} _ {\epsilon ; \beta} - R ^ {\alpha} _ {\epsilon ; \alpha} = 0. \\ \end{array}
$$

Thus the divergence of the Einstein tensor

$$
\mathbf {G} _ {\epsilon ; \nu} ^ {\nu} = \left(\mathbf {R} _ {\epsilon} ^ {\nu} - \frac {1}{2} \delta_ {\epsilon} ^ {\nu} \mathbf {R}\right); \nu = \mathbf {R} _ {\epsilon ; \nu} ^ {\nu} - \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {R} _ {; \epsilon}
$$

must vanish.

Solution 9.17. Choose $\mathbf{A}, \mathbf{B}, \mathbf{C}, \mathbf{D}$ to be mutually orthogonal at a point $\mathbf{P}$ and to satisfy $\mathbf{A} \cdot \mathbf{A} = -1$ , $\mathbf{B} \cdot \mathbf{B} = \mathbf{C} \cdot \mathbf{C} = \mathbf{D} \cdot \mathbf{D} = 1$ . Since spacetime is

Riemann flat, parallel transport around a closed curve does not change a vector, so we can define A,B,C,D away from P by parallel transport.

If we do this we have e.g.

$$
\frac {\partial \mathrm {A} _ {a}}{\partial \mathrm {x} ^ {\beta}} = - \Gamma_ {a \beta} ^ {\gamma} \mathrm {A} _ {\gamma}
$$

therefore

$$
\frac {\partial \mathbf {A} _ {a}}{\partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\beta}} = \frac {\partial \mathbf {A} _ {a}}{\partial \mathbf {x} ^ {a}}
$$

so we can choose $\mathbf{A}_a = \phi_{,a}$ , and similarly for $\mathbf{B},\mathbf{C}$ , and $\mathbf{D}$ . We can summarize by defining

$$
\mathbf {A} = \mathbf {W} ^ {(0)}, \quad \mathbf {B} = \mathbf {W} ^ {(1)}, \quad \mathbf {C} = \mathbf {W} ^ {(2)}, \quad \mathbf {D} = \mathbf {W} ^ {(3)}
$$

and we can define four functions $\Phi^{(0)},\Phi^{(1)},\Phi^{(2)},\Phi^{(3)}$ such that

$$
\boldsymbol {W} ^ {(\mu)} = \nabla \Phi^ {(\mu)}.
$$

Since inner products are preserved by parallel transport

$$
\mathbf {W} ^ {(\mu)} \cdot \mathbf {W} ^ {(\nu)} = [ \mathbf {W} ^ {(\mu)} \cdot \mathbf {W} ^ {(\nu)} ] _ {\mathbf {P}} = \eta^ {\mu \nu},
$$

and this tells us that

$$
\mathbf {g} ^ {\mu \nu} \frac {\partial \Phi^ {(\alpha)}}{\partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\mu}} \frac {\partial \Phi^ {(\beta)}}{\partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\nu}} = \eta^ {\alpha \beta}
$$

and hence there exists a coordinate transformation, namely

$$
\overline {{\mathbf {x}}} ^ {\mu} = \Phi^ {(\mu)} (\mathbf {x} ^ {\mu})
$$

which takes the metric to the Minkowskian form.

Solution 9.18. If the Weyl tensor vanishes, the metric can be written in the conformally flat form

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = \mathrm {e} ^ {2 \phi} \mathrm {d s} _ {0} ^ {2} = \mathrm {e} ^ {2 \phi} \eta_ {\mu \nu} \mathrm {d x} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {d x} ^ {\nu}
$$

where $\phi$ is a function of $\mathbf{x}^a$ . The null geodesics

$$
\mathrm {d t} = \underline {{\mathbf {n}}} \cdot \underline {{\mathrm {d x}}}, \quad \underline {{\mathbf {n}}} \cdot \underline {{\mathbf {n}}} = 1
$$

of $\mathbf{ds}_0^2$ remain null curves for $\mathbf{ds}^2$ . If we can show that they remain null geodesics, then clearly a cone of null geodesics, will remain a cone; there will be no preferred direction for squashing of the cross section.

To show the curves $\mathrm{dt} = \underline{\mathbf{n}}\cdot \underline{\mathbf{dx}}$ are geodesics we need only show that

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathbf {x} ^ {\alpha}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda^ {2}} = \frac {\mathrm {d p} ^ {\alpha}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} = 0
$$

for null geodesics. Note that the Christoffel symbols are

$$
\Gamma_ {\gamma \beta} ^ {a} = \eta^ {a \mu} [ - \phi_ {, \mu} \eta_ {\gamma \beta} + \phi_ {, \beta} \eta_ {\gamma \mu} + \phi_ {, \gamma} \eta_ {\mu \beta} ]
$$

so that

$$
\mathbf {p} ^ {\gamma} \mathbf {p} ^ {\beta} \Gamma_ {\gamma \beta} ^ {\alpha} = 2 \mathbf {p} ^ {\alpha} (\nabla \phi \cdot \mathbf {p})
$$

and

$$
0 = \frac {\mathrm {d p} ^ {\alpha}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} + \Gamma^ {\alpha} _ {\beta \gamma} \mathrm {p} ^ {\beta} \mathrm {p} ^ {\gamma} = \frac {\mathrm {d p} ^ {\alpha}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} + 2 \mathrm {p} ^ {\alpha} (\nabla \phi \cdot \mathbf {p}) .
$$

By rescaling $\lambda$ , then we have $\mathrm{dp}^{\alpha} / \mathrm{d}\lambda = 0$ and the straight null lines of $\mathrm{ds}_0^2$ are null geodesics of $\mathrm{ds}^2$ .

Notice that, while beams of light have undistorted cross section the cross sectional area, like all areas, is affected by the scaling factor $e^{2\phi}$ .

Since $\phi$ is in general anisotropic, beams propagating in different directions will grow in cross section at different rates.

Section 9.19. In a conformally flat spacetime the Weyl tensor vanishes, so the Riemann tensor can easily be computed from the Ricci tensor

$$
\mathrm {R} _ {\mu \nu} = - \left(\log | \mathrm {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}\right) _ {, \mu \nu} + \Gamma_ {\mu \nu , \alpha} ^ {\alpha} + \left(\log | \mathrm {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}\right) _ {, \alpha} \Gamma_ {\mu \nu} ^ {\alpha} - \Gamma_ {\mu \alpha} ^ {\beta} \Gamma_ {\nu \beta} ^ {\alpha}.
$$

The expressions we need are

$$
\begin{array}{l} \Gamma_ {\gamma \beta} ^ {a} = - \phi^ {, a} \eta_ {\gamma \beta} + \phi_ {, \beta} \delta_ {\gamma} ^ {a} + \phi_ {, \gamma} \delta_ {\beta} ^ {a} \\ \Gamma_ {\gamma \beta , a} ^ {a} = - \nabla^ {2} \phi \eta_ {\gamma \beta} + 2 \phi , \beta \gamma \\ \Gamma_ {\gamma \beta} ^ {a} \Gamma_ {\mu a} ^ {\gamma} = 6 \phi_ {, \mu} \phi_ {, \beta} - 2 (\nabla \phi) ^ {2} \eta_ {\mu \beta} \\ - \mathbf {g} = \mathbf {e} ^ {8 \phi}, \quad \log | \mathbf {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} = 4 \phi \\ \left(\log \left| \mathbf {g} \right| ^ {\frac {1}{2}}\right) _ {, \alpha} \Gamma_ {\gamma \beta} ^ {\alpha} = - 4 (\nabla \phi) ^ {2} \eta_ {\gamma \beta} + 8 \phi , \beta \phi , \gamma \\ \left(\log | \mathbf {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}\right), \gamma \beta = 4 \phi , \gamma \beta . \\ \end{array}
$$

In the above expressions raising and lowering have been done with $\eta_{\mu \nu}$ , e.g.

$$
\phi^ {, a} \equiv \eta^ {\mu a} \phi_ {, \mu}; \quad \nabla^ {2} \phi \equiv \eta^ {\mu a} \phi_ {, \mu a}; \quad (\nabla \phi) ^ {2} \equiv \eta^ {\mu a} \phi_ {, \mu} \phi_ {, a}.
$$

The Ricci tensor and scalar are then

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {R} _ {\mu \nu} = - 2 \phi_ {, \mu \nu} + 2 \phi_ {, \mu} \phi_ {, \nu} - \eta_ {\mu \nu} [ \nabla^ {2} \phi + 2 (\nabla \phi) ^ {2} ] \\ \mathbf {R} \equiv \mathbf {R} _ {\mu} ^ {\mu} = \mathbf {e} ^ {- 2 \phi} \eta^ {\mu \nu} \mathbf {R} _ {\mu \nu} = - 6 \mathbf {e} ^ {- 2 \phi} [ \nabla^ {2} \phi + (\nabla \phi) ^ {2} ] \\ \end{array}
$$

and the Riemann tensor is

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathsf {R} _ {\alpha \beta \gamma \delta} = \mathsf {C} _ {\alpha \beta \gamma \delta} + \frac {1}{2} \left(\mathsf {g} _ {\alpha \gamma} \mathsf {R} _ {\beta \delta} - \mathsf {g} _ {\alpha \delta} \mathsf {R} _ {\beta \gamma} - \mathsf {g} _ {\beta \gamma} \mathsf {R} _ {\alpha \delta} + \mathsf {g} _ {\beta \delta} \mathsf {R} _ {\alpha \gamma}\right) \\ - \frac {1}{6} \left(\mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \gamma} \mathbf {g} _ {\beta \delta} - \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \delta} \mathbf {g} _ {\beta \gamma}\right) \mathbf {R} \\ = \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {e} ^ {2 \phi} (\eta_ {\alpha \gamma} \mathrm {R} _ {\beta \delta} - \eta_ {\alpha \delta} \mathrm {R} _ {\beta \gamma} - \eta_ {\beta \gamma} \mathrm {R} _ {\alpha \delta} + \eta_ {\beta \delta} \mathrm {R} _ {\alpha \gamma}) \\ - \frac {1}{6} \mathrm {e} ^ {4 \phi} (\eta_ {\alpha \gamma} \eta_ {\beta \delta} - \eta_ {\alpha \delta} \eta_ {\beta \gamma}) \mathsf {R} \\ = \mathrm {e} ^ {2 \phi} [ \eta_ {\alpha \gamma} (\phi , \beta \phi , \delta - \phi , \beta \delta) - \eta_ {\alpha \delta} (\phi , \beta \phi , \gamma - \phi , \beta \gamma) \\ - \eta_ {\beta \gamma} (\phi , a \phi , \delta - \phi , a \delta) + \eta_ {\beta \delta} (\phi , a \phi , \gamma - \phi , a \gamma) \\ \left. + (\nabla \phi) ^ {2} (- \eta_ {a \gamma} \eta_ {\beta \delta} + \eta_ {a \delta} \eta_ {\beta \gamma}) ] \right.. \\ \end{array}
$$

Solution 9.20. We use the same orthonormal tetrad as in Problem 8.27:

$$
\tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\widehat {\mathbf {t}}} = \mathbf {e} ^ {\alpha} \widetilde {\mathbf {d t}}, \quad \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\widehat {\mathbf {r}}} = \mathbf {e} ^ {\beta} \widetilde {\mathbf {d r}}, \quad \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\widehat {\boldsymbol {\theta}}} = \mathbf {e} ^ {\gamma} \widetilde {\mathbf {d \theta}}, \quad \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\widehat {\boldsymbol {\phi}}} = \mathbf {e} ^ {\gamma} \sin \theta \widetilde {\mathbf {d \phi}}. \tag {1}
$$

In Problem 8.27, we computed the 6 connection 1-forms $\hat{\omega}_{\hat{\nu}}^{\hat{\mu}}$ from the equations

$$
\mathrm {d} \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\mu}} = - \tilde {\omega} _ {\hat {\nu}} ^ {\hat {\mu}} \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\nu}}, \quad \tilde {\omega} _ {\hat {\mu} \hat {\nu}} = - \tilde {\omega} _ {\hat {\nu}} \hat {\mu}. \tag {2}
$$

The Riemann tensor is most easily found from the 6 curvature 2-forms $\mathcal{R}^{\hat{\mu}}_{\hat{\nu}}$

$$
\mathcal {R} _ {\hat {\nu}} ^ {\hat {\mu}} = \mathrm {d} \tilde {\omega} _ {\hat {\nu}} ^ {\hat {\mu}} + \tilde {\omega} _ {\hat {\alpha}} ^ {\hat {\mu}} \wedge \tilde {\omega} _ {\hat {\nu}} ^ {\hat {\alpha}} \tag {3}
$$

$$
\mathcal {R} ^ {\hat {\mu} \hat {\nu}} = \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {R} ^ {\hat {\mu} \hat {\nu}} _ {\hat {\alpha}} \hat {\beta} \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\alpha}} \wedge \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\beta}} \tag {4}
$$

The advantage of this method is that only non-zero components of the Riemann tensor are computed. Using the results of Problem 8.27 in Equation (3), we find

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathcal {R} _ {\widehat {\mathrm {r}}} ^ {\mathrm {t}} = \mathbf {d} \left(a ^ {\prime} \mathrm {e} ^ {(a - \beta)} \widetilde {\mathrm {d t}} + \dot {\beta} \mathrm {e} ^ {\beta - a} \widetilde {\mathrm {d r}}\right) + \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\widehat {\theta}} ^ {\mathrm {t}} \wedge \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\widehat {\mathrm {r}}} ^ {\widehat {\theta}} + \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\widehat {\phi}} ^ {\mathrm {t}} \wedge \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\widehat {\mathrm {r}}} ^ {\widehat {\phi}} \\ = \left[ a ^ {\prime \prime} + \left(a ^ {\prime}\right) ^ {2} - a ^ {\prime} \beta^ {\prime} \right] e ^ {a - \beta} \widetilde {d r} \wedge \widetilde {d t} + \left(\ddot {\beta} + \dot {\beta} ^ {2} - \dot {a} \dot {\beta}\right) e ^ {\beta - a} \widetilde {d t} \wedge \widetilde {d r} \\ = \left[ e ^ {- 2 a} \left(\ddot {\beta} + \dot {\beta} ^ {2} - \dot {a} \dot {\beta}\right) - e ^ {- 2 \beta} \left(a ^ {\prime \prime} + a ^ {\prime 2} - a ^ {\prime} \beta^ {\prime}\right) \right] \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {t}} \wedge \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {r}}. \tag {5} \\ \end{array}
$$

Here a dot denotes $\partial/\partial t$ and a prime $\partial/\partial r$ . Notice how the term $\mathrm{d}\tilde{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_{\hat{\mathbf{r}}}^{\hat{\mathbf{t}}}$ is computed by writing $\tilde{\boldsymbol{\omega}}_{\hat{\mathbf{r}}}^{\hat{\mathbf{t}}}$ in the coordinate basis and using $\mathrm{dd} = 0$ . The remaining curvature 2-forms are

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathcal {R} ^ {\hat {\mathrm {t}}} _ {\hat {\theta}} = \mathbf {d} \left(\mathrm {e} ^ {\gamma - a} \dot {\gamma} \widetilde {\mathrm {d} \theta}\right) + \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\hat {\mathrm {r}}} ^ {\mathrm {t}} \wedge \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\mathrm {r}}} _ {\hat {\theta}} + \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\mathrm {t}} _ {\hat {\phi}} \wedge \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\phi}} _ {\hat {\theta}} \\ = [ \mathrm {e} ^ {- 2 a} (\ddot {\gamma} + \dot {\gamma} ^ {2} - \dot {\alpha} \dot {\gamma}) - \mathrm {e} ^ {- 2 \beta} a ^ {\prime} \gamma^ {\prime} ] \tilde {\omega} ^ {\mathrm {t}} \wedge \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\theta}} \\ + e ^ {- (a + \beta)} \left(\dot {\gamma} \gamma^ {\prime} - a ^ {\prime} \dot {\gamma} + \dot {\gamma} ^ {\prime} - \gamma^ {\prime} \dot {\beta}\right) \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {r}} \wedge \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\theta}} \tag {6} \\ \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \hat {\mathcal {R}} ^ {\mathrm {t}} \hat {\phi} = \mathrm {d} \left(\mathrm {e} ^ {\gamma - a} \dot {\gamma} \sin \theta \widetilde {\mathrm {d} \phi}\right) + \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\mathrm {r}} ^ {\mathrm {t}} \wedge \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\mathrm {r}} ^ {\mathrm {t}} \hat {\phi} + \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\mathrm {t}} ^ {\mathrm {t}} \hat {\theta} \wedge \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\mathrm {t}} ^ {\mathrm {t}} \hat {\phi} \\ = \left[ e ^ {- 2 \alpha} \left(\ddot {\gamma} + \dot {\gamma} ^ {2} - \dot {\alpha} \dot {\gamma}\right) - e ^ {- 2 \beta} \alpha^ {\prime} \gamma^ {\prime} \right] \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {t}} \wedge \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\phi}} \\ + e ^ {- (a + \beta)} \left(\dot {\gamma} \gamma^ {\prime} - a ^ {\prime} \dot {\gamma} + \dot {\gamma} ^ {\prime} - \gamma^ {\prime} \dot {\beta}\right) \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\tau}} \wedge \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\phi}} \tag {7} \\ \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathcal {R} ^ {\widehat {\mathbf {r}}} _ {\widehat {\theta}} = - \mathbf {d} \left(\mathrm {e} ^ {\gamma - \beta} \gamma^ {\prime} \widetilde {\mathbf {d} \theta}\right) + \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\widehat {\mathbf {t}}} ^ {\widehat {\mathbf {r}}} \wedge \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\mathbf {t}} _ {\widehat {\theta}} + \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\widehat {\phi}} ^ {\widehat {\mathbf {r}}} \wedge \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\widehat {\phi}} _ {\widehat {\theta}} \\ = \left[ e ^ {- 2 \beta} \left(y ^ {\prime \prime} + \gamma^ {\prime 2} - \beta^ {\prime} \gamma^ {\prime}\right) - e ^ {- 2 a} \dot {\beta} \dot {\gamma} \right] \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\theta}} \wedge \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {r}} \\ + \mathrm {e} ^ {- (\alpha + \beta)} \left(\dot {\gamma} \gamma^ {\prime} - \alpha^ {\prime} \dot {\gamma} + \dot {\gamma} ^ {\prime} - \gamma^ {\prime} \dot {\beta}\right) \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\theta}} \wedge \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\mathrm {t}}} \tag {8} \\ \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathcal {R} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} _ {\hat {\phi}} = - \mathrm {d} \left(\mathrm {e} ^ {\gamma - \beta} \gamma^ {\prime} \sin \theta \widetilde {\mathrm {d} \phi}\right) + \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} \wedge \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} _ {\hat {\phi}} + \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} _ {\hat {\theta}} \wedge \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\theta}} _ {\hat {\phi}} \\ = \left[ e ^ {- 2 \beta} \left(\gamma^ {\prime \prime} + \gamma^ {\prime 2} - \beta^ {\prime} \gamma^ {\prime}\right) - e ^ {- 2 a} \dot {\beta} \dot {y} \right] \tilde {\omega} \hat {\phi} \wedge \tilde {\omega} \hat {\mathbf {r}} \\ + \mathrm {e} ^ {- (a + \beta)} \left(\dot {\gamma} \gamma^ {\prime} - a ^ {\prime} \dot {\gamma} + \dot {\gamma} ^ {\prime} - \gamma^ {\prime} \dot {\beta}\right) \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\phi}} \wedge \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\mathrm {t}}} \tag {9} \\ \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathcal {R} _ {\widehat {\phi}} ^ {\widehat {\theta}} = - \mathbf {d} (\cos \theta \widetilde {\mathbf {d} \phi}) + \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\widehat {\mathbf {t}}} ^ {\widehat {\theta}} \wedge \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\widehat {\phi}} ^ {\mathbf {t}} + \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\widehat {\mathbf {r}}} ^ {\widehat {\theta}} \wedge \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\widehat {\phi}} ^ {\mathbf {r}} \\ = \left(\mathrm {e} ^ {- 2 \gamma} + \mathrm {e} ^ {- 2 a} \dot {\gamma} ^ {2} - \mathrm {e} ^ {- 2 \beta} \gamma^ {\prime 2}\right) \tilde {\omega} \hat {\theta} \wedge \tilde {\omega} \hat {\phi}. \tag {10} \\ \end{array}
$$

We can now read off the non-zero components of the Riemann tensor from Equation (4):

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {R} ^ {\widehat {\mathrm {t r}}} _ {\widetilde {\mathrm {t r}}} \\ = \mathbf {A} \end{array}
$$

$$
\mathbf {R} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {t}} \hat {\boldsymbol {\theta}}} _ {\hat {\mathbf {t}} \hat {\boldsymbol {\theta}}} = \mathbf {R} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {t}} \hat {\boldsymbol {\phi}}} _ {\hat {\mathbf {t}} \hat {\boldsymbol {\phi}}} = \mathbf {B}
$$

$$
\mathbf {R} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {t}} \hat {\boldsymbol {\theta}}} _ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} \hat {\boldsymbol {\theta}} = \mathbf {R} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {t}} \hat {\boldsymbol {\phi}}} _ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} \hat {\boldsymbol {\phi}} = - \mathbf {R} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {r}} \hat {\boldsymbol {\theta}}} _ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} \hat {\boldsymbol {\theta}} = - \mathbf {R} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {r}} \hat {\boldsymbol {\phi}}} _ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} \hat {\boldsymbol {\phi}} = \mathbf {C}
$$

$$
\mathbf {R} \hat {\theta} \hat {\phi} _ {\theta} \hat {\phi} = \mathbf {D}
$$

$$
\mathbf {R} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} \hat {\boldsymbol {\phi}} _ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} \hat {\boldsymbol {\phi}} = \mathbf {R} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} \hat {\boldsymbol {\theta}} _ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} \hat {\boldsymbol {\theta}} = \mathbf {E} \tag {11}
$$

where

$$
\mathbf {A} = \mathbf {e} ^ {- 2 a} (\ddot {\beta} + \dot {\beta} ^ {2} - \dot {\alpha} \dot {\beta}) - \mathbf {e} ^ {- 2 \beta} (a ^ {\prime \prime} + a ^ {\prime 2} - a ^ {\prime} \beta^ {\prime})
$$

$$
\mathbf {B} = \mathbf {e} ^ {- 2 a} \left(\ddot {\gamma} + \dot {\gamma} ^ {2} - \dot {\alpha} \dot {\gamma}\right) - \mathbf {e} ^ {- 2 \beta} a ^ {\prime} \gamma^ {\prime}
$$

$$
\mathbf {C} = \mathbf {e} ^ {- (\boldsymbol {a} + \boldsymbol {\beta})} (\dot {\boldsymbol {y}} ^ {\prime} + \dot {\boldsymbol {y}} \boldsymbol {\gamma} ^ {\prime} - \boldsymbol {a} ^ {\prime} \dot {\boldsymbol {\gamma}} - \dot {\boldsymbol {\beta}} \boldsymbol {\gamma} ^ {\prime})
$$

$$
\mathbf {D} = \mathbf {e} ^ {- 2 \gamma} + \mathbf {e} ^ {- 2 a} \dot {\gamma} ^ {2} - \mathbf {e} ^ {- 2 \beta} \gamma^ {2}
$$

$$
E = e ^ {- 2 \alpha} \dot {\beta} \dot {\gamma} - e ^ {- 2 \beta} \left(\gamma^ {\prime \prime} + \gamma^ {\prime 2} - \beta^ {\prime} \gamma^ {\prime}\right). \tag {12}
$$

The Ricci tensor is obtained by contracting the Riemann tensor:

$$
\mathbf {R} ^ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\alpha}}} \hat {\boldsymbol {\beta}} = \mathbf {R} ^ {\hat {\gamma} \hat {\boldsymbol {\alpha}}} \hat {\boldsymbol {\gamma}} \hat {\boldsymbol {\beta}}.
$$

This gives

$$
\mathbf {R} _ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} = \mathbf {A} + 2 \mathbf {B}
$$

$$
\mathbf {R} _ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} = 2 \mathbf {C}
$$

$$
\mathbf {R} _ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} = \mathbf {A} + 2 \mathbf {E}
$$

$$
\mathbf {R} ^ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta}}} _ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta}}} = \mathbf {R} ^ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\phi}}} _ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\phi}}} = \mathbf {B} + \mathbf {D} + \mathbf {E}
$$

$$
\mathbf {R} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} _ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta}}} = \mathbf {R} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} _ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\phi}}} = \mathbf {R} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} _ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta}}} = \mathbf {R} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} _ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\phi}}} = \mathbf {R} ^ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta}}} _ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\phi}}} = \mathbf {0}. \tag {13}
$$

The scalar curvature is

$$
\mathbf {R} = \mathbf {R} _ {\hat {a}} ^ {\hat {a}} = 2 \mathbf {A} + 4 \mathbf {B} + 2 \mathbf {D} + 4 \mathbf {E}. \tag {14}
$$

The Einstein tensor is found from

$$
\mathbf {G} ^ {\hat {\alpha}} \hat {\beta} = \mathbf {R} ^ {\hat {\alpha}} \hat {\beta} - \frac {1}{2} \delta^ {\hat {\alpha}} \hat {\beta} \mathbf {R}
$$

i.e.

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {G} _ {\hat {\mathrm {t}}} = - (\mathrm {D} + 2 \mathrm {E}) \\ \mathbf {G} _ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} = 2 \mathbf {C} \\ \mathbf {G} _ {\hat {\tau}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} = - (\mathrm {D} + 2 \mathrm {B}) \\ \mathbf {G} ^ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta}}} _ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta}}} = \mathbf {G} ^ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\phi}}} _ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\phi}}} = - (\mathbf {A} + \mathbf {B} + \mathbf {E}) \\ \mathbf {G} _ {\hat {\theta}} ^ {\hat {\mathrm {t}}} = \mathbf {G} _ {\hat {\phi}} ^ {\hat {\mathrm {t}}} = \mathbf {G} _ {\hat {\theta}} ^ {\hat {\mathrm {r}}} = \mathbf {G} _ {\hat {\phi}} ^ {\hat {\mathrm {r}}} = \mathbf {G} _ {\hat {\phi}} ^ {\hat {\theta}} = 0 \tag {15} \\ \end{array}
$$

Solution 9.21. Choose an orthonormal basis $\mathbf{e}_{\hat{\alpha}}$ and construct a complex, null tetrad basis oriented so that the wave vector $\nabla \mathbf{u} \equiv \mathbf{k}$ is one of the tetrad legs:

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {k} = (2) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \left(\mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} + \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {z}}} ^ {\prime}\right) \\ l = (2) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \left(e _ {\hat {t}} - e _ {\hat {z}}\right) \\ \mathbf {m} = (2) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \left(\mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {x}}} ^ {\prime} + \mathbf {i} \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {y}}} ^ {\prime}\right) \\ \overline {{\mathbf {m}}} = (2) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \left(\mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {x}}} - \mathrm {i} \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {y}}}\right) \tag {1} \\ \end{array}
$$

and consider the components of the Riemann tensor in this basis: e.g. $\mathbf{R}_{1\mathfrak{m}\overline{\mathfrak{m}} 1} = \mathbf{R}_{\alpha \beta \gamma \delta}1^{\alpha}\mathfrak{m}^{\beta}\overline{\mathfrak{m}}^{\gamma}\mathfrak{l}^{\delta}$ etc. Since the Riemann tensor is a function only of retarded time and since the only nonvanishing dot products of the basis vectors in Equation (1) are

$$
- \mathbf {k} \cdot \mathbf {l} = \mathbf {m} \cdot \overline {{\mathbf {m}}} = 1, \tag {2}
$$

one finds

$$
R _ {a b c d, p} = 0 \tag {3}
$$

where $(\mathbf{a},\mathbf{b},\mathbf{c},\mathbf{d})$ range over $(\mathbf{k},\mathbf{l},\mathbf{m},\overline{\mathbf{m}})$ and $(\mathbf{p},\mathbf{q},\mathbf{r}\dots)$ range over $(\mathbf{k},\mathbf{m},\overline{\mathbf{m}})$ . Now, consider the following subset of Bianchi identities for the Riemann tensor:

$$
R _ {a b [ p q, 1 ]} = 0 \tag {4}
$$

where 1 is the 1 index of Equation (1). Using Equation (3), we get

$$
R _ {a b p q, 1} = 0 \tag {5}
$$

from Equation (4), and Equations (5) and (3) imply

$$
R _ {a b p q} = R _ {p q a b} = 0, \tag {6}
$$

aside from a trivial, nonwavelike constant. Consequently, all nonvanishing components of the Riemann tensor must have the form $\mathbf{R}_{\mathrm{plq1}}$ and, taking into account the symmetries of the Riemann tensor, we see that there are only six independent components. These six components correspond to the number of degrees of freedom in the most general gravitational wave in the generic metric theory of gravity. Einstein's theory has only two degrees of freedom in a gravitational wave.

Solution 9.22. In a local Lorentz frame, the acceleration of any particle is related to $\mathbf{F}^{\mu \nu}$ by the Lorentz force law:

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathbf {x} ^ {\alpha}}{\mathrm {d} r ^ {2}} = \frac {\mathrm {e}}{\mathrm {m}} \mathbf {F} ^ {\alpha} _ {\beta} \mathrm {u} ^ {\beta}.
$$

It is convenient to think of the 6 components of $\mathbf{F}_{\beta}^{\alpha}$ as the vectors $\underline{\mathbf{E}}$ and $\underline{\mathbf{B}}$ . In the comoving frame of one particle, it has zero velocity and so feels no magnetic forces. Measuring its three components of acceleration gives $\underline{\mathbf{E}}$ . Measuring the acceleration of a second particle as seen in that frame gives only two components of $\underline{\mathbf{B}}$ , since the components of $\underline{\mathbf{B}}$ along the direction of motion of the second particle produces no acceleration. Thus at least three particles are required to measure all components of $\mathbf{F}_{\beta}^{\alpha}$ .

The reader is invited to figure out the answer for the Riemann tensor from the geodesic deviation equation

$$
\frac {\mathrm {D} ^ {2} \xi^ {\alpha}}{\mathrm {d} \tau^ {2}} = - \mathrm {R} _ {\beta \gamma \delta} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {u} ^ {\beta} \xi^ {\gamma} \mathrm {u} ^ {\delta}.
$$

Solution 9.23. Choose a coordinate system geared to the surface. Let $\mathbf{x}^1, \mathbf{x}^2$ vary, and $\mathbf{x}^3, \mathbf{x}^4$ be constant on the surface. The only contravariant components of $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{B}$ are then the 1 and 2 components. We are interested then only in the Riemann components with indices 1 and 2, but there is only one such independent component, $\mathbb{R}_{1212}$ . The nonvanishing Riemann components of this type are related by symmetries which can be expressed as

$$
R _ {i j k 1} \propto (g _ {i k} g _ {i 1} - g _ {i 1} g _ {j k}) \quad i, j, k, 1 = 1, 2.
$$

It follows immediately that in this coordinate system $\mathbf{K}$ is independent of $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{B}$ . Since $\mathbf{K}$ is clearly coordinate independent the desired result is proved.

Solution 9.24. Let the interior of the circuit be subdivided into infinitesimal parallelograms, and consider the change in angle for one small rectangle. If $\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{v}$ represent the sides of one rectangle then from Problems 9.23 and 9.11 we have that the change in $\mathbf{A}$ after transport is

![](images/1f1bd8068fbd78fbf78d386dcffbf8ae753fc88af060de901e39f49477076b35.jpg)  
Circuit $\pmb{\Sigma}$ - approximated with rectangles

$$
\delta \mathbf {A} _ {\alpha} = - \mathbf {R} _ {\alpha \beta \mu \nu} \mathbf {A} ^ {\beta} \mathbf {u} ^ {\mu} \mathbf {v} ^ {\nu} = \mathbf {K} (\mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \mu} \mathbf {g} _ {\beta \nu} - \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \nu} \mathbf {g} _ {\beta \mu}) \mathbf {A} ^ {\beta} \mathbf {u} ^ {\mu} \mathbf {v} ^ {\nu}.
$$

Note that $\mathbf{A}^{\alpha}\delta \mathbf{A}_{\alpha} = \frac{1}{2}\delta (\mathbf{A}\cdot \mathbf{A}) = 0$ so the length of A is unchanged. The change in angle $\theta$ between A and B can be calculated from

$$
\mathbf {B} ^ {\alpha} \delta \mathbf {A} _ {\alpha} = \delta (\mathbf {A} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {B} _ {\alpha}) = | \mathbf {A} | | \mathbf {B} | \delta (\cos \theta) = | \mathbf {A} | | \mathbf {B} | \sin \theta \delta \theta
$$

and

$$
\mathbf {B} ^ {\alpha} \delta \mathbf {A} _ {\alpha} = \mathbf {K} [ (\mathbf {B} \cdot \mathbf {u}) (\mathbf {A} \cdot \mathbf {v}) - (\mathbf {B} \cdot \mathbf {v}) (\mathbf {A} \cdot \mathbf {u}) ] .
$$

Now choose a locally flat coordinate system on the surface with $\mathbf{u} \sim \mathbf{e}_1$ and $\mathbf{v} \sim \mathbf{e}_2$ then

$$
\begin{array}{l} \left| \left| \mathbf {A} \right| \left| \mathbf {B} \right| \sin \theta \delta \theta \right| = \mathrm {K} (\mathbf {B} ^ {1} \mathbf {A} ^ {2} - \mathbf {B} ^ {2} \mathbf {A} ^ {1}) \mathbf {u v} \\ = K | \mathbf {A} \times \mathbf {B} | u v = K \sin \theta | \mathbf {A} | | \mathbf {B} | \cdot (a r e a \enspace \text {e n c l o s e d}). \\ \end{array}
$$

Hence, for transport around a small rectangle $|\delta \theta| = K\delta \Sigma$ . If we now consider a more general circuit, the change in A due to transport around that circuit will be equal to the change in A due to transport around all the rectangles into which the circuit is divided, and the area of the circuit is the sum of the areas of the rectangles, thus $|\Delta \theta| = K\Delta \Sigma$ .

Solution 9.25. Define

$$
\mathrm {W} _ {a \gamma \beta \delta} \equiv \mathrm {K} \left(\mathrm {g} _ {a \beta} \mathrm {g} _ {\gamma \delta} - \mathrm {g} _ {a \delta} \mathrm {g} _ {\beta \gamma}\right) - \mathrm {R} _ {a \gamma \beta \delta} \tag {1}
$$

and note that $\mathbb{W}_{\alpha \gamma \beta \delta}$ has the same symmetries as $\mathbb{R}_{\alpha \gamma \beta \delta}$ . If $\mathbf{K}$ is independent of the vectors $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{B}$ at the given point, then by the definition of $\mathbf{K}$ (Problem 9.23)

$$
\mathrm {W} _ {\alpha \gamma \beta \delta} \mathrm {A} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {A} ^ {\beta} \mathrm {B} ^ {\gamma} \mathrm {B} ^ {\delta} = 0 \tag {2}
$$

for all A, B, thus

$$
\mathrm {W} _ {a \gamma \beta \delta} + \mathrm {W} _ {\beta \delta a \gamma} + \mathrm {W} _ {a \delta \beta \gamma} + \mathrm {W} _ {\beta \gamma a \delta} = 0. \tag {3}
$$

The symmetries of $\mathbb{W}_{\alpha \gamma \beta \delta}$ allow us to rewrite Equation (3) as

$$
\mathrm {W} _ {\alpha \gamma \beta \delta} = \mathrm {W} _ {\alpha \delta \gamma \beta}. \tag {4}
$$

Permute $(\gamma \beta \delta)$ cyclically:

$$
\mathrm {W} _ {\alpha \delta \gamma \beta} = \mathrm {W} _ {\alpha \beta \delta \gamma}. \tag {5}
$$

Substitute Equations (4) and (5) in the cyclic identity

$$
\mathrm {W} _ {\alpha \gamma \beta \delta} + \mathrm {W} _ {\alpha \delta \gamma \beta} + \mathrm {W} _ {\alpha \beta \delta \gamma} = 0 \tag {6}
$$

and find

$$
\mathbf {W} _ {\alpha \gamma \beta \delta} = 0
$$

which is the required result.

Solution 9.26. Since the metric is always covariantly constant

$$
\mathsf {R} _ {a \beta \gamma \delta ; \lambda} = \mathsf {K} _ {, \lambda} (\mathsf {g} _ {a \gamma} \mathsf {g} _ {\beta \delta} - \mathsf {g} _ {a \delta} \mathsf {g} _ {\beta \gamma}) .
$$

Now substitute in the Bianchi identities

$$
0 = \mathrm {R} _ {a} \beta \gamma \delta ; \lambda + \mathrm {R} _ {a} \beta \lambda \gamma ; \delta + \mathrm {R} _ {a} \beta \delta \lambda ; \gamma
$$

and contract on $\alpha, \gamma$ and $\beta, \delta$ to find $K_{,\lambda} = 0$ i.e. $K$ is constant.

Solution 9.27. By contracting we see

$$
\begin{array}{l} R _ {\mu \kappa} = 3 K g _ {\mu \kappa} \\ R = 1 2 K. \\ \end{array}
$$

The definition of the Weyl tensor is

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {C} _ {\lambda \mu \nu \kappa} = \mathrm {R} _ {\lambda \mu \nu \kappa} - \frac {1}{2} \left(\mathrm {g} _ {\lambda \nu} \mathrm {R} _ {\mu \kappa} - \mathrm {g} _ {\lambda \kappa} \mathrm {R} _ {\mu \nu} - \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \nu} \mathrm {R} _ {\lambda \kappa} + \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \kappa} \mathrm {R} _ {\lambda \nu}\right) \\ + \frac {1}{6} \left(\mathrm {g} _ {\lambda \nu} \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \kappa} - \mathrm {g} _ {\lambda \kappa} \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \nu}\right) \mathrm {R}, \\ \end{array}
$$

so that

$$
\begin{array}{l} C _ {\lambda \mu \nu \kappa} = K (g _ {\lambda \nu} g _ {\mu \kappa} - g _ {\lambda \mu} g _ {\nu \kappa}) - \frac {1}{2} \cdot 3 K (2 g _ {\lambda \nu} g _ {\mu \kappa} - 2 g _ {\lambda \mu} g _ {\nu \kappa}) \\ + \frac {1}{6} \cdot 1 2 K \left(g _ {\lambda \nu} g _ {\mu \kappa} - g _ {\lambda \kappa} g _ {\mu \nu}\right) = 0. \\ \end{array}
$$

Solution 9.28. Since $\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{u} = 0$ we have immediately

$$
\begin{array}{l} u ^ {\alpha} \left(n ^ {\beta} u _ {\beta}\right); a = 0 = n ^ {\beta}; a u ^ {\alpha} u _ {\beta} + n ^ {\beta} u _ {\beta}; a u ^ {\alpha} \\ = - K _ {\alpha \beta} u ^ {\alpha} u ^ {\beta} + 2 n \cdot \xi . \\ \end{array}
$$

Solution 9.29. The metric has the form

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - \mathrm {d} \tau^ {2} + \mathrm {g} _ {\mathrm {i j}} \mathrm {d x} ^ {\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {d x} ^ {\mathrm {j}}
$$

where

$$
\mathbf {g} _ {\mathbf {i j}} = \mathsf {a} ^ {2} (\boldsymbol {\tau}) \gamma_ {\mathbf {i j}} (\mathbf {x} ^ {\mathbf {k}}) .
$$

The normal vector to the $\tau =$ constant surfaces is $\mathbf{n} = \partial /\partial \tau$ . Thus

$$
\mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {i j}} = - \mathbf {e} _ {\mathrm {j}} \cdot \nabla_ {\mathrm {i}} \mathbf {n} = \mathbf {n} \cdot \nabla_ {\mathrm {i}} \mathbf {e} _ {\mathrm {j}} = \Gamma_ {\mathrm {n j i}} = - \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {g} _ {\mathrm {i j}, \mathrm {n}} = \frac {- \mathrm {a} , r}{\mathrm {a}} \mathbf {g} _ {\mathrm {i j}}.
$$

Solution 9.30. From the definition of the Lie derivative (Problem 8.13) we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {n}} \mathrm {P} _ {\alpha \beta} = \mathrm {P} _ {\alpha \beta ; \gamma} \mathrm {n} ^ {\gamma} + \mathrm {P} _ {\gamma \beta} \mathrm {n} ^ {\gamma}; \alpha + \mathrm {P} _ {\alpha \gamma} \mathrm {n} ^ {\gamma}; \beta \\ = \left(\mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \beta} - \mathrm {n} _ {\alpha} \mathrm {n} _ {\beta}\right); \gamma \mathrm {n} ^ {\gamma} + \left(\mathrm {g} _ {\gamma \beta} - \mathrm {n} _ {\gamma} \mathrm {n} _ {\beta}\right) \mathrm {n} ^ {\gamma}; \alpha + \left(\mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \gamma} - \mathrm {n} _ {\alpha} \mathrm {n} _ {\gamma}\right) \mathrm {n} ^ {\gamma}; \beta \\ = - \left(\mathrm {n} _ {\alpha} \mathrm {n} _ {\beta}\right); \gamma \mathrm {n} ^ {\gamma} + \mathrm {n} _ {\beta}; \alpha + \mathrm {n} _ {\alpha}; \beta \tag {1} \\ \end{array}
$$

where we have used $\mathfrak{n}_{\gamma}\mathfrak{n}^{\gamma};a = \frac{1}{2} (\mathfrak{n}_{\gamma}\mathfrak{n}^{\gamma});a = 0.$ If $\mathbf{e}_i$ are three basis vectors in the hypersurface, then

$$
\mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {i j}} = - \mathrm {e} _ {\mathrm {j}} \cdot \nabla_ {\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {n} = - \mathrm {n} _ {\mathrm {i}; \mathrm {j}} = - \frac {1}{2} \left(\mathrm {n} _ {\mathrm {i}; \mathrm {j}} + \mathrm {n} _ {\mathrm {j}; \mathrm {i}}\right) \tag {2}
$$

where we have used the fact that $\mathbf{K}_{ij}$ is symmetric. If we are using a general coordinate system (i.e. not one in which $i, j$ lie in the hypersurface), then Equation (2) can be rewritten

$$
\mathrm {K} _ {\alpha \beta} = - \frac {1}{2} \left(\mathrm {n} _ {\gamma ; \delta} + \mathrm {n} _ {\delta ; \gamma}\right) \mathrm {P} _ {\alpha} ^ {\gamma} \mathrm {P} _ {\beta} ^ {\delta} \tag {3}
$$

i.e. as the symmetrized covariant derivative of $\mathbf{n}$ projected into the hypersurface. Using the explicit form of $\mathbf{P}_a^\gamma$ in Equation (3), and comparing with Equation (1), we find

$$
\mathrm {K} _ {\alpha \beta} = - \frac {1}{2} \mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {n}} \mathrm {P} _ {\alpha \beta}.
$$

Solution 9.31. Let the surface be $\mathbf{x}^3 = 0$ , where $(\mathbf{x}^1, \mathbf{x}^2, \mathbf{x}^3)$ are Gaussian normal coordinates (see Problem 8.25) based on this surface. Then

the area is

$$
A = \int g ^ {\frac {1}{2}} d x ^ {1} d x ^ {2} \tag {1}
$$

where $\mathbf{g}$ is the determinant of the two-dimensional metric $\mathbf{g}_{ij}$ in the surface. Vary the area by displacing surface elements a distance $\delta \mathbf{x}^3$ along the normal $\mathbf{n} = \partial/\partial \mathbf{x}^3$ :

$$
\delta \mathbf {A} = \int \delta \mathbf {g} ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {1} \mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {2}.
$$

In Problem 21.1 it is shown that

$$
\delta \mathbf {g} ^ {\frac {1}{2}} = \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {g} ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {g} ^ {\mathrm {i j}} \delta \mathbf {g} _ {\mathrm {i j}} = \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {g} ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {g} ^ {\mathrm {i j}} \mathbf {g} _ {\mathrm {i j}, 3} \delta \mathbf {x} ^ {3}.
$$

But we have chosen Gaussian normal coordinates, so

$$
\mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {i j}} = - \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {g} _ {\mathrm {i j}, 3}
$$

$$
\delta A = - \int g ^ {i j} K _ {i j} g ^ {\frac {1}{2}} d x ^ {1} d x ^ {2} \delta x ^ {3}.
$$

Since $\mathbf{A}$ is a minimum, $\delta \mathbf{A} = 0$ for all $\delta \mathbf{x}^3$ , and hence we must have

$$
\mathbf {K} = \mathbf {g} ^ {\mathbf {i j}} \mathbf {K} _ {\mathbf {i j}} = 0.
$$

Solution 9.32. Let $\mathbf{e}_i$ be a coordinate basis in $\Sigma$ ; the equations to be derived are tensor equations and so will be valid independent of the basis used to derive them. The vectors $\mathbf{e}_i$ along with $\mathbf{n}$ form a basis for the spacetime, so

$$
\left(^ {(4)} \nabla_ {i} e _ {j} = \alpha_ {i j} n + \beta^ {k} _ {i j} e _ {k}\right). \tag {1}
$$

The coefficients in Equation (1) can be found by dotting in $\mathbf{n}$ and $\mathbf{e_k}$ $(\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{e_i} = 0)$ :

$$
\varepsilon a _ {i j} = \mathbf {n} \cdot^ {(4)} \nabla_ {i} \mathbf {e} _ {j} = ^ {(4)} \nabla_ {i} (\mathbf {n} \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {j}) - \mathbf {e} _ {j} \cdot^ {(4)} \nabla_ {i} \mathbf {n} = 0 + K _ {i j}
$$

$$
\beta_ {\mathbf {k i j}} = \mathbf {e} _ {\mathbf {k}} \cdot^ {(4)} \nabla_ {\mathbf {i}} \mathbf {e} _ {\mathbf {j}} = ^ {(4)} \Gamma_ {\mathbf {k j i}} = ^ {(3)} \Gamma_ {\mathbf {k j i}}.
$$

The last equality follows from the fact that the Christoffel symbol can be computed directly from ${}^{(3)}\mathbf{g}_{ij}$ . With these coefficients Equation (1) becomes the "Gauss-Weingarten equation."

$$
\left(^ {(4)} \nabla_ {i} e _ {j} = \varepsilon K _ {i j} n + ^ {(3)} \Gamma_ {j i} ^ {k} e _ {k}\right). \tag {2}
$$

The Riemann tensor can be computed from derivatives of the $\Gamma$ 's; it is somewhat quicker to use the curvature operator (see Problem 9.12):

$$
\mathbf {R} _ {\alpha \beta \gamma \delta} = \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha} \cdot \mathbf {R} (\mathbf {e} _ {\gamma}, \mathbf {e} _ {\delta}) \mathbf {e} _ {\beta} \tag {3}
$$

where

$$
\mathbf {R} \left(\mathbf {e} _ {\gamma}, \mathbf {e} _ {\delta}\right) = \left[ \nabla_ {\gamma}, \nabla_ {\delta} \right] - \nabla_ {\left[ \mathbf {e} _ {\gamma}, \mathbf {e} _ {\delta} \right]} . \tag {4}
$$

Evaluate Equation (4) for $\mathbf{e}_{\mathbf{j}}$ and $\mathbf{e}_{\mathbf{k}}$ . The last term vanishes because $[\mathbf{e}_{\mathbf{j}}, \mathbf{e}_{\mathbf{k}}] = 0$ for a coordinate basis, and

$$
\begin{array}{l} { } ^ { ( 4 ) } \nabla _ { j } { } ^ { ( 4 ) } \nabla _ { k } \mathbf { e } _ { i } = { } ^ { ( 4 ) } \nabla _ { j } ( \varepsilon K _ { i k } \mathbf { n } + { } ^ { ( 3 ) } \Gamma _ { i k } ^ { m } \mathbf { e } _ { m } ) \\ = \varepsilon K _ {i k, j} n - \varepsilon K _ {i k} K _ {j} ^ {m} e _ {m} + ^ {(3)} \Gamma_ {i k, j} ^ {m} e _ {m} \\ + ^ {(3)} \Gamma_ {i k} ^ {m} \left(\varepsilon K _ {j m} n + ^ {(3)} \Gamma_ {m j} ^ {n} e _ {n}\right). \tag {5} \\ \end{array}
$$

Evaluate Equation (5) with $j$ and $k$ interchanged and subtract the two equations. The result is

$$
\mathbf {R} \left(\mathbf {e} _ {j}, \mathbf {e} _ {k}\right) \mathbf {e} _ {i} = \varepsilon \left(\mathrm {K} _ {i k} | _ {j} - \mathrm {K} _ {i j} | _ {k}\right) n + \mathbf {e} _ {m} \left[ \varepsilon \left(\mathrm {K} _ {i j} \mathrm {K} _ {k} ^ {\mathrm {m}} - \mathrm {K} _ {i k} \mathrm {K} _ {j} ^ {\mathrm {m}}\right) + ^ {(3)} \mathrm {R} _ {i j k} ^ {\mathrm {m}} \right]. (6)
$$

Dotting Equation (6) with $\mathbf{n}$ and $\mathbf{e}_{\mathbf{n}}$ respectively gives the Gauss-Codazzi equations.

The components of the Riemann tensor with two $\mathbf{n}$ 's can be found from $\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{e}_{\mathbf{k}}, \mathbf{n}) \mathbf{n}$ . Since $\mathbf{n}$ has been chosen to be a coordinate basis vector, $[\mathbf{n}, \mathbf{e}_{\mathbf{k}}] = 0$ so the last term in Equation (4) vanishes and thus

$$
R \left(e _ {k}, n\right) n = ^ {(4)} \nabla_ {k} ^ {\prime} ^ {(4)} \nabla_ {n} n - ^ {(4)} \nabla_ {n} ^ {(4)} \nabla_ {k} n.
$$

But ${}^{(4)}\nabla_{\mathbf{n}}\mathfrak{n} = 0$ because $\mathbf{n}$ is tangent to a geodesic (Gaussian normal coordinates), so that

$$
\mathbf {e} _ {\mathbf {i}} \cdot \mathbf {R} (\mathbf {e} _ {\mathbf {k}}, \mathbf {n}) \mathbf {n} = \mathbf {e} _ {\mathbf {i}} \cdot \mathbf {\Sigma} ^ {(4)} \nabla_ {\mathbf {n}} (\mathbf {K} _ {\mathbf {k}} ^ {\mathrm {m}} \mathbf {e} _ {\mathbf {m}}) = \mathbf {\Sigma} \mathbf {K} _ {\mathbf {k i}, \mathbf {n}} - \mathbf {K} _ {\mathbf {k}} ^ {\mathrm {m}} \mathbf {e} _ {\mathbf {m}} \cdot \nabla_ {\mathbf {n}} \mathbf {e} _ {\mathbf {i}}.
$$

Now $[\mathbf{e},\mathbf{n}] = 0$ implies that

$$
\left(^ {(4)} \nabla_ {\mathbf {n}} \mathbf {e} _ {\mathbf {i}} = ^ {(4)} \nabla_ {\mathbf {i}} \mathbf {n} = - K _ {\mathbf {i}} ^ {\mathbf {j}} \mathbf {e} _ {\mathbf {j}} \right.
$$

so finally

$$
\mathrm {R} _ {\text {i n k n}} = \varepsilon \mathrm {R} _ {\text {i n k}} ^ {\mathrm {n}} = \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {k i}, \mathrm {n}} + \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {j k}}.
$$

Solution 9.33. The Riemann components were found in Problem 9.32. Contraction gives

$$
\begin{array}{l} { } ^ { ( 4 ) } \mathrm { R } _ { j } ^ { i } = g ^ { i k } ( { } ^ { ( 4 ) } \mathrm { R } _ { k n j } ^ { n } + { } ^ { ( 4 ) } \mathrm { R } _ { k m j } ^ { m } ) \\ = ^ {(3)} \mathrm {R} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} + \varepsilon \left(\mathrm {g} ^ {\mathrm {i k}} \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {k j}, \mathrm {n}} + 2 \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {m}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {m}} - \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {K}\right) \tag {1} \\ \end{array}
$$

where $\mathbf{K} \equiv \mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{i}}^{\mathbf{i}}$ . Now $\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{\mu},\mathbf{n}}^{\mathbf{ik}} = -\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{\mu},\mathbf{n}}^{\mathbf{im}}\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{\mu},\mathbf{s}}^{\mathbf{ks}}\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{\mu},\mathbf{n}}$ (see e.g. Problem 21.1) and $-\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{ms},\mathbf{n}} = 2\mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{ms}}$ in Gaussian normal coordinates, so

$$
\mathrm {g} _ {, \mathrm {n}} ^ {\mathrm {i k}} = 2 \mathrm {g} ^ {\mathrm {i m}} \mathrm {g} ^ {\mathrm {k s}} \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {m s}} = 2 \mathrm {K} ^ {\mathrm {i k}}.
$$

Equation (1) can now be rewritten:

$$
\begin{array}{l} \left(^ {(4)} \mathrm {R} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} = \right. ^ {(3)} \mathrm {R} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} + \varepsilon \left(\mathrm {g} ^ {\mathrm {i k}} \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {k j}, \mathrm {n}} + \mathrm {g} ^ {\mathrm {i k}} _ {\mathrm {, n}} \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {k j}} - \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {K}\right) \\ = ^ {(3)} \mathrm {R} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} + \varepsilon \left(\mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {j}, \mathrm {n}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} - \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {K}\right). \tag {2} \\ \end{array}
$$

For the other components of the Ricci tensor we have:

$$
\left(^ {(4)} \mathrm {R} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {n}} = \varepsilon^ {(4)} \mathrm {R} _ {\mathrm {n a j}} ^ {\alpha}\right) = - ^ {(4)} \mathrm {R} _ {\mathrm {i j}} ^ {\mathrm {n i}} = \varepsilon \left(\mathrm {K} _ {\left| \mathrm {j} \right.} - \mathrm {K} _ {\left. \mathrm {j} \right| \mathrm {i}} ^ {\mathrm {i}}\right) \tag {3}
$$

$$
\left(^ {(4)} \mathrm {R} _ {\mathrm {n}} ^ {\mathrm {n}} = \varepsilon^ {(4)} \mathrm {R} _ {\mathrm {n i n}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} = \varepsilon \mathrm {g} ^ {\mathrm {i j}} \left(\mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {i j}, \mathrm {n}} + \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {i m}} \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {m}}\right) = \varepsilon \left(\mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {, n}} - \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {i m}} \mathrm {K} ^ {\mathrm {m i}}\right). \right. \tag {4}
$$

The Ricci scalar is then

$$
\left(^ {(4)} \mathbf {R} = ^ {(4)} \mathbf {R} _ {\mathrm {n}} ^ {\mathrm {n}} + ^ {(4)} \mathbf {R} _ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} = ^ {(3)} \mathbf {R} + \varepsilon \left(2 \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {, n}} - \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {i m}} \mathrm {K} ^ {\mathrm {i m}} - \mathrm {K} ^ {2}\right) \right. \tag {5}
$$

and the components of the Einstein tensor are:

$$
G _ {n} ^ {n} = ^ {(4)} R _ {n} ^ {n} - \frac {1}{2} ^ {(4)} R = - \frac {1}{2} ^ {(3)} R + \frac {1}{2} \varepsilon \left(K ^ {2} - K _ {\mathrm {i m}} K ^ {\mathrm {i m}}\right) \tag {6}
$$

$$
\mathrm {G} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {n}} = \mathrm {(4)} \mathrm {R} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {n}} = \varepsilon \left(\mathrm {K} _ {\left| \mathrm {j} - \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {j} \mid \mathrm {i}} ^ {\mathrm {i}}\right)}\right) \tag {7}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {G} _ {j} ^ {i} = \mathbf {\delta} ^ {(4)} \mathbf {R} _ {j} ^ {i} - \frac {1}{2} \delta_ {j} ^ {i} \mathbf {\delta} ^ {(4)} \mathbf {R} \\ = ^ {(3)} \mathrm {G} _ {j} ^ {i} + \varepsilon \left[ \mathrm {K} _ {j, n} ^ {i} - \mathrm {K} _ {j} ^ {i} K - \frac {1}{2} \delta_ {j} ^ {i} \left(2 \mathrm {K} _ {, n} - \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {i m}} \mathrm {K} ^ {\mathrm {i m}} - \mathrm {K} ^ {2}\right) \right]. \tag {8} \\ \end{array}
$$

Equating (6) and (7) to the corresponding components of the stress-energy tensor gives "initial value equations" for the gravitational field.

Solution 9.34.

(i) Since there is no favored direction in the spherical surface the extrinsic curvature tensor must be $\mathbf{K}_{ij} \propto \delta_{ij}$ in an orthonormal basis; this implies that any vector is an eigenvector. From the definition of $\mathbf{K}$ as the rate of change of $\mathbf{n}$ , the eigenvalue must be $-1/\text{sphere's radius} = -1/a$ .

We can give a mathematical development of these intuitive results by going to the usual spherical coordinates $\mathbf{r}$ , $\theta$ , $\phi$ . These are clearly Gaussian normal coordinates and

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = \mathbf {\Phi} ^ {(2)} \mathrm {g} _ {\mathrm {i k}} \mathrm {d x} ^ {\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {d x} ^ {\mathrm {k}} = \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \left(\mathrm {d} \theta^ {2} + \sin^ {2} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2}\right).
$$

Now $\mathbf{K}_{ij} = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{g}_{ij,n} = -\frac{1}{2}\mathbf{g}_{ij,r}$ so

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {K} _ {\widehat {\theta} \widehat {\theta}} = \frac {1}{\mathrm {g} _ {\theta \theta}} \mathrm {K} _ {\theta \theta} = \frac {1}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}} \left(- \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {r} ^ {2}\right), \mathrm {r} = - 1 / \mathrm {a} \\ \mathrm {K} _ {\hat {\phi} \hat {\phi}} = \frac {1}{\mathrm {g} _ {\phi \phi}} \mathrm {K} _ {\phi \phi} = \frac {1}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2} \sin^ {2} \theta} \left(- \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \sin^ {2} \theta\right), \mathrm {r} = - 1 / \mathrm {a}. \tag {1} \\ \end{array}
$$

(ii) Again we can argue intuitively: The “special” directions must be the axial and circumferential directions. From the definition of $\mathbf{K}$ the values of the curvature (rate of change of $\mathbf{n}$ ) in these directions should be 0 and $-1/a$ .

Mathematically: Again the usual coordinates are Gaussian normal and the 2-geometry is

$$
\mathrm {d} \mathbf {s} ^ {2} = \mathbf {r} ^ {2} \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2} + \mathrm {d} z ^ {2}.
$$

The orthonormal components of $\mathbf{K}$ are then

$$
\mathrm {K} _ {\hat {\phi} \hat {\phi}} = \frac {1}{\mathrm {g} _ {\phi \phi}} \left(- \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {g} _ {\phi \phi , \mathrm {r}}\right) = - 1 / \mathrm {a}
$$

$$
\mathrm {K} _ {\widehat {z} \widehat {z}} = \frac {1}{\mathrm {g} _ {z z}} \left(- \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {g} _ {z z, \mathrm {r}}\right) = 0 \tag {2}
$$

and our intuitive answers are correct.

(iii) In cartesian coordinates

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = \mathrm {d x} ^ {2} + \mathrm {d y} ^ {2} + \mathrm {d z} ^ {2} \tag {3}
$$

the surface is

$$
\mathrm {f} (\mathbf {x}, \mathbf {y}, \mathbf {z}) = 0 \tag {4}
$$

where

$$
f = - \frac {1}{2} \left(a x ^ {2} + 2 b x y + c y ^ {2}\right) + z. \tag {5}
$$

The unit normal is

$$
\mathbf {n} = \nabla f / | \nabla f | = \frac {1}{N} [ - (\mathbf {a x} + \mathbf {b y}) \mathbf {e} _ {\mathbf {x}} - (\mathbf {b x} + \mathbf {c y}) \mathbf {e} _ {\mathbf {y}} + (\mathbf {e} _ {\mathbf {z}}) ] \tag {6}
$$

where

$$
\mathbf {N} \equiv \left[ (\mathbf {a x} + \mathbf {b y}) ^ {2} + (\mathbf {b x} + \mathbf {c y}) ^ {2} + 1 \right] ^ {\frac {1}{2}}. \tag {7}
$$

Note that since $\mathbf{n} = \mathbf{e}_{\mathbf{z}}$ at the origin, $\mathbf{e}_{\mathbf{x}}$ and $\mathbf{e}_{\mathbf{y}}$ can be used as basis vectors in the surface there. Thus

$$
\mathbf {K} _ {i j} = - \mathbf {e} _ {j} \cdot \nabla_ {i} \mathbf {n} = - n _ {j; i} = - n _ {j, i}
$$

since the connection coefficients vanish for the metric (3). Because $\mathbf{N}_{,\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{N}_{,\mathbf{y}} = 0$ at the origin, we have

$$
K _ {i j} = \left[ \begin{array}{c c} a & b \\ b & c \end{array} \right].
$$

The secular equation is

$$
0 = \det \left(\mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {i j}} - \mathrm {K g} _ {\mathrm {i j}}\right) = \left| \begin{array}{c c} \mathrm {a} - \mathrm {K} & \mathrm {b} \\ \mathrm {b} & \mathrm {c} - \mathrm {K} \end{array} \right| \tag {8}
$$

where $\mathbf{g}_{ij}$ is found by restricting (3) to the surface (5). Since $dz = 0$ at the origin, $\mathbf{g}_{ij} = \delta_{ij}$ at the origin. The principal curvatures are found from Equation (8) to be

$$
\mathrm {K} _ {\pm} = \frac {c + a}{2} \pm \frac {c - a}{2} \left[ 1 + \frac {4 b ^ {2}}{(c - a) ^ {2}} \right] ^ {\frac {1}{2}}. \tag {9}
$$

With these eigenvalues, the principal vectors are found from

$$
\left[ \begin{array}{c c} a - K _ {\pm} & b \\ b & c - K _ {\pm} \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{l} V _ {\pm} ^ {x} \\ V _ {\pm} ^ {y} \end{array} \right] = 0. \tag {10}
$$

The solutions are:

$$
\begin{array}{l} V _ {\pm} = \left(e _ {x} + a _ {\pm} e _ {y}\right) / \beta_ {\pm} \\ a _ {\pm} \equiv \gamma \left[ 1 \pm (1 + \gamma^ {- 2}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \right] \\ \beta_ {\pm} \equiv (1 + \alpha_ {\pm} ^ {2}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \\ \gamma \equiv (a - c) / 2 b. \\ \end{array}
$$

Solution 9.35. From the Gauss-Codazzi equation (Problem 9.32), we have

$$
{ } ^ { ( 3 ) } \mathrm { R } _ { i j k } ^ { m } = { } ^ { ( 2 ) } \mathrm { R } _ { i j k } ^ { m } + \varepsilon \left( \mathrm { K } _ { i j } \mathrm { K } _ { k } ^ { m } - \mathrm { K } _ { i k } \mathrm { K } _ { j } ^ { m } \right) .
$$

Contract on $m$ and $j$ and on $i$ and $k$ , and set $(3)R_{ijk}^{m} = 0$ because the 3-space is flat. Thus find

$$
\left(^ {(2)} \mathrm {R} = - \varepsilon \left(\mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} - \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {j}}\right) . \right. \tag {1}
$$

At any point $\mathbf{P}$ on $\Sigma$ , choose the principal directions as axes and let the coordinates $\mathbf{x}^i$ measure lengths away from $\mathbf{P}$ . Then $(2)_{\mathbf{g}_{ij}} = \delta_{ij}$ at $\mathbf{P}$ and $\mathbf{K}_{ij}$ is diagonal, with the principal curvatures as the diagonal elements. Hence

$$
\left(^ {2}\right) \mathbf {R} = - \varepsilon \left[ \frac {1}{\rho_ {1} ^ {2}} + \frac {1}{\rho_ {2} ^ {2}} - \left(\frac {1}{\rho_ {1}} + \frac {1}{\rho_ {2}}\right) ^ {2} \right] = \frac {2}{\rho_ {1} \rho_ {2}},
$$

where we have set $\varepsilon = \mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{n} = +1$ .

For $\Sigma$ 3-dimensional in a flat 4-space, Equation (1) gives

$$
\begin{array}{l} \left(^ {3}\right) \mathbf {R} = - \varepsilon \left[ \frac {1}{\rho_ {1} ^ {2}} + \frac {1}{\rho_ {2} ^ {2}} + \frac {1}{\rho_ {3} ^ {2}} - \left(\frac {1}{\rho_ {1}} + \frac {1}{\rho_ {2}} + \frac {1}{\rho_ {3}}\right) ^ {2} \right] \\ = \varepsilon \left(\frac {2}{\rho_ {1} \rho_ {2}} + \frac {2}{\rho_ {2} \rho_ {3}} + \frac {2}{\rho_ {1} \rho_ {3}}\right). \\ \end{array}
$$

# CHAPTER 10: SOLUTIONS

Solution 10.1. The metric is $\mathrm{ds}^2 = \mathrm{d}\theta^2 +\sin^2\theta \mathrm{d}\phi^2$ ; the connection coefficients are $\Gamma_{\theta \phi}^{\phi} = \Gamma_{\phi \theta}^{\phi} = \cot \theta ;\Gamma_{\phi \phi}^{\theta} = -\sin \theta \cos \theta ;$ all others are zero. Now:

$$
\xi_ {\alpha ; \beta} + \xi_ {\beta ; \alpha} = \xi_ {\alpha , \beta} + \xi_ {\beta , \alpha} - 2 \Gamma_ {\alpha \beta} ^ {\mu} \xi_ {\mu} = 0.
$$

$$
\alpha = \beta = \phi : \xi_ {\phi , \phi} = - \xi_ {\theta} \sin \theta \cos \theta \tag {1}
$$

$$
\alpha = \beta = \theta : \xi_ {\theta , \theta} = 0; \quad \therefore \xi_ {\theta} = \mathrm {f} (\phi) \tag {2}
$$

$$
\alpha = \theta , \beta = \phi : \xi_ {\theta , \phi} + \xi_ {\phi , \theta} = 2 \cot \theta \xi_ {\phi}. \tag {3}
$$

Substitution of Equation (2) in Equation (1) gives $\xi_{\phi, \phi} = -\mathrm{f}(\phi) \sin \theta \cos \theta$ which implies

$$
\xi_ {\phi} = - \mathbf {F} (\phi) \sin \theta \cos \theta + \mathbf {g} (\theta) \tag {4}
$$

where $\mathbf{F}(\phi) \equiv \int \mathrm{d}\mathbf{f}\mathrm{d}\phi$ . Next substitute Equations (4) and (2) in Equation (3):

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {f}}{\mathrm {d} \phi} + \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {g}}{\mathrm {d} \theta} - \mathbf {F} (\cos^ {2} \theta - \sin^ {2} \theta) = 2 \cot \theta \mathbf {g} - 2 \cos^ {2} \theta \mathbf {F}
$$

or

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {f}}{\mathrm {d} \phi} + \mathbf {F} (\phi) = - \left(\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {g}}{\mathrm {d} \theta} - 2 \cot \theta \mathbf {g} (\theta)\right).
$$

Since the left side is a function only of $\phi$ and the right side only of $\theta$ , each side must be a constant:

$$
\mathrm {d f} / \mathrm {d} \phi + \int \mathrm {f} \mathrm {d} \phi = \mathrm {b}, \tag {5}
$$

$$
\mathrm {d} \mathbf {g} / \mathrm {d} \theta - 2 \cot \theta \mathbf {g} = - \mathbf {b}. \tag {6}
$$

Equation (6) has an integrating factor $\exp [-2\int \cot \theta \mathrm{d}\theta ] = \sin^{-2}\theta$ , giving

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} \theta} \left(\frac {\mathrm {g}}{\sin^ {2} \theta}\right) = \frac {- \mathrm {b}}{\sin^ {2} \theta}
$$

$$
\mathbf {g} (\theta) = (\mathbf {b} \cot \theta + \mathbf {c}) \sin^ {2} \theta . \tag {7}
$$

Equation (5) is solved by differentiation:

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathbf {f}}{\mathrm {d} \phi^ {2}} + \mathbf {f} = 0,
$$

$$
\mathbf {f} = \mathrm {d} \cos \phi + \mathbf {e} \sin \phi
$$

$$
\mathbf {F} = \mathrm {d} \sin \phi - \mathrm {e} \cos \phi .
$$

(The integration constant is included in $\mathbf{g}(\theta)$ .) Substituting this result in Equation (5)

$$
- d \sin \phi + e \cos \phi + d \sin \phi - e \cos \phi = b
$$

shows that $\mathsf{b} = 0$ , and hence from Equations (2) and (7) that

$$
\xi_ {\theta} = \mathrm {d} \cos \phi + \mathrm {e} \sin \phi = \xi^ {\theta}
$$

$$
\xi_ {\phi} = c \sin^ {2} \theta - \sin \theta \cos \theta (d \sin \phi - e \cos \phi) = \sin^ {2} \theta \xi^ {\phi}.
$$

Thus

$$
\boldsymbol {\xi} = (\mathrm {d} \cos \phi + \mathrm {e} \sin \phi) \frac {\partial}{\partial \theta} + [ \mathrm {c} - \cot \theta (\mathrm {d} \sin \phi - \mathrm {e} \cos \phi) ] \frac {\partial}{\partial \phi}
$$

is the most general Killing vector. Note that it is a linear combination of the three Killing vectors

$$
\frac {\partial}{\partial \phi}
$$

$$
- \left(\cos \phi \frac {\partial}{\partial \theta} - \cot \theta \sin \phi \frac {\partial}{\partial \phi}\right)
$$

$$
\sin \phi \frac {\partial}{\partial \theta} + \cot \theta \cos \phi \frac {\partial}{\partial \phi}
$$

which are just the usual angular momentum operators $\mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{z}}$ , $\mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{x}}$ and $\mathbf{L}_{\mathbf{y}}$ , the generators of the rotation group.

Solution 10.2. It is straightforward to prove this statement in component notation. Alternately, “dot” $\mathfrak{L}_{\pmb{\xi}}$ with two arbitrary vector fields A and B:

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = \mathbf {A} \cdot \mathcal {L} _ {\xi} \mathbf {g} \cdot \mathbf {B} = \mathcal {L} _ {\xi} (\mathbf {A} \cdot \mathbf {B}) - \mathbf {B} \cdot \mathcal {L} _ {\xi} \mathbf {A} - \mathbf {A}, \mathcal {L} _ {\xi} \mathbf {B} \\ = \nabla_ {\xi} (\mathbf {A} \cdot \mathbf {B}) - \mathbf {B} \cdot \left(\nabla_ {\xi} \mathbf {A} - \nabla_ {\mathbf {A}} \xi\right) - \mathbf {A} \cdot \left(\nabla_ {\xi} \mathbf {B} - \nabla_ {\mathbf {B}} \xi\right) \\ = \mathbf {B} \cdot \nabla_ {\mathbf {A}} \boldsymbol {\xi} + \mathbf {A} \cdot \nabla_ {\mathbf {B}} \boldsymbol {\xi} = 2 \mathbf {A} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {B} ^ {\beta} \xi_ {(a; \beta)}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Since $\mathbf{A}$ and $\mathbf{B}$ are arbitrary, $\xi_{(a;\beta)}$ must vanish. To interpret this result geometrically recall that the Lie derivative of any geometrical quantity, $\phi_{\mathbf{A}}$ , is the functional change of that quantity, $\delta \phi_{\mathbf{A}} \equiv \phi_{\mathbf{A}}^{\mathrm{new}}(\mathbf{x}^{a}) - \phi_{\mathbf{A}}^{\mathrm{old}}(\mathbf{x}^{a})$ under a displacement of coordinates by $\xi$ . This is equivalent to the change in $\phi_{\mathbf{A}}$ upon moving it in the manifold along $\xi$ . For the Lie derivative of $\mathbf{g}$ along $\xi$ to vanish, therefore, requires that the geometry to be unchanged as one moves in the $\xi$ direction, i.e. $\xi$ represents a direction of symmetry of spacetime.

# Solution 10.3.

(a) We use the equivalence proven in Problem 10.2. If $\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{v}$ are Killing vectors then $\mathcal{L}_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{g} = \mathcal{L}_{\mathbf{v}}\mathbf{g} = 0$ and, with the result of Problem 8.16,

$$
\mathcal {L} _ {[ \mathbf {u}, \mathbf {v} ]} \mathbf {g} = (\mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {u}} \mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {v}} - \mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {v}} \mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {u}}) \mathbf {g} = 0.
$$

Thus $[\mathbf{u},\mathbf{v}]$ is a Killing vector.

(b) If a and b are constants, then

$$
(\mathsf {a u} _ {\alpha} + \mathsf {b v} _ {\alpha}); \beta = \mathsf {a u} _ {\alpha}; \beta + \mathsf {b v} _ {\alpha}; \beta .
$$

Thus if $\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{v}$ satisfy Killing's equation, so does $\mathbf{a}\mathbf{u} + \mathbf{b}\mathbf{v}$ .

Solution 10.4. The three Killing vectors describing rotations are just the angular momentum operators, $\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{z}} = \mathbf{x}\partial_{\mathbf{y}} - \mathbf{y}\partial_{\mathbf{x}}$ , etc. (See Problem 10.1.) At a point $(\mathbf{x}_0,\mathbf{y}_0,\mathbf{z}_0)$

$$
\begin{array}{l} J _ {z} = (- y _ {0}, x _ {0}, 0) \\ \mathbf {J _ {y}} = (z _ {0}, 0, - \mathbf {x} _ {0}) \\ \mathrm {J} _ {\mathbf {x}} = \left(0, - z _ {0}, y _ {0}\right), \\ \end{array}
$$

so $\mathbf{J_y} = -(z_0 / y_0)\mathbf{J_z} - (x_0 / y_0)\mathbf{J_x}$ and the three vectors only span a 2-dimensional surface at the point.

Suppose, however, that for some constants $a$ and $b$

$$
a J _ {x} + b J _ {y} + J _ {z} = 0
$$

were true at every point, then we would have

$$
a \left[ J _ {x}, J _ {y} \right] + b \left[ J _ {y}, J _ {y} \right] + \left[ J _ {z}, J _ {y} \right] = 0.
$$

This requires $\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{z}} \propto \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{x}}$ , a result which is obviously false. Thus, over the whole sphere, $\mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{x}}, \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{y}}, \mathbf{J}_{\mathbf{z}}$ span a 3-dimensional space. The explanation is that 2-spheres are 2-dimensional, but orientations of 2-spheres are 3-dimensional (e.g. the 3 Euler angles).

Solution 10.5. The commutator must be a Killing field according to Problem 10.3. By hypothesis any Killing vector must be a sum (with constant coefficients) of $\xi_{(t)}$ and $\xi_{(\phi)}$ :

$$
\left[ \boldsymbol {\xi} _ {(\phi)}, \boldsymbol {\xi} _ {(t)} \right] = a \boldsymbol {\xi} _ {(\phi)} + b \boldsymbol {\xi} _ {(t)}.
$$

At infinity $\xi_{(\phi)}\rightarrow \partial /\partial \phi$ and $\pmb{\xi}_{(t)}\rightarrow \partial /\partial t$ so the commutator $\to 0$ . This means that a and b must be zero, i.e. that the commutator vanishes everywhere. (B. Carter, [Commun. Math. Phys. 17, 233 (1970)] has proven that $[\pmb {\xi}_{(\phi)},\pmb {\xi}_{(t)}] = 0$ under very general conditions even when there are other Killing vectors.)

Solution 10.6. For the commutation of second derivatives of any vector

$$
\xi_ {\mu ; \nu \lambda} - \xi_ {\mu ; \lambda \nu} = \mathsf {R} _ {\mu \sigma \lambda \nu} \xi^ {\sigma}.
$$

Now we use the Killing equation $\xi_{\mu ;\nu} = -\xi_{\nu ;\mu}$ and contract on $\mu$ and $\lambda$

$$
\xi^ {\nu ; \lambda} _ {; \lambda} + R _ {\sigma} ^ {\nu} \xi^ {\sigma} = - (\xi_ {; \mu} ^ {\mu}) ^ {; \nu}.
$$

The term on the right vanishes since $\xi_{; \mu}^{\mu} = 0$ by the Killing equation, and we are left with the desired result. The variational principle is easy to write down if we note that $\xi_{\mu; \nu}$ is antisymmetric, just like the electromagnetic field tensor $\mathbf{F}_{\mu \nu}$ . We know that the Lagrangian $\frac{1}{4} \mathbf{F}_{\mu \nu} \mathbf{F}^{\mu \nu}$ gives a term $\mathbf{F}_{; \nu}^{\mu \nu}$ when we write $\mathbf{F}_{\mu \nu} = \mathbf{A}_{\nu; \mu} - \mathbf{A}_{\mu; \nu} = \mathbf{A}_{\nu, \mu} - \mathbf{A}_{\mu, \nu}$ and vary $\mathbf{A}_{\mu}$ . Thus the Lagrangian density can be taken to be

$$
\mathcal {L} = \xi_ {\mu ; \nu} \xi^ {\mu ; \nu} - \frac {1}{2} R _ {\mu \nu} \xi^ {\mu} \xi^ {\nu}
$$

and $\delta \int \mathcal{L}|\mathbf{g}|^{\frac{1}{2}}\mathrm{d}^{4}\mathbf{x} = 0$ will give the correct equation. This can be verified by finding the Euler-Lagrange equations for this Lagrangian.

Solution 10.7. For any vector $\xi$

$$
\xi_ {\sigma ; \rho \mu} - \xi_ {\sigma ; \mu \rho} = \mathrm {R} _ {\sigma \rho \mu} ^ {\lambda} \xi_ {\lambda} \tag {1}
$$

by the definition of $\mathbf{R}_{\alpha \beta \gamma} \delta$ . Add to Equation (1) its two permutations and use the cyclic identity for the Riemann tensor

$$
\mathbf {R} _ {\sigma \rho \mu} ^ {\lambda} + \mathbf {R} _ {\mu \sigma \rho} ^ {\lambda} + \mathbf {R} _ {\rho \mu \sigma} ^ {\lambda} = 0
$$

to obtain the identity

$$
0 = \xi_ {\sigma ; \rho \mu} - \xi_ {\sigma ; \mu \rho} + \xi_ {\mu ; \sigma \rho} - \xi_ {\mu ; \rho \sigma} + \xi_ {\rho ; \mu \sigma} - \xi_ {\rho ; \sigma \mu}. (2)
$$

For a Killing vector, Equation (2) becomes

$$
0 = \xi_ {\sigma ; \rho \mu} - \xi_ {\sigma ; \mu \rho} - \xi_ {\mu ; \rho \sigma}, (3)
$$

and substituting Equation (3) into Equation (1), one obtains

$$
\xi_ {\mu ; \rho \sigma} = \mathrm {R} _ {\sigma \rho \mu} ^ {\lambda} \xi_ {\lambda}. \tag {4}
$$

Solution 10.8. We first show that "stationarity" is equivalent to the existence of a time coordinate for which $\mathbf{g}_{\alpha \beta, t} = 0$ . Choose the time coordinate such that $\xi = \partial/\partial t$ , then (see Problems 10.2, 8.13) $\mathcal{L}_{\xi} g = 0$ , or

$$
\mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \beta , \gamma} \xi^ {\gamma} + \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \gamma} \xi_ {, \beta} ^ {\gamma} + \mathbf {g} _ {\beta \gamma} \xi_ {, \alpha} ^ {\gamma} = 0 .
$$

Since $\xi^{\gamma} = (1,0,0,0)$ this proves that $\mathbf{g}_{\alpha \beta ,t} = 0$

Time reversibility (definition (i)) means $\mathbf{g}_{\alpha \beta}$ is independent of $t$ and $\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{ti}} = 0$ . To show equivalence of the definitions it remains to show that $\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{ti}} = 0$ is equivalent to the hypersurface orthogonality of $\xi = \partial/\partial t$ .

If $\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{ti}} = 0$ , then $\xi_{\alpha} = \mathbf{g}_{\alpha \beta}\xi^{\beta} = \mathbf{g}_{\alpha t} = 0$ unless $\alpha = t$ . Hence $\xi_{\alpha}$ is proportional to $t, a$ i.e. $\xi$ is orthogonal to the surfaces $t = \text{constant}$ . If $\xi$ is hypersurface orthogonal (see Problem 7.23),

$$
\xi_ {[ \alpha ; \beta} \xi_ {\gamma ]} = 0.
$$

With Killing's equation $\xi_{(\alpha ;\beta)} = 0$ , this becomes

$$
\xi_ {a; \beta} \xi_ {\gamma} + \xi_ {\gamma ; a} \xi_ {\beta} + \xi_ {\beta ; \gamma} \xi_ {a} = 0.
$$

Dotting with $\xi^a$ and using Killing's equation on the first and third terms gives

$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {1}{2} \left(\xi_ {\alpha ; \beta} - \xi_ {\beta ; \alpha}\right) \xi^ {2} + \frac {1}{2} \xi^ {2} _ {, \alpha} \xi_ {\beta} - \frac {1}{2} \xi^ {2}, _ {\beta} \xi_ {\alpha} = 0 \\ \xi^ {2} \equiv \boldsymbol {\xi} \cdot \boldsymbol {\xi} \\ \end{array}
$$

that is

$$
(\xi^ {- 2} \xi_ {\beta}) _ {; \alpha} - (\xi^ {- 2} \xi_ {\alpha}) _ {; \beta} = 0 .
$$

Thus $\xi^{-2}\xi_{a}$ is a gradient and

$$
\xi_ {a} = \xi^ {2} \mathrm {h} _ {, a}
$$

for some h. Since $\xi^2 = \mathbf{g}_{tt}$ and $\xi_{\alpha} = \mathbf{g}_{\alpha t}$ , we have $\mathbf{g}_{\alpha t} = \mathbf{g}_{tt} \mathbf{h}_{,a}$ .

Putting $\alpha = t$ we see that $h_{,t} = 1$ or $h = t + f(x^i)$ . By choosing a new time coordinate $t' = t + f(x^i)$ , we have

$$
\mathbf {g} _ {\mathrm {i t} ^ {\prime}} = \mathbf {g} _ {\mathrm {t t}} h _ {, i} = \mathbf {g} _ {\mathrm {t t}} t _ {, i} ^ {\prime} = 0 ,
$$

also

$$
\xi^ {a ^ {\prime}} = \frac {\partial_ {\mathbf {x}} a ^ {\prime}}{\partial_ {\mathbf {x}} \beta} \xi^ {\beta} = \frac {\partial_ {\mathbf {x}} a ^ {\prime}}{\partial t} = (1, 0, 0, 0)
$$

so $\mathbf{g}_{\alpha \beta}$ is still independent of t'.

Solution 10.9. Choose a coordinate system in which the metric is $\eta_{\mu \nu}$ (all of the Christoffel symbols vanish). Then Killing's equation for a Killing vector $\pmb{\xi}$ becomes

$$
2 \xi_ {(\mu ; \nu)} = \xi_ {\mu , \nu} + \xi_ {\nu , \mu} = 0. \tag {1}
$$

We now enumerate and classify linearly independent solutions of Equation (1):

(i) Translation Killing vectors:

$$
\xi_ {i} ^ {\mu} = a _ {i} ^ {\mu} \quad i = 1 - 4 \tag {2}
$$

where $\mathbf{a}_{\mathrm{i}}$ are constant vectors.

(ii) Rotational Killing vectors:

$$
\xi_ {\mathrm {k}} ^ {0} = 0, \quad \xi_ {\mathrm {k}} ^ {1} = \varepsilon^ {1 \mathrm {k m}} \mathrm {x} _ {\mathrm {m}} \quad \mathrm {k} = 1 - 3. \tag {3}
$$

Check:

$$
\begin{array}{l} \xi_ {\mathbf {k} (\mu , \nu)} = \xi_ {\mathbf {k} (\mathrm {i}, \mathrm {j})} = \mathrm {x} _ {\mathrm {m}, (\mathrm {j}} \varepsilon_ {\mathbf {i}) \mathrm {k m}} \\ = \delta_ {\mathrm {m} (\mathrm {j})} \varepsilon_ {\mathrm {i}) \mathrm {k m}} = - \varepsilon_ {\mathrm {k} (\mathrm {i j})} = 0. \\ \end{array}
$$

(iii) Boost Killing vectors:

$$
\xi_ {\mu} ^ {k} = \delta_ {\mu} ^ {[ 0} x ^ {k ]} k = 1, 3. \tag {4}
$$

Check:

$$
\xi_ {(\mu , \nu)} ^ {\mathrm {k}} = \frac {1}{2} \left[ \delta_ {\mu} ^ {[ 0} \delta_ {\nu} ^ {\mathrm {k} ]} + \delta_ {\nu} ^ {[ 0} \delta_ {\mu} ^ {\mathrm {k} ]} \right] = 0.
$$

There are ten Killing vectors in Equations (2)-(4), given in the coordinate system in which $\mathbf{g}_{\mu \nu} = (-1, 1, 1, 1)$ . In any other coordinate system one can find the components of the $\xi_{i}^{\mu}$ , for $i = 1 - 10$ , by transforming them as vectors.

Solution 10.10. The change in $\mathbf{u} \cdot \pmb{\xi}$ along the geodesic is given by

$$
\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} (\mathbf {u} \cdot \boldsymbol {\xi}) = (\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {u}) \cdot \boldsymbol {\xi} + \mathbf {u} \cdot (\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \boldsymbol {\xi}).
$$
