Now $\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{u} = 0$ since $\mathbf{u}$ is tangent to a geodesic, and $\mathbf{u} \cdot \nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\pmb{\xi} = 0$ since $\pmb{\xi}$ is a Killing vector. In components,

$$
\mathbf {u} \cdot \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \boldsymbol {\xi} = \mathbf {u} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {u} ^ {\beta} \xi_ {\alpha ; \beta} = \mathbf {u} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {u} ^ {\beta} \xi_ {(\alpha ; \beta)} = 0.
$$

Thus $\mathbf{u} \cdot \pmb{\xi} = \text{constant}$ along a geodesic.

Solution 10.11. The divergence of $\mathbf{J}$ is

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {J} _ {; \mu} ^ {\mu} = (\mathrm {T} ^ {\mu \nu} \xi_ {\nu}) _ {; \mu} \\ = \mathrm {T} _ {; \mu} ^ {\mu \nu} \xi_ {\nu} + \mathrm {T} ^ {\mu \nu} \xi_ {\nu ; \mu} \\ = 0 + \mathrm {T} ^ {\mu \nu} \xi_ {(\nu ; \mu)} = 0. \\ \end{array}
$$

When $\pmb{\xi} = \partial/\partial t$ , then $\mathbf{J}^{\mu} = \mathbf{T}_{0}^{\mu} = -\mathbf{T}^{\mu 0}$ at infinity where $\mathbf{g}_{00} = -\mathbf{1}$ . Thus $\mathbf{J}$ is the negative of the energy flux vector for a stationary observer.

Solution 10.12. From Problem 10.11, the integrand $\mathbf{J}^{\alpha} \equiv \mathbf{T}^{\alpha}_{\beta} \xi^{\beta}$ is a conserved quantity: $\mathbf{J}^{\alpha}_{;a} = 0$ . The result then follows from the identity $\mathbf{J}^{\alpha}_{;a} = (|\mathbf{g}|^{\frac{1}{2}} \mathbf{J}^{\alpha})_{,a} |\mathbf{g}|^{-\frac{1}{2}}$ and from Gauss's theorem:

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = \int J _ {; a} ^ {a} | g | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} d ^ {4} x = \int (| g | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} J ^ {a}) _ {, a} d ^ {4} x = \oint J ^ {a} d ^ {3} \Sigma_ {a} \\ = \int_ {\mathrm {F} _ {2}} \mathrm {J} ^ {a} \mathrm {d} ^ {3} \Sigma_ {a} - \int_ {\mathrm {F} _ {1}} \mathrm {J} ^ {a} \mathrm {d} ^ {3} \Sigma_ {a}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Here we have assumed that $\mathbf{J} \rightarrow 0$ sufficiently rapidly at infinity that the contributions to the integral from the edges can be neglected. Thus the integral is independent of the spacelike hypersurface $\mathbf{F}$ .

Solution 10.13. From Problem 10.11, we know that for each Killing vector $\pmb{\xi}$ , $\mathbf{J}^{\mu} \equiv \mathbf{T}^{\mu \nu} \pmb{\xi}_{\nu}$ is a conserved vector, i.e.

$$
\mathrm {J} _ {; \mu} ^ {\mu} = | \mathrm {g} | ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \left(| \mathrm {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {J} ^ {\alpha}\right) _ {, \alpha} = 0. \tag {1}
$$

From Problem 10.9, we know that in flat spacetime there are ten linearly independent Killing vectors $\xi_{\mathbf{(i)}}^{\mu}$ , $i = 1 - 10$ . Thus there are ten conserved vectors

$$
\mathbf {J} _ {(\mathbf {i})} ^ {\mu} \equiv \mathbf {T} ^ {\mu \nu} \xi_ {(\mathbf {i}) \nu}. \tag {2}
$$

From Equations (1) and (2), one may construct ten globally conserved

quantities

$$
Q _ {(i)} \equiv \int | g | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} J _ {(i)} ^ {0} d ^ {3} x, \quad i = 1 - 1 0 \tag {3}
$$

since

$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {\mathrm {d} Q _ {(i)}}{\mathrm {d} t} = \int \left(| g | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} J _ {(i)} ^ {0}\right), _ {0} \mathrm {d} ^ {3} x = - \int \left(| g | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} J _ {(i)} ^ {k}\right), _ {k} \mathrm {d} ^ {3} x \\ = - \int | g | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} J _ {(i)} ^ {k} d ^ {2} \Sigma_ {k}. \\ \end{array}
$$

This last expression represents the flux of the $\mathbf{J}_{(\mathbf{i})}$ current through the (2-dimensional) surface bounding the integration volume. If $\mathbf{T}$ falls off fast enough at large distances, and the integration volume is infinite, this flux term vanishes and $\frac{\mathrm{d}\mathbf{Q}_{(\mathbf{i})}}{\mathrm{d}t} = 0$ . [Alternative proof: apply Solution 10.12.]

The four Q's derived from translation Killing vectors correspond to conserved energy and momentum; the three derived from rotation Killing vectors correspond to conserved angular momenta. To see the meaning of the three derived from boost Killing vectors, write (e.g. in flat cartesian coordinates with $\xi_{\nu} = [x, -t, 0, 0]$ )

$$
\begin{array}{l} Q = \int T ^ {0 \nu} \xi_ {\nu} d x d y d z \\ = \int x T ^ {0 0} d x d y d z - t \int T ^ {0 x} d x d y d z \\ = \mathrm {x} _ {\left(\mathrm {C}. \mathrm {M}. \right.)} \mathrm {M} _ {\left(\mathrm {C}. \mathrm {M}. \right.)} - t \mathrm {P} _ {\mathrm {x} _ {\left(\mathrm {C}. \mathrm {M}. \right.)}} = \mathrm {M} _ {\left(\mathrm {C}. \mathrm {M}. \right.)} \left(\mathrm {x} _ {\left(\mathrm {C}. \mathrm {M}. \right.)} - \mathrm {v} _ {\left(\mathrm {C}. \mathrm {M}. \right.)} t\right) \\ \end{array}
$$

which is a component of the conserved “origin of uniform motion” for the center of mass (C.M.) of the system. $(\mathbf{x}_{(\mathbf{C},\mathbf{M},\cdot)}$ is defined to be $\int \mathbf{x} \, \mathrm{T}^{00} \, \mathrm{d}\mathbf{x} \, \mathrm{d}\mathbf{y} \, \mathrm{d}\mathbf{z} / \int \mathbf{T}^{00} \, \mathrm{d}\mathbf{x} \, \mathrm{d}\mathbf{y} \, \mathrm{d}\mathbf{z}.$

Solution 10.14. The components of $\mathbf{u}$ are

$$
\mathsf {u} _ {\alpha} = \xi_ {\alpha} / (- \xi_ {\gamma} \xi^ {\gamma}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}
$$

(the minus sign is chosen to make the term in parentheses positive), so

$$
\mathsf {a} _ {\alpha} = \mathsf {u} _ {\alpha ; \beta} \mathsf {u} ^ {\beta} = \left[ \frac {\xi_ {\alpha ; \beta}}{(- \xi_ {\gamma} \xi^ {\gamma}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} + \frac {\xi_ {\alpha} \xi_ {\gamma ; \beta} \xi^ {\gamma}}{(- \xi_ {\tau} \xi^ {\tau}) ^ {\frac {3}{2}}} \right] \frac {\xi^ {\beta}}{(- \xi_ {\mu} \xi^ {\mu}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} .
$$

The second term vanishes because $\xi_{\gamma ;\beta}\xi^{\gamma}\xi^{\beta} = \xi_{(\gamma ;\beta)}\xi^{\gamma}\xi^{\beta} = 0$ . Since $\xi_{a;\beta} = -\xi_{\beta;a}$ , the first term gives

$$
\mathsf {a} _ {\alpha} = \frac {\xi_ {\beta ; \alpha} \xi^ {\beta}}{+ \xi_ {\gamma} \xi^ {\gamma}} = \frac {1}{2} \frac {(\xi_ {\beta} \xi^ {\beta}) _ {; \alpha}}{(\xi_ {\gamma} \xi^ {\gamma})} = \frac {1}{2} \left[ \log (- \xi_ {\beta} \xi^ {\beta}) \right] _ {, \alpha}.
$$

Solution 10.15. Consider a particle of rest mass $\mu$ and conserved energy $\mathbf{E} = -\mathbf{p} \cdot \boldsymbol{\xi}$ where $\mathbf{p}$ is the 4-momentum and $\boldsymbol{\xi}$ is the time Killing vector. Not all values of $\mathbf{E} / \mu$ are possible for trajectories through a given point in spacetime. For example, particles at radial infinity must have $\mathbf{E} / \mu \geq 1$ . To find the bound on $\mathbf{E} / \mu$ for a general point, pick an orthonormal frame at the point. The 4-velocity of a particle has components $\mathbf{u} = (\gamma, \gamma \mathbf{v})$

with $\underline{\mathbf{v}}$ a 3-vector and $\gamma = (1 - \mathbf{v}^2)^{-\frac{1}{2}}$ ; the time Killing vector has components $\xi = (\xi_0, \xi)$ , with $\underline{\xi}$ a 3-vector. The particle's ratio of energy to rest mass is given by

$$
\mathbf {E} / \mu = - \mathbf {u} \cdot \boldsymbol {\xi} _ {(t)} = \gamma (\xi_ {0} - \underline {{v}} \cdot \underline {{\xi}}) \tag {1}
$$

where the dot denotes the scalar product in the local Euclidean 3-space. Evidently, a necessary (but not a sufficient) condition for an extremum (hence a bound) on $\mathbf{E} / \mu$ is

$$
\underline {{\mathbf {v}}} \cdot \underline {{\xi}} = \pm \mathbf {v} \xi . \tag {2}
$$

Here $\mathbf{v} \equiv |\underline{\mathbf{v}}|$ , $\xi \equiv |\underline{\xi}|$ . Now we distinguish two cases: If $\xi$ is spacelike (e.g. in the ergosphere of a Kerr blackhole), then we have $\xi_0 < \xi$ ; and inspection of Equation (1) shows that all values of $\mathrm{E} / \mu$ are possible, that is $-\infty < \mathrm{E} / \mu < +\infty$ . This is the answer for regions in which $\xi$ is spacelike. The infinite limits correspond to $\mathbf{v} \rightarrow \mathbf{1}$ with the two signs of Equation (2). If, instead, $\xi$ is timelike (e.g. at radial infinity), so that $\xi_0 > \xi$ , then the right-hand side of Equation (1) is always positive, and there is a nontrivial lower bound on $\mathrm{E} / \mu$ . Writing Equation (1) and using Equation (2) with the upper sign, we obtain

$$
(\xi^ {2} + \mathrm {E} ^ {2} / \mu^ {2}) \mathrm {v} ^ {2} - 2 \xi \xi_ {0} \mathrm {v} + (\xi_ {0} ^ {2} - \mathrm {E} ^ {2} / \mu^ {2}) = 0.
$$

The extremum in $\mathbf{E} / \mu$ is obtained by setting the discriminant of this equation, a quadratic in $\mathbf{v}$ , equal to zero; this gives

$$
0 = (\mathrm {E} / \mu) ^ {2} \left[ (\mathrm {E} / \mu) ^ {2} - \xi^ {2} + \xi_ {0} ^ {2} \right].
$$

The root $\mathbf{E} / \mu = 0$ is spurious, and the lower bound on $\mathbf{E} / \mu$ is

$$
(\mathrm {E} / \mu) ^ {2} = \dot {\xi} _ {0} ^ {2} - \dot {\xi} ^ {2} = - \boldsymbol {\xi} \cdot \boldsymbol {\xi}.
$$

We see that the allowed range of $\mathbf{E} / \mu$ at a point depends only on the norm of the time Killing vector at that point:

$$
(- \boldsymbol {\xi} \cdot \boldsymbol {\xi}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \leq \mathbf {E} / \mu <   + \infty
$$

for $\pmb{\xi}$ timelike.

Solution 10.16. For a test electromagnetic field, all we have to check is that Maxwell's equations are satisfied in the given background metric. If $\mathbf{A}^{\mu}$ , the vector potential, is a Killing vector, then

$$
\mathrm {A} _ {; \mu} ^ {\mu} = 0
$$

i.e. the Lorentz condition is satisfied, and the wave equation

$$
\mathrm {A} _ {\nu} ^ {\mu ; \nu} - \mathrm {R} _ {\nu} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {A} ^ {\nu} = 0
$$

is satisfied by Problem 10.6 and the fact that $\mathbf{R}_{\nu}^{\mu} = 0$ in vacuum.

If in Minkowski space in spherical polar coordinates $\mathbf{A} \propto \partial/\partial \phi$ , i.e. only $\mathbf{A}^{\phi} = \mathbf{constant}$ is nonzero, then $\mathbf{A}^{\hat{\phi}} \propto \mathbf{r} \sin \theta$ , or in conventional 3-dimensional notation,

$$
\underline {{\mathbf {A}}} = \text {a r} \sin \theta \underline {{\mathbf {e}}} _ {\hat {\phi}} \quad (\mathbf {a} = \text {c o n s t a n t}) .
$$

Hence $\underline{\mathbf{E}} = 0$ and $\underline{\mathbf{B}} = \nabla \times \underline{\mathbf{A}} = 2\mathbf{a}(\cos \theta \underline{\mathbf{e}_{\hat{\mathbf{r}}}} - \sin \theta \mathbf{e}_{\hat{\theta}}) = 2\mathbf{a}\underline{\mathbf{e}_{\hat{\mathbf{z}}}}$ . Thus there is a uniform magnetic field parallel to the $z$ axis. (This problem is due to Robert M. Wald.)

# CHAPTER 11: SOLUTIONS

Solution 11.1.

$$
\begin{array}{l} d J / d \tau = \frac {d (\Delta x)}{d \tau} \otimes p + \Delta x \otimes \frac {d p}{d \tau} - \frac {d p}{d \tau} \otimes \Delta x - p \otimes \frac {d (\Delta x)}{d \tau} \tag {i} \\ = \mathbf {u} \otimes \mathbf {p} - \mathbf {p} \otimes \mathbf {u} \\ = \frac {1}{\mathrm {m}} \mathbf {p} \otimes \mathbf {p} - \mathbf {p} \otimes \frac {1}{\mathrm {m}} \mathbf {p} = 0. \\ \end{array}
$$

(ii) Consider times immediately before and immediately after the collision. Since all particles are at the same point at the collision,

$$
\left. \Delta \mathbf {x} _ {(\mathbf {k})} \right| _ {\text {b e f o r e}} = \left. \Delta \mathbf {x} _ {(\mathbf {k})} \right| _ {\text {a f t e r}} = \Delta \mathbf {x},
$$

(where $\pmb{\Delta}\mathbf{x}$ is the displacement to the point) and thus

$$
\begin{array}{l} \sum_ {(k)} \left. J _ {(k)} \right| _ {a f t e r} = \Delta x \otimes \sum_ {(k)} \left. p _ {(k)} \right| _ {a f t e r} - \left(\sum_ {(k)} \left. p _ {(k)} \right| _ {a f t e r}\right) \otimes \Delta x \\ = \Delta \mathbf {x} \otimes \sum_ {(\mathbf {k})} \left. \right.\left. \mathbf {p} _ {(\mathbf {k})} \right| _ {\text {b e f o r e}} - \left(\sum_ {(\mathbf {k})} \left. \mathbf {p} _ {(\mathbf {k})} \right| _ {\text {b e f o r e}}\right) \otimes \Delta \mathbf {x} \\ = \sum_ {(k)} J _ {(k)} | _ {b e f o r e}, \\ \end{array}
$$

since total momentum $\sum_{\mathbf{(k)}}\mathbf{p}_{\mathbf{(k)}}$ is conserved.

Solution 11.2.

(a) For the '‘angular momentum density’' $\mathbf{J}^{\alpha \beta \gamma}$ defined by

$$
\mathbf {J} ^ {\alpha \beta \gamma} = _ {\bullet} 2 \mathbf {x} ^ {[ \alpha} \mathbf {T} ^ {\beta ] \gamma} = \mathbf {x} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {T} ^ {\beta \gamma} - \mathbf {x} ^ {\beta} \mathbf {T} ^ {\alpha \gamma}
$$

we have

$$
\mathbf {J} ^ {\alpha \beta \gamma} _ {, \gamma} = \delta_ {\gamma} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {T} ^ {\beta \gamma} + \mathbf {x} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {T} ^ {\beta \gamma} _ {, \gamma} - \delta_ {\gamma} ^ {\beta} \mathbf {T} ^ {\alpha \gamma} - \mathbf {x} ^ {\beta} \mathbf {T} ^ {\alpha \gamma} _ {, \gamma} = \mathbf {T} ^ {\beta a} - \mathbf {T} ^ {\alpha \beta} = 0 .
$$

Thus $\mathbf{J}^{\alpha \beta} \equiv \int \mathbf{J}^{\alpha \beta \gamma} \mathrm{d}^3 \Sigma_{\gamma}$ is a conserved quantity, by Gauss' theorem.

$$
\mathrm {(b)} \qquad \mathbf {J} ^ {\alpha \beta} (\mathbf {x} ^ {\sigma} + \mathbf {a} ^ {\sigma}) = \int \left(\mathbf {a} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {T} ^ {\beta \gamma} - \mathbf {a} ^ {\beta} \mathbf {T} ^ {\alpha \gamma}\right) \mathbf {d} ^ {3} \boldsymbol {\Sigma} _ {\gamma} + \mathbf {J} ^ {\alpha \beta} (\mathbf {x} ^ {\sigma}).
$$

Thus $\mathbf{J}^{\alpha \beta}$ is not invariant under coordinate translations (different angular momentum about different points).

(c) Consider the time derivative of the spin:

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d S} _ {\alpha}}{\mathrm {d t}} = - \frac {1}{2} \varepsilon_ {\alpha} \beta \gamma \delta \left(\frac {\mathrm {d J} ^ {\beta \gamma}}{\mathrm {d t}} \mathrm {u} ^ {\delta} + \mathrm {J} ^ {\beta \gamma} \frac {\mathrm {d u} ^ {\delta}}{\mathrm {d t}}\right).
$$

The first term on the right vanishes from part (a). The second term vanishes because the system has no forces acting on it $(\mathrm{du}^{\delta} / \mathrm{dt} = 0)$ . Thus $\mathrm{dS}^{\alpha} / \mathrm{dt} = 0$ .

(d) From (b),

$$
\mathsf {J} ^ {\alpha \beta} (\mathbf {x} ^ {\sigma} + \mathbf {a} ^ {\sigma}) = \mathbf {a} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {P} ^ {\beta} - \mathbf {a} ^ {\beta} \mathbf {P} ^ {\alpha} + \mathsf {J} ^ {\alpha \beta} (\mathbf {x} ^ {\sigma}).
$$

and thus

$$
\mathsf {S} _ {\alpha} (\mathbf {x} ^ {\sigma} + \mathsf {a} ^ {\sigma}) = - \frac {1}{2} \varepsilon_ {\alpha \beta \gamma \delta} (\mathsf {a} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {P} ^ {\beta} - \mathsf {a} ^ {\beta} \mathbf {P} ^ {\alpha}) \mathbf {P} ^ {\delta} / | \mathbf {P} | + \mathsf {S} _ {\alpha} (\mathbf {x} ^ {\sigma}) .
$$

The first term on the right vanishes ( $\varepsilon$ is totally antisymmetric and $\mathbf{P}^{\mu}$ occurs quadratically) so $\mathbf{S}_a(\mathbf{x}^\sigma +\mathbf{a}^\sigma) = \mathbf{S}_a(\mathbf{x}^\sigma)$ .

Solution 11.3. From the definition of $S_{a}$ ,

$$
\mathsf {u} ^ {\alpha} \mathsf {S} _ {\alpha} = - \frac {1}{2} \varepsilon_ {\alpha \beta \gamma \delta} \mathsf {J} ^ {\beta \gamma} \mathsf {u} ^ {\delta} \mathsf {u} ^ {\alpha} = 0
$$

by antisymmetry of $\varepsilon$ and symmetry of $\mathfrak{u}^{\delta}\mathfrak{u}^{a}$

Solution 11.4. Consider an observer in the local inertial frame comoving with the center of mass of the gyroscope. Because there are no torques,

he sees no precession of the spin axis, so $\underline{\mathrm{dS}}/\mathrm{dt} = 0$ . But in this frame the 4-velocity of the gyroscope is $\mathbf{u} = (1,0)$ , so we can write the condition of zero torque as

$$
\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {S} = \mathbf {g} \mathbf {u}
$$

where $\mathbf{g}$ is some constant of proportionality. We find $\mathbf{g}$ from the fact that $\mathbf{S} \cdot \mathbf{u} = 0$ :

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} (\mathbf {S} \cdot \mathbf {u}) = (\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {S}) \cdot \mathbf {u} + \mathbf {S} \cdot (\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {u}) \\ = \mathbf {g} \mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {u} + \mathbf {S} \cdot \mathbf {a} \\ = - \mathbf {g} + \mathbf {S} \cdot \mathbf {a}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Thus

$$
\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {S} = (\mathbf {S} \cdot \mathbf {a}) \mathbf {u}
$$

which is the equation for Fermi-Walker transport

$$
\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {S} = (\mathbf {S} \cdot \mathbf {a}) \mathbf {u} - (\mathbf {S} \cdot \mathbf {u}) \mathbf {a}
$$

with $\mathbf{S} \cdot \mathbf{u} = 0$ .

Solution 11.5.

(a) About the center of mass in the center-of-momentum frame: $\int x^i T^{00}d^3 x = 0$ (center of mass) and $\int T^{i0}d^3 x = 0$ (center of momentum).

Thus we have

$$
J ^ {i 0} = \int d ^ {3} x (x ^ {i} T ^ {0 0} - t T ^ {i 0}) = 0,
$$

or, in frame independent notation $\mathbf{J}^{\alpha \beta}\mathbf{u}_{\beta} = 0$

(b) From the definition of $S_{\delta}$ :

$$
- \varepsilon^ {\alpha \beta \gamma \delta} \mathbf {S} _ {\gamma} \mathbf {u} _ {\delta} = \frac {1}{2} \varepsilon^ {\alpha \beta \gamma \delta} \varepsilon_ {\gamma \mu \nu \sigma} \mathbf {J} ^ {\mu \nu} \mathbf {u} ^ {\sigma} \mathbf {u} _ {\delta}.
$$

Now perform the summation over $\gamma$ [see Problems 3.27, 3.28]:

$$
- \frac {1}{2} \delta_ {\mu \nu \sigma} ^ {\alpha \beta \delta} \mathbf {J} ^ {\mu \nu} \mathbf {u} ^ {\sigma} \mathbf {u} _ {\delta} = - \mathbf {J} ^ {\alpha \beta} \mathbf {u} ^ {\delta} \mathbf {u} _ {\delta} = \mathbf {J} ^ {\alpha \beta} \equiv \mathsf {S} ^ {\alpha \beta}.
$$

[Only the $\mathbf{J}^{\alpha \beta}$ terms are kept in the sum; by part (a) we cannot have a $\delta$ index on $\mathbf{J}$ due to the presence of $\mathfrak{u}_{\delta}$ .]

Solution 11.6. First we calculate the total angular momentum. We work in the center-of-momentum frame and take as our origin 0 the event at which the center of mass of A and that of B would collide. About this origin both systems A and B have only intrinsic angular momentum (see Problem 11.5), so

$$
\mathbf {J} _ {\mathbf {A} + \mathbf {B}} ^ {\alpha \beta} (\mathbf {0}) = \mathbf {S} _ {\mathbf {A}} ^ {\alpha \beta} + \mathbf {S} _ {\mathbf {B}} ^ {\alpha \beta} = - \varepsilon^ {\alpha \beta \mu \nu} (\mathbf {S} _ {\mathbf {A} ^ {\mu} \mathbf {A} ^ {\nu}} + \mathbf {S} _ {\mathbf {B} ^ {\mu} \mathbf {B} ^ {\nu}})
$$

and by conservation of angular momentum this is equal to $\int_{\mathbf{C}}^{\alpha \beta} (\mathbf{0}) \, \mathrm{d}\mathbf{C}$ . We can now use this result in the definition of the spin vector to find

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {\Delta} _ {\mathbf {C} ^ {\sigma}} = - \frac {1}{2} \varepsilon_ {\sigma a \beta \lambda} \mathbf {\Delta} _ {\mathbf {C}} ^ {a \beta} \mathbf {\Delta} _ {\mathbf {C}} ^ {\mathbf {u} ^ {\lambda}} \\ = \frac {1}{2} \varepsilon_ {\sigma \alpha \beta \lambda} \varepsilon^ {\alpha \beta \mu \nu} _ {\mathbf {A} ^ {\mu} \mathbf {A} ^ {\nu}} (\mathbf {S} _ {\mu} \mathbf {u} _ {\nu} + \mathbf {S} _ {\mu} \mathbf {\Gamma} _ {\mathbf {B}} \mathbf {u} _ {\nu}) \mathbf {\Gamma} _ {\mathbf {C}} ^ {\lambda}. \\ \end{array}
$$

According to Problem 3.28 (see also Problem 3.27)

$$
\varepsilon_ {\sigma \alpha \beta \lambda} \varepsilon^ {\alpha \beta \mu \nu} = - 2 \delta_ {\sigma \lambda} ^ {\mu \nu}
$$

so that

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathop{\rm S}\limits_{\mathbf{C}}\sigma = -\delta^{\mu \nu}_{\sigma \lambda}(\mathop{\rm S}\limits_{\mathbf{A}}{}^{\mu}\mathop{\rm u}\limits_{\mathbf{A}} + \mathop{\rm S}\limits_{\mathbf{B}}{}^{\mu}\mathop{\rm u}\limits_{\mathbf{B}})^{\mathsf{u}^{\lambda}}_{\mathbf{C}} \\ = - \mathrm {S} _ {\sigma} (\mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {u}) - \mathrm {S} _ {\sigma} (\mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {u}) + \mathrm {u} _ {\sigma} (\mathbf {S} \cdot \mathbf {u}) + \mathrm {u} _ {\sigma} (\mathbf {S} \cdot \mathbf {u})   . \\ \end{array}
$$

We can now substitute $\frac{(\mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P})}{|\mathbf{P} + \mathbf{P}|}$ for $\mathbf{u}$ to complete the solution.

Solution 11.7. The basic equation (see Problem 11.4) is

$$
\mathrm {d} \mathbf {S} / \mathrm {d} \tau = \mathbf {u} (\mathbf {a} \cdot \mathbf {S}).
$$

In the lab frame, the world line of the particle is

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {x} = \mathbf {r} \cos \omega t \\ \mathbf {y} = \mathbf {r} \sin \omega t \\ \end{array}
$$

(where $\omega$ and $\mathbf{r}$ are constants) and hence

$$
u ^ {0} = \gamma , \quad u ^ {x} = \gamma \frac {d x}{d t} = - \omega r \gamma \sin \omega t, \quad u ^ {y} = \gamma \omega r \cos \omega t, \quad u ^ {z} = 0
$$

where $\gamma \equiv (1 - r^2\omega^2)^{-\frac{1}{2}}$ . Now $\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{du} / \mathbf{dt}$ so

$$
\mathsf {a} ^ {0} = 0 (\gamma = \mathsf {c o n s t}), \quad \mathsf {a} ^ {\mathbf {x}} = \gamma \frac {\mathsf {d u} ^ {\mathbf {x}}}{\mathsf {d t}}, \quad \mathsf {a} ^ {\mathbf {y}} = - \gamma^ {2} \omega^ {2} \mathbf {r} \sin \omega \mathsf {t}, \quad \mathsf {a} ^ {\mathbf {z}} = 0
$$

and hence

$$
\mathbf {S} \cdot \mathbf {a} = \mathbf {S} ^ {\mathbf {x}} \mathbf {a} ^ {\mathbf {x}} + \mathbf {S} ^ {\mathbf {y}} \mathbf {a} ^ {\mathbf {y}} = - \omega^ {2} \mathbf {r} \gamma^ {2} \cos \omega t \mathbf {S} ^ {\mathbf {x}} - \omega^ {2} \mathbf {r} \gamma^ {2} \sin \omega t \mathbf {S} ^ {\mathbf {y}}.
$$

We have therefore

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {S} ^ {0}}{\mathrm {d} \tau} = \gamma \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {S} ^ {0}}{\mathrm {d} t} = \mathbf {u} ^ {0} (\mathbf {a} \cdot \mathbf {S}) = \gamma (\mathbf {a} \cdot \mathbf {S}) \tag {1}
$$

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} S ^ {\mathbf {x}}}{\mathrm {d} r} = \gamma \frac {\mathrm {d} S ^ {\mathbf {x}}}{\mathrm {d} t} = u ^ {\mathbf {x}} (\mathbf {a} \cdot \mathbf {S}) = - \omega r \gamma \sin \omega t (\mathbf {a} \cdot \mathbf {S}) \tag {2}
$$

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {S} ^ {\mathbf {y}}}{\mathrm {d} \tau} = \gamma \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {S} ^ {\mathbf {y}}}{\mathrm {d} t} = \mathbf {u} ^ {\mathbf {y}} (\mathbf {a} \cdot \mathbf {S}) = \omega r \gamma \cos \omega t (\mathbf {a} \cdot \mathbf {S}) \tag {3}
$$

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} S ^ {z}}{\mathrm {d} r} = 0. \tag {4}
$$

Introduce radial and tangential components:

$$
S ^ {x} = S ^ {r} \cos \omega t - S ^ {\theta} \sin \omega t
$$

$$
\mathbf {S} ^ {\mathbf {y}} = \mathbf {S} ^ {\mathbf {r}} \sin \omega t + \mathbf {S} ^ {\theta} \cos \omega t.
$$

Equations (2) and (3) imply:

$$
\mathrm {d} \mathbf {S} ^ {\mathbf {r}} / \mathrm {d} t = \omega \mathbf {S} ^ {\theta} \tag {5}
$$

$$
\mathrm {d} \mathbf {S} ^ {\theta} / \mathrm {d t} = - \omega \gamma^ {2} \mathbf {S} ^ {\mathbf {r}}. \tag {6}
$$

Equations (5) and (6) imply:

$$
\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathbf {S} ^ {\mathrm {r}} / \mathrm {d t} ^ {2} = - \omega^ {2} \gamma^ {2} \mathbf {S} ^ {\mathrm {r}} \Longrightarrow \mathbf {S} ^ {\mathrm {r}} = \mathrm {A} \cos (\omega \gamma t + \alpha), \mathrm {A}, \alpha \text {c o n s t .} \tag {7}
$$

and from Equation (5)

$$
\mathrm {S} ^ {\theta} = - \gamma \mathrm {A} \sin (\omega \gamma t + \alpha). \tag {8}
$$

From Equations (4), (7), (8) we obtain

$$
S ^ {x} = A \left[ \cos \omega t \cos (\omega y t + a) + \gamma \sin \omega t \sin (\omega y t + a) \right] \tag {9a}
$$

$$
\mathbf {S} ^ {\mathcal {Y}} = \mathbf {A} [ \sin \omega t \cos (\omega \gamma t + a) - \gamma \cos \omega t \sin (\omega \gamma t + a) ] \tag {9b}
$$

$$
S ^ {z} = \text {c o n s t .} \tag {9c}
$$

Consider initial conditions $S^{\mathbf{x}} = \hbar (2)^{-\frac{1}{2}}, S^{\mathbf{y}} = 0, S^{\mathbf{z}} = \frac{1}{2} \hbar$ . (Although we are not treating the electron spin quantum mechanically, we want $S^2 = 3\hbar^2 / 4$ .) These conditions imply $\alpha = 0$ , $A = \hbar (2)^{-\frac{1}{2}}$ ; and Equations (9) can be written as

$$
S ^ {x} + i S ^ {y} = 2 ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \hbar [ e ^ {- i (\gamma - 1) \omega t} + i (1 - \gamma) \sin (\omega \gamma t) e ^ {i \omega t} ]. \tag {10}
$$

The first term on the right hand side of Equation (10) and Equation (9c) indicate a precession around the $z$ -axis with angular velocity

$$
\omega_ {\text {T h o m a s}} = (\gamma - 1) \omega \approx \frac {1}{2} v ^ {2} \omega \tag {11}
$$

while the second term in Equation (10) is small $(1 - \gamma \approx -\frac{1}{2} v^2)$ for an electron in an atom.

Solution 11.8. Do the computation in the local comoving Lorentz frame of the center of mass of the body. Treating the center of mass as the reference point in the equation of geodesic deviation, the relative acceleration of a mass element at position $\mathbf{x}^{\hat{\mathbf{j}}}$ is

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathbf {x} ^ {\hat {\mathrm {j}}}}{\mathrm {d t} ^ {2}} = - R ^ {\hat {\mathrm {j}}} _ {\hat {\mathrm {0 k}}} \hat {\mathrm {0}} ^ {\mathbf {x} ^ {\hat {\mathrm {k}}}}. \tag {1}
$$

The $i^{\text{th}}$ component of the torque per unit volume is then $-\varepsilon_{\hat{i}1\hat{j}}\mathbf{x}^{\hat{l}}\rho \mathbf{R}^{\hat{j}}\hat{0}\hat{k}\hat{0}^{\hat{x}^{\hat{k}}}$ , where $\rho$ is the mass density at $\mathbf{x}^{\hat{j}}$ . The total torque, which is just the time derivative of the intrinsic angular momentum, is

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {S} _ {\hat {\mathbf {i}}}}{\mathrm {d} t} = - \varepsilon_ {\hat {\mathbf {i}} \hat {\mathbf {1}} \hat {\mathbf {j}}} \mathbf {R} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {j}}} \hat {\mathbf {0}} \hat {\mathbf {k}} \hat {\mathbf {0}} \int \rho \mathbf {x} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {i}}} \mathbf {x} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {k}}} \mathrm {d} ^ {3} \mathbf {x} \tag {2}
$$

if we approximate $\mathbb{R}_{\hat{0}\hat{k}\hat{0}}^{\hat{j}}$ as constant over the body. Because of the symmetry properties of $\varepsilon_{\hat{i}\hat{j}}\hat{R}_{\hat{0}\hat{k}\hat{0}}^{\hat{j}}$ , Equation (2) is equivalent to

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} S _ {\widehat {\mathbf {j}}}}{\mathrm {d} t} = - \varepsilon_ {\widehat {\mathbf {i}} \widehat {\mathbf {j}}} t _ {\widehat {\mathbf {1 k}}} R _ {\widehat {\mathbf {j}} \widehat {\mathbf {0}} \widehat {\mathbf {k}} \widehat {\mathbf {0}}}, \tag {3}
$$

where

$$
t _ {\widehat {1} \widehat {k}} \equiv \int \rho (x ^ {\widehat {1}} x ^ {\widehat {k}} - \frac {1}{3} r ^ {2} \delta^ {\widehat {1} \widehat {k}}) d ^ {3} x.
$$

If one now defines the quadrupole tensor, $t^{\alpha \beta}$ to be such that $t^{\alpha \beta} u_{\beta} = 0$ i.e. to have only spatial components in a comoving local frame, then the invariant tensor expression of Equation (3) is

$$
\frac {\mathrm {D S} ^ {\kappa}}{\mathrm {d} \tau} = \varepsilon^ {\kappa \beta a \mu} \mathrm {u} _ {\mu} \mathrm {u} ^ {\sigma} \mathrm {u} ^ {\lambda} \mathrm {t} \beta \eta \mathrm {R} ^ {\eta} \sigma a \lambda . \tag {4}
$$

Note that either in the case of a spherical body, or a body small enough so that Riemann $\times$ (size of body)² $\approx 0$ , Equation (4) reduces to $\mathrm{DS}^K / \mathrm{d}\tau = 0$ . (Cf. Problem 11.4 with $a = 0$ .)

Solution 11.9. We shall use two coordinate systems: the XYZ coordinates are the spatial coordinates of a local Lorentz frame with spatial origin at the center of mass of the Earth. The ecliptic plane is the XY plane, and we shall assume that the Sun and the Moon move in circular orbits in this plane as seen from the Earth. The xyz coordinates are the spatial coordinates of a similar Lorentz frame, but with the $z$ -axis parallel to the Earth's angular momentum $\underline{\mathbf{J}}$ . Choose the $x$ -axis parallel to the $X$ -axis. The relation between basis vectors for the two coordinate systems is

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathfrak {E} _ {\mathbf {x}} = \mathfrak {E} _ {\mathbf {X}} \\ \underline {{\mathbf {e}}} _ {\mathbf {Y}} = \cos \psi \underline {{\mathbf {e}}} _ {\mathbf {Y}} - \sin \psi \underline {{\mathbf {e}}} _ {\mathbf {Z}} \\ \underline {{\mathbf {e}}} _ {\mathbf {z}} = \sin \psi \underline {{\mathbf {e}}} _ {\mathbf {Y}} + \cos \psi \underline {{\mathbf {e}}} _ {\mathbf {Z}}. \tag {1} \\ \end{array}
$$

![](images/0480b8c388be7b86069d1daaff3dd4074426821c3c9f50cf2f99b7799b964b0c.jpg)

Here $\psi \approx 23\frac{1}{2}^0$ is the angle between $\underline{\mathbf{J}}$ and the $Z$ -axis, which remains constant if we ignore the small nutation. As time goes on, $\underline{\mathbf{J}}$ precesses about the $Z$ -axis with a period $\mathbf{T}$ which we wish to find.

Let $\underline{\mathbf{e}}_{\mathrm{s}}$ be a unit vector pointing to the Sun. If the sun has polar coordinates $(\mathbf{r}_{\mathrm{s}},\theta ,\phi)$ with respect to the xyz coordinates and $(\mathbf{r_s},\frac{\pi}{2},\phi_{\mathrm{s}})$ with respect to the XYZ coordinates, then

$$
\begin{array}{l} \cos \theta = \underline {{\mathbf {e}}} _ {\mathrm {z}} \cdot \underline {{\mathbf {e}}} _ {\mathrm {s}} \\ = (\sin \psi \underline {{\mathrm {e}}} _ {\mathbf {Y}} + \cos \psi \underline {{\mathrm {e}}} _ {\mathbf {Z}}) \cdot (\cos \phi_ {\mathrm {s}} \underline {{\mathrm {e}}} _ {\mathbf {X}} + \sin \phi_ {\mathrm {s}} \underline {{\mathrm {e}}} _ {\mathbf {Y}}) \\ = \sin \psi \sin \phi_ {s} \\ \end{array}
$$

and similarly,

$$
\sin \theta \sin \phi = \underline {{e}} _ {\mathbf {y}} \cdot \underline {{e}} _ {\mathbf {s}} = \cos \psi \sin \phi_ {\mathbf {s}}
$$

$$
\sin \theta \cos \phi = \underline {{\mathbf {e}}} _ {\mathbf {x}} \cdot \underline {{\mathbf {e}}} _ {\mathbf {s}} = \cos \phi_ {\mathbf {s}}.
$$

From Problem 11.8, the equation of motion of the spin vector $\underline{\mathbf{J}}$ of the Earth in an external gravitational field is

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \underline {{J}}}{\mathrm {d} t} = \underline {{N}}
$$

where the torque is

$$
\mathrm {N} _ {\mathrm {i}} = - \varepsilon_ {\mathrm {i j k}} \mathrm {t} _ {\mathrm {j m}} \mathrm {R} _ {\mathrm {k 0 m 0}}.
$$

Here the "reduced quadrupole moment tensor" is

$$
\mathbf {t} _ {\mathrm {j m}} = \int \rho \left(\mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {m}} - \frac {1}{3} \mathbf {\nabla r} ^ {2} \delta^ {\mathrm {j m}}\right) \mathbf {d} ^ {3} \mathbf {x} .
$$

Take the Earth to be spheroidal, with moment of inertia C about the rotation axis and A about an equatorial axis. Then in xyz coordinates

$$
C = \int \rho (x ^ {2} + y ^ {2}) d ^ {3} x
$$

$$
A = \int \rho (x ^ {2} + z ^ {2}) d ^ {3} x = \int \rho (y ^ {2} + z ^ {2}) d ^ {3} x.
$$

The nonzero components of $t_{\mathrm{jm}}$ are

$$
t _ {z z} = - \frac {2}{3} (C - A)
$$

$$
t _ {x x} = t _ {y y} = \frac {1}{2} (C - A)
$$

so that

$$
N _ {x} = - (C - A) R _ {z 0 y 0}
$$

$$
N _ {y} = (C - A) R _ {z 0 x 0}
$$

$$
\mathrm {N} _ {\mathrm {z}} = 0.
$$

In the weak-field, slow-motion limit,

$$
R _ {z 0 i 0} = \frac {\partial^ {2} \Phi}{\partial z \partial x ^ {i}}
$$

where $\Phi$ is the Newtonian gravitational potential:

$$
\Phi = \Phi_ {\mathrm {S U N}} + \Phi_ {\mathrm {M O O N}}.
$$

Since

$$
\Phi_ {\mathrm {S U N}} (\mathrm {x}, \mathrm {y}, z) = \frac {\mathrm {M} _ {\mathrm {s}}}{[ (\mathrm {x} - \mathrm {x} _ {\mathrm {s}}) ^ {2} + (\mathrm {y} - \mathrm {y} _ {\mathrm {s}}) ^ {2} + (\mathrm {z} - z _ {\mathrm {s}}) ^ {2} ] ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}
$$

we find

$$
\begin{array}{l} \left. \frac {\partial^ {2} \Phi}{\partial z \partial x} \right| _ {0} = - \frac {3 M _ {s} \sin \theta \cos \theta \cos \phi}{r _ {s} ^ {3}} \\ \left. \frac {\partial^ {2} \Phi}{\partial z \partial y} \right| _ {0} = - \frac {3 M _ {s} \sin \theta \cos \theta \sin \phi}{r _ {s} ^ {3}}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Thus $\underline{\mathbf{N}}_{\mathrm{SUN}}$ has $(\mathbf{x},\mathbf{y},\mathbf{z})$ components

$$
\begin{array}{l} \underset {\sim} {N} _ {\mathrm {S U N}} = 3 \mathrm {a} (\mathrm {C} - \mathrm {A}) \sin \theta \cos \theta (\sin \phi , - \cos \phi , 0) \\ = 3 a (C - A) \sin \psi \sin \phi_ {s} (\cos \psi \sin \phi_ {s}, - \cos \phi_ {s}, 0) \\ \end{array}
$$

where $a \equiv M_{s} / r_{s}^{3}$ . Since the Sun's angular velocity about the Earth is much greater than the precession angular velocity we wish to find, we can average $N_{\text{SUN}}$ over the Sun's orbit. The term $\sin^2 \phi_{\text{s}}$ gives $\frac{1}{2}$ , while $\sin \phi_{\text{s}} \cos \phi_{\text{s}}$ averages to zero. Thus the only nonzero component of

$\underline{\mathbf{N}}_{\mathrm{SUN}}$ is

$$
\mathrm {N} _ {\mathbf {x}} ^ {\text {S U N}} = \mathrm {N} _ {\mathbf {x}} ^ {\text {S U N}} = \frac {3}{2} \mathrm {a} (\mathrm {C} - \mathrm {A}) \sin \psi \cos \psi .
$$

A similar expression holds for the average torque exerted by the Moon, with a replaced by $\mathbf{b} = \mathbf{M}_{\mathbf{M}} / \mathbf{r}_{\mathbf{M}}^{3}$ . Thus, at $t = 0$ , there is a torque in the X direction of magnitude

$$
\mathbf {N} = \frac {3}{2} (a + b) (C - A) \sin \psi \cos \psi .
$$

In a small time dt therefore $\underline{\mathbf{J}}$ changes by an amount $\mathrm{d}\mathbf{J} = \mathbf{N}\mathrm{dt}$ perpendicular to itself. Thus $\underline{\mathbf{J}}$ rotates about the $Z$ axis by an amount

$$
\mathrm {d} \chi = \frac {\mathrm {d J}}{\mathrm {J} \sin \psi} = \frac {\mathrm {N d t}}{\mathrm {J} \sin \psi}.
$$

The precession period is the time for $\chi$ to change by $2\pi$ :

$$
\mathrm {T} = \frac {2 \pi \mathrm {J} \sin \psi}{\mathrm {N}}.
$$

Put $\mathbf{J} = \mathbf{C}\omega$ , where $\omega$ is the angular velocity of rotation of the Earth.

Then

$$
T = \frac {4 \pi}{3} \frac {C}{C - A} \frac {1}{\cos \psi} \frac {\omega}{a + b}.
$$

Putting

$$
C / (C - A) = 3 0 5. 3
$$

$$
\psi = 2 3. 4 5 ^ {0}
$$

$$
\omega = 7. 2 9 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5} \sec^ {- 1}
$$

$$
\mathrm {M} _ {\mathrm {s}} = 1. 9 8 9 \times 1 0 ^ {3 3} \mathrm {g}
$$

$$
r _ {s} = 1. 4 9 6 \times 1 0 ^ {1 3} c m
$$

$$
M _ {M} = 7. 3 4 9 \times 1 0 ^ {2 5} g
$$

$$
\mathrm {r} _ {\mathrm {M}} = 3. 8 4 4 \times 1 0 ^ {1 0} \mathrm {c m}
$$

we find

$$
\mathrm {T} = 2 5, 6 0 0 \text {y e a r s}.
$$

This agrees with observation to better than $1\%$ ; some error in the calculation arises because we treated the orbits as circular and because the Moon's orbit is not exactly in the ecliptic.

Solution 11.10. The observers have 4-velocity $\mathbf{u} = \pmb{\xi} / |\pmb{\xi}|$ , where $|\pmb{\xi}| = (-\pmb{\xi} \cdot \pmb{\xi})^{\frac{1}{2}}$ . Thus

$$
\mathcal {L} _ {\boldsymbol {\xi}} \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {0}} = \mathcal {L} _ {\boldsymbol {\xi}} \mathbf {u} = [ \boldsymbol {\xi}, \mathbf {u} ] = [ \nabla_ {\boldsymbol {\xi}} (- \boldsymbol {\xi} \cdot \boldsymbol {\xi}) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} ] \boldsymbol {\xi}
$$

where we have used the fact that $[\pmb {\xi},\pmb {\xi}] = 0$ .But

$$
\nabla_ {\pmb {\xi}} (\pmb {\xi} \cdot \pmb {\xi}) = (\xi^ {a} \xi_ {a}); \beta \xi^ {\beta} = 2 \xi_ {a}; \beta \xi^ {a} \xi^ {\beta} = 0,
$$

since $\xi_{(\alpha ;\beta)} = 0$ by Killing's equation. Thus $\mathcal{L}_{\pmb{\xi}}\mathbf{e}_{\hat{0}} = 0$ . Also $\mathcal{L}_{\pmb{\xi}}\mathbf{e}_{\hat{j}} = 0$

since $e_{\hat{j}}$ connects points of equal $t$ along any observer's world line. (See Problem 8.14.)

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathcal {L} _ {\boldsymbol {\xi}} \mathbf {Q} = \mathcal {L} _ {\boldsymbol {\xi}} \left(\mathrm {Q} ^ {\hat {\alpha} \dots \hat {\beta}} \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\alpha}} \otimes \dots \otimes \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\beta}}\right) \tag {2} \\ = \left(\mathcal {Q} _ {\boldsymbol {\xi}} Q ^ {\hat {\alpha} \dots \hat {\beta}}\right) \mathrm {e} _ {\hat {\alpha}} \otimes \dots \otimes \mathrm {e} _ {\hat {\beta}} + Q ^ {\hat {\alpha} \dots \hat {\beta}} \left(\mathcal {Q} _ {\boldsymbol {\xi}} \mathrm {e} _ {\hat {\alpha}}\right) \otimes \dots \otimes \mathrm {e} _ {\hat {\beta}} \\ + \dots + Q ^ {\hat {\alpha} \dots \hat {\beta}} e _ {\hat {\alpha}} \otimes \dots \otimes (\mathcal {L} _ {\boldsymbol {\xi}} e _ {\hat {\beta}}) . \\ \end{array}
$$

Only the first term on the right-hand side is nonvanishing by part (1), so

$$
\left(\mathscr {L} _ {\boldsymbol {\xi}} \mathbf {Q}\right) ^ {\hat {\alpha} \dots \hat {\beta}} = \mathscr {L} _ {\boldsymbol {\xi}} \left(\mathrm {Q} ^ {\hat {\alpha} \dots \hat {\beta}}\right) = \nabla_ {\boldsymbol {\xi}} \mathrm {Q} ^ {\hat {\alpha} \dots \hat {\beta}} = \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d t}} \mathrm {Q} ^ {\hat {\alpha} \dots \hat {\beta}}
$$

(since $\mathbf{Q}^{\hat{\alpha}\dots \hat{\beta}}$ is just a scalar function). Measured in units of proper time, the rate is

$$
\frac {d}{d \hat {t}} Q ^ {\hat {\alpha} \dots \hat {\beta}} = \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} Q ^ {\hat {\alpha} \dots \hat {\beta}} = \frac {1}{| \boldsymbol {\xi} |} \nabla_ {\boldsymbol {\xi}} Q ^ {\hat {\alpha} \dots \hat {\beta}} = \frac {1}{| \boldsymbol {\xi} |} \frac {d}{d t} Q ^ {\hat {\alpha} \dots \hat {\beta}}.
$$

(3) In the observer's local rest frame, the precession equation is

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {S} _ {\hat {\mathbf {j}}}}{\mathrm {d} \hat {\mathbf {t}}} = \varepsilon_ {\hat {\mathbf {j}} \hat {\mathbf {k}} \hat {\mathbf {l}}} \omega^ {\hat {\mathbf {k}}} \mathbf {S} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {l}}}
$$

where $\mathbf{S}$ is the gyroscope spin vector. Now $\varepsilon_{\widehat{\mathbf{j}}\widehat{\mathbf{k}}\widehat{\mathbf{l}}} = \varepsilon_{\widehat{\mathbf{0j}}\widehat{\mathbf{k}}\widehat{\mathbf{l}}} = \mathfrak{u}^{\widehat{\alpha}}\varepsilon_{\widehat{\mathbf{a}}\widehat{\mathbf{j}}\widehat{\mathbf{k}}\widehat{\mathbf{l}}}$ , so the 4-dimensional version of the precession equation is

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathsf {S} ^ {\hat {\beta}}}{\mathrm {d} \hat {\mathbf {t}}} = \mathsf {u} _ {\hat {\alpha}} \varepsilon^ {\hat {\alpha} \hat {\beta} \hat {\gamma} \hat {\delta}} \omega_ {\hat {\gamma}} \mathsf {S} _ {\hat {\delta}}
$$

that is

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} S ^ {\hat {\beta}}}{\mathrm {d} t} = \xi_ {\hat {\alpha}} \varepsilon^ {\hat {\alpha} \hat {\beta} \hat {\gamma} \hat {\delta}} \omega_ {\hat {\gamma}} S _ {\hat {\delta}}. \tag {1}
$$

We wish to derive an equation with the form of Equation (1) and read off $\omega$ . We know that S is Fermi-Walker transported (see Problem 11.4), i.e.

$$
\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {S} = (\mathbf {S} \cdot \mathbf {a}) \mathbf {u} \tag {2}
$$

and

$$
\mathbf {S} \cdot \mathbf {u} = 0. \tag {3}
$$

Equation (2) becomes

$$
\nabla_ {\boldsymbol {\xi}} \mathbf {S} = (\mathbf {S} \cdot \nabla_ {\boldsymbol {\xi}} \boldsymbol {\xi}) \boldsymbol {\xi} / | \boldsymbol {\xi} | ^ {2} \tag {4}
$$

and Equation (3) implies

$$
\mathbf {S} \cdot \boldsymbol {\xi} = 0. \tag {5}
$$

From part (2),

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {S}}{\mathrm {d} t} = \mathcal {L} _ {\boldsymbol {\xi}} \mathbf {S} = \nabla_ {\boldsymbol {\xi}} \mathbf {S} - \nabla_ {\mathbf {S}} \boldsymbol {\xi}. \tag {6}
$$

Since $\pmb{\xi}$ plays the part of a 4-velocity (unnormized), it is convenient to decompose $\nabla \pmb{\xi}$ analogously to the decomposition of $\nabla \mathbf{u}$ (see Problem 5.18). Since $\pmb{\xi}$ is a Killing vector, $\nabla \pmb{\xi}$ is antisymmetric, so we write

$$
\nabla \boldsymbol {\xi} = \boldsymbol {\omega} + \mathbf {A} \otimes \boldsymbol {\xi} - \boldsymbol {\xi} \otimes \mathbf {A} \tag {7}
$$

where $\omega$ is antisymmetric and $\boldsymbol{\omega} \cdot \boldsymbol{\xi} = 0$ . Since

$$
(\nabla \boldsymbol {\xi}) \cdot \boldsymbol {\xi} = \nabla_ {\boldsymbol {\xi}} \boldsymbol {\xi} = - \boldsymbol {\xi} \cdot (\nabla \boldsymbol {\xi})
$$

we must have

$$
\mathbf {A} = (\nabla_ {\pmb {\xi}} \pmb {\xi}) (\pmb {\xi} \cdot \pmb {\xi}) ^ {- 1}.
$$

Thus Equations (6) and (4) give

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {S}}{\mathrm {d} t} = - (\mathbf {S} \cdot \nabla_ {\boldsymbol {\xi}} \boldsymbol {\xi}) \boldsymbol {\xi} (\boldsymbol {\xi} \cdot \boldsymbol {\xi}) ^ {- 1} + \mathbf {S} \cdot \nabla \boldsymbol {\xi} = \mathbf {S} \cdot \boldsymbol {\omega}. \tag {8}
$$

(In the first equality we used $\nabla_{\mathbf{S}}\pmb {\xi} = (\nabla \pmb {\xi})\cdot \mathbf{S} = -\mathbf{S}\cdot \nabla \pmb {\xi}.$ ) We now want to express $\omega$ in terms of a vector as in Equation (1). It is convenient to work in component notation to keep track of which slots are contracted into which. Equation (7) is

$$
\xi_ {\alpha ; \beta} = \omega_ {\alpha \beta} + \mathbf {A} _ {\alpha} \xi_ {\beta} - \xi_ {\alpha} \mathbf {A} _ {\beta}
$$

and from this we can get a vector independent of $\mathbf{A}$ :

$$
\mathbf {B} ^ {\lambda} \equiv \varepsilon^ {\lambda \alpha \beta \gamma} \xi_ {\alpha ; \beta} \xi_ {\gamma} = \varepsilon^ {\lambda \alpha \beta \gamma} \omega_ {\alpha \beta} \xi_ {\gamma}. \tag {9}
$$

Express $\omega_{\alpha \beta}$ in terms of $\mathbf{B}$ :

$$
\begin{array}{l} \varepsilon_ {\lambda \rho \sigma \kappa} \mathbf {B} ^ {\lambda} = - \omega_ {\alpha \beta} \xi_ {\gamma} \delta_ {\rho \sigma \kappa} ^ {\alpha \beta \gamma} \\ \varepsilon_ {\lambda \rho \sigma \kappa} \mathbf {B} ^ {\lambda} \xi^ {\kappa} = - \omega_ {\alpha \beta} \xi_ {\gamma} \delta_ {\rho \sigma \kappa} ^ {\alpha \beta \gamma} \xi^ {\kappa} \\ \end{array}
$$

Since $\omega_{\alpha \beta} \xi^{\beta} = 0$ , the only non-vanishing terms occur when $\gamma = \kappa$ , so

$$
\varepsilon_ {\lambda \rho \sigma \kappa} \mathrm {B} ^ {\lambda} \xi^ {\kappa} = - 2 \omega_ {\rho \sigma} \xi_ {\kappa} \xi^ {\kappa}.
$$

Substitute in Equation (8),

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {S} ^ {\hat {\beta}}}{\mathrm {d} t} = \mathsf {S} _ {\hat {\alpha}} \omega^ {\hat {a} \hat {\beta}} = - \frac {1}{2} \mathsf {S} _ {\hat {\alpha}} \varepsilon^ {\hat {\gamma} \hat {a} \hat {\beta} \hat {\kappa}} \mathsf {B} _ {\hat {\gamma}} \xi_ {\hat {\kappa}} (\xi_ {\hat {\delta}} \xi^ {\hat {\delta}}) ^ {- 1}
$$

which is of the form of Equation (1) with

$$
\omega^ {\gamma} = \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {B} ^ {\gamma} (\xi_ {a} \xi^ {a}) ^ {- 1}
$$

and, by Equation (9) this is equal to $\varepsilon^{\gamma a\beta \sigma}\xi_{a;\beta}\xi_{\sigma} / 2\xi_{a}\xi^{a}$ .

(4) The vanishing of $\omega$ occurs if and only if $\xi_{[\alpha; \beta} \xi_{\sigma]} = 0$ . But this is the condition that $\xi$ be hypersurface-orthogonal (Problem 7.23) i.e. that the metric be static. (See Problem 10.8.)

Solution 11.11. The equation of transport for the gyroscope spin is

$$
\frac {\mathrm {D S} ^ {\alpha}}{\mathrm {d} \tau} = (\mathbf {S} \cdot \mathbf {a}) \mathbf {u} ^ {\alpha}, \quad \mathbf {S} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {u} _ {\alpha} = 0. \tag {1}
$$

The locally measured time derivative of the $j^{th}$ component (relative to a local Lorentz frame) of the spin vector is

$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} \tau} \mathbf {S} _ {\hat {\mathbf {j}}} = \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} \tau} (\mathbf {S} \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {j}}}) = \frac {\mathrm {D}}{\mathrm {d} \tau} (\mathbf {S} \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {j}}}) = \mathbf {S} \cdot \frac {\mathrm {D}}{\mathrm {d} \tau} \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {j}}} \\ = \mathbf {S} \cdot \Gamma_ {\hat {\mathrm {j}} \hat {0}} ^ {\hat {a}} \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {a}} = \Gamma_ {\hat {\mathrm {j}} \hat {0}} ^ {\hat {a}} \mathbf {S} _ {\hat {a}} = \Gamma_ {\hat {\mathrm {j}} \hat {0}} \mathbf {S} ^ {\hat {1}} \tag {2} \\ \end{array}
$$

where we have used Equation (1) and the definition of the Christoffel symbols. Now, using the fact that the Christoffel symbols, in an orthonormal frame, are antisymmetric in the first two indicates, Equation (2)

may be written as

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {S} ^ {\hat {\mathrm {j}}}}{\mathrm {d t}} = \varepsilon_ {\hat {\mathrm {j k l}}} \Omega_ {\hat {\mathrm {k}}} \mathbf {S} _ {\hat {\mathrm {l}}}, \tag {3a}
$$

where

$$
\Omega_ {\hat {\mathbf {k}}} \equiv \frac {1}{2} \varepsilon_ {\hat {\mathbf {k}} \hat {\mathbf {l}} \hat {\mathbf {j}}} \Gamma_ {\hat {\mathbf {l}} \hat {\mathbf {j}} \hat {\mathbf {0}}} . \tag {3b}
$$

We must now compute the Christoffel symbols, $\Gamma_{\widehat{1} \widehat{0}}$ . The approximate metric of the earth is

$$
d s ^ {2} = - (1 + 2 \phi) d t ^ {2} + (1 - 2 \phi) \delta_ {j k} d x ^ {i} d x ^ {k} - 4 h _ {j} d x ^ {j} d t. \tag {4}
$$

Here

$$
\phi = - \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r} = 0 (\epsilon^ {2})
$$

$$
\underline {{\underline {{\mathbf {h}}}}} = \frac {\underline {{\mathbf {J} \times \mathbf {r}}}}{\mathbf {r} ^ {3}} = 0 (\epsilon^ {3}) \tag {5}
$$

where $\underline{\mathbf{J}}$ is the angular momentum of the earth. We are working in the Newtonian limit, where velocities are $\mathcal{O}(\epsilon)$ , and will keep terms of order $\epsilon^3$ . A coordinate-stationary observer in the metric (4) has orthonormal basis 1-forms

$$
\tilde {\omega} ^ {\overline {{0}}} = (1 + \phi) \widetilde {\mathrm {d t}} + 2 h _ {j} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {j}, \quad \tilde {\omega} ^ {\overline {{j}}} = (1 - \phi) \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {j}. \tag {6}
$$

The dual basis vectors, found from the relation $< \tilde{\omega}^{\overline{\alpha}}$ , $e_{\overline{\beta}} > = \delta^{\overline{\alpha}_{\overline{\beta}}}$ are

$$
\mathbf {e} _ {\overline {{0}}} = (1 - \phi) \partial / \partial t, \quad \mathbf {e} _ {\overline {{j}}} = (1 + \phi) \frac {\partial}{\partial x ^ {j}} - 2 h _ {j} \partial / \partial t. \tag {7}
$$

If the gyroscope has coordinate velocity $\mathbf{v_j}$ , related to the 4-velocity by

$$
u ^ {j} = v _ {j} u ^ {0}, \quad u ^ {0} = 1 - \phi + \frac {1}{2} v ^ {2} \quad (f r o m u \cdot u = - 1), \tag {8}
$$

then in the stationary observer's frame

$$
\mathbf {v} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {-} = \frac {\mathbf {u} ^ {\overline {{\mathrm {j}}}}}{\mathbf {u} ^ {\overline {{0}}}} = <   \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\overline {{\mathrm {j}}}}, \mathbf {u} > / <   \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\overline {{0}}}, \mathbf {u} > = (1 - 2 \phi) \mathbf {v} _ {\mathrm {j}}. \tag {9}
$$

The basis vectors in the orthonormal frame comoving with the gyroscope are obtained from those of Equation (7) by a Lorentz transformation, a

boost -v:

$$
\mathbf {e} _ {\widehat {\alpha}} = \Lambda_ {\widehat {\alpha}} ^ {\overline {{\alpha}}} \mathbf {e} _ {\overline {{\alpha}}}
$$

where

$$
\begin{array}{l} \Lambda_ {\hat {0}} ^ {\overline {{0}}} = \gamma \equiv 1 + \frac {1}{2} v ^ {2}, \quad \Lambda_ {\hat {j}} ^ {\overline {{0}}} \equiv \gamma v _ {\overline {{j}}} = \Lambda_ {\hat {0}} ^ {\hat {j}}, \tag {10} \\ \Lambda_ {\widehat {\mathbf {k}}} ^ {\overline {{\mathbf {j}}}} = \delta^ {\mathbf {j k}} + (\gamma - 1) \mathbf {v} ^ {\overline {{\mathbf {j}}}} \mathbf {v} ^ {\overline {{\mathbf {k}}}} / \mathbf {v} ^ {2}. \\ \end{array}
$$

This gives

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {0}} = \left(1 - \phi + \frac {1}{2} v ^ {2}\right) \partial / \partial t + \left(1 - \phi + \frac {1}{2} v ^ {2}\right) v ^ {k} \partial / \partial x ^ {k} \tag {11} \\ \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {j}}} = \left[ \left(1 - 3 \phi + \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {\nabla v} ^ {2}\right) \mathbf {v} _ {\mathbf {j}} - 2 \mathbf {h} _ {\mathbf {j}} \right] \partial / \partial t + \left[ \delta_ {\mathbf {j k}} (1 + \phi) + \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {v} _ {\mathbf {j}} \mathbf {v} _ {\mathbf {k}} \right] \partial / \partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathbf {k}} . \\ \end{array}
$$

The Christoffel symbols in an orthonormal frame can be found from the

formula

$$
\Gamma_ {\hat {\mu} \hat {\nu} \hat {\alpha}} = \frac {1}{2} \left(\mathrm {c} _ {\hat {\mu} \hat {\nu} \hat {\alpha}} + \mathrm {c} _ {\hat {\mu} \hat {\alpha} \hat {\nu}} - \mathrm {c} _ {\hat {\nu} \hat {\alpha} \hat {\mu}}\right) \tag {12}
$$

$$
\mathbf {c} _ {\hat {\mu} \hat {\nu} \hat {a}} = [ \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mu}}, \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\nu}} ] \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {a}}.
$$

Using Equation (11) and working to $\mathcal{O}(\epsilon^3)$ , we find

$$
\begin{array}{l} [ \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {j}}}, \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {0}}} ] = \left(- \phi_ {, \mathbf {j}} + \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {v} _ {, \mathbf {j}} ^ {2} - \mathbf {v} _ {\mathbf {j}, \mathbf {t}} - \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {v} ^ {2} \mathbf {v} _ {\mathbf {j}, \mathbf {t}} - \mathbf {v} _ {\mathbf {k}} \mathbf {v} _ {\mathbf {j}, \mathbf {k}}\right) \partial / \partial t \\ + \left[ \mathbf {v} _ {\mathbf {k}, \mathbf {j}} - \phi_ {, \mathbf {j}} \mathbf {v} _ {\mathbf {k}} + \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {v} _ {, \mathbf {j}} ^ {2} \mathbf {v} _ {\mathbf {k}} + \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {v} ^ {2} \mathbf {v} _ {\mathbf {k}, \mathbf {j}} + \mathbf {v} _ {\mathbf {j}} \mathbf {v} _ {\mathbf {k}, \mathbf {t}} \right. \\ \left. + \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {v} _ {\mathrm {j}} \mathbf {v} _ {\mathrm {m}} \mathbf {v} _ {\mathrm {k , m}} - \frac {1}{2} (\mathbf {v} _ {\mathrm {j}} \mathbf {v} _ {\mathrm {k}}) _ {, \mathrm {t}} - \mathbf {v} _ {\mathrm {m}} \phi_ {, \mathrm {m}} \delta_ {\mathrm {j k}} - \mathbf {v} _ {\mathrm {m}} \frac {1}{2} (\mathbf {v} _ {\mathrm {j}} \mathbf {v} _ {\mathrm {k}}) _ {, \mathrm {m}} \right] \partial / \partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {k}}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Since $(\partial/\partial \mathbf{x}^{\alpha}) \cdot (\partial/\partial \mathbf{x}^{\beta}) = \mathbf{g}_{\alpha \beta}$ , we get

$$
\begin{array}{l} [ \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {j}}}, \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {0}}} ] \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {k}}} = \left(1 - \phi + \frac {1}{2} v ^ {2}\right) v _ {\mathbf {k}, j} + v _ {\mathbf {k}} v _ {\mathbf {j}, t} + v _ {\mathbf {j}} v _ {\mathbf {k}, t} + \frac {1}{2} v _ {\mathbf {j}} v _ {\mathbf {m}} v _ {\mathbf {k}, m} + v _ {\mathbf {m}} v _ {\mathbf {k}} v _ {\mathbf {j}, m} \\ + \frac {1}{2} v _ {m} v _ {k} v _ {m, j} - \frac {1}{2} (v _ {j} v _ {k}), _ {t} - v _ {m} \phi , \delta_ {j k} - v _ {m} \frac {1}{2} (v _ {j} v _ {k}), _ {m}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Thus

$$
\begin{array}{l} \left[ \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathrm {j}}}, \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathrm {0}}} \right] \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathrm {k}}} - \left[ \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathrm {k}}}, \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathrm {0}}} \right] \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathrm {j}}} = \left(1 - \phi + \frac {1}{2} v ^ {2}\right) \left(v _ {\mathrm {k}, \mathrm {j}} - v _ {\mathrm {j}, \mathrm {k}}\right) \tag {13} \\ + \frac {1}{2} v _ {m} \left(v _ {k} v _ {j, m} - v _ {j} v _ {k, m} + v _ {k} v _ {m, j} - v _ {j} v _ {m, k}\right) \\ \end{array}
$$

and

$$
\begin{array}{l} \left[ \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {j}}}, \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {k}}} \right] = \left[ \left(1 - 2 \phi + \frac {1}{2} v ^ {2}\right) v _ {k, j} + v _ {j} v _ {k, t} - 3 \phi , _ {j} v _ {k} + \frac {1}{2} v, _ {j} ^ {2} v _ {k} - 2 h _ {k, j} \right. \\ \left. + \frac {1}{2} v _ {j} v _ {m} v _ {k, m} \right] \partial / \partial t \\ + \left[ \phi , _ {j} \delta_ {\mathrm {k m}} + \frac {1}{2} \left(\mathrm {v} _ {\mathrm {k}} \mathrm {v} _ {\mathrm {m}}\right), _ {j} + \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {v} _ {\mathrm {j}} \left(\mathrm {v} _ {\mathrm {k}} \mathrm {v} _ {\mathrm {m}}\right), _ {\mathrm {t}} \right] \partial / \partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {m}} - \{j \leftrightarrow k \} \\ \end{array}
$$

so

$$
\begin{array}{l} \left[ \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathrm {j}}}, \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathrm {k}}} \right] \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {0}} = \left(1 - \phi + \frac {1}{2} v ^ {2}\right) \left(v _ {\mathrm {j}, \mathrm {k}} - v _ {\mathrm {k}, \mathrm {j}}\right) + 4 \left(\phi , _ {\mathrm {j}} v _ {\mathrm {k}} - \phi , _ {\mathrm {k}} v _ {\mathrm {j}}\right) + 2 \left(h _ {\mathrm {k}, \mathrm {j}} - h _ {\mathrm {j}, \mathrm {k}}\right) \\ + \mathrm {v} _ {\mathrm {k}} \mathrm {v} _ {\mathrm {j}, \mathrm {t}} - \mathrm {v} _ {\mathrm {j}} \mathrm {v} _ {\mathrm {k}, \mathrm {t}} + \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {v} _ {\mathrm {m}} \left(\mathrm {v} _ {\mathrm {j}} \mathrm {v} _ {\mathrm {m}, \mathrm {k}} - \mathrm {v} _ {\mathrm {k}} \mathrm {v} _ {\mathrm {m}, \mathrm {j}} + \mathrm {v} _ {\mathrm {k}} \mathrm {v} _ {\mathrm {j}, \mathrm {m}} - \mathrm {v} _ {\mathrm {j}} \mathrm {v} _ {\mathrm {k}, \mathrm {m}}\right). \\ \end{array}
$$

The Christoffel symbols are then

$$
\begin{array}{l} \Gamma_ {\hat {\mathbf {j}} \hat {\mathbf {k}} \hat {\mathbf {0}}} = \frac {1}{2} \left(\left[ \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {j}}}, \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {k}}} \right] \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {0}}} + \left[ \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {j}}}, \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {0}}} \right] \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {k}}} - \left[ \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {k}}}, \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {0}}} \right] \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {j}}}\right) \\ = 2 \left(\phi_ {, j} v _ {k} - \phi_ {, k} v _ {j}\right) + h _ {k, j} - h _ {j, k} + \frac {1}{2} \left(v _ {k} \frac {d v _ {j}}{d r} - v _ {j} \frac {d v _ {k}}{d r}\right) \tag {15} \\ \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {v} _ {\mathrm {j}}}{\mathrm {d} \tau} \equiv \frac {\partial \mathbf {v} _ {\mathrm {j}}}{\partial \mathrm {t}} + \mathbf {v} _ {\mathrm {m}} \frac {\partial \mathbf {v} _ {\mathrm {j}}}{\partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {m}}} = - \phi , _ {\mathrm {j}} + a _ {\mathrm {j}}. \tag {16}
$$

Here $\underline{\mathbf{a}}$ is the acceleration produced by non-inertial forces; we get $\underline{\mathbf{a}} = 0$ for geodesic motion. Thus

$$
\Gamma_ {\widehat {\mathbf {j}} \widehat {\mathbf {k}} \widehat {\mathbf {0}}} = \frac {3}{2} \left(\phi , _ {\mathrm {j}} v _ {\mathrm {k}} - \phi , _ {\mathrm {k}} v _ {\mathrm {j}}\right) + h _ {\mathrm {k}, \mathrm {j}} - h _ {\mathrm {j}, \mathrm {k}} \tag {17}
$$

and Equation (3b) can be written

$$
\underline {{\Omega}} = \underline {{\nabla}} \times \underline {{h}} + \frac {3}{2} \underline {{\nabla}} \phi \times \underline {{v}}. \tag {18}
$$

Had we not set $\underline{\mathbf{a}} = 0$ , there would have been an extra term $\frac{1}{2}\underline{\mathbf{a}}\times \underline{\mathbf{v}}$ which is the Thomas precession.

The term $\frac{3}{2} \nabla \phi \times \underline{\mathbf{v}}$ is called the “geodetic precession.” For a particle in a circular orbit of radius $r$ ,

$$
\underline {{\Omega}} _ {\text {g e o d e t i c}} = \frac {3}{2} \frac {\mathbb {M}}{\mathbf {r} ^ {2}} \left(\mathrm {v} _ {\bar {\theta}} \mathrm {e} _ {\bar {\phi}} - \mathrm {v} _ {\bar {\phi}} \mathrm {e} _ {\bar {\theta}}\right)
$$

where

$$
v _ {\overline {{\theta}}} = \left(\frac {M}{r}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \frac {\left(\sin^ {2} \theta - \sin^ {2} a\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{\sin \theta}
$$

$$
v _ {\phi} = \left(\frac {M}{r}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \frac {\sin a}{\sin \theta}
$$

are the spherical components of the particle's velocity and $\alpha$ is the inclination of the orbital plane to the polar axis. To this order, it does not matter what we use, indices with or without carets. In order of magnitude,

$$
\Omega_ {\mathrm {g}} \sim \frac {3}{2} \left(\frac {\mathrm {M}}{\overline {{\mathrm {R}}}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \frac {\mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {R} ^ {2}} \sim 8 ” \mathrm {p e r y e a r}
$$

where $\mathbf{R}$ is the radius of the earth. The term independent of $\mathbf{v}$ is the Lens-Thirring precession:

$$
\underline {{\Omega}} _ {\mathrm {L . T .}} = \frac {1}{\mathrm {r} ^ {3}} \left(- \underline {{J}} + \frac {3 (\underline {{J}} \cdot \underline {{r}}) \underline {{r}}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}}\right) \sim \frac {\mathrm {J}}{\mathrm {R} ^ {3}} \sim 0. 1 ^ {\prime \prime} \text {p e r y e a r}.
$$

This term could also have been obtained from Problem 11.10 since, to order $\epsilon^3$ ,

$$
\Omega^ {\hat {j}} \sim \Omega^ {j} \approx \frac {\varepsilon^ {j 0 k 1} \xi_ {0} \xi_ {[ k , 1 ]}}{2 \xi^ {\gamma} \xi_ {\gamma}}.
$$

Here

$$
\boldsymbol {\xi} ^ {0} = 1, \quad \boldsymbol {\xi} _ {0} = - (1 + 2 \phi) \quad \text {a n d} \quad \boldsymbol {\xi} _ {j} = - 2 h _ {j},
$$

so

$$
\Omega^ {\hat {\mathbf {j}}} = \varepsilon_ {\mathbf {j k 1}} \mathbf {h} _ {\mathbf {1 , k}}, \quad \underline {{\Omega}} = \nabla \times \underline {{\mathbf {h}}} .
$$

# CHAPTER 12: SOLUTIONS

Solution 12.1. Equating gravitational acceleration $\mathbf{Gm} / \mathbf{r}^2$ to centripetal acceleration $\omega^2\mathbf{r}$ gives Kepler's law: $\omega = (\mathrm{Gm} / \mathrm{r}^{3})^{\frac{1}{2}}$ . The average density inside the sphere of radius $\mathbf{r}$ is $\overline{\rho} = 3\mathrm{m} / 4\pi \mathrm{r}^3$ , so $\omega = (4\pi \mathrm{G}\overline{\rho} /3)^{\frac{1}{2}}$ . This result means, for example, that a grain of sand would orbit a steel ball bearing at the ball bearings surface with about the same $90\mathrm{min}$ . period as a satellite orbiting just above the earth's surface.

Solution 12.2. Spring tides occur when the sun and the moon are in the same line as the earth (new moon or full moon); neap tides occur when the sun and the moon are at right angles. Let the height of the tide be $h$ . For a rough estimate, take the sun and the moon to be in the equatorial plane. An ocean element on the equator at high tide is in equilibrium between the earth's gravitational acceleration $g(r) = -M_{\oplus} / r^2$ at a height $r = r_{\oplus} + h$ ( $M_{\oplus} =$ mass of earth, $r_{\oplus} =$ radius of earth) and the tidal accelerations of the sun and moon relative to the center of the earth:

$$
0 = \mathrm {g} \left(\mathrm {r} _ {\oplus} + \mathrm {h}\right) + \mathrm {r} _ {\oplus} \mathrm {R} _ {2 0 2 0} ^ {\text {s u n}} + \mathrm {r} _ {\oplus} \mathrm {R} _ {2 0 2 0} ^ {\text {m o o n}}. \tag {1}
$$

An element $90^{\circ}$ away in longitude experiences low tide:

$$
0 = \mathrm {g} \left(\mathrm {r} _ {\oplus} - \mathrm {h}\right) + \mathrm {r} _ {\oplus} \mathrm {R} _ {1 0 1 0} ^ {\text {s u n}} + \mathrm {r} _ {\oplus} \mathrm {R} _ {1 0 1 0} ^ {\text {m o o n}}. \tag {2}
$$

![](images/7b54b6144b0bcc46010cd8f4a331e30d3fd66d6bba5e88ba04c421d12272f472.jpg)

![](images/503bdaa313be494256b720df0b57328cb0802d3667c36f7109770a82663deeae.jpg)

![](images/44e76afff23502649c1692b06b2fdda8ca8ac068c5ea6b71d9d4c21464404e99.jpg)  
Sun

The difference of these two equations gives us

$$
0 = 2 \mathrm {h} \mathrm {g} ^ {\prime} (\mathrm {r} _ {\oplus}) + \mathrm {r} _ {\oplus} \left(\mathrm {R} _ {2 0 2 0} ^ {\text {s u n}} - \mathrm {R} _ {1 0 1 0} ^ {\text {s u n}} + \mathrm {R} _ {2 0 2 0} ^ {\text {m o o n}} - \mathrm {R} _ {1 0 1 0} ^ {\text {m o o n}}\right). \tag {3}
$$

Note that if we had put in centrifugal forces, they would have cancelled in Equation (3). Compute the Riemann tensor in the Newtonian limit (see Problem 12.12):

$$
\mathrm {R} _ {1 0 1 0} ^ {\mathrm {s u n}} = \left. \frac {\partial^ {2} \Phi^ {\mathrm {s u n}}}{\partial^ {2} x ^ {2}} \right| _ {x = y \approx 0} = - \frac {\partial^ {2}}{\partial x ^ {2}} \left[ \frac {\mathrm {M} _ {\odot}}{\left[ (\mathrm {x} - \mathrm {x} _ {\mathrm {s u n}}) ^ {2} + (\mathrm {y} - \mathrm {y} _ {\mathrm {s u n}}) ^ {2} + (\mathrm {z} - \mathrm {z} _ {\mathrm {s u n}}) ^ {2} \right] ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \right]
$$

and similarly for the other components. This gives

$$
R _ {1 0 1 0} ^ {\text {s u n}} \left(x _ {\text {s u n}} = z _ {\text {s u n}} = 0, y _ {\text {s u n}} = R\right) = M _ {O} / R ^ {3}
$$

$$
\mathrm {R} _ {1 0 1 0} ^ {\text {s u n}} \left(\mathbf {y} _ {\text {s u n}} = \mathbf {z} _ {\text {s u n}} = 0, \mathbf {x} _ {\text {s u n}} = \mathrm {R}\right) = - 2 \mathrm {M} _ {\odot} / \mathrm {R} ^ {3}
$$

$$
R _ {2 0 2 0} ^ {\text {s u n}} \left(x _ {\text {s u n}} = z _ {\text {s u n}} = 0, y _ {\text {s u n}} = R\right) = - 2 M _ {\odot} / R ^ {3}
$$

$$
R _ {2 0 2 0} ^ {\text {s u n}} \left(y _ {\text {s u n}} = z _ {\text {s u n}} = 0, x _ {\text {s u n}} = R\right) = M _ {\Theta} / R ^ {3}
$$

and similar expressions for the moon. Spring tides occur when the sun and the moon are in a line, say both on the y-axis. Equation (3) then gives

$$
h _ {\text {s p r i n g}} = \frac {3}{4} \left(\frac {M _ {\odot}}{R _ {\odot} ^ {3}} + \frac {M _ {\text {m o o n}}}{R _ {\text {m o o n}} ^ {3}}\right) \frac {r _ {\oplus} ^ {4}}{M _ {\oplus}} = 3 9 \mathrm {c m}.
$$

Neap tides occur when e.g. the moon is on the y-axis and the sun on the x-axis. This gives

$$
h _ {n e a p} = \frac {3}{4} \left(\frac {M _ {m o o n}}{R _ {m o o n} ^ {3}} - \frac {M _ {\odot}}{R _ {\odot} ^ {3}}\right) \frac {r _ {\oplus} ^ {4}}{M _ {\oplus}} = 1 5 c m.
$$

The actual tides are, of course, considerably larger than this in many places because of hydrodynamical effects (like "sloshing" of water in shallow seas and against irregular coastlines).

Solution 12.3. To good approximation the solid earth responds linearly to the tidal driving force of the Riemann tensor. (This is not true for the hydrodynamic ocean tides.) Thus the fourier spectrum of the earth tides will be the same as that of the Riemann tensor at earth. Since (in Newtonian order at least) the tidal force is linear in its sources we can treat the sun and moon separately. The gravitational potential of a mass M at a position x, y, z in geocentric coordinates is

$$
U = M / \left(x ^ {2} + y ^ {2} + z ^ {2}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}.
$$

The Newtonian components of the Riemann tensor are thus

$$
\begin{array}{l} R _ {x 0 x 0} = \partial^ {2} U / \partial x ^ {2} = \frac {M}{r ^ {3}} \left(\frac {3 x ^ {2}}{r ^ {2}} - 1\right) \tag {1} \\ R _ {x 0 y 0} = \partial^ {2} U / \partial x \partial y = \frac {M}{r ^ {3}} \left(\frac {3 x y}{r ^ {2}}\right) \\ \end{array}
$$

etc. for yy, zz, yz, xz. In the frame of the rotating earth, the apparent orbit of the sun or moon is given by the standard formula of Keplerian spherical astronomy (see e.g. MTW, p. 648)

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {x} = \mathrm {r} \cos (\bar {\omega} + \mathrm {v}) \cos \phi \mathrm {t} - \mathrm {r} \sin (\bar {\omega} + \mathrm {v}) \cos \epsilon \sin \phi \mathrm {t} \\ \mathrm {y} = \mathrm {r} \sin (\bar {\omega} + \mathrm {v}) \cos \epsilon \cos \phi \mathrm {t} + \mathrm {r} \cos (\bar {\omega} + \mathrm {v}) \sin \phi \mathrm {t} \\ z = r \sin (\bar {\omega} + v) \sin \epsilon . \tag {2} \\ \end{array}
$$

Here $\overline{\omega}$ and $\epsilon$ are constants, the longitude of perigee and inclination to the equatorial plane, respectively. The angle $\phi t$ is the hour angle of the rotating earth,

$$
\phi t = \frac {2 \pi}{1 \text {s i d e r e a l} \text {d a y}} t
$$

r is the "radius vector" and v is the "true anomaly," given by

$$
r = a (1 - e \cos E), \quad \cos v = (\cos E - e) (1 - e \cos E) ^ {- 1}
$$

where $a$ is the semimajor axis, $e$ is the eccentricity and $E$ is the eccentric anomaly defined by

$$
E - e \sin E = \frac {2 \pi}{T} t \equiv \Omega t
$$

and where $\mathbf{T}$ is the period of the orbit. Working only to first order in the (small) eccentricity $e$ , we compute

$$
\mathbf {E} = \boldsymbol {\Omega} t + e \sin \boldsymbol {\Omega} t
$$

$$
\cos E = \cos \Omega t - e \sin^ {2} \Omega t \tag {3}
$$

$$
r ^ {- 3} = a ^ {- 3} (1 + 3 e \cos \Omega t) \tag {3}
$$

$$
\cos v = \cos \Omega t + e \cos 2 \Omega t - e.
$$

Now substituting Equation (3) into Equation (2) and substituting the result into Equation (1), we get

$$
R _ {j 0 k 0} = \frac {M}{a ^ {3}} \times (\text {c o n s t a n t - t e r m s} + \text {t i m e v a r i e n g t e r m s});
$$

the generic time-varying term has the form (here scos means either cos or sin and an exponent mN means any integer between zero and m inclusive)

$$
\begin{array}{l} \text {i n c l u s i v e)} \\ 3 (\text {s c o s} ^ {2} \Omega t - 2 \text {e s c o s} \Omega t + 2 \text {e s c o s} \Omega t \text {s c o s} 2 \Omega t) \times \left\{ \begin{array}{l} \sin^ {2} \epsilon \quad \text {o r} \\ \sin \epsilon \cos^ {\mathbf {N}} \epsilon \text {s c o s} \phi t \quad \text {o r} \\ \cos^ {2} \mathbf {N} \epsilon \text {s c o s} ^ {2} \phi t. \end{array} \right. \end{array}
$$

This expression is analyzed into individual frequencies by repeated use of the trigonometric identities for products of scos's in terms of sum and difference frequencies. The following components result:

Angular Frequency

(i) 2φ

(ii)

(iii) $2\Omega \pm 2\phi$

(iv) $2\Omega \pm \phi$

(v) 2Ω

(vi) $\Omega \pm 2\phi$

(vii) $\Omega \pm \phi$

(viii) 0

(ix) $3\Omega \pm 2\phi$

(x) $3\Omega \pm \phi$

(xi) 3Ω

Amplitudes (leading order in e)   

<table><tr><td>3/4 cos2Nε</td></tr><tr><td>3/2 sin ε cosNε</td></tr><tr><td>3/8 cos2Nε</td></tr><tr><td>3/4 sin ε cosNε</td></tr><tr><td>3/2 sin2ε and 3/4 cos2Nε</td></tr><tr><td>3e cos2Nε</td></tr><tr><td>6e sin ε cosNε</td></tr><tr><td>6e(sin2ε or 1/2 cos2Nε)</td></tr><tr><td>3/2 e cos2Nε</td></tr><tr><td>3e sin ε cosNε</td></tr><tr><td>3e sin2ε</td></tr></table>

For the Earth-Sun system: $\epsilon = 23\frac{1}{2}^{\circ}$ , $\mathbf{e} = .017$ , $\Omega = 2\pi / 1$ year, and $\phi = 2\pi / 1$ sidereal day. For the Earth-Moon system $\mathbf{e} = .054$ , $\Omega = 2\pi / 1$ sidereal month, $\phi = 2\pi / 1$ sidereal day, and $\epsilon$ varies between $23\frac{1}{2}^{\circ} \pm 5^{\circ}$ due to the 18.6 year nutation of the moon's node. (This induces further splitting of the spectrum that we have neglected.) With these values, and using the fact that $(\mathbb{M} / \mathfrak{a}^{3})$ is 2.2 times larger for the moon's orbit than for the sun's, we get the following estimate for the ordering of former components by strength ( $\mathbb{M} = \text{moon}$ , $\mathbb{S} = \text{sun}$ ):

M(i), M(v), M(ii), M(iii), S(i), S(v), M(iv), S(ii), S(iii), M(vi), M(viii)…

Solution 12.4. Laplace first realized that there must be a difference in positions. The position of the sun in the sky is displaced by the finite velocity of light; in other words, is aberrated by the velocity $\mathbf{v}$ of the earth in its orbit, by an angle $\theta = \mathbf{v} / \mathbf{c}$ . The Coulomb gravitational field is not aberrated: If the force of gravity pointed in this direction, there would be a component of solar acceleration $(\mathrm{GM}_{\odot} / r^{2})(\mathrm{v} / \mathrm{c})$ in the direction of the earth's motion, so the earth's energy would increase at a rate given by

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} (\mathrm {E} / \mathrm {M} _ {\oplus})}{\mathrm {d t}} = \frac {\mathrm {G M} _ {\odot}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}} \frac {\mathrm {v} ^ {2}}{\mathrm {c}}
$$

but $\mathrm{E} / \mathrm{M}_{\oplus} = -\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{GM}_{\odot} / \mathrm{r},$ so the energy loss rate implies $\mathrm{dr} / \mathrm{dt} = 2\mathrm{v}^2 /\mathrm{c}$ Since $\mathbf{v}^2 = \mathbf{G}\mathbf{M}_{\odot} / \mathbf{r}$ this is easily integrated to give

$$
\mathbf {t} - \mathbf {t} _ {0} = \frac {\mathbf {c}}{4 \mathbf {G M}} \left(\mathbf {r} ^ {2} - \mathbf {r} _ {0} ^ {2}\right) .
$$

In particular, the earth's orbit has $\mathbf{r} = 1.5 \times 10^{13}$ cm, $\mathbf{v} = 30$ km/sec, the radius of the sun is $\mathbf{r}_0 = 7 \times 10^{10}$ cm, so $\theta \approx 10^{-4}$ , and $t - t_0 \approx 1.3 \times 10^{10}$ sec $\approx 400$ years. This is much less than the known geological time during which the radius of the earth's orbit is known to have been constant.

Solution 12.5. Light pressure within the star is given by $\mathrm{P_{rad}} = (1 / 3)\mathrm{U_{rad}}$ , where $\mathrm{U_{rad}}$ is the energy density of radiation. Since the light diffuses outward in many scatterings, $\mathrm{U_{rad}}$ satisfies a diffusion equation and the radiative flux $\mathbf{F}$ is proportional to a gradient in $\mathrm{U_{rad}}$ .

$$
\mathbf {F} = \frac {- c}{3 \kappa \rho} \nabla \mathbf {U} _ {\text {r a d .}}
$$

where $\kappa$ is the opacity (given here by the Thompson value $\kappa_{\mathbf{T}} = \sigma_{\mathbf{T}} / m_{\mathbf{p}} = .4 \, \mathrm{cm}^2/\mathrm{gm}$ ). The relation $\mathbf{L} = 4\pi r^2 \mathbf{F}$ between flux and total luminosity $\mathbf{L}$ of the star gives

$$
\mathrm {d P} _ {\text {r a d .}} = - \frac {\kappa \rho}{4 \pi c} \frac {\mathrm {L} \mathrm {d r}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}}
$$

which is the net light pressure on a unit area slab of matter of thickness dr. We equate this to the downward gravitational force $-\mathbf{GM}\rho \mathrm{dr} / \mathbf{r}^2$ to get the Eddington limit

$$
L = \frac {4 \pi G M c}{\kappa_ {T}} = 1. 3 8 \times 1 0 ^ {3 8} \left(\frac {M}{M _ {\odot}}\right) \text {e r g s / s e c}.
$$

Microscopically, we could also get this result by noting that since the scattering cross section is forward-backward symmetric $(\propto 1 + \cos^2\theta)$ , a photon of momentum $p$ deposits on the average a momentum $p$ to the electron per collision, and a radial component $p_r$ . The energy of the photon is $pc$ , but its contribution to the outward luminosity is reduced by the factor $v_r / c = p_r / p$ . Thus the radial momentum deposited to an electron at radius $r$ per unit time is just proportional to the net radial luminosity $\mathcal{L}$ (constant at all radii)

$$
\frac {\left(\text {m o m e n t u m}\right)}{\left(\text {t i m e}\right)} = \quad \frac {\mathrm {L}}{4 \pi \mathrm {r} ^ {2} (\mathrm {f h} \omega)} \quad \times \quad \sigma_ {\mathrm {T}} \quad \times \quad \left(\frac {\mathrm {f h} \omega}{\mathrm {c}}\right) = \frac {\mathrm {L} \sigma_ {\mathrm {T}}}{4 \pi \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \mathrm {c}}.
$$

equivalent radial cross section momentum photons per area per photon per time

Momentum per unit time is a force, and we equate this to the inward gravitational force on the proton which is associated with the electron:

$$
\frac {\mathrm {L} \sigma_ {\mathrm {T}}}{4 \pi \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \mathrm {c}} = \frac {\mathrm {G M m} _ {\mathrm {p}}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}}
$$

which gives the same result as above.

Solution 12.6. Let $\phi^{\mathbf{g}}$ and $\phi^{\mathbf{e}}$ be respectively the gravitational and electrostatic potentials. The potential energy of an electron is then

$$
\Phi = \mathrm {m} \phi^ {\mathbf {g}} + \mathrm {e} \phi^ {\mathbf {e}},
$$

$(\mathbf{m}, \mathbf{e} = \text{electron mass, charge})$ and the force on an electron is $\underline{\mathbf{F}} = -\nabla \Phi$ . In a static situation there can be no force component tangential to the conducting surface, and hence $\Phi$ must be a constant on the inside surface. Now there is neither mass $(\nabla^2 \phi^{\mathrm{g}} = 0)$ nor charge $(\nabla^2 \phi^{\mathrm{e}} = 0)$ inside the container so $\nabla^2 \Phi = 0$ . The solution to Laplace's equation $\nabla^2 \Phi = 0$ inside the conductor equipotential is then $\Phi = \text{constant}$ . If an electron is then introduced into this field, it will feel no force. [Note: Since the electron is introduced at the center of the container, image-charge forces can be ignored by symmetry.]

Solution 12.7. As the contents are heated, mass-energy is added according to $c^2 dM = \frac{7}{2} kN dT$ ( $\frac{7}{2}$ since air is diatomic; $N$ is the total number of molecules in the cylinder). However the center of gravity of the gas rises which acts to decrease the weight (distance to earth's center increases). The position of the center of gravity is:

$$
z _ {c g} = \int_ {0} ^ {h} \rho z d z / \int_ {0} ^ {h} \rho d z, \quad \rho = \rho_ {0} \exp \left(- \frac {m g}{k T} z\right),
$$

so the change of $z_{\mathrm{cg}}$ with temperature is given by

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} z _ {\mathrm {c g}}}{\mathrm {d} \mathrm {T}} = \frac {\mathrm {k}}{\mathrm {m g}} \left[ 1 - \left(\frac {\eta}{\sinh \eta}\right) ^ {2} \right], \qquad \eta \equiv \frac {\mathrm {m g h}}{2 \mathrm {k T}}
$$

where $m$ is the average mass of an air molecule.

The weight change is thus

$$
\mathrm {d W} = \left(\mathrm {g} \frac {\mathrm {d M}}{\mathrm {d T}} + \mathrm {M} \frac {\mathrm {d g}}{\mathrm {d z} _ {\mathrm {c g}}} \frac {\mathrm {d z} _ {\mathrm {c g}}}{\mathrm {d T}}\right) \mathrm {d T} = \left(\frac {7}{2} \frac {\mathrm {g k N}}{\mathrm {c} ^ {2}} - \mathrm {m N} \frac {2 \mathrm {g}}{\mathrm {R} _ {\oplus}} \frac {\mathrm {k}}{\mathrm {m g}} \left[ 1 - \frac {\eta^ {2}}{\sinh^ {2} \eta} \right]\right) \mathrm {d T}.
$$

The right-hand side is negative when

$$
1 - \frac {\eta^ {2}}{\sinh^ {2} \eta} > \frac {7}{4} \frac {\mathrm {g} \mathrm {R} _ {\oplus}}{\mathrm {c} ^ {2}} = . 1 2 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 8}.
$$

This implies that $\eta > 6.05 \times 10^{-5}$ and that $h = 2kT\eta / mg > 218$ cm.

Solution 12.8. Since Poisson's equation is $\mathbf{U}_{,\mathbf{k}\mathbf{k}} = 4\pi \rho_{0}$ we have

$$
\mathrm {T} _ {\mathrm {j k}, \mathrm {k}} = \frac {1}{4 \pi} \left(\mathrm {U} _ {\mathrm {, j k}} \mathrm {U} _ {\mathrm {, k}} + \mathrm {U} _ {\mathrm {, j}} \mathrm {U} _ {\mathrm {, k k}} - \mathrm {U} _ {\mathrm {, n j}} \mathrm {U} _ {\mathrm {, n}}\right) = \rho_ {0} \mathrm {U} _ {\mathrm {, j}}.
$$

Thus the Newtonian equation of motion can be written as

$$
\rho_ {0} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {v} _ {\mathrm {j}}}{\mathrm {d} t} = - \frac {\partial t _ {\mathrm {j k}}}{\partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {k}}} - \rho_ {0} \frac {\partial \mathbf {U}}{\partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {j}}} = \frac {- \partial}{\partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {k}}} \left(\mathbf {T} _ {\mathrm {j k}} + t _ {\mathrm {j k}}\right) _ {\mathrm {, k}}. \tag {1}
$$

Now use the Newtonian equation of mass conservation

$$
\partial \rho_ {0} / \partial t + \left(\rho_ {0} v _ {\cdot k} ^ {k}\right) _ {, k} = 0. \tag {2}
$$

From Equation (2) and the expression for the convective derivative $\mathrm{d} / \mathrm{d}t = \partial /\partial t + \mathbf{v_k}\partial /\partial \mathbf{x^k}$ , one can write

$$
\rho_ {0} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {v} _ {\mathrm {j}}}{\mathrm {d} t} = \rho_ {0} \left(\frac {\partial \mathbf {v} _ {\mathrm {j}}}{\mathrm {d} t} + \mathbf {v} ^ {\mathbf {k}} \mathbf {v} _ {\mathrm {j}, \mathbf {k}} ^ {\mathrm {j}}\right) = (\rho_ {0} \mathbf {v} _ {\mathrm {j}}) _ {\mathrm {, t}} + (\rho_ {0} \mathbf {v} _ {\mathrm {j}} \mathbf {v} _ {\mathbf {k}}) _ {\mathrm {, k}}
$$

and thus Equation (1) can be written as

$$
\left(\rho_ {0} \mathrm {v} _ {\mathrm {j}}\right) _ {, \mathrm {t}} + \left(\mathrm {T} _ {\mathrm {j k}} + \mathrm {t} _ {\mathrm {j k}} + \rho_ {0} \mathrm {v} _ {\mathrm {j}} \mathrm {v} _ {\mathrm {k}}\right) _ {, \mathrm {k}} = 0. \tag {3}
$$

Solution 12.9. Consider a virtual displacement $\underline{\delta}$ ; the energy must be stationary in $\underline{\delta}$ . If we include in the energy, the gravitational potential energy of the work done on or by forces, then the change in energy for any one force is

$$
\mathrm {d E} _ {\mathrm {i}} = - \underline {{F}} _ {\mathrm {i}} \cdot \underline {{\delta}} + (\mathrm {d E} _ {\mathrm {i}}) (\underline {{g}} / c ^ {2}) \cdot \underline {{x}} _ {\mathrm {i}}
$$

and the change in energy from raising the object mass is

$$
\mathrm {d E} = - \underline {{\mathbf {M}}} \underline {{\mathbf {g}}} \cdot \underline {{\delta}}.
$$

Thus

$$
\mathrm {d E} _ {\text {t o t a l}} = - \sum_ {\sim} \underset {\sim} {\mathrm {F} _ {\mathrm {i}}} \cdot \underset {\sim} {\delta} \left(1 - \frac {\underset {\sim} {\mathrm {g}} \cdot \underset {\sim} {\mathrm {x} _ {\mathrm {i}}}}{\mathrm {c} ^ {2}}\right) - \underset {\sim} {\mathrm {M g}} \cdot \underset {\sim} {\delta}
$$

for any $\underline{\delta}$ and hence

$$
\sum_ {i} \underset {\sim} {F} _ {i} \left(1 - \frac {\underline {{g}} \cdot \underset {\sim} {x} _ {i}}{c ^ {2}}\right) = - M g.
$$

Note that by taking the gravitational potential relative to the center of gravity, we avoid having to add the gravitational potential energy of the gravitational potential energy. (This argument is due to K. Nordtvedt.)

Solution 12.10. From the previous problem

$$
\sum \underset {\sim} {F} _ {i} \left(1 - \underset {\sim} {g} \cdot \underset {\sim} {x} _ {i} / c ^ {2}\right) = - M \underset {\sim} {g};
$$

If $p$ is pressure and $dA$ is directed surface area, then $F = pdA$ and the above result becomes

$$
- \int p (x) \left(1 - \underline {{g}} \cdot \underline {{x}} / c ^ {2}\right) d \underline {{A}} = - M g
$$

or

$$
- \int p d \underline {{A}} + \int p g \cdot \frac {\underline {{x}}}{c ^ {2}} d \underline {{A}} = - M g
$$

for $\underline{\mathbf{g}}$ constant. Now with $\underline{\mathbf{g}} = -\frac{\mathbf{GM}}{\mathbf{r}^2}\underline{\mathbf{e}}_{\mathbf{r}},\quad \underline{\mathbf{x}} = \mathrm{d}\mathbf{r}\underline{\mathbf{e}}_{\mathbf{r}},\quad \mathrm{d}\underline{\mathbf{A}} = \pm \mathrm{d}\mathbf{A}\underline{\mathbf{e}}_{\mathbf{r}},$ we have

$$
- \mathrm {A} [ \mathrm {p} (\mathrm {r} + \mathrm {d r}) - \mathrm {p} (\mathrm {r}) ] \underline {{\mathrm {e}}} _ {\mathrm {r}} - \mathrm {d r} \frac {\mathrm {G M}}{\mathrm {c} ^ {2} \mathrm {r} ^ {2}} \underline {{\mathrm {e}}} _ {\mathrm {r}} \int \mathrm {p d A} = - \underline {{\mathrm {M g}}} = \mathrm {A d r} \rho \frac {\mathrm {G M}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}} \underline {{\mathrm {e}}} _ {\mathrm {r}}
$$

and thus

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {p}}{\mathrm {d} \mathbf {r}} = - \frac {\mathrm {G M}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}} \left(\rho + \frac {\mathrm {p}}{\mathrm {c} ^ {2}}\right).
$$

This result holds in any theory of gravity that satisfies $\mathbf{T}^{\mu \nu}$ ; $\nu = 0$ (such theories are called metric theories) and is independent of the field equations. Each theory, however, gives its own prescription for finding $\mathbf{M}(\mathbf{r})$ , which is defined such that the local acceleration of gravity is $\mathbf{g} = -\mathbf{GM} / \mathbf{r}^2$ .

Solution 12.11. The preferred coordinates of Newtonian theory are "universal time" $t$ and Galilean space coordinates $x^j$ . The equation of motion

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {j}}}{\mathrm {d t} ^ {2}} = - \frac {\partial \Phi}{\partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {j}}}
$$

can be written in 4-dimensional form:

$$
\frac {d ^ {2} t}{d \lambda^ {2}} = 0
$$

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {j}}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda^ {2}} + \frac {\partial \Phi}{\partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {j}}} \bigg (\frac {\mathrm {d t}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} \bigg) ^ {2} = 0.
$$

From this we read off the Christoffel symbols:

$$
\Gamma_ {0 0} ^ {j} = \frac {\partial \Phi}{\partial \mathbf {x} ^ {j}}; \quad \text {a l l o t h e r} \Gamma_ {\beta \gamma} ^ {\alpha} \text {v a n i s h}.
$$

The standard formula for the components of the Riemann tensor gives

$$
R _ {0 k 0} ^ {j} = - R _ {0 0 k} ^ {j} = \frac {\partial^ {2} \Phi}{\partial x ^ {j} \partial x ^ {k}}; \quad \text {a l l o t h e r} \quad R _ {\beta \gamma \delta} ^ {\alpha} \text {v a n i s h}.
$$

Suppose these were derivable from a metric, then we would have

$$
R _ {j 0 k 0} = g _ {j \alpha} R _ {0 k 0} ^ {\alpha} = g _ {j m} \frac {\partial^ {2} \Phi}{\partial x ^ {m} \partial x ^ {k}},
$$

but

$$
\mathrm {R} _ {0 \mathrm {j k 0}} = \mathrm {g} _ {0 \alpha} \mathrm {R} _ {\mathrm {j k 0}} ^ {\alpha} = 0 \neq - \mathrm {R} _ {\mathrm {j 0 k 0}}.
$$

This violates the antisymmetry property $\mathbf{R}_{a\beta \gamma \delta}$ has if it is derived from a metric.

Solution 12.12. Let the Newtonian test-particle trajectories be

$$
\mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {j}} = \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {j}} (t, n).
$$

Here $n$ is a parameter telling which trajectory we are considering. The vector

$$
\underline {{\mathbf {n}}} = \frac {\partial}{\partial \mathbf {n}} = \frac {\partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathbf {k}}}{\partial \mathbf {n}} \frac {\partial}{\partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathbf {k}}} = \mathbf {n} ^ {\mathbf {k}} \frac {\partial}{\partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathbf {k}}}
$$

is a connecting vector between neighboring trajectories. The relative acceleration of neighboring trajectories is

$$
\frac {\partial^ {2} \mathbf {n} ^ {\mathrm {j}}}{\partial t ^ {2}} = \frac {\partial^ {2}}{\partial t ^ {2}} \left(\frac {\partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {j}}}{\partial \mathbf {n}}\right) = \frac {\partial}{\partial \mathbf {n}} \left(\frac {\partial^ {2} \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {j}}}{\partial t ^ {2}}\right) = \frac {\partial}{\partial \mathbf {n}} \left(- \frac {\partial \boldsymbol {\Phi}}{\partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {j}}}\right) = - \mathbf {n} ^ {\mathrm {k}} \frac {\partial}{\partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {k}}} \left(\frac {\partial \boldsymbol {\Phi}}{\partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {j}}}\right) = - \mathbf {n} ^ {\mathrm {k}} \frac {\partial^ {2} \boldsymbol {\Phi}}{\partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {k}} \partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {j}}},
$$

where we have used the equation of motion $\partial^2\mathbf{x}^{\mathrm{j}} / \partial t^2 = -\partial \Phi /\partial \mathbf{x}^{\mathrm{j}}$ . The equation of geodesic deviation is

$$
\frac {\mathrm {D} ^ {2} \mathfrak {n} ^ {a}}{\mathrm {d} \tau^ {2}} = - \mathrm {R} _ {\beta \gamma \delta} ^ {a} \mathfrak {n} ^ {\gamma} \mathrm {u} ^ {\beta} \mathrm {u} ^ {\delta} .
$$

In the Newtonian limit (velocities $\sim \epsilon$ , gravitational fields $\sim \epsilon^2$ )

$$
u ^ {0} = 1 + \mathcal {O} (\epsilon), \quad u ^ {j} = \mathcal {O} (\epsilon), \quad \Gamma_ {\beta \gamma} ^ {\alpha} = \mathcal {O} (\epsilon^ {2})
$$

and $\mathfrak{n}$ , which connects events of equal proper time, connects events of equal coordinate time to $\mathcal{O}(\epsilon)$ . Thus, $\frac{\mathrm{D}}{\mathrm{d}\tau} = \frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}t} + \mathcal{O}(\epsilon)$ and so

$$
\frac {d ^ {2} n ^ {j}}{d t ^ {2}} = - R _ {0 k 0} ^ {j} n ^ {k} + \mathcal {O} (\epsilon).
$$

The Riemann components are then

$$
R _ {0 k 0} ^ {j} = \frac {\partial^ {2} \Phi}{\partial x ^ {k} \partial x ^ {j}}
$$
