in the Newtonian limit. Note that, unless the velocities involved approach the speed of light, the other components of $\mathbf{R}_{\beta \gamma \delta}^{\alpha}$ do not enter into the equation of relative motion of test particles.

Solution 12.13. Newtonian gravity is characterized by instant “action at a distance,” where simultaneity is defined by the slices of a ‘universal time’ field $t$ . We want a relativistically invariant expression for the gravitational acceleration at a point in terms of the matter density $T^{\mu \nu}$ in the rest of the universe. Spacetime is taken to have the Minkowski metric everywhere. Only $T^{00}$ should enter, so we first dot into two universal time vectors:

$$
\nabla \mathbf {t} \cdot \mathbf {T} \cdot \nabla \mathbf {t} \quad (\mathrm {o r} \quad \mathbf {t} _ {, \alpha} \mathbf {T} ^ {\alpha \beta} \mathbf {t}, \beta) .
$$

Thus the inverse square law acceleration of a particle at position $\mathbf{x}$ becomes

$$
\mathbf {a} _ {\text {g r a v}} = \mathrm {G} \int_ {\mathrm {t} = \text {c o n s t a n t}} 1 \frac {\nabla \mathrm {t} \cdot \mathrm {T} \left(\mathrm {x} ^ {\prime}\right) \cdot \nabla \mathrm {t}}{\left(\mathrm {l} \cdot \mathrm {l}\right) ^ {\frac {3}{2}}} \mathrm {d} ^ {3} \mathrm {x} ^ {\prime}
$$

where $l$ is the spacelike vector connecting $x'$ to $x$ and $d^3 x'$ is the proper volume element on the constant $t$ slice. This is not yet consistent with special relativity, because $a$ is not necessarily orthogonal to the particle's 4-velocity $u$ . We must apply a projection operator, giving a final answer

$$
\mathbf {a} _ {\text {g r a v}} = \mathrm {G} \int \frac {\nabla \mathbf {t} \cdot \mathbf {T} \cdot \nabla \mathbf {t}}{(1 \cdot 1) ^ {\frac {3}{2}}} [ \mathbf {l} + (\mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {l}) \mathbf {u} ] d ^ {3} x ^ {\prime}.
$$

This is manifestly consistent with special relativity because it is (i) written in terms of geometrical objects, and (ii) defines a geometrical object (a with $\mathbf{a} \cdot \mathbf{u} = 0$ ). Signals can be sent faster than light by changing the distribution of masses at a point. The gravitational field then changes on surfaces of constant t. Since these surfaces are spacelike, they are outside the lightcone of every observer. Nevertheless the theory is not acausal; since every observer's worldline is timelike, t is always increasing in his future direction. Likewise, t increases along any light ray he emits. So any signal can at best connect events of constant t; no sequence of signals can return to an observer at an earlier t than they started. This is to be contrasted with the case of tachyons (Problem 1.6) which are acausal. The reason for the difference is that this problem has some “pregeometry” in the scalar field t, which slices up spacetime in a universally time-ordered fashion. Tachyons “slice” spacetime in a manner that varies from observer to observer.

Solution 12.14. Take the separation as $\underline{\eta} = (\text{separation})\underline{e}_z$ ; then due to gravitational forces

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \eta^ {z}}{\mathrm {d t} ^ {2}} = - \mathsf {R} _ {\beta \gamma \delta} ^ {z} \mathsf {u} ^ {\beta} \eta^ {\gamma} \mathsf {u} ^ {\delta}.
$$

Since the particles start from rest, if we confine our observations to short time, $\mathbf{u}^{\mathrm{j}} \approx 0$ , so that

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \eta^ {z}}{\mathrm {d} t ^ {2}} = - \mathrm {R} _ {0 z 0} ^ {z} \eta^ {z}.
$$

The Riemann component for a weak field is easily calculated

$$
\mathsf {R} _ {0 z 0} ^ {\mathsf {z}} = \frac {\partial^ {2}}{\partial \mathsf {z} ^ {2}} \left(- \frac {\mathsf {M}}{\mathsf {z}}\right) = - \frac {2 \mathsf {M}}{\mathsf {z} ^ {3}}.
$$

Due to the E field, there is an acceleration of the lower particle

$$
\underline {{\underline {{\mathbf {a}}}}} = \frac {\mathbf {q}}{\mathbf {m}} \mathbf {E} \underline {{\underline {{\mathbf {e}}}}} _ {z}.
$$

Thus

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \eta^ {z}}{\mathrm {d t} ^ {2}} = \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{z ^ {3}} \eta^ {z} - \frac {\mathrm {q}}{\mathrm {m}} \mathrm {E}.
$$

It may seem disturbing at first that the two terms look as if they can be comparable. But suppose our laboratory is limited to a chunk of spacetime $\Delta \mathbf{x}^{\mu} \approx \mathbf{L}$ . Then at the end of the experiment the gravitational forces will have caused a change in $\eta^{z}$ :

$$
\delta \eta^ {z} \sim \frac {1}{2} \frac {2 \mathbb {M}}{z ^ {3}} \eta^ {z} \mathrm {L} ^ {2}.
$$

But $\eta^z < L$ so

$$
\delta \eta^ {z} = \mathcal {O} (\mathrm {L} ^ {3}).
$$

The change in $\eta^z$ due to the electric field is

$$
\delta \eta^ {z} \sim - \frac {\mathrm {q}}{\mathrm {m}} \cdot \mathrm {E} \cdot \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {L} ^ {2} = \mathcal {O} (\mathrm {L} ^ {2})
$$

so, if we sufficiently limit the “‘size’” of our experiment we can ignore gravitational effects.

Solution 12.15. The geometrical object which tells about the charge is a scalar, vector, 2-tensor, etc. for spins 0, 1, 2, etc. For example, the charge density of a particle of rest-charge $\mathbf{q}_0$ moving along a worldline $\mathbf{x}^{\mu} = \mathbf{z}^{\mu}(\tau)$ with 4-velocity $\mathbf{u}$ is

$$
\rho \left(\mathbf {x} ^ {\sigma}\right) = q _ {0} \int \delta^ {4} \left[ \mathbf {x} ^ {\mu} - z ^ {\mu} (\tau) \right] d \tau \quad (s c a l a r)
$$

$$
J ^ {\mu} \left(\mathbf {x} ^ {\sigma}\right) = q _ {0} \int u ^ {\mu} \delta^ {4} \left[ \mathbf {x} ^ {\sigma} - z ^ {\sigma} (r) \right] d r \tag {1}
$$

$$
\mathrm {T} ^ {\mu \nu} (\mathbf {x} ^ {\sigma}) = q _ {0} \int \mathrm {u} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {u} ^ {\nu} \delta^ {4} [ \mathbf {x} ^ {\sigma} - \mathbf {z} ^ {\sigma} (\tau) ] \mathrm {d} \tau \quad \text {(s p i n 2)}
$$

$$
\mathrm {T} ^ {\mu \nu \dots \rho} (\mathbf {x} ^ {\sigma}) = q _ {0} \int u ^ {\mu} u ^ {\nu} \dots u ^ {\rho} \delta^ {4} [ \mathbf {x} ^ {\sigma} - z ^ {\sigma} (\tau) ] d \tau . \quad (\text {s p i n} \mathbf {s})
$$

The charge of a particle in a laboratory volume, measured by the "Coulomb" part of its interaction in the lab is

$$
\begin{array}{l} q = \int T ^ {0 0 \dots 0} d ^ {3} x (labframe) \\ = (- 1) ^ {s} \int T ^ {\alpha \beta \dots \gamma} u _ {\alpha} ^ {L A B} u _ {\beta} ^ {L A B} \dots u _ {\gamma} ^ {L A B} d ^ {3} x. (2) \\ \end{array}
$$

Substituting Equation (1) into Equation (2) gives the measured charge of a single particle

$$
\begin{array}{l} q = q _ {0} \int d ^ {3} x \int d \tau (- u \cdot u ^ {\mathrm {L A B}}) ^ {s} \delta^ {4} [ x ^ {\sigma} - z ^ {\sigma} (r) ] \\ = q _ {0} \int d ^ {3} x d t \left(\frac {d \tau}{d t}\right) (- u \cdot u ^ {\mathrm {L A B}}) ^ {s} \delta^ {4} [ x ^ {\sigma} - z ^ {\sigma} (\tau) ]. \\ \end{array}
$$

Integrating out the delta function, and using $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{u}^{\mathrm{LAB}} = -\gamma$ and $\mathrm{d}\tau / \mathrm{d}t = 1 / \gamma$ , we get $q = q_0 \gamma^{s-1} \approx q_0 \left(1 + \frac{s-1}{2} v^2\right)$ for $v << c$ . Now for a perfect gas we have $<v^2> = 3kT / m_0$ . So when we sum the charges of all the particles in the gas we get

$$
\mathrm {Q} = \sum_ {\mathbf {n}} \mathbf {q} = \mathrm {n q} _ {0} \left[ 1 + \frac {\mathbf {s} - 1}{2} \left(\frac {3 \mathrm {k T}}{\mathbf {m} _ {0}}\right) \right],
$$

and from the temperature dependence given in the problem we now read off $\mathbf{s} = 5$ .

Solution 12.16. Suppose, to the contrary, that there were another spin-2 field. Since it is massless, and has infinite range, it must admit weak, plane waves as solutions. Also since the field is spin-2 it must couple to a symmetric, traceless "charge" tensor $\mathbf{J}^{\mu \nu}$ . Moreover, for weak fields and slow velocities, "charged" particles which are much smaller than a wavelength must couple predominantly through their $\mathbf{J}^{00}$ (i.e. the "Coulomb" limit).

Consider a plane wave incident on two particles (initially located at the same point) of charge $J^{00} = q_1$ , $J^{00} = q_2$ . For a sinusoidal plane wave of a definite linear polarization the particles must execute linear sinusoidal motion whose amplitude is proportional to their charge and inversely proportional to their mass. Hence, at an instant in time, we can define a polarization vector (analog of the electric field E) for the field

$$
\underline {{{\mathsf {V}}}} \equiv \left(\frac {\mathsf {q} _ {1}}{\mathsf {m} _ {1}} - \frac {\mathsf {q} _ {2}}{\mathsf {m} _ {2}}\right) ^ {- 1} (\underline {{{\mathsf {x}}}} _ {1} - \underline {{{\mathsf {x}}}} _ {2})
$$

which is independent of the numerical value of $\mathbf{q}_1, \mathbf{q}_2, \mathbf{m}_1, \mathbf{m}_2$ . But this is a contradiction! Since the field has spin-2 structure, a polarization state should be unchanged by $180^\circ$ rotation, but under $180^\circ$ rotation $\underline{\mathbf{V}} \rightarrow -\underline{\mathbf{V}}$ implying $\underline{\mathbf{V}} = 0$ . What is wrong? We assumed that the particles responded differently to the field. We now see that $\underline{\mathbf{x}}_1 = \underline{\mathbf{x}}_2$ for all charges and masses is the only possibility. This is equivalent to saying that all particles have a fixed charge-to-mass ratio: $\mathbf{q} / \mathbf{m} = \text{constant}$ . By choice of units we can make $\mathbf{q} = \mathbf{m}$ . Hence, in the weak field, plane-wave limit $\mathbf{J}^{00} = \mathbf{T}^{00}$ , the stress energy. But by Lorentz invariance we must have $\mathbf{J}^{\mu \nu} = \mathbf{T}^{\mu \nu}$ (e.g. we cannot introduce $\mathbf{T}_{\mu}^{\mu}$ since this is not spin-2).

We have shown that our "arbitrary" spin-2 field is coupled to $\mathbf{T}^{\mu \nu}$ . Hence, it is just a piece of gravity, i.e. whatever we measure experimentally as gravity. (No claim that it is general relativity is made.) R. P. Feynman has pointed out that the assumption of a "point" particle, with no intrinsic polarization vector, has entered crucially. Thus it is possible to have the field couple to individual particles with spin; but for unpolarized bulk matter, there can be no net force.

Solution 12.17. In c.g.s. units we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} c = 2. 9 9 8 \times 1 0 ^ {1 0} c m / s e c \\ c ^ {2} = 8. 9 9 8 \times 1 0 ^ {2 0} \mathrm {e r g / g m} \\ G / c ^ {2} = 0. 7 4 2 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 2 8} c m / g m \\ G ^ {\frac {1}{2}} = 2. 5 8 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4} e s u / g m. \\ \end{array}
$$

(The value of $\mathbf{G}^{\frac{1}{2}}$ comes from the fact that in Coulomb's or Newton's law, force $= e^{2} / r$ or $\mathbf{G}m^{2} / r^{2}$ .) Thus, in gravitational units, the values are

(a) $\pi = \hbar \left(\frac{G}{c^2}\right)c\left(\frac{1}{c^2}\right) = \frac{\hbar G}{c^3} \simeq \frac{(\text{erg sec})(\text{cm/gm})(\text{cm/sec})}{(\text{erg/gm})} \simeq 2.611 \times 10^{-66} \text{cm}^2$   
(b) $e = e\left(\frac{G}{c^2}\right)\left(\frac{1}{G^{\frac{1}{2}}}\right) \simeq \frac{(esu)(cm / gm)}{(esu / gm)} \simeq \frac{eG^{\frac{1}{2}}}{c^2} 1.381 \times 10^{-34}cm$   
(c) $\frac{\mathrm{e}}{\mathrm{m}} = \frac{\mathrm{e}}{\mathrm{G}^{\frac{1}{2}}\mathrm{m}} \simeq \frac{(\mathrm{esu})}{(\mathrm{gm})(\mathrm{esu} / \mathrm{gm})} \simeq 2.042 \times 10^{21}$   
(d) $\mathbf{M}_{\odot} = \frac{\mathbf{G}\mathbf{M}_{\odot}}{\mathbf{c}^{2}}\simeq (\mathbf{g}\mathbf{m})(\mathbf{cm} / \mathbf{gm})\simeq 1.48\times 10^{5}\mathbf{cm}$   
(e) $\mathrm{L}_{\odot} = \mathrm{L}_{\odot}\left(\frac{\mathrm{G}}{\mathrm{c}^{2}}\right)\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{c}^{2}}\right)\left(\frac{1}{\mathrm{c}}\right) \simeq \frac{(\mathrm{erg / sec})(\mathrm{cm / gm})}{(\mathrm{erg / gm})(\mathrm{cm / sec})} \simeq 1.07 \times 10^{-26}$   
(f) $300^{0}\mathrm{K} = \frac{(300^{0}\mathrm{K})\mathrm{k}\left(\frac{\mathrm{G}}{\mathrm{c}^{2}}\right)}{\mathrm{c}^{2}}\simeq \frac{(^{\circ}\mathrm{K})(\mathrm{erg} / ^{\circ}\mathrm{K})(\mathrm{cm} / \mathrm{gm})}{(\mathrm{erg} / \mathrm{gm})}\simeq 3.42\times 10^{-63}\mathrm{cm}$   
(g) (1 year) = (1 year)c = 9.460×1017cm   
(h) $(1\mathrm{volt}) = \left(\frac{1}{299.8}\frac{\mathrm{erg}}{\mathrm{esu}}\right)\mathrm{G}^{\frac{1}{2}}\left(\frac{1}{c^{2}}\right)\simeq \frac{(\mathrm{erg / esu})(\mathrm{esu / gm})}{(\mathrm{erg / gm})}\simeq 9.58\times 10^{-28}.$

Solution 12.18.

$$
\begin{array}{l} L ^ {*} = \left(\mathrm {H G} / \mathrm {c} ^ {3}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} = 1. 6 1 6 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3 3} \mathrm {c m} \\ \mathrm {T} ^ {*} = \left(\mathrm {f r G} / \mathrm {c} ^ {5}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} = 5. 3 9 1 \times 1 0 ^ {- 4 4} \sec \\ \mathbf {M} ^ {*} = (\hbar \mathrm {c} / \mathrm {G}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} = 2. 1 7 7 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5} \mathrm {g m}. \\ \end{array}
$$

These combinations were noticed by Planck, immediately after he discovered his constant. They are now called the Planck length, mass, and time. They are uniquely defined up to factors of order unity (e.g. using $h$ instead of $\pi$ ), since by dimensional analysis there is only one way of

mapping three dimensional quantities into three specified dimensional results (i.e. there are no free dimensionless combinations).

Solution 12.19. Force balance gives us

$$
\mathrm {G m} _ {\mathbf {N}} ^ {2} / \mathrm {r} ^ {2} = \mathrm {m} _ {\mathbf {N}} \omega^ {2} \left(\frac {1}{2} \mathrm {r}\right).
$$

According to Bohr quantization the separation radius must satisfy

$$
2 m \omega \left(\frac {1}{2} r\right) ^ {2} = n \pi .
$$

From these two equations, the separation is

$$
r = 2 n ^ {2} \hbar^ {2} / G m _ {N} ^ {3}.
$$

The lowest energy level is the $n = 1$ level. In this level the separation is

$$
r = 2 \hbar^ {2} / G m _ {N} ^ {3} = 6 \times 1 0 ^ {2 4} c m \approx 6 \times 1 0 ^ {6} l i g h t y e a r s!
$$

# CHAPTER 13: SOLUTIONS

Solution 13.1. According to the Bianchi identities the Einstein tensor $\mathbf{R}_{\mu \nu} - \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{g}_{\mu \nu}\mathbf{R}$ is divergenceless so that the divergence of our generalized field equation is

$$
\left(\frac {1}{2} - a\right) \mathrm {R} _ {, \mu} = 8 \pi \mathrm {T} _ {\mu} ^ {\nu} _ {; \nu}.
$$

On the other hand, if we contract our field equations first and then differentiate we find

$$
(1 - 4 a) R _ {, \mu} = 8 \pi T _ {, \mu}
$$

so that the equations of motion must be

$$
\mathrm {T} _ {\mu ; \nu} ^ {\nu} = \kappa \mathrm {T} _ {, \mu}, \quad \kappa \equiv \frac {\frac {1}{2} - a}{1 - 4 a}.
$$

In the Newtonian limit, and for a fluid with density $\rho$ and negligible pressures, the $\mu = 0$ component of this equation is

$$
\frac {\partial \rho}{\partial t} + \nabla \cdot (\rho \underline {{v}}) = \kappa \frac {\partial \rho}{\partial t}
$$

where $\underline{\mathbf{v}}$ is the velocity of fluid flow. If $\kappa$ were not zero (i.e. if $\alpha$ were not $\frac{1}{2}$ ) this would differ from the Newtonian continuity equation, and violate the conservation of mass in Newtonian order.

Solution 13.2. The vanishing of the Weyl tensor allows us to write the metric in the conformally flat form

$$
\mathbf {g} _ {\mu \nu} = \mathbf {e} ^ {2 \phi} \eta_ {\mu \nu}
$$

where $\phi \ll 1$ in the Newtonian limit (nearly flat spacetime). We have then (see Problem 9.19)

$$
\textbf {R} \approx - 6 \nabla^ {2} \phi .
$$

For nonrelativistic stress energies $\mathbf{T}_{\mu}^{\mu} \approx \mathbf{T}_{0}^{0} \approx -\rho$ and the field equations become

$$
- 6 \phi_ {, \alpha \beta} \eta^ {\alpha \beta} = \kappa \mathbf {T} = - \kappa \rho .
$$

In the Newtonian limit time variations will be slow compared to spatial variations (in $c = 1$ units) so we have

$$
6 \phi_ {, i j} \delta^ {i j} = \kappa \rho .
$$

For $\kappa = 24\pi$ this is the usual Newtonian equation for the Newtonian potential $\phi$ . The solution to Problem 12.11 implies that Newtonian trajectories are geodesics in a geometry with $g_{00} \approx -(1 + 2\phi)$ . Thus in the Newtonian limit the proposed theory agrees with Newtonian theory and $\phi$ plays the role of the Newtonian potential.

For the metric of a massive object like the sun the function $\phi$ must fall off far from the object where spacetime approaches flatness. To see that light is not deflected we need only notice [see Problem 9.18] that the null geodesics of $\mathbf{g}_{\mu \nu} = \mathrm{e}^{2\phi}\eta_{\mu \nu}$ are identical to the null geodesics of $\eta_{\mu \nu}$ , which are certainly not deflected by the massive object. Thus when we compare, in the asymptotically flat distant regions, the directions of a photon's motion before and after it interacts with the massive object, we will observe no deflection.

Near the earth's surface the metric is of the form

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = \mathrm {e} ^ {2 \phi (z)} (- \mathrm {d t} ^ {2} + \mathrm {d x} ^ {2} + \mathrm {d y} ^ {2} + \mathrm {d z} ^ {2})
$$

where $z$ corresponds to height above the surface. If particles are to fall with the proper acceleration we must have $\phi \approx -gz$ . From the geodesic equation we find that for the energy of a photon moving vertically

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} p ^ {0}}{\mathrm {d} z} = - \Gamma_ {0 z} ^ {0} p ^ {0} = - \phi_ {, z} p ^ {0}.
$$

Thus a photon loses energy at the same rate as a particle and the proposed theory agrees with Pound-Rebka experiment, as any theory based on geodesic motion must.

Solution 13.3. The static field equation for the Brans-Dicke scalar field is

$$
\nabla^ {2} \phi = \frac {8 \pi \mathrm {T}}{(3 + 2 \omega)}. \tag {1}
$$

Now, for a spherical shell of mass M, radius R,

$$
T = - \rho = \frac {M \delta (r - R)}{4 \pi r ^ {2}} \tag {2}
$$

so that the $\phi$ field inside and outside the shell is

$$
\phi_ {I} = \phi_ {1}, \quad r <   R \tag {3a}
$$

$$
\phi_ {\mathrm {I I}} = \phi_ {\infty} + \frac {2}{3 + 2 \omega} \frac {\mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}, \quad \mathrm {r} > \mathrm {R} \tag {3b}
$$

where $\phi_{\mathbf{I}}$ and $\phi_{\infty}$ are constants. Matching $\phi_{\mathbf{I}}$ and $\phi_{\mathbf{II}}$ at $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{R}$ gives

$$
\phi_ {1} = \phi_ {\infty} + \frac {2}{3 + 2 \omega} \frac {\mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {R}}. \tag {4}
$$

Now, we also know that in the Brans-Dicke theory, the local gravitational constant, in terms of $\phi$ , is (Weinberg, Equation 9.9.11)

$$
G = \phi^ {- 1} \left(\frac {4 + 2 \omega}{3 + 2 \omega}\right). \tag {5}
$$

Thus

$$
\phi_ {\infty} = \frac {4 + 2 \omega}{3 + 2 \omega} \frac {1}{G _ {\infty}}
$$

$$
\mathrm {G} _ {1} = \phi_ {1} ^ {- 1} \left(\frac {4 + 2 \omega}{3 + 2 \omega}\right) \approx \left(\frac {4 + 2 \omega}{3 + 2 \omega}\right) \phi_ {\infty} ^ {- 1} \left[ 1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\phi_ {\infty} \mathrm {R} (3 + 2 \omega)} \right]
$$

and finally

$$
\mathrm {G} _ {1} = \mathrm {G} _ {\infty} \left[ 1 - \frac {\mathrm {G} _ {\infty} \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {R}} \frac {1}{(2 + \omega)} \right]. \tag {6}
$$

This solution is correct only to lowest order in $\mathbf{M} / \mathbf{R}$ , not only because of the terms neglected here, but also because in higher order one would have to account for the changed metric $\mathbf{g}_{\mu \nu}$ due to the mass and use the curved space $\nabla$ in Equation (1) rather than the flat space one.

Solution 13.4. If there is to be no preferred frame for the vacuum, the stress-energy of the vacuum must look the same in any Lorentz frame. The tensor $\eta_{\mu \nu}$ looks the same in any Lorentz frame so that the stress-energy $\rho_{\mathrm{vac}}\eta_{\mu \nu}$ , or in general coordinates $\rho_{\mathrm{vac}}g_{\mu \nu}$ , does not single out any frame. [The uniqueness of $g_{\mu \nu}$ can be seen as follows: if there is no preferred frame there can be no preferred vectors, hence no preferred eigenvectors. This is only possible if all vectors are eigenvectors, but if $S_{\mu}^{\nu}V_{\nu} = KV_{\mu}$ for all $K$ , then $S_{\mu}^{\nu}$ must be proportional to $\delta_{\mu}^{\nu}$ .]

The field equations with vacuum and matter sources are

$$
R ^ {\mu \nu} - \frac {1}{2} g ^ {\mu \nu} R = 8 \pi \left(T _ {\text {m a t t e r}} ^ {\mu \nu} + g ^ {\mu \nu} \rho_ {\text {v a c}}\right).
$$

When we compare this with the field equations including the cosmological term

$$
\mathrm {R} ^ {\mu \nu} - \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {g} ^ {\mu \nu} \mathrm {R} + \Lambda \mathrm {g} ^ {\mu \nu} = 8 \pi \mathrm {T} _ {\text {m a t t e r}} ^ {\mu \nu}
$$

we see that we can identify the cosmological constant with the vacuum energy density according to $\Lambda = 8\pi \rho_{\mathrm{vac}}$ .

If the vacuum contains particles of mass $m$ at a separation of $\lambda \sim \hbar / mc$ then the energy density would be of order $m(mc / \hbar)^3$ . This is of order $10^4$ grams/cc for electrons and $10^{17}$ grams/cc for protons: both absurdly large.

If the vacuum energy is to be considered as arising from the gravitational interaction, of energy $\sim G\frac{m^2}{\lambda}$ , of nearby particles, the equivalent mass density would be $c^{-2}(\mathrm{Gm}^2 /\lambda) / \lambda^3 = \mathrm{Gm}^6 c^2 /\pi^4$ . For protons this is of order $10^{-22}$ grams/cc and for electrons $10^{-41}$ grams/cc. The density of our Galaxy is $10^{-23}$ grams/cc so the $10^{-22}$ grams/cc

associated with protons would have measurable consequences even for Galactic dynamics. The $10^{-41}$ grams/cc associated with electrons, on the other hand, is small even compared with cosmological densities $(10^{-31}$ grams/cc) and would have no measurable effects.

These arguments should be considered only suggestive and somewhat ad hoc. On dimensional grounds $\rho_{\mathrm{vac}}$ must be of form

$$
\frac {\mathrm {m} ^ {4} \mathrm {c} ^ {3}}{\hbar^ {3}} \left(\frac {\mathrm {G m} ^ {2}}{\mathrm {c h}}\right) ^ {\mathrm {n}}.
$$

For $n = 1$ we get somewhat reasonable mass densities. The Zel'dovich argument is an attempt to give a physical justification for $n = 1$ .

Solution 13.5. If we contract the Einstein field equations we get a relation between the trace of $\mathbf{T}_{\mu \nu}$ and the Ricci scalar: $\mathbf{R} = -8\pi \mathbf{T}_{\mu}^{\mu}$ . In the local Lorentz frame in which $\mathbf{T}_{\mu}^{\nu}$ is diagonal, $\mathbf{T}_{\mu}^{\mu} = -\rho + \mathbf{p}_{\mathbf{x}} + \mathbf{p}_{\mathbf{y}} + \mathbf{p}_{\mathbf{z}}$ where $\mathbf{p}_{\mathbf{x}}, \mathbf{p}_{\mathbf{y}}, \mathbf{p}_{\mathbf{z}}$ are the (principal) pressures. For all known equations of state $\rho \geq 3p$ so $\mathbf{T}_{\mu}^{\mu}$ should always be negative and $\mathbf{R}$ should be positive.

The electromagnetic stress-energy is traceless (see Problem 4.16) so $\mathbf{R} = 0$ if the only stress-energy is electromagnetic.

Solution 13.6. Given $\mathbf{T}_{\alpha}^{\beta}$ in his own orthonormal frame, the observer can find its four eigenvalues and eigenvectors:

$$
\mathrm {T} _ {\alpha} ^ {\beta} \mathrm {W} _ {\beta} = \lambda \mathrm {W} _ {\alpha}. \tag {1}
$$

We are given that one eigenvector is timelike. Normalize it so that $\mathbf{W}_{\alpha}\mathbf{W}^{\alpha} = -1$ . If its components are $\mathbf{W}^{\alpha} = (\gamma, \gamma \mathbf{v}^{\mathrm{j}})$ , then perform a Lorentz transformation with velocity $\mathbf{v}^{\mathrm{j}}$ to the rest frame of $\mathbf{W}$ . In that frame $\mathbf{W}^{\hat{\alpha}} = (1, 0)$ and Equation (1) gives $\mathbf{T}^{\hat{0}\hat{0}} = -\lambda_{\text{timelike}} \equiv \rho$ , and $\mathbf{T}^{\hat{0}\hat{\mathbf{k}}} = 0$ . The $3 \times 3$ matrix $\mathbf{T}_{\hat{\mathbf{j}}\hat{\mathbf{k}}}$ can be diagonalized by a spatial rotation, and from Equation (1) we see that the diagonal elements are the remaining

eigenvalues $\lambda_{\mathbf{i}} = \mathbf{p}_{\mathbf{i}}$ with $\mathbf{i} = 1,2,3$ . Now let $\mathbf{u}$ be an arbitrary

4-velocity, with components $\mathbf{u}^{\alpha} = (\overline{\gamma},\overline{\gamma}\overline{\mathbf{v}}^{\mathrm{j}})$ in the frame of the observer for whom $\mathbf{T}^{\alpha \beta}$ is diagonal. The weak energy condition is that $\mathbf{T}^{\alpha \beta}\mathbf{u}_{\alpha}\mathbf{u}_{\beta}\geq 0$ that is

$$
\rho + \bar {\mathrm {v}} _ {1} ^ {2} \mathrm {p} _ {1} + \bar {\mathrm {v}} _ {2} ^ {2} \mathrm {p} _ {2} + \bar {\mathrm {v}} _ {3} ^ {2} \mathrm {p} _ {3} \geq 0 \tag {2}
$$

where $\overline{\mathbf{v}}^{\mathbf{j}}$ is arbitrary and $\overline{\mathbf{v}}^2\leq 1$ . The necessary and sufficient condition that Equation (2) be satisfied is

$$
\rho \geq 0 \quad \rho + p _ {i} \geq 0 \tag {3}
$$

a test which can be made by the original observer after he has solved Equation (1). For the case when $\mathbf{T}^{\alpha \beta}$ has no timelike eigenvector, see Hawking and Ellis, p. 89.

Solution 13.7. For $n = 0$ the statement reads $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{u} \leq 0$ and is trivial. For $n = 1$ the statement is $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{T} \cdot \mathbf{u} \geq 0$ for all timelike $\mathbf{u}$ . This is just the weak energy condition. For $n = 2$ the statement is that $(\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{T}) \cdot (\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{T}) \leq 0$ or that $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{T}$ must be nonspacelike. But for an observer with 4-velocity $\mathbf{u}$ , $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{T} = (-\rho, [\text{Energy flux}]^{\mathrm{i}})$ so, by the dominant energy condition, $|\rho| > |\text{Energy flux}|$ and $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{T}$ is indeed nonspacelike.

Now consider any $n$ and the statement

$$
(- 1) ^ {n} \mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {T} \cdot \mathbf {T} \dots \mathbf {T} \cdot \mathbf {u} \leq 0.
$$

Since $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{T}$ is nonspacelike, the statement for any $\mathfrak{n}$ is equivalent to the statement for $\mathfrak{n}-2$ . But we have shown that the dominant energy condition implies the $\mathfrak{n}=1$ and $\mathfrak{n}=2$ cases so it follows that the dominant energy condition implies the statement for all $\mathfrak{n}$ . The converse follows immediately, any stress energy satisfying the statement for $\mathfrak{n}=1$ and $\mathfrak{n}=2$ satisfies all the requirements of the dominant energy condition.

Solution 13.8. Yes. Write down a metric $\mathbf{g}_{\mu \nu}$ such that $\mathbf{g}_{\mu \nu} = \eta_{\mu \nu}$ for $t < 0$ (this is clearly empty space) and $\mathbf{g}_{\mu \nu} =$ (arbitrary functions of your

choice) for $t \geq 0$ . The only constraint on the arbitrary functions is that they be twice differentiable and match smoothly to the flat-space values at the $t = 0$ surface. Now from this metric $\mathbf{g}_{\mu \nu}$ we can calculate the Ricci tensor and scalar and we can define a tensor $\mathbf{T}_{\mu \nu} \equiv (8\pi)^{-1}(\mathbf{R}_{\mu \nu} - \frac{1}{2}\mathbf{g}_{\mu \nu}\mathbf{R})$ . This tensor is obviously symmetric, and by the contracted Bianchi identities is divergenceless. If we fill spacetime with a stress-energy equal to this, then $\mathbf{g}_{\mu \nu}$ is a solution of the Einstein field equations. (However, for a physically meaningful solution there must be further requirements for $\mathbf{T}_{\mu \nu}$ , e.g. energy density should be everywhere nonnegative. This will not in general be true for the procedure above.)

Solution 13.9. We use Killing's equation $\xi_{(\alpha ;\beta)} = 0$ and the "hypersurface-orthogonal" statement (see Problem 10.8) of staticity $\xi_{[\alpha ;\beta}\xi_{\gamma ]} = 0$ , to derive

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = (\xi_ {[ \alpha ; \beta} \xi_ {\gamma ]}) ^ {j \gamma} \\ = \frac {1}{3} \left(\xi_ {a; \beta} \xi_ {\gamma} + \xi_ {\gamma ; a} \xi_ {\beta} + \xi_ {\beta ; \gamma} \xi_ {a}\right) ^ {i \gamma} \\ = \frac {1}{3} \left(R _ {\lambda \gamma \beta a} \xi^ {\lambda} \xi^ {\gamma} + R _ {\lambda \alpha \gamma} ^ {\gamma} \xi^ {\lambda} \xi_ {\beta} + R _ {\lambda \gamma \beta} ^ {\gamma} \xi^ {\lambda} \xi_ {a}\right) \\ \end{array}
$$

where we have used the result $\xi_{\mu ;a\beta} = \mathbf{R}_{\gamma \beta a\mu}\xi^{\gamma}$ from Problem 10.7. The first term above vanishes by symmetry and the remaining terms give Ricci components, so we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = \xi^ {\lambda} R _ {\lambda [ a} \xi_ {\beta ]} = 8 \pi \xi^ {\lambda} \left(T _ {\lambda [ a} - \frac {1}{2} g _ {\lambda [ a} T\right) \xi_ {\beta ]} \\ = 8 \pi \xi^ {\lambda} \left(\left(\rho + p\right) u _ {\lambda} u _ {[ \alpha} + \frac {1}{2} (\rho - p) g _ {\lambda} [ a)\right) \xi_ {\beta ]}. \\ \end{array}
$$

The last term vanishes trivially so we can conclude $\mathbf{u}_{[\alpha}\xi_{\beta ]} = 0$ and hence $\mathbf{u}\propto \pmb{\xi}$

A less rigorous proof is possible if the equivalent “stationary and time reversible” condition for staticity (see Problem 10.8) is used. In this case we see immediately that if $\mathbf{u}$ is not parallel to $\pmb{\xi}$ then $T^{0i} \neq 0$ in the “time reversible frame.” But this implies $G^{0i} \neq 0$ which is incompatible with time reversibility of the metric.

Solution 13.10. From the Bianchi identities $\mathbf{G}_{; \mu}^{\nu \mu} \equiv 0$ we have

$$
\frac {\partial}{\partial \mathbf {t}} \mathbf {G} ^ {\nu 0} \equiv - \mathbf {G} _ {, \mathbf {i}} ^ {\nu \mathbf {i}} - \mathbf {G} ^ {\sigma \mu} \boldsymbol {\Gamma} _ {\sigma \mu} ^ {\nu} - \mathbf {G} ^ {\nu \sigma} \boldsymbol {\Gamma} _ {\sigma \mu} ^ {\mu}.
$$

Terms on the right cannot involve third derivatives of the metric with respect to time, hence $\mathbf{G}^{\nu 0}$ cannot contain second time derivatives. It follows then that the four equations $\mathbf{G}^{\nu 0} = 8\pi \mathbf{T}^{\nu 0}$ serve as initial value equations, i.e. constraints on the data on the Cauchy hypersurface, while $\mathbf{G}^{ij} = 8\pi \mathbf{T}^{ij}$ are the dynamical equations.

An examination of the constraints on initial data is obscured by the arbitrariness in the metric associated with the arbitrariness in the coordinate system. To eliminate this confusion we can choose at the outset four constraints on the metric. A convenient choice is Gaussian normal coordinates: $\mathbf{g}_{00} = -1$ and $\mathbf{g}_{0\mathrm{i}} = 0$ . We now appear to have six field variables $\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{ij}}$ , six dynamical equations $\mathbf{G}^{\mathrm{ij}} = 8\pi \mathbf{T}^{\mathrm{ij}}$ which we can solve for $\partial^2\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{ij}} / \partial t^2$ , and four initial value equations $\mathbf{G}^{\nu 0} = 8\pi \mathbf{T}^{\nu 0}$ giving relations among the initial data $\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{ij}}$ and $\partial \mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{ij}} / \partial t$ on the initial hypersurface.

We can now differentiate the four initial value equations with respect to time and use the dynamical equations to eliminate terms of the form $\partial^2\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{ij}} / \partial t^2$ . This generates four new relations among the initial value data. [These new relations must be independent of the original four because $\partial G^{\nu 0} / \partial t = 8\pi \partial T^{\nu 0} / \partial t$ , and we can specify $\partial T^{\nu 0} / \partial t$ independently of any previously considered constraints.]

We have now eight constraints on the twelve initial value functions $\mathbf{g}_{ij}$ and $\partial \mathbf{g}_{ij} / \partial t$ . It follows that we can independently specify four initial value functions on the hypersurface.

If we think of these four initial value functions as the values of two field variables and their time derivatives on the cauchy hypersurface, then we can picture the time evolution of the gravitational field as being described by the dynamics of these two fields. For this reason we usually speak of the gravitational field as having two dynamical degrees of freedom.

Solution 13.11. The components of the Landau-Lifschitz pseudotensor are given by the rather awesome expression

$$
\begin{array}{l} (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {a \beta} _ {\mathrm {L} - \mathrm {L}} = (1 6 \pi) ^ {- 1} \left[ g ^ {a \beta}, _ {\lambda} g ^ {\lambda \mu}, _ {\mu} - g ^ {a \lambda}, _ {\lambda} g ^ {\beta \mu}, _ {\mu} + \frac {1}{2} g ^ {a \beta} g _ {\lambda \mu} ^ {\lambda \nu}, _ {\rho} g ^ {\rho \mu}, _ {\nu} \right. \\ - \mathrm {g} ^ {\alpha \lambda} \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \nu} \mathfrak {g} ^ {\beta \nu} _ {, \rho} \mathfrak {g} ^ {\mu \rho} _ {, \lambda} - \mathrm {g} ^ {\beta \lambda} \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \nu} \mathfrak {g} ^ {a \nu} _ {, \rho} \mathfrak {g} ^ {\mu \rho} _ {, \lambda} + \underline {{\mathrm {g} _ {\lambda \mu} \mathrm {g} ^ {\nu \rho} _ {\mathfrak {g}} ^ {a \lambda} _ {, \nu} \mathfrak {g} ^ {\beta \mu}}} _ {, \rho} \\ \left. + \frac {1}{8} \left(2 \mathrm {g} ^ {\alpha \lambda} \mathrm {g} ^ {\beta \mu} - \mathrm {g} ^ {\alpha \beta} \mathrm {g} ^ {\lambda \mu}\right) \left(2 \mathrm {g} _ {\nu \rho} \mathrm {g} _ {\sigma \tau} - \mathrm {g} _ {\sigma \rho} \mathrm {g} _ {\nu \tau}\right) \mathfrak {g} ^ {\nu \tau} _ {, \lambda} \mathfrak {g} ^ {\rho \sigma} _ {, \mu} \right] \\ \end{array}
$$

where

$$
g ^ {a \beta} \equiv (- g) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} g ^ {a \beta}.
$$

For the metric we are considering

$$
g ^ {0 0} = - (1 - 2 \phi) ^ {\frac {3}{2}} (1 + 2 \Phi) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}}, \quad g ^ {i j} = (1 - 4 \Phi^ {2}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \delta^ {i j}, \quad g ^ {0 i} = 0.
$$

Notice that all terms in the expression for $t^{a\beta}$ involve the product of the derivatives of two $g$ 's, so to lowest order $t^{a\beta}$ will go as the product of the first derivative of $\Phi$ . We need to keep therefore, only the terms proportioned to $\Phi_{,i}$ when we calculate the derivatives of the $g$ 's:

$$
{ } ^ { \mathbf { g } } _ { , \mathrm { i } } ^ { 0 0 } \approx 4 \phi _ { , \mathrm { i } } \qquad \mathrm { g } _ { , \mathrm { k } } ^ { \mathrm { i j } } \approx - 4 \phi \phi _ { , \mathrm { k } } \delta ^ { \mathrm { i j } } \approx 0 .
$$

If we keep only the $g_{,i}^{00}$ type terms, only the underlined terms in the expression for $t_{\mathbf{L} - \mathbf{L}}^{\alpha \beta}$ survive and we find

$$
t _ {L - L} ^ {\alpha \beta} = (1 6 \pi) ^ {- 1} \left[ - g _ {, i} ^ {i j} g ^ {\alpha 0} g ^ {\beta 0} g _ {, j} + \frac {1}{8} \left(2 g ^ {\alpha 1} g ^ {\beta m} - g ^ {\alpha \beta} g _ {, j} ^ {l m}\right) g _ {, 1} ^ {0 0} g _ {, m} ^ {0 0} \right].
$$

From this, with $\mathbf{g}^{\alpha \beta} \approx \eta^{\alpha \beta}$ and $\mathfrak{g}_{,i}^{00} = 4\phi_{,i}$ we easily find

$$
\begin{array}{l} t _ {L - L} ^ {0 0} = (1 6 \pi) ^ {- 1} \left[ - \delta^ {i j} g _ {, i} ^ {0 0} g _ {, j} ^ {0 0} + \frac {1}{8} \delta^ {1 m} g _ {, 1} ^ {0 0} g _ {, m} ^ {0 0} \right] = \frac {- 7}{8 \pi} \delta^ {i j} \Phi_ {, i} \Phi_ {, j} \\ t _ {\mathbf {L} - \mathbf {L}} ^ {0 \mathrm {i}} = 0 \\ \end{array}
$$

$$
t _ {\mathrm {L} - \mathrm {L}} ^ {\mathrm {i j}} = (1 6 \pi) ^ {- 1} \left[ \frac {1}{8} \left(2 \delta^ {\mathrm {i l}} \delta^ {\mathrm {j m}} - \delta^ {\mathrm {i j}} \delta^ {\mathrm {l m}} g _ {, 1 g} ^ {0 0}, _ {\mathrm {m}} ^ {0 0} \right] \right. = (4 \pi) ^ {- 1} \left(\Phi_ {, \mathrm {i}} \Phi_ {, \mathrm {j}} - \frac {1}{2} \delta_ {\mathrm {i j}} \Phi_ {, \mathrm {m}} \Phi^ {\mathrm {, m}}\right).
$$

Note that the spatial components $t_{\mathbf{L} - \mathbf{L}}^{\mathbf{ij}}$ agree with the stress tensor given in Problem 12.8.

Solution 13.12. At a given point the value of a scalar is unchanged so

$$
\phi^ {\text {n e w}} (\mathbf {x} ^ {\text {n e w}} (\mathrm {P})) = \phi^ {\text {o l d}} (\mathbf {x} ^ {\text {o l d}} (\mathrm {P})) = \phi^ {\text {o l d}} (\mathbf {x} ^ {\text {n e w}} (\mathrm {P}) - \boldsymbol {\xi} (\mathrm {P}))
$$

$$
\phi^ {\mathrm {n e w}} (\mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {n e w}}) \approx \phi^ {\mathrm {o l d}} (\mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {n e w}}) - \xi^ {a} \phi_ {, a}.
$$

For a vector field

$$
\begin{array}{l} V _ {\mu} ^ {n e w} = \frac {\partial \left(x ^ {o l d}\right) ^ {\nu}}{\partial \left(x ^ {n e w}\right) ^ {\mu}} V _ {\nu} ^ {o l d} \left(x ^ {o l d}\right) \\ = \left(\delta_ {\mu} ^ {\nu} - \xi_ {, \mu} ^ {\nu}\right) \left(V _ {\nu} ^ {\text {o l d}} \left(x ^ {\text {n e w}}\right) - V _ {\nu , \sigma} ^ {\text {o l d}} \xi^ {\sigma}\right) \\ \approx \mathrm {V} _ {\mu} ^ {\mathrm {o l d}} (\mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {n e w}}) - \mathrm {V} _ {\nu} \xi_ {, \mu} ^ {\nu} - \mathrm {V} _ {\mu , \sigma} \xi^ {\sigma}. \\ \end{array}
$$

For a tensor field, similarly

$$
\begin{array}{l} T _ {\mu \nu} ^ {n e w} \left(x ^ {n e w}\right) = \frac {\partial \left(x ^ {o l d}\right) ^ {\alpha}}{\partial \left(x ^ {n e w}\right) ^ {\mu}} \frac {\partial \left(x ^ {o l d}\right) ^ {\beta}}{\partial \left(x ^ {n e w}\right) ^ {\nu}} T _ {\alpha \beta} ^ {o l d} \left(x ^ {o l d}\right) \\ = \left(\delta_ {\mu} ^ {a} - \xi_ {, \mu} ^ {a}\right) \left(\delta_ {\nu} ^ {\beta} - \xi_ {, \nu} ^ {\beta}\right) \left(\mathrm {T} _ {\alpha \beta} ^ {\mathrm {o l d}} \left(\mathrm {x} ^ {\mathrm {n e w}}\right) - \mathrm {T} _ {\alpha \beta , \sigma} \xi^ {\sigma}\right) \\ \approx \mathrm {T} _ {\mu \nu} ^ {\mathrm {o l d}} - \mathrm {T} _ {\mu \beta} \xi_ {, \nu} ^ {\beta} - \mathrm {T} _ {\beta \nu} \xi_ {, \mu} ^ {\beta} - \mathrm {T} _ {\mu \nu , \sigma} \xi^ {\sigma}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Notice that

$$
\xi_ {\mu ; \nu} = \mathsf {g} _ {\mu \sigma} \xi_ {; \nu} ^ {\sigma} = \mathsf {g} _ {\mu \sigma} (\xi_ {, \nu} ^ {\sigma} + \xi^ {\alpha} \Gamma_ {a \nu} ^ {\sigma}) = \mathsf {g} _ {\mu \sigma} \xi_ {, \nu} ^ {\sigma} + \xi^ {\alpha} \Gamma_ {\mu a \nu}
$$

and that

$$
\xi_ {(\mu ; \nu)} = \mathbf {g} _ {\sigma (\mu} \xi_ {, \nu)} ^ {\sigma}) + \xi^ {\alpha} \left(\frac {1}{2} \mathbf {g} _ {\mu \nu , \alpha}\right) .
$$

For the metric tensor then

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \nu} ^ {\text {n e w}} (\mathbf {x} ^ {\text {n e w}}) = \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \nu} ^ {\text {o l d}} (\mathbf {x} ^ {\text {n e w}}) - \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \nu , \sigma} \xi^ {\sigma} - 2 \mathrm {g} _ {\sigma (\mu} \xi^ {\sigma}, \nu) \\ = \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \nu} ^ {\mathrm {o l d}} \left(\mathrm {x} ^ {\mathrm {n e w}}\right) - 2 \xi_ {(\mu ; \nu)} \\ = \eta_ {\mu \nu} + h _ {\mu \nu} ^ {o l d} \left(x ^ {n e w}\right) - 2 \xi_ {(\mu ; \nu)} \\ \end{array}
$$

and we have

$$
\mathrm {h} _ {\mu \nu} ^ {\mathrm {n e w}} (\mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {n e w}}) = \mathrm {h} _ {\mu \nu} ^ {\mathrm {o l d}} (\mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {n e w}}) - 2 \xi_ {(\mu ; \nu)}.
$$

Since, to lowest order, the metric is Minkowskian we can take

$$
\xi_ {(\mu ; \nu)} = \xi_ {(\mu , \nu)} + \mathcal {O} (\xi h).
$$

Solution 13.13. Since the Christoffel symbols $\Gamma_{a\mu \beta} = \frac{1}{2} (\mathtt{h}_{a\beta ,\mu} + \mathtt{h}_{a\mu ,\beta} - \mathtt{h}_{\beta \mu ,a})$ are of order $h$ we may ignore the terms that are products of the $\Gamma$ 's in the formula relating the Riemann components to the $\Gamma$ 's, thus we are left with

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {R} _ {\alpha \mu \beta \nu} \approx \mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \lambda} \left(\Gamma_ {\mu \nu , \beta} ^ {\lambda} - \Gamma_ {\mu \beta , \nu} ^ {\lambda}\right) \approx 2 \Gamma_ {\alpha \mu [ \nu , \beta ]} \\ = \left(\mathrm {h} _ {\alpha [ \nu , \beta ], \mu} - \mathrm {h} _ {\mu [ \nu , \beta ], \alpha}\right) \\ \end{array}
$$

and the formula is verified.

Under a gauge transformation $\mathrm{h}_{\mu \nu} \rightarrow \mathrm{h}_{\mu \nu} - 2\xi_{(\mu, \nu)}$ and

$$
\mathsf {R} _ {a \mu \beta \nu} \to \mathsf {R} _ {a \mu \beta \nu} - \xi_ {a [, \nu , \beta ], \mu} + \xi_ {\mu [, \nu , \beta ], a} - \xi_ {\nu [, a, \beta ], \mu} + \xi_ {\nu [, \mu , \beta ], a}.
$$

Since partial derivatives commute the added terms cancel and the form of the Riemann tensor is unchanged.

Solution 13.14. Under a gauge transformation (see Problem 13.12) the metric perturbations become

$$
\mathsf {h} _ {\alpha \beta} ^ {\prime} = \mathsf {h} _ {\alpha \beta} - 2 \xi_ {(\alpha , \beta)} .
$$

If we contract this we see that the trace $(\mathbf{h} \equiv \mathbf{h}_{\sigma}^{\sigma} \equiv \eta^{\sigma \lambda} \mathbf{h}_{\sigma \lambda})$ transforms

as $\mathbf{h}' = \mathbf{h} - 2\xi_{, \sigma}^{\sigma}$ , (where $\xi^{\sigma} \equiv \eta^{\sigma \lambda} \xi_{\lambda}$ ). The transformation law for $\overline{\mathbf{h}}_{\alpha \beta}$ is then

$$
\begin{array}{l} \overline {{\mathrm {h}}} _ {\alpha} ^ {\prime} \beta = \mathrm {h} _ {\alpha} ^ {\prime} \beta - \frac {1}{2} \eta_ {\alpha} \beta \mathrm {h} ^ {\prime} = \mathrm {h} _ {\alpha} \beta - 2 \xi_ {(\alpha , \beta)} - \frac {1}{2} \eta_ {\alpha} \beta (\mathrm {h} - 2 \xi_ {, \sigma} ^ {\sigma}) \\ = \overline {{\mathsf {h}}} _ {\alpha \beta} - \xi_ {\alpha , \beta} - \xi_ {\beta , \alpha} + \eta_ {\alpha \beta} \xi^ {\sigma} _ {, \sigma} \\ \end{array}
$$

and

$$
\begin{array}{l} \overline {{\mathfrak {h}}} _ {\alpha} ^ {\prime} \beta^ {\prime} ^ {\beta} = \eta^ {\lambda \beta} \overline {{\mathfrak {h}}} _ {\alpha} ^ {\prime} \beta , \lambda = \overline {{\mathfrak {h}}} _ {\alpha} \beta^ {\prime} ^ {\beta} - \xi_ {\alpha ,} \beta^ {\prime} ^ {\beta} - \xi_ {\beta ,} ^ {\prime} ^ {\beta} + \xi_ {\sigma ,} ^ {\prime} ^ {\sigma}, \\ = \bar {h} _ {\alpha} \beta^ {\prime} ^ {\beta} - \xi_ {\alpha}, \beta^ {\prime} ^ {\beta} = \bar {h} _ {\alpha} \beta^ {\prime} ^ {\beta} - \square \xi_ {\alpha}. \\ \end{array}
$$

So we need only choose the four functions $\xi_{\alpha}$ such that they satisfy the familiar wave equation $\square \xi_{\alpha} = \overline{h}_{\alpha} \beta^{\prime \beta}$ .

Such a solution can always be found (e.g. the retarded integral of classical electromagnetic wave theory). The solution, of course, is not unique. We can always add to $\xi_{a}$ any solution of $\square \xi_{a} = 0$ .

Solution 13.15. In Problem 13.13 we found an expression for the linearized Riemann tensor. By contracting this we can get the linearized Ricci tensor

$$
\mathbf {R} _ {\mu \nu} = \frac {1}{2} \left(\mathbf {h} _ {\nu \alpha}, ^ {\alpha}, \mu + \mathbf {h} _ {\mu \alpha}, ^ {\alpha}, \nu - \square \mathbf {h} _ {\mu \nu} - \mathbf {h} _ {, \mu \nu}\right)
$$

where $\mathbf{h} \equiv \mathbf{h}_{\sigma}^{\sigma}$ . In the Lorentz gauge $\overline{\mathbf{h}}_{\alpha \beta}^{\prime \beta} = 0$ implies $\mathbf{h}_{\alpha \beta}^{\prime \beta} = \frac{1}{2} \mathbf{h}_{, \alpha}$ so that

$$
R _ {\mu \nu} = \frac {1}{2} (h _ {, \mu \nu} - \square h _ {\mu \nu} - h _ {, \mu \nu}) = - \frac {1}{2} \square h _ {\mu \nu}
$$

and by contracting again we have $\mathbf{R} = -\frac{1}{2}\square \mathbf{h}$ . The field equations then are

$$
\mathrm {R} _ {\mu \nu} - \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \nu} \mathrm {R} = - \frac {1}{2} \left(\square \mathrm {h} _ {\mu \nu} - \frac {1}{2} \eta_ {\mu \nu} \square \mathrm {h}\right) = - \frac {1}{2} \square \overline {{\mathrm {h}}} _ {\mu \nu}
$$

so that

$$
\square \bar {h} _ {\mu \nu} = - 1 6 \pi T _ {\mu \nu}.
$$

Solution 13.16. For $\overline{\mathbf{h}}_{\mu \nu}$ to satisfy the Lorentz gauge condition (see Problem 13.14), and the linearized field equations, we must have

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = \overline {{\mathrm {h}}} _ {\mu \nu} ^ {\nu} = \operatorname {R e} \left[ \mathrm {i k} ^ {\nu} \mathrm {A} _ {\mu \nu} \mathrm {e} ^ {\mathrm {i k} _ {\alpha} \mathrm {x} ^ {\alpha}} \right] \\ 0 = \overline {{\mathrm {h}}} _ {\mu \nu , \sigma} ^ {\prime \sigma} = \operatorname {R e} \left[ - \mathrm {k} _ {\sigma} \mathrm {k} ^ {\sigma} \mathrm {A} _ {\mu \nu} \mathrm {e} ^ {\mathrm {i k} _ {\alpha} \mathrm {x} ^ {\alpha}} \right]. \\ \end{array}
$$

So the 4-vector $\mathbf{k}$ must be null and must be orthogonal to $\mathbf{A}$ .

The gauge transformation of $\overline{\mathfrak{h}}_{\mu \nu}$ is given in Problem 13.14 as

$$
\overline {{\mathsf {h}}} _ {\mu \nu} ^ {\mathsf {n e w}} = \overline {{\mathsf {h}}} _ {\mu \nu} ^ {\mathsf {o l d}} - 2 \xi_ {(\mu , \nu)} + \eta_ {\mu \nu} \xi_ {, \sigma} ^ {\sigma}.
$$

Clearly the gauge transformation must be of a plane wave $\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i}\mathbf{k}_{\alpha}x^{\alpha}}$ form. With $\xi_{\mu} = -\mathrm{iC}_{\mu}\mathrm{e}^{\mathrm{i}\mathbf{k}_{\alpha}x^{\alpha}}$ we have

$$
\mathbf {A} _ {\mu \nu} ^ {\mathrm {n e w}} = \mathbf {A} _ {\mu \nu} ^ {\mathrm {o l d}} - (\mathbf {C} _ {\mu} \mathbf {k} _ {\nu} + \mathbf {C} _ {\nu} \mathbf {k} _ {\mu}) + \eta_ {\mu \nu} \mathbf {C} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {k} _ {\alpha}.
$$

If this equation is contracted with $\mathbf{k}^{\mu}$ we see that

$$
A _ {\mu \nu} ^ {n e w} k ^ {\mu} = - k ^ {\nu} C _ {\mu} k ^ {\mu} + k ^ {\nu} C ^ {a} k _ {a} = 0
$$

so A remains orthogonal to k. We can now apply the transversetraceless condition, $(\mathbf{A}^{\mathrm{new}})_{\mu}^{\mu} = 0$ and $A_{\mu 0}^{\mathrm{new}} = 0$ , to our equation to get

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = \left(\mathbf {A} ^ {\circ 1 \mathrm {d}}\right) _ {\mu} ^ {\mu} + 2 \mathbf {C} \cdot \mathbf {k} \\ 0 = A _ {\mu 0} ^ {o l d} - \left(C _ {0} k _ {\mu} + C _ {\mu} k _ {0}\right) + \eta_ {\mu 0} C \cdot k. \\ \end{array}
$$

These can be combined into

$$
0 = \mathbf {A} _ {\mu 0} ^ {\mathrm {o l d}} - (\mathbf {C} _ {0} \mathbf {k} _ {\mu} + \mathbf {C} _ {\mu} \mathbf {k} _ {0}) - \frac {1}{2} \eta_ {\mu 0} \mathbf {A} _ {\mu} ^ {\mathrm {o l d}} \mathbf {\Gamma} _ {\mu} ^ {\mu}.
$$

Taking $\mu = 0$ we have $\mathbf{C}_0 = (2\mathbf{k}_0)^{-1}\left(\mathbf{A}_{00}^{old} + \frac{1}{2} (\mathbf{A}^{old})_\mu^\mu\right)$ . The spatial components are then easily found from

$$
0 = A _ {i 0} ^ {\mathrm {o l d}} - \left(C _ {0} k _ {i} + C _ {i} k _ {0}\right).
$$

Solution 13.17. For a beam of light moving in the $x$ -direction nonzero components of the stress-energy tensor are $\mathbf{T}_{00} = \mathbf{T}_{xx} = -\mathbf{T}_{0x}$ . From the form of the linearized field equations

$$
\square \bar {h} _ {\alpha \beta} \equiv \bar {h} _ {\alpha \beta , \gamma}, ^ {\gamma} = - 1 6 \pi T _ {\alpha \beta}
$$

and from the fact that all components of $\mathbf{h}_{\alpha \beta}$ should be retarded integrals over the source, the solutions must be related in the same way that the sources are:

$$
\overline {{\mathbf {h}}} _ {0 0} = \overline {{\mathbf {h}}} _ {\mathbf {x x}} = - \overline {{\mathbf {h}}} _ {0 \mathbf {x}},
$$

with all other components zero. Since the trace of $\overline{\mathbf{h}}_{\alpha \beta}$ is zero, $\overline{\mathbf{h}}_{\alpha \beta} = \mathbf{h}_{\alpha \beta}$ and the above relation is also true if we remove the bars on the h's.

For a photon moving in the $x$ -direction with affine parameter $\lambda$ ,

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathbf {y}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda^ {2}} = - \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {\alpha}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {\beta}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} \Gamma_ {\alpha \beta} ^ {\mathbf {y}} = - \left(\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {t}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda}\right) ^ {2} \left(\Gamma_ {\mathbf {\Lambda} _ {0 0}} ^ {\mathbf {y}} + \Gamma_ {\mathbf {\Lambda} _ {\mathbf {x x}}} ^ {\mathbf {y}} + 2 \Gamma_ {\mathbf {\Lambda} _ {0 \mathbf {x}}} ^ {\mathbf {y}}\right).
$$

But the right hand side vanishes

$$
2 \Gamma^ {\mathbf {y}} _ {0 \mathbf {x}} + \Gamma^ {\mathbf {y}} _ {0 0} + \Gamma^ {\mathbf {y}} _ {\mathbf {x x}} = - \frac {1}{2} \left(h _ {0 0, \mathbf {y}} + h _ {\mathbf {x x}, \mathbf {y}} - 2 h _ {0 \mathbf {x}, \mathbf {y}}\right) = 0
$$

so that $\mathrm{d}^2\mathrm{y} / \mathrm{d}\lambda^2 = 0$ and similarly $\mathrm{d}^2 z / \mathrm{d}\lambda^2 = 0$ . Thus the photon continues to move parallel to the beam of light and therefore two thin beams of light initially moving parallel will not attract each other to the lowest order in each of their mass-energies. (In fact, it turns out that there is no attraction even in higher order.)

Solution 13.18. From linearized theory we know that in the Lorentz gauge

$$
\square \overline {{\mathbf {h}}} _ {\alpha} \beta = - 1 6 \pi \mathbf {T} _ {\alpha} \beta .
$$

Since the source, and hence the field, is stationary this becomes a Poisson equation $\nabla^2\overline{\mathbf{h}}_{\alpha}\beta = -16\pi \mathbf{T}_{\alpha}\beta$ . To calculate $\overline{\mathbf{h}}_{0\mathbf{y}}$ we need $\mathbf{T}_{0\mathbf{y}}$ . For a spherical mass configuration of mass density $\rho$ rotating (in the

positive sense about the $z$ -axis) with angular velocity $\Omega$ , we have $\mathbf{T}_{0y} = r\Omega\rho \sin \theta \cos \phi$ . Notice that $\mathbf{T}_{0y}$ is proportional to the real part of the spherical harmonic $\mathbf{Y}_{11}(\theta, \phi)$ and hence $\overline{\mathbf{h}}_{0y}$ will also be. We therefore write $\overline{\mathbf{h}}_{0y} = \mathbf{f}(\mathbf{r}) \sin \theta \cos \phi$ and the Poisson equation becomes

$$
\frac {1}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}} \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d r}} \left[ \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \right. \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d r}} \left. \mathrm {f (r)} \right] - \frac {2 \mathrm {f (r)}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}} = 1 6 \pi \mathrm {r} \Omega \rho .
$$

If the source is a spherical shell of mass M, radius R, then $\rho = \mathbf{M}\delta (\mathbf{r} - \mathbf{R}) / 4\pi \mathbf{R}^2$ and the equation can easily be integrated to show

$$
f (r) = - \frac {4 M \Omega}{3} \times \left[ \begin{array}{l l l} r / R & f o r & r <   R \\ \left(\frac {R}{r}\right) ^ {2} & f o r & r > R \end{array} \right.
$$

and hence inside the shell

$$
\mathbf {g} _ {0 \mathbf {y}} \approx \mathbf {h} _ {0 \mathbf {y}} = \overline {{{\mathbf {h}}}} _ {0 \mathbf {y}} = - \frac {4 \Omega}{3} \textsf {M} \frac {\mathbf {r}}{R} \sin \theta \cos \phi .
$$

By symmetry

$$
\mathbf {g} _ {\hat {0} \hat {\phi}} = \left. \mathbf {g} _ {0 \mathbf {y}} \right| _ {\phi = 0} = - \frac {4}{3} \Omega \mathrm {M} \left(\frac {\mathbf {r}}{\mathbf {R}}\right) \sin \theta
$$

and

$$
\omega = - \frac {\mathrm {g} _ {0} \phi}{\mathrm {g} _ {\phi} \phi} = - \frac {\mathrm {g} _ {0} \hat {\phi}}{\left(\mathrm {g} _ {\phi} \phi\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} = - \frac {\mathrm {g} _ {0} \hat {\phi}}{\mathrm {r} \sin \theta} = \frac {4 \Omega \mathrm {M}}{3 \mathrm {R}}.
$$

Inside the shell then locally inertial frames will rotate relative to inertial frames far away. Mach's principle states that the inertial properties of spacetime depend on the motion of distant matter. The dragging of inertial frames certainly is an example of the influence of matter on the inertial properties of spacetime. The constancy of $\omega$ suggests that the influence of distant matter on inertial properties might be viewed as a $\frac{1}{r}$ law, so that the inertial properties are not primarily determined by nearby matter, but rather the influence of all the matter in the universe must be considered. (But aside from a few idealized examples such as this one, no one has ever been very successful in “deriving” Mach's principal from the field equations of general relativity.)

Solution 13.19. The equations of motion $\mathbf{T}^{\mu \nu}_{;\nu} = 0$ are clearly gauge invariant, but in the Lorentz gauge (see Problem 13.15) $\mathbf{T}^{\mu \nu}_{,\nu} = -(16\pi)^{-1}\square \overline{\mathbf{h}}^{\mu \nu}_{,\nu} = 0$ rather than $\mathbf{T}_{\mu \nu}^{j\nu} = 0$ . The difference between $\mathbf{T}^{\mu \nu}_{;\nu}$ and $\mathbf{T}^{\mu \nu}_{,\nu}$ is of order $\mathbf{T}\Gamma$ . But both $\mathbf{T}$ and $\Gamma$ are of the order of $\mathfrak{h}_{\alpha \beta}$ so the inconsistent terms are of second order in the deviation of the metric from flatness.

Solution 13.20. By the principle of equivalence, the acceleration of a body is independent of the magnitude of its mass, and depends only on other masses with which it gravitates. Thus a positive mass attracts both positive and negative masses, and a negative mass repels them both.

Here, then, the acceleration of the negative mass is toward the positive mass, and of magnitude $\mathbf{GM} / \ell^2$ . (Since $\ell >> \mathbf{M}$ , we are in the Newtonian limit.) The acceleration of the positive mass is in the same direction and of the same magnitude: the two masses try to chase each other.

The problem is more complicated once the particles are moving, because each sees the retarded field of the other.

Since we are given $\mathbf{M} \ll \ell$ , we shall solve the problem using linearized theory which will be justified as long as their distance in a frame comoving with either remains much greater than $\mathbf{M}$ . The equation

$$
\square \bar {h} _ {\mu \nu} = - 1 6 \pi T _ {\mu \nu} \tag {1}
$$

has the retarded integral solution

$$
\bar {h} _ {\mu \nu} (t, \underline {{x}}) = 4 \int \frac {T _ {\mu \nu} \left(t ^ {\prime} , \underline {{x}} ^ {\prime}\right)}{\left| \underline {{x}} - \underline {{x}} ^ {\prime} \right|} \delta \left(t ^ {\prime} + \left| \underline {{x}} - \underline {{x}} ^ {\prime} \right| - t\right) d ^ {3} x ^ {\prime} d t ^ {\prime}. \tag {2}
$$

In linearized theory we can superpose the $\mathbf{T}_{\mu \nu}$ 's (that is the $\mathbf{h}_{\mu \nu}$ 's) of each particle. For a particle of mass $m$ moving along a world-line

$$
\mathbf {x} ^ {\mu} = \mathbf {z} ^ {\mu} (\tau) \tag {3}
$$

we have

$$
T _ {\mu \nu} (t, \underline {{x}}) = m \int u _ {\mu} u _ {\nu} \delta^ {4} (x - z (r)) d r = m u _ {\mu} u _ {\nu} \delta^ {3} (\underset {\sim} {x} - \underset {\sim} {z} (t)) / u ^ {0} \tag {4}
$$

where we have used $\mathrm{d}r = \mathrm{d}t / \mathrm{u}^0$ to do the integral. Substitute Equation (4) in Equation (2):

$$
\begin{array}{l} \bar {h} _ {\mu \nu} (t, \underline {{x}}) = 4 m \int \frac {u _ {\mu} u _ {\nu} \delta^ {3} [ \underline {{x}} ^ {\prime} - \underline {{z}} (\mathrm {t} ^ {\prime}) ] \delta (\mathrm {t} ^ {\prime} + | \underline {{x}} - \underline {{x}} ^ {\prime} | - t) d ^ {3} x ^ {\prime} d t ^ {\prime}}{u ^ {0} | \underline {{x}} - \underline {{x}} ^ {\prime} |} \\ = 4 \mathrm {m} \int \frac {\mathrm {u} _ {\mu} \left(\mathrm {t} ^ {\prime}\right) \mathrm {u} _ {\nu} \left(\mathrm {t} ^ {\prime}\right) \delta \left(\mathrm {t} ^ {\prime} + \left| \underline {{\mathrm {x}}} - \underline {{z}} \left(\mathrm {t} ^ {\prime}\right) \right| - \mathrm {t}\right) \mathrm {d t} ^ {\prime}}{\mathrm {u} ^ {0} \left(\mathrm {t} ^ {\prime}\right) \left| \underline {{\mathrm {x}}} - \underline {{z}} \left(\mathrm {t} ^ {\prime}\right) \right|}. \tag {5} \\ \end{array}
$$

By symmetry everything depends only on one spatial coordinate, say x.

Let

$$
\mathrm {R} \left(\mathrm {t} ^ {\prime}\right) = | \mathrm {x} - \mathrm {z} \left(\mathrm {t} ^ {\prime}\right) | = \epsilon [ \mathrm {x} - \mathrm {z} \left(\mathrm {t} ^ {\prime}\right) ] \tag {6}
$$

where $\epsilon = \pm 1$ for $x \gtrless z$ . Then

$$
\bar {h} _ {\mu \nu} (t, x) = 4 m \int^ {\infty} \frac {u _ {\mu} u _ {\nu} \delta \left(t ^ {\prime} + R - t\right) d t ^ {\prime}}{u ^ {0} R}. \tag {7}
$$

The 4-velocity $\mathbf{u}$ is determined from the equation of motion

$$
\mathrm {d} \mathbf {u} ^ {\mu} / \mathrm {d} \tau = - \Gamma_ {\alpha \beta} ^ {\mu} \mathbf {u} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {u} ^ {\beta}
$$

i.e.

$$
\mathrm {d} u ^ {\mu} / \mathrm {d} \tau + \eta^ {\mu \nu} u ^ {\alpha} u ^ {\beta} \left(h _ {\nu a, \beta} + h _ {\nu \beta , a} - h _ {a \beta , \nu}\right) / 2 = 0. \tag {8}
$$

Here the $\mathbf{h}_{\alpha \beta}$ of particle 2 evaluated at the position of particle 1 is used to find $\mathfrak{u}^{\mu}$ of particle 1 and vice versa. By symmetry $\mathfrak{u}^{\mathbf{y}} = \mathfrak{u}^{\mathbf{z}} = 0$ for both particles. Write out Equation (8) for $\mu = 0$ and $\mu = \mathbf{x}$ , using $\mathbf{h}_{00} = \mathbf{h}_{\mathbf{xx}}$ (easily verified from Equation (7)):

$$
\mathrm {d} u ^ {0} / \mathrm {d} \tau = \frac {1}{2} h _ {0 0, 0} \left(u ^ {0 ^ {2}} - u ^ {x ^ {2}}\right) + h _ {0 0, x} u ^ {0} u ^ {x} + h _ {0 x, x} u ^ {x ^ {2}} \tag {10}
$$

$$
\mathrm {d} u ^ {x} / \mathrm {d} \tau = \frac {1}{2} h _ {0 0, x} \left(u ^ {0 ^ {2}} - u ^ {x ^ {2}}\right) - h _ {0 x, 0} u ^ {0 ^ {2}} - h _ {0 0, 0} u ^ {0} u ^ {x}. \tag {11}
$$

Now evaluate the derivatives of the $\mathfrak{h}_{\alpha \beta}$ from Equation (7):

$$
\overline {{\mathbf {h}}} _ {0 0, 0} = 4 \mathrm {m} \frac {\partial}{\partial t} \int \frac {\mathbf {u} ^ {0} (\mathbf {t} ^ {\prime}) \delta (\mathbf {t} ^ {\prime} + \mathbf {R} (\mathbf {t} ^ {\prime}) - \mathbf {t}) \mathrm {d t} ^ {\prime}}{\mathbf {R} (\mathbf {t} ^ {\prime})} = - 4 \mathrm {m} \int \frac {\mathbf {u} ^ {0} \delta^ {\prime} (\mathbf {t} ^ {\prime} + \mathbf {R} - \mathbf {t}) \mathrm {d t} ^ {\prime}}{\mathbf {R}}
$$

where $\delta'$ denotes differentiation with respect to the argument of the delta function. Let

$$
\mathbf {f} \left(\mathbf {t} ^ {\prime}\right) = \mathbf {t} ^ {\prime} + \mathbf {R} \left(\mathbf {t} ^ {\prime}\right)
$$

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} f}{\mathrm {d} t ^ {\prime}} = 1 - \epsilon v (t ^ {\prime}), \quad v = \frac {\mathrm {d} z}{\mathrm {d} t ^ {\prime}}.
$$

Then

$$
\begin{array}{l} \overline {{{\mathsf {h}}}} _ {0 0, 0} = - 4 \mathrm {m} \int \frac {\mathsf {u} ^ {0}}{\mathsf {R}} \left[ \frac {\mathsf {d}}{\mathsf {d f}} \delta (\mathsf {f} - \mathsf {t}) \right] \frac {\mathsf {d t} ^ {\prime}}{\mathsf {d f}} \mathsf {d f} \\ = 4 \mathrm {m} \int \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d f}} \left[ \frac {\mathrm {u} ^ {0}}{\mathrm {R}} \frac {\mathrm {d t} ^ {\prime}}{\mathrm {d f}} \right] \delta (\mathrm {f} - \mathrm {t}) \mathrm {d f} \\ = 4 m \left[ \frac {\mathrm {d} t ^ {\prime}}{\mathrm {d} f} \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} t ^ {\prime}} \left(\frac {u ^ {0}}{R} \frac {\mathrm {d} t ^ {\prime}}{\mathrm {d} f}\right) \right] _ {f = t} \\ = 4 \mathrm {m} \left[ \frac {1}{1 - \epsilon \mathrm {v}} \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d t} ^ {\prime}} \frac {\mathrm {u} ^ {0}}{(1 - \epsilon \mathrm {v}) \mathrm {R}} \right] _ {\mathrm {t} ^ {\prime} = \mathrm {t} - \mathrm {R} \left(\mathrm {t} ^ {\prime}\right)}. \tag {12} \\ \end{array}
$$

Since $\mathrm{du}^0/\mathrm{dt}' \sim \mathfrak{m}$ , to linear order in $\mathfrak{m}$ we need not differentiate $\mathbf{u}^0$ and $\mathbf{v}$ in Equation (12). Thus

$$
\overline {{\mathrm {h}}} _ {0 0, 0} = 4 \mathrm {m} \left[ \frac {\epsilon \mathrm {v u} ^ {0}}{(1 - \epsilon \mathrm {v}) ^ {2} \mathrm {R} ^ {2}} \right] _ {\text {r e t .}} \tag {13}
$$

where ret. means evaluated at the retarded time $t' = t - R(t')$ . But

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {v} \left(t ^ {\prime}\right) = \mathbf {v} (t) + (\mathrm {d v} / \mathrm {d t}) \left(t ^ {\prime} - t\right) + \dots \\ = \mathbf {v} (t) + \mathcal {O} (m) \\ \end{array}
$$

and similarly for $\mathbf{u}^0 = (1 - \mathbf{v}^2)^{-\frac{1}{2}}$ and

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {R} \left(\mathrm {t} ^ {\prime}\right) = \epsilon \left[ \mathrm {x} - \mathrm {z} \left(\mathrm {t} ^ {\prime}\right) \right] \\ = \epsilon [ \mathbf {x} - \mathbf {z} (t) - \mathbf {v} (t) (t ^ {\prime} - t) ] + \mathcal {O} (m) \\ = \epsilon [ x - z (t) + v (t) R \left(t ^ {\prime}\right) ], \\ \end{array}
$$

thus

$$
R (t ^ {\prime}) (1 - \epsilon v) = \epsilon [ x - z (t) ].
$$

Since $\overline{\mathbf{h}}_{00,0}$ is being evaluated at the position of the other particle, we have

$$
\overline {{\mathrm {h}}} _ {0 0, 0} = 4 \mathrm {m} \frac {\epsilon \mathrm {v u} ^ {0}}{\left(z _ {1} - z _ {2}\right) ^ {2}} \tag {14}
$$

where the quantities are evaluated at the instantaneous time $t$ . Similarly

$$
\overline {{\mathrm {h}}} _ {0 \mathbf {x}, 0} = \frac {- \mathrm {u} ^ {\mathbf {x}}}{\mathrm {u} ^ {0}} \overline {{\mathrm {h}}} _ {0 0, 0} \tag {15}
$$

$$
\overline {{\mathrm {h}}} _ {\mathbf {x x}, 0} = \left(\frac {\mathrm {u} ^ {\mathbf {x}}}{\mathrm {u} ^ {0}}\right) ^ {2} \overline {{\mathrm {h}}} _ {0 0, 0}. \tag {16}
$$

The spatial derivatives can be evaluated by noting that $\mathbf{x}$ occurs only in $\mathbb{R}$ in Equation (7). Thus

$$
\begin{array}{l} \bar {h} _ {0 0, x} = \epsilon \frac {\partial}{\partial R} 4 m \int \frac {u ^ {0} \delta \left(t ^ {\prime} + R - t\right) d t ^ {\prime}}{R} \\ = 4 \mathrm {m} \epsilon \left[ - \int \frac {\mathrm {u} ^ {0} \delta \left(\mathrm {t} ^ {\prime} + \mathrm {R} - \mathrm {t}\right) \mathrm {d t} ^ {\prime}}{\mathrm {R} ^ {2}} + \int \frac {\mathrm {u} ^ {0} \delta^ {\prime} \left(\mathrm {t} ^ {\prime} + \mathrm {R} - \mathrm {t}\right) \mathrm {d t} ^ {\prime}}{\mathrm {R}} \right] \\ = 4 \mathrm {m} \epsilon \left[ - \int \frac {\mathrm {u} ^ {0}}{\mathrm {R} ^ {2}} \delta (\mathrm {f} - \mathrm {t}) \frac {\mathrm {d t} ^ {\prime}}{\mathrm {d f}} \mathrm {d f} + \int \frac {\mathrm {u} ^ {0}}{\mathrm {R}} \frac {\mathrm {d t} ^ {\prime}}{\mathrm {d f}} \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d f}} \delta (\mathrm {f} - \mathrm {t}) \mathrm {d f} \right] \\ = - 4 \mathrm {m} \epsilon \left[ \frac {\mathrm {u} ^ {0}}{(1 - \epsilon \mathrm {v}) \mathrm {R} ^ {2}} + \frac {1}{(1 - \epsilon \mathrm {v})} \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d t} ^ {\prime}} \left\{\frac {\mathrm {u} ^ {0}}{(1 - \epsilon \mathrm {v}) \mathrm {R}} \right\} \right] _ {\text {r e t .}} \\ = - 4 \mathrm {m} \epsilon \left[ \frac {\mathrm {u} ^ {0}}{(1 - \epsilon \mathrm {v}) \mathrm {R} ^ {2}} + \frac {\mathrm {u} ^ {0} \epsilon \mathrm {v}}{(1 - \epsilon \mathrm {v}) ^ {2} \mathrm {R} ^ {2}} \right] _ {\text {r e t .}} \\ = - \frac {4 \mathrm {m} \epsilon \mathrm {u} ^ {0}}{\left(z _ {1} - z _ {2}\right) ^ {2}}. \tag {17} \\ \end{array}
$$

Similarly

$$
\overline {{h}} _ {0 x, x} = - \frac {u ^ {x}}{u ^ {0}} \bar {h} _ {0 0, x} \tag {18}
$$

$$
\overline {{\mathrm {h}}} _ {\mathbf {x x}, \mathbf {x}} = \left(\frac {\mathrm {u} ^ {\mathbf {x}}}{\mathrm {u} ^ {0}}\right) ^ {2} \overline {{\mathrm {h}}} _ {0 0, \mathbf {x}}. \tag {19}
$$

Note that the gauge condition $\mathbf{h}_{\mu, \alpha}^{\alpha} = 0$ is satisfied by Equations (14)-(19). Write out the equations of motion, Equations (10) and (11), for particle 1 letting $\mathbf{u}^{0} = \gamma$ , $\mathbf{u}^{\mathbf{x}} = \gamma \mathbf{v}$ , $\mathbf{g} = \mathbf{m} / (\mathbf{z}_1 - \mathbf{z}_2)^2$ . Take particle 1 to be the positive mass particle at $z_1 > z_2$ , so that $\epsilon = +1$ and the source mass in Equations (14)-(19) is negative.

$$
\mathrm {d} \gamma_ {1} / \mathrm {d} \tau = - \mathbf {g} \gamma_ {2} v _ {2} \left(1 + v _ {2} ^ {2}\right) + 2 \mathbf {g} \gamma_ {2} \left(1 + v _ {2} ^ {2}\right) \gamma_ {1} ^ {2} v _ {1} - 4 \mathbf {g} \gamma_ {2} v _ {2} \gamma_ {1} ^ {2} v _ {1} ^ {2} \tag {20}
$$

$$
d \left(\gamma_ {1} v _ {1}\right) / d \tau = g \gamma_ {2} \left(1 + v _ {2} ^ {2}\right) - 4 g \gamma_ {2} v _ {2} ^ {2} \gamma_ {1} ^ {2} + 2 g \gamma_ {2} v _ {2} \left(1 + v _ {2} ^ {2}\right) \gamma_ {1} ^ {2} v _ {1}. \tag {21}
$$

Now the equations for $\mathrm{d}\gamma_{2} / \mathrm{d}\tau_{2}$ and $\mathrm{d}(\gamma_2\mathbf{v}_2) / \mathrm{d}\tau_2$ have the same right-hand sides as Equations (20)-(21), with $\mathbf{v}_2\leftrightarrow \mathbf{v}_1$ . ( $\varepsilon \mathfrak{m}$ keeps the same sign.) Since $\mathbf{v}_1 = \mathbf{v}_2$ at $t = 0$ , then $\mathbf{v}_1 = \mathbf{v}_2$ for all t. Thus $z_{1} - z_{2}$ is constant for all t, i.e. $z_{1} - z_{2} = \ell$ . We can drop the subscripts 1 and 2 in Equations (20) and (21) and get, after some simplification

$$
\mathrm {d} \gamma / \mathrm {d} \tau = \mathrm {g v} / \gamma \tag {22}
$$

$$
\mathrm {d} (\gamma \mathrm {v}) / \mathrm {d} \tau - \mathrm {g} / \gamma \tag {23}
$$

These equations are not independent, because of the relation $\gamma = (1 - v^2)^{-\frac{1}{2}}$ . We find then that the differential equation for the motion is

$$
\mathrm {d} \mathbf {v} / \mathrm {d} \tau = \mathbf {g} \left(1 - \mathbf {v} ^ {2}\right) ^ {2}, \tag {24}
$$

and hence

$$
2 \mathrm {g} \tau = \mathrm {v} / \left(1 - \mathrm {v} ^ {2}\right) + \tanh  ^ {- 1} \mathrm {v} \tag {25}
$$

where $\tau = 0$ when $\mathbf{v} = 0$ . Thus

$$
\frac {d z}{d v} = \frac {d z}{d t} \frac {d t}{d r} \frac {d r}{d v} = \frac {v}{g} (1 - v ^ {2}) ^ {- 5 / 2}
$$

implies

$$
z _ {2} = \frac {1}{3 \mathrm {g}} \left[ (1 - v ^ {2}) ^ {- \frac {3}{2}} - 1 \right] \tag {26}
$$

$$
\mathbf {z} _ {1} = \mathbf {z} _ {2} + \ell
$$

and similarly

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} t}{\mathrm {d} v} = \frac {\mathrm {d} t}{\mathrm {d} r} \frac {\mathrm {d} r}{\mathrm {d} v} = \frac {1}{g} (1 - v ^ {2}) ^ {- 5 / 2} \tag {27}
$$

implies

$$
t = \frac {v}{g} \left[ \left(1 - v ^ {2}\right) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} + \frac {v ^ {2}}{3} \left(1 - v ^ {2}\right) ^ {- \frac {3}{2}} \right]. \tag {28}
$$

Equations (26) and (28) are parametric equations for the trajectories with $\mathbf{v}$ as parameter. Note that while the coordinate separation of the particles remains constant, the proper separation as measured by an observer comoving with one of the particles increases, approximately as $\gamma \ell$ . Proof: Suppose particle 1 has velocity $\mathbf{v}$ at some instant $t_1$ at position $z_1(t_1)$ . The Lorentz transformation to the locally comoving inertial frame is

$$
\mathbf {x} ^ {\prime} = \gamma [ \mathbf {x} - \mathbf {z} _ {1} (t _ {1}) - \mathbf {v} (t - t _ {1}) ]
$$

$$
\mathbf {t} ^ {\prime} = \gamma \left[ \mathbf {t} - \mathbf {t _ {1}} \right. - \mathbf {v} \left\{\mathbf {x} - \mathbf {z _ {1}} (\mathbf {t _ {1}}) \right\} \mathrm {.}
$$

Thus the trajectory of particle 2 in these coordinates is $z_2'(t_2')$ where

$$
z _ {2} ^ {\prime} = \gamma \left[ z _ {2} (t _ {2}) - z _ {1} (t _ {1}) - v (t _ {2} - t _ {1}) \right]
$$

$$
\mathsf {t} _ {2} ^ {\prime} = \gamma \left[ \mathsf {t} _ {2} - \mathsf {t} _ {1} \right. - \mathrm {v} \{\mathsf {z} _ {2} (\mathsf {t} _ {2}) - \mathsf {z} _ {1} (\mathsf {t} _ {1}) \} ].
$$

The distance to particle 2 is $-z_2'(t_2' = 0)$ . Let

$$
\beta = \mathbf {z} _ {2} (\mathfrak {t} _ {2}) - \mathbf {z} _ {1} (\mathfrak {t} _ {1}).
$$

Now $t_2' = 0$ implies

$$
\mathsf {t} _ {2} = \mathsf {t} _ {1} + \mathsf {v} \beta
$$

so

$$
\begin{array}{l} \beta = z _ {2} \left(t _ {1} + v \beta\right) - z _ {1} \left(t _ {1}\right) \\ \approx \left. z _ {2} (t _ {1}) + v \beta \mathrm {d} z _ {2} / \mathrm {d} t \right| _ {t = t _ {1}} + \frac {v ^ {2} \beta^ {2}}{z} \left. \frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} z _ {2}}{\mathrm {d} t ^ {2}} \right| _ {t = t _ {1}} - z _ {1} (t _ {1}) \\ = - \ell + v ^ {2} \beta + \mathcal {O} (m / L). \\ \end{array}
$$

Solve for $\beta$ :

$$
\beta = \frac {- \ell}{(1 - v ^ {2})}.
$$

Thus

$$
- \mathbf {z} _ {2} ^ {\prime} (\mathbf {t} _ {2} ^ {\prime} = 0) = - \gamma (\beta - \mathbf {v} ^ {2} \beta) \approx \gamma \ell .
$$

# CHAPTER 14: SOLUTIONS

Solution 14.1. First we determine what is the appropriate $\mathbf{T}^{\mu \nu}$ for a point particle, say of mass m. In the momentarily comoving frame it should have only a $\mathbf{T}^{00}$ component, and this should be a $\delta$ -function of

position, so

$$
\mathbf {T} ^ {\mu \nu} \propto \int \delta^ {4} (\mathbf {x} ^ {\alpha} - \mathbf {x} ^ {\alpha} (\tau)) \mathbf {u} ^ {\mu} \mathbf {u} ^ {\nu} d \tau
$$

where $\mathbf{x}^{\alpha}(r)$ is the particle's trajectory in spacetime as a function of its proper time $r$ . Now we want $\mathbf{T}^{\mu \nu}$ to transform like a tensor. The product $\mathbf{u}^{\mu}\mathbf{u}^{\nu}$ already transforms as a tensor, but $\delta^{4}(\mathbf{x}^{\alpha} - \mathbf{x}^{\alpha}(r))$ is not a scalar; $\delta^{4}(\mathbf{x}^{\alpha} - \mathbf{x}^{\alpha}(r))(-\mathbf{g})^{-\frac{1}{2}}$ , however, is a scalar:

$$
1 = \operatorname {s c a l a r} = \int \delta^ {4} \left(\mathrm {x} ^ {a} - \mathrm {x} ^ {a} (\tau)\right) \mathrm {d} ^ {4} \mathrm {x} = \int \frac {\delta^ {4}}{(- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d} ^ {4} \mathrm {x}. \tag {1}
$$

Thus, normalizing to mass $\mathfrak{m}$ , we have

$$
\mathrm {T} ^ {\mu \nu} = \mathrm {m} \int \delta^ {4} \left(\mathrm {x} ^ {\alpha} - \mathrm {x} ^ {\alpha} (\tau)\right) \frac {\mathrm {u} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {u} ^ {\nu}}{(- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \mathrm {d} \tau \equiv \int \rho \mathrm {u} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {u} ^ {\nu} \mathrm {d} \tau \tag {2}
$$

where $\rho$ contains all the terms except the u's. Now

$$
0 = \mathrm {T} ^ {\mu \nu} _ {; \nu} = \int [ (\rho \mathrm {u} ^ {\nu}) _ {; \nu} \mathrm {u} ^ {\mu} + (\rho \mathrm {u} ^ {\nu}) \mathrm {u} _ {; \nu} ^ {\mu} ] \mathrm {d} \tau . \tag {3}
$$

Dotting with $\mathfrak{u}^{\mu}$ gives

$$
0 = \int \left[ - \left(\rho \mathbf {u} ^ {\nu}\right) _ {; \nu} + \rho \left(\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {u}\right) \cdot \mathbf {u} \right] d \tau . \tag {4}
$$

The second term vanishes because 4-acceleration $\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{u}$ is orthogonal to 4-velocity. We conclude therefore that the first term vanishes and hence, from Equation (3)

$$
0 = \int (\rho u ^ {\nu}) u _ {; \nu} ^ {\mu} d \tau .
$$

This implies that $\mathfrak{u}^{\nu}\mathfrak{u}_{;\nu}^{\mu} = 0$ wherever the (delta function) $\rho$ is nonzero, that is, wherever the particle is! But $\mathfrak{u}^{\nu}\mathfrak{u}_{;\nu}^{\mu} = 0$ is just the geodesic equation.

Solution 14.2. If a system is in thermal equilibrium, then no net energy

![](images/56ae6dfa1bda01950cfca030e9030893277ae15542f7781bada7001c3d67c165.jpg)

will flow between two locations A and B if they are allowed to exchange energy. In particular, imagine a "light pipe" between the two locations which transmits a thermal flux of photons. Since the pipe and the system are static, there is no change in a photon's energy when it reflects internally in the pipe. There is a change in energy between A and B due to

the gravitational redshift: $(\mathrm{h}\nu)_{\mathrm{A}} / (\mathrm{h}\nu)_{\mathrm{B}} = (-\mathrm{g}_{00}^{\mathrm{B}})^{\frac{1}{2}} / (-\mathrm{g}_{00}^{\mathrm{A}})^{\frac{1}{2}}$ . Now use the fact that a black-body intensity $\mathbf{B}_{\nu}(\mathbf{T}_{\mathbf{A}})$ at A is changed by any kind of redshift to precisely another black-body spectrum with a temperature $\mathbf{T}_{\mathbf{B}} = [(\mathrm{h}\nu)_{\mathbf{B}} / (\mathrm{h}\nu)_{\mathbf{A}}]\mathbf{T}_{\mathbf{A}}$ . If thermal equilibrium holds, this must be the ambient temperature at B, so that the fluxes up the pipe and down the pipe are identical. Thus $\mathbf{T}_{\mathbf{B}} = [(-\mathrm{g}_{00}^{\mathbf{A}})^{\frac{1}{2}} / (-\mathrm{g}_{00}^{\mathbf{B}})^{\frac{1}{2}}]\mathbf{T}_{\mathbf{A}}$ , and

$$
\mathrm {T} (- \mathrm {g} _ {0 0}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} = \text {c o n s t a n t}
$$

throughout the system.

Solution 14.3. For a perfect fluid the stress-energy tensor is $\mathbf{T}^{\mu \nu} = (\rho + p) \mathbf{u}^{\mu} \mathbf{u}^{\nu} + p \mathbf{g}^{\mu \nu}$ , thus the equations of motions read

$$
0 = \mathrm {T} _ {; \nu} ^ {\mu \nu} = (\rho + \mathrm {p}) _ {; \nu} \mathrm {u} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {u} ^ {\nu} + (\rho + \mathrm {p}) \left(\mathrm {u} _ {; \nu} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {u} ^ {\nu} + \mathrm {u} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {u} _ {; \nu} ^ {\nu}\right) + \mathrm {p} _ {, \nu} \mathrm {g} ^ {\mu \nu}.
$$

To get the Euler equation, project this equation perpendicular to $\mathbf{u}$ using the projection tensor

$$
\mathrm {P} _ {\alpha \beta} = \mathrm {u} _ {\alpha} \mathrm {u} _ {\beta} + \mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \beta}.
$$

The result is

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = \mathrm {P} _ {\alpha \mu} \mathrm {T} _ {\nu} ^ {\mu \nu} \\ = 0 + P _ {a \mu} (\rho + p) u ^ {\mu} _ {; \nu} u ^ {\nu} + 0 + p _ {, \nu} g ^ {\mu \nu} P _ {a \mu} \\ = (\rho + p) u _ {\alpha ; \nu} u ^ {\nu} + p, a + p, \nu u ^ {\nu} u _ {\alpha} \\ \end{array}
$$

which is the Euler equation.

To take the Newtonian limit (see Solution 14.8), write the Euler equation as

$$
\rho_ {0} (1 + \pi + p / \rho_ {0}) (u _ {j, \nu} u ^ {\nu} - \Gamma_ {\nu j} ^ {a} u _ {a} u ^ {\nu}) = - p _ {, j} - u _ {j} d p / d \tau .
$$

Set $\mathbf{u}^0 = 1$ , $\mathbf{u}_0 = -1$ , $\mathbf{u}_j = \mathbf{v}_j$ and get

$$
\rho_ {0} \left(\mathrm {d v} _ {\mathrm {j}} / \mathrm {d} \tau + \Gamma_ {0 \mathrm {j}} ^ {0}\right) \approx - \mathrm {p},
$$

But

$$
\Gamma_ {0 j} ^ {0} \approx - \Gamma_ {0 0 j} = - \frac {1}{2} g _ {0 0, j} \approx \phi_ {, j}.
$$

Thus

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {v} _ {\mathrm {j}}}{\mathrm {d} \tau} = - \phi_ {, \mathrm {j}} - \frac {1}{\rho_ {0}} \mathbf {p} _ {, \mathrm {j}}.
$$

Solution 14.4. The Euler equation for a perfect fluid is

$$
(\rho + p) \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {u} = - \nabla p - \mathbf {u} \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} p.
$$

Hydrostatic equilibrium implies the existence of a time Killing vector $\pmb{\xi}$ . The fluid 4-velocity, according to Problem 13.9, must be parallel to this Killing vector, so

$$
\mathbf {u} = \boldsymbol {\xi} / | \boldsymbol {\xi} |.
$$

(In components, this means that only $\mathbf{u}^{\mathbf{i}} = 0$ , where $\pmb{\xi} = \partial/\partial t$ .) From Problem 10.14 we know

$$
\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {u} = \frac {1}{2} \nabla \log | \boldsymbol {\xi} \cdot \boldsymbol {\xi} |.
$$

We also know that $\partial \mathfrak{p} / \partial t = 0$ , or $\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathfrak{p}\propto \nabla_{\boldsymbol{\xi}}\mathfrak{p} = 0$ , and therefore

$$
\nabla \mathfrak {p} = - (\rho + \mathfrak {p}) \nabla \log | \boldsymbol {\xi} \cdot \boldsymbol {\xi} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}.
$$

But $\pmb{\xi} \cdot \pmb{\xi} = (\partial/\partial t) \cdot (\partial/\partial t) = \mathbf{g}_{00}$ , so $|\pmb{\xi} \cdot \pmb{\xi}|^{\frac{1}{2}} = (-\mathbf{g}_{00})^{\frac{1}{2}}$ , from which the result follows.

In the Newtonian limit, $\mathfrak{p} \ll \rho$ and $\mathbf{g}_{00} \approx -(1 + 2\Phi)$ where the Newtonian potential $\Phi \ll 1$ . Thus

$$
\frac {\partial \mathrm {p}}{\partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\nu}} \approx - \rho \frac {\partial}{\partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\nu}} \frac {1}{2} \log (1 + 2 \Phi) \approx - \rho \frac {\partial \Phi}{\partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\nu}}..
$$

Solution 14.5. Using $\mathfrak{p} = \frac{1}{3}\rho$ in the equation of hydrostatic equilibrium (Problem 14.4) $\mathfrak{p}_{,\lambda} = (\rho +\mathfrak{p})\left[\log (-\mathfrak{g}_{00})^{\frac{1}{2}}\right]_{,\lambda}$ we get

$$
- \log (- g _ {0 0}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} = \frac {1}{4} \int \frac {d \rho}{\rho}.
$$

This integrates to $\rho = \text{constant} \times (-\mathbf{g}_{00})^{-2}$ . As long as $\mathbf{g}_{00}$ is finite, therefore, $\rho$ cannot vanish and thus there is no free surface.

Solution 14.6. A static uniform gravitational field has a line element

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = \mathrm {g} _ {\mathrm {t t}} \mathrm {d t} ^ {2} + \mathrm {g} _ {\mathrm {z z}} \mathrm {d z} ^ {2} + \mathrm {d x} ^ {2} + \mathrm {d y} ^ {2} \tag {1}
$$

where $\mathbf{g}_{tt}$ and $\mathbf{g}_{zz}$ depend only on $z$ . The nonzero Christoffel symbols are easily computed (Problem 7.6). The weight as measured by a scale at $z = 0$ is $\mathbf{W}(0)$ , where

$$
W (z) = \int T ^ {\widehat {z} \widehat {z}} d x d y = \int g _ {z z} T ^ {z z} d x d y. \tag {2}
$$

We wish to show that $W(0)$ depends only on $T^{\widehat{tt}} = g_{tt}T^{tt}$ and on no other $T^{\widehat{a}\widehat{\beta}}$ . We have

$$
0 = \mathrm {T} ^ {\alpha \beta}; \beta = | \mathrm {g} | ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \left(| \mathrm {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {T} ^ {\alpha \beta}\right), \beta + \Gamma^ {\alpha} _ {\beta \gamma} \mathrm {T} ^ {\beta \gamma}. \tag {3}
$$

Take $\alpha = t$ and integrate over $x, y$ , using the fact that $g_{\alpha \beta}$ is independent of $x, y$ and $t$ :

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = \int T ^ {t t} _ {, t} d x d y + \int T ^ {t x} _ {, x} d x d y + \int T ^ {t y} _ {, y} d x d y + | g | ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} (| g | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \int T ^ {t z} d x d y) _ {, z} \\ + 2 \Gamma^ {t} _ {t z} \int T ^ {t z} d x d y. \tag {4} \\ \end{array}
$$

The $1^{\text{st}}$ term vanishes because $\mathbf{T}^{\text{tt}}$ is time-independent. The $2^{\text{nd}}$ and $3^{\text{rd}}$ terms give terms proportional to $\mathbf{T}^{\text{tx}}$ and $\mathbf{T}^{\text{ty}}$ on the boundary of the integration region. These vanish for a boundary outside the container.

Equation (4) can be put in the form

$$
0 = | \mathrm {g} | ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {g} _ {\mathrm {t t}} ^ {- 1} \left(| \mathrm {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {g} _ {\mathrm {t t}} \int \mathrm {T} ^ {\mathrm {t z}} \mathrm {d x} \mathrm {d y}\right) _ {, z}. \tag {5}
$$

Thus $|g|^{\frac{1}{2}} g_{tt} \int T^{tz} \, dx \, dy$ is independent of $z$ . Since this term vanishes above the top of the container, it vanishes everywhere.

Now put $a = z$ in Equation (3) and integrate over $x$ and $y$ :

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = \int T ^ {z t}, _ {t} d x d y + \int T ^ {z x}, _ {x} d x d y + \int T ^ {z y}, _ {y} d x d y + | g | ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} (| g | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \int T ^ {z z} d x d y), _ {z} \\ + \Gamma^ {z} _ {t t} \int \mathrm {T} ^ {t t} d x d y + \Gamma^ {z} _ {z z} \int \mathrm {T} ^ {z z} d x d y. \tag {6} \\ \end{array}
$$

The $1^{\text{st}}$ term vanishes as above, while the $2^{\text{nd}}$ and $3^{\text{rd}}$ give boundary contributions which vanish. Using Equation (2), we find

$$
\left(\left| \mathbf {g} _ {\mathrm {t t}} \right| ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {W}\right) _ {, z} = \left| \mathbf {g} _ {\mathrm {t t}} \right| ^ {\frac {1}{2}} _ {, z} \int \mathrm {T} ^ {\widehat {\mathrm {t t}}} \mathrm {d x} \mathrm {d y}. \tag {7}
$$

Integrate Equation (7) from $z = 0$ to the top of the container, and use the fact that $W(top) = 0$ to get

$$
W (0) = \left| g _ {t t} \right| _ {z = 0} ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \int_ {0} ^ {\text {t o p}} d z \int d x d y \left| g _ {t t} \right| ^ {\frac {1}{2}}, z T ^ {\hat {t} \hat {t}}, \tag {8}
$$

which depends only on the component $\mathbf{T}^{\widehat{\mathbf{t}}\mathbf{t}}$ . (Solution due to W. Unruh.)

Solution 14.7. Since the gravitational field is stationary, there is a time-Killing vector $\pmb{\xi} = \partial/\partial t$ . Dot this into the Euler equation (Problem 14.3) for the flow of a perfect gas:

$$
(\rho + \mathrm {p}) \boldsymbol {\xi} \cdot \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {u} = - \boldsymbol {\xi} \cdot \nabla \mathrm {p} - \boldsymbol {\xi} \cdot \mathbf {u} \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {p}. \tag {1}
$$

But

$$
\boldsymbol {\xi} \cdot \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {u} = \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} (\boldsymbol {\xi} \cdot \mathbf {u}) - (\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \boldsymbol {\xi}) \cdot \mathbf {u} = \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} (\boldsymbol {\xi} \cdot \mathbf {u})
$$

since by Killing's equation $\nabla \pmb{\xi}$ is antisymmetric. Also, $\pmb{\xi} \cdot \nabla \mathbf{p} = \partial \mathbf{p} / \partial t = 0$ for stationary flow. Putting $\mathbf{u}_0 = \mathbf{u} \cdot \pmb{\xi}$ , we find $(\rho + \mathfrak{p}) \mathrm{d}\mathbf{u}_0 / \mathrm{d}\tau = \mathbf{u}_0 \mathrm{d}\mathbf{p} / \mathrm{d}\tau$ [Equation 2]. For adiabatic flow of a perfect fluid, the first law of thermodynamics (Problem 5.19) is $\mathrm{d}\rho = (\rho + \mathfrak{p}) \mathrm{d}\mathfrak{n} / \mathfrak{n}$ . [Equation 3]. From Equations (2) and (3)

$$
\frac {d u _ {0}}{u _ {0}} = \frac {d n}{n} - \frac {d (\rho + p)}{\rho + p}
$$

and hence $\mathbf{u}_0 = \text{constant} \times \frac{\mathbf{n}}{\rho + \mathbf{p}}$ .

Solution 14.8. The Newtonian limit of relativistic hydrodynamics is obtained by choosing a global, nearly-Lorentz, frame in which

$$
\begin{array}{l} \rho = \rho_ {0} (1 + \pi), \quad \pi \ll 1 \\ \mathbf {g} _ {0 0} = - (1 + 2 \phi), \quad | \phi | \ll 1 \\ p / \rho_ {0} \stackrel {\cdot} {<  } <   1, \quad v ^ {2} \ll 1. \\ \end{array}
$$

Here $\rho_0 = \mathfrak{nm}_{\mathbf{B}}$ is the rest-mass density ( $\mathfrak{m}_{\mathbf{B}} = \text{mean baryon rest mass}$ ), $\pi$ is the specific internal energy, $\phi$ is the Newtonian potential and $\mathbf{v}$ is 3-velocity of the fluid. Since

$$
\mathbf {u} ^ {\widehat {0}} = \left(1 - \mathbf {v} ^ {2}\right) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \approx 1 + \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {v} ^ {2},
$$

and

$$
\mathsf {u} _ {0} = \left(- \mathsf {g} _ {0 0}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathsf {u} ^ {\hat {0}} \approx 1 + \frac {1}{2} \mathsf {v} ^ {2} + \phi ,
$$

the Bernoulli equation becomes

$$
\left(1 + \frac {1}{2} v ^ {2} + \phi\right) = \frac {\text {c o n s t a n t} \times n}{\rho_ {0} (1 + \pi + p / \rho_ {0})}
$$

that is

$$
\left(1 + \frac {1}{2} v ^ {2} + \phi\right) \left(1 + \pi + p / \rho_ {0}\right) = c o n s t a n t
$$

and finally

$$
\frac {1}{2} v ^ {2} + \phi + \pi + p / \rho_ {0} = c o n s t a n t.
$$

# Solution 14.9.

(a) The condition that $\pmb{\xi}$ be a connecting vector between the world lines of neighboring particles can be written

$$
\mathcal {L} _ {\mathbf {u}} \boldsymbol {\xi} = a \mathbf {u} \tag {1}
$$
