where $\alpha$ is a scalar function. In Problem 8.14, $\alpha$ was zero since $\pmb{\xi}$ connected events of equal proper time; here we find $\alpha$ from the condition $\pmb{\xi}^{\hat{0}} = \pmb{\xi} \cdot \mathbf{u} = 0$ along the world lines:

$$
0 = \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} (\boldsymbol {\xi} \cdot \mathbf {u}) = \boldsymbol {\xi} \cdot \mathbf {a} + \mathbf {u} \cdot \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \boldsymbol {\xi}. \tag {2}
$$

Compare this with Equation (1) dotted with $\mathbf{u}$ :

$$
\mathbf {u} \cdot (\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \boldsymbol {\xi} - \nabla_ {\boldsymbol {\xi}} \mathbf {u}) = - a
$$

and find $\pmb{a} = \pmb{\xi} \cdot \mathbf{a}$ . (Recall that $\mathbf{u} \cdot \nabla_{\pmb{\xi}} \mathbf{u} = \frac{1}{2} \nabla_{\pmb{\xi}} (\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{u}) = 0$ .) Thus we have

$$
\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \boldsymbol {\xi} = \nabla_ {\boldsymbol {\xi}} \mathbf {u} + \cdot (\boldsymbol {\xi} \cdot \mathbf {a}) \mathbf {u}. \tag {3}
$$

The condition for rigid body motion, $\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}(\pmb {\xi}\cdot \pmb {\xi}) = 0$ , is equivalent to $\pmb {\xi}\cdot \nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\pmb {\xi} = 0$ , or by Equation (3) to

$$
\xi^ {\alpha} \xi^ {\beta} (\mathsf {u} _ {\alpha ; \beta} + \mathsf {u} _ {\alpha} \mathsf {a} _ {\beta}) = 0.
$$

Since this must be true for any $\pmb{\xi}$ it follows that

$$
\mathrm {u} _ {(a; \beta)} + \mathrm {u} _ {(a a \beta)} = 0
$$

and hence (see Problem 5.18 for definitions) that

$$
\sigma_ {\alpha \beta} + \frac {1}{3} \theta \mathrm {P} _ {\alpha \beta} = \mathrm {u} _ {(\alpha ; \beta)} + \mathrm {u} _ {(\alpha} \mathrm {a} _ {\beta)} = 0.
$$

Rigid body motion therefore can occur if and only if $\sigma_{\alpha \beta} = \theta = 0$

(b) The symmetry $\sigma^{\alpha\beta} = \sigma^{\beta\alpha}$ reduces the condition $\sigma^{\alpha\beta} = 0$ to 10 equations. The 5 identities $\mathbf{u}^{\alpha}\sigma_{\alpha\beta}$ and $\mathbf{P}^{\alpha\beta}\sigma_{\alpha\beta} = \sigma_{\alpha}^{\alpha} = 0$ leave 5 independent equations. With the condition $\theta = 0$ , we have 6 independent constraints altogether.

The rigid-body conditions do not constrain $\mathfrak{a}^{\alpha}$ or $\omega^{\alpha \beta}$ . Each of these has 3 independent components ( $\mathfrak{a}^{\alpha}\mathfrak{u}_{\alpha} = 0$ , $\omega^{\alpha \beta} = -\omega^{\beta \alpha}$ ) so there are 6 degrees of freedom as in nonrelativistic rigid-body motion.

Solution 14.10. From the definition of $\theta = \nabla \cdot \mathbf{u}$ we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {\mathrm {d} \theta}{\mathrm {d} \tau} = u ^ {\beta} \left(u _ {; \alpha} ^ {\alpha}\right); \beta = u ^ {\beta} u _ {; \alpha \beta} ^ {\alpha} = u ^ {\beta} \left(u _ {; \beta \alpha} ^ {\alpha} - R ^ {\alpha} \beta \alpha \gamma u ^ {\gamma}\right) \tag {1} \\ = \mathfrak {u} ^ {\beta} \mathfrak {u} _ {; \beta \alpha} ^ {\alpha} - \mathrm {R} _ {\beta \gamma} \mathfrak {u} ^ {\beta} \mathfrak {u} ^ {\gamma} . \\ \end{array}
$$

But

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {u} _ {; \beta \alpha} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {u} ^ {\beta} = (\mathrm {u} _ {; \beta} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {u} ^ {\beta}) _ {; \alpha} - \mathrm {u} _ {; \beta} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {u} ^ {\beta} _ {; \alpha} \\ = \mathrm {a} _ {; \alpha} ^ {\alpha} - \left(\omega_ {\beta} ^ {\alpha} + \sigma_ {\beta} ^ {\alpha} + \frac {1}{3} \theta \mathrm {P} _ {\beta} ^ {\alpha} - \mathrm {a} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {u} _ {\beta}\right) \\ \times \left(\omega^ {\beta} _ {\alpha} + \sigma^ {\beta} _ {\alpha} + \frac {1}{3} \theta \mathrm {P} ^ {\beta} _ {\alpha} - \mathrm {a} ^ {\beta} \mathrm {u} _ {\alpha}\right) \tag {2} \\ \end{array}
$$

where we have used Problem 5.18. Using the symmetry properties of $\omega_{\alpha \beta}$ , $\sigma_{\alpha \beta}$ , and $\mathbf{P}_{\alpha \beta}$ and the fact that they are orthogonal to $\mathbf{u}$ , Equations (1) and (2) imply

$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {\mathrm {d} \theta}{\mathrm {d} r} = \mathsf {a} _ {; \alpha} ^ {\alpha} - \omega^ {\alpha} \beta^ {\omega \beta} _ {\alpha} - \sigma^ {\alpha} _ {\beta} \sigma^ {\beta} _ {\alpha} - \frac {1}{3} \theta^ {2} - \mathrm {R} _ {\alpha \beta} \mathrm {u} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {u} ^ {\beta} \\ = a _ {; \alpha} ^ {\alpha} + 2 \omega^ {2} - 2 \sigma^ {2} - \frac {1}{3} \theta^ {2} - R _ {\alpha \beta} u ^ {\alpha} u ^ {\beta}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Solution 14.11. Since the fluid flows on geodesics $\mathsf{a}_{\alpha} \equiv \mathsf{u}^{\beta} \mathsf{u}_{\alpha; \beta} = 0$ and since the flow is shear and expansion free $\sigma_{\alpha \beta} = \theta = 0$ . The decomposition of $\nabla \mathbf{u}$ in Problem 5.18 then gives

$$
\mathrm {u} _ {\alpha}; \beta = \omega_ {\alpha} \beta .
$$

But $\omega$ is antisymmetric, so $\mathbf{u}_{(\alpha ;\beta)} = 0$ and hence $\mathbf{u}$ satisfies Killing's equation.

Solution 14.12. In geometrical language the observer sets up his local coordinates $\mathbf{x}^{\alpha}$ as follows: If $\mathbf{u}$ is the 4-velocity of the center of his box along its worldline, he chooses $\mathbf{e}_0 = \mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{e}_{\alpha} \cdot \mathbf{e}_{\beta} = \eta_{\alpha \beta}$ along his world line. From each point $\mathbf{P}(\tau)$ on his world-line ( $\tau =$ proper time), he sends out spatial geodesics orthogonal to $\mathbf{u}$ , with affine parameter equal to proper length $\mathbf{s}$ . If $\mathbf{n}$ is the tangent vector to the spatial geodesic through the point $\mathbf{P}$ near his world-line, then he assigns to $\mathbf{P}$ the coordinates $\mathbf{x}^0 = \tau$ , $\mathbf{x}^{\mathbf{k}} = \mathbf{sn}^{\mathbf{k}}$ .

The equation of motion is now

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathbf {x} ^ {\alpha}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda^ {2}} + \Gamma^ {\alpha} \beta_ {\gamma} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {\beta}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {\gamma}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} = 0 \tag {1}
$$

where we can take $\lambda$ to be the proper time of the particle. The 4-velocity of the particle is $\mathrm{dx}^{\alpha} / \mathrm{d}\lambda = (\gamma, \gamma \underline{\mathbf{v}})$ , where $\gamma = (1 - \mathbf{v}^{2})^{-\frac{1}{2}}$ . First change $\mathrm{d} / \mathrm{d}\lambda$ to $\mathrm{d} / \mathrm{d}t$ in Equation (1) using $\mathrm{d} / \mathrm{d}\lambda = \gamma \mathrm{d} / \mathrm{d}t$ :

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathbf {x} ^ {\alpha}}{\mathrm {d t} ^ {2}} + \frac {1}{\gamma} \frac {\mathrm {d} \gamma}{\mathrm {d t}} \frac {\mathrm {d x} ^ {\alpha}}{\mathrm {d t}} + \Gamma^ {\alpha} \beta \gamma \frac {\mathrm {d x} ^ {\beta}}{\mathrm {d t}} \frac {\mathrm {d x} ^ {\gamma}}{\mathrm {d t}} = 0. \tag {2}
$$

Putting $a = 0$ , we get

$$
\frac {1}{\gamma} \frac {\mathrm {d} \gamma}{\mathrm {d} t} + \Gamma^ {0} _ {\beta \gamma} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {\beta}}{\mathrm {d} t} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {\gamma}}{\mathrm {d} t} = 0.
$$

Substitute this in Equation (2) and get, for $\alpha = j$

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {v} ^ {\mathrm {j}}}{\mathrm {d} t} + (- \mathbf {v} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \Gamma^ {0} _ {\beta \gamma} + \Gamma^ {\mathrm {j}} _ {\beta \gamma}) \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {\beta}}{\mathrm {d} t} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {\gamma}}{\mathrm {d} t} = 0. \tag {3}
$$

To first order in $\mathbf{v}$ , Equation (3) is

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {v} ^ {\mathrm {j}}}{\mathrm {d} t} - \mathbf {v} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \Gamma_ {0 0} ^ {0} + \Gamma_ {0 0} ^ {\mathrm {j}} + 2 \Gamma_ {\mathrm {k} 0} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \mathbf {v} ^ {\mathrm {k}} = 0. \tag {4}
$$

Thus, to first order in $\underline{\mathbf{x}}$ and $\underline{\mathbf{y}}$ , we have

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathrm {v} ^ {\mathrm {j}}}{\mathrm {d} t} = \left. \mathrm {v} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \Gamma_ {0 0} ^ {\mid} \right| _ {\underset {\mathrm {x} = 0} {\mid}} - \left. \Gamma_ {0 0} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \right| _ {\underset {\mathrm {x} = 0} {\mid}} - \left. \mathrm {x} ^ {\mathrm {k}} \Gamma_ {0 0, \mathrm {k}} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \right| _ {\underset {\mathrm {x} = 0} {\mid}} - 2 \mathrm {v} ^ {\mathrm {k}} \Gamma_ {\mathrm {k} 0} ^ {\mathrm {j}} | _ {\underset {\mathrm {x} = 0} {\mid}}. \tag {5}
$$

The Christoffel symbols at $\underline{\mathbf{x}} = 0$ are found using a result of Problem 7.17:

$$
\Gamma_ {\alpha 0} ^ {\beta} \mathbf {e} _ {\beta} = \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {e} _ {\alpha} = - \Omega_ {\alpha} ^ {\beta} \mathbf {e} _ {\beta}
$$

$$
\Gamma_ {\beta \alpha 0} = - \Omega_ {\beta \alpha} \equiv a _ {\alpha} u _ {\beta} - a _ {\beta} u _ {\alpha} + \varepsilon_ {\alpha \beta \lambda \sigma} u ^ {\lambda} \omega^ {\sigma}.
$$

Since $\mathbf{u}^{\alpha} = (1,\underline{0}),\mathbf{u}_{\alpha} = (-1,\underline{0}),\mathbf{a}_{\alpha} = (0,\underline{\mathbf{a}}),\boldsymbol{\omega}^{\sigma} = (0,\underline{\boldsymbol{\omega}})$ we get

$$
\Gamma_ {\beta a 0} = 0 \quad \text {i f} \quad \beta = a
$$

$$
\Gamma_ {0 j 0} = - \Gamma_ {j 0 0} = - a _ {j}
$$

$$
\Gamma_ {\mathrm {k j} 0} = - \Gamma_ {\mathrm {j k} 0} = \varepsilon_ {\mathrm {j k} 0 \mathrm {m}} \omega^ {\mathrm {m}} = \varepsilon_ {\mathrm {j k m}} \omega^ {\mathrm {m}} \tag {6}
$$

The $\Gamma$ 's with no 0-index are found from the condition that the coordinate lines $x^{\alpha} = (r, \mathfrak{sn}^{\mathbf{k}})$ , where $r$ and $\mathfrak{n}^{\mathbf{k}}$ are independent of $\mathbf{s}$ , are geodesics. Thus,

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = \frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathbf {x} ^ {\alpha}}{\mathrm {d s} ^ {2}} + \Gamma^ {\alpha} \beta \gamma \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {\beta}}{\mathrm {d s}} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {\gamma}}{\mathrm {d s}} \\ = 0 + \Gamma_ {j k} ^ {\alpha} n ^ {j} n ^ {k}. \\ \end{array}
$$

This is satisfied at $\underline{\mathbf{x}} = 0$ for arbitrary $\underline{\mathbf{n}}$ , so

$$
\Gamma_ {\alpha \mathrm {i k}} = 0. \tag {7}
$$

We find $\Gamma_{00,\mathbf{k}}^{\mathbf{j}}$ from the expression for the Riemann tensor:

$$
\mathsf {R} _ {\beta \gamma \delta} ^ {\alpha} = \Gamma_ {\beta \delta , \gamma} ^ {\alpha} - \Gamma_ {\beta \gamma , \delta} ^ {\alpha} + \Gamma_ {\gamma \mu} ^ {\alpha} \Gamma_ {\beta \delta} ^ {\mu} - \Gamma_ {\delta \mu} ^ {\alpha} \Gamma_ {\beta \gamma} ^ {\mu}.
$$

This gives

$$
\Gamma_ {0 0, \mathbf {k}} ^ {\mathrm {j}} = \mathsf {R} _ {0 \mathbf {k} 0} ^ {\mathrm {j}} + \Gamma_ {0 \mathbf {k}, 0} ^ {\mathrm {j}} - \Gamma_ {\mathbf {k} \mu} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \Gamma_ {0 0} ^ {\mu} + \Gamma_ {0 \mu} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \Gamma_ {0 \mathbf {k}} ^ {\mu}.
$$

At $\underline{\mathbf{x}} = 0$

$$
\Gamma_ {0 k, 0} ^ {j} = - \varepsilon_ {j k m} \omega^ {m},
$$

$$
\Gamma_ {\mathbf {k} \mu} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \Gamma_ {0 0} ^ {\mu} = 0
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \Gamma_ {0 \mu} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \Gamma_ {0 \mathrm {k}} ^ {\mu} = \Gamma_ {0 0} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \Gamma_ {0 \mathrm {k}} ^ {0} + \Gamma_ {0 \mathrm {m}} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \Gamma_ {0 \mathrm {k}} ^ {\mathrm {m}} \\ = a _ {j} a _ {k} + \varepsilon_ {\min } \omega^ {n} \varepsilon_ {k m 1} \omega^ {1}, \\ \end{array}
$$

and thus

$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {v} ^ {\mathrm {j}}}{\mathrm {d} t} = - a _ {\mathrm {j}} - \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {k}} \left(\mathrm {R} _ {0 \mathrm {k} 0} ^ {\mathrm {j}} - \varepsilon_ {\mathrm {j k m}} \omega^ {\mathrm {m}} _ {\mathrm {, 0}} + a _ {\mathrm {j}} a _ {\mathrm {k}} + \varepsilon_ {\mathrm {m j n}} \omega^ {\mathrm {n}} \varepsilon_ {\mathrm {k m} 1} \omega^ {\mathrm {1}}\right) + 2 \varepsilon_ {\mathrm {j k m}} \omega^ {\mathrm {m}} \mathbf {v} ^ {\mathrm {k}} \\ = - a _ {j} \left(1 + \underline {{a}} \cdot \underline {{x}}\right) - R ^ {j} _ {0 k 0} x ^ {k} + \left(\underline {{x}} \times \underline {{\omega}}, _ {0}\right) ^ {j} - \left[ \left(\underline {{x}} \times \underline {{\omega}}\right) \times \underline {{\omega}} \right] ^ {j} + 2 \left(\underline {{v}} \times \underline {{\omega}}\right) ^ {j}. \\ \end{array}
$$

The first term is the “‘inertial force’” due to the acceleration of the reference frame. The $1 + \underline{a} \cdot \underline{x}$ is a relativistic correction factor (see MTW Exercise 37-4). The second term is the “‘true’” gravitational force. In the weak-field limit, a Newtonian physicist would separate $\underline{a}$ into a contribution from the local acceleration of gravity, $\partial \Phi / \partial x_{j}$ , plus an “absolute” acceleration $\underline{a}_{\mathrm{abs}}$ . Thus,

$$
\begin{array}{l} a _ {j} + R _ {0 k 0} ^ {j} x ^ {k} = \left(a _ {j}\right) _ {a b s} + \partial \Phi / \partial x ^ {j} | _ {\underline {{x}} = 0} + \partial^ {2} \Phi / \partial x ^ {j} \partial x ^ {k} | _ {\underline {{x}} = 0} x ^ {k} \\ = \left. \left(a _ {j}\right) _ {\text {a b s}} + \partial \Phi / \partial x ^ {j} \right| _ {\underset {\sim} {x} = x \underset {\sim} {p a r t i c l e}}. \\ \end{array}
$$

The terms involving $\omega$ are the same as in nonrelativistic mechanics; the second and third are the centrifugal and Coriolis forces respectively.

Solution 14.13. Setting the divergence of the stress-energy tensor to zero, we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = 4 \pi \mathrm {T} _ {\mu ; \nu} ^ {\nu} = \Phi_ {, \mu ; \nu} \Phi^ {, \nu} + \Phi_ {, \mu} \Phi^ {, \nu}; \nu - \frac {1}{2} \delta_ {\mu} ^ {\nu} (2 \bar {\Phi} _ {\alpha ; \nu} \Phi^ {, a}) \\ = (\Phi_ {, \mu ; \nu} - \Phi_ {, \nu ; \mu}) \Phi^ {, \nu} + \Phi_ {, \mu} \Phi^ {, \nu} _ {; \nu}. \\ \end{array}
$$

But second covariant derivatives of a scalar commute $(\Phi_{[,\mu ;\nu ]} = \Phi_{[,\mu ,\nu ]} + \Phi_{,\sigma}\Gamma^{\sigma}_{[\mu \nu ]} = 0)$ so only the last term in our equation survives and the equation of motion must be

$$
\square \Phi \equiv \Phi_ {; \nu} ^ {\nu} = 0.
$$

Solution 14.14. In the proposed Equation (2) the term $\frac{1}{6} R\Phi$ is independent of the size of the laboratory in the same way that the $\rho$ term is. In principle then, if we measure the $\Phi$ field we can measure the Ricci curvature; the conformally invariant Equation (2) then violates the spirit of the strong equivalence principle.

To find the anomalous R-term forces between two particles, let us go to the local Lorentz frame in which one of the particles is stationary. The $\Phi$ field due to this stationary particle of charge $\mu_{1}$ satisfies

$$
\Phi_ {, j} ^ {, j} - \frac {1}{6} R \Phi = \mu_ {1} \delta (\mathbf {r}) .
$$

In writing this equation we have assumed a global (or at least laboratory sized) inertial coordinate system. That is, we have ignored aspects of curvature other than the Ricci scalar. Presumably (and in fact) these other aspects of curvature do not violate strong equivalence.

The equation for $\Phi$ has the solution

$$
\Phi = - \frac {\mu_ {1}}{\mathrm {r}} \exp \left\{- \mathrm {r} / a \sqrt {6} \right\}, \quad a = R ^ {- \frac {1}{2}}
$$

so that the force on a particle of charge $\mu_{2}$ is

$$
\begin{array}{l} F ^ {r} = \mu_ {2} \Phi , r = \mu_ {1} \mu_ {2} / r ^ {2} [ 1 + r / \sqrt {6 a} ] \exp \{- r / a \sqrt {6} \} \\ \approx \mu_ {1} \mu_ {2} / \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \left[ 1 + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} / 1 2 \mathrm {a} ^ {2} + \mathcal {O} (\mathrm {r} ^ {3} / \mathrm {a} ^ {3}) \right]. \\ \end{array}
$$

The anomalous R-term force $\approx \mu_1\mu_2 / 12a^2$ is then independent of particle separation, clearly violating the strong equivalence principle.

From the Einstein equations it is clear that the magnitude of the Ricci scalar is of the order of the density of mass-energy, so that the ratio of anomalous forces to “ordinary” scalar forces is

$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {\mathrm {r} ^ {2}}{1 2 \mathrm {a} ^ {2}} \sim \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \rho_ {\text {m a s s - e n e r g y}} \\ \sim [ r (c m.) ] ^ {2} \left[ \rho / \rho_ {\text {n u c l e a r}} \right] \cdot 1 0 ^ {- 1 4}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Thus, even if the scalar forces are being measured inside matter at nuclear densities, $\rho_{\mathrm{nuclear}} = 10^{14} \mathrm{~g/cm}^3$ , a very large ( $r \sim 100 \mathrm{~km}$ ) matter filled "laboratory" is necessary before the R-term forces become important. In any practical experiment the R-term forces would be miniscule.

Solution 14.15. According to Problem 7.7 (i) and Maxwell's equations

$$
4 \pi J ^ {\mu} = F _ {; \nu} ^ {\mu \nu} = \frac {1}{| g | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} (| g | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} F ^ {\mu \nu}) _ {, \nu}
$$

$$
4 \pi \mathrm {J} _ {; \mu} ^ {\mu} = 4 \pi \frac {1}{| \mathrm {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \left(\mathrm {J} ^ {\mu} | \mathrm {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}\right) _ {, \mu} = \frac {1}{| \mathrm {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \left(| \mathrm {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {F} ^ {\mu \nu}\right) _ {, \nu \mu}.
$$

Since $\mathbf{F}$ is antisymmetric this must vanish.

Solution 14.16. In flat spacetime we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {F} _ {, \nu} ^ {\mu \nu} = 4 \pi \mathrm {J} ^ {\mu} \\ \mathbf {F} _ {\mu \nu} = \mathbf {A} _ {\nu , \mu} - \mathbf {A} _ {\mu , \nu} \\ \end{array}
$$

and hence

$$
- \mathrm {A} ^ {\mu , \nu}, _ {\nu} + \mathrm {A} ^ {\nu , \mu}, _ {\nu} = 4 \pi \mathrm {J} ^ {\mu}. \tag {1}
$$

If we interchange $\mu$ and $\nu$ differentiation on the second term, as is allowed in flat spacetime, and then apply the “comma to semicolon” rule we get

$$
- \mathrm {A} ^ {\mu ; \nu}; _ {\nu} + \mathrm {A} ^ {\nu}; _ {\nu} ^ {; \mu} = 4 \pi \mathrm {J} ^ {\mu}. \tag {2}
$$

If, on the other hand, we apply the rule directly to Equation (1) we get

$$
- \mathrm {A} _ {; \nu} ^ {\mu ; \nu} + \mathrm {A} _ {; \nu} ^ {\nu ; \mu} = 4 \pi \mathrm {J} ^ {\mu}.
$$

The derivates on the second term can be exchanged by introducing a curvature term (see Problem 9.8)

$$
- \mathrm {A} _ {; \nu} ^ {\mu ; \nu} + \mathrm {A} _ {; \nu} ^ {\nu ; \mu} + \mathrm {R} _ {\sigma} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {A} ^ {\sigma} = 4 \pi \mathrm {J} ^ {\mu}. \tag {3}
$$

Equations (2) and (3) have, in principle, measurably different consequences. As of 1975, there is no experimental evidence to tell which is correct.

Solution 14.17. If the curvature coupling term is of the sort found in Problem 14.16, we have (in a Lorentz gauge i.e. $\mathbf{A}_{; \mu}^{\mu} = 0$ ) $\square \mathbf{A}^{\mu} + (\mathbf{R}_{\nu}^{\mu} \mathbf{A}^{\nu}) = 4\pi \mathbf{J}^{\mu}$ , where the term in parentheses may or may not be present. Since the Ricci tensor vanishes in vacuum, the only hope of detecting its term would be by experiments in matter (e.g. in glass). The first term is of order $\mathbf{A}^{\mu}$ [minimum $(\frac{\mathbf{c}}{\nu}, \ell)]^{-2}$ . The magnitude of the Ricci tensor is (by the field equations) of order $\mathbf{G} \rho / c^{2} \approx .74 \times 10^{-28} \mathrm{~cm}^{-2} \times \left(\frac{\rho}{\mathbf{gm} \cdot \mathrm{cm}^{-3}}\right)$ . The fractional correction of the second term is thus

$$
\frac {\delta \mathrm {A} ^ {\mu}}{\mathrm {A} ^ {\mu}} \sim \left(\frac {\operatorname* {m i n i m u m} \left(\frac {\mathsf {C}}{\nu} , \ell\right) (\rho / \mathrm {g m .} \cdot \mathrm {c m} ^ {- 3}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{1 . 1 6 \times 1 0 ^ {1 4} \mathrm {c m}}\right) ^ {2}.
$$

Since an earthbound processes is presumably no larger than the size of the earth $\sim 6 \times 10^{8} \mathrm{~cm}$ , this is bounded by $(\delta \mathrm{A}^{\mu} / \mathrm{A}^{\mu}) \lesssim (5 \times 10^{-6})^{2}$ for

$\rho = 1$ . One can also dream up fancier curvature coupling terms like $\mathrm{KR}^{\alpha \beta \gamma \delta} \mathrm{R}_{\alpha \beta \gamma \delta} \mathrm{A}^{\mu}$ where $\mathbf{K}$ is a dimensional constant. Because there is no natural value to assume for $\mathbf{K}$ , there is no a priori way to estimate the magnitude of the effect resulting from such terms. Since the equations are similar to the Proca equation $\square \mathrm{A}^{\mu} + \mathrm{mA}^{\mu} = 0$ , experiments to measure the photon rest mass do put some limits on $\mathbf{K}$ , but so far they are not useful ones.

Solution 14.18. For the electromagnetic stress-energy

$$
4 \pi \mathrm {T} ^ {\mu \nu} = - \left(\mathrm {F} _ {\alpha} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {F} ^ {\alpha \nu} + \frac {1}{4} \mathrm {g} ^ {\mu \nu} \mathrm {F} _ {\alpha} \beta^ {\mathrm {F} ^ {\alpha} \beta}\right) \tag {1}
$$

$$
4 \pi \mathrm {T} ^ {\mu \nu} _ {; \nu} = - \left(\mathrm {F} _ {\alpha ; \nu} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {F} ^ {\alpha \nu} + \mathrm {F} _ {\alpha} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {F} ^ {\alpha \nu} _ {; \nu} + \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {F} _ {\alpha} \beta \mathrm {F} ^ {\alpha \beta ; \mu}\right) = 0. \tag {2}
$$

Now, the first and last terms above equal

$$
\begin{array}{l} - \mathbf {F} ^ {\alpha \beta} \mathbf {g} ^ {\mu \tau} \left(\frac {1}{2} \mathbf {F} _ {\alpha \beta ; \tau} + \mathbf {F} _ {\tau \alpha ; \beta}\right) = - \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {F} ^ {\alpha \beta} \mathbf {g} ^ {\mu \tau} \left(\mathbf {F} _ {\alpha \beta ; \tau} + \mathbf {F} _ {\tau \alpha ; \beta} - \mathbf {F} _ {\tau \beta ; \alpha}\right) \\ = - \frac {1}{2} F ^ {\alpha \beta} g ^ {\mu \tau} \left(F _ {\alpha \beta ; r} + F _ {r \alpha ; \beta} + F _ {\beta r; \alpha}\right) \\ \propto \mathrm {F} ^ {\alpha \beta} \mathrm {g} ^ {\mu r} \mathrm {F} _ {[ \alpha \beta ; r ]} = 0. \\ \end{array}
$$

If $\mathbf{F}$ is derivable from a potential $\mathbf{A}$ in the usual way, then $\mathbf{F}[\alpha \beta; \tau] = 0$ and we are left with

$$
0 = 4 \pi \mathrm {T} _ {; \nu} ^ {\mu \nu} = - \mathrm {F} _ {a} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {F} _ {; \nu} ^ {a \nu}.
$$

This implies that $\mathbf{F}_{; \nu}^{a \nu} = 0$ except in the case that the determinant of the coefficients $\mathbf{F}_{\alpha}^{\mu}$ vanishes. But $\det (\mathbf{F}_{\alpha}^{\mu}) = -(\underline{\mathbf{E}} \cdot \underline{\mathbf{B}})^2$ .

Solution 14.19. First note that $\mathbf{H}$ has been normalized to have dimensions of $(\text{mass})^2$ ; this means that Hamilton's equations are

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} ^ {\mu} / \mathrm {d} \lambda = \partial \mathrm {H} / \partial \pi_ {\mu} (1) \\ \partial \pi_ {\mu} / \mathrm {d} \lambda = - \partial \mathrm {H} / \partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\mu} (2) \\ \end{array}
$$

where $\lambda$ is an affine parameter ( $= \tau / m$ for a particle of mass $m$ where $\tau$ is proper time). If $H$ were divided by $m$ , one would replace $d / d\lambda$ by $d / d\tau$ in Equations (1) and (2); $d / d\lambda$ has the advantage of being valid for massless particles as well.

Since the particle's 4-momentum is $\mathfrak{p}^{\mu} = \mathrm{d}\mathbf{x}^{\mu} / \mathrm{d}\lambda$ , and since $\mathbf{g}^{\mu \nu}$ and $\mathbf{A}_{\mu}$ are functions of $\mathbf{x}^{\alpha}$ not $\pi_{\alpha}$ , Equation (1) gives

$$
\mathrm {p} ^ {\mu} = \mathrm {g} ^ {\mu \nu} \left(\pi_ {\nu} - \mathrm {e A} _ {\nu}\right). \tag {3}
$$

Equation (2) gives

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \pi_ {\alpha}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} = - \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {g} _ {, \alpha} ^ {\mu \nu} (\pi_ {\mu} - \mathsf {e A} _ {\mu}) (\pi_ {\nu} - \mathsf {e A} _ {\nu}) + \mathbf {g} ^ {\mu \nu} \mathsf {e A} _ {\mu , \alpha} (\pi_ {\nu} - \mathsf {e A} _ {\nu}).
$$

But from Equation (3) we get

$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {\mathrm {d} \pi_ {a}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} = \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} \left(\mathrm {g} _ {a \mu} \mathrm {p} ^ {\mu} + \mathrm {e A} _ {a}\right) \\ = \mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \mu} \frac {\mathrm {d p} ^ {\mu}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} + \mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \mu , \beta} \mathrm {p} ^ {\beta} \mathrm {p} ^ {\mu} + \mathrm {e A} _ {\alpha , \beta} \mathrm {p} ^ {\beta} \\ \end{array}
$$

(where we have used $\mathrm{d} / \mathrm{d}\lambda = \mathrm{p}^{\beta}\partial /\partial \mathbf{x}^{\beta})$ . Multiplying by $\mathbf{g}^{\alpha \gamma}$ , we find

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d p} ^ {\gamma}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} + \mathbf {g} ^ {\alpha \gamma} \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \mu , \beta} \mathbf {p} ^ {\beta} \mathbf {p} ^ {\mu} + \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {g} ^ {\alpha \gamma} \mathbf {g} _ {, \alpha} ^ {\mu \nu} \mathbf {p} _ {\mu} \mathbf {p} _ {\nu} =. \mathbf {g} ^ {\alpha \gamma} \mathbf {e} (\mathbf {A} _ {\mu , \alpha} - \mathbf {A} _ {\alpha , \mu}) \mathbf {p} ^ {\mu}.
$$

Now rearrange the derivatives of $\mathfrak{g}_{\alpha \beta}$ to form a Christoffel symbol.

Since

$$
0 = (\mathbf {g} ^ {\mu \beta} \mathbf {g} _ {\beta \gamma}) _ {, a} = \mathbf {g} ^ {\mu \beta} _ {, a} \mathbf {g} _ {\beta \gamma} + \mathbf {g} ^ {\mu \beta} \mathbf {g} _ {\beta \gamma , a}
$$

we have on multiplying by $\mathbf{g}^{\gamma \nu}$

$$
\mathbf {g} _ {, a} ^ {\mu \nu} = - \mathbf {g} ^ {\gamma \nu} \mathbf {g} ^ {\mu \beta} \mathbf {g} _ {\beta \gamma , a}.
$$

Thus

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d p} ^ {\gamma}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} + \mathbf {g} ^ {\alpha \gamma} \Bigl (\mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \mu , \beta} - \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {\Gamma} \mathbf {g} _ {\beta \mu , a} \Bigr) \mathbf {p} ^ {\beta} \mathbf {p} ^ {\mu} = \mathbf {g} ^ {\alpha \gamma} \mathbf {e F} _ {\alpha \mu} \mathbf {p} ^ {\mu} .
$$

Since

$$
\mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \mu , \beta} \mathrm {p} ^ {\beta} \mathrm {p} ^ {\mu} = \frac {1}{2} \left(\mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \mu , \beta} + \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \beta , \mu}\right) \mathrm {p} ^ {\beta} \mathrm {p} ^ {\mu},
$$

we get, finally

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d p} ^ {\gamma}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} + \Gamma_ {\beta \mu} ^ {\gamma} \mathsf {p} ^ {\beta} \mathsf {p} ^ {\mu} = \mathsf {e F} _ {\mu} ^ {\gamma} \mathsf {p} ^ {\mu}
$$

which is the equation of motion.

Solution 14.20. For an uncharged test particle, $\mathbf{p} \cdot \boldsymbol{\xi}$ is conserved. We can guess that for a charged particle, $\boldsymbol{\pi} \cdot \boldsymbol{\xi}$ is conserved, where $\boldsymbol{\pi}$ is the canonical momentum $\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{eA}$ (see Problem 14.19). One way of seeing this is to note that corresponding to $\boldsymbol{\xi}$ there is an ignorable coordinate, i.e. we can choose coordinates so that the Hamiltonian $\mathbf{H}$ is independent of one coordinate. The conjugate canonical momentum is then a conserved quantity.

Alternatively, we can verify directly from the equation of motion

$$
\nabla_ {\mathbf {p}} \mathbf {p} = e \mathbf {F} \cdot \mathbf {p}
$$

that

$$
\begin{array}{l} \nabla_ {\mathbf {p}} (\boldsymbol {\pi} \cdot \boldsymbol {\xi}) = (\nabla_ {\mathbf {p}} \mathbf {p}) \cdot \boldsymbol {\xi} + \mathbf {p} \cdot \nabla_ {\mathbf {p}} \boldsymbol {\xi} + e (\nabla_ {\mathbf {p}} \mathbf {A}) \cdot \boldsymbol {\xi} + e \mathbf {A} \cdot \nabla_ {\mathbf {p}} \boldsymbol {\xi} \\ = \mathbf {e} \boldsymbol {\xi} \cdot \mathbf {F} \cdot \mathbf {p} + 0 + \mathbf {e} (\nabla_ {\mathbf {p}} \mathbf {A}) \cdot \boldsymbol {\xi} - \mathbf {e p} \cdot \nabla_ {\mathbf {A}} \boldsymbol {\xi}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Here we have used Killing's equation to set the second term to zero and to rewrite the last term. Note that the order of vectors in the dot products in the first line is immaterial, but $\mathbf{F}$ has two "slots" and we must keep the vectors in the correct positions. Now the fact that the electromagnetic field admits an ignorable coordinate is expressed by the relation

$$
0 = \mathcal {L} _ {\boldsymbol {\xi}} \mathbf {A} = \nabla_ {\boldsymbol {\xi}} \mathbf {A} - \nabla_ {\mathbf {A}} \boldsymbol {\xi}.
$$

Using this in the last term, we get

$$
\begin{array}{l} \nabla_ {\mathbf {p}} (\pi \cdot \boldsymbol {\xi}) = e \boldsymbol {\xi} \cdot \mathbf {F} \cdot \mathbf {p} + e \boldsymbol {\xi} \cdot (\nabla \mathbf {A}) \cdot \mathbf {p} - e \mathbf {p} \cdot (\nabla \mathbf {A}) ^ {\prime} \cdot \boldsymbol {\xi} \\ = e \boldsymbol {\xi} \cdot \mathbf {F} \cdot \mathbf {p} - e \boldsymbol {\xi} \cdot \mathbf {F} \cdot \mathbf {p} \\ = 0 \\ \end{array}
$$

where we have used the fact that $\mathbf{F}$ is $\nabla \mathbf{A}$ antisymmetrized. Thus $\pi \cdot \xi$ is a conserved quantity along the particle's trajectory.

Solution 14.21. Under the conformal transformation the four Maxwell

equations

$$
\mathbf {F} _ {[ \alpha \beta , \nu ]} = 0 = \tilde {\mathbf {F}} _ {[ \alpha \beta , \nu ]}
$$

are clearly invariant. The other four Maxwell equations are

$$
\mathbf {F} _ {; \nu} ^ {\mu \nu} = \left| \mathbf {g} \right| ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \left[ \mathbf {F} ^ {\mu \nu} | \mathbf {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \right] _ {, \nu} = 4 \pi \mathbf {J} ^ {\mu} .
$$

Since

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {F} ^ {\mu \nu} = \mathbf {g} ^ {\mu \alpha} \mathbf {g} ^ {\nu \beta} \tilde {\mathbf {F}} _ {\alpha \beta} = \mathbf {f} ^ {2} \tilde {\mathbf {g}} ^ {\mu \alpha} \tilde {\mathbf {g}} ^ {\nu \beta} \tilde {\mathbf {F}} _ {\alpha \beta} = \mathbf {f} ^ {2} \tilde {\mathbf {F}} ^ {\mu \nu} \\ \tilde {\mathbf {g}} \equiv \operatorname {D e t} \left(\tilde {\mathbf {g}} _ {\alpha \beta}\right) = \mathbf {f} ^ {4} \mathbf {g} \\ \end{array}
$$

it follows that $\left|\mathbf{g}\right|^{\frac{1}{2}}\mathbf{F}^{\mu \nu} = \left|\tilde{\mathbf{g}}\right|^{\frac{1}{2}}\tilde{\mathbf{F}}^{\mu \nu}$ so that

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {F} _ {; \nu} ^ {\mu \nu} = \frac {\mathrm {f} ^ {2}}{\left| \tilde {\mathrm {g}} \right| ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \left[ \left| \tilde {\mathrm {g}} \right| ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \tilde {\mathrm {F}} ^ {\mu \nu} \right] _ {, \nu} = 4 \pi \mathrm {J} ^ {\mu} = 4 \pi \mathrm {g} ^ {\mu a} \mathrm {J} _ {a} \\ = 4 \pi (\mathrm {f g} ^ {\mu a}) \mathrm {f} \tilde {\mathrm {J}} _ {a} = 4 \pi \mathrm {f} ^ {2} \tilde {\mathrm {J}} ^ {\mu}, \\ \end{array}
$$

and therefore

$$
\tilde {\mathrm {F}} _ {; \nu} ^ {\mu \nu} = 4 \pi \tilde {\mathrm {J}} ^ {\mu}.
$$

# CHAPTER 15: SOLUTIONS

Solution 15.1. When the particle moves in the equatorial plane, we have $L^2 = p_\phi^2$ , which is obviously a conserved constant since $p_\phi$ itself is. ( $\pmb{\xi} = \partial/\partial \pmb{\phi}$ is a Killing vector so $\pmb{\xi} \cdot \mathbf{p}$ is conserved; see Problem 10.10.) But by spherical symmetry the motion is always in the equatorial plane of some rotated coordinate system. If we could write this $p_\phi^2$ as an invariant quantity (under rotations) and then evaluated the invariant in the original system, we would be done.

At some instant when the particle is at radius $r$ the covariant 4-velocity $\tilde{\mathbf{p}}$ has components $(\mathfrak{p}_t, \mathfrak{p}_r, \mathfrak{p}_\theta, \mathfrak{p}_\phi)$ . Consider the "reduced" 4-velocity $\tilde{\mathbf{p}}_a^{\text{red}} = (0, 0, \mathfrak{r}\mathfrak{p}_\theta, \mathfrak{r}\mathfrak{p}_\phi)$ whose construction from $\tilde{\mathbf{p}}_a$ (e.g. by projection) is independent of $\theta$ and $\phi$ . Now when the motion is equatorial $\mathfrak{p}_\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \pi/2$ we have

$$
L ^ {2} = g ^ {\alpha \beta} \tilde {p} _ {\alpha} ^ {\text {r e d}} \tilde {p} _ {\beta} ^ {\text {r e d}} = g ^ {\phi \phi} r ^ {2} p _ {\phi} ^ {2} = p _ {\phi} ^ {2} = \text {c o n s e r v e d}.
$$

But in general

$$
\mathbf {L} ^ {2} = \mathbf {g} ^ {\theta \theta} \mathbf {r} ^ {2} \mathbf {p} _ {\theta} ^ {2} + \mathbf {g} ^ {\phi \phi} \mathbf {r} ^ {2} \mathbf {p} _ {\phi} ^ {2} = \mathbf {p} _ {\theta} ^ {2} + \frac {\mathbf {p} _ {\phi} ^ {2}}{\sin^ {2} \theta}.
$$

So this must be conserved in general, q.e.d.

# Solution 15.2.

(a) Using the spherical symmetry of the metric we orient the coordinate axes so that the particle is at $\theta = \pi /2$ with $\dot{\theta} = 0$ at $\tau = 0$ ; then the unique solution of the geodesic equation

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} r} \left(r ^ {2} \dot {\theta}\right) = r ^ {2} \sin \theta \cos \theta \dot {\phi} ^ {2}
$$

is $\theta = \pi /2$ forall $\pmb{\tau}$

(b) Using the constant of motion $\mathbf{L}^2$ of Problem 15.1, we have

$$
\left(\frac {\mathrm {d} \theta}{\mathrm {d} \lambda}\right) ^ {2} = \left(\mathrm {g} ^ {\theta \theta} \mathrm {p} _ {\theta}\right) ^ {2} = \frac {1}{\mathrm {r} ^ {4}} \left(\mathrm {L} ^ {2} - \frac {\mathrm {p} _ {\phi} ^ {2}}{\sin^ {2} \theta}\right). \tag {1}
$$

Suppose that the unperturbed orbit is $\theta = \pi /2$ , $\mathbf{L} = \mathfrak{p}_{\phi} = \mathbf{K}$ . Let the particle be perturbed out of the plane of the orbit i.e. $\theta = \pi /2 + \delta \theta$ , $\mathbf{L} = \mathbf{K} + \delta \mathbf{L}$ , $\mathfrak{p}_{\phi} = \mathbf{K} + \delta \mathfrak{p}_{\phi}$ . To first order in $\delta \mathbf{L}$ and $\delta \mathfrak{p}_{\phi}$ and second order in $\delta \theta$ , Equation (1) gives

$$
\left[ \frac {\mathrm {d} (\delta \theta)}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} \right] ^ {2} = \frac {1}{\mathrm {r} ^ {4}} \left[ 2 \mathrm {K} \delta \mathrm {L} - 2 \mathrm {K} \delta \mathrm {p} _ {\phi} - \mathrm {K} ^ {2} (\delta \theta) ^ {2} \right]
$$

and hence

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} (\delta \theta)}{\mathrm {d} \lambda^ {2}} = - \frac {\mathrm {K} ^ {2}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {4}} \delta \theta .
$$

This shows that $\delta \theta$ oscillates about $\theta = \pi / 2$ and does not grow in time. Hence the orbits are stably planar.

Solution 15.3. We have two constants for radial fall: $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{u} = -1$ and $\mathbf{u} \cdot \partial/\partial t = \mathbf{u}_0 = -(1 - 2\mathbf{M}/\mathbf{r})\mathbf{u}^0$ . From these we get

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {u} = - 1 = - (1 - 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r}) \left(\mathrm {u} ^ {0}\right) ^ {2} + (1 - 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r}) ^ {- 1} \left(\mathrm {u} ^ {\mathrm {r}}\right) ^ {2} \\ = \left[ - (1 - 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r}) + (1 - 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r}) ^ {- 1} (\mathrm {d r} / \mathrm {d t}) ^ {2} \right] \left(\mathrm {u} ^ {0}\right) ^ {2} \\ = \left[ - (1 - 2 M / r) + (1 - 2 M / r) ^ {- 1} (d r / d t) ^ {2} \right] \left(u _ {0}\right) ^ {2} (1 - 2 M / r) ^ {2}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Solving for $(\mathrm{dr} / \mathrm{dt})^2$ we find

$$
\left(\mathrm {d r} / \mathrm {d t}\right) ^ {2} = (1 - 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r}) ^ {2} \left[ 1 - (1 - 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r}) \left(\mathrm {u} _ {0}\right) ^ {- 2} \right].
$$

A stationary observer measures time intervals $\hat{\mathbf{d}}\mathbf{t} = (1 - 2\mathbf{M} / \mathbf{r})^{\frac{1}{2}}\mathbf{d}\mathbf{t}$ and radial distances $\hat{\mathbf{d}}\mathbf{r} = (1 - 2\mathbf{M} / \mathbf{r})^{-\frac{1}{2}}\mathbf{d}\mathbf{r}$ so he measures velocity

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \hat {\mathbf {r}}}{\mathrm {d} \hat {\mathbf {t}}} = (1 - 2 \mathbf {M} / \mathbf {r}) ^ {- 1} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {r}}{\mathrm {d} t}.
$$

Notice that independent of the value of $\mathbf{u}_0$ this locally measured velocity approaches the velocity of light as r approaches 2M.

Solution 15.4. From the first integrals of the geodesic equation (see

Problem 15.3)

$$
u _ {0} = - \tilde {\mathbf {E}} = \text {c o n s t a n t}
$$

$$
\mathrm {g} ^ {0 0} \mathrm {u} _ {0} ^ {2} + \mathrm {g} _ {\mathrm {r r}} \left(\mathrm {u} ^ {\mathrm {r}}\right) ^ {2} = - 1
$$

we get

$$
u ^ {0} = \frac {\mathrm {d} t}{\mathrm {d} r} = \frac {\tilde {E}}{1 - 2 M / r} \tag {1}
$$

$$
u ^ {r} = \frac {d r}{d r} = - \left(\tilde {E} ^ {2} - 1 + 2 M / r\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}. \tag {2}
$$

The minus sign for the square root in Equation (2) corresponds to an infalling particle.

Case (i). For this case $\mathrm{dr} / \mathrm{d}\tau = 0$ at $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{R}$ implies $2\mathbf{M} / \mathbf{R} = 1 - \tilde{\mathbf{E}}^2$ , i.e. $\tilde{\mathbf{E}} < 1$ . Thus Equation (2) can be rewritten

$$
\mathrm {d} \tau = \frac {\mathrm {d r}}{\left(\frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}} - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {R}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \tag {3}
$$

This can be integrated in closed form to give

$$
\tau = \left(\frac {\mathrm {R} ^ {3}}{8 \mathrm {M}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \left[ 2 \left(\frac {\mathrm {r}}{\mathrm {R}} - \frac {\mathrm {r} ^ {2}}{\mathrm {R} ^ {2}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} + \cos^ {- 1} \left(\frac {2 \mathrm {r}}{\mathrm {R}} - 1\right) \right] \tag {4}
$$

where the constant of integration has been chosen so that $\tau = 0$ at $r = R$ . It is convenient to rewrite Equation (4) in parametric form by introducing the "cycloid parameter"

$$
\eta = \cos^ {- 1} \left(\frac {2 r}{R} - 1\right)
$$

so that we have

$$
r = \frac {1}{2} R (1 + \cos \eta) \quad (\eta = 0 \text {a t} r = R) \tag {5}
$$

$$
\tau = \left(\frac {\mathrm {R} ^ {3}}{8 \mathrm {M}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} (\eta + \sin \eta). \tag {6}
$$

Equation (1) gives

$$
\begin{array}{l} t = \int \frac {\tilde {E} d \tau}{\left(1 - \frac {2 M}{r}\right)} = \tilde {E} \int \frac {d \tau}{d \eta} \frac {1}{1 - \frac {2 M}{r}} d \eta \\ = \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {R}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \int \frac {\left(\frac {\mathrm {R} ^ {3}}{8 \mathrm {M}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} (1 + \cos \eta) \mathrm {d} \eta}{1 - 4 \mathrm {M} [ \mathrm {R} (1 + \cos \eta) ] ^ {- 1}}. \\ \end{array}
$$

From a table of integrals, we find

$$
\frac {t}{2 \mathrm {M}} = \log \left| \frac {\left(\frac {\mathrm {R}}{2 \mathrm {M}} - 1\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} + \tan \left(\frac {\eta}{2}\right)}{\left(\frac {\mathrm {R}}{2 \mathrm {M}} - 1\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} - \tan \left(\frac {\eta}{2}\right)} \right| + \left(\frac {\mathrm {R}}{2 \mathrm {M}} - 1\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \left[ \eta + \frac {\mathrm {R}}{4 \mathrm {M}} (\eta + \sin \eta) \right] \tag {7}
$$

where the constant of integration has been chosen so that $t = 0$ at $\eta = 0$ i.e. at $r = R$ . Note that $t \to \infty$ when $\tan(\eta/2) \to (R/2M - 1)^{\frac{1}{2}}$ i.e. when $r \to 2M$ .

Case (ii). Here $\tilde{\mathbf{E}} = 1$ and Equation (2) gives

$$
\tau = - \frac {2}{3} \left(\frac {\mathrm {r} ^ {3}}{2 \mathrm {M}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} + \text {c o n s t a n t} \tag {8}
$$

and since

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} t}{\mathrm {d} r} = \frac {\mathrm {d} t / \mathrm {d} \tau}{\mathrm {d} r / \mathrm {d} \tau}
$$

we get

$$
t = - \frac {2}{3} \left(\frac {r ^ {3}}{2 M}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} - 4 M \left(\frac {r}{2 M}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} + 2 M \log \left| \frac {\left(\frac {r}{2 M}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} + 1}{\left(\frac {r}{2 M}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} - 1} \right| + \text {c o n s t a n t}. \tag {9}
$$

Case (iii). By analogy with case (i), choose R such that

$$
\frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {R}} = \tilde {\mathrm {E}} ^ {2} - 1 = \left(1 - \mathrm {v} _ {\infty} ^ {2}\right) ^ {- 1} - 1 = \frac {\mathrm {v} _ {\infty} ^ {2}}{1 - \mathrm {v} _ {\infty} ^ {2}} .
$$

Then changing the sign of $\mathbf{R}$ in Equation (4) gives

$$
\tau = - \left(\frac {\mathrm {R} ^ {3}}{8 \mathrm {M}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \left[ 2 \left(\frac {\mathrm {r}}{\mathrm {R}} + \frac {\mathrm {r} ^ {2}}{\mathrm {R} ^ {2}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} - \cosh^ {- 1} \left(\frac {2 \mathrm {r}}{\mathrm {R}} + 1\right) \right]. \tag {10}
$$

Note that $\tau = 0$ at $\mathbf{r} = 0$ , and $\tau = -\infty$ at $\mathbf{r} = \infty$ . Put

$$
\eta = \cosh^ {- 1} \left(\frac {2 r}{R} + 1\right)
$$

and get

$$
r = \frac {R}{2} (\cosh \eta - 1) \quad (r = 0 \text {a t} \eta = 0) \tag {11}
$$

$$
\tau = - \left(\frac {\mathrm {R} ^ {3}}{8 \mathrm {M}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} (\sinh \eta - \eta). \tag {12}
$$

The equation corresponding to Equation (7) is

$$
\frac {t}{2 \mathbb {M}} = \log \left| \frac {\left(\frac {\mathrm {R}}{2 \mathbb {M}} + 1\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} + \coth \frac {\eta}{2}}{\left(\frac {\mathrm {R}}{2 \mathbb {M}} + 1\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} - \coth \frac {\eta}{2}} \right| - \left(\frac {\mathrm {R}}{2 \mathbb {M}} + 1\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \left[ \eta + \frac {\mathrm {R}}{4 \mathbb {M}} (\sinh \eta - \eta) \right]. \tag {13}
$$

Note that $t$ goes from $-\infty$ to $+\infty$ as $r$ goes from $\infty$ to $2M$ .

Solution 15.5. Choose the equatorial plane to be $\theta = \pi /2$ . Then $\mathbf{u}^{\theta} = 0$ . One first integral of the motion is the normalization of the 4-velocity

which implies

$$
\mathrm {g} ^ {0 0} \mathrm {u} _ {0} ^ {2} + \mathrm {g} ^ {\phi \phi} \mathrm {u} _ {\phi} ^ {2} + \mathrm {g} _ {\mathrm {r r}} \mathrm {u} ^ {\mathrm {r}} ^ {2} = - 1. \tag {1}
$$

From the Killing vectors $\partial/\partial t$ and $\partial/\partial \phi$ two more first integrals are

$$
u _ {\phi} = \tilde {L} = \text {c o n s t a n t}
$$

$$
u _ {0} = - \bar {\mathbf {E}} = \text {c o n s t a n t}
$$

where $\tilde{\mathbf{L}}$ and $\tilde{\mathbf{E}}$ are respectively the angular momentum per unit rest mass and energy per unit rest mass. Equation (1) now implies

$$
\left(\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {r}}{\mathrm {d} \tau}\right) ^ {2} = \frac {- 1 - \mathbf {g} ^ {0 0} \tilde {\mathbf {E}} ^ {2} - \mathbf {g} ^ {\phi \phi} \tilde {\mathbf {L}} ^ {2}}{\mathbf {g} _ {\mathbf {r r}}} \tag {2}
$$

or with the explicit metric functions:

$$
\mathrm {d} \mathbf {r} / \mathrm {d} \tau = \pm \left\{\left[ - 1 + \tilde {\mathrm {E}} ^ {2} \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {m}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) ^ {- 1} - \frac {\tilde {\mathrm {L}} ^ {2}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}} \right] \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {m}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) \right\} ^ {\frac {1}{2}}. \tag {3}
$$

From $\mathbf{u}^{\phi} = \mathbf{g}^{\phi}\tilde{\mathbf{L}}$ we have $\mathrm{d}\phi /\mathrm{d}\tau = \tilde{\mathrm{L}} /\mathrm{r}^2$ . Combining this with Equation (3) gives us finally

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {r}}{\mathrm {d} \phi} = \pm \frac {\mathbf {r} ^ {2}}{\tilde {\mathbf {L}}} \left\{\left[ - 1 + \tilde {\mathbf {E}} ^ {2} (1 - 2 \mathbf {M} / \mathbf {r}) ^ {- 1} - \frac {\tilde {\mathbf {L}} ^ {2}}{\mathbf {r} ^ {2}} \right] (1 - 2 \mathbf {M} / \mathbf {r}) \right\} ^ {\frac {1}{2}}.
$$

Solution 15.6. Choose $\theta = \pi /2$ and $\mathbf{p}^{\theta} = 0$ . If $\lambda$ is an affine parameter such that $\mathbf{p}^{\mathbf{r}} = \mathrm{d}\mathbf{r} / \mathrm{d}\lambda$ and $\mathbf{p}^{\phi} = \mathrm{d}\phi /\mathrm{d}\lambda = \mathbf{r}^{-2}\mathbf{p}_{\phi}$ , then

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {r}}{\mathrm {d} \phi} = \frac {\mathbf {p} ^ {\mathbf {r}}}{\mathbf {p} ^ {\phi}}.
$$

From $\mathbf{p} \cdot \mathbf{p} = 0$ we get a relation amongst $\mathbf{p}^{\mathbf{r}}$ , $\mathbf{p}^{\phi}$ , and $\mathbf{p}^{0}$ which can be solved for $\mathrm{dr} / \mathrm{d}\phi$ :

$$
\left(\mathrm {d} \mathbf {r} / \mathrm {d} \phi\right) ^ {2} = \mathrm {r} ^ {4} (1 - 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r}) [ \gamma (1 - 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r}) ^ {- 1} - 1 / \mathrm {r} ^ {2} ] \tag {1}
$$

where $\gamma \equiv p_0^2 /p_\phi^2 = \text{constant}$ . If we now introduce $u\equiv M / r$ , Equation (1) becomes

$$
\left(u ^ {\prime}\right) ^ {2} = (1 - 2 u) \left[ \gamma M ^ {2} (1 - 2 u) ^ {- 1} - u ^ {2} \right] = \gamma M ^ {2} - u ^ {2} + 2 u ^ {3} \tag {2}
$$

where $\mathbf{\nabla}^{\prime}$ denotes $\mathrm{d} / \mathrm{d}\phi$ . If we differentiate this equation we get the very simple second-order equation

$$
u ^ {\prime \prime} + u = 3 u ^ {2}. \tag {3}
$$

In the limit $\mathbf{M} / \mathbf{b} \ll 1$ the zeroth order solution can be taken to be the "straight line" solution $r \sin \phi = b$ or

$$
\mathbf {u} _ {0} = (\mathbf {M} / \mathbf {b}) \sin \phi .
$$

Now write $\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{u}_0 + \mathbf{u}_1 + \dots$ with $\mathbf{u}_1 \ll 1$ so that Equation (3) is approximately

$$
\mathbf {u} _ {1} ^ {\prime \prime} + \mathbf {u} _ {1} \approx 3 \mathbf {u} _ {0} ^ {2} = 3 \left(\frac {\mathbf {M}}{\mathbf {b}}\right) ^ {2} \sin^ {2} \phi = \frac {3}{2} \left(\frac {\mathbf {M}}{\mathbf {b}}\right) ^ {2} \left(1 - \cos 2 \phi\right).
$$

This can be solved by inspection, and we get

$$
u \approx \left(\frac {M}{b}\right) \sin \phi + \frac {1}{2} \left(\frac {M}{b}\right) ^ {2} (3 + \cos 2 \phi).
$$

Now we can find the total deflection angle by calculating the two angles at which $r = \infty (u = 0)$ . These angles must satisfy:

$$
2 \sin \phi \approx - (\mathrm {M} / \mathrm {b}) (3 + \cos 2 \phi)
$$

$$
\phi \approx - 2 (\mathrm {M} / \mathrm {b}) \quad \text {a n d} \quad \phi \approx \pi + 2 (\mathrm {M} / \mathrm {b}).
$$

Thus the total deflection angle is 4M/b.

# Solution 15.7.

(a) The orbital equation was derived in Problem 15.5. If we introduce $\mathbf{u} \equiv \mathbf{M} / \mathbf{r}$ and two new parameters $\epsilon$ and $\mathbf{u}_0$ in place of $\tilde{\mathbf{E}}$ , $\tilde{\mathbf{L}}$ , we can rewrite the orbital equation as

$$
(\mathrm {d} \mathsf {u} / \mathrm {d} \phi) ^ {2} + (\mathsf {u} - \mathsf {u} _ {0}) ^ {2} - \epsilon^ {2} \mathsf {u} _ {0} ^ {2} = 6 \mathsf {u} _ {0} (\mathsf {u} - \mathsf {u} _ {0}) ^ {2} + 2 (\mathsf {u} - \mathsf {u} _ {0}) ^ {3}.
$$

(See also MTW, Equation 25.47 or Weinberg, Equation 8.4.29.) The terms on the right are the relativistic terms; in Newtonian gravitation theory, the right side would be zero.

The lowest order solution, the Newtonian solution, is

$$
\mathbf {u} = \mathbf {u} _ {0} (1 + \epsilon \cos \phi)
$$

where $\epsilon$ is the eccentricity. Now note that the first correction term is

$$
6 u _ {0} (u - u _ {0}) ^ {2} = \mathcal {O} (u _ {0} ^ {3} \epsilon^ {2}) = \mathcal {O} \left(\frac {m ^ {3}}{r _ {0} ^ {2}} \epsilon^ {2}\right)
$$

and the second is $(\mathbf{u} - \mathbf{u}_0)^3 = \mathcal{O}\left(\frac{\mathbf{m}^3}{\mathbf{r}_0^3}\epsilon^3\right)$ so we can ignore it! Thus, first order corrections can be found from

$$
(\mathrm {d} \mathsf {u} / \mathrm {d} \phi) ^ {2} + (1 - 6 \mathsf {u} _ {0}) (\mathsf {u} - \mathsf {u} _ {0}) ^ {2} = \mathsf {u} _ {0} ^ {2} \epsilon^ {2}.
$$

Now define $\psi \equiv (1 - 6\mathfrak{u}_0)^{\frac{1}{2}}\phi$ $\mu = \mathfrak{u} - \mathfrak{u}_0$ so that

$$
\left(\mathrm {d} u / \mathrm {d} \psi\right) ^ {2} + \mu^ {2} = u _ {0} ^ {2} \epsilon^ {2} / \left(1 - 6 u _ {0}\right).
$$

Note that the solution is periodic in $\psi$ so that $\mathbf{r}$ is periodic in $\psi = (1 - 6\mathbf{u}_0)^{\frac{1}{2}}\phi$ . One orbit corresponds therefore to $\psi = 2\pi$ or $\phi = 2\pi (1 - 6\mathbf{M} / \mathbf{r}_0)^{-\frac{1}{2}}$ so that the change in the periastron is given by

$$
\left. \frac {\delta \phi}{2 \pi} \right| _ {\text {p e r o r b i t}} = \frac {3 M}{r _ {0}}.
$$

[For another way of doing this, better suited to large eccentricity, see A. S. Eddington, The Mathematical Theory of Relativity (Cambridge University Press (1922), Section 40).]

(b) From the Newtonian orbital equations, derive

$$
\left(\frac {\mathrm {d} u}{\mathrm {d} \phi}\right) ^ {2} + u ^ {2} + \frac {2 \Phi (u)}{L ^ {2}} = \frac {2 E _ {\infty}}{L ^ {2}}.
$$

With $\Phi = -\mathbf{M} / \mathbf{r} - \mathbf{A}\mathbf{M} / \mathbf{r}^3$ , this becomes

$$
\left(\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {u}}{\mathrm {d} \phi}\right) ^ {2} + \mathbf {u} ^ {2} - \frac {2 \mathbf {m u}}{\mathbf {L} ^ {2}} - \frac {2 \mathbf {a m u} ^ {3}}{\mathbf {L} ^ {2}} = \frac {2 \mathbf {E} _ {\infty}}{\mathbf {L} ^ {2}}.
$$

Put this in the form

$$
\left(\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {u}}{\mathrm {d} \phi}\right) ^ {2} + (1 - \mathbf {c}) (\mathbf {u} - \mathbf {u} _ {0}) ^ {2} - \frac {2 \mathbf {a m}}{\mathbf {L} ^ {2}} (\mathbf {u} - \mathbf {u} _ {0}) ^ {3} = \text {c o n s t a n t}.
$$

By comparing terms of order $\mathbf{u}^2$ , conclude that $c = 6a\mathbf{u}_0\mathbf{m} / \mathbf{L}^2$ . For nearly circular orbits $\mathbf{L}^2 \approx \mathbf{mr}$ so that $c = 6a / r_0^2$ . From our experience with part (a) we know that the solution is periodic in the variable $(1 + c)^{\frac{1}{2}}\phi$ so that the angular spread from periastron to periastron is

$$
\Delta \phi = \frac {2 \pi}{(1 - c) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} = \frac {2 \pi}{\left(\frac {1 - 6 a}{r _ {0} ^ {2}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}
$$

and therefore

$$
\left. \frac {\delta \phi}{2 \pi} \right| _ {\text {p e r o r b i t}} = \frac {3 a}{r _ {0} ^ {2}}.
$$

(The periastron advances if $a > 0$ (oblate) and regresses if $a < 0$ (prolate).)

(c) In $\mathbf{c} = \mathbf{G} = 1$ units $\mathbf{M}_{\odot} = 1.5 \, \mathrm{km}$ . For Mercury $\mathbf{r}_0 = .58 \times 10^8 \, \mathrm{km}$ . So the relativistic advance is

$$
\begin{array}{l} \delta \phi = . 1 0 5 \text {s e c o n d s o f a r c p e r o r i b t} \\ = 4 2 \text {s e c o n d s o f a r c p e r c e n t u r y} \\ \end{array}
$$

since the orbital period is .241 years. If this advance were due to the oblateness of the sun, then for the other planets we would have,

$$
\begin{array}{l} \delta \phi = (4 2 \sec / c e n t u r y) \left(r _ {M e r c.} / r\right) ^ {2} (\text {P e r i o d o f M e r c . / P e r i o d}) \\ = (4 2 \sec / c e n t u r y) \left(r _ {M e r c.} / r\right) ^ {\frac {7}{2}}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Numerical answers:

<table><tr><td></td><td>rMerc</td><td>general relativity</td><td>oblateness</td></tr><tr><td></td><td>r</td><td>sec/century</td><td>sec/century</td></tr><tr><td>Mercury</td><td>1</td><td>42</td><td>42</td></tr><tr><td>Venus</td><td>.536</td><td>8.8</td><td>4.7</td></tr><tr><td>Earth</td><td>.386</td><td>3.9</td><td>1.51</td></tr><tr><td>Mars</td><td>.245</td><td>1.25</td><td>.3</td></tr></table>

Solution 15.8. We solve the problem in two stages: First, compute the laser frequency as measured by a static observer at $\mathbf{r}$ ("SOR"). Second, compute the redshift between this observer and a static observer at infinity (SOI).

Let $\mathbf{u}_{\mathbb{R}}$ be the 4-velocity of the rocket and $\mathbf{p}_{\mathbb{R}}$ the photon momentum at $\mathbf{r}$ .

$$
\frac {\nu_ {\mathrm {S O R}}}{\nu_ {0}} = \frac {\mathbf {u} _ {\mathrm {S O R}} \cdot \mathbf {p} _ {\mathrm {R}}}{\mathbf {u} _ {\mathrm {R}} \cdot \mathbf {p} _ {\mathrm {R}}} \tag {1}
$$

If $\mathbf{v}$ is the proper relative velocity between the rocket (R) and SOR observer, then in the rocket frame Equation (1) gives us

$$
\frac {\nu_ {\mathrm {S O R}}}{\nu_ {0}} = \frac {\gamma \nu_ {0} (1 + \mathrm {v} \cos a)}{\nu_ {0}}
$$

or

$$
\nu_ {\mathbf {S O R}} = \gamma \nu_ {0} (1 + v \cos a) \tag {2}
$$

where $\gamma \equiv (1 - v^2)^{-\frac{1}{2}}$ . To compute $v$ , recall that circular orbits have an angular velocity

$$
\Omega \equiv \frac {\mathrm {d} \phi}{\mathrm {d} t}
$$

exactly the same as Kepler's Third Law in Newtonian gravity:

$$
\Omega = \left(\frac {\mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {3}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}. \tag {3}
$$

(See Problem 17.4.) Thus

$$
v = \frac {d \widehat {\phi}}{d \widehat {t}} = \frac {r d \phi}{\left(1 - 2 M / r\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} d t} = \left[ \frac {M}{r (1 - 2 M / r)} \right] ^ {\frac {1}{2}}. \tag {4}
$$

The observer at infinity then measures a frequency

$$
\nu_ {\mathrm {S O I}} = \frac {\nu_ {\mathrm {S O R}}}{\mathrm {u} _ {\mathrm {S O R}} ^ {0}} = \nu_ {\mathrm {S O R}} (1 - 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}
$$

$$
\nu_ {\mathrm {S O I}} = \gamma \nu_ {0} (1 + \mathrm {v} \cos \alpha) (1 - 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}
$$

where $\mathbf{v}$ is given by Equation (4).

Solution 15.9.

(a) From the first integral of the motion, one has the equation

$$
\left(\mathrm {d r} / \mathrm {d t}\right) ^ {2} + \mathrm {V} ^ {2} (\mathrm {r}) = \tilde {\mathrm {E}} ^ {2} \tag {1}
$$

where $\mathbf{V}$ is as defined in Problem 15.11. If one now uses

$$
\mathrm {d} \phi / \mathrm {d} \tau = \mathrm {p} ^ {\phi} = \tilde {\mathrm {L}} / \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \tag {2}
$$

and defines the variable $\mathbf{u} \equiv \mathbf{M} / \mathbf{r}$ , then an equation relating $\mathbf{u}$ and $\phi$ may be obtained:

$$
\left(\frac {\mathrm {d} u}{\mathrm {d} \phi}\right) ^ {2} = \frac {\tilde {\mathrm {E}} ^ {2} - (1 - 2 u) \left(1 + \bar {\mathrm {L}} ^ {2} u ^ {2}\right)}{\bar {\mathrm {L}} ^ {2}} \tag {3}
$$

where $\overline{\mathbf{L}}\equiv \tilde{\mathbf{L}} /\mathbf{M}$ . Differentiate Equation (3) with respect to $\phi$ ..

$$
2 u ^ {\prime} u ^ {\prime \prime} = - (2 u \bar {L} ^ {2} - 2 - 6 \bar {L} ^ {2} u ^ {2}) u ^ {\prime} \bar {L} ^ {- 2}
$$

or

$$
\mathrm {u} ^ {\prime \prime} + \mathrm {u} = \frac {1}{\bar {\mathrm {L}} ^ {2}} + 3 \mathrm {u} ^ {2}. \tag {4}
$$

For large impact parameter, the right side of Equation (4) is small. Let $\mathfrak{u} = \mathfrak{u}_0 + \epsilon \mathfrak{v}$ , with the right hand side of Equation (4) = $\mathcal{O}(\epsilon)$ , then

$$
\mathrm {u} _ {0} = \mathrm {A} \cos \phi , \quad \mathrm {A} = \text {c o n s t a n t}, \tag {5}
$$

for suitable choice of $\mathbf{x}, \mathbf{y},$ axes.

Substitution of $\mathfrak{u}_0$ into Equation (4) yields

$$
v ^ {\prime \prime} + v = \bar {L} ^ {- 2} + 3 A ^ {2} \cos^ {2} \phi = \bar {L} ^ {- 2} + \frac {3}{2} A ^ {2} (1 + \cos 2 \phi). \tag {6}
$$

The solution to Equation (6) is

$$
\mathbf {v} = \overline {{{\mathbf {L}}}} ^ {- 2} + \frac {3}{2} \mathbf {A} ^ {2} - \frac {\mathbf {A} ^ {2}}{2} \cos 2 \phi = \overline {{{\mathbf {L}}}} ^ {- 2} + 2 \mathbf {A} ^ {2} - \mathbf {A} ^ {2} \cos^ {2} \phi
$$

and hence

$$
\mathbf {u} = \mathbf {A} \cos \phi - \mathbf {A} ^ {2} \cos^ {2} \phi + \overline {{\mathbf {L}}} ^ {- 2} + 2 \mathbf {A} ^ {2}. \tag {7}
$$

The asymptotes of the orbit are given by $\mathfrak{u} = 0$ i.e.

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {A} ^ {2} \cos^ {2} \phi - \mathbf {A} \cos \phi - \mathbf {B} = 0 \\ \mathbf {B} \equiv \frac {1}{\overline {{\mathbf {L}}} ^ {2}} + 2 \mathbf {A} ^ {2} \\ \cos \phi = \frac {\mathrm {A} - \left(\mathrm {A} ^ {2} + 4 \mathrm {A} ^ {2} \mathrm {B}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{2 \mathrm {A} ^ {2}} \approx - \frac {\mathrm {B}}{\mathrm {A}}. \tag {8} \\ \end{array}
$$

Since B/A is a small quantity, the solution to Equation (8) is

$$
\phi \approx \pi / 2 - \mathrm {B} / \mathrm {A}. \tag {9}
$$

Since there is equal deflection at both asymptotes, $(\phi \approx \pi /2, - \pi /2)$ the total deflection angle is

$$
\Delta \phi = 2 \mathrm {B} / \mathrm {A}. \tag {10}
$$

If $b$ is the distance of closest approach, then by the definition of $A$ in Equation (5)

$$
A = M / b. \tag {11}
$$

Also,

$$
\begin{array}{l} \overline {{L}} ^ {2} = \frac {L ^ {2}}{M ^ {2} m ^ {2}} = \frac {b ^ {2} (E ^ {2} - m ^ {2})}{M ^ {2} m ^ {2}} = \frac {b ^ {2} \beta^ {2}}{M ^ {2} (1 - \beta^ {2})} \\ \mathbf {B} = \frac {\mathbf {M} ^ {2} \left(1 - \beta^ {2}\right)}{\mathbf {b} ^ {2} \beta^ {2}} + \frac {2 \mathbf {M} ^ {2}}{\mathbf {b} ^ {2}} = \frac {\mathbf {M} ^ {2} \left(1 + \beta^ {2}\right)}{\mathbf {b} ^ {2} \beta^ {2}}. \tag {12} \\ \end{array}
$$

Combining Equations (10), (11), (12) one has

$$
\Delta \phi_ {\text {g r a v}} = \frac {2 \mathrm {M} \left(1 + \beta^ {2}\right)}{\mathrm {b} \beta^ {2}}. \tag {13}
$$

(b) The differential equation describing the trajectory is

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} t} (\mathrm {m y} \dot {\mathrm {r}}) = \gamma \mathrm {m r} \omega^ {2} - \frac {\mathrm {Z e} ^ {2}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}} \tag {14a}
$$

$$
\mathrm {m y r} ^ {2} \omega \equiv \mathrm {L} = \text {c o n s t a n t} \tag {14b}
$$

$$
m y - \frac {Z e ^ {2}}{r} \equiv E = c o n s t a n t. \tag {14c}
$$

From Equation (14b), $\mathrm{d} / \mathrm{d}t = (\mathrm{L} / \mathrm{myr}^2)\mathrm{d} / \mathrm{d}\phi$ so Equation (14a) becomes

$$
\frac {d ^ {2}}{d \phi^ {2}} u + \left(1 - \frac {Z ^ {2} e ^ {4}}{L ^ {2}}\right) u = \frac {Z e ^ {2} E}{L ^ {2}} \tag {15}
$$

where $\mathfrak{u}\equiv 1 / \mathfrak{r}$ . The solution to Equation (15) is

$$
u = \frac {1}{b} \cos r \phi + \frac {Z e ^ {2} E}{L ^ {2} - Z ^ {2} e ^ {4}}, \tag {16a}
$$

$$
\tau \equiv \left(1 - \frac {Z ^ {2} e ^ {4}}{L ^ {2}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}, \tag {16b}
$$

which reduces, for large L, to

$$
u = \frac {1}{b} \cos \phi + \frac {Z e ^ {2} E}{L ^ {2}}. \tag {17}
$$

Now, the asymptotes are given by the equation

$$
0 = \frac {1}{b} \cos \phi + \frac {Z e ^ {2} E}{L ^ {2}} ,
$$

or $\phi \approx \frac{\mathrm{bZe}^2\mathrm{E}}{\mathrm{L}}$ so that the total deflection angle is

$$
\Delta \phi_ {\mathrm {E M}} = \frac {2 \mathrm {b Z e} ^ {2} \mathrm {E}}{\mathrm {L}}. \tag {18}
$$

From the relations,

$$
E = m \left(1 - \beta^ {2}\right) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}}
$$

$$
L ^ {2} = \frac {b ^ {2} m ^ {2} \beta^ {2}}{1 - \beta^ {2}}
$$

Equation (18) becomes

$$
\Delta \phi_ {\mathbf {E M}} = \frac {2 Z e ^ {2} \left(1 - \beta^ {2}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{m b \beta^ {2}}. \tag {19}
$$

Equations (13) and (19) differ because of the difference in the tensor nature of gravity and vector nature of electromagnetism: the fields Lorentz transform differently and this induces a different dependence of the deflection on $\beta$ when $\beta$ is not small.

Solution 15.10. It is convenient to work in "outgoing Eddington-Finkelstein coordinates": Define $\mathbf{r}^*$ by $\mathrm{dr}^* / \mathrm{dr} = (1 - 2\mathbf{M} / \mathbf{r})^{-1}$ , that is

$$
\mathbf {r} ^ {*} = \mathbf {r} + 2 \mathbf {M} \log (\mathbf {r} / 2 \mathbf {M} - 1) + \text {c o n s t a n t}
$$

and define a retarded time coordinate

$$
\mathbf {u} = \mathbf {t} - \mathbf {r} ^ {*}
$$

The Schwarzschild metric in these coordinates is

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - (1 - 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r}) \mathrm {d u} ^ {2} - 2 \mathrm {d u d r} + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \mathrm {d} \Omega^ {2}. \tag {1}
$$

Putting $\mathrm{ds} = 0$ , we see that outgoing radial photons travel along lines $\mathbf{u} =$ constant. The redshift is calculated by comparing the time between reception of photons and emission of photons:

$$
\frac {\lambda_ {\infty}}{\lambda_ {\text {e m i t t e r}}} = \frac {(\Delta t) _ {\infty}}{(\Delta \tau) _ {\text {e m}}} = \frac {(\Delta u) _ {\infty}}{(\Delta \tau) _ {\text {e m}}} = \left. \frac {(\Delta u) _ {\text {e m}}}{(\Delta \tau) _ {\text {e m}}} = \frac {\mathrm {d} u}{\mathrm {d} \tau} \right| _ {\text {e m}}. \tag {2}
$$

Note how we can relate times at infinity to times at the emitter by using $u =$ constant along the rays.

We must therefore find the component $\mathrm{du} / \mathrm{d}\tau = \mathbf{U}^{\mathbf{u}}$ of the emitter's 4-velocity $\mathbf{U}$ as a function of t.

Now u and t are both ignorable coordinates and

$$
\mathrm {U} _ {\mathrm {t}} = \mathrm {U} _ {\mathrm {u}} = \text {c o n s t a n t} = - \tilde {\mathrm {E}} \tag {3}
$$

and $\mathbf{U}\cdot \mathbf{U} = -\mathbf{1}$ implies

![](images/cd41dda0fa8b84e6846d5afb92b49f93edb7e171ebda80df7eebe73c610b9a3f.jpg)

$$
U _ {r} = \frac {- \tilde {E} - \left(\tilde {E} ^ {2} - 1 + 2 M / r\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{1 - 2 M / r}, \tag {4}
$$

therefore

$$
\mathbf {U} ^ {\mathrm {u}} = \mathbf {g} ^ {\mathrm {u u}} \mathbf {U} _ {\mathrm {u}} + \mathbf {g} ^ {\mathrm {u r}} \mathbf {U} _ {\mathrm {r}} = 0 + \left[ \tilde {\mathbf {E}} + \left(\tilde {\mathbf {E}} ^ {2} - 1 + 2 \mathbf {M} / \mathbf {r}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \right] (1 - 2 \mathbf {M} / \mathbf {r}) ^ {- 1} \qquad (5)
$$

and

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {U} ^ {\mathrm {r}} = \mathbf {g} ^ {\mathrm {u r}} \mathbf {U} _ {\mathrm {u}} + \mathbf {g} ^ {\mathrm {r r}} \mathbf {U} _ {\mathrm {r}} = \tilde {\mathbf {E}} + \left[ - \tilde {\mathbf {E}} - \left(\tilde {\mathbf {E}} ^ {2} - 1 + 2 \mathbf {M} / \mathbf {r}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \right] \\ = - \left(\tilde {\mathrm {E}} ^ {2} - 1 + 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}. \tag {6} \\ \end{array}
$$

Thus

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {r}}{\mathrm {d} u} = \frac {\mathbf {U} ^ {r}}{\mathbf {U} ^ {u}} = \frac {- \left(\tilde {\mathbf {E}} ^ {2} - 1 + 2 \mathbf {M} / \mathbf {r}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} (1 - 2 \mathbf {M} / \mathbf {r})}{\tilde {\mathbf {E}} + \left(\tilde {\mathbf {E}} ^ {2} - 1 + 2 \mathbf {M} / \mathbf {r}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \tag {7}
$$

Near $\mathbf{r} = 2\mathbf{M}$ , Equation (7) gives

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {d} \mathbf {u} \approx - 2 (1 - 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r}) ^ {- 1} \mathrm {d} \mathbf {r} \approx - 2 (\mathrm {r} / 2 \mathrm {M} - 1) ^ {- 1} \mathrm {d} \mathbf {r} \\ u \approx - 4 M \log (r / 2 M - 1) + c o n s t a n t \\ 1 - 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r} \sim \exp (- \mathrm {u} / 4 \mathrm {M}). \\ \end{array}
$$

Thus Equation (5) implies that, as $\mathbf{r} \to 2\mathbf{M}$

$$
U ^ {u} \sim e ^ {+ u / 4 M}.
$$

But $\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{t} +$ constant for an observer at fixed (large) $\mathbf{r}$ , so finally

$$
\lambda_ {\text {e m i t t e r}} / \lambda_ {\infty} \sim \exp (- t / 4 M).
$$

Solution 15.11. For particle motion $\mathbf{u}_{\mathfrak{t}} \equiv \tilde{\mathbf{E}}$ and $\mathbf{u}_{\phi} \equiv \tilde{\mathbf{L}}$ are both constants of motion, so $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{u} = -1$ gives us

$$
\left(\mathrm {d} \mathbf {r} / \mathrm {d} \tau\right) ^ {2} + \mathbf {V} ^ {2} (\mathbf {r}) = \tilde {\mathbf {E}} ^ {2} \tag {1a}
$$

$$
V \equiv \left[ \left(1 - 2 M / r\right) \left(1 + \tilde {L} ^ {2} / r ^ {2}\right) \right] ^ {\frac {1}{2}}. \tag {1b}
$$

(See also MTW, Equation 25.15 or Weinberg, Equation 8.4.13.) By setting $\partial \mathbf{V}^2 / \partial \mathbf{r} = 0$ we can find the maximum of $\mathbf{V}^2$

$$
V _ {\max } ^ {2} = \frac {\bar {L} ^ {2} + 3 6 + (\bar {L} ^ {2} - 1 2) (1 - 1 2 / \bar {L} ^ {2}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{5 4} \tag {2a}
$$

$$
\bar {\mathrm {L}} \equiv \tilde {\mathrm {L}} / \mathrm {M}. \tag {2b}
$$

If a particle has $\overline{\mathbf{E}} >\mathbf{V}_{\max}$ , it is captured. Thus, the limiting $\overline{\mathbf{L}}$ for capture is found by equating

$$
\tilde {\mathbf {E}} ^ {2} = \mathbf {V} _ {\max } ^ {2}. \tag {3}
$$

(a) For large E (large $\overline{\mathbf{L}}$ ), Equation (2a) reduces to

$$
\mathrm {V} _ {\mathrm {m a x}} ^ {2} \approx \frac {\overline {{{\mathrm {L}}} ^ {2} + 3 6 + (\overline {{{\mathrm {L}}} ^ {2} - 1 2) (1 - 6 / \overline {{{\mathrm {L}}} ^ {2}})}}}{5 4} = \frac {\overline {{{\mathrm {L}}} ^ {2} + 9}}{2 7},
$$

and Equation (3) becomes

$$
\overline {{\mathrm {L}}} _ {\text {c r i t}} ^ {2} = 2 7 \tilde {\mathrm {E}} ^ {2} - 9. \tag {4}
$$

Corresponding to $\mathbf{L}_{\mathrm{crit}}$ is a critical impact parameter, $\mathsf{b}_{\mathrm{crit}}$ , given by

$$
\mathsf {b} _ {\mathsf {c r i t}} = \frac {\mathsf {L} _ {\mathsf {c r i t}}}{\mathsf {p}} = \frac {\mathsf {L} _ {\mathsf {c r i t}}}{(\mathsf {E} ^ {2} - \mathsf {m} ^ {2}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} ,
$$

where $m$ is the particle mass. Capture takes place for impact parameters $b < b_{\text{crit}}$ . Thus the cross section for capture then becomes

$$
\begin{array}{l} \sigma_ {\mathrm {c a p}} = \pi \mathrm {b} _ {\mathrm {c r i t}} ^ {2} = \frac {\pi \mathrm {L} _ {\mathrm {c r i t}} ^ {2}}{(\mathrm {E} ^ {2} - \mathrm {m} ^ {2})} = \frac {\pi \mathrm {M} ^ {2} \bar {\mathrm {L}} _ {\mathrm {c r i t}} ^ {2}}{(\tilde {\mathrm {E}} ^ {2} - 1)} \\ \approx \frac {\pi \mathrm {M} ^ {2}}{\tilde {\mathrm {E}} ^ {2}} \left(1 + \frac {1}{\tilde {\mathrm {E}} ^ {2}}\right) (2 7 \tilde {\mathrm {E}} ^ {2} - 9) \\ \end{array}
$$

or

$$
\pi \mathrm {b} _ {\mathrm {c r i t}} ^ {2} \approx 2 7 \pi \mathrm {M} ^ {2} \left(1 + \frac {2}{3 \tilde {\mathrm {E}} ^ {2}}\right). \tag {5}
$$

(b) For $\tilde{\mathbf{E}}\approx 1$ (small $\beta$ ) we have $\tilde{\mathbf{E}}^2\approx 1 + \beta^2$ and Equation (3) can be approximated by

$$
1 8 + 5 4 \beta^ {2} \approx \bar {\mathrm {L}} ^ {2} + (\bar {\mathrm {L}} ^ {2} - 1 2) ^ {\frac {3}{2}} / \bar {\mathrm {L}}.
$$

We can solve for $\overline{\mathbf{L}}^2$ to first order in $\beta^2$ to find

$$
\overline {{L}} _ {\text {c r i t}} ^ {2} = 1 6 (1 + 2 \beta^ {2}) + \mathcal {O} (\beta^ {4})
$$

and

$$
\sigma_ {\mathrm {c a p t}} = \pi \mathrm {b} _ {\mathrm {c r i t}} ^ {2} = \frac {\pi \mathrm {M} ^ {2} \overline {{\mathrm {L}}} _ {\mathrm {c r i t}} ^ {2}}{(\tilde {\mathrm {E}} ^ {2} - 1)} \approx \frac {1 6 \pi \mathrm {M} ^ {2}}{\beta^ {2}}. \tag {6}
$$

Solution 15.12. Choose Paul's orbit to be in the $\theta = \pi /2$ plane. Since the orbit is circular $\mathbf{u}^{\mathbf{r}} = 0$ and from the r-component of the geodesic equation, $\mathrm{Du}^{\mathrm{r}} / \mathrm{d}\tau = 0$ , we get

$$
\Gamma_ {t t} ^ {\mathbf {r}} \left(\mathbf {u} ^ {\mathbf {t}}\right) ^ {2} + \Gamma_ {\phi \phi} ^ {\mathbf {r}} \left(\mathbf {u} ^ {\phi}\right) ^ {2} = 0
$$

and hence

$$
\omega^ {2} \equiv \left(\frac {\mathrm {d} \phi}{\mathrm {d} t}\right) ^ {2} = \left(\frac {\mathrm {u} \phi}{\mathrm {u} ^ {\mathrm {t}}}\right) ^ {2} = - \frac {\Gamma_ {\mathrm {t t}} ^ {\mathrm {r}}}{\Gamma_ {\phi \phi} ^ {\mathrm {r}}} = \frac {\mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {3}} = \frac {1}{6 4 \mathrm {M} ^ {2}}
$$

for Paul's orbit. Since $\mathrm{d}\phi/\mathrm{d}t = 1/8\mathbf{M}$ and he traverses $20\pi$ radians between meetings, the coordinate time elapsed between meetings is

$$
\Delta t = 1 6 0 \pi \mathrm {M}.
$$

From $\mathbf{g}_{tt}(\mathbf{u}^{\mathrm{t}})^2 +\mathbf{g}_{\phi \phi}(\mathbf{u}^{\phi})^2 = -1$ we get

$$
\mathrm {u} ^ {\mathrm {t}} \equiv \frac {\mathrm {d t}}{\mathrm {d r}} = \left(1 - \frac {3 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} = 2
$$

for Paul's orbit. Thus Paul measures an elapsed proper time

$$
\Delta \tau_ {\text {P a u l}} = \frac {1}{2} \Delta t = 8 0 \pi \mathrm {M} \approx 2 5 1. 5 \mathrm {M}.
$$

For Peter's orbit we use the equations describing radial fall (Problem 15.4). The time for Peter to fall from $\mathbf{R}$ to $\mathbf{r} = 4\mathbf{M}$ must be $\frac{1}{2}\Delta t = 80\pi \mathbf{M}$ . We must find the values of $\mathbf{R}$ and $\eta$ corresponding to a lapse in $t$ of $80\pi \mathbf{M}$ . We can then find Peter's proper time from Solution 15.4, Equation (6):

$$
\Delta \tau_ {\text {P e t e r}} = 2 \left(\frac {\mathrm {R} ^ {3}}{8 \mathrm {M}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} (\eta + \sin \eta). \tag {1}
$$

The equations for R and $\eta$ are Equations (7) and (5) of Solution 15.4

$$
\begin{array}{l} 4 0 \pi = \log \left[ \frac {\left(\mathrm {X} - 1\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} + \tan \frac {1}{2} \eta}{\left(\mathrm {X} - 1\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} - \tan \frac {1}{2} \eta} \right] + (\mathrm {X} - 1) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \left[ \eta + \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {X} (\eta + \sin \eta) \right] (2) \\ 4 = \mathrm {X} (1 + \cos \eta) (3) \\ \end{array}
$$

where $\mathbf{X} = \mathbf{R} / 2\mathbf{M}$ .

Now use physical intuition: Peter travels outward for a considerable time while Paul orbits. Therefore $\mathbf{X}$ should be quite large and $\eta \approx \pi$ . Since logarithms are slowly varying functions, ignore the logarithm term and approximate

$$
4 0 \pi \approx \mathrm {X} ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \left(\pi + \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {X} \pi\right) .
$$

This gives $\mathbf{X}^{\frac{3}{2}} \approx 80\pi$ and $\mathbf{X} \approx 18.5$ . From Equation (3) then, $1 + \cos \eta \approx .216$ implies $\eta = 2.47$ . More accurately,

$$
\begin{array}{l} \eta = 2. 4 6 0 2 9 \quad X = \frac {R}{2 M} = 1 7. 9 1 7 3 7 \\ \Delta \tau_ {\text {P e t e r}} = 4 6 8. 7 2 \mathrm {M}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Paul measures a much smaller (about half) proper time interval than Peter. This makes sense because Paul remains in a highly relativistic orbit deep within a gravitational well - both factors make his clocks "go slow." Peter spends most of his time at fairly small velocities and not very deep in a gravitational well (remember $\mathbb{R} / 2\mathbb{M} \sim 18$ ).

Solution 15.13. We clearly need a transformation such that

$$
r ^ {2} = e ^ {2 \mu - r ^ {2}} \tag {1a}
$$

$$
e ^ {2 \Lambda} d r ^ {2} = e ^ {2 \mu} d \overline {{r}} ^ {2}. \tag {1b}
$$

When these are combined, we get a differential equation for the transformation $\overline{\mathbf{r}} = \overline{\mathbf{r}}(\mathbf{r})$ in terms of the known function $\Lambda$ :

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \overline {{r}}}{\mathrm {r}} = \mathrm {e} ^ {\Lambda} \frac {\mathrm {d} r}{\mathrm {r}}.
$$

This can immediately be integrated, and our solution is

$$
\bar {\mathrm {r}} = \text {c o n s t a n t} \times \exp \left(\int_ {r} \frac {\mathrm {e} ^ {\Lambda}}{\mathrm {r}} \mathrm {d r}\right) \tag {2a}
$$

$$
\mathrm {e} ^ {2 \mu} = \mathrm {r} ^ {2} / \overline {{\mathrm {r}}} ^ {2}. \tag {2b}
$$

For the Schwarzschild metric $e^{\Lambda} = (1 - 2M / r)^{-\frac{1}{2}}$ and we have from Equation (2a)

$$
\overline {{r}} = \text {c o n s t a n t} \times \exp \left(\int \frac {\mathrm {d r}}{\left(r ^ {2} - 2 \mathrm {M r}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}\right)
$$

which gives

$$
\mathrm {r} = \bar {\mathrm {r}} (1 + \mathrm {M} / 2 \bar {\mathrm {r}}) ^ {2} \tag {3}
$$

if we choose the integration constant so that $\mathbf{r} \to \overline{\mathbf{r}}$ as $\mathbf{r} \to \infty$ . Equation (3) can also be expressed as

$$
\overline {{\mathbf {r}}} = \frac {1}{2} [ \mathbf {r} - \mathbf {M} \pm (\mathbf {r} (\mathbf {r} - 2 \mathbf {M})) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} ]. \tag {4}
$$

From Equation (2b) we have

$$
\mathrm {e} ^ {2 \mu} = (1 + \mathrm {M} / 2 \overline {{\mathrm {r}}}) ^ {4}. \tag {5}
$$

From this it follows that the area of a sphere with $r$ and $t$ constant is

$$
A = (1 + M / 2 \bar {r}) ^ {4} \bar {r} ^ {2} \int d \theta \sin \theta d \phi = 4 \pi \bar {r} ^ {2} (1 + M / 2 \bar {r}) ^ {4}. \tag {6}
$$

In constructing the embedding diagram note that (i) according to Equation (4) the mapping from $\mathbf{r}$ to $\overline{\mathbf{r}}$ is double valued and (ii) the coordinate $\overline{\mathbf{r}}$ only describes the region of the Schwarzschild geometry with $\mathbf{r} \geq 2\mathbf{M}$ (In Equation (4), $\overline{\mathbf{r}}$ is complex for $\mathbf{r} < 2\mathbf{M}$ .)

The embedding diagram for a surface of constant $t$ and $r$ requires a function $z(r)$ such that

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = \mathrm {d z} ^ {2} + \mathrm {d r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2} = \left[ 1 + (\mathrm {d z} / \mathrm {d r}) ^ {2} \right] \mathrm {d r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2} \\ = (1 - 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r}) ^ {- 1} \mathrm {d r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2}. \\ \end{array}
$$

The solution is

$$
z = \left[ 8 M (r - 2 M) \right] ^ {\frac {1}{2}}. \tag {7}
$$

This parabola is plotted in the accompanying figure. The embedding surface is the paraboloid generated by rotating this curve about the $r$ axis.

![](images/c3ba25776dfdbfc3b547340d0fe9fdff2dab633f747f4b17557416899999aa94.jpg)

# Solution 15.14.

(a) Let the boost be in the $\mathbf{e}_{\mathbf{x}}^{\hat{\mathbf{\alpha}}}^{\hat{\mathbf{\alpha}}}$ direction. For a velocity parameter $\psi = \tanh^{-1}\beta$ the transformation is given by

$$
\Lambda_ {\hat {t}} ^ {\hat {t}} = \Lambda_ {\hat {x}} ^ {\hat {x}} = \cosh \psi \quad \Lambda_ {\hat {x}} ^ {\hat {t}} = \Lambda_ {\hat {t}} ^ {\hat {x}} = \sinh \psi
$$

so that

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathsf {R} _ {\hat {\mathbf {t}} ^ {\prime} \hat {\mathbf {x}} ^ {\prime} \hat {\mathbf {t}} ^ {\prime} \hat {\mathbf {x}} ^ {\prime}} = \Lambda_ {\hat {\mathbf {t}} ^ {\prime}} ^ {a} \Lambda_ {\hat {\mathbf {x}} ^ {\prime}} ^ {\beta} \Lambda_ {\hat {\mathbf {t}} ^ {\prime}} ^ {\gamma} \Lambda_ {\hat {\mathbf {x}}} ^ {\delta} \mathsf {R} _ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\alpha}}} \hat {\boldsymbol {\beta}} \hat {\boldsymbol {\gamma}} \hat {\boldsymbol {\delta}} \\ = \cosh^ {4} \psi R _ {\hat {t} \hat {x} \hat {t} \hat {x}} + \cosh^ {2} \psi \sinh^ {2} \psi R _ {\hat {t} \hat {x} \hat {x} \hat {t}} \\ + \sinh^ {2} \psi \cosh^ {2} \psi R _ {\hat {x} \hat {t} \hat {t} \hat {x}} + \sinh^ {4} \psi R _ {\hat {x} \hat {t} \hat {x} \hat {t}} \\ = (\cosh^ {4} \psi - 2 \sinh^ {2} \psi \cosh^ {2} \psi + \sinh^ {4} \psi) R _ {\hat {\mathbf {t}} \hat {\mathbf {x}}} \hat {\mathbf {t}} \hat {\mathbf {x}} \\ = (\cosh^ {2} \psi - \sinh^ {2} \psi) ^ {2} R _ {\hat {\textbf {t}} \hat {\textbf {x}}} \hat {\textbf {t}} \hat {\textbf {x}} \\ = R _ {\hat {t} \hat {x} \hat {t} \hat {x}} ^ {-} \\ \end{array}
$$

(b) In the Schwarzschild geometry the nonvanishing physical components of the Riemann tensor are

$$
\mathrm {R} _ {\mathrm {t r t r}} = \mathrm {R} _ {\hat {\theta}} \hat {\phi} \hat {\theta} \hat {\phi} = - 2 \mathrm {M / r ^ {3}}
$$

$$
\mathrm {R} _ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} \hat {\theta} _ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} \hat {\theta} = \mathrm {R} _ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} \hat {\phi} _ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} \hat {\phi} = - \mathrm {R} _ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} \hat {\theta} _ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} \hat {\theta} = - \mathrm {R} _ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} \hat {\phi} _ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} \hat {\phi} = \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r} ^ {3}
$$

and the components related to these by symmetry. To show that all physical components are invariant for a radial boost we can explicitly calculate 20 independent, boosted components in a straightforward manner. Another way is to define, in the Schwarzschild geometry, the vectors

$$
\mathbf {l} \equiv \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} + \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} \qquad \mathbf {n} \equiv \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} - \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} \qquad \mathbf {m} \equiv \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\theta}}} + \mathbf {i} \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\phi}}}
$$

and to observe that the Riemann tensor can be written as a sum of products of the vectors:

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {R} = \frac {1}{2} \frac {\mathbf {M}}{\mathbf {r} ^ {3}} \left\{- (\mathbf {n} \wedge \mathbf {l}) (\mathbf {n} \wedge \mathbf {l}) + (\mathbf {m} \wedge \mathbf {m} ^ {*}) (\mathbf {m} \wedge \mathbf {m} ^ {*}) \right. \\ + \operatorname {R e a l} \left[ (\mathbf {n} \wedge \mathbf {m}) (\mathbf {l} \wedge \mathbf {m} ^ {*}) + (\mathbf {l} \wedge \mathbf {m} ^ {*}) (\mathbf {n} \wedge \mathbf {m}) \right] \}. \\ \end{array}
$$

For a boost in the $r$ -direction with velocity parameter $\psi$ , clearly the components of $m$ are invariant. From $e_{\hat{t}} = \cosh \psi e_{\hat{t}'} - \sinh \psi e_{\hat{r}'}$ and $e_{\hat{r}} = \cosh \psi e_{\hat{r}'} - \sinh \psi e_{\hat{t}'}$ it follows that

$$
\mathbf {l} = \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} ^ {\wedge} + \mathbf {e} _ {\mathbf {r}} ^ {\wedge} = \mathbf {e} ^ {- \psi} \left(\mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} ^ {\wedge^ {\prime}} + \mathbf {e} _ {\mathbf {r}} ^ {\wedge^ {\prime}}\right)
$$

$$
\mathbf {n} = \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} - \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} = \mathbf {e} ^ {\psi} \left(\mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {t}} ^ {\prime}} + \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {r}} ^ {\prime}}\right)
$$

so that

$$
1 _ {\hat {\mu} ^ {\prime}} n _ {\hat {\nu} ^ {\prime}} = \dot {1} _ {\hat {\mu}} n _ {\hat {\nu}}.
$$

From the form of $\mathbf{R}$ then, it is clear that all components are invariant.

To see that this same conclusion does not apply to boosts in other directions, notice that for a boost in the $\phi$ direction, with velocity parameter $\psi$

$$
\begin{array}{l} R _ {\hat {r} ^ {\prime} \hat {t} ^ {\prime} \hat {r} ^ {\prime}} \hat {\phi} ^ {\prime} = \Lambda^ {a} _ {\hat {t} ^ {\prime}} \Lambda^ {\beta} _ {\hat {\phi} ^ {\prime}} R _ {\hat {r} a \hat {r} \beta} \\ = \sinh \psi \cosh \psi \left(R _ {\hat {r} \hat {t} \hat {r} \hat {t}} ^ {\prime} + R _ {\hat {r} \hat {\phi} \hat {\sigma} \hat {\phi}} ^ {\prime}\right) \\ = - \left(\mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r} ^ {3}\right) \sinh \psi \cosh \psi \\ \neq \mathrm {R} _ {\hat {\mathrm {f}} \hat {\mathrm {r}} \hat {\mathrm {f}}} ^ {-} \hat {\phi}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Solution 15.15. In Kruskal coordinates, the Schwarzschild geometry is given by

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = \frac {3 2 M ^ {3}}{\mathrm {r}} \mathrm {e} ^ {- \mathrm {r} / 2 \mathrm {M}} (- \mathrm {d v} ^ {2} + \mathrm {d u} ^ {2}) + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \mathrm {d} \Omega^ {2} \tag {1a}
$$

where

$$
\left(\frac {\mathrm {r}}{2 \mathrm {M}} - 1\right) \mathrm {e} ^ {\mathrm {r} / 2 \mathrm {M}} = \mathrm {u} ^ {2} - \mathrm {v} ^ {2}. \tag {1b}
$$

Setting $\mathbf{v} = \mathbf{\theta}$ constant, and $\theta = \pi /2$ in Equation (1a) and equating the line element to a Euclidean surface of revolution gives

$$
\frac {3 2 \mathrm {M} ^ {3}}{\mathrm {r}} \mathrm {e} ^ {- \mathrm {r} / 2 \mathrm {M}} \mathrm {d u} ^ {2} + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2} = [ 1 + (\mathrm {d z} / \mathrm {d r}) ^ {2} ] \mathrm {d r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2} \tag {2}
$$

or

$$
1 + \left(\frac {\mathrm {d} z}{\mathrm {d r}}\right) ^ {2} = \frac {3 2 \mathrm {M} ^ {3}}{\mathrm {r}} \mathrm {e} ^ {- \mathrm {r} / 2 \mathrm {M}} \left(\frac {\mathrm {d u}}{\mathrm {d r}}\right) ^ {2}.
$$

We can calculate du/dr from Equation (1b) to arrive at

$$
1 + \left(\frac {\mathrm {d} z}{\mathrm {d r}}\right) ^ {2} = \frac {\mathrm {r}}{2 \mathrm {M}} \frac {\mathrm {e} ^ {\mathrm {r} / 2 \mathrm {M}}}{(\mathrm {r} / 2 \mathrm {M} - 1) \mathrm {e} ^ {\mathrm {r} / 2 \mathrm {M}} + \mathrm {v} ^ {2}}. \tag {3}
$$

For embedding, $z$ must be a real function, so we must have

$$
\frac {\mathrm {r} / 2 \mathrm {M} \mathrm {e} ^ {\mathrm {r} / 2 \mathrm {M}}}{(\mathrm {r} / 2 \mathrm {M} - 1) \mathrm {e} ^ {\mathrm {r} / 2 \mathrm {M}} + \mathrm {v} ^ {2}} \geq 1. \tag {4}
$$

From Equation (4), we conclude

$$
r / 2 M \geq \log v ^ {2}.
$$

Thus, for $|v| > 1$ , there will be a minimum value of $r$ inside which the embedding breaks down.

Geometrically, the necessary condition for embedding is that

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} (\text {c i r c u m f e r e n c e})}{\mathrm {d} (\text {p r o p e r r a d i u s})} \leq 2 \pi .
$$

We now consider the general condition on dv/du which allows Euclidean embedding: Suppose we have a slice described by $\mathbf{v}(\mathbf{u})$ . Then

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = \frac {3 2 \mathrm {M} ^ {3}}{\mathrm {r}} \mathrm {e} ^ {- \mathrm {r} / 2 \mathrm {M}} \left[ 1 - \left(\frac {\mathrm {d v}}{\mathrm {d u}}\right) ^ {2} \right] \mathrm {d u} ^ {2} + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2} \tag {6} \\ = \frac {3 2 M ^ {3}}{r} e ^ {- r / 2 M} \left[ 1 - \left(\frac {d v}{d u}\right) ^ {2} \right] \left[ \frac {(r / 8 M ^ {2}) e ^ {r / 2 M}}{(u - v \frac {d v}{d u})} \right] ^ {2} d r ^ {2} + r ^ {2} d \phi^ {2} \\ \end{array}
$$

and we require

$$
\frac {\mathrm {r}}{2 \mathrm {M}} \mathrm {e} ^ {\mathrm {r} / 2 \mathrm {M}} \left[ 1 - \left(\frac {\mathrm {d v}}{\mathrm {d u}}\right) ^ {2} \right] \geq \left(\mathrm {u} - \mathrm {v} \frac {\mathrm {d v}}{\mathrm {d u}}\right) ^ {2} \tag {7}
$$

at each r.

Solution 15.16. To avoid the suggestive connotations of coordinate names, relabel $\mathbf{r}$ and $\mathbf{t}$ as follows: $\mathbf{r} \rightarrow \mathbf{z}$ , $\mathbf{t} \rightarrow \mathbf{w}$ . The metric then takes the form

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - \mathrm {d w} ^ {2} + \frac {4}{9} \left[ \frac {(9 / 2) \mathrm {M}}{z - \mathrm {w}} \right] ^ {\frac {2}{3}} \mathrm {d z} ^ {2} + \left[ (9 / 2) \mathrm {M} (z - \mathrm {w}) ^ {2} \right] ^ {\frac {2}{3}} \mathrm {d} \Omega^ {2}. \tag {1}
$$

Now, define a new coordinate, $\mathbf{r}$ , by

$$
r = \left[ \frac {9}{2} M (z - w) ^ {2} \right] ^ {\frac {1}{3}}. \tag {2}
$$

(We are led to do this because Equation (1) is manifestly spherically symmetric and therefore, the coefficient of $\mathrm{d}\Omega^2$ is a geometrically defined curvature radius.) With r thus defined we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} - \left(\frac {2 r ^ {3}}{9 M}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} + z = w (3a) \\ \mathrm {d} \mathbf {w} = \mathrm {d} \mathbf {z} - \left(\frac {\mathbf {r}}{2 \mathbf {M}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d} \mathbf {r}. (3b) \\ \end{array}
$$

Substituting Equations (2), (3) into Equation (1) gives:

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) \mathrm {d z} ^ {2} + 2 \left(\frac {\mathrm {r}}{2 \mathrm {M}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d z} \mathrm {d r} - \frac {\mathrm {r}}{2 \mathrm {M}} \mathrm {d r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \mathrm {d} \Omega^ {2}. \tag {4}
$$

Next we want to try to diagonalize the $\mathbf{dz}^2$ , $\mathbf{dz} \, \mathrm{d}\mathbf{r}$ , and $\mathbf{dr}^2$ terms: Define a coordinate $t$ and a function $F(r)$ by

$$
\mathbf {z} = \mathbf {t} + \mathbf {F} (\mathbf {r}), \tag {5}
$$

substitute this into Equation (4):

$$
\begin{array}{l} d s ^ {2} = - \left(1 - \frac {2 M}{r}\right) \left(d t ^ {2} + 2 F ^ {\prime} d t d r + F ^ {\prime 2} d r ^ {2}\right) \\ + 2 \left(\frac {\mathrm {r}}{2 \mathrm {M}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d r} (\mathrm {d t} + \mathrm {F} ^ {\prime} \mathrm {d r}) - \cdot \frac {\mathrm {r}}{2 \mathrm {M}} \mathrm {d r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \mathrm {d} \Omega^ {2}, \tag {6} \\ \end{array}
$$

and choose $\mathbf{F}$ so that metric is diagonal, i.e.

$$
\left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) \mathrm {F} ^ {\prime} = \left(\frac {\mathrm {r}}{2 \mathrm {M}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}. \tag {7}
$$

With this choice for $\mathbf{F}^{\prime}$ , Equation (6) becomes

$$
