\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) \mathrm {d t} ^ {2} - \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) ^ {- 1} \left(\frac {\mathrm {r}}{2 \mathrm {M}}\right) \mathrm {d r} ^ {2} \\ + 2 \left(\frac {\mathrm {r}}{2 \mathrm {M}}\right) \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) ^ {- 1} \mathrm {d r} ^ {2} - \frac {\mathrm {r}}{2 \mathrm {M}} \mathrm {d r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \mathrm {d} \Omega^ {2} \\ \end{array}
$$

or

$$
d s ^ {2} = - \left(1 - \frac {2 M}{r}\right) d t ^ {2} + \left(1 - \frac {2 M}{r}\right) ^ {- 1} d r ^ {2} + r ^ {2} d \Omega^ {2} \tag {8}
$$

which is the Schwarzschild metric in curvature coordinates and is, of course, a static spacetime. The original coordinates of Equation (1) are called “Lemaitre coordinates.” Its time coordinate $w$ measures the proper time of freely-infalling observers; each observer moves along a line $z =$ constant.

Solution 15.17. A coordinate-stationary observer in Problem 15.16 satisfies $z =$ constant (see solution to 15.16). By Equation (5) of Solution 15.16, this implies

$$
\mathrm {d t} = - \mathrm {F} ^ {\prime} \mathrm {d r} = - \left(\frac {\mathrm {r}}{2 \mathrm {M}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) ^ {- 1} \mathrm {d r} \tag {1}
$$

according to Equation (7) of 15.16. From this equation we get

$$
\left(\mathrm {d r} / \mathrm {d t}\right) ^ {2} = (2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r}) \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) ^ {2}. \tag {2}
$$

Comparison of Equation (2) with Equation (3) of Solution 15.3 (with $\mathfrak{u}_0 = 1$ for a particle falling from rest at infinity) shows that Equation (2) represents a radially falling particle, with unit energy-to-rest-mass ratio at infinity.

Solution 15.18. The relativistic Bernoulli equation (Problem 14.7)

$$
u _ {0} = \left(\frac {n}{\rho + p}\right) \left(\frac {n _ {\infty}}{p _ {\infty} + \rho_ {\infty}}\right) ^ {- 1} \tag {1}
$$

can be expressed in terms of the fluid's proper radial velocity $\mathbf{v} = (1 - 2\mathbf{M} / \mathbf{r})\mathrm{d}\mathbf{r} / \mathrm{d}t$ , by using the relationship between $\mathbf{u}_0$ and $\mathrm{dr} / \mathrm{dt}$

$$
\mathrm {g} _ {0 0} \left(\mathrm {u} ^ {0}\right) ^ {2} + \mathrm {g} _ {\mathrm {r r}} \left(\mathrm {u} ^ {\mathrm {r}}\right) ^ {2} = - 1,
$$

to yield

$$
v ^ {2} = 1 - \frac {\left(1 - \frac {2 M}{r}\right) \left(\frac {\rho + p}{n}\right) ^ {2}}{\left(\frac {\rho_ {\infty} + p _ {\infty}}{n _ {\infty}}\right) ^ {2}}. \tag {2}
$$

One can now rest mass conservation $(\mathbf{u}^{\alpha}\mathbf{n}|\mathbf{g}|^{\frac{1}{2}})_{,\alpha} = 0$ to obtain (after again expressing $\mathbf{u}^{\mathbf{r}}$ in terms of $\mathbf{v}$ )

$$
\frac {\mathrm {v} \left(1 - 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {n r} ^ {2}}{\left(1 - \mathrm {v} ^ {2}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} = \text {c o n s t a n t} \equiv \frac {\dot {\mathrm {M}}}{4 \pi}. \tag {3}
$$

Incidentally, in this solution $\mathfrak{n}$ is a rest mass density, not a number density: $\mathfrak{n}_{\text{here}} \equiv \mathfrak{m}_{\mathfrak{p}} \mathfrak{n}_{\text{usual}}$ .

Now, we would like to express $n$ in terms of the sound speed $a$ (using $p = Kn^{\gamma}$ ; compare Solution 5.25):

$$
a ^ {2} = \frac {\mathrm {d p}}{\mathrm {d} \rho} = \frac {\gamma \mathrm {K n} ^ {\gamma - 1}}{1 + \gamma \mathrm {K n} ^ {\gamma - 1} / (\gamma - 1)}.
$$

This implies

$$
\gamma \mathrm {K n} ^ {\gamma - 1} = \frac {\mathrm {a} ^ {2}}{1 - \mathrm {a} ^ {2} / (\gamma - 1)} = \frac {\mathrm {a} ^ {2} (\rho + \mathrm {p})}{\mathrm {n}} \tag {4}
$$

or

$$
n = \left[ \frac {a ^ {2}}{\gamma K \left(1 - a ^ {2} / (\gamma - 1)\right)} \right] ^ {\frac {1}{\gamma} - 1}. \tag {5}
$$

For the purposes of finding a self-consistent solution for some constant M and some v(r), Equations (2), (3) and (5) can be thought of as defining M as a function of r and v. We can then find the radius at which the flow becomes supersonic by setting v = a and setting dM = 0. First we must express r in terms of a. Using Equations (3) and (4), (with v=a), we get

$$
r = \frac {2 M}{1 - \left(1 - a ^ {2} / (y - 1)\right) ^ {2} \left(1 - a ^ {2}\right) \tau_ {\infty}} \tag {6}
$$

where

$$
\tau_ {\infty} \equiv \left(\frac {\rho + \mathrm {p}}{\mathrm {n}}\right) _ {\infty}.
$$

Substituting Equations (6), (5) into Equation (3), we get

$$
\frac {\dot {\mathrm {M}}}{4 \pi} = \frac {4 \mathrm {M} ^ {2} \mathrm {a} \left(1 - \frac {\mathrm {a} ^ {2}}{\gamma - 1}\right) \left[ \frac {\mathrm {a} ^ {2}}{\gamma \mathrm {K} \left(1 - \mathrm {a} ^ {2} / (\gamma - 1)\right)} \right] ^ {\frac {1}{\gamma - 1}}}{\left[ 1 - \left(1 - \mathrm {a} ^ {2}\right) \left(1 - \mathrm {a} ^ {2} / (\gamma - 1)\right) ^ {2} r _ {\infty} \right] ^ {2}}. \tag {7}
$$

Now, we expand Equation (4)

$$
\tau_ {\infty} \approx 1 + \frac {a _ {\infty} ^ {2}}{\gamma - 1}
$$

and work to lowest order in a. From $d\log M / da = 0$ , we have

$$
a ^ {2} = \frac {2 a _ {\infty} ^ {2}}{5 - 3 \gamma}, \quad \gamma \neq 5 / 3 \tag {8a}
$$

$$
a ^ {2} = 2 \cdot 3 ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} a _ {\infty}, \quad \gamma = 5 / 3. \tag {8b}
$$

Using Equations (8) and Equation (6), we conclude finally that the radius at which the flow becomes supersonic is

$$
r _ {s} = \left(\frac {5 - 3 \gamma}{8}\right) \frac {2 M}{a _ {\infty} ^ {2}} \quad \gamma \neq 5 / 3 \tag {9a}
$$

$$
\mathrm {r} _ {\mathrm {s}} = \frac {3 ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {M}}{4 \mathrm {a} _ {\infty}} \quad \gamma = 5 / 3. \tag {9b}
$$

Solution 15.19. The form of $\square \Phi$ for a diagonal metric is

$$
\square \Phi = \nabla \cdot (\nabla \Phi) = (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \left[ (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {g} ^ {\alpha \beta} \Phi_ {, \beta} \right], a \tag {1}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \square \Phi = (- \mathbf {g}) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \left[ \left\{\left(- \mathbf {g}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {g} ^ {0 0} \Phi , _ {0} \right\}, _ {0} + \left\{\left(- \mathbf {g}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {g} ^ {\mathrm {r r}} \Phi , _ {\mathrm {r}} \right\}, _ {\mathrm {r}} \right. \tag {2} \\ + \{(- g) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} g ^ {\theta} \theta \Phi , \theta \}, \theta + \{(- g) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} g ^ {\phi} \phi \Phi , \phi \}, \phi \rbrack . \\ \end{array}
$$

In the Schwarzschild metric, curvature coordinates,

$$
\mathrm {g} ^ {0 0} = - \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) ^ {- 1}, \quad \mathrm {g} ^ {\mathrm {r r}} = \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) \tag {3}
$$

and $\mathbf{g}^{\theta \theta}$ and $\mathbf{g}^{\phi \phi}$ have their flat space form. It is clear that the third and fourth terms of Equation (2) have their flat space form (with flat space angular operator $\mathbf{L}^2$ ), and thus, Equation (2) has the form

$$
\square \Phi = - \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) ^ {- 1} \Phi , _ {0 0} + \frac {1}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}} \left[ \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) \Phi , _ {\mathrm {r}} \right], _ {\mathrm {r}} + \frac {\mathrm {L} ^ {2} \Phi}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}}. \tag {4}
$$

The field equations $\square \Phi = 0$ then imply that

$$
0 = \Phi , _ {0 0} - \frac {1}{r ^ {2}} \left(1 - \frac {2 M}{r}\right) \left[ r ^ {2} \left(1 - \frac {2 M}{r}\right) \Phi , _ {r} \right], _ {r} - \frac {L ^ {2}}{r ^ {2}} \left(1 - \frac {2 M}{r}\right) \Phi . \tag {5}
$$

Let $\Phi$ have the form

$$
\Phi = \frac {1}{r} \psi (r, t) Y _ {\ell m} (\theta , \phi). \tag {6}
$$

Substituting Equation (6) into Equation (5), we have

$$
\psi_ {, 0 0} - \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) \left[ \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) \psi_ {, \mathrm {r}} \right] _ {, \mathrm {r}} + \mathrm {V} _ {\ell} (\mathrm {r}) \psi = 0 \tag {7a}
$$

where

$$
\mathrm {V} _ {\ell} (\mathrm {r}) = \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) \left[ \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {3}} + \frac {\ell (\ell + 1)}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}} \right] \quad . \tag {7b}
$$

Solution 15.20. The Brans-Dicke field equations can be put into the form

$$
\mathrm {G} ^ {\mu \nu} + \mathrm {F} ^ {\mu \nu} \left(\Phi_ {; \alpha}, \Phi_ {; \alpha} \beta\right) = \mathrm {T} ^ {\mu \nu}, \tag {1a}
$$

$$
\square \Phi = T _ {\mu} ^ {\mu} \tag {1b}
$$

where every term of $\mathbf{F}^{\mu \nu}$ is proportional to derivatives of the scalar field $\Phi$ . In vacuum, a solution of Equation (1b) is

$$
\Phi = \text {c o n s t a n t}. \tag {2}
$$

Substituting Equation (2) into (1a), one obtains

$$
\mathbf {G} ^ {\mu \nu} = 0,
$$

which are the vacuum Einstein equation. Consequently, the Schwarzschild metric is a solution of the vacuum Brans-Dicke equations. (The most general static, spherically symmetric vacuum metric in Brans-Dicke theory has two arbitrary parameters corresponding to mass and scalar charge. A spherical black hole has zero scalar charge and is the Schwarzschild solution.)

# CHAPTER 16: SOLUTIONS

Solution 16.1. Since an observer's time axis points along his 4-velocity, if he is stationary in the $(t,r,\theta,\phi)$ coordinate system then his time axis must be along $\partial/\partial t$ (and normalized properly). Accordingly, take $\mathbf{e}_{\hat{\mathbf{o}}} = \mathbf{f}\partial/\partial t$ , where $\mathbf{f}$ is to be determined by normalization:

$$
- \mathbf {1} = \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\boldsymbol {0}}} \cdot \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\boldsymbol {0}}} = \mathbf {f} ^ {2} \frac {\partial}{\partial t} \cdot \frac {\partial}{\partial t} = \mathbf {f} ^ {2} \mathbf {g} _ {0 0} = - \mathbf {f} ^ {2} \mathbf {e} ^ {2 \hat {\boldsymbol {\Phi}}}.
$$

Thus $\mathbf{f} = \mathbf{e}^{-\Phi}$ and

$$
\mathbf {e} _ {\hat {0}} = \mathbf {e} ^ {- \Phi} \frac {\partial}{\partial t}. \tag {1}
$$

The three directions, $\partial/\partial\theta$ , $\partial/\partial\phi$ , $\partial/\partial\mathbf{r}$ are all orthogonal to $\partial/\partial t$ and choosing basis vectors along them, with unit normalization (same technique as above) yields

$$
\mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} = \mathrm {e} ^ {- \mu} \frac {\partial}{\partial \mathrm {r}}, \quad \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\theta}} = \mathrm {e} ^ {- \mu} \mathrm {r} ^ {- 1} \frac {\partial}{\partial \theta}, \quad \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\phi}} = \mathrm {e} ^ {- \mu} (\mathrm {r} \sin \theta) ^ {- 1} \frac {\partial}{\partial \phi}. \tag {2}
$$

The basis vectors in Equations (1) and (2) constitute an orthonormal proper reference frame for a stationary observer. Dual 1-forms, $\tilde{\omega}^{\hat{a}}$ , are found by the requirement

$$
<   \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {a}}, e _ {\hat {\beta}} > = \delta^ {a} \beta \tag {3}
$$

and are easily seen to be

$$
\tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {0}} = \mathrm {e} ^ {\phi} \widetilde {\mathrm {d t}}, \quad \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\mathrm {r}}} = \mathrm {e} ^ {\mu} \widetilde {\mathrm {d r}}, \quad \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\theta}} = \mathrm {e} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {r} \widetilde {\mathrm {d} \theta}, \quad \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\phi}} = \mathrm {e} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {r} \sin \theta \widetilde {\mathrm {d} \phi}. \tag {4}
$$

Solution 16.2. The observer makes measurements in his local orthonormal

frame $\mathbf{e}_{\hat{\mathfrak{t}}} = \mathbf{e}^{-\Phi}\mathbf{e}_{\mathfrak{t}},\quad \mathbf{e}_{\hat{\mathfrak{r}}} = \mathbf{e}^{-\lambda}\mathbf{e}_{\mathfrak{r}},\quad \mathbf{e}_{\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}} = \mathbf{r}^{-1}\mathbf{e}_{\boldsymbol{\theta}},\quad \mathbf{e}_{\hat{\boldsymbol{\phi}}} = (\mathbf{r}\sin \theta)^{-1}\mathbf{e}_{\boldsymbol{\phi}};$

$$
\tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {t}} = e ^ {\Phi} \widetilde {d t}, \quad \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {r}} = e ^ {\lambda} \widetilde {d r}, \quad \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\theta}} = r \widetilde {d \theta}, \quad \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\phi}} = r \sin \theta d \widetilde {\phi}.
$$

Here the metric is as given in the introduction, except $(1 - 2\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{r})^{-1}\equiv \mathrm{e}^{2\lambda}$ . The volume of a fluid element which he measures is

$$
\begin{array}{l} V = \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {r}} \wedge \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\theta}} \wedge \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {\phi}} \\ = e ^ {\lambda} r ^ {2} \sin \theta \widetilde {d r} \wedge \widetilde {d \theta} \wedge \widetilde {d \phi} \\ = e ^ {\lambda} r ^ {2} \sin \theta d r d \theta d \phi . \\ \end{array}
$$

The pressure at opposite points on the vertical walls of the fluid element are the same, but because of the radial pressure gradient, the top and bottom pressures are not:

$$
\left| p _ {t o p} - p _ {b o t t o m} \right| = \left| p _ {, r} \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {r}} \right| = \left| p _ {, r} e ^ {- \lambda} \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {r}} \right|.
$$

Thus,

$$
\begin{array}{l} | \mathbf {F} _ {\text {b u o y ,}} | = | (\mathrm {p} _ {\text {t o p}} - \mathrm {p} _ {\text {b o t t o m}}) \times \text {a r e a} | \\ = | \mathbf {p} _ {r, r} e ^ {- \lambda_ {\omega} \hat {r} _ {\omega} \hat {\theta} _ {\omega} \hat {\phi}} | \\ = | p _ {r} e ^ {- \lambda V} |. \\ \end{array}
$$

Since the direction of the buoyant force is radial,

$$
\mathbf {F} _ {\text {b u o y ,}} = \mathrm {e} ^ {- \lambda} \mathrm {p} _ {, r} \mathrm {V e} _ {\hat {\mathrm {r}}} = \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {m}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {p} _ {, r} \mathrm {V e} _ {\hat {\mathrm {r}}} .
$$

Using the OV equation for $\mathbf{p}_{\cdot ,\mathbf{r}}$ we find

$$
\mathbf {F} _ {\text {g r a v .}} = - \mathbf {F} _ {\text {b u o y .}} = - \frac {(\rho + p) (m + 4 \pi r ^ {3} p) V e _ {\mathrm {f}}}{r ^ {2} \left(1 - \frac {2 m}{r}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}
$$

The Newtonian result is

$$
\mathrm {p} _ {\mathrm {r}} \mathrm {V e} _ {\mathrm {r}} = - \frac {\rho \mathrm {m V}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}} \mathrm {e} _ {\mathrm {r}} ^ {\prime}.
$$

Solution 16.3. A spherically symmetric metric can depend only on $t$ , $dt$ , $r$ , $dr$ , and $d\theta^2 + \sin^2\theta d\phi^2$ :

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - \mathrm {A} (\mathrm {r}, \mathrm {t}) \mathrm {d t} ^ {2} + \mathrm {B} (\mathrm {r}, \mathrm {t}) \mathrm {d r} ^ {2} + 2 \mathrm {C} (\mathrm {r}, \mathrm {t}) \mathrm {d r} \mathrm {d t} + \mathrm {D} (\mathrm {r}, \mathrm {t}) \left(\mathrm {d} \theta^ {2} + \sin^ {2} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2}\right).
$$

Choose a new radial variable $\mathbf{r}' = \mathbf{D}^{\frac{1}{2}}(\mathbf{r}, t)$ . Dropping the prime, we have

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - \mathrm {E} (\mathrm {r}, \mathrm {t}) \mathrm {d t} ^ {2} + \mathrm {F} (\mathrm {r}, \mathrm {t}) \mathrm {d r} ^ {2} + 2 \mathrm {G} (\mathrm {r}, \mathrm {t}) \mathrm {d r} \mathrm {d t} + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \left(\mathrm {d} \theta^ {2} + \sin^ {2} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2}\right).
$$

Eliminate G by defining a new time variable

$$
\mathrm {d} t ^ {\prime} = \mathrm {H} (\mathrm {r}, \mathrm {t}) [ \mathrm {E} (\mathrm {r}, \mathrm {t}) \mathrm {d} t - \mathrm {G} (\mathrm {r}, \mathrm {t}) \mathrm {d r} ]
$$

where $H$ is an integrating factor chosen to make the right-hand side a perfect differential. The metric then takes the standard form (with the prime dropped)

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - \mathrm {e} ^ {2 \Phi (\mathrm {r}, \mathrm {t})} \mathrm {d t} ^ {2} + \mathrm {e} ^ {2 \lambda (\mathrm {r}, \mathrm {t})} \mathrm {d r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \left(\mathrm {d} \theta^ {2} + \sin^ {2} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2}\right).
$$

From Problem 9.20, the Einstein tensor has nontrivial components

$$
G _ {\hat {t} \hat {t}} = 2 e ^ {- 2 \lambda} \lambda_ {, r} / r + (1 - e ^ {- 2 \lambda}) / r ^ {2} \tag {1}
$$

$$
\mathbf {G} _ {\hat {\mathrm {t r}}} = 2 \mathrm {e} ^ {- (\Phi + \lambda)} \lambda_ {, t} / \mathrm {r} \tag {2}
$$

$$
\mathrm {G} _ {\mathrm {r r}} ^ {\hat {\lambda}} = \left(\mathrm {e} ^ {- 2 \lambda} - 1\right) / \mathrm {r} ^ {2} + 2 \mathrm {e} ^ {- 2 \lambda} \Phi_ {, \mathrm {r}} / \mathrm {r} \tag {3}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {G} \hat {\theta} \hat {\theta} = \mathbf {G} _ {\hat {\phi}} \hat {\phi} = e ^ {- 2 \lambda} \left(\Phi_ {, r r} + \Phi_ {, r} ^ {2} - \Phi_ {, r} \lambda , r + \Phi_ {, r} / r - \lambda , r / r\right) \tag {4} \\ - \mathrm {e} ^ {- 2 \Phi} \left(\lambda_ {, \mathrm {t t}} + \lambda_ {, \mathrm {t}} ^ {2} - \Phi_ {, \mathrm {t}} \lambda_ {, \mathrm {t}}\right). \\ \end{array}
$$

When the vacuum field equations $\mathbf{G}_{\hat{\alpha}\hat{\beta}} = 0$ are imposed, Equation (2) shows that $\lambda$ is independent of $t$ and Equation (1) becomes

$$
\frac {2 \mathrm {d} \lambda}{1 - \mathrm {e} ^ {2 \lambda}} = \frac {\mathrm {d r}}{\mathrm {r}}
$$

from which we find

$$
e ^ {2 \lambda} = \left(1 - \frac {2 M}{r}\right) ^ {- 1}. \tag {5}
$$

The constant of integration has been chosen as 2M for convenience. Equations (3) and (4) are now equivalent equations determining $\Phi$ . (They

had to be equivalent because of the identity $\mathbf{G}^{\hat{\beta}}_{\hat{\alpha};\hat{\beta}} = 0$ .) The quickest way to find $\Phi$ is to add Equation (1) to Equation (3). This gives $\Phi_{,r} = -\lambda_{,r}$ so $e^{2\Phi} = f(t)e^{-2\lambda}$ . By defining a new time variable $dt' = f^{\frac{1}{2}}(t)dt$ , $f$ can be eliminated, so the final form of the metric is

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) \mathrm {d t} ^ {2} + \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) ^ {- 1} \mathrm {d r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} (\mathrm {d} \theta^ {2} + \sin^ {2} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2}).
$$

This is the Schwarzschild metric which is static.

Note: The r coordinate used here is defined by $4\pi \mathbf{r}^2 =$ surface area of spheres defined by the spherical symmetry. If r were not a monotonic function, it could not be used as a coordinate. However, a detailed investigation of this case (MTW, p. 845) shows there are no other solutions.

Solution 16.4. From Birkhoff's Theorem (see Problem 16.3), we know that the line element in vacuum for a spherically symmetric system is the Schwarzschild solution. Inside a self-gravitating hollow sphere we therefore have a Schwarzschild solution, but here the point $\mathbf{r} = 0$ is not a singularity; therefore, the mass $\mathbf{M}$ (which is actually an integration constant in the solution of Einstein's equations) must be set to zero to avoid infinite values of the $\mathbf{M} / \mathbf{r}$ terms in the metric. The metric inside the hollow sphere is therefore a flat space metric and consequently a test particle there experiences no forces.

Solution 16.5. The vacuum field equations for the scalar field $\Phi$ and metric $\mathbf{g}_{\mu \nu}$ in the Brans-Dicke theory are

$$
\begin{array}{l} R _ {\alpha \beta} - \frac {1}{2} g _ {\alpha \beta} R = \frac {\omega}{\Phi^ {2}} \left(\Phi_ {, \alpha} \Phi_ {, \beta} - \frac {1}{2} g _ {\alpha \beta} \Phi_ {, \gamma} \Phi^ {, \gamma}\right) (1) \\ + \Phi^ {- 1} \left(\Phi_ {, a; \beta} - \mathrm {g} _ {a \beta} \square \Phi\right) \\ \square \Phi = 0. (2) \\ \end{array}
$$

If the standard form of the spherically symmetric static metric $(-e^{2U}dt^2 + e^{2\lambda}dr^2 + r^2 d\Omega^2)$ is used, $\square \Phi = 0$ becomes

$$
\left(\mathrm {e} ^ {\mathrm {U} - \lambda} _ {\mathrm {r}} ^ {2} \Phi_ {, \mathrm {r}}\right) _ {, \mathrm {r}} = 0. \tag {3}
$$

The general solution is

$$
\Phi = a \int_ {r} ^ {\infty} \frac {e ^ {\lambda - U}}{r ^ {2}} d r + b. \tag {4}
$$

Near $\mathbf{r} = 0$ the metric functions $e^{2\mathbf{U}}$ and $e^{2\lambda}$ approach unity. (The metric is Lorentzian near $\mathbf{r} = 0$ .) The integral in Equation (4) therefore blows up near $\mathbf{r} = 0$ and $\Phi$ takes the form

$$
\Phi \approx a / r + b \tag {5}
$$

near the origin. But if $a$ is nonzero, the right hand side of Equation (1) is of order $a^2 / r^2$ . (This is not a coordinate effect; the trace of the right hand side is of order $a^2 / r^2$ so by Equation (1) the scalar curvature blows up as $a^2 / r^2$ .) For a solution regular at the origin, then, a must be zero, i.e. $\Phi$ must be constant. For $\Phi$ constant, Equation (1) reduces to the vacuum static spherically symmetric field equations of general relativity. From Birkhoff's theorem we know that the only solution to these equations which is well behaved at $r = 0$ is $g_{\mu \nu} = \eta_{\mu \nu}$ .

Solution 16.6. Examine the components at any point in an orthonormal tetrad $[\mathbf{e}_{\hat{\mathbf{t}}}, \mathbf{e}_{\hat{\mathbf{r}}}, \mathbf{e}_{\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}}, \mathbf{e}_{\hat{\boldsymbol{\phi}}}]$ . Spherical symmetry implies the components are invariant under rotations:

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\theta}} \rightarrow \cos a \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\theta}} + \sin a \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\phi}} \\ \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\phi}} \rightarrow - \sin \alpha \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\theta}} + \cos \alpha \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\phi}}. \\ \end{array}
$$

The components $\mathbf{T}_{\hat{\mathbf{t}}\hat{\mathbf{t}}}$ , $\mathbf{T}_{\hat{\mathbf{t}}\hat{\mathbf{r}}}$ and $\mathbf{T}_{\hat{\mathbf{r}}\hat{\mathbf{r}}}$ are invariant under rotations. The pair $(\mathbf{T}_{\hat{\mathbf{t}}\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}}, \mathbf{T}_{\hat{\mathbf{t}}\hat{\boldsymbol{\phi}}})$ transforms like a 2-dimensional vector under rotations and so is not invariant. Thus $\mathbf{T}_{\hat{\mathbf{t}}\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}} = \mathbf{T}_{\hat{\mathbf{t}}\hat{\boldsymbol{\phi}}} = 0$ and similarly $\mathbf{T}_{\hat{\mathbf{r}}\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}} = \mathbf{T}_{\hat{\mathbf{r}}\hat{\boldsymbol{\phi}}} = 0$ . The only 2-dimensional matrix invariant under rotations is a multiple of the identity matrix, so

$$
\left( \begin{array}{c c} \mathbf {T} _ {\widehat {\theta}} \widehat {\theta} & \mathbf {T} _ {\widehat {\theta}} \widehat {\phi} \\ \mathbf {T} _ {\widehat {\phi}} \widehat {\theta} & \mathbf {T} _ {\widehat {\phi}} \widehat {\phi} \end{array} \right) = \mathbf {T} _ {\widehat {\theta}} \widehat {\theta} \left( \begin{array}{c c} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array} \right).
$$

Thus, there are four independent components: $\mathbf{T}_{\hat{\mathbf{t}}\hat{\mathbf{t}}}$ , $\mathbf{T}_{\hat{\mathbf{t}}\hat{\mathbf{r}}}$ , $\mathbf{T}_{\hat{\mathbf{r}}\hat{\mathbf{r}}}$ and $\mathbf{T}_{\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}\hat{\boldsymbol{\theta}}} = \mathbf{T}_{\hat{\boldsymbol{\phi}}\hat{\boldsymbol{\phi}}}$ .

Solution 16.7. The standard form for the metric of a static, spherically symmetric star is

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - \mathrm {e} ^ {2 \Phi} \mathrm {d t} ^ {2} + \mathrm {e} ^ {2 \Lambda} \mathrm {d r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \mathrm {d} \Omega^ {2}. \tag {1}
$$

The equation $\mathbf{T}^{\alpha \beta}$ ; $\beta = 0$ is most easily evaluated by recalling that it is equivalent to the first law of thermodynamics

$$
\mathrm {d} \rho / \mathrm {d} \tau = \frac {\rho + \mathrm {p}}{\mathrm {n}} \frac {\mathrm {d n}}{\mathrm {d} \tau} \tag {2}
$$

(see Problem 5.19) and the Euler equation

$$
\left(\rho + p\right) u _ {a}; \beta u ^ {\beta} = - p, a - p, \beta u ^ {\beta} u _ {a} \tag {3}
$$

(see Problem 14.3). For a static star, both sides of Equation (2) vanish identically. The only nonzero component of $\mathbf{u}$ is $\mathbf{u}^{\mathrm{t}}$ and $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{u} = -1$ implies $\mathbf{u}^{\mathrm{t}} = \mathbf{e}^{-\Phi}$ . Since only $\mathbf{p}_{,\mathbf{r}}$ is nonzero, the only nontrivial component of Equation (3) is

$$
\begin{array}{l} - p, r = (\rho + p) u _ {r; \beta} u ^ {\beta} = - (\rho + p) \Gamma_ {r \beta} ^ {\alpha} u _ {\alpha} u ^ {\beta} \\ = - (\rho + p) \Gamma_ {r t} ^ {t} u _ {t} u ^ {t} = (\rho + p) \Phi_ {, r}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Solution 16.8. Let $\mathfrak{m}(\mathfrak{r})$ be a solution to the Newtonian equations for a polytropic star, namely

$$
\mathrm {d m} / \mathrm {d r} = 4 \pi \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \rho \tag {1}
$$

$$
\mathrm {d p} / \mathrm {d r} = - \frac {\mathrm {G m} \rho}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}} \tag {2}
$$

$$
\mathbf {p} = \mathrm {K} \rho^ {\gamma}. \tag {3}
$$

When $\gamma = 4 / 3$ the star is neutrally stable; this is seen in the fact that

$$
\tilde {\mathbf {m}} (\mathbf {r}) = \mathbf {m} (\alpha \mathbf {r})
$$

$$
\tilde {\rho} (\mathrm {r}) = \alpha^ {3} \rho (\alpha \mathrm {r})
$$

$$
\tilde {\mathbf {p}} (\mathbf {r}) = \alpha^ {4} \mathbf {p} (\alpha \mathbf {r})
$$

is also a solution to Equations (1), (2), (3) and has the same total mass $\mathbf{m}(\infty)$ and adiabat $\mathbf{K}$ .

The relativistic version of Equation (1) is identical; Equation (3), the given equation of state, is also identical by hypothesis. The relativistic form of Equation (2) is [Weinberg, Equation 11.1.13 or MTW, Equation 23.22]

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {p}}{\mathrm {d} \mathbf {r}} = - \frac {\mathrm {G m} \rho}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}} \left(1 + \frac {\mathrm {p}}{\rho}\right) \left(1 + \frac {4 \pi \mathrm {r} ^ {3} \mathrm {p}}{\mathrm {m}}\right) \left(1 - \frac {\mathrm {m}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) ^ {- 1}. \tag {4}
$$

Since the star we are considering is nearly Newtonian, the terms in brackets are all very close to unity. Hence, one can find an exact solution to Equation (4) which is nearly equal to the Newtonian solution to Equation (2) [e.g. by substituting into Equation (4) to find a small correction to $\mathfrak{p}$ and by iterating]. Call this solution $\hat{\mathfrak{m}}(\mathbf{r})$ , $\hat{\rho}(\mathbf{r})$ , $\hat{\mathfrak{p}}(\mathbf{r})$ . From this generate

$$
\tilde {\mathrm {m}} (\mathrm {r}) = \hat {\mathrm {m}} (\alpha \mathrm {r})
$$

$$
\tilde {\rho} (\mathrm {r}) = a ^ {3} \hat {\rho} (a \mathrm {r})
$$

$$
\tilde {\mathrm {p}} (\mathrm {r}) = \alpha^ {4} \hat {\mathrm {p}} (\alpha \mathrm {r}).
$$

Equations (1) and (3) remain equalities, but the ratio of the right hand side of Equation (4) to the left hand side is, to lowest order,

$$
\frac {\text {r i g h t}}{\text {l e f t}} = 1 + (\alpha - 1) \left[ \frac {\widehat {\mathrm {p}}}{\widehat {\rho}} + 4 \pi (\alpha \mathrm {r}) ^ {3} \frac {\widehat {\mathrm {p}}}{\widehat {\mathrm {m}}} + \frac {2 \widehat {\mathrm {m}}}{\alpha \mathrm {r}} \right]. \tag {5}
$$

For $a > 1$ , which corresponds to shrinking the star to smaller radius, the right side is greater than the left side, hence the pressure gradient is everywhere insufficient to support the star and it must go to a smaller

radius yet. This cannot be an equilibrium because it would still be almost Newtonian and the same argument would apply. Therefore, $\gamma = 4 / 3$ is unstable, and the range of stability must begin at $4 / 3 + \varepsilon$ .

Solution 16.9. Increasing the mass of a degenerate star makes the material more compact until the Fermi energy of the gas (electron gas in case of white dwarf; neutron gas in the case of neutron star) rises to a relativistic level. If A is the number of baryons in the star, and R is the radius of the star, then the Fermi energy is approximately (in the relativistic regime)

$$
E _ {F} \sim \frac {\pi c}{R} A ^ {\frac {1}{3}} \tag {1}
$$

(In this equation we have ignored such details as the fact that there are twice as many pressure supporting fermions per baryon in a neutron star as in a white dwarf.) The gravitational mass-energy per fermion is approximately

$$
E _ {G} \sim - \frac {G A m _ {B} ^ {2}}{R} \tag {2}
$$

where $\mathfrak{m}_{\mathbf{B}}$ is the mass of a baryon.

Note that both the compressional and gravitational energy depend in the same way upon R. The decisive question is therefore the sign of the total coefficient of $\mathbf{R}^{-1}$ . When it is positive, R will increase, the Fermi energy will become nonrelativistic, the energy of compression will then fall faster than $\mathbf{R}^{-1}$ and a stable equilibrium will be achieved at some finite R. When the coefficient is negative, collapse will set in and continue. The critical number of baryons at which the star becomes unstable is thus obtained by equating $\mathbf{E}_{\mathbf{G}}$ and $\mathbf{E}_{\mathbf{F}}$ :

$$
\mathrm {A} _ {\text {c r i t .}} = \left(\frac {\hbar c}{G m _ {B} ^ {2}}\right) ^ {\frac {3}{2}} \approx 1 0 ^ {5 7}. \tag {3}
$$

Equation (3) corresponds to a critical limiting mass of

$$
\mathrm {M} _ {\text {c r i t .}} = \mathrm {m} _ {\mathrm {B}} \mathrm {A} _ {\text {c r i t .}} \approx 2 \mathrm {M} _ {\odot} \tag {4}
$$

and limiting radii of

$$
\begin{array}{l} R _ {c r i t.} \sim \bar {n c} A _ {c r i t.} ^ {\frac {1}{3}} \times \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} \left(m _ {e} c ^ {2}\right) ^ {- 1} & \text {f o r w h i t e d w a r f} \\ \left(m _ {B} c ^ {2}\right) ^ {- 1} & \text {f o r n e u t r o n s t a r} \end{array} \right. \\ \approx \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} 5 \times 1 0 ^ {8} \mathrm {c m} & \text {f o r w h i t e d w a r f} \\ 2. 7 \times 1 0 ^ {5} \mathrm {c m} & \text {f o r n e u t r o n s t a r} \end{array} \right. \\ \end{array}
$$

Solution 16.10. At a particular point $\mathbf{P}$ let an observer construct a sphere $S$ passing through $\mathbf{P}$ and defined such that the geometry is the same at all points on $S$ . Then, from the line element, the measured area of this sphere will be

$$
\mathbf {A} (\mathbf {r}) = 4 \pi \mathbf {r} ^ {2}. \tag {1}
$$

The gradient of the scalar function $\mathbf{A}(\mathbf{r})$ is a 1-form, i.e.

$$
\nabla \mathbf {A} (\mathbf {r}) \equiv \widetilde {\mathrm {d A}} = 8 \pi \mathrm {r} \widetilde {\mathrm {d r}}. \tag {2}
$$

Thus,

$$
\widetilde {\mathrm {d A}} \cdot \widetilde {\mathrm {d A}} = 1 6 \pi^ {2} \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \widetilde {\mathrm {d r}} \cdot \widetilde {\mathrm {d r}} = 1 6 \pi^ {2} \mathrm {r} ^ {2} (1 - 2 \mathrm {m} / \mathrm {r}) \tag {3}
$$

and

$$
\mathrm {m} (\mathrm {r}) = \frac {\left(\mathrm {A} / \pi\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{4} \left(1 - \frac {\widetilde {\mathrm {d A}} \cdot \widetilde {\mathrm {d A}}}{1 6 \pi \mathrm {A}}\right). \tag {4}
$$

Solution 16.11.

(a) Under the transformation, the metric

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) \mathrm {d t} ^ {2} + \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) ^ {- 1} \mathrm {d r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \mathrm {d} \Omega^ {2} \tag {1}
$$

becomes

$$
d s ^ {2} = - \left(1 - \frac {2 M}{r}\right) d u ^ {2} - 2 d u d r + r ^ {2} d \Omega^ {2}. \tag {2}
$$

(b) We wish to compute the Einstein tensor $\mathbf{G}_{\alpha \beta}$ when $\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{M}(\mathbf{u})$ . The Christoffel symbols are most easily found from the Lagrangian for geodesics (see Problem 7.25):

$$
L = - \left(1 - \frac {2 M}{r}\right) \dot {u} ^ {2} - 2 \dot {u} \dot {r} + r ^ {2} \dot {\theta} ^ {2} + r ^ {2} \sin^ {2} \theta \dot {\phi} ^ {2}. \tag {3}
$$

The Euler-Lagrange equation

$$
\frac {d}{d s} \left(\frac {\partial L}{\partial \dot {r}}\right) - \frac {\partial L}{\partial r} = 0 \tag {4}
$$

givesthegeodesic equation

$$
\ddot {u} - \left(\mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r} ^ {2}\right) \dot {u} ^ {2} + \mathrm {r} \dot {\theta} ^ {2} + \mathrm {r} \sin^ {2} \theta \dot {\phi} ^ {2} = 0 \tag {5}
$$

from which we read off the nonzero Christoffel symbols

$$
\Gamma_ {u u} ^ {u} = - \frac {M}{r ^ {2}}, \qquad \Gamma_ {\theta \theta} ^ {u} = r, \qquad \Gamma_ {\phi \phi} ^ {u} = r \sin^ {2} \theta .
$$

Similarly, Equation (4) with $\mathfrak{r}$ replaced by $\mathfrak{u}$ , $\theta$ and $\phi$ gives the remaining Christoffel symbols

$$
\Gamma_ {\mathrm {u u}} ^ {\mathrm {r}} = \frac {\mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}} \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) - \frac {\mathrm {M} ^ {\prime}}{\mathrm {r}}, \qquad \Gamma_ {\theta \theta} ^ {\mathrm {r}} = - \mathrm {r} \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right),
$$

$$
\Gamma_ {\phi \phi} ^ {r} = - \left(1 - \frac {2 M}{r}\right) r \sin^ {2} \theta , \quad \Gamma_ {u r} ^ {r} = \frac {M}{r ^ {2}}, \quad \Gamma_ {r \theta} ^ {\theta} = \frac {1}{r},
$$

$$
\Gamma_ {\phi \phi} ^ {\theta} = - \sin \theta \cos \theta , \quad \Gamma_ {r \phi} ^ {\phi} = \frac {1}{r}, \quad \Gamma_ {\theta \phi} ^ {\phi} = \cot \theta .
$$

These all have their Schwarzschild values except $\Gamma_{uu}^{r}$ . From the formula for the Ricci tensor (MTW, Equation 8.51b)

$$
\mathrm {R} _ {\alpha \beta} = \Gamma_ {\alpha \beta , \gamma} ^ {\gamma} - (\log | \mathrm {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}) _ {, \alpha \beta} + (\log | \mathrm {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}) _ {, \gamma} \Gamma_ {\alpha \beta} ^ {\gamma} - \Gamma_ {\beta \delta} ^ {\gamma} \Gamma_ {a \gamma} ^ {\delta}
$$

we see that all the $\mathbf{R}_{\alpha \beta}$ will have their Schwarzschild values (i.e. zero) except

$$
R _ {u u} = - 2 M ^ {\prime} / r ^ {2}.
$$

The Ricci scalar is

$$
R = g ^ {u u} R _ {u u} = 0
$$

so the only nonzero component of the Einstein tensor is

$$
G _ {u u} = - 2 M ^ {\prime} / r ^ {2}
$$

which implies that

$$
\mathrm {T} _ {\mathrm {u u}} = - \mathrm {M} ^ {\prime} / 4 \pi \mathrm {r} ^ {2},
$$

and that all other components of $\mathbf{T}$ are zero. Since the vector $\mathbf{k} = \nabla \mathbf{u}$ , that is $\mathbf{k}_{\alpha} = (1,0,0,0)$ , is null, the stress-energy tensor

$$
\mathbf {T} = - \frac {\mathbf {M} ^ {\prime}}{4 \pi \mathbf {r} ^ {2}} \mathbf {k} \otimes \mathbf {k}
$$

corresponds to a pure radiation field. Thus a physical interpretation of this solution (called the Vaidya metric) is the exterior metric of a spherical star whose mass is decreasing because of the energy carried off in radiation.

Solution 16.12. The equations of stellar structure are (MTW, Equation 23.5 or Weinberg, Chapter 11.1)

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - \mathrm {e} ^ {2 \Phi} \mathrm {d t} ^ {2} + \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {m}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) ^ {- 1} \mathrm {d r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \mathrm {d} \Omega^ {2} \tag {1}
$$

$$
m = \int_ {0} ^ {r} 4 \pi r ^ {2} \rho d r \tag {2}
$$

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {p}}{\mathrm {d} \mathbf {r}} = - \frac {(\rho + \mathrm {p}) (\mathrm {m} + 4 \pi \mathrm {r} ^ {3} \mathrm {p})}{\mathrm {r} (\mathrm {r} - 2 \mathrm {m})} \tag {3}
$$

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \Phi}{\mathrm {d} r} = \frac {\mathrm {m} + 4 \pi \mathrm {r} ^ {3} \mathrm {p}}{\mathrm {r} (\mathrm {r} - 2 \mathrm {m})}. \tag {4}
$$

Since $\rho = \rho_0$ , a constant, Equation (2) gives

$$
m = 4 \pi r ^ {3} \rho_ {0} / 3 \tag {5}
$$

and

$$
\mathrm {M} = 4 \pi \mathrm {R} ^ {3} \rho_ {0} / 3, \tag {6}
$$

where $\mathbf{R}$ is the radius and $\mathbf{M}$ the total mass for the star. Substitute Equation (5) in Equation (3) to get

$$
\frac {d p}{4 \pi (\rho_ {0} + p) \left(\frac {1}{3} \rho_ {0} + p\right)} = - \frac {r d r}{1 - (8 \pi / 3) r ^ {2} \rho_ {0}}.
$$

Integrate and solve for p:

$$
\frac {\mathrm {p}}{\rho_ {0}} = \frac {\left(1 - 2 \mathrm {M r} ^ {2} / \mathrm {R} ^ {3}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} - \left(1 - 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {R}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{3 \left(1 - 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {R}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} - \left(1 - 2 \mathrm {M r} ^ {2} / \mathrm {R} ^ {3}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \tag {7}
$$

The constant of integration has been determined by demanding $p = 0$ at $r = R$ . The quantity $\Phi$ is most easily found from the equation:

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \Phi}{\mathrm {d} p} = \frac {\mathrm {d} \Phi / \mathrm {d} r}{\mathrm {d} p / \mathrm {d} r} = - \frac {1}{\rho_ {0} + p}.
$$

Integrate to find

$$
\begin{array}{l} e ^ {\Phi} = \text {c o n s t a n t} / (\rho_ {0} + p) \\ = \frac {3}{2} (1 - 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {R}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} - \frac {1}{2} (1 - 2 \mathrm {M r} ^ {2} / \mathrm {R} ^ {3}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \tag {8} \\ \end{array}
$$

where Equation (7) has been used to fix the constant of integration so that

$$
e ^ {\Phi (r = R)} = (1 - 2 M / R) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}.
$$

(That is, so that the metric will match smoothly to the Schwarzschild metric outside the star.)

We thus have a 1-parameter family of stellar models, conveniently parametrized by the central pressure

$$
\frac {P _ {C}}{\rho_ {0}} = \frac {1 - (1 - 2 M / R) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{3 (1 - 2 M / R) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} - 1}. \tag {9}
$$

The central pressure becomes infinite when

$$
3 \left(1 - \frac {2 M}{R}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} - 1 = 0
$$

i.e. the limiting value of $\mathbf{R} / 2\mathbf{M}$ is $9 / 8$

The dominant energy condition demands $p_c < \rho_0$ , so that the right side of Equation (9) is $< 1$ . This implies $R / 2M > 4 / 3$ .

Solution 16.13. A relativistic zero-temperature Fermi gas has the equation of state $p = 1/3 \rho$ , so the equations of structure (see Problem 16.12) read

$$
\mathrm {d} \rho / \mathrm {d r} = - \frac {4 \rho (\mathrm {m} + 4 \pi \mathrm {r} ^ {3} \rho / 3)}{\mathrm {r} (\mathrm {r} - 2 \mathrm {m})} \tag {1}
$$

$$
\mathrm {d m} / \mathrm {d r} = 4 \pi \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \rho . \tag {2}
$$

Substitution of the suggested solution $\mathfrak{m}(\mathbf{r}) = 3\mathbf{r} / 14$ into Equation (2) gives $\rho (\mathbf{r}) = (3 / 14)(4\pi \mathbf{r}^2)^{-1}$ ; substitution of this $\rho$ and $\mathfrak{m}$ into Equation (1) verifies that they are a solution. From the equation of state $\mathfrak{p}(\mathbf{r}) = (1 / 14)(4\pi \mathbf{r}^2)^{-1}$ . To find $\mathfrak{n}(\mathbf{r})$ we use the relations for a relativistic Fermi gas (see Problem 5.24)

$$
n = \int_ {0} ^ {p _ {F}} \left(\frac {2}{h ^ {3}}\right) 4 \pi p ^ {2} d p = \frac {8 \pi}{3 h ^ {3}} p _ {F} ^ {3} \tag {3}
$$

$$
\rho = \int_ {0} ^ {\mathrm {P} _ {\mathrm {F}}} \mathrm {p} \left(\frac {2}{\mathrm {h} ^ {3}}\right) 4 \pi \mathrm {p} ^ {2} \mathrm {d p} = \frac {8 \pi}{4 \mathrm {h} ^ {3}} \mathrm {p} _ {\mathrm {F}} ^ {3}. \tag {4}
$$

Eliminating $p_{F}$ (the Fermi momentum) from Equations (3) and (4) gives

$$
\mathbf {n} (\mathbf {r}) = \frac {8 \pi}{3 h ^ {3}} \left(\frac {4 h ^ {3} \rho}{8 \pi}\right) ^ {\frac {3}{4}}
$$

and substitution of $\rho (\mathbf{r})$ gives

$$
n (r) = \frac {K}{r ^ {\frac {3}{2}}} , \quad K = \frac {8 \pi}{3 h ^ {3}} \left(\frac {4 h ^ {3}}{8 \pi} \cdot \frac {3}{1 4} \cdot \frac {1}{4 \pi}\right) ^ {\frac {3}{4}}. \tag {5}
$$

The total number of particles inside radius $r$ is

$$
\begin{array}{l} N (r) = \int_ {0} ^ {r} n (r) d (\text {p r o p e r v o l u m e}) \\ = \int_ {0} ^ {r} n (r) e ^ {\Lambda} 4 \pi r ^ {2} d r \\ = \frac {1 4 \pi}{3} \mathrm {K r} ^ {\frac {3}{2}} \tag {6} \\ \end{array}
$$

which is finite for all $r$ . [Note: In deriving Equation (6) we have used

$$
\mathrm {e} ^ {2 \Lambda} \equiv \mathrm {g} _ {\tau \mathrm {r}} = \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {m (r)}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) ^ {- 1} = 7 / 4 ].
$$

The 3-geometry of the $t =$ constant hypersurface has the metric

$$
\left(^ {3}\right) d s ^ {2} = g _ {r r} d r ^ {2} + r ^ {2} d \Omega^ {2} = (7 / 4) d r ^ {2} + r ^ {2} d \Omega^ {2}.
$$

This obviously has a conical singularity at the origin, because a 2-sphere of circumference $2\pi r$ will have a radius of $(7/4)^{\frac{1}{2}}r$ which is greater than $r$ .

The embedding equation for a radial cut is

$$
\left(3\right) d s ^ {2} = (7 / 4) d r ^ {2} = d r ^ {2} + d z ^ {2}
$$

which implies

$$
z = \pm (3 / 4) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} r
$$

![](images/263bc62826c1a9c8178ac607b195b70b4ca04a9853f62e3db1f9eadd06d3b64d.jpg)

Solution 16.14. The metric cannot be continuous across the shell in curvature coordinates, because $\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{rr}} = (1 - 2\mathfrak{m}(\mathbf{r}) / \mathbf{r})^{-1}$ and $\mathfrak{m}(\mathbf{r})$ changes across the shell. Therefore we use isotropic coordinates, in which the metric will be continuous and (matching flat space inside to mass M outside) has the form

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - \mathrm {e} ^ {\gamma} \mathrm {d t} ^ {2} + \mathrm {e} ^ {\alpha} \left(\mathrm {d r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \mathrm {d} \Omega^ {2}\right) \tag {1}
$$

$$
e ^ {\gamma} = \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} \left(1 - \frac {M}{2 r}\right) ^ {2} \left(1 + \frac {M}{2 r}\right) ^ {- 2} & r > R \\ \left(1 - \frac {M}{2 R}\right) ^ {2} \left(1 + \frac {M}{2 R}\right) ^ {- 2} & r <   R \end{array} \right. \tag {2a}
$$

$$
e ^ {\alpha} = \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} \left(1 + \frac {M}{2 r}\right) ^ {4} & r > R \\ \left(1 + \frac {M}{2 R}\right) ^ {4} & r <   R. \end{array} \right. \tag {2b}
$$

In Equations (1) and (2) we have used the fact that the geometry is Schwarzschild outside the shell and flat in the interior (Birkhoff's Theorem - see Problem 16.3). Also, the surface of the shell is located at $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{R}$ (in isotropic radial coordinate distance).

Next we define the integrated stress, $\Lambda_{\hat{\beta}}^{\hat{a}}$ in the following invariant manner

$$
\Lambda^ {\hat {a}} _ {\hat {\beta}} \equiv \underset {\epsilon \rightarrow 0} {\operatorname {L I M}} \int_ {R - \epsilon} ^ {R + \epsilon} T ^ {\hat {a}} _ {\hat {\beta}} d \hat {r} = \underset {\epsilon \rightarrow 0} {\operatorname {L I M}} \int_ {R - \epsilon} ^ {R + \epsilon} T ^ {\hat {a}} _ {\hat {\beta}} e ^ {\frac {1}{2} a} d r. \tag {3}
$$

The components of $\Lambda$ may be evaluated by using the Einstein field equations and by noting that, according to Equations (2) and (3), only terms in $\mathbf{G}_{\nu}^{\mu}$ with second derivatives of the metric potentials can contribute.

The components of $\mathbf{G}_{\nu}^{\mu}$ for the metric of Equation (1) are (see Problem 9.20)

$$
G _ {0} ^ {0} \sim a ^ {\prime \prime} e ^ {- a} \sim - \frac {d}{d r ^ {2}} (e ^ {- a}) \tag {4a}
$$

$$
\mathrm {G} _ {\mathrm {r}} ^ {\mathrm {r}} \sim 0 \tag {4b}
$$

$$
\mathbf {G} _ {\theta} ^ {\theta} = \mathbf {G} _ {\phi} ^ {\phi} \sim \frac {1}{2} \left. \mathrm {e} ^ {- a} (a ^ {\prime \prime} + \gamma^ {\prime \prime}) \right. \sim - \frac {1}{2} \left[ \frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2}}{\mathrm {d r} ^ {2}} (\mathrm {e} ^ {- a}) - \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d r}} (\mathrm {e} ^ {- a} \gamma^ {\prime}) \right]
$$

where $\sim$ denotes the process of discarding all terms not involving second derivatives. Using Equations (3) and (4), and the Einstein field equations, we get

$$
\Lambda^{\widehat{0}}_{0} = \Lambda^{0}_{0} = -(8\pi)^{-1}\lim_{\epsilon \to 0}\int_{R - \epsilon}^{R + \epsilon}\mathrm{e}^{\alpha /2}\frac{\mathrm{d}^{2}}{\mathrm{d}r^{2}} (\mathrm{e}^{-\alpha})\mathrm{d}r\sim -(8\pi)^{-1}\lim_{\epsilon \to 0}\mathrm{e}^{\alpha /2}\frac{\mathrm{d}}{\mathrm{d}r} (\mathrm{e}^{-\alpha})\bigg|_{R - \epsilon}^{R + \epsilon}
$$

or

$$
\Lambda_ {\widehat {0}} ^ {\widehat {0}} = \frac {- \mathrm {M}}{4 \pi \mathrm {R} ^ {2} (1 + \mathrm {M} / 2 \mathrm {R}) ^ {3}} \tag {4c}
$$

$$
\Lambda_ {\mathbf {r}} ^ {\hat {\mathbf {r}}} = \Lambda_ {\mathbf {r}} ^ {\mathbf {r}} = 0 \tag {5}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \Lambda^ {\widehat {\theta}} _ {\widehat {\theta}} = \Lambda^ {\widehat {\phi}} _ {\widehat {\phi}} = - (8 \pi) ^ {- 1} \lim  _ {\epsilon \rightarrow 0} \int_ {R - \epsilon} ^ {R + \epsilon} \frac {1}{2} e ^ {\alpha / 2} \left[ \frac {d ^ {2}}{d r ^ {2}} (e ^ {- \alpha}) - \frac {d}{d r} (e ^ {- \alpha} \gamma^ {\prime}) \right] d r \\ = - (8 \pi) ^ {- 1} \underset {\epsilon \rightarrow 0} {\mathrm {L I M}} \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {e} ^ {\alpha / 2} \left[ \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d r}} (\mathrm {e} ^ {- \alpha}) - \mathrm {e} ^ {- \alpha} \gamma^ {\prime} \right] \Big | _ {\substack {\mathrm {R} + \epsilon\\\mathrm {R} - \epsilon}} ^ {\mathrm {R} + \epsilon} \\ \end{array}
$$

or

$$
\Lambda^ {\widehat {\theta}} _ {\widehat {\theta}} = \frac {\mathbb {M}}{8 \pi \mathbb {R} ^ {2}} \left(\frac {\mathbb {M} / 2 \mathbb {R}}{1 - \mathbb {M} / 2 \mathbb {R}}\right) \left(1 + \frac {\mathbb {M}}{2 \mathbb {R}}\right) ^ {- 3}. \tag {6}
$$

To have proper interpretation of Equations (4) to (6) we must remember that $\mathbf{R}$ denotes the radius of the shell in isotropic coordinates. In curvature coordinates, the radius $\mathcal{R}$ is

$$
\mathcal {R} = \mathrm {R} (1 + \mathrm {M} / 2 \mathrm {R}) ^ {2}. \tag {7}
$$

The proper surface mass density is just

$$
\frac {\mathrm {M}}{4 \pi \mathcal {R} ^ {2}} = \frac {\mathrm {M}}{4 \pi \mathrm {R} ^ {2} (1 + \mathrm {M} / 2 \mathrm {R}) ^ {4}}. \tag {8}
$$

The Newtonian limit of Equations (4) to (6) is just

$$
\Lambda_ {\hat {0}} ^ {\hat {0}} \rightarrow - \frac {M}{4 \pi R ^ {2}}
$$

$$
\Lambda_ {\widehat {\mathbf {r}}} ^ {\widehat {\mathbf {r}}} = 0
$$

$$
\Lambda \hat {\theta} _ {\hat {\theta}} = \Lambda \hat {\phi} _ {\hat {\phi}} \rightarrow \frac {\mathrm {M} ^ {2}}{1 6 \pi \mathrm {R} ^ {3}}.
$$

Solution 16.15. The dominant energy condition requires

$$
\left| \mathbf {T} ^ {\widehat {0} \widehat {0}} \right| > \left| \mathbf {T} ^ {\widehat {\mathrm {k}} \widehat {\mathrm {k}}} \right|. \tag {1}
$$

Using the results of Problem 16.14, and integrating Equation (1) through the thin shell, we have the inequality

$$
\left(1 + \frac {\mathrm {M}}{2 \mathrm {R}}\right) ^ {- 3} > \frac {1}{2} \frac {\mathrm {M} / 2 \mathrm {R}}{1 - \mathrm {M} / 2 \mathrm {R}} \left(1 + \frac {\mathrm {M}}{2 \mathrm {R}}\right) ^ {- 3} \tag {2}
$$

where $\mathbf{R}$ is the isotropic radial coordinate. Letting $\mathbf{x} = \mathbf{M} / 2\mathbf{R}$ we have $2 - 2\mathbf{x} > \mathbf{x}$ or $\mathbf{x} < 2/3$ , giving $\mathbf{R} > \frac{3}{4}\mathbf{M}$ , or for the curvature radial coordinate $\mathcal{R}$

$$
\mathcal {R} = \mathrm {R} \left(1 + \frac {\mathrm {M}}{2 \mathrm {R}}\right) ^ {2} > \frac {2 5}{1 2} \mathrm {M}, \tag {3}
$$

about 1.04 Schwarzschild radii.

Solution 16.16. In isotropic coordinates (see Problem 16.14), the redshift to radial infinity is

$$
\begin{array}{l} z = \frac {\lambda_ {\text {i n f i n i t y}}}{\lambda_ {\text {s u r f a c e}}} - 1 = \frac {\left(- g _ {0 0}\right) _ {\infty} ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{\left(- g _ {0 0}\right) _ {\mathrm {S}} ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} - 1 = \left(\mathrm {e} ^ {- \gamma / 2}\right) _ {\mathrm {S}} - 1 \\ = \frac {1 + \mathrm {M} / 2 \mathrm {R}}{1 - \mathrm {M} / 2 \mathrm {R}} - 1 = \frac {\mathrm {M} / \mathrm {R}}{1 - \mathrm {M} / 2 \mathrm {R}}. \\ \end{array}
$$

The integrated energy density and transverse pressure are respectively

$$
- \Lambda_ {\hat {0}} ^ {\hat {0}} = \frac {\mathsf {M}}{4 \pi \mathsf {R} ^ {2}} \left(1 + \frac {\mathsf {M}}{2 \mathsf {R}}\right) ^ {- 3},
$$

$$
\Lambda^ {\widehat {\theta}} _ {\widehat {\theta}} = \Lambda^ {\widehat {\phi}} _ {\widehat {\phi}} = \frac {\mathrm {M}}{8 \pi \mathrm {R} ^ {2}} \left(\frac {\mathrm {M} / 2 \mathrm {R}}{1 - \mathrm {M} / 2 \mathrm {R}}\right) \left(1 + \frac {\mathrm {M}}{2 \mathrm {R}}\right) ^ {- 3}.
$$

Hence the ratio of integrated pressure to energy is

$$
\frac {\Lambda \hat {\theta} _ {\widehat {\theta}}}{- \Lambda_ {\widehat {0}} ^ {\widehat {0}}} = \frac {\mathrm {M} / 4 \mathrm {R}}{1 - \mathrm {M} / 2 \mathrm {R}} = \frac {1}{4} z
$$

so the redshift to radial infinity is

$$
z = - 4 \Lambda \widehat {\theta} _ {\widehat {\theta}} / \Lambda \widehat {0} _ {\widehat {0}}.
$$

If the dominant energy condition is satisfied, $|\Lambda_{\hat{\theta}}^{\hat{\theta}_0}| \leq |\Lambda_{\hat{0}}^0|$ , so $z \leq 4$ .

Solution 16.17. The non-vanishing components of the fluid 4-velocity are $\mathbf{u}^{\mathrm{t}}$ and $\mathbf{u}^{\phi} = \Omega \mathbf{u}^{\mathrm{t}}$ , i.e.

$$
\mathbf {u} = \mathbf {u} ^ {\mathrm {t}} \left(\frac {\partial}{\partial \mathbf {t}} + \Omega \frac {\partial}{\partial \phi}\right).
$$

Let

$$
\pmb {\xi} = \frac {\partial}{\partial t} + \Omega \frac {\partial}{\partial \phi} = \pmb {\xi} _ {(t)} + \Omega \pmb {\xi} _ {(\phi)}.
$$

Then $\pmb{\xi}$ is a Killing vector, since $\Omega$ is a constant. By Problem 10.14, we have

$$
\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {u} = \nabla \log | \boldsymbol {\xi} \cdot \boldsymbol {\xi} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}
$$

where, since $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{u} = -1$ , $|\pmb{\xi} \cdot \pmb{\xi}|^{\frac{1}{2}} = 1 / \mathbf{u}^{\mathbf{t}}$ . Thus, the Euler equation (see Problem 14.3) for the fluid becomes

$$
(\rho + p) \nabla \log (u ^ {t}) ^ {- 1} = - \nabla p - (\nabla_ {(u ^ {t} \xi)} p) u.
$$

Since $\mathfrak{p}$ is independent of $t$ and $\phi$ , it follows that $\nabla_{\xi} \mathfrak{p} = 0$ . Thus,

$$
(\rho + p) \nabla \log u ^ {t} = \nabla p.
$$

Solution 16.18. First, note that $\mathbf{dp} = (\rho + \mathfrak{p})\mathbf{d}\log \mathfrak{u}^{\mathfrak{t}}$ (hydrostatic equilibrium; see Problem 16.17). Next take the exterior derivative of this equation, and use $\mathbf{ddp} = 0$ to get

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = \mathrm {d} (\rho + \mathrm {p}) \wedge \mathrm {d} \log \mathrm {u} ^ {\mathrm {t}} \\ = \mathbf {d} (\rho + \mathfrak {p}) \wedge (\rho + \mathfrak {p}) ^ {- 1} \mathbf {d} \rho \\ \end{array}
$$

which implies that

$$
\mathrm {d} \rho \wedge \mathrm {d p} = 0,
$$

and therefore that the surface $\rho =$ constant coincides with the surface $\mathbf{p} =$ constant.

Solution 16.19. The surface is a surface of constant $\mathfrak{p}$ (that is $\mathfrak{p} = 0$ ). By Problem 16.18, it is a surface of constant $\rho$ , and also of constant $\mathfrak{u}^{\mathfrak{t}}$ , since

$$
\mathrm {d p} \propto \mathrm {d u} ^ {\mathrm {t}}.
$$

But, in the notation of Solution 16.17,

$$
\left(u ^ {t}\right) ^ {- 1} = \left| \boldsymbol {\xi} \cdot \boldsymbol {\xi} \right| ^ {\frac {1}{2}} = \left| g _ {t t} + 2 g _ {t} \phi \Omega + g _ {\phi} \phi \Omega^ {2} \right| ^ {\frac {1}{2}}
$$

so on the surface

$$
\mathbf {g} _ {\mathrm {t t}} + 2 \mathbf {g} _ {\mathrm {t} \phi} \boldsymbol {\Omega} + \mathbf {g} _ {\phi \phi} \boldsymbol {\Omega} ^ {2} = \mathrm {c o n s t a n t}.
$$

Problem 16.20. If $\mathbf{p}$ is the 4-momentum of a photon and $\mathbf{u}_{\mathrm{em}}$ , $\mathbf{u}_{\infty}$ are the 4-velocities respectively of an emitting point on the star and of the observer at $\infty$ , then the ratio of emitted and observed frequencies is

$$
\frac {\nu_ {\mathrm {e m}}}{\nu_ {\infty}} = \frac {\mathbf {p} \cdot \mathbf {u} _ {\mathrm {e m}}}{\mathbf {p} \cdot \mathbf {u} _ {\infty}}.
$$

But $\mathbf{u}_{\mathrm{em}}$ has only $t$ and $\phi$ components, so

$$
\mathbf {p} \cdot \mathbf {u} _ {\mathrm {e m}} = \mathbf {p} _ {\mathrm {t}} \mathbf {u} ^ {\mathrm {t}} + \mathbf {p} _ {\phi} \mathbf {u} ^ {\phi} = \mathbf {p} _ {\mathrm {t}} \mathbf {u} ^ {\mathrm {t}} (1 + \Omega \ell)
$$

where $\Omega \equiv \mathbf{u}^{\phi} / \mathbf{u}^{\mathrm{t}}$ is the angular velocity of the star and $\ell \equiv p_{\phi} / p_{t}$ is the impact parameter of the photon relative to the axis of rotation. Note that $p_{\phi}$ and $p_t$ are conserved along the photon's trajectory. Also, $\mathbf{p} \cdot \mathbf{u}_{\infty} = p_t$ since $\mathbf{u}^{\mathrm{t}} = 1$ for an observer at infinity and thus

$$
z = u ^ {t} (1 + \Omega \ell) - 1 = \frac {1 + \Omega \ell}{\left| g _ {t t} + 2 \Omega g _ {t} \phi + \Omega^ {2} g _ {\phi} \phi \right| _ {e m} ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} - 1.
$$

Since $\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{tt}} + 2\Omega \mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{t}\phi} + \Omega^{2}\mathbf{g}_{\phi \phi}$ is constant on the surface of a rigidly rotating star (see Problem 16.19), the variation in $z$ across the surface of the star is

$$
\Delta z = \frac {\Omega \Delta \ell}{| g _ {t t} + 2 \Omega g _ {t \phi} + \Omega^ {2} g _ {\phi \phi} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}
$$

All photons reaching an observer on the axis must have $\mathbf{p}_{\phi} = 0$ , i.e. $\ell = 0$ , i.e. $\Delta \ell = 0$ . Thus $\Delta z = 0$ for such an observer.

Solution 16.21. The configuration of perfect fluid is convectively stable if its mass (i.e. rest mass + energy) does not change under a redistribution of baryons. We first calculate its change in mass, $\delta \mathbf{M}$ , due to an addition of $\delta \mathbf{A}$ baryons from infinity.

A distant astrophysicist drops a total mass-energy $\mu_{\mathbf{B}}\delta \mathbf{A} + \mathbf{W}_0$ onto the fluid, where $\mu_{\mathbf{B}}$ is the average rest mass of the baryons and $\mathbf{W}_0$ is additional energy to be used for injecting the baryons into the fluid. A static observer at radius $r$ catches the baryons and energy and measures a total mass-energy of

$$
\mathrm {W} = \mathrm {u} ^ {0} \mathrm {p} _ {0} = \mathrm {e} ^ {- \Phi} \left(\mu_ {\mathrm {B}} \delta \mathrm {A} + \mathrm {W} _ {0}\right) \tag {1}
$$

where $\mathbf{e}^{-\Phi}$ is the redshift factor and comes from the metric

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - \mathrm {e} ^ {2 \Phi} \mathrm {d t} ^ {2} + (1 - 2 \mathrm {m} / \mathrm {r}) ^ {- 1} \mathrm {d r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \mathrm {d} \Omega^ {2}. \tag {2}
$$

With energy W, the local observer must heat and compress the baryons to local thermodynamic conditions and then open a space for them in the star

$$
\mathrm {W} _ {\delta \mathbf {A} _ {\text {(l o c a l c o n d i t i o n s)}}} = (\rho / \mathfrak {n}) \delta \mathbf {A} \tag {3a}
$$

$$
\mathrm {W} _ {\text {o p e n}} = \mathrm {p} \delta \mathrm {V} = (\mathrm {p} / \mathrm {n}) \delta \mathrm {A} \tag {3b}
$$

where $n$ is baryon number density. (Note that, since we have assumed that the star is already at an extremum of total mass-energy, the change in energy of the star due to readjustment of its structure as mass is displaced for the $\delta A$ baryons can be neglected.) The excess energy left over is

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {W} _ {\mathbf {e x}} (\mathrm {r}) = \mathrm {W} - \left(\mathrm {W} _ {\delta \mathbf {A}} + \mathrm {W} _ {\text {o p e n}}\right) \\ = \mathrm {e} ^ {- \Phi} \left(\mu_ {\mathrm {B}} \delta \mathrm {A} + \mathrm {W} _ {0}\right) - \frac {(\rho + \mathrm {p})}{\mathrm {n}} \delta \mathrm {A}. \tag {4} \\ \end{array}
$$

The local observer then converts a fraction $(1 - e^{\Phi})$ of $W_{\text{excess}}$ into kinetic energy so that he can throw the rest to the observer at infinity, who then catches energy

$$
\mathrm {W} _ {\mathrm {e x}} (\infty) = \mathrm {W} _ {\mathrm {e x}} (\mathrm {r}) \mathrm {e} ^ {\Phi} = \mu_ {\mathrm {B}} \delta \mathrm {A} + \mathrm {W} _ {0} - \mathrm {e} ^ {\Phi} \frac {(\rho + \mathrm {p})}{\mathrm {n}} \delta \mathrm {A}. \tag {5}
$$

The observer at infinity, therefore, measures a mass increase of the star, $\delta \mathbf{M}$ . of

$$
\delta \mathrm {M} = e ^ {\Phi} \frac {(\rho + p)}{\mathrm {n}} \delta \mathrm {A} = e ^ {\Phi} \left(\frac {\partial \rho}{\partial \mathrm {n}}\right) _ {\mathrm {S}} \delta \mathrm {A} \tag {6}
$$

where the second relation follows from the first law of thermodynamics.

For convective stability we require $\delta \mathbf{M}$ to be independent of $\mathbf{r}$ , i.e.

$$
e ^ {\Phi} (\rho + p) / n = \text {c o n s t a n t}. \tag {7}
$$

We can easily show, using the Euler equation,

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {p}}{\mathrm {d} \mathbf {r}} = - (\rho + \mathbf {p}) \frac {\mathrm {d} \Phi}{\mathrm {d} \mathbf {r}} \tag {8}
$$

that Equation (7) is equivalent to adiabatic gradients of thermodynamic variables. Taking the derivative of Equation (7) with respect to $\mathbf{r}$ gives

$$
- \frac {(\rho + p)}{n} \frac {d n}{d r} + \frac {d \rho}{d r} + \frac {d p}{d r} + (\rho + p) \frac {d \Phi}{d r} = 0. \tag {9}
$$

Using Equation (8), we can put Equation (9) in the form

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \rho}{\mathrm {d r}} = \frac {(\rho + \mathrm {p})}{\mathrm {n}} \frac {\mathrm {d n}}{\mathrm {d r}} = \left(\frac {\partial \rho}{\partial \mathrm {n}}\right) _ {\mathrm {s}} \frac {\mathrm {d n}}{\mathrm {d r}}. \tag {10}
$$

From this equation, it is obvious that the fluid must be isentropic.

Solution 16.22. Begin with the Euler equation for a rigidly rotating star:

$$
(\rho + \mathrm {p}) ^ {- 1} \partial \mathrm {p} / \partial \mathrm {x} ^ {\mu} = \partial \log \mathrm {u} ^ {0} / \partial \mathrm {x} ^ {\mu} \tag {1}
$$

and compare it with the equation for constant injection energy

$$
(\rho + p) / n u ^ {0} = \text {c o n s t a n t}. \tag {2}
$$

Take the derivative of the logarithm of Equation (2) and substitute in from Equation (1), to obtain

$$
(\rho + \mathrm {p}) ^ {- 1} \partial \mathrm {p} / \partial \mathrm {x} ^ {\mu} = \mathrm {n} ^ {- 1} \partial \mathrm {n} / \partial \mathrm {x} ^ {\mu}. \tag {3}
$$

Equation (3) is the first law of thermodynamics specialized to isentropic systems.

Solution 16.23. Since $\mathbf{T}_{\nu}^{\mu}$ vanishes outside the star, we can take the volume integral to be over all space at time $t$ . The field equations imply

$$
8 \pi \left(\mathrm {T} _ {\nu} ^ {\mu} - \frac {1}{2} \delta_ {\nu} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {T}\right) = \mathrm {R} _ {\nu} ^ {\mu}
$$

so the mass integral is

$$
\begin{array}{l} I = - \int (2 T _ {\nu} ^ {\mu} - \delta_ {\nu} ^ {\mu} T) \xi^ {\nu} d ^ {3} \Sigma_ {\mu} \\ = - (4 \pi) ^ {- 1} \int \mathrm {R} _ {\nu} ^ {\mu} \xi^ {\nu} \mathrm {d} ^ {3} \Sigma_ {\mu} \\ = (4 \pi) ^ {- 1} \int \xi^ {\mu ; \nu} _ {; \nu} d ^ {3} \Sigma_ {\mu} \\ = (8 \pi) ^ {- 1} \oint \xi^ {\mu ; \nu} d ^ {2} \Sigma_ {\mu \nu}. \\ \end{array}
$$

[The third equality follows from Problem 10.6; the last equality follows from Stokes' theorem, Problem 8.10(c).] If we choose coordinates $(\mathbf{t}, \mathbf{r}, \theta, \phi)$ which reduce to spherical polar coordinates at infinity, then

$$
\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \Sigma_ {\mu \nu} = \frac {1}{2 !} \varepsilon_ {\mu \nu \alpha \beta} \frac {\partial (\mathbf {x} ^ {\alpha} , \mathbf {x} ^ {\beta})}{\partial (\theta , \phi)} \mathrm {d} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi = \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \sin \theta \mathrm {d} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi
$$

and thus

$$
\xi^ {\mu ; \nu} \mathrm {d} ^ {2} \Sigma_ {\mu \nu} = 2 \xi^ {\mathrm {t}; \mathrm {r}} \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \sin \theta \mathrm {d} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi .
$$

The asymptotic form of the metric is

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) \mathrm {d t} ^ {2} - \frac {4 \mathrm {J} \sin^ {2} \theta}{\mathrm {r}} \mathrm {d t} \mathrm {d} \phi + \left(1 + \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) \left(\mathrm {d r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \mathrm {d} \theta^ {2} + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \sin^ {2} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2}\right).
$$

Since the only nonzero component of $\pmb{\xi}_{(t)}$ is $\pmb{\xi}^{\mathrm{t}} = 1$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \xi^ {t; r} \approx \xi_ {r} ^ {t} \\ = \xi_ {, r} ^ {t} + \Gamma_ {a r} ^ {t} \xi^ {\alpha} = 0 + \Gamma_ {t r} ^ {t} \\ \approx - \frac {1}{2} g _ {t t, r} = M / r ^ {2}. \\ \end{array}
$$

For the mass integral then

$$
I = (8 \pi) ^ {- 1} \int \frac {2 M}{r ^ {2}} r ^ {2} \sin \theta d \theta d \phi = M
$$

as required. Similarly, we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} S = \int T _ {\nu} ^ {\mu} \xi_ {(\phi)} ^ {\nu} d ^ {3} \Sigma_ {\mu} \\ = (8 \pi) ^ {- 1} \int \mathrm {R} _ {\nu} ^ {\mu} \xi_ {(\phi)} ^ {\nu} \mathrm {d} ^ {3} \Sigma_ {\mu} - (1 6 \pi) ^ {- 1} \int \mathrm {R} \xi_ {(\phi)} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {d} ^ {3} \Sigma_ {\mu}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Since $\mathrm{d}^3 \Sigma_{\phi} = 0$ the second term vanishes and

$$
\begin{array}{l} S = - (8 \pi) ^ {- 1} \int \xi^ {\mu ; \nu} _ {; \nu} d ^ {3} \Sigma_ {\mu} - 0 = - (1 6 \pi) ^ {- 1} \int \xi^ {\mu ; \nu} d ^ {2} \Sigma_ {\mu \nu} \\ = - (1 6 \pi) ^ {- 1} \oint 2 \xi^ {t; r} r ^ {2} \sin \theta d \theta d \phi . \\ \end{array}
$$

But

$$
\begin{array}{l} \xi^ {t; r} \approx \xi_ {, r} ^ {t} + \Gamma_ {a r} ^ {t} \xi^ {a} = 0 + \Gamma_ {\phi_ {r}} ^ {t} \\ = \mathbf {g} ^ {\mathrm {t t}} \Gamma_ {\mathbf {t} \phi \mathbf {r}} + \mathbf {g} ^ {\mathrm {t} \phi} \Gamma_ {\phi \phi \mathbf {r}} \\ \approx - \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {g} _ {\mathrm {t}} \phi , \mathrm {r} + \left(\frac {- 2 \mathrm {J}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {3}}\right) \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {g} _ {\phi \phi , \mathrm {r}} \\ = - 3 J \sin^ {2} \theta / r ^ {2}, \\ \end{array}
$$

and thus

$$
S = (1 6 \pi) ^ {- 1} \int 6 J \sin^ {3} \theta d \theta d \phi = J.
$$

Solution 16.24. In curvature coordinates, $\xi_{(t)}^{\nu} = \delta_{t}^{\nu}$ and $\mathrm{d}^3\Sigma_\mu = \delta_\mu^t |\mathbf{g}|^\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{d}\mathbf{r}\mathrm{d}\theta \mathrm{d}\phi$ , thus the integral is

$$
\begin{array}{l} I = - \int (2 T _ {\nu} ^ {\mu} - \delta_ {\nu} ^ {\mu} T) \xi_ {(t)} ^ {\nu} d ^ {3} \Sigma_ {\mu} \\ = - \int \left(2 T _ {t} ^ {t} - T\right) e ^ {\Phi + \lambda} r ^ {2} \sin \theta d \theta d \phi d r. \\ \end{array}
$$

But

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {T} _ {t} ^ {t} = (\rho + p) u ^ {t} u _ {t} + p \delta_ {t} ^ {t} = - \rho \\ T = - (\rho + p) + 4 p = 3 p - \rho . \\ \end{array}
$$

Since everything is independent of $\theta$ and $\phi$ , the integration over angles gives $4\pi$ and therefore

$$
I = \int_ {0} ^ {R} (\rho + 3 p) e ^ {\Phi + \lambda} 4 \pi r ^ {2} d r. \tag {1}
$$

We can transform this by integration by parts. Since

$$
\mathrm {m} (\mathrm {r}) = \int_ {0} ^ {\mathrm {r}} 4 \pi \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \rho \mathrm {d r} \tag {2}
$$

we have

$$
\int_ {0} ^ {\mathbf {R}} \rho e ^ {\Phi + \lambda} 4 \pi r ^ {2} d r = [ m e ^ {\Phi + \lambda} ] _ {0} ^ {\mathbf {R}} - \int_ {0} ^ {\mathbf {R}} m (e ^ {\Phi + \lambda}) ^ {\prime} d r \tag {3}
$$

where a prime denotes $\mathrm{d} / \mathrm{dr}$ . The stellar structure equations tell us $\mathfrak{p}'$ so we write

$$
\int_ {0} ^ {\mathrm {R}} 3 \mathrm {p e} ^ {\Phi + \lambda} 4 \pi \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \mathrm {d r} = [ \mathrm {p e} ^ {\Phi + \lambda} 4 \pi \mathrm {r} ^ {3} ] _ {0} ^ {\mathrm {R}} - \int_ {0} ^ {\mathrm {R}} 4 \pi \mathrm {r} ^ {3} (\mathrm {p e} ^ {\Phi + \lambda}) ^ {\prime} \mathrm {d r}. \tag {4}
$$

The first term on the right in Equation (3) is $\mathfrak{m}(\mathbb{R}) = \mathbb{M}$ , since $\mathfrak{m} = 0$ at $\mathbf{r} = 0$ and $\Phi = -\lambda$ at $\mathbf{r} = \mathbb{R}$ . The first term on the right in Equation (4) is zero since $\mathfrak{p} = 0$ at $\mathbf{r} = \mathbb{R}$ . Thus, $\mathbf{I} = \mathbf{M}$ provided the sum of the remaining terms in Equations (3) and (4) vanish. This sum is

$$
\begin{array}{l} \int_ {0} ^ {\mathrm {R}} \mathrm {m} \left(\mathrm {e} ^ {\Phi + \lambda}\right) ^ {\prime} \mathrm {d r} + \int_ {0} ^ {\mathrm {R}} 4 \pi \mathrm {r} ^ {3} \left(\mathrm {p e} ^ {\Phi + \lambda}\right) ^ {\prime} \mathrm {d r} \\ = \int_ {0} ^ {\mathrm {R}} \mathrm {d r e} ^ {\Phi + \lambda} \left[ \mathrm {m} \left(\Phi^ {\prime} + \lambda^ {\prime}\right) + 4 \pi \mathrm {r} ^ {3} \left\{\mathrm {p} ^ {\prime} + \mathrm {p} \left(\Phi^ {\prime} + \lambda^ {\prime}\right) \right\} \right]. \tag {5} \\ \end{array}
$$

Now

$$
e ^ {2 \lambda} = (1 - 2 m / r) ^ {- 1}
$$

so we get

$$
\lambda^ {\prime} = \frac {(4 \pi r ^ {3} \rho - m)}{r (r - 2 m)}.
$$

If this result and the relations

$$
\Phi^ {\prime} = \frac {\mathrm {m} + 4 \pi \mathrm {r} ^ {3} \rho}{\mathrm {r} (\mathrm {r} - 2 \mathrm {m})}
$$

$$
p ^ {\prime} = \frac {- (\rho + p) (m + 4 \pi r ^ {3} p)}{r (r - 2 m)}
$$

are substituted in the expression in Equation (5), all terms cancel and it reduces to zero as required.

Solution 16.25. Let $\theta, \phi$ and the radial coordinate $\mathbf{R}$ be comoving with the fluid of the star. We can then write the metric as

$$
\begin{array}{l} d s ^ {2} = g _ {t t} d t ^ {2} + g _ {t R} d R d t + g _ {R R} d R ^ {2} + g _ {t i} d t d x ^ {i} \\ + \mathbf {g} _ {\mathbb {R} \mathrm {i}} \mathrm {d} R \mathrm {d x} ^ {\mathrm {i}} + \mathbf {g} _ {\mathrm {i j}} \mathrm {d x} ^ {\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {d x} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \quad (\mathrm {i}, \mathrm {j} = \theta , \phi) \\ \end{array}
$$

where $\mathbf{i}, \mathbf{j} = \theta, \phi$ . If the metric is spherically symmetric, then for $\mathrm{dt} = \mathrm{dR} = 0$ we must have

$$
d s ^ {2} = g _ {i j} d x ^ {i} d x ^ {j} = r ^ {2} (R, t) \left(d \theta^ {2} + \sin^ {2} \theta d \phi^ {2}\right).
$$

Furthermore, $\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{ti}}$ and $\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{Ri}}$ define vectors in the 2-dimensional $\theta, \phi$ space, so by the isotropy of spherical shells $\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{ti}}$ and $\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{Ri}}$ must vanish. Finally, we can choose a new time coordinate $\mathbf{T} = \mathbf{T}(\mathbf{t}, \mathbf{r})$ to remove the $\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{tr}}$ term, without affecting the comoving status of the radial coordinate, and we have

$$
d s ^ {2} = g _ {T T} d T ^ {2} + g _ {R R} d R ^ {2} + r ^ {2} (R, T) d \Omega^ {2}.
$$

Thus in general we can have both comoving coordinates and a diagonal metric.

Now consider the motion of a fluid element. From the fluid 4-velocity

$$
\mathbf {u} = \left(- \mathbf {g} _ {\mathbf {T T}}\right) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {e} _ {\mathbf {T}}
$$

we have the fluid acceleration:

$$
\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {u} = \mathbf {e} _ {\mathrm {R}} \left[ \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {u} ^ {\mathrm {R}}}{\mathrm {d} \tau} + (\mathbf {u} ^ {\mathrm {T}}) ^ {2} \boldsymbol {\Gamma} _ {\mathrm {T T}} ^ {\mathrm {R}} \right] = - \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {e} _ {\mathrm {R}} (\mathbf {u} ^ {\mathrm {T}}) ^ {2} \mathbf {g} ^ {\mathrm {R R}} \mathbf {g} _ {\mathrm {T T}, \mathrm {R}}.
$$

It follows then that the fluid acceleration is zero (and hence the pressure gradient vanishes) if and only $\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{T}\mathbf{T}}$ is a function of $\mathbf{T}$ only. But if that is so, we can define a new time coordinate $\tau (\mathbf{T})$ from the equation

$$
\mathrm {d} \tau / \mathrm {d} \mathbf {T} = \left(- \mathbf {g} _ {\mathbf {T T}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}
$$

representing proper time for the fluid. Thus the three nice properties are all satisfied if and only if the pressure gradient is zero; since the pressure must be zero on the surface of the star, a vanishing pressure gradient is equivalent to zero pressure.

Solution 16.26.

(a) The first law of thermodynamics (see Problem 5.19) in the comoving frame is

$$
\frac {\dot {\rho}}{\rho + \mathfrak {p}} = \frac {\dot {\mathfrak {n}}}{\mathfrak {n}}
$$

where $n$ is baryon density. The number of baryons in a shell of thickness $dR$ is $(4\pi nr^2 e^{\Lambda})dR$ , so the law of conservation of baryons in this shell

$$
\partial (\mathrm {n r} ^ {2} \mathrm {e} ^ {\Lambda}) / \partial t = 0
$$

and thus

$$
\frac {\dot {\rho}}{\rho + \mathbf {p}} = - \frac {2 \dot {\mathbf {r}}}{\mathbf {r}} - \dot {\boldsymbol {\Lambda}}.
$$

We can now combine this with

$$
G _ {R} ^ {\hat {t}} = 0 = \frac {2}{r} e ^ {- \Phi - \lambda} \left(\dot {r} ^ {\prime} - \dot {r} \Phi^ {\prime} - r ^ {\prime} \dot {\Lambda}\right)
$$

(see Problem 9.20) and with

$$
\mathrm {T} _ {\mathrm {R}; \beta} ^ {\beta} = 0 = \mathrm {p} ^ {\prime} + (\mathrm {p} + \rho) \Phi^ {\prime}
$$

to arrive at

$$
- \dot {\rho} = (\rho + p) \left(\frac {2 \dot {\mathbf {r}}}{\mathbf {r}} + \frac {\dot {\mathbf {r}} ^ {\prime}}{\mathbf {r} ^ {\prime}}\right) + \frac {\dot {\mathbf {r}} \dot {\mathbf {p}} ^ {\prime}}{\mathbf {r} ^ {\prime}}.
$$

This relation can now be substituted in the expression for $\dot{\mathfrak{m}}$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \dot {\mathrm {m}} = 4 \pi \int_ {0} ^ {\mathrm {R}} \left(2 \mathrm {r} \mathrm {r} \mathrm {r} ^ {\prime} \rho + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \dot {\mathrm {r}} ^ {\prime} \rho + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \mathrm {r} ^ {\prime} \dot {\rho}\right) \mathrm {d R} \\ = - 4 \pi \int_ {0} ^ {R} \left(2 \bar {r r} ^ {\prime} \dot {p} + r ^ {2} \dot {r} ^ {\prime} p + r ^ {2} \dot {r} p ^ {\prime}\right) d R \\ = - 4 \pi \int_ {0} ^ {R} \left(r ^ {2} \dot {r} p\right) ^ {\prime} d R. \\ \end{array}
$$

Since $\mathbf{r}^2\dot{\mathbf{r}}\mathbf{p} = 0$ at $\mathbf{R} = 0$ it follows that

$$
\dot {\mathbf {m}} = - 4 \pi \mathbf {r} ^ {2} \mathbf {p} \dot {\mathbf {r}}.
$$

(b) From $\mathbf{G}_{\hat{\mathbf{t}}}^{\hat{\mathbf{t}}} = -8\pi \rho$ (see Problem 9.20) we have

$$
8 \pi \rho r ^ {2} = 1 - r ^ {\prime 2} e ^ {- 2 \Lambda} + \dot {r} ^ {2} e ^ {- 2 \Phi} + 2 r \dot {r} e ^ {- 2 \Phi} \dot {\Lambda} + 2 r e ^ {- 2 \Lambda} (r ^ {\prime} \Lambda^ {\prime} - r ^ {\prime \prime}).
$$

Now use $\mathbf{G}_{\widehat{\mathbf{R}}}^{\widehat{\mathbf{t}}} = 0$ to eliminate $\dot{\Lambda}$ , and note that $8\pi r^{2} \rho r'$ is an exact differential with respect to $\mathbf{R}$

$$
\begin{array}{l} 8 \pi \rho r ^ {2} r ^ {\prime} = r ^ {\prime} - (r ^ {\prime}) ^ {3} e ^ {- 2 \Lambda} + \dot {r} ^ {2} r ^ {\prime} e ^ {- 2 \Phi} + 2 r \dot {r} e ^ {- 2 \Phi} (\dot {r} ^ {\prime} - \dot {r} \Phi^ {\prime}) \\ + 2 \mathrm {r r} ^ {\prime} \mathrm {e} ^ {- 2 \Lambda} \left(\mathrm {r} ^ {\prime} \Lambda^ {\prime} - \mathrm {r} ^ {\prime \prime}\right) \\ = \left[ r - e ^ {- 2 \Lambda} r (r ^ {\prime}) ^ {2} + r (\dot {r}) ^ {2} e ^ {- 2 \Phi} \right] ^ {\prime}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Integration gives the desired relation

$$
\begin{array}{l} 2 m = r \left(1 - e ^ {- 2 \Lambda} \left(r ^ {\prime}\right) ^ {2} + \dot {r} ^ {2} e ^ {- 2 \Phi}\right) \\ = \mathrm {r} (1 - \Gamma^ {2} + \mathrm {U} ^ {2}). \\ \end{array}
$$

Solution 16.27. From Problem 16.26 we have

$$
\mathrm {d r} / \mathrm {d} \tau = U \tag {1}
$$

$$
\mathrm {d m} / \mathrm {d} \tau = - 4 \pi \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \mathrm {p U} \tag {2}
$$

$$
U = \frac {d r}{d r} = \pm \left[ \Gamma^ {2} + \left(\frac {2 m}{r} - 1\right) \right] ^ {\frac {1}{2}}. \tag {3}
$$

The first two equations show that $\mathrm{dr} / \mathrm{d}\tau$ and $\mathrm{dm} / \mathrm{d}\tau$ have opposite signs, so for the shell to pass from $2\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{r} < 1$ to $2\mathrm{m} / \mathrm{r} > 1$ it must be that

$$
\dot {\mathbf {r}} \leq 0, \quad \dot {\mathbf {m}} \geq 0
$$

upon passage through $\mathbf{r} = 2\mathbf{m}$ . From Equation (3) then

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {r}}{\mathrm {d} \tau} = - \left[ \Gamma^ {2} + \left(\frac {2 \mathrm {m}}{\mathrm {r}} - 1\right) \right] ^ {\frac {1}{2}}.
$$

The radius therefore will continue to fall as long as $\Gamma^2 + \left(\frac{2\mathfrak{m}}{\mathfrak{r}} - 1\right) > 0$ , but as long as the radius is decreasing, the mass must be increasing and the factor $\frac{2\mathfrak{m}}{\mathfrak{r}} - 1$ continues to increase, so the collapse cannot stop.

Suppose the shell has reached the point $2\mathfrak{m} / \mathfrak{r} - 1 = \varepsilon > 0$ . Since this factor continues to increase, then

$$
\mathrm {d} \mathbf {r} / \mathrm {d} \tau \leq \varepsilon^ {\frac {1}{2}}
$$

and the shell will reach $r = 0$ in a proper time $\leq 2m / \varepsilon^{\frac{1}{2}}$ . [This solution is due to J. M. Bardeen.]

Solution 16.28. In Problem 16.25 we saw that we can choose $\Phi = 0$ for pressure-free collapse. From $G_{R}^{R} = 0$ (see Problem 9.20) with $\Phi = 0$ we have then

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = 2 \mathrm {r} \ddot {\mathrm {r}} + 1 - \mathrm {r} ^ {\prime 2} \mathrm {e} ^ {- 2 \Lambda} + \dot {\mathrm {r}} ^ {2} \\ = 2 \mathrm {r} \ddot {\mathrm {r}} + 1 - \Gamma^ {2} + \mathrm {U} ^ {2} \\ \end{array}
$$

and therefore (see Problem 16.26)

$$
\ddot {\mathbf {r}} = \frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathbf {r}}{\mathrm {d} r ^ {2}} = - \frac {\mathbf {M}}{\mathbf {r} ^ {2}}.
$$

Solution 16.29. From the dynamical equation for mass (see Problem 16.26)

$$
\dot {\mathbf {m}} = - 4 \pi \mathbf {p} \mathbf {r} ^ {2} \dot {\mathbf {r}} = 0
$$

mass is clearly time independent, as is physically obvious. By differentiating the $\Gamma^2$ equation in Problem 16.26 we get

$$
2 \Gamma \dot {\Gamma} = 2 U \dot {U} + \frac {2 m}{r ^ {2}} \dot {r} = 2 \dot {r} \left(\ddot {r} + \frac {m}{r ^ {2}}\right)
$$

and using the result of Problem 16.28 we see that $\Gamma$ , too, is time independent.

The dynamical equation for $\mathbf{r}(\mathbf{R},\tau)$ is now a simple first order equation and can be solved parametrically:

Case (i) $\Gamma^2 - 1 < 0$ :

$$
r = \frac {m (b)}{1 - \Gamma^ {2}} (1 + \cos \eta) \tag {1a}
$$

$$
\tau = \frac {\mathrm {m} (\mathrm {b})}{(1 - \Gamma^ {2}) ^ {\frac {3}{2}}} (\eta + \sin \eta) + \mathrm {F} (\mathrm {b}) \tag {1b}
$$

where $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{b})$ is an arbitrary function.

$$
\underline {{\text {C a s e (i i)}}} \quad \Gamma^ {2} - 1 = 0:
$$

$$
r = \left\{\frac {3}{2} (m (b)) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} [ g (b) - r ] \right\} ^ {\frac {2}{3}} \tag {2}
$$

where $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{b})$ is an arbitrary function.

$$
\underline {{\text {C a s e (i i i)}}} \quad \Gamma^ {2} - 1 > 0:
$$

$$
r = \frac {m (b)}{\Gamma^ {2} - 1} (\cosh \eta - 1) \tag {3a}
$$

$$
\tau = \frac {\mathrm {m} (\mathrm {b})}{(\Gamma^ {2} - 1) ^ {\frac {3}{2}}} (\sinh \eta - \eta) + \mathrm {H} (\mathrm {b}) \tag {3b}
$$

where $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{b})$ is an arbitrary function.

Note that there are three free functions - i.e. functions that we can choose by choosing the physical situation. By choosing $\mathfrak{m}(\mathfrak{b})$ we can choose the distribution of mass at some initial time and forever after. The integration "constants" $\mathbf{F}(\mathbf{b})$ , $\mathbf{g}(\mathbf{b})$ , $\mathbf{H}(\mathbf{b})$ correspond to the choice of the $\mathbf{r}$ value of each fluid element on an initial hypersurface, i.e. the choice of $\mathbf{r}(\mathbf{b}, t = 0)$ .

The choice of $\Gamma^2 (\mathfrak{b})$ corresponds to the choice of the velocity of a fluid element on an initial hypersurface. By comparison with the equation for a radial geodesic, $\Gamma^2 -1$ can be thought of as the conserved "energy at $\infty$ ' for the fluid shell. Therefore, it should not be surprising that there are three solution regimes: we can choose to give a fluid shell less than the escape velocity (a), precisely the escape velocity (b), or greater than the escape velocity (c). Note that the sign of t can be reversed in the above solutions so that, for example, solution (c) can correspond to shells falling inward. Note that in general if the three free functions are not chosen carefully, mass shells are going to cross!

# Solution 16.30.

(i) The stress tensor is $\mathbf{T}_{\alpha \beta} = \rho \mathbf{u}_{\alpha}\mathbf{u}_{\beta}$ . The assumptions of spherical symmetry and uniform density are equivalent to assuming isotropy and homogeneity, so the metric must be a Friedmann solution:

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - \mathrm {d} \tau^ {2} + \mathrm {a} ^ {2} (\tau) [ \mathrm {d} \chi^ {2} + \Sigma^ {2} (\chi) (\mathrm {d} \theta^ {2} + \sin^ {2} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2}) ] \tag {1}
$$

where

$$
\Sigma = \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} \sin \chi , & k = 1 \\ \chi , & k = 0 \\ \sinh \chi , & k = - 1. \end{array} \right.
$$

The surface of the star is at some constant value of the "radial" coordinate, $\chi = \chi_0$ say. From Equation (1), the proper circumferential radius of the star is $R = a(r)\Sigma (\chi_0)$ . The Einstein field equations (see Problems 19.16, 19.18) for zero pressure are

$$
\begin{array}{l} \left(\frac {a , r}{a}\right) ^ {2} = - \frac {k}{a ^ {2}} + \frac {8 \pi \rho}{3} (2) \\ \rho a ^ {3} = \text {c o n s t a n t}. (3) \\ \end{array}
$$

Equation (2) can be rewritten as

$$
\rho = \frac {3}{8 \pi} \left[ \left(\frac {\mathrm {R} _ {, \tau}}{\mathrm {R}}\right) ^ {2} + \frac {\mathrm {k} \Sigma^ {2} \left(\chi_ {0}\right)}{\mathrm {R} ^ {2}} \right]. \tag {4}
$$

Thus, if $\mathbf{R}_{,\tau} = 0$ at any finite value of $\mathbf{R}$ , $\mathbf{k}$ must be $+1$ so that $\rho$ is positive. Conversely, if $\mathbf{k} = -1$ , $\mathbf{R}_{,\tau}$ is never zero. The case $\mathbf{k} = 0$ corresponds to $\mathbf{R}_{,\tau} \to 0$ as $\mathbf{R} \to \infty$ .

(ii) The Euler equation for zero pressure implies $\nabla_{\mathbf{u}}\mathbf{u} = 0$ , i.e. each fluid element moves along a geodesic. By spherical symmetry the geodesics are radial.

(iii) We shall do the matching for $k = +1$ ; the cases $k = 0$ or $k = -1$ are similar. For $k = +1$ , Equations (2) and (3) give

$$
\left(a, r\right) ^ {2} = a _ {\mathrm {m}} / a - 1 \tag {5}
$$

where the constant in Equation (3) has been fixed by setting $\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{a}_{\mathfrak{m}} =$ maximum of a, when $\mathbf{a}_{,r} = 0$ . Equation (5) can be integrated as it stands; it is convenient, however, to introduce a new time parameter by

$$
\mathrm {d} r = \operatorname {a d} \eta . \tag {6}
$$

Equations (5) and (6) then give

$$
\begin{array}{l} a = \frac {1}{2} a _ {m} (1 + \cos \eta) (7) \\ r = \frac {1}{2} a _ {\mathrm {m}} (\eta + \sin \eta). (8) \\ \end{array}
$$

The constants of integration have been chosen to make $\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{a}_{\mathbf{m}}$ and $r = 0$ at $\eta = 0$ , and to make $\mathbf{a} = 0$ and $r = \pi \mathbf{a}_{\mathbf{m}} / 2$ at $\eta = \pi$ .

The intrinsic 3-geometry of the surface of the star, as measured from the interior, is found by putting $\chi = \chi_0$ in Equation (1):

$$
\begin{array}{l} \left. ^ {(3)} d s ^ {2} = - d \tau^ {2} + a ^ {2} (\tau) \sin^ {2} \chi_ {0} d \Omega^ {2} \right. \\ = a ^ {2} (\eta) \left(- d \eta^ {2} + \sin^ {2} \chi_ {0} d \Omega^ {2}\right). \tag {9} \\ \end{array}
$$

The exterior metric is

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - (1 - 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r}) \mathrm {d t} ^ {2} + (1 - 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r}) ^ {- 1} \mathrm {d r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \mathrm {d} \Omega^ {2}. \tag {10}
$$

The surface of the star is at $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{R}(r)$ , and from the equations for radial geodesics (see Problem 15.4), $\mathbf{R}(r)$ is given by

$$
R = \frac {1}{2} R _ {i} (1 + \cos \eta) \tag {11}
$$

$$
\tau = \left(\frac {\mathrm {R} _ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {3}}{8 \mathrm {M}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} (\eta + \sin \eta) \tag {12}
$$

$$
u ^ {t} = \frac {d t}{d r} = \frac {\left(1 - 2 M / R _ {i}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{1 - 2 M / R}. \tag {13}
$$

Thus the geometry of the surface is

$$
\begin{array}{l} \left(^ {(3)} \mathrm {d} s _ {+} ^ {2} = - \mathrm {d} \tau^ {2} + R ^ {2} (r) \mathrm {d} \Omega^ {2} \right. \\ = - \left(\frac {\mathrm {R} _ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {3}}{8 \mathrm {M}}\right) (1 + \cos \eta) ^ {2} \mathrm {d} \eta^ {2} + \frac {\mathrm {R} _ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {2}}{4} (1 + \cos \eta) ^ {2} \mathrm {d} \Omega^ {2}. \tag {14} \\ \end{array}
$$

Comparison with Equation (9) shows that the 3-geometries match provided we identify

$$
\mathrm {R} _ {\mathrm {i}} = \mathrm {a} _ {\mathrm {m}} \sin \chi_ {0}, \quad 2 \mathrm {M} = \mathrm {a} _ {\mathrm {m}} \sin^ {3} \chi_ {0}. \tag {15}
$$

Now compute the extrinsic curvature $\mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{ij}}^{(-)}$ in the interior. The normal to the surface is

$$
\mathbf {n} = \mathbf {a} ^ {- 1} \partial / \partial \chi \tag {16}
$$

(recall that $\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{n} = 1$ ), while the vectors $\mathbf{u} = \partial/\partial \tau$ , $\partial/\partial \theta$ and $\partial/\partial \phi$ lie in the surface. Let the indices $i, j$ range over $\tau, \theta, \phi$ . Then

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {i j}} \equiv - \mathrm {e} _ {\mathrm {i}} \cdot \nabla_ {\mathrm {j}} \mathrm {n} = - \mathrm {e} _ {\mathrm {i}} \cdot \Gamma_ {\mathrm {n j}} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {e} _ {\alpha} = - \mathrm {g} _ {\mathrm {i} \alpha} \Gamma_ {\mathrm {n j}} ^ {\alpha} \\ = - \Gamma_ {\text {i n j}} = - \frac {1}{2} \left(\mathrm {g} _ {\text {i n}, \mathrm {j}} + \mathrm {g} _ {\mathrm {i j}, \mathrm {n}} - \mathrm {g} _ {\mathrm {n j}, \mathrm {i}}\right) = - \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {g} _ {\mathrm {i j}, \mathrm {n}} \tag {17} \\ \end{array}
$$

since $\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{in}} = \mathbf{a}^{-1}\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{i}\chi} = 0$ . From the metric we see that

$$
\mathrm {K} _ {\tau \tau} ^ {(-)} = \mathrm {K} _ {\tau \theta} ^ {(-)} \doteq \mathrm {K} _ {\tau \phi} ^ {(-)} = \mathrm {K} _ {\theta \phi} ^ {(-)} = 0 \tag {18}
$$

$$
\mathrm {K} _ {\theta \theta} ^ {(-)} = \mathrm {K} _ {\phi \phi} ^ {(-)} \sin^ {- 2} \theta = - \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {a} _ {\mathrm {m}} (1 + \cos \eta) \sin \chi_ {0} \cos \chi_ {0}. \tag {19}
$$

In the exterior metric, the 4-velocity is

$$
\mathbf {u} = \mathbf {u} ^ {\mathbf {t}} \mathbf {e} _ {\mathbf {t}} + \mathbf {u} ^ {\mathbf {r}} \mathbf {e} _ {\mathbf {r}}.
$$

The normal vector

$$
\mathbf {n} = \mathbf {n} ^ {\mathrm {t}} \mathbf {e} _ {\mathrm {t}} + \mathbf {n} ^ {\mathrm {r}} \mathbf {e} _ {\mathrm {r}}
$$

satisfies

$$
\mathbf {n} \cdot \mathbf {n} = 1 = \mathrm {g} _ {\mathrm {t t}} \left(\mathrm {n} ^ {\mathrm {t}}\right) ^ {2} + \mathrm {g} _ {\mathrm {r r}} \left(\mathrm {n} ^ {\mathrm {r}}\right) ^ {2} \tag {20}
$$

$$
\mathbf {n} \cdot \mathbf {u} = 0 = \mathrm {n} ^ {\mathrm {t}} \mathrm {u} _ {\mathrm {t}} + \mathrm {n} ^ {\mathrm {r}} \mathrm {u} _ {\mathrm {r}}. \tag {21}
$$

Since

$$
\mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {u} = - 1 = \mathrm {g} ^ {\mathrm {t t}} \left(\mathrm {u} _ {\mathrm {t}}\right) ^ {2} + \mathrm {g} ^ {\mathrm {r r}} \left(\mathrm {u} _ {\mathrm {r}}\right) ^ {2} \tag {22}
$$

and

$$
