\mathbf {g} ^ {\mathrm {r r}} = \left(\mathbf {g} _ {\mathrm {r r}}\right) ^ {- 1} = - \left(\mathbf {g} ^ {\mathrm {t t}}\right) ^ {- 1} = - \mathbf {g} _ {\mathrm {t t}} = 1 - 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r}
$$

the equations above imply that

$$
\mathrm {n} ^ {\mathrm {t}} = \mathrm {u} _ {\mathrm {r}}, \quad \mathrm {n} ^ {\mathrm {r}} = - \mathrm {u} _ {\mathrm {t}}. \tag {23}
$$

Let $i, j$ range over $\tau, \theta, \phi$ as before. Then Equation (17) holds for the exterior metric, since $\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{in}} = \mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{e}_{\mathrm{i}} = 0$ . Equation (18) holds for the exterior metric because $\mathbf{e}_{\tau} \cdot \mathbf{e}_{\tau} = \mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{u} = -1$ , $\mathbf{e}_{\tau} \cdot \mathbf{e}_{\theta} = \mathbf{e}_{\tau} \cdot \mathbf{e}_{\phi} = \mathbf{e}_{\theta} \cdot \mathbf{e}_{\phi} = 0$ . Also,

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {K} _ {\theta \theta} ^ {(+)} = \mathrm {K} _ {\phi \phi} ^ {(+)} \sin^ {- 2} \theta = - \frac {1}{2} (\mathrm {r} ^ {2}) _ {\mathrm {, n}} = - \frac {1}{2} (\mathrm {r} ^ {2}) _ {\mathrm {, r}} \mathrm {n} ^ {\mathrm {r}} \\ = r u _ {t} = - R \left(1 - 2 M / R _ {i}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \\ = - \frac {1}{2} R _ {i} (1 + \cos \eta) (1 - 2 M / R _ {i}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \\ = - \frac {1}{2} a _ {m} \sin \chi_ {0} \cos \chi_ {0} (1 + \cos \eta) \\ \end{array}
$$

where we have used Equations (23), (13), (11) and (15). Thus $\mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{ij}}^{(+)} = \mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{ij}}^{(-)}$ and the proof is complete.

# CHAPTER 17: SOLUTIONS

Solution 17.1. One finds the mass and angular momentum of an asymptotically Minkowskian (flat) metric by finding a coordinate system in which it takes the form

$$
\mathrm {g} _ {0 0} = - \left(1 - \frac {2 \tilde {\mathrm {M}}}{\mathrm {r}} + \mathcal {O} (\mathrm {r} ^ {- 2})\right) \tag {1}
$$

$$
\mathbf {g} _ {0 j} = - \left(4 \varepsilon_ {j k l} \tilde {\mathbf {S}} \frac {\mathbf {k} \mathbf {x} ^ {1}}{\mathbf {r} ^ {3}} + \mathcal {O} (\mathbf {r} ^ {- 3})\right). \tag {2}
$$

The constants $\tilde{\mathbf{M}}$ and $\tilde{\mathbf{S}}^k$ which occur in the expansion are then the mass and intrinsic angular momentum (see Weinberg, Section 9.4 or MTW, Section 19.3).

Expanding the Kerr metric in Boyer-Lindquist coordinates in powers of $\mathbf{r}^{-1}$ gives the leading terms

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}} + \dots\right) \mathrm {d t} ^ {2} - \left(\frac {4 \mathrm {a M}}{\mathrm {r}} \sin^ {2} \theta + \dots\right) \mathrm {d t} \mathrm {d} \phi \\ + (1 + \dots) [ d r ^ {2} + r ^ {2} (d \theta^ {2} + \sin^ {2} \theta d \phi^ {2}) ] \\ \end{array}
$$

Transforming to cartesian coordinates by $\mathbf{x} \equiv \mathbf{r} \sin \theta \cos \phi$ , $\mathbf{y} \equiv \mathbf{r} \sin \theta \sin \phi$ , $\mathbf{z} \equiv \mathbf{r} \cos \theta$ gives

$$
\begin{array}{l} d s ^ {2} = - \left(1 - \frac {2 M}{r} + \dots\right) d t ^ {2} - \left(\frac {4 a M}{r ^ {3}} + \dots\right) (x d y - y d x) d t \tag {3} \\ + (1 + \dots) (\mathrm {d} x ^ {2} + \mathrm {d} y ^ {2} + \mathrm {d} z ^ {2}) \\ \end{array}
$$

and comparison with Equations (1) and (2) gives at once

$$
\tilde {\mathbf {M}} = \mathbf {M}
$$

$$
\tilde {\mathbf {S}} = \mathbf {a M} \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {z}}} ^ {-}.
$$

Solution 17.2. Suppose the car has mass $m$ and length $L$ before crushing and length $L'$ and "lumpiness" $h$ afterward, and the hole has mass $M$ . Suppose the internal stress per mass that steel can support is $\epsilon (\sim 0.1$ electron volt per nuclear mass; see Problem 5.6). Comparing gravitational pressure forces to internal stresses, we see that the conditions for crushing are

$$
\left(\frac {\mathrm {G M}}{\mathrm {L} ^ {2}}\right) \mathrm {L} > \epsilon \quad \text {(c o n d i t i o n f o r c r u s h i n g t o b e g i n)} \tag {1}
$$

$$
\left(\frac {\mathrm {G M}}{\mathrm {L} ^ {\prime 2}}\right) \mathrm {h} > \epsilon \quad \text {(c o n d i t i o n f o r l u m p s n o l a r g e r} \quad \text {t h a n h a f t e r c r u s h i n g e n d s)}. \tag {2}
$$

Since cars typically crush to .1 of their original dimension, we see that the two inequalities are equivalent for $\mathrm{h} / \mathrm{L}' = .1$ , which is a reasonable value. Taking $\mathrm{L}' \sim 100\mathrm{cm}$ and $\epsilon \sim 10^{9}\mathrm{ergs/gm}$ gives $\mathbf{M} \geq 10^{18}\mathbf{gm}$ .

The time to crush a wreck is set by the free-fall time $(\mathbf{L}^3 / \mathbf{GM})^{\frac{1}{2}} \sim 10^{-5}$ sec, so on the order of $10^{8}$ can be processed per hour.

![](images/da43ea8bb883b4f6cd4f1906c647aee588bbf241435046b57efdb9ec1910b8a3.jpg)

![](images/cfc1f7270a1e9eafcd11c827ad0663569101bb4c0edce51945a628043740a953.jpg)

Solution 17.3. For the rocket ship to be moving along a timelike world-line, its 4-velocity must satisfy (from the Schwarzschild metric)

$$
\mathbf {1} = - \mathbf {u} \cdot \mathbf {u} = \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathsf {M}}{\mathsf {r}}\right) \left(\frac {\mathrm {d t}}{\mathrm {d} \tau}\right) ^ {2} - \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathsf {M}}{\mathsf {r}}\right) ^ {- 1} \left(\frac {\mathrm {d r}}{\mathrm {d} \tau}\right) ^ {2} - \mathsf {r} ^ {2} \left(\frac {\mathrm {d} \theta}{\mathrm {d} \tau}\right) ^ {2} - \mathsf {r} ^ {2} \sin^ {2} \theta \left(\frac {\mathrm {d} \phi}{\mathrm {d} \tau}\right) ^ {2}.
$$

Inside the horizon all of these terms are negative, except the one in $(\mathrm{dr} / \mathrm{dr})^2$ , so

$$
\left(\frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}} - 1\right) ^ {- 1} \left(\frac {\mathrm {d r}}{\mathrm {d r}}\right) ^ {2} > 1.
$$

We also know (e.g. from the Eddington-Finkelstein or Kruskal picture) that the sign of $\mathrm{dr} / \mathrm{d}\tau$ must be negative for a “future directed” (i.e. physical) observer and thus,

$$
\mathrm {d r} <   - \left(\frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}} - 1\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d} \tau
$$

and

$$
\begin{array}{l} \tau_ {\max } = - \int_ {2 M} ^ {0} \left(\frac {2 M}{r} - 1\right) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} d r \\ = \left[ r ^ {\frac {1}{2}} (2 M - r) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} + M \cos^ {- 1} \left(\frac {r}{M} - 1\right) \right] _ {2 M} ^ {0} = \pi M. \\ \end{array}
$$

Solution 17.4. We derive the case for a Kerr black hole and then obtain the Schwarzschild case by setting $\mathbf{a} = 0$ . The Kerr metric is (in the usual Boyer-Lindquist coordinates)

$$
\begin{array}{l} d s ^ {2} = - \left(1 - \frac {2 M r}{\Sigma}\right) d t ^ {2} - \left(4 M a r \frac {\sin^ {2} \theta}{\Sigma}\right) d t d \phi + \frac {\Sigma}{\Delta} d r ^ {2} \tag {1} \\ + \Sigma d \theta^ {2} + \left(r ^ {2} + a ^ {2} + 2 M a ^ {2} r \frac {\sin^ {2} \theta}{\Sigma}\right) \sin^ {2} \theta d \phi^ {2} \\ \end{array}
$$

where

$$
\Delta \equiv r ^ {2} - 2 M r + a ^ {2}, \quad \Sigma \equiv r ^ {2} + a ^ {2} \cos^ {2} \theta .
$$

The geodesic equation is

$$
\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathrm {x} ^ {\mu} / \mathrm {d} \tau^ {2} + \Gamma_ {\alpha \beta} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {u} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {u} ^ {\beta} = 0 \tag {2}
$$

but the first term vanishes since $\mathbf{u}^{\mathrm{t}}$ and $\mathbf{u}^{\phi}$ , the nonvanishing components of $\mathbf{u}$ , are both constant. The $r$ component of Equation (2) is

$$
0 = \Gamma_ {\mathrm {r} \alpha \beta} \mathrm {u} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {u} ^ {\beta} = \left(\Gamma_ {\mathrm {r t t}} \mathrm {d t} ^ {2} + 2 \Gamma_ {\mathrm {r t}} \phi \mathrm {d t} \mathrm {d} \phi + \Gamma_ {\mathrm {r}} \phi \phi \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2}\right) (\mathrm {d} \tau) ^ {- 2}. \tag {3}
$$

Since the metric coefficients depend only on $\mathbf{r}$ and $\theta$ , the relevant $\Gamma$ 's in the equatorial $(\theta = \pi / 2)$ plane are

$$
\Gamma_ {\mathrm {r t t}} = - \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {g} _ {\mathrm {t t}, \mathrm {r}} = \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r} ^ {2}
$$

$$
2 \Gamma_ {\mathrm {r t} \phi} = - \mathrm {g} _ {\mathrm {t} \phi , \mathrm {r}} = - 2 \mathrm {M a} / \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \tag {4}
$$

$$
\Gamma_ {\mathbf {r} \phi \phi} = - \frac {1}{2} g _ {\phi \phi , \mathbf {r}} = \frac {\mathrm {M a} ^ {2}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}} - \mathrm {r}.
$$

So if $\Omega \equiv \mathrm{d}\phi/\mathrm{d}t$ , Equations (3) and (4) give

$$
0 = \Omega^ {2} \left(\frac {\mathrm {M a} ^ {2}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}} - \mathrm {r}\right) - \frac {2 \mathrm {M a}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}} \Omega + \frac {\mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}}, \tag {5}
$$

a quadratic whose two roots (corresponding to direct and retrograde orbits) are

$$
\Omega = \mathrm {M} ^ {\frac {1}{2}} / \left(\pm \mathrm {r} ^ {\frac {3}{2}} + \mathrm {a M} ^ {\frac {1}{2}}\right). \tag {6}
$$

For $a = 0$ this becomes $\Omega^2 = \mathbf{M} / \mathbf{r}^3$ which (by coincidence in these coordinates) is exactly the Newtonian Kepler's law.

Solution 17.5. The Reissner-Nordström metric is

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - \mathrm {A d t} ^ {2} + \mathrm {A} ^ {- 1} \mathrm {d r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} (\mathrm {d} \theta^ {2} + \sin^ {2} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2})
$$

where $\mathbf{A} \equiv 1 - 2\mathbf{M} / \mathbf{r} + \mathbf{Q}^2 / \mathbf{r}^2$ . We first find the Keplerian frequency $\Omega \equiv \frac{\mathrm{d}\phi}{\mathrm{d}t}$ of circular orbits, in the same manner as in Solution 17.4. Here

$$
\Gamma_ {\mathrm {r t t}} = - \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {g} _ {\mathrm {t t}, \mathrm {r}} = \frac {\mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}} - \frac {\mathrm {Q} ^ {2}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {3}}
$$

$$
2 \Gamma_ {\mathrm {r} t \phi} = - \mathbf {g} _ {\mathrm {t} \phi , \mathrm {r}} = 0
$$

$$
\Gamma_ {r \phi \phi} = - \frac {1}{2} g _ {\phi \phi , r} = - r
$$

so the quadratic equation for $\Omega$ becomes

$$
\Omega^ {2} \mathrm {r} - \left(\frac {\mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}} - \frac {\mathrm {Q} ^ {2}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {3}}\right) = 0
$$

and the orbital frequency is

$$
\Omega = \pm \left(\frac {M}{r ^ {3}} - \frac {Q ^ {2}}{r ^ {4}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}.
$$

The proper velocity of the orbiting observer relative to a coordinate stationary observer is

$$
\hat {\mathrm {v}} = \frac {\mathrm {d} \hat {\phi}}{\mathrm {d} \hat {\mathrm {t}}} = \frac {\mathrm {r} \mathrm {d} \phi}{\mathrm {A} ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d t}} = \left(\frac {\mathrm {M r} - \mathrm {Q} ^ {2}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2} - 2 \mathrm {M r} + \mathrm {Q} ^ {2}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}.
$$

(Carets indicate orthonormal components in the stationary frame.) In the stationary frame the local electromagnetic field has only the component

$$
E _ {\mathrm {r}} = \frac {Q}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}}.
$$

Lorentz transforming this with the standard relations for E-M fields gives, in the orbiting frame, all components zero except

$$
E _ {\mathrm {r}} = (1 - \hat {\mathrm {v}} ^ {2}) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \frac {Q}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}} = \frac {Q}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}} \left(\frac {\mathrm {r} ^ {2} - 2 \mathrm {M r} + Q ^ {2}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2} - 3 \mathrm {M r} + 2 Q ^ {2}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}
$$

$$
\mathbf {B} _ {\hat {\theta}} = (1 - \hat {\mathbf {v}} ^ {2}) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \hat {\mathbf {v}} \frac {\mathrm {Q}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}} = \frac {\mathrm {Q}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}} \left(\frac {\mathrm {M r} - \mathrm {Q} ^ {2}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2} - 3 \mathrm {M r} + 2 \mathrm {Q} ^ {2}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}.
$$

Solution 17.6. The specific angular momentum $a$ and charge $e$ of a Kerr-Newman black hole of mass $m$ cannot be arbitrarily large, but must satisfy the inequality

$$
\frac {a ^ {2}}{c ^ {2}} + \frac {G e ^ {2}}{c ^ {4}} \leq \frac {G ^ {2}}{c ^ {4}} M ^ {2}. \tag {1}
$$

(Here the G's and c's have been put in for clarity.) Equation (1) follows from the requirement that there exist a horizon, located at

$$
\mathbf {r _ {H}} = \mathbf {M} + (\mathbf {M} ^ {2} - \mathbf {e} ^ {2} - \mathbf {a} ^ {2}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}.
$$

If Equation (1) is violated, one has a "naked singularity," which also turns out to be acausal. Measurements of the electron indicate that its spin, charge and mass give the result that the $a^2$ term is of order $10^{-22} \mathrm{~cm}^2$ , the $e^2$ term of order $10^{-68} \mathrm{~cm}^2$ and the $m^2$ term of order $10^{-110} \mathrm{~cm}^2$ . Thus, the inequality is rather violently violated and the electron is not a black hole.

Solution 17.7. Since we have three relations for the components of the 4-momentum $(\mathbf{p} \cdot \mathbf{p} = -\mathbf{m}^2, \mathbf{p}_t = \mathbf{E}, \mathbf{p}_\phi = \mathbf{L})$ the orbital equations can be reduced to the form

$$
\left(\mathrm {d} \mathbf {r} / \mathrm {d} \lambda\right) ^ {2} = \mathrm {V} (\mathrm {E}, \mathrm {L}, \mathrm {r})
$$

where $\lambda$ is an affine parameter and $V$ is some effective potential. The condition for a circular orbit is that $\mathrm{dr} / \mathrm{d}\lambda$ remain zero, i.e.

$$
V (E, L, r) = 0 \tag {1}
$$

$$
\mathrm {V} ^ {\prime} (\mathrm {E}, \mathrm {L}, \mathrm {r}) = 0 \tag {2}
$$

where $\mathbf{V}' \equiv \partial \mathbf{V} / \partial \mathbf{r}$ . By the implicit function theorem, Equations (1) and (2) can be solved for

$$
\mathbf {E} = \mathbf {E} (\mathbf {r}), \quad \mathbf {L} = \mathbf {L} (\mathbf {r})
$$

provided

$$
\left| \begin{array}{l l} \frac {\partial \mathrm {V}}{\partial \mathrm {E}} & \frac {\partial \mathrm {V}}{\partial \mathrm {L}} \\ \frac {\partial \mathrm {V} ^ {\prime}}{\partial \mathrm {E}} & \frac {\partial \mathrm {V} ^ {\prime}}{\partial \mathrm {L}} \end{array} \right| \neq 0. \tag {3}
$$

(This condition is in fact satisfied.) Then $\mathrm{dE} / \mathrm{dr}$ and $\mathrm{dL} / \mathrm{dr}$ can be found by differentiating Equations (1) and (2):

$$
0 = \frac {\mathrm {d} V}{\mathrm {d r}} = \frac {\partial V}{\partial E} \frac {\mathrm {d} E}{\mathrm {d r}} + \frac {\partial V}{\partial L} \frac {\mathrm {d} L}{\mathrm {d r}} + V ^ {\prime} \tag {4}
$$

$$
0 = \frac {\mathrm {d} V ^ {\prime}}{\mathrm {d r}} = \frac {\partial V ^ {\prime}}{\partial \mathrm {E}} \frac {\mathrm {d E}}{\mathrm {d r}} + \frac {\partial V ^ {\prime}}{\partial \mathrm {L}} \frac {\partial \mathrm {L}}{\partial \mathrm {r}} + V ^ {\prime \prime}. \tag {5}
$$

Now consider an orbit at $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{r}_0$ perturbed to $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{r}_0 + \epsilon$ . The perturbed orbit equation is

$$
(\mathrm {d r} / \mathrm {d} \lambda) ^ {2} = \mathrm {V} (\mathbf {r} _ {0}) + \epsilon \mathrm {V} ^ {\prime} (\mathbf {r} _ {0}) + \frac {1}{2} \epsilon^ {2} \mathrm {V} ^ {\prime \prime} (\mathbf {r} _ {0}) + \dots
$$

where $\mathbf{V}(\mathbf{r}_0) = 0$ and $\mathbf{V}'(\mathbf{r}_0) = 0$ (condition for unperturbed circular orbit). The $\mathbf{V}''$ term then governs stability and $\mathbf{V}''$ must be negative if the orbit is stable. For a marginally stable orbit we have $\mathbf{V}'' = 0$ in addition to $\mathbf{V} = 0$ and $\mathbf{V}' = 0$ , so the only solution to Equations (4) and (5) is $\mathrm{dE} / \mathrm{d}\mathbf{r} = 0$ and $\mathrm{dL} / \mathrm{d}\mathbf{r} = 0$ . It is physically clear that these extrema are minima.

Solution 17.8.

(a) From $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{u} = -1$ and $\Omega \equiv \mathbf{u}^{\phi} / \mathbf{u}^{0}$ we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} - 1 = \mathrm {g} _ {0 0} \left(\mathrm {u} ^ {0}\right) ^ {2} + 2 \mathrm {g} _ {0 \phi} \mathrm {u} ^ {0} \mathrm {u} ^ {\phi} + \mathrm {g} _ {\phi \phi} \left(\mathrm {u} ^ {\phi}\right) ^ {2} \tag {1} \\ = \left(\mathrm {u} ^ {0}\right) ^ {2} \left(\mathrm {g} _ {0 0} + 2 \Omega \mathrm {g} _ {0 \phi} + \Omega^ {2} \mathrm {g} _ {\phi \phi}\right). \\ \end{array}
$$

and hence

$$
\mathbf {u} ^ {0} = (- \mathbf {g} _ {0 0} - 2 \Omega \mathbf {g} _ {0 \phi} - \Omega^ {2} \mathbf {g} _ {\phi \phi}) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}}
$$

where, in the $\theta = \pi /2$ plane,

$$
- \mathrm {g} _ {0 0} = 1 - 2 \mathrm {M} / \mathrm {r}, \quad - \mathrm {g} _ {0 \phi} = 2 \mathrm {M a} / \mathrm {r}, \quad - \mathrm {g} _ {\phi \phi} = - \left(\mathrm {r} ^ {3} + \mathrm {a} ^ {2} \mathrm {r} + 2 \mathrm {M a} ^ {2}\right) / \mathrm {r}.
$$

The other components are easily found in terms of $\mathbf{u}^0$ :

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {u} ^ {\phi} = \boldsymbol {\Omega} \mathbf {u} ^ {0} \\ u _ {0} = g _ {0 0} u ^ {0} + g _ {0 \phi} u ^ {\phi} = u ^ {0} \left(g _ {0 0} + \Omega g _ {0 \phi}\right) \\ u _ {\phi} = g _ {0 \phi} u ^ {0} + g _ {\phi \phi} u ^ {\phi} = u ^ {0} \left(g _ {0 \phi} + \Omega g _ {\phi \phi}\right). \\ \end{array}
$$

(b) From Equation (1) we have

$$
\mathrm {Y} \equiv \mathrm {g} _ {0 0} + 2 \Omega \mathrm {g} _ {0 \phi} + \Omega^ {2} \mathrm {g} _ {\phi \phi} <   0. \tag {2}
$$

The observer can be coordinate stationary $(\Omega = 0)$ only where $\mathbf{g}_{00} < 0$ , i.e. only for $\mathbf{r} > \mathbf{r}_0 = 2\mathbf{M}$ in the equatorial plane.

(c) The discriminant of $\mathbf{Y}$ defined in Equation (2) is

$$
(\mathbf {g} _ {0 \phi}) ^ {2} - \mathbf {g} _ {0 0} \mathbf {g} _ {\phi \phi} = \mathbf {r} ^ {2} - 2 \mathbf {M r} + \mathbf {a} ^ {2} = (\mathbf {r} - \mathbf {r} _ {+}) (\mathbf {r} - \mathbf {r} _ {-}),
$$

and is negative if $\mathbf{r}_{-} < \mathbf{r} < \mathbf{r}_{+}$ . If the discriminant of $\mathbf{Y}$ is negative, then $\mathbf{Y}$ has the same sign for any $\Omega$ , i.e. the same sign as $\mathbf{g}_{\phi \phi}$ which is positive. This violates Equation (2), which assumed only timelike motion and constant $\mathbf{r}$ . The observer therefore cannot remain at constant $\mathbf{r}$ .

Note that as $\mathbf{r} \to \mathbf{r}_+$ , then $\Omega$ goes to $-\mathbf{g}_{0\phi} / \mathbf{g}_{00} = \mathbf{a} / 2\mathbf{M}\mathbf{r}_+$ .

Solution 17.9. The conserved energy of a particle is the dot product of its 4-momentum with the time Killing vector $\pmb{\xi}_{(t)} = \partial/\partial t$ :

$$
\mathbf {E} = - \mathbf {p} \cdot \left(\frac {\partial}{\partial t}\right) = - \mathbf {p} _ {t}. \tag {1}
$$

The Kerr metric is of the form

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - \mathrm {e} ^ {2 \nu} \mathrm {d t} ^ {2} + \mathrm {e} ^ {2 \psi} (\mathrm {d} \phi - \omega \mathrm {d t}) ^ {2} + \mathrm {e} ^ {2 \mu_ {1}} \mathrm {d r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {e} ^ {2 \mu_ {2}} \mathrm {d} \theta^ {2}. \tag {2}
$$

The contravariant components of $\mathbf{g}^{\alpha \beta}$ are

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {g} ^ {\mathrm {t t}} = - \mathbf {e} ^ {- 2 \nu} \quad \mathbf {g} ^ {\mathrm {t} \phi} = - \omega \mathbf {e} ^ {- 2 \nu} \quad \mathbf {g} ^ {\phi \phi} = \mathbf {e} ^ {- 2 \psi} - \omega^ {2} \mathbf {e} ^ {- 2 \nu} \tag {3} \\ \mathbf {g} ^ {\mathrm {r r}} = \mathbf {e} ^ {- 2 \mu_ {1}} \qquad \mathbf {g} ^ {\theta \theta} = \mathbf {e} ^ {- 2 \mu_ {2}}. \\ \end{array}
$$

If $\mu$ is the mass of the particle, then

$$
\begin{array}{l} - \mu^ {2} = \mathbf {p} \cdot \mathbf {p} = - \mathrm {e} ^ {- 2 \nu} \mathrm {p} _ {\mathrm {t}} ^ {2} - \mathrm {e} ^ {- 2 \nu} 2 \omega \mathrm {p} _ {\mathrm {t}} \mathrm {p} _ {\phi} \tag {4} \\ + \left(\mathrm {e} ^ {- 2 \psi} - \mathrm {e} ^ {- 2 \nu} \omega^ {2}\right) \mathrm {p} _ {\phi} ^ {2} + \mathrm {e} ^ {- 2 \mu_ {1}} \mathrm {p} _ {\mathrm {r}} ^ {2} + \mathrm {e} ^ {- 2 \mu_ {2}} \mathrm {p} _ {\theta} ^ {2}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Solving the quadratic equation (4) for $\mathbf{E} = \mathbf{p}_{\mathrm{t}}$ we get

$$
E = \omega p _ {\phi} + \left[ e ^ {2 \nu - 2 \psi} p _ {\phi} ^ {2} + e ^ {2 \nu} \left(e ^ {- 2 \mu_ {1}} p _ {r} ^ {2} + e ^ {- 2 \mu_ {2}} p _ {\theta} ^ {2} + \mu^ {2}\right) \right] ^ {\frac {1}{2}}. \tag {5}
$$

The sign of the square root in Equation (5) is positive so that $\mathbf{E} = +\mu$ for a particle at rest at infinity. If $\mathbf{E}$ is to be negative, we must have $\mathsf{p}_{\phi}$ negative and

$$
\left[ \mathrm {e} ^ {2 \nu - 2 \psi} \mathrm {p} _ {\phi} ^ {2} + \mathrm {e} ^ {2 \nu} \left(\mathrm {e} ^ {- 2 \mu_ {1}} \mathrm {p} _ {\mathrm {r}} ^ {2} + \mathrm {e} ^ {- 2 \mu_ {2}} \mathrm {p} _ {\theta} ^ {2} + \mu^ {2}\right) \right] ^ {\frac {1}{2}} <   - \omega \mathrm {p} _ {\phi}. \tag {6}
$$

The boundary of the region of negative $\mathbf{E}$ can be found by letting $\mathfrak{p}_{\mathbf{r}} = \mathfrak{p}_{\theta} = 0$ , and $\mu \to 0$ (i.e. highly relativistic particle). This gives $e^{2\nu - 2\psi} < \omega^2$ which is equivalent to $\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{tt}} > 0$ . That is, the orbit must be inside the ergosphere. (This result also follows from Problem 10.15.)

When a rocket ship fires a bullet we have conservation of 4-momentum

$$
\mathbf {p} _ {\text {b e f o r e}} = \mathbf {p} _ {\text {a f t e r}} + \mathbf {p} _ {\text {b u l l e t}}. \tag {7}
$$

Dotting this into the time Killing vector gives

$$
E _ {\text {b e f o r e}} = E _ {\text {a f t e r}} + E _ {\text {b u l l e t}}. \tag {8}
$$

Since the rocket ship can fall into the ergosphere from infinity, we have $\mathbf{E}_{\text{before}} > \mu$ . In the ergosphere it can fire a bullet sufficiently fast with negative $p_{\phi}$ so that $\mathbf{E}_{\text{bullet}} < 0$ . Then, by Equation (7), $\mathbf{E}_{\text{after}} > \mathbf{E}_{\text{before}}$ and the rocket coasts off to infinity with increased total energy. Note that since both the rocket's trajectory and the bullet's negative energy trajectory are timelike, the bullet can be fired from the rocket with a locally measured velocity less than $c$ .

Solution 17.10. In Problem 17.8 we saw that $\Omega \to \mathfrak{a} / 2\mathbf{M}\mathbf{r}_{+}$ when $\theta = \pi /2$ To prove this is so for any $\theta$ we write, in the notation of Problem 17.9:

$$
\Omega = \frac {\mathrm {p} ^ {\phi}}{\mathrm {p} ^ {\mathrm {t}}} = \frac {\mathrm {g} ^ {\phi \phi} \mathrm {p} _ {\phi} + \mathrm {g} ^ {\phi \mathrm {t}} \mathrm {p} _ {\mathrm {t}}}{\mathrm {g} ^ {\mathrm {t t}} \mathrm {p} _ {\mathrm {t}} + \mathrm {g} ^ {\phi \mathrm {t}} \mathrm {p} _ {\phi}} = \omega - \mathrm {e} ^ {2 \nu} \frac {\mathrm {e} ^ {- 2 \psi} \mathrm {p} _ {\phi}}{\mathrm {p} _ {\mathrm {t}} + \omega \mathrm {p} _ {\phi}}.
$$

At $\Delta = 0$ (that is, on the horizon) the metric function $e^{-2\psi}$ is finite but $e^{2\nu}$ vanishes, hence $\Omega = \omega$ and at $\Delta = 0$

$$
\omega = \frac {- g _ {\phi t}}{g _ {\phi \phi}} = \frac {a}{2 M r _ {+}}.
$$

Solution 17.11. Start with the $\mathbf{r}$ and $\theta$ equations for orbital motion in the Kerr geometry (see e.g. MTW, Equation 33.32):

$$
\left(\mathrm {r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {a} ^ {2} \cos \theta\right) \mathrm {d r} / \mathrm {d} \tau = \pm \mathrm {V} _ {\mathrm {r}} ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \tag {1}
$$

$$
\left(\mathrm {r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {a} ^ {2} \cos \theta\right) \mathrm {d} \theta / \mathrm {d} \tau = \pm \mathrm {V} _ {\theta} ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \tag {2}
$$

where

$$
V _ {r} = \left[ E \left(r ^ {2} + a ^ {2}\right) - L a \right] ^ {2} - \Delta \left[ r ^ {2} + (L - a E) ^ {2} + Q \right] \tag {3}
$$

$$
V _ {\theta} = Q - \cos^ {2} \theta [ a ^ {2} (1 - E ^ {2}) + L ^ {2} / \sin^ {2} \theta ] \tag {4}
$$

$$
\Delta \equiv r ^ {2} - 2 M r + a ^ {2}
$$

and where E, L, Q are constants of the motion. To have $\theta$ motion across $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \pi$ we must have $V_{\theta}$ positive at both $\theta = 0$ and $\theta = \pi$ . By Equation (4) this implies $L = 0$ and $Q > a^2(1 - E^2)$ . Equation (3) then is

$$
V _ {r} = E ^ {2} \left(r ^ {2} + a ^ {2}\right) ^ {2} - \Delta \left[ r ^ {2} + a ^ {2} + I \right] \tag {5}
$$

where

$$
I \equiv Q - a ^ {2} (1 - E ^ {2}) > 0.
$$

The condition that the orbit be at constant coordinate $\mathbf{r}$ is $\mathrm{V_r} = 0$ and $\mathrm{dV_r / dr} = 0$ (condition for a turning point and a perpetual turning point). The condition on the derivative gives

$$
0 = 2 \mathrm {E} ^ {2} \mathrm {r} \left(\mathrm {r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {a} ^ {2}\right) - (\mathrm {r} - \mathrm {M}) \mathrm {E} ^ {2} \left(\mathrm {r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {a} ^ {2}\right) ^ {2} / \Delta - \mathrm {r} \Delta \tag {6}
$$

where $\mathbf{V}_{\mathbf{r}} = 0$ and Equation (5) have been used to eliminate the constant I. This result can be rewritten

$$
\mathbf {E} ^ {2} = \mathrm {r} \Delta^ {2} \left[ \left(\mathrm {r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {a} ^ {2}\right) \left(\mathrm {r} ^ {3} - 3 \mathrm {M r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {a} ^ {2} \mathrm {r} + \mathrm {a} ^ {2} \mathrm {M}\right) \right] ^ {- 1}. \tag {7}
$$

For very large $r$ we have $E \approx \left(1 - \frac{M}{2r} + \dots\right)$ which is the Newtonian binding energy of a circular orbit. As we decrease $r$ , Equation (7) tells how $E$ changes. Clearly, since the numerator is positive, the only way Equation (7) can fail to provide a solution is for the denominator to go to zero (its going to $\infty$ is obviously impossible outside the horizon). Thus there exist circular polar orbits down to a radius $r$ given by

$$
\mathrm {r} ^ {3} - 3 \mathrm {M r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {a} ^ {2} \mathrm {r} + \mathrm {a} ^ {2} \mathrm {M} = 0. \tag {8}
$$

This is easily solved with the cubic formula to give the minimum radius of a polar orbit

$$
\mathbf {r} = \mathbb {M} \left[ \right. 1 + \frac {2 (3 - \tilde {\mathsf {a}} ^ {2}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{3 ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \cos \left\{ \right.\frac {1}{3} \left. \cos^ {- 1} \left(\frac {3 ^ {\frac {3}{2}} (1 - \tilde {\mathsf {a}} ^ {2})}{(3 - \tilde {\mathsf {a}} ^ {2}) ^ {\frac {3}{2}}}\right)\right\}\left. \right]
$$

where $\tilde{\mathbf{a}}\equiv \mathbf{a} / \mathbf{M}$ . Note that $\mathbf{E}^2\rightarrow \infty$ in the limiting case, so the limiting case is a photon orbit. The limiting radius decreases from $\mathbf{r} = 3\mathbf{M}$ for $\mathbf{a} = 0$ to $\mathbf{r} = (1 + 2^{\frac{1}{2}})\mathbf{M}$ for $\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{M}$ .

Solution 17.12. Let $\pmb{\xi}$ be the timelike Killing vector present because the black hole is static. Then Killing's equation tells us

$$
\xi_ {\alpha ; \beta} = - \xi_ {\beta ; \alpha} \tag {1}
$$

and the requirement for a static geometry (see Problem 10.8) is

$$
\xi_ {[ \alpha ; \beta} \xi_ {\gamma ]} = 0. \tag {2}
$$

A static observer has 4-velocity parallel to $\pmb{\xi}$ , i.e.

$$
\mathbf {u} = \boldsymbol {\xi} / \mathbf {v} ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \tag {3}
$$

$$
v = - \xi^ {a} \xi_ {a}. \tag {4}
$$

For a photon of momentum $\mathbf{p}$ , the infinite red-shift surface occurs when

$$
0 = (\mathbf {p} \cdot \mathbf {u}) _ {\infty} / (\mathbf {p} \cdot \mathbf {u}) _ {\text {e m i t t e r}} = v _ {\text {e m i t t e r}} ^ {\frac {1}{2}}
$$

where we have used the fact that $\mathbf{p} \cdot \boldsymbol{\xi}$ is constant along the geodesic trajectory of the photon and $\mathbf{v}$ is normalized to unity at infinity. Thus, the ergosurface is the surface at which $\boldsymbol{\xi}$ becomes null. We now prove that the ergosurface, $\mathbf{v} = 0$ , is a null hypersurface, i.e. that its normal vector is null: We use Equation (1) to write Equation (2) in the form

$$
\xi_ {\alpha ; \beta} \xi_ {\gamma} + \xi_ {\gamma ; \alpha} \xi_ {\beta} - \xi_ {\gamma ; \beta} \xi_ {\alpha} = 0, \tag {5}
$$

then we dot $\xi^{\gamma}$ into Equation (5):

$$
v \xi_ {\alpha ; \beta} + v, [ \alpha \xi_ {\beta} ] = 0. \tag {6}
$$

Equation (6) shows that $\mathbf{v}_{,a}$ is parallel to $\xi_{a}$ wherever $\mathbf{v} = 0$ . But $\mathbf{v}_{,a}$ is the normal to $\mathbf{v} = 0$ , i.e. the normal is a null vector.

The proof has to be modified in the degenerate case when $\mathbf{v}_{,a} = 0$ , since it is possible that $\mathbf{v} = 0$ in a finite region, not just on a hypersurface. However, a detailed investigation [B. Carter, J. Math. Phys. 10, 70, (1969)] shows that this is not so.

The theorem proven here corresponds to a Schwarzschild black hole, where $\mathbf{r} = 2\mathbf{M}$ is both the horizon and infinite red-shift surface for static observers (in fact, for all observers). A Kerr black hole is an example of the case where the ergosurface does not coincide with the horizon.

Solution 17.13. At the outer horizon $(\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{r}_{+})$ the metric with $\mathrm{dt} = \mathrm{dr} = 0$ becomes

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = \left(\mathrm {r} _ {+} ^ {2} + \mathrm {a} ^ {2} \cos^ {2} \theta\right) \mathrm {d} \theta^ {2} + \frac {\left(\mathrm {r} _ {+} ^ {2} + \mathrm {a} ^ {2}\right) ^ {2} \sin^ {2} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2}}{\left(\mathrm {r} _ {+} ^ {2} + \mathrm {a} ^ {2} \cos^ {2} \theta\right)} .
$$

The area of the horizon is then

$$
A = \iint g ^ {\frac {1}{2}} d \theta d \phi = \iint \left(r _ {+} ^ {2} + a ^ {2}\right) \sin \theta d \theta d \phi = 4 \pi \left(r _ {+} ^ {2} + a ^ {2}\right).
$$

Since $\mathbf{r}_{+}$ is the larger solution of $\Delta = 0$ we have $\mathbf{r}_{+} = \mathbf{M} + (\mathbf{M}^{2} + \mathbf{Q}^{2} - \mathbf{a}^{2})^{\frac{1}{2}}$ from which the result follows.

Solution 17.14. The two initial black holes have the same area, so the total initial surface area $(Q = 0$ in Problem 17.13) is

$$
\mathbf {A} _ {\mathbf {i}} = 1 6 \pi \mathbf {M} _ {\mathbf {1}} \left[ \mathbf {M} _ {\mathbf {1}} + \left(\mathbf {M} _ {\mathbf {1}} ^ {2} - a ^ {2}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \right].
$$

The final black hole is a Schwarzschild black hole, so

$$
\mathbf {A _ {f}} = 4 \pi (2 \mathbf {M} _ {2}) ^ {2} = 1 6 \pi \mathbf {M} _ {2} ^ {2}.
$$

The inequality $\mathbf{A}_{\mathbf{f}} \geq \mathbf{A}_{\mathbf{i}}$ becomes

$$
\mathbf {M} _ {2} ^ {2} \geq \mathbf {M} _ {1} ^ {2} + \mathbf {M} _ {1} (\mathbf {M} _ {1} ^ {2} - \mathbf {a} ^ {2}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} .
$$

If $a = M_1$ , this means $M_2 \geq M_1$ . The total initial mass was $2M_1$ , so the final mass must be at least half of this. According to Hawking's theorem then, up to $50\%$ of the original mass can be radiated away!

This is the most efficient of the collisions without charge. Suppose two black holes of masses $\mathbf{M}_1$ and $\mathbf{M}_2$ , and angular momentum parameters $\mathbf{a}_1$ and $\mathbf{a}_2$ coalesce to form a black hole of mass $\mathbf{M}_3$ , and parameter $\mathbf{a}_3$ ; then $\mathbf{A}_{\mathrm{f}} \geq \mathbf{A}_{\mathrm{i}}$ implies that

$$
\mathsf {M} _ {1} \left[ \mathsf {M} _ {1} + (\mathsf {M} _ {1} ^ {2} - \mathsf {a} _ {1} ^ {2}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \right] + \mathsf {M} _ {2} \left[ \mathsf {M} _ {2} + (\mathsf {M} _ {2} ^ {2} - \mathsf {a} _ {2} ^ {2}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \right] \leq \mathsf {M} _ {3} \left[ \mathsf {M} _ {3} + (\mathsf {M} _ {3} ^ {2} - \mathsf {a} _ {3} ^ {2}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \right].
$$

One gets the most energy out when the equality obtains. Moreover, for given $\mathbb{M}_1$ , $\mathbb{M}_2$ , $\mathbf{a}_1$ , $\mathbf{a}_2$ the smallest $\mathbb{M}_3$ (hence the largest radiation of energy) will occur for $\mathbf{a}_3 = 0$ . Similarly, for given $\mathbb{M}_3$ (with $\mathbf{a}_3 = 0$ ), the largest $\mathbb{M}_1 + \mathbb{M}_2$ (hence the largest radiation of energy) will occur for $|\mathbf{a}_1| = |\mathbf{a}_2| = \mathbb{M}_1 = \mathbb{M}_2$ . Since $\mathbf{a}_3 = 0$ , conservation of angular momentum implies that $\mathbf{a}_1 = -\mathbf{a}_2$ . This is just the situation we worked in detail above. The conclusion, therefore, is that when two Kerr holes collide and coalesce, no more than $50\%$ of their original mass can be radiated away.

Solution 17.15. The area A and “reduced area” $\tilde{\mathbf{A}}$ of a Kerr black hole are

$$
A \equiv 8 \pi \tilde {A} = 8 \pi \left[ M ^ {2} + \left(M ^ {4} - J ^ {2}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \right] \tag {1}
$$

or equivalently,

$$
\tilde {\mathbf {A}} ^ {2} - 2 \tilde {\mathbf {A}} \mathbf {M} ^ {2} + \mathbf {J} ^ {2} = 0. \tag {2}
$$

Taking the first variation, we have

$$
\left(\tilde {\mathbf {A}} - \mathbf {M} ^ {2}\right) \delta \tilde {\mathbf {A}} = 2 \tilde {\mathbf {A}} \mathbf {M} \delta \mathbf {M} - \mathbf {J} \delta \mathbf {J}. \tag {3}
$$

The left-hand side is nonnegative by the second law. Let a particular wave mode have the $t$ and $\phi$ dependence $\exp(-\mathrm{i}\omega t + \mathrm{i}m\phi)$ . All scalar, electromagnetic and gravitational waves satisfy

$$
\delta \mathbf {M} = \frac {\omega}{\mathrm {m}} \delta \mathbf {J} \tag {4}
$$

so we have

$$
\left(2 \mathrm {A M} - \mathrm {J} \frac {\mathrm {m}}{\omega}\right) \delta \mathrm {M} \geq 0. \tag {5}
$$

For an amplified wave conservation of energy requires that the mass of the hole decrease, so we want $\delta \mathbf{M} < 0$ ; thus the term in parentheses in Equation (5) must be negative. This condition can be rewritten as

$$
\frac {2 \mathrm {M r} _ {+}}{\mathrm {a}} - \frac {\mathrm {m}}{\bar {\omega}} <   0 \tag {6}
$$

where $\mathbf{r}_{+}$ is the coordinate radius of the event horizon, or (if we take into account that $\mathfrak{m}$ and $\omega$ can have either sign) the condition is

$$
0 \leqslant \omega \leqslant \frac {\mathrm {m a}}{2 \mathrm {M r} _ {+}} \tag {7}
$$

where $\lesseqqgtr$ means "lies between." Note that $a / 2\mathrm{Mr}_{+}$ is just $\Omega$ the "angular velocity of the black hole" (see Problem 17.10).

Solution 17.16.

(a) Using the results of Problem 7.7,

$$
\square \Phi = (- \mathbf {g}) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \left[ (- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {g} ^ {\alpha \beta} \Phi_ {, \alpha} \right], _ {\beta},
$$

and $(-g)^{\frac{1}{2}} = (r^{2} + a^{2}\cos^{2}\theta)\sin \theta$ , we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = \left[ - \frac {\left(\mathrm {r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {a} ^ {2}\right) ^ {2}}{\Delta} + \mathrm {a} ^ {2} \sin^ {2} \theta \right] \frac {\partial^ {2} \Phi}{\partial t ^ {2}} - \frac {\operatorname {M a r}}{\Delta} \frac {\partial^ {2} \Phi}{\partial t \partial \phi} + \left(\frac {1}{\sin^ {2} \theta} - \frac {\mathrm {a} ^ {2}}{\Delta}\right) \frac {\partial^ {2} \Phi}{\partial \phi^ {2}} \tag {1} \\ + \frac {\partial}{\partial \mathbf {r}} \left(\Delta \frac {\partial}{\partial \mathbf {r}} \Phi\right) + \frac {1}{\sin \theta} \frac {\partial}{\partial \theta} \left(\sin \theta \frac {\partial \Phi}{\partial \theta}\right). \\ \end{array}
$$

(b) In Equation (1) we take the $t$ and $\phi$ dependence to be

$$
\Phi (t, r, \theta , \phi) = e ^ {- i \omega t} e ^ {i m \phi} R (r) S (\theta)
$$

and we divide through by $\Phi$ . This gives us

$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {1}{R} \frac {d}{d r} \left(\Delta \frac {d R}{d r}\right) + \omega^ {2} \frac {\left(r ^ {2} + a ^ {2}\right) ^ {2}}{\Delta} - \frac {4 M a r \omega m}{\Delta} + \frac {a ^ {2} m ^ {2}}{\Delta} \tag {2} \\ = \frac {- 1}{S \sin \theta} \frac {d}{d \theta} \left(\sin \theta \frac {d S}{d \theta}\right) + a ^ {2} \omega^ {2} \sin^ {2} \theta + \frac {m ^ {2}}{\sin^ {2} \theta}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Since the left-hand side of Equation (2) is a function of $\mathbf{r}$ alone and the right-hand side must be a function of $\theta$ alone; each side must be a constant $A$ , thus

$$
\frac {1}{\sin \theta} \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} \theta} \left(\sin \theta \frac {\mathrm {d} S}{\mathrm {d} \theta}\right) - \left(a ^ {2} \omega^ {2} \sin^ {2} \theta + \frac {\mathrm {m} ^ {2}}{\sin^ {2} \theta} - A\right) S = 0 \tag {3}
$$

and

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} \mathbf {r}} \left(\Delta \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {R}}{\mathrm {d} \mathbf {r}}\right) + \left[ \frac {\omega^ {2} \left(\mathrm {r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {a} ^ {2}\right) ^ {2} - 4 \operatorname {M a r} \omega \mathrm {m} + \mathrm {a} ^ {2} \mathrm {m} ^ {2}}{\Delta} - \mathrm {A} \right] \mathbf {R} = 0. \tag {4}
$$

Since S must be regular at $\theta = 0$ and $\pi$ , Equation (3) is an eigenvalue equation for A. The function S is in fact a "spheroidal wave function"; in the simplest case when $a\omega = 0$ , we have $S = P_{\ell_m}(\cos \theta)$ and $A = \ell (\ell +1)$

(c) It is convenient (but not essential) to introduce a “tortoise” coordinate $\mathbf{r}^*$ which satisfies $\mathrm{dr}^* / \mathrm{dr} = (\mathbf{r}^2 + \mathbf{a}^2) / \Delta$ . Note that the interval $(\mathbf{r}_+, \infty)$ in the r-coordinate is stretched to $(- \infty, \infty)$ in $\mathbf{r}^*$ . Equation (4) now becomes

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathrm {R}}{\mathrm {d r} ^ {* 2}} + \frac {2 \mathrm {r} \Delta}{\left(\mathrm {r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {a} ^ {2}\right) ^ {2}} \frac {\mathrm {d R}}{\mathrm {d r} ^ {*}} + \left[ \omega^ {2} + \frac {\mathrm {a} ^ {2} \mathrm {m} ^ {2} - 4 \operatorname {M a r m} \omega - \Delta \mathrm {A}}{\left(\mathrm {r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {a} ^ {2}\right) ^ {2}} \right] \mathrm {R} = 0. \tag {5}
$$

As $\mathbf{r} \to \infty$ , we get

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathrm {R}}{\mathrm {d r} ^ {* 2}} + \frac {2}{\mathrm {r}} \frac {\mathrm {d R}}{\mathrm {d r} ^ {*}} + \omega^ {2} \mathrm {R} \approx 0
$$

which has solutions

$$
R \sim r ^ {- 1} e ^ {\pm i \omega r ^ {*}}
$$

corresponding to ingoing and outgoing waves.

(d) As $\Delta \to 0$ , Equation (5) gives

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathrm {R}}{\mathrm {d r} ^ {* 2}} + \left[ \omega^ {2} - \frac {2 \mathrm {a m} \omega}{2 \mathrm {M r} _ {+}} + \frac {\mathrm {a} ^ {2} \mathrm {m} ^ {2}}{(2 \mathrm {M r} _ {+}) ^ {2}} \right] \mathrm {R} = 0
$$

and hence

$$
\mathrm {R} \sim \mathrm {e} ^ {\pm \mathrm {i} (\omega - \mathrm {m} \omega_ {+}) \mathrm {r} ^ {*}}
$$

where

$$
\omega_ {+} \equiv a / 2 M r _ {+}.
$$

(e) Since all physical observers at the horizon are related by Lorentz transformations, they will all agree whether a particular solution represents ingoing or outgoing waves. Thus we can calculate with any convenient observer. We choose an observer at constant $\mathbf{r}$ just outside the horizon. Since he is inside the ergosphere, he is dragged around in the positive $\phi$ direction with some $\Omega = \frac{\mathrm{d}\phi}{\mathrm{d}t} > 0$ where, from Problem 17.10, $\Omega = \omega_{+}$ . This observer sees the local (t,r) dependence of the solution

$$
\Phi = e ^ {- i \omega t} e ^ {i m \phi} e ^ {\pm i (\omega - m \omega_ {+}) r ^ {*}} S (\theta) \tag {6}
$$

as

$$
\Phi = e ^ {- i (\omega - m \omega_ {+}) t} e ^ {\pm i (\omega - m \omega_ {+}) r ^ {*}} e ^ {i m \tilde {\phi}} S (\theta).
$$

where we have put $\tilde{\phi} = \phi -\omega_{+}\mathbf{t}$ . Thus $\mathbf{e}^{-\mathbf{i}(\omega -\mathbf{m}\omega_{+})\mathbf{r}^{*}}$ corresponds to in-going waves.

(f) The stress-energy tensor for a scalar field is

$$
4 \pi \mathrm {T} _ {\alpha \beta} = \Phi_ {, (\alpha} \Phi_ {, \beta)} ^ {*} - \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \beta} | \Phi_ {, \gamma} \Phi_ {, \gamma} |.
$$

(The complex conjugation is necessary because the representation of $\Phi$ is complex.) The energy flux vector is $\mathbf{J}_{\beta} = -\mathbf{T}_{\alpha}\beta \xi^{\alpha}$ , where $\pmb{\xi}$ is the time Killing vector $\partial/\partial t$ (see Problem 10.11). The energy flux into the horizon is found by integrating the radial component of $\mathbf{J}$ over the

2-surface $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{r}_{+}$

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {E}}{\mathrm {d} t} = \int \mathrm {T} _ {\mathrm {t}} ^ {\mathrm {r}} | \mathbf {g} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi .
$$

Since

$$
\begin{array}{l} 4 \pi \mathrm {T} _ {\mathrm {t}} ^ {\mathrm {r}} = \operatorname {R e} \left(\Phi_ {, \mathrm {t}} \Phi^ {*}, \mathrm {r}\right) = \operatorname {R e} \left(\Phi_ {, \mathrm {t}} \Phi^ {*} \frac {\mathrm {r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {a} ^ {2}}{\sum}\right) \\ = \omega (\omega - \mathrm {m} \omega_ {+}) S ^ {2} (\theta) \frac {2 \mathrm {M r} _ {+}}{\sum}, \\ \end{array}
$$

we have

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathrm {E}}{\mathrm {d} t} = \omega (\omega - \mathrm {m} \omega_ {+}) \frac {2 \mathrm {M r} _ {+}}{4 \pi} \int \mathrm {S} ^ {2} (\theta) \sin \theta \mathrm {d} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi
$$

and $\mathrm{dE} / \mathrm{dt}$ is negative, i.e. energy flows out of the horizon if $\omega -\mathfrak{m}\omega_{+} < 0$ i.e. if $0 <   \omega /\mathfrak{m} <   \omega_{+}$ in agreement with Problem 17.15.

Solution 17.17. Suppose charge $\delta Q$ and energy $\delta E$ are dropped into the hole. We then have

$$
\delta \left(\mathrm {Q} ^ {2} - \mathrm {M} ^ {2}\right) = 2 \mathrm {Q} \delta \mathrm {Q} - 2 \mathrm {M} \delta \mathrm {E}. \tag {1}
$$

Not all values of $\delta Q$ and $\delta E$ are allowed, however. The radial "effective potential" equation (see e.g. MTW, Equation 33.32) for a charged particle falling in the Reissner-Nordström geometry is

$$
\mathrm {r} ^ {2} \frac {\mathrm {d r}}{\mathrm {d} \tau} = - \left\{\left(\mathrm {r} ^ {2} \delta \mathrm {E} - Q \delta \mathrm {Q r}\right) ^ {2} - \Delta \left(\mu_ {0} ^ {2} \mathrm {r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {L} _ {z} ^ {2} + \mathcal {Z}\right) \right\} ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \tag {2}
$$

where $\mathbf{E}, \mathbf{L}_z$ and $\mathcal{Q}$ are conserved quantities, and $\mu_0$ is the particle rest mass. A particle which crosses the event horizon into the black hole has $\mathrm{d}\mathbf{r} / \mathrm{d}\tau \leq 0$ at the event horizon $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{r}_{+}$ . Since $\Delta = 0$ at $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{r}_{+}$ , (2) becomes

$$
r ^ {2} \frac {d r}{d r} = - \left(r _ {+} ^ {2} \delta E - Q \delta Q r _ {+}\right) \leq 0 \tag {3}
$$

and hence $\delta \mathrm{E} > \mathrm{Q}\delta \mathrm{Q} / \mathrm{r}_{+}$ . Combining this with Equation (1) we see that

$$
\delta \left(Q ^ {2} - M ^ {2}\right) \leq 2 \delta E \left(\mathrm {r} _ {+} - M\right) = 2 \delta E \left(M ^ {2} - Q ^ {2}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}. \tag {4}
$$

As $\mathbf{Q}^2 \to \mathbf{M}^2$ the right side goes to zero as a square root, so no sequence of adding $\delta \mathbf{E}$ 's can make the integrated left side positive.

Solution 17.18.

$$
\begin{array}{l} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {t}} \cdot \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {t}} = \left| \mathbf {g} _ {t t} - \omega^ {2} \mathbf {g} _ {\phi \phi} \right| ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \widetilde {\mathrm {d} t} \cdot \left| \mathbf {g} _ {t t} - \omega^ {2} \mathbf {g} _ {\phi \phi} \right| ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \widetilde {\mathrm {d} t} \tag {a} \\ = - \left(\mathbf {g} _ {t t} - \omega^ {2} \mathbf {g} _ {\phi \phi}\right) \mathbf {g} ^ {t t}. \\ \end{array}
$$

[The minus sign is chosen because $\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{tt}} - \omega^2\mathbf{g}_{\phi \phi} < 0$ .] The Kerr metric has only the $\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{t}\phi}$ term off the diagonal. Inverting $\mathbf{g}_{\alpha \beta}$ we find $\mathbf{g}^{\mathrm{tt}} = \mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{tt}} - \omega^2\mathbf{g}_{\phi \phi}$ and $\tilde{\boldsymbol{\omega}}^{\hat{\mathbf{t}}}\cdot \tilde{\boldsymbol{\omega}}^{\hat{\mathbf{t}}} = -1$ . The calculation of other inner products to verify $\tilde{\boldsymbol{\omega}}^{\hat{\boldsymbol{\alpha}}}\cdot \tilde{\boldsymbol{\omega}}^{\hat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}} = \eta^{\boldsymbol{\alpha}\boldsymbol{\beta}}$ is similar.

(b) The dual basis $\mathbf{e}_{\hat{\alpha}}$ is defined by $<  \tilde{\pmb{\omega}}^{\hat{\alpha}},\mathbf{e}_{\hat{\beta}} > = \delta_{\beta}^{a}$ . If $\tilde{\pmb{\omega}}^{\hat{\alpha}} = \mathbf{A}_{\beta}^{\hat{\alpha}}\tilde{\pmb{\omega}}^{\beta}$ and $\mathbf{e}_{\hat{\beta}} = \mathbf{B}_{\hat{\beta}}^{\gamma}\mathbf{e}_{\gamma}$ , then the matrix $\mathbf{B}_{\hat{\beta}}^{\gamma}$ must be the transposed inverse of $\mathbf{A}_{\beta}^{\alpha}$ . To find $\mathbf{B}$ from A we can separate the t-phi piece from the (diagonal) r-0 piece:

$$
\left[ \begin{array}{c} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\mathfrak {t}}} \\ \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {\phi}} \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{c c} | \mathrm {g} _ {\mathrm {t t}} - \omega^ {2} \mathrm {g} _ {\phi \phi} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} & 0 \\ - \omega (\mathrm {g} _ {\phi \phi}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} & (\mathrm {g} _ {\phi \phi}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{c} \widetilde {\mathrm {d t}} \\ \widetilde {\mathrm {d f}} \end{array} \right]
$$

implies that

$$
\left[ \begin{array}{c} \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} \\ \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\boldsymbol {\phi}}} \end{array} \right] = \left[ \begin{array}{c c} | \mathbf {g} _ {\mathbf {t t}} - \omega^ {2} \mathbf {g} _ {\boldsymbol {\phi} \boldsymbol {\phi}} | ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} & \omega | \mathbf {g} _ {\mathbf {t t}} - \omega^ {2} \mathbf {g} _ {\boldsymbol {\phi} \boldsymbol {\phi}} | ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \\ 0 & (\mathbf {g} _ {\boldsymbol {\phi} \boldsymbol {\phi}}) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \end{array} \right] \left[ \begin{array}{c} \mathbf {e} _ {\mathbf {t}} \\ \mathbf {e} _ {\boldsymbol {\phi}} \end{array} \right].
$$

Since the $r-\theta$ piece is diagonal, it is trivially inverted:

$$
\mathbf {e} _ {\mathrm {\hat {r}}} = (\Delta / \Sigma) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {e} _ {\mathrm {\hat {r}}} \qquad \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\theta}} = \Sigma^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {e} _ {\theta}.
$$

(c) The 4-velocity has zero rotation if $\omega_{\alpha}\beta = 0$ (see Problem 5.18) or equivalently if $\mathbf{u}$ is hypersurface orthogonal:

$$
\mathrm {u} _ {[ \alpha ; \beta} \mathrm {u} _ {\gamma ]} = 0
$$

(see Problem 7.23). The quickest way to check this condition is to use forms and the equivalence of $\mathfrak{u}[\hat{\alpha};\hat{\beta}]$ and $\mathbf{d}\tilde{\boldsymbol{\omega}}^{\mathrm{t}}$ . The condition $\mathfrak{u}[\hat{\alpha};\hat{\beta}\mathbf{u}_{\hat{\gamma}}] = 0$ is then equivalent to $\mathbf{d}\tilde{\boldsymbol{\omega}}^{\mathrm{t}}\wedge \tilde{\boldsymbol{\omega}}^{\mathrm{t}} = 0$ . But $\tilde{\boldsymbol{\omega}}^{\mathrm{t}} = \boldsymbol{a}\mathrm{d}t$ where $a = |\mathbf{g}_{\mathrm{tt}} - \omega^{2}\mathbf{g}_{\phi \phi}|^{\frac{1}{2}} = a(\mathbf{r},\theta)$ and thus

$$
\mathrm {d} \tilde {\omega} ^ {\mathrm {t}} = a, _ {\mathrm {r}} \widetilde {\mathrm {d r}} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d t}} + a, _ {\theta} \widetilde {\mathrm {d} \theta} \wedge \widetilde {\mathrm {d t}}
$$

so $\mathbf{d}\widetilde{\boldsymbol{\omega}}^{\mathrm{t}}\wedge \widetilde{\boldsymbol{\omega}}^{\mathrm{t}} = 0$ since $\widetilde{\mathrm{dt}}\wedge \widetilde{\mathrm{dt}} = 0$

(d) The 4-velocity of the ZAMO is

$$
\mathbf {u} = \mathbf {e} _ {\hat {\mathbf {t}}} = \left| \mathbf {g} _ {\mathbf {t t}} - \omega^ {2} \mathbf {g} _ {\phi \phi} \right| ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \left(\mathbf {e} _ {\mathbf {t}} + \omega \mathbf {e} _ {\phi}\right).
$$

The vectors $\mathbf{e}_t$ and $\mathbf{e}_{\phi}$ are Killing vectors. The vector field $\mathbf{e}_t + \omega \mathbf{e}_{\phi}$ is not a Killing vector because $\omega$ is not constant. However any particular ZAMO is moving along the Killing vector $\pmb{\xi} = \mathbf{e}_t + \omega_0\mathbf{e}_{\phi}$ ( $\omega_0$ a fixed constant, which is chosen to equal $\omega$ at the radius of the particular ZAMO under consideration). Thus the results of Problem 10.14 apply and we get

$$
\mathbf {a} = \frac {1}{2} \nabla \log | \pmb {\xi} \cdot \pmb {\xi} | = \frac {1}{2} \nabla \log | \mathbf {g} _ {t t} + 2 \omega_ {0} \mathbf {g} _ {t \phi} + \omega_ {0} ^ {2} \mathbf {g} _ {\phi \phi} |.
$$

When we set $\mathbf{g}_{t\phi} = -\omega \mathbf{g}_{\phi \phi}$ and evaluate at $\omega = \omega_0$ this becomes

$$
\mathbf {a} = \frac {1}{2} \nabla \log \left| \mathbf {g} _ {\mathrm {t t}} - \omega^ {2} \mathbf {g} _ {\phi \phi} \right|.
$$

Solution 17.19. In Boyer-Lindquist coordinates, for $t =$ constant and $r = r_+$ , the Kerr metric gives the metric of the horizon:

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = \left(\mathrm {r} _ {+} ^ {2} + \mathrm {a} ^ {2} \cos^ {2} \theta\right) \mathrm {d} \theta^ {2} + \left(2 \mathrm {M r} _ {+}\right) ^ {2} \sin^ {2} \theta \left(\mathrm {r} _ {+} ^ {2} + \mathrm {a} ^ {2} \cos^ {2} \theta\right) ^ {- 1} \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2}. \tag {1}
$$

(We have used the relation $\mathbf{r}_+^2 + \mathbf{a}^2 = 2\mathbf{M}\mathbf{r}_+$ in $\mathbf{g}_{\phi \phi}$ .) The Gaussian curvature $K$ of a 2-surface is the same as the Riemannian curvature of Problem 9.23, where the Riemann tensor is computed from the

2-dimensional metric. It is convenient to use an orthonormal basis dyad. For a metric of the form

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = \mathrm {e} ^ {2 a _ {1}} \left(\mathrm {d x} ^ {1}\right) ^ {2} + \mathrm {e} ^ {2 a _ {2}} \left(\mathrm {d x} ^ {2}\right) ^ {2} \tag {2}
$$

we let

$$
\tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {1}} = \mathrm {e} ^ {\alpha_ {1}} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {1}, \quad \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {2}} = \mathrm {e} ^ {\alpha_ {2}} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {2} \tag {3}
$$

and we have

$$
\mathrm {K} = \mathrm {R} _ {\hat {1} \hat {2} \hat {1} \hat {2}} = \mathrm {R} _ {1 2 1 2} \mathrm {e} ^ {- 2 \left(\alpha_ {1} + \alpha_ {2}\right)}. \tag {4}
$$

The quickest way to find the Riemann tensor is with forms as in Problems 8.27 and 9.20. Since

$$
\mathbf {d} \widetilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\widehat {1}} = \mathrm {e} ^ {\alpha_ {1}} \widetilde {\mathbf {d}} _ {\alpha_ {1}} \wedge \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}} ^ {1} = \mathrm {e} ^ {\alpha_ {1}} \alpha_ {1, 2} \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}} ^ {2} \wedge \widetilde {\mathbf {d x}} ^ {1} = \alpha_ {1, 2} \mathrm {e} ^ {- \alpha_ {2}} \widetilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\widehat {2}} \wedge \widetilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\widehat {1}}
$$

and a similar expression with 1 and 2 interchanged, we find

$$
\tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\hat {2}} ^ {\hat {1}} = \mathrm {e} ^ {- a _ {2}} a _ {1, 2} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {1}} - \mathrm {e} ^ {- a _ {1}} a _ {2, 1} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} ^ {\hat {2}}. \tag {5}
$$

(Recall that $\mathbf{d}\tilde{\pmb{\omega}}^{\hat{\alpha}} = -\tilde{\pmb{\omega}}^{\hat{\alpha}}\hat{\beta} \wedge \tilde{\pmb{\omega}}^{\hat{\beta}}$ and $\tilde{\pmb{\omega}}_{\hat{\alpha}}\hat{\beta} = -\tilde{\pmb{\omega}}_{\hat{\beta}}\hat{\alpha}$ .) The only nontrivial curvature form is

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathcal {R} _ {\widehat {\widehat {\mathbf {2}}}} ^ {\widehat {\widehat {\mathbf {1}}}} = \mathbf {d} \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\widehat {\widehat {\mathbf {2}}}} ^ {\widehat {\widehat {\mathbf {1}}}} + \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\widehat {\widehat {\boldsymbol {\alpha}}}} ^ {\widehat {\widehat {\mathbf {1}}}} \wedge \tilde {\boldsymbol {\omega}} _ {\widehat {\widehat {\mathbf {2}}}} ^ {\widehat {\widehat {\boldsymbol {\alpha}}}} \\ = \mathrm {d} \left(a _ {1, 2} \mathrm {e} ^ {a _ {1} - a _ {2}} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {1} - a _ {2, 1} \mathrm {e} ^ {a _ {2} - a _ {1}} \widetilde {\mathrm {d x}} ^ {2}\right) + 0 \\ = \left. \left(a _ {1, 2} e ^ {a _ {1} - a _ {2}}\right) _ {, 2} \widetilde {d x} ^ {2} \wedge \widetilde {d x} ^ {1} - \left(a _ {2, 1} e ^ {a _ {2} - a _ {1}}\right) _ {, 1} \widetilde {d x} ^ {1} \wedge \widetilde {d x} ^ {2} \right. \\ = - \mathrm {e} ^ {- (\alpha_ {1} + \alpha_ {2})} \left[ \left(\mathrm {e} _ {, 1} ^ {\alpha_ {2}} \mathrm {e} ^ {- \alpha_ {1}}\right) _ {, 1} + \left(\mathrm {e} _ {, 2} ^ {\alpha_ {1}} \mathrm {e} ^ {- \alpha_ {2}}\right) _ {, 2} \right] \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {1}} \wedge \tilde {\omega} ^ {\hat {2}}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Since $\mathcal{R}_{\hat{1}\hat{2}} = \mathbf{R}_{\hat{1}\hat{2}\hat{1}\hat{2}}\tilde{\pmb{\omega}}^{\hat{1}}\wedge \tilde{\pmb{\omega}}^{\hat{2}}$ we have

$$
\mathrm {K} = \mathrm {R} _ {\hat {1} \hat {2} \hat {1} \hat {2}} = - \mathrm {e} ^ {- \left(\alpha_ {1} + \alpha_ {2}\right)} \left[ \left(\mathrm {e} _ {, 1} ^ {\alpha_ {2}} \mathrm {e} ^ {- \alpha_ {1}}\right) _ {, 1} + \left(\mathrm {e} _ {, 2} ^ {\alpha_ {1}} \mathrm {e} ^ {- \alpha_ {2}}\right) _ {, 2} \right]. \tag {6}
$$

Using the explicit form of the metric in Equation (1), with $\theta = \mathbf{x}^1$ and $\phi = \mathbf{x}^2$ , we find from Equation (6) that

$$
K = 2 M r _ {+} \frac {r _ {+} ^ {2} - 3 a ^ {2} \cos^ {2} \theta}{\left(r _ {+} ^ {2} + a ^ {2} \cos^ {2} \theta\right) ^ {3}}. \tag {7}
$$

Note that if $r_+^2 - 3a^2 < 0$ , then $K$ is negative for a range of $\theta$ around the poles $(\theta = 0, \pi)$ . But when $K$ is negative, the surface cannot be globally embedded in a Euclidean 3-space. Since $r_+ = M + (M^2 - a^2)^{\frac{1}{2}}$ , the condition that $r_+^2 - 3a^2$ be less than zero is equivalent to $a > 3^{\frac{1}{2}}M / 2$ .

The Gauss-Bonnet theorem says that

$$
\int \mathrm {K d} ^ {2} \mathrm {S} = 2 \pi \chi
$$

where $\chi$ is the Euler characteristic of the surface ( $\chi = 2$ for a sphere, 0 for a torus, etc.). Since

$$
\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathrm {S} = (\mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi = 2 \mathrm {M r} _ {+} \sin \theta \mathrm {d} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi
$$

we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} \chi = (2 \pi) ^ {- 1} \int_ {0} ^ {2 \pi} d \phi \int_ {0} ^ {\pi} (2 M r _ {+}) ^ {2} \frac {r _ {+} ^ {2} - 3 a ^ {2} \cos^ {2} \theta}{\left(r _ {+} ^ {2} + a ^ {2} \cos^ {2} \theta\right) ^ {3}} \sin \theta d \theta \\ = (2 \mathrm {M r} _ {+}) ^ {2} 2 \int_ {0} ^ {1} \frac {\mathrm {r} _ {+} ^ {2} - 3 a ^ {2} x ^ {2}}{\left(\mathrm {r} _ {+} ^ {2} + a ^ {2} x ^ {2}\right) ^ {3}} d x \\ = (2 \mathrm {M r} _ {+}) ^ {2} 2 \left[ \frac {\mathrm {x}}{\left(\mathrm {r} _ {+} ^ {2} + \mathrm {a} ^ {2} \mathrm {x} ^ {2}\right) ^ {2}} \right] _ {0} ^ {1} \\ = 2 \\ \end{array}
$$

so the surface is topologically a 2-sphere. (For further details see L. Smarr, Phys. Rev. D. 7, 289, (1973).)

Solution 17.20. The energy amplification described in Problems 17.15 and 17.16 corresponds to stimulated emission in quantum-mechanical language. If we know the rate for stimulated emission, we can find the rate for spontaneous emission as follows:

Let $\mathfrak{p}$ be the probability per unit of phase space of the spontaneous emission of a quantum. If we send $\mathbf{N}$ quanta in, the probability of getting $\mathbf{N} + 1$ out by stimulated emission is $(\mathbf{N} + 1)\mathfrak{p}$ , since the quanta (gravitons, photons) are bosons. To simplify the argument, assume $\mathfrak{p} \ll 1$ so we do not have to consider the emission of more than one extra quantum; dimensionally the answer would not be changed if we were to include this refinement. The expectation value of the excess number of quanta emitted is

$$
\begin{array}{l} <   \Delta N > = (\text {e x c e s s n u m b e r}) \times (\text {p r o b a b i l i t y o f t h a t n u m b e r}) \\ = 1 \times (N + 1) p. \\ \end{array}
$$

The amplification is

$$
\mathrm {A} \equiv \frac {<   \Delta \mathrm {N} >}{\mathrm {N}} = \left(1 + \frac {1}{\mathrm {N}}\right) \mathrm {p} \approx \mathrm {p} \tag {1}
$$

in the classical limit $(\mathbf{N}\rightarrow \infty)$ . Fermi's Golden Rule says that the rate of spontaneous emission is

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} N}{\mathrm {d} t} \sim \int p. \tag {2}
$$

phase space

In Equation (2) replace $p$ by $A$ and recall that the classical effect is described in terms of modes with “quantum numbers” $\ell$ and $m$ and a

frequency $\omega$ . Thus

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathrm {N}}{\mathrm {d} t} \sim \sum_ {\ell , \mathrm {m}} \int \mathrm {A} \mathrm {d} \omega . \tag {3}
$$

Now A goes rapidly to zero for large $\ell$ and m, as in all barrier-penetration problems. Also from Problems 17.15 and 17.16, A is nonzero for $\omega \sim 1 / \mathbb{M}$ over a range $\Delta \omega \sim 1 / \mathbb{M}$ , where M is the mass of the black hole. Since A is a number of order unity for low $\ell$ and m, replace A by 1 in Equation (3) and drop the summation. Then

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {N}}{\mathrm {d} t} \sim \Delta \omega \sim \frac {1}{\mathbf {M}}.
$$

The hole loses energy at a rate

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {E}}{\mathrm {d} t} \sim \hbar \omega \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {N}}{\mathrm {d} t} \sim \frac {\hbar}{M ^ {2}}.
$$

This implies a loss of angular momentum at a rate

$$
\frac {d J}{d t} \sim \frac {m}{\omega} \frac {d E}{d t} \sim \frac {\hbar}{M}.
$$

Thus the time-scale for angular momentum loss is

$$
\tau \sim J / (d J / d t) \sim M ^ {2} (\pi / M) \sim M ^ {3} / \pi \sim (M / 1 0 ^ {1 5} g m) ^ {3} 1 0 ^ {1 0} \text {y e a r s}.
$$

Thus for $\mathbf{M} \lesssim 10^{15} \mathbf{g}\mathbf{m}$ , $\mathbf{J}$ will already have become essentially zero by this process. For $\mathbf{M} \sim 10^{33} \mathbf{g}\mathbf{m}$ , the fraction lost will be $\sim 10^{-54}$ in $10^{10}$ years.

Solution 17.21. For all static spacetimes, the “surface of infinite red-shift” $(\mathbf{g}_{00} = 0)$ coincides with the horizon (Problem 17.12). Therefore,

if the above metric describes a black hole, its surface is at $\mathbf{g}_{00} = 0$ or $\overline{\rho} = \frac{1}{2}$ , where $\overline{\rho} \equiv \rho / m$ . We will now show that, in fact, the surface $\overline{\rho} = \frac{1}{2}$ is not contained in the same manifold as the one which becomes asymptotically flat $(\overline{\rho} \to \infty)$ and thus cannot be reached by objects in our universe.

A glance at the $\mathbf{g}_{\rho \rho}$ portion of the metric shows that $\overline{\rho} = 1.5$ is an infinite proper radial distance away from any $\overline{\rho} > 1.5$ . To see that $\overline{\rho} = 1.5$ is also infinite proper time away and thus removed from the manifold it is sufficient to consider radial null geodesics. (The reader can verify that for nonradial or timelike geodesics, matters get even worse):

$$
\mathbf {g} _ {\rho \rho} (\mathfrak {p} ^ {\rho}) ^ {2} + \mathbf {g} ^ {0 0} (\mathfrak {p} _ {0}) ^ {2} = 0
$$

or

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \rho}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} = \left(\overline {{\rho}} - \frac {3}{2}\right) \frac {\left(\overline {{\rho}} + \frac {1}{2}\right)}{\left(\overline {{\rho}} - \frac {1}{2}\right) ^ {2}} p _ {0}
$$

where $\lambda$ is an affine parameter. Remembering that $\mathfrak{p}_0$ is a constant of the motion (stationary metric), the above equations show that

$$
p _ {0} ^ {- 1} \int_ {\bar {\rho}} ^ {\bar {\rho} = \frac {3}{2}} \frac {(\bar {\rho} - \frac {1}{2}) ^ {2} d \rho}{(\bar {\rho} - \frac {3}{2}) (\bar {\rho} + \frac {1}{2})} = \int_ {\lambda} ^ {\lambda_ {0}} d \lambda ,
$$

Since the integral on the left diverges at $\overline{\rho} = \frac{3}{2}$ , the $\overline{\rho} = \frac{3}{2}$ surface is an infinite affine distance away. There are no singularities for $\overline{\rho} \geq \frac{3}{2}$ and $\overline{\rho}$ is a monotonically decreasing coordinate for radially infalling photons and particles, so $\overline{\rho} = \frac{1}{2}$ , as well as $\overline{\rho} = \frac{3}{2}$ , must be off the physical manifold.

The above conclusions also indicate that the surface of a star collapsing to form the given vacuum metric can never reach $\overline{\rho} = \frac{3}{2}$ ; thus for realistic physical systems, the given metric is realizable only for $\overline{\rho} > \frac{3}{2}$ .

# CHAPTER 18: SOLUTIONS

Solution 18.1. To find the gravitational radiation we use the square of the third time derivative of the quadrupole moment

$$
\text {P o w e r} = \frac {\mathrm {G}}{5 \mathrm {c} ^ {5}} \left(\ddagger\right) ^ {2} \sim \frac {\mathrm {G M} ^ {2} \mathrm {L} ^ {4}}{5 \mathrm {c} ^ {5} \mathrm {t} ^ {6}}.
$$

The appropriate parameters for the mean Massachusetts motorist: $\mathbf{M} =$ mass of fist and forearm $\sim 2 \times 10^{3} \mathrm{~gm}$ , $\mathbf{L} =$ length of forearm $\sim 50 \mathrm{~cm}$ , $\mathbf{t} =$ period of average shake $\sim .2$ sec. The calculation in cgs units then yields a radiative power on the order of $2 \times 10^{-43}$ ergs/sec.

To calculate his total energy expended, use the fact that muscles are almost totally nonconservative, so every shake uses up an energy roughly equal to the arms maximum kinetic energy:

$$
\text {P o w e r} = \frac {1}{2} M \frac {L ^ {2}}{t ^ {2}} \cdot \frac {1}{t} \approx 3 \times 1 0 ^ {8} \text {e r g / s e c}.
$$

The fractional "efficiency" is thus $\sim 10^{-51}$ .

Solution 18.2. The gravitational binding energy of the system is of order $\mathbf{M}^2 / \mathbf{R}$ and the kinetic energy is of order $\mathbf{MR}^2 / \mathbf{T}_{\mathrm{dyn}}^2$ , where $\mathbf{T}_{\mathrm{dyn}}$ characterizes motions of the system. For a system in equilibrium the virial theorem gives

$$
\mathrm {T} _ {\mathrm {d y n}} ^ {2} \sim \mathrm {R} ^ {3} / \mathrm {M}.
$$

The rate at which energy is radiated gravitationally is governed by the square of the third time derivative of the quadrupole moment or roughly

$$
\mathrm {P} ^ {\mathrm {G W}} \sim \mathrm {M} ^ {2} \mathrm {R} ^ {4} / \mathrm {T} _ {\mathrm {d y n}} ^ {6}.
$$

The time for radiation reaction to affect the system is the time it takes to radiate a significant fraction of its energy as gravitational waves:

$$
\mathrm {T} = \frac {\mathrm {K . E .}}{\mathrm {P} ^ {\mathrm {G W}}} \sim \frac {\mathrm {T} _ {\mathrm {d y n}} ^ {4}}{\mathrm {M R} ^ {2}} \sim \frac {\mathrm {R T} _ {\mathrm {d y n}} ^ {2}}{\mathrm {M} ^ {2}}.
$$

The ratio of this gravitational wave characteristic time to the dynamical time is then of order

$$
\frac {\mathrm {T}}{\mathrm {T} _ {\mathrm {d y n}}} \sim \frac {\mathrm {R T} _ {\mathrm {d y n}}}{\mathrm {M} ^ {2}} \sim \left(\frac {\mathrm {R}}{\mathrm {M}}\right) ^ {\frac {5}{2}}.
$$

Evidently gravitational radiation reaction forces play an appreciable role only in relativistically compact systems.

Solution 18.3. The three independent orientations of the dipole correspond to the three components of a three dimensional vector. Since the quadrupole is a second rank, three dimensional tensor (9 component) which is symmetric (3 constraints) and traceless (1 constraint) there are five independent “orientations.” An example of five independent quadrupole tensors is the five tensors with all components zero except:

(i) $\mathrm{I}_{\mathbf{zz}} = -\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{I}_{\mathbf{xx}} = -\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{I}_{\mathbf{yy}} = 1$

(ii) $\mathrm{I_{yy} = -\frac{1}{2}I_{xx} = -\frac{1}{2}I_{zz} = 1}$

(iii) $\mathbf{I}_{\mathbf{xy}} = \mathbf{I}_{\mathbf{yx}}$

(iv) $\mathbf{I}_{\mathbf{x}z} = \mathbf{I}_{\mathbf{z}\mathbf{x}}$

(v) $\mathbf{I}_{\mathbf{yz}} = \mathbf{I}_{\mathbf{zy}}$

Solution 18.4. Assume the rod to be in the xy plane rotating about the $z$ axis. If $\mu$ is the mass per unit length of the rod the time dependent parts of the reduced quadrupole tensor are

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {T} _ {\mathbf {x x}} = \mu \cos^ {2} (\omega t) \cdot 2 \int_ {0} ^ {\ell / 2} \mathbf {x} ^ {2} d \mathbf {x} \\ = \frac {\mu \ell^ {3}}{1 2} \cos^ {2} \omega t = \frac {M \ell^ {2}}{2 4} \cos (2 \omega t) + \text {c o n s t a n t} \\ \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {I} _ {\mathrm {y y}} = - \frac {\mathrm {M} \ell^ {2}}{2 4} \cos 2 \omega t + \text {c o n s t a n t} \\ \mathbf {F} _ {\mathrm {x y}} = \mathbf {F} _ {\mathrm {y x}} = \frac {M \ell^ {2}}{2 4} \sin 2 \omega t, \\ \end{array}
$$

so that

$$
\begin{array}{l} L _ {G W} = \frac {1}{5} <   \ddot {F} _ {j k} \dot {F} _ {j k} > = \frac {(2 \omega) ^ {6}}{5} \left(\frac {M l ^ {2}}{2 4}\right) ^ {2} <   2 \cos^ {2} 2 \omega t + 2 \sin^ {2} 2 \omega t > \\ = \frac {2}{4 5} \omega^ {6} M ^ {2} \ell^ {4}. (\text {N o t e u n i t s w i t h} G = c = 1!) \\ \end{array}
$$

To calculate the charge centrifuged to the ends of the bar, balance electrostatic and centripetal forces:

$$
\left| e \nabla \Phi \right| = r m \omega^ {2},
$$

where $\Phi$ is the electrostatic potential and $e$ and $m$ are the charge and mass of an electron. Thus the charge density induced will be of order

$$
\rho \sim - \nabla^ {2} \Phi \sim - (\mathrm {m} / \mathrm {e}) \omega^ {2}.
$$

This charge depletion in the main part of the rod will of course be balanced by electrons squashed against the ends with charge $(\mathfrak{m} / \mathfrak{e})\omega^{2}\ell \mathbf{A}$ where $\mathbf{A} =$ rod cross section. The rod thus becomes an electric quadrupole with quadrupole moment of order $(\mathrm{m / e})\omega^{2}\ell^{3}\mathrm{A}$ . The electric quadrupole radiation due to this induced moment will be roughly $\omega^6$ times the square of the quadrupole moment, or roughly $\omega^{10}(\mathrm{m / e})^{2}\ell^{6}\mathrm{A}^{2}$ . The ratio of electromagnetic damping to gravitational damping is then

$$
\frac {\mathrm {L} _ {\mathrm {E M}}}{\mathrm {L} _ {\mathrm {G W}}} \sim \frac {\omega^ {1 0} (\mathrm {m / e}) ^ {2} \ell^ {6} \mathrm {A} ^ {2}}{\omega^ {6} \rho^ {2} \mathrm {A} ^ {2} \ell^ {6}} = \left[ \frac {(\mathrm {m / e}) ^ {2} \omega^ {2}}{\rho} \right] ^ {2}.
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \begin{array}{l} \text {I n G = c = 1 u n i t s (m / e) \approx \frac {1}{2} \times 1 0 ^ {- 2 1} a n d \rho (1 0 g / c m ^ {3}) \approx 1 0 ^ {- 2 7} a n d} \\ \omega (1 k H _ {z}) \approx \frac {1}{3} \times 1 0 ^ {- 7} \text {s o t h a t} \end{array} \\ \mathrm {L} _ {\mathrm {E M}} / \mathrm {L} _ {\mathrm {G W}} \sim 1 0 ^ {- 1 8}. \\ \end{array}
$$

For once gravitational radiation wins out.

Solution 18.5. The radiation reaction force on an element of the source is

$$
\mathrm {d F} _ {\mathrm {i}} = - \Phi_ {, \mathrm {i}} \rho \mathrm {d} ^ {3} \mathrm {x}.
$$

The rate at which the source element loses energy is the rate at which the reaction forces do work on the source element: $\mathbf{v}^{\mathrm{i}}\mathbf{d}\mathbf{F}_{\mathrm{i}}$ where $\mathbf{v}$ is the velocity of the source element. For the whole source, then

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {E}}{\mathrm {d} t} = - \int \Phi_ {, \mathbf {i}} \mathbf {v} ^ {\mathbf {i}} \rho \mathrm {d} ^ {3} \mathbf {x} = \int \Phi (\mathbf {v} ^ {\mathbf {i}} \rho) _ {, \mathbf {i}} \mathrm {d} ^ {3} \mathbf {x}.
$$

Here we have used Gauss' divergence theorem and evaluated the surface integral on a surface outside the source to obtain the second equality. The equation of continuity tells us that $\nabla \cdot (\underline{\nu}\rho) = -\partial \rho /\partial t$ so

$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {\mathrm {d} E}{\mathrm {d} t} = - \int \Phi \frac {\partial \rho}{\partial t} d ^ {3} x = - \frac {1}{5} \mathbf {f} _ {j k} ^ {(5)} \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} t} \int x ^ {j} x ^ {k} \rho d ^ {3} x \\ = - \frac {1}{5} \mathbf {f} _ {\mathrm {j k}} ^ {(5)} \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d t}} \left(\mathbf {f} _ {\mathrm {j k}} + \frac {1}{3} \delta_ {\mathrm {j k}} \int \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \rho \mathrm {d} ^ {3} x\right). \\ \end{array}
$$

Since $\pmb{\mathcal{I}}_{\mathbf{jk}}\delta_{\mathbf{jk}} = 0$ we drop the final term to arrive at

$$
\mathrm {d E} / \mathrm {d t} = - \left(\frac {1}{5}\right) \dot {\mathbf {f}} _ {\mathbf {j k}} ^ {(5)} \dot {\mathbf {f}} _ {\mathbf {j k}}.
$$

Now we time average over several oscillations. Since secular changes in parameters of the source are assumed to be small over the several oscilla-

tions, we have

$$
\frac {1}{\mathrm {T}} \int_ {0} ^ {\mathrm {T}} \mathbf {\ddot {t}} _ {\mathbf {j k}} ^ {(5)} \dot {\mathbf {\ddot {t}}} _ {\mathbf {j k}} \mathrm {d t} \approx \frac {1}{\mathrm {T}} \int_ {0} ^ {\mathrm {T}} \mathbf {\ddot {t}} _ {\mathbf {j k}} \dot {\mathbf {\ddot {t}}} _ {\mathbf {j k}} \mathrm {d t}
$$

and

$$
\frac {d E}{d t} = - \frac {1}{5} <   \underbrace {\cdots} _ {j k} \underbrace {\cdots} _ {j k} >.
$$

To find the rate of loss of angular momentum we use the fact that for an element of the source

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {J}}{\mathrm {d} t} = \text {r a d i a t i o n r e a c t i o n t o r q u e} = \mathbf {r} \times \mathrm {d} \mathbf {F} = - \mathbf {r} \times \nabla \Phi \rho \mathrm {d} ^ {3} \mathbf {x}
$$

and for the whole source

$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {d J ^ {i}}{d t} = - \varepsilon^ {i j k} \int x ^ {j} \Phi_ {, k} \rho d ^ {3} x \\ = - \varepsilon^ {\mathrm {i j k}} \int \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \left(\frac {2}{5} \mathbf {f} _ {\mathrm {k m}} ^ {(5)} \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {m}}\right) \rho \mathrm {d} ^ {3} \mathbf {x} \\ = - \frac {2}{5} \varepsilon^ {\mathrm {i j k}} \mathbf {F} _ {\mathrm {k m}} ^ {(5)} \int \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {m}} \rho \mathrm {d} ^ {3} \mathbf {x} = - \frac {2}{5} \varepsilon^ {\mathrm {i j k}} \mathbf {F} _ {\mathrm {k m}} ^ {(5)} \mathbf {F} _ {\mathrm {j m}}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Time averaging, and integrating (in time) by parts as above gives us

$$
\frac {d J ^ {i}}{d t} = - \frac {2}{5} \varepsilon^ {i j k} <   \ddot {\pi} _ {k m} \ddot {\pi} _ {j m} >.
$$

Solution 18.6. Let $\mathfrak{m}_1, \mathfrak{m}_2$ and $\mathbf{r}_1, \mathbf{r}_2$ be respectively the masses, and distances from the center of mass, of the two stars rotating at angular frequency $\omega$ . From Newtonian mechanics we know that $\mathbf{r}_1\mathfrak{m}_1 = \mathbf{r}_2\mathfrak{m}_2 = \mathbb{R}\mu$ , where $\mathbb{R} \equiv \mathbf{r}_1 + \mathbf{r}_2$ and $\mu = \text{reduced mass} = \mathfrak{m}_1\mathfrak{m}_2 / (\mathfrak{m}_1 + \mathfrak{m}_2)$ . The total energy of the stars can now be written as a function of $\mathbb{M}$ , $\mathbb{R}$ , and $\mu$ :

$$
\mathbf {E} = \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {r _ {1}} ^ {2} \mathbf {m _ {1}} + \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {r _ {2}} ^ {2} \mathbf {m _ {2}} \quad \omega^ {2} - \frac {\mathbf {m _ {1}} \mathbf {m _ {2}}}{\mathbf {R}} = \frac {1}{2} \omega^ {2} \mathbf {R} \mu - \frac {\mu \mathbf {M}}{\mathbf {R}} = - \frac {1}{2} \frac {\mu \mathbf {M}}{\mathbf {R}}.
$$

Here $\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{m}_1 + \mathbf{m}_2$ and Kepler's laws have been used.

The power dissipated as gravitational waves is easily calculated once the reduced quadrupole moment,

$$
\mathbf {\Phi} _ {j k} \equiv \int \rho \left(\mathbf {x} _ {j} \mathbf {x} _ {k} - \frac {1}{3} \mathbf {\nabla r} ^ {2} \delta_ {j k}\right) \mathrm {d} ^ {3} \mathbf {x},
$$

is evaluated. Note that the "reducing term" $\int \frac{1}{3} \rho r^2 \delta_{jk} d^3 x$ will be constant in time and can be ignored. Suppose the $z$ axis to be the axis of

rotation and $\phi$ the azimuthal angle from the $x$ -axis to the line joining the stars, then (aside from time independent terms) we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} I _ {x x} = \left(r _ {1} ^ {2} m _ {1} + r _ {2} ^ {2} m _ {2}\right) \cos^ {2} \phi = \frac {1}{2} R ^ {2} \mu \cos 2 \phi + c o n s t a n t \\ I _ {y y} = - \frac {1}{2} R ^ {2} \mu \cos 2 \phi + c o n s t a n t \\ I _ {x y} = I _ {y x} = \frac {1}{2} R ^ {2} \mu \sin 2 \phi . \\ \end{array}
$$

Since $\phi = \omega t$ , it is straightforward to differentiate three times, perform the sum $\mathbf{\Phi}_{jk}\mathbf{\Phi}_{jk}$ and time average to find

$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {1}{5} <   \ddot {\tau} _ {j k} \ddot {\tau} _ {j k} > = \frac {1}{5} (2 \omega) ^ {6} \left(\frac {1}{2} R ^ {2} \mu\right) ^ {2} <   \sin^ {2} 2 \omega t + \sin^ {2} 2 \omega t + 2 \cos^ {2} 2 \omega t > \\ = \frac {3 2}{5} \omega^ {6} R ^ {4} \mu^ {2} = \frac {3 2}{5} \frac {M ^ {3} \mu^ {2}}{R ^ {5}} = P _ {G W}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Since this power is radiated at the expense of orbital energy, it follows that

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d E}}{\mathrm {d t}} = \frac {1}{2} \frac {\mu \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {R} ^ {2}} \frac {\mathrm {d R}}{\mathrm {d t}} = - \mathrm {P _ {G W}} = - \frac {3 2}{5} \frac {\mathrm {M} ^ {3} \mu^ {2}}{\mathrm {R} ^ {5}} .
$$

The resulting differential equation

$$
\mathrm {R} ^ {3} \frac {\mathrm {d R}}{\mathrm {d t}} = - \frac {6 4}{5} \mathrm {M} ^ {2} \mu
$$

is easily integrated to give

$$
R ^ {4} = - (2 5 6 / 5) M ^ {2} \mu t + c o n s t a n t.
$$

If we denote by $t_0$ the time at which the separation goes to zero according to this formula, then

$$
\mathsf {R} ^ {4} = \frac {2 5 6}{5} \mathsf {M} ^ {2} \mu (\mathsf {t} _ {0} - \mathsf {t}) .
$$

We can find the time $t_0$ by noting that at $t = 0$ , $R$ must be the present separation $R_{\text{now}}$ , thus

$$
t _ {0} = \frac {5 R _ {\mathrm {n o w}} ^ {4}}{2 5 6 \mathrm {M} ^ {2} \mu}.
$$

Solution 18.7. In terms of the total orbital energy $\mathbf{E}$ and angular momentum $\mathbf{L}$ , the semimajor axis and eccentricity are

$$
a = - m _ {1} m _ {1} / 2 E \tag {1}
$$

$$
e ^ {2} = 1 + \frac {2 E L ^ {2} \left(m _ {1} + m _ {2}\right)}{m _ {1} ^ {3} m _ {2} ^ {3}} \tag {2}
$$

so that

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} a}{\mathrm {d} t} = \frac {m _ {1} m _ {2}}{2 E ^ {2}} \frac {\mathrm {d} E}{\mathrm {d} t} \tag {3}
$$

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {e}}{\mathrm {d} t} = \frac {\mathrm {m} _ {1} + \mathrm {m} _ {2}}{\mathrm {m} _ {1} ^ {3} \mathrm {m} _ {2} ^ {3} \mathrm {e}} \left(\mathrm {L} ^ {2} \frac {\mathrm {d E}}{\mathrm {d t}} + 2 \mathrm {E L} \frac {\mathrm {d L}}{\mathrm {d t}}\right). \tag {4}
$$

If the separation of the two particles is $\mathbf{r}$ , where

$$
r = \frac {a \left(1 - e ^ {2}\right)}{1 + e \cos \theta} \tag {5}
$$

(see figure), then

$$
\mathbf {r} _ {1} = \frac {\mathbf {m} _ {2}}{\mathbf {m} _ {1} + \mathbf {m} _ {2}} \mathbf {r}
$$

$$
\mathbf {r} _ {2} = \frac {\mathbf {m} _ {1}}{\mathbf {m} _ {1} + \mathbf {m} _ {2}} \mathbf {r}
$$

so the components of the quadrupole tensor are

$$
I _ {x x} = m _ {1} x _ {1} ^ {2} + m _ {2} x _ {2} ^ {2} = \frac {m _ {1} m _ {2}}{m _ {1} + m _ {2}} r ^ {2} \cos^ {2} \theta
$$

![](images/c10df870c1f14883a972caca37b0c38a825b71f25231dc036ce9a146443dfb8f.jpg)

$$
\mathbf {I} _ {\mathbf {y y}} = \frac {\mathbf {m _ {1}} \mathbf {m _ {2}}}{\mathbf {m _ {1}} + \mathbf {m _ {2}}} \mathbf {r} ^ {2} \sin^ {2} \theta
$$

$$
I _ {x y} = \frac {m _ {1} m _ {2}}{m _ {1} + m _ {2}} r ^ {2} \sin \theta \cos \theta \tag {6}
$$

$$
\mathbf {I} \equiv \mathbf {I _ {x x}} + \mathbf {I _ {y y}} = \frac {\mathbf {m _ {1}} \mathbf {m _ {2}}}{\mathbf {m _ {1}} + \mathbf {m _ {2}}} \mathbf {\Omega r ^ {2}} .
$$

According to the Newtonian equations of motion

$$
\dot {\theta} = \frac {\left[ \left(\mathrm {m} _ {1} + \mathrm {m} _ {2}\right) \mathrm {a} \left(1 - \mathrm {e} ^ {2}\right) \right] ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {2}} \tag {7}
$$

so Equation (5) gives

$$
\dot {\mathbf {r}} = \mathrm {e} \sin \theta \left[ \frac {\mathrm {m} _ {1} + \mathrm {m} _ {2}}{\mathrm {a} \left(1 - \mathrm {e} ^ {2}\right)} \right] ^ {\frac {1}{2}}. \tag {8}
$$

Now we compute the successive time derivatives of $\mathbf{I}_{ij}$ , using Equations (5), (7) and (8) to simplify the expressions. The results are

$$
\dot {\mathrm {I}} _ {\mathbf {x x}} = \frac {- 2 \mathrm {m} _ {1} \mathrm {m} _ {2} \mathrm {r} \cos \theta \sin \theta}{[ (\mathrm {m} _ {1} + \mathrm {m} _ {2}) \mathrm {a} (1 - \mathrm {e} ^ {2}) ] ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \tag {9}
$$

$$
\ddot {\mathrm {I}} _ {\mathbf {x x}} = \frac {- 2 \mathrm {m} _ {1} \mathrm {m} _ {2}}{\mathrm {a} \left(1 - \mathrm {e} ^ {2}\right)} (\cos 2 \theta + \mathrm {e} \cos^ {3} \theta) \tag {10}
$$

$$
\dot {\mathrm {I}} _ {\mathbf {x x}} = \frac {2 \mathrm {m} _ {1} \mathrm {m} _ {2}}{\mathrm {a} \left(1 - \mathrm {e} ^ {2}\right)} (2 \sin 2 \theta + 3 \mathrm {e} \cos^ {2} \theta \sin \theta) \dot {\theta} \tag {11}
$$

$$
\dot {\mathbf {I}} _ {\mathbf {y y}} = \frac {2 \mathrm {m} _ {1} \mathrm {m} _ {2}}{\left[ \left(\mathrm {m} _ {1} + \mathrm {m} _ {2}\right) \mathrm {a} \left(1 - \mathrm {e} ^ {2}\right) \right] ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \mathrm {r} (\sin \theta \cos \theta + \mathrm {e} \sin \theta) \tag {12}
$$

$$
\ddot {\mathrm {I}} _ {\mathrm {y y}} = \frac {2 \mathrm {m} _ {1} \mathrm {m} _ {2}}{\mathrm {a} \left(1 - \mathrm {e} ^ {2}\right)} \left(\cos 2 \theta + \mathrm {e} \cos \theta + \mathrm {e} \cos^ {3} \theta + \mathrm {e} ^ {2}\right) \tag {13}
$$

$$
\dot {\mathbf {I}} _ {\mathbf {y y}} = \frac {- 2 \mathrm {m} _ {1} \mathrm {m} _ {2}}{\mathrm {a} \left(1 - \mathrm {e} ^ {2}\right)} (2 \sin 2 \theta + \mathrm {e} \sin \theta + 3 \mathrm {e} \cos^ {2} \theta \sin \theta) \dot {\theta} \tag {14}
$$

$$
\dot {\mathbf {I}} _ {\mathbf {x y}} = \frac {\mathrm {m} _ {1} \mathrm {m} _ {2} \mathrm {r} \left(\cos^ {2} \theta - \sin^ {2} \theta + \mathrm {e} \cos \theta\right)}{\left[ \left(\mathrm {m} _ {1} + \mathrm {m} _ {2}\right) \mathrm {a} \left(1 - \mathrm {e} ^ {2}\right) \right] ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \tag {15}
$$

$$
\ddot {\mathrm {I}} _ {\mathrm {x y}} = \frac {- 2 \mathrm {m} _ {1} \mathrm {m} _ {2}}{\mathrm {a} \left(1 - \mathrm {e} ^ {2}\right)} (\sin 2 \theta + \mathrm {e} \sin \theta + \mathrm {e} \sin \theta \cos^ {2} \theta) \tag {16}
$$

$$
\dot {\mathrm {I}} _ {\mathrm {x y}} = \frac {- 2 \mathrm {m} _ {1} \mathrm {m} _ {2}}{\mathrm {a} \left(1 - \mathrm {e} ^ {2}\right)} (2 \cos 2 \theta - \mathrm {e} \cos \theta + 3 \mathrm {e} \cos^ {3} \theta) \dot {\theta} \tag {17}
$$

$$
\cdots \mathrm {I} = \cdots \mathrm {I} _ {\mathbf {x x}} + \cdots \mathrm {I} _ {\mathbf {y y}} = \frac {- 2 \mathrm {m} _ {1} \mathrm {m} _ {2} \mathrm {e} \sin \theta \dot {\theta}}{\mathrm {a} \left(1 - \mathrm {e} ^ {2}\right)}. \tag {18}
$$

The rate of loss of energy is then:

$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {E}}{\mathrm {d} t} = - \frac {1}{5} \overset {\dots} {\mathbf {I}} _ {\mathbf {j k}} ^ {\dots} \overset {\dots} {\mathbf {I}} _ {\mathbf {j k}} = - \frac {1}{5} \left(\overset {\dots} {\mathbf {I}} _ {\mathbf {j k}} ^ {\dots} \overset {\dots} {\mathbf {I}} _ {\mathbf {j k}} - \frac {1}{3} \overset {\dots} {\mathbf {I}} ^ {2}\right) \\ = - \frac {1}{5} \left(\stackrel {\dots} {\textbf {I}} _ {\textbf {x x}} ^ {2} + 2 \stackrel {\dots} {\textbf {I}} _ {\textbf {x y}} ^ {2} + \stackrel {\dots} {\textbf {I}} _ {\textbf {y y}} ^ {2} - \frac {1}{3} \stackrel {\dots} {\textbf {I}} ^ {2}\right) \\ = \frac {- 8 m _ {1} ^ {2} m _ {2} ^ {2}}{1 5 a ^ {2} (1 - e ^ {2}) ^ {2}} \left[ 1 2 (1 + e \cos \theta) ^ {2} + e ^ {2} \sin^ {2} \theta \right] \dot {\theta} ^ {2} \\ \end{array}
$$

where we have used Equations (9)-(18) and straightforward algebraic simplifications. Now we must average over an orbit. The period of an orbit is (Kepler's third law)

$$
\mathrm {T} = \int_ {0} ^ {2 \pi} \frac {1}{\dot {\theta}} \mathrm {d} \theta = \frac {2 \pi \mathrm {a} ^ {\frac {3}{2}}}{(\mathrm {m} _ {1} + \mathrm {m} _ {2}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}
$$

so that

$$
\begin{array}{l} \left\langle \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {E}}{\mathrm {d} t} \right\rangle = \frac {1}{\mathrm {T}} \int_ {0} ^ {\mathrm {T}} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {E}}{\mathrm {d} t} \mathrm {d} t = \frac {1}{\mathrm {T}} \int_ {0} ^ {2 \pi} \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {E}}{\mathrm {d} t} \frac {1}{\dot {\theta}} \mathrm {d} \theta \\ = \frac {- 3 2}{5} \frac {\mathrm {m} _ {1} ^ {2} \mathrm {m} _ {2} ^ {2} \left(\mathrm {m} _ {1} + \mathrm {m} _ {2}\right)}{\mathrm {a} ^ {5} \left(1 - \mathrm {e} ^ {2}\right) ^ {7 / 2}} \left(1 + \frac {7 3}{2 4} \mathrm {e} ^ {2} + \frac {3 7}{9 6} \mathrm {e} ^ {4}\right). \tag {19} \\ \end{array}
$$

Similarly, the rate of loss of angular momentum is

$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {d L}{d t} = \frac {- 2}{5} \varepsilon_ {z i j} \ddot {\mathbf {I}} _ {i k} \dddot {\mathbf {I}} _ {k j} = \frac {- 2}{5} \varepsilon_ {z i j} \dddot {\mathbf {I}} _ {i k} \dddot {\mathbf {I}} _ {k j} \\ = \frac {- 2}{5} \left[ \overset {\prime} {I} _ {\mathbf {x y}} \left(\overset {\prime} {I} _ {\mathbf {y y}} - \overset {\prime} {I} _ {\mathbf {x x}}\right) + \overset {\prime} {I} _ {\mathbf {x y}} \left(\overset {\prime} {I} _ {\mathbf {x x}} - \overset {\prime} {I} _ {\mathbf {y y}}\right) \right] \\ = \frac {- 8}{5} \frac {m _ {1} ^ {2} m _ {2} ^ {2}}{a ^ {2} \left(1 - e ^ {2}\right) ^ {2}} [ 4 + 1 0 e \cos \theta + e ^ {2} (9 \cos^ {2} \theta - 1) \\ + \mathrm {e} ^ {3} (3 \cos^ {3} \theta - \cos \theta) ] \dot {\theta} \\ \end{array}
$$

which when time averaged gives us

$$
\begin{array}{l} \left\langle \frac {\mathrm {d} L}{\mathrm {d} t} \right\rangle = \frac {1}{T} \int_ {0} ^ {2 \pi} \frac {\mathrm {d} L}{\mathrm {d} t} \frac {1}{\dot {\theta}} \mathrm {d} \theta \\ = \frac {- 3 2}{5} \frac {\mathrm {m} _ {1} ^ {2} \mathrm {m} _ {2} ^ {2} \left(\mathrm {m} _ {1} + \mathrm {m} _ {2}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{\mathrm {a} ^ {7 / 2} \left(1 - \mathrm {e} ^ {2}\right) ^ {2}} \left(1 + \frac {7}{8} \mathrm {e} ^ {2}\right). \tag {20} \\ \end{array}
$$

From Equations (3) and (4) we have, finally:

$$
\begin{array}{l} \left\langle \frac {\mathrm {d} a}{\mathrm {d} t} \right\rangle = \frac {2 a ^ {2}}{\mathrm {m} _ {1} \mathrm {m} _ {2}} \left\langle \frac {\mathrm {d} E}{\mathrm {d} t} \right\rangle \\ = \frac {- 6 4}{5} \frac {\mathrm {m} _ {1} \mathrm {m} _ {2} \left(\mathrm {m} _ {1} + \mathrm {m} _ {2}\right)}{\mathrm {a} ^ {3} \left(1 - \mathrm {e} ^ {2}\right) ^ {7 / 2}} \left(1 + \frac {7 3}{2 4} \mathrm {e} ^ {2} + \frac {3 7}{9 6} \mathrm {e} ^ {4}\right) \tag {21} \\ \end{array}
$$

$$
