\begin{array}{l} \left\langle \frac {d e}{d t} \right\rangle = \frac {m _ {1} + m _ {2}}{m _ {1} m _ {2} e} \left[ \frac {a (1 - e ^ {2})}{m _ {1} + m _ {2}} \left\langle \frac {d E}{d t} \right\rangle - \frac {\left(1 - e ^ {2}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{a ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \left(m _ {1} + m _ {2}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \left\langle \frac {d L}{d t} \right\rangle \right] \\ = \frac {- 3 0 4}{1 5} \frac {\mathrm {m} _ {1} \mathrm {m} _ {2} \left(\mathrm {m} _ {1} + \mathrm {m} _ {2}\right) \mathrm {e}}{\mathrm {a} ^ {4} \left(1 - \mathrm {e} ^ {2}\right) ^ {5 / 2}} \left(1 + \frac {1 2 1}{3 0 4} \mathrm {e} ^ {2}\right). \tag {22} \\ \end{array}
$$

From Equation (22) we see that $\mathrm{de} / \mathrm{dt}$ is negative, so the eccentricity decreases because of radiation reaction.

If we put $e = 0$ in Equation (21), we recover the results of Problem 18.6. For the integration of Equations (21) and (22) when $e \neq 0$ , see P. C. Peters, Phys. Rev. 136, 1224 (1964).

Solution 18.8. The Ricci tensor for the wave must vanish. By contracting the result in Problem 13.13, we have then

$$
0 = 2 \mathrm {R} _ {\mu \nu} = \mathrm {h} _ {\nu , a \mu} ^ {a} + \mathrm {h} _ {\mu , a \nu} ^ {a} - \mathrm {h} _ {\mu \nu} ^ {, a} a - \mathrm {h} _ {, \mu \nu}.
$$

Since the perturbations are functions of $\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{t} - \mathbf{x}$ , the third term $\square \mathrm{h}_{\mu \nu}$ automatically vanishes and the components $\mathbb{R}_{22}, \mathbb{R}_{23}, \mathbb{R}_{33}$ automatically vanish. For $\mu, \nu = 0,2$ we find

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2}}{\mathrm {d u} ^ {2}} \left(\mathrm {h} _ {2} ^ {0} - \mathrm {h} _ {2} ^ {1}\right) = 0.
$$

From this we conclude $\mathbf{h}_2^0 = \mathbf{h}_2^1$ . [The integration constants are of no consequence; they are not wavelike; they carry no energy, and since they make no contribution to the Riemann tensor, they are gauge removable.]

Similarly we have

$$
\mathbf {h} _ {3} ^ {0} = \mathbf {h} _ {3} ^ {1} \quad \text {f r o m} \quad \mu , \nu_ {\cdot} = 0, 3
$$

$$
\mathrm {h} _ {0} ^ {0} - \mathrm {h} _ {0} ^ {1} = \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {h} \quad \text {f r o m} \quad \mu , \nu = 0, 0
$$

$$
\mathrm {h} _ {2} ^ {2} + \mathrm {h} _ {3} ^ {3} = 0 \quad \text {f r o m} \quad \mu , \nu = 1, 0.
$$

Other choices of $\mu \nu$ give no further, independent relations.

These four constraints leave us with six independent components of $\mathbf{h}_{\alpha \beta}$ . A gauge transformation of the form $\xi_{\mu}(t - x)$ leaves the $\mathbf{h}_{\alpha \beta}$ as functions only of $t - x$ and hence does not invalidate our four constraints above. We choose then a gauge transformation of this type which annihilates $\mathbf{h}_{00}, \mathbf{h}_{11}, \mathbf{h}_{02}, \mathbf{h}_{03}$ and which leaves us with only the quantities $\mathbf{h}_{23} = \mathbf{h}_{32}$ and $\mathbf{h}_{22} - \mathbf{h}_{33}$ which are invariant for such a transformation. The explicit form of the gauge transformation is

$$
\begin{array}{l} \xi_ {0} (u) = \frac {1}{2} \int^ {u} h _ {0 0} (\tilde {u}) d \tilde {u} \quad \xi_ {1} (u) = - \frac {1}{2} \int^ {u} h _ {1 1} (\tilde {u}) d \tilde {u} \\ \xi_ {2} (u) = \int^ {u} h _ {0 2} (\tilde {u}) d \tilde {u} \quad \xi_ {3} (u) = \int^ {u} h _ {0 3} (\tilde {u}) d \tilde {u}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Now we consider whether these are the same components as we would get by projecting out the transverse traceless part of the perturbation. Since we have $\mathbf{h} = \mathbf{h}_0^0 + \mathbf{h}_1^1$ projecting out the transverse part is equivalent to projecting out the TT part. But projecting out the transverse part leaves $\mathbf{h}_{22}, \mathbf{h}_{23}, \mathbf{h}_{33}$ invariant, as did our gauge transformation. Thus we get the same components either way.

Solution 18.9. Let the $z$ axis be the symmetry axis and let $\phi$ be the cyclic axial coordinate. At an instant of time, $t =$ constant, in the asymptotically flat region far outside the sources through which the waves are propagating, the angular momentum inside a closed 2-surface $r = r_1$ is (by symmetry only an axial component)

$$
J _ {1} = - (1 6 \pi) ^ {- 1} \int_ {\mathrm {r} = \mathrm {r} _ {1}} \xi^ {\mu ; \nu} \mathrm {d} ^ {2} \Sigma_ {\mu \nu} \tag {1}
$$

where $\pmb{\xi} = \partial/\partial \phi$ . (See Problem 16.23. The proof goes through even if there is no time Killing vector and even in the presence of waves since waves modify only the space-space parts of the metric - see MTW, Equation 19.5.) Similarly, the angular momentum inside a closed 2-surface $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{r}_2(\mathbf{r}_2 > \mathbf{r}_1)$ is

$$
J _ {2} = - (1 6 \pi) ^ {- 1} \int_ {\mathrm {r} = \mathrm {r} _ {2}} \xi^ {\mu ; \nu} \mathrm {d} ^ {2} \Sigma_ {\mu \nu}. \tag {2}
$$

Thus the angular momentum of the waves between $\mathbf{r}_1$ and $\mathbf{r}_2$ is

$$
\begin{array}{l} J _ {2} - J _ {1} = - (1 6 \pi) ^ {- 1} \int_ {\mathbf {r} = \mathbf {r} _ {2}} \xi^ {\mu ; \nu}   d ^ {2} \Sigma_ {\mu \nu} + (1 6 \pi) ^ {- 1} \int_ {\mathbf {r} = \mathbf {r} _ {1}} \xi^ {\mu ; \nu}   d ^ {2} \Sigma_ {\mu \nu} \\ = \int_ {\mathbf {r} _ {1} \leq \mathbf {r} \leq \mathbf {r} _ {2}} \mathrm {T} _ {\nu} ^ {\mu} \xi^ {\nu} \mathrm {d} ^ {3} \Sigma_ {\mu} \\ \end{array}
$$

where we have used Stokes' theorem as in Solution 16.23. Since $\mathbf{T}_{\nu}^{\mu}$ vanishes for $\mathbf{r}_1 \leq \mathbf{r} \leq \mathbf{r}_2$ , $\mathbf{J}_2 - \mathbf{J}_1$ is zero and so the angular momentum flux of the waves vanishes.

Solution 18.10. A plane wave propagating in the $z$ -direction can be described at the position of a detector by

$$
h _ {x x} = - h _ {y y} = R e \left\{A _ {+} e ^ {- i \omega t} \right\} \quad h _ {x y} = h _ {y x} = R e \left\{A _ {\times} e ^ {- i \omega t} \right\}.
$$

If the plane wave is monochromatic (and hence $100\%$ polarized) $\mathbf{A}_{+}$ and $\mathbf{A}_{\times}$ will be constant. More generally if we are considering waves in a narrow bandwidth about $\omega$ , $\mathbf{A}_{+}$ and $\mathbf{A}_{\times}$ will be slow functions of time (i.e. slow compared to the wave oscillations). Since the intensity is proportional to the absolute squares of the A's we can define a polarization tensor

$$
\rho_ {a b} \equiv \frac {\left. <   A _ {a} A _ {b} ^ {*} > \right.}{\left| A _ {+} \right| ^ {2} + \left| A _ {\times} \right| ^ {2}}
$$

in analogy with the electromagnetic approach. [See for example Landau and Lifschitz, p. 122ff.] Following this analogy further we notice that this matrix is hermitian with a unit trace so we can write it as

$$
\rho_ {\mathrm {a b}} = \frac {1}{2} \left[ \begin{array}{c c} 1 + \xi_ {3} & \xi_ {1} - \mathrm {i} \xi_ {2} \\ \vdots & \vdots \\ \xi_ {1} + \mathrm {i} \xi_ {2} & 1 - \xi_ {3} \end{array} \right].
$$

As in electromagnetism $\xi_1, \xi_2, \xi_3$ will be called the Stokes parameters of the wave. [Note: Some authors call these the normalized Stokes parameters.]

These parameters characterize the polarization state of the wave. To get insight into the meaning of the parameters, let us consider a mixture of (1) unpolarized waves, (2) circularly polarized waves, and (3) linearly polarized waves with an orientation rotated about the $x$ and $y$ axis by an angle $\psi$ . Mathematically these three cases correspond to superpositions of

(1) $\mathbf{A}_{+} = \mathbf{G}_{1}(\mathbf{t})$ , $\mathbf{A}_{\times} = \mathbf{G}_{2}(\mathbf{t})$ with $< |\mathbf{G}_{1}(\mathbf{t})|^{2}> = < |\mathbf{G}_{2}(\mathbf{t})|^{2}> \equiv < |\mathbf{G}|^{2}>$ and with $< \mathbf{G}_{1}(\mathbf{t})\mathbf{G}_{2}^{*}(\mathbf{t})> = 0$   
(2) $\mathbf{A}_{+} = \mathbf{H}(t)$ $\mathbf{A}_{\times} = \pm \mathrm{iH}(t)$   
(3) $\mathbf{A}_{+} = \mathbf{F}(t)\cos 2\psi$ $\mathbf{A}_{\times} = \mathbf{F}(t)\sin 2\psi$

We assume of course that $\mathbf{G}_1, \mathbf{G}_2, \mathbf{F},$ and $\mathbf{H}$ are uncorrelated. The polarization matrix is then

$$
\begin{array}{l} \rho = \frac {1}{I} \left[ \begin{array}{l l} <   | F | ^ {2} > \cos^ {2} 2 \psi + <   | G | > ^ {2} + <   | H | > ^ {2} & <   | F | ^ {2} > \sin 2 \psi \cos 2 \psi \mp i <   | H | ^ {2} > \\ <   | F | ^ {2} > \sin 2 \psi \cos 2 \psi \pm i <   | H | ^ {2} > & <   | F | ^ {2} > \sin^ {2} 2 \psi + <   | G | ^ {2} > + <   | H | ^ {2} > \end{array} \right] \\ \mathbf {I} \equiv <   | \mathbf {F} | ^ {2} > + 2 <   | \mathbf {G} | ^ {2} > + 2 <   | \mathbf {H} | ^ {2} > \\ \end{array}
$$

so that

$$
\xi_ {3} = \frac {1}{\mathrm {I}} <   | \mathrm {F} | ^ {2} > \cos 4 \psi \qquad \xi_ {1} = \frac {1}{\mathrm {I}} <   | \mathrm {F} | ^ {2} > \sin 4 \psi \qquad \xi_ {2} = \pm \frac {2}{\mathrm {I}} <   | \mathrm {H} | ^ {2} >.
$$

We see finally that the polarization properties of the radiation can be found from the Stokes parameters in a manner very similar to that of electromagnetism.

Fraction of radiation linearly polarized $= \left(\xi_{3}^{2} + \xi_{1}^{2}\right)^{\frac{1}{2}}$

Orientation of linear polarization: tan $4\psi = \xi_1 / \xi_3$

Fraction of radiation circularly polarized $= |\xi_2|$ ( $\xi_2$ positive for right circular polarization)

Fraction of radiation polarized $= (\xi_1^2 + \xi_2^2 + \xi_3^2)^{\frac{1}{2}}$ .

Solution 18.11. The motion is most easily analyzed in a nearly Newtonian coordinate system. The motion of the particles (and the observer) then has the form

$$
\ddot {\mathbf {x}} \sim \mathrm {R x} \sim \dot {\mathbf {h x}}.
$$

Here $R$ denotes the magnitude of the Riemann tensor for the waves and $h$ denotes the (dimensionless) metric perturbations for the waves. Since the waves are weak and oscillatory, the positions are fairly constant so we can integrate the equation of motion to get

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {x} (t) - \mathbf {x} _ {0} = \mathbf {x} _ {0} h (t) + a t + \beta + \mathcal {O} \left(h ^ {2}\right) \\ \mathbf {x} _ {0} = \text {i n i t i a l p o s i t i o n}. \\ \end{array}
$$

If we ignore the higher order corrections, we see that $\alpha$ and $\beta$ must be zero since the particles were at rest in their initial positions before the wave burst arrived. After passage of the waves $h = 0$ and the particles must again be at rest in their initial positions.

Solution 18.12. The normal modes of the rod (taken to be oriented along the $x$ -axis) are found from the equation

$$
\frac {\partial^ {2} \xi}{\partial t ^ {2}} + \frac {1}{r} \frac {\partial \xi}{\partial t} - a ^ {2} \frac {\partial^ {2} \xi}{\partial x ^ {2}} = 0. \tag {1}
$$

Here $\xi(\mathbf{x}, t)$ is the displacement of an element of the rod, $\tau$ is the damping time and $a$ is the speed of sound. The modes are of the form

$$
\xi = e ^ {- i \omega t} e ^ {- t / 2 \tau} u (x) \tag {2}
$$

where

$$
a ^ {2} \frac {d ^ {2} u}{d x ^ {2}} + \left(\omega^ {2} + \frac {1}{4 r ^ {2}}\right) u = 0. \tag {3}
$$

Since the ends of the rod are free,

$$
\left. \frac {\partial \xi}{\partial \mathbf {x}} \right| _ {\mathbf {x} = \pm \mathbf {L} / 2} = 0 \tag {4}
$$

where $\mathbf{L}$ is the length of rod. The solutions of Equation (3) satisfying Equation (4) are

$$
u _ {n} = \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} \sin n \pi x / L, & n \text {o d d} \\ \cos n \pi x / L, & n \text {e v e n} \end{array} \right. \tag {5}
$$

with frequencies $\omega_{\mathbf{n}} = \pi \pi a / L$ if we assume $\omega_{\mathrm{n}} >> 1 / \tau$

Consider a gravitational wave propagating in the $z$ -direction. The force field which it induces is

$$
F _ {j} = - x ^ {k} R _ {j 0 k 0} (t - z). \tag {6}
$$

If we take the rod to be at $z = 0$ , we must add a driving term

$$
\mathbf {F} (t, \mathbf {x}) = - \mathbf {x} \mathrm {R} _ {\mathbf {x} 0 \mathbf {x} 0} (t) \tag {7}
$$

to the right-hand side of Equation (1). We can express the resulting displacement as a superposition of normal modes:

$$
\xi = \sum_ {n} B _ {n} (t) u _ {n} (x). \tag {8}
$$

The $\mathbf{u}_{\mathbf{n}}$ are orthogonal, so Equation (1) gives

$$
\ddot {\mathrm {B}} _ {\mathrm {n}} + \frac {1}{\tau} \dot {\mathrm {B}} _ {\mathrm {n}} + \omega_ {\mathrm {n}} ^ {2} \mathrm {B} _ {\mathrm {n}} = \frac {\int_ {- L / 2} ^ {L / 2} \mathrm {F u} _ {\mathrm {n}} \mathrm {d x}}{\int_ {- L / 2} ^ {L / 2} \mathrm {u} _ {\mathrm {n}} ^ {2} \mathrm {d x}}. \tag {9}
$$

Since $\mathbf{F}$ is antisymmetric about $x = 0$ , only the modes with $n$ odd contribute. For $n$ odd the right hand side of Equation (9) is

$$
- \mathrm {R _ {x 0 x 0} (t) L / (2 \pi^ {2} n ^ {2})}.
$$

If the rod is driven on resonance, i.e. $\mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{x}0\mathbf{x}0}\sim \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{i}\omega_{\mathbf{n}}t}$ , Equation (9) with $\mathbf{B_n}\sim \mathrm{e}^{-\mathrm{i}\omega_{\mathbf{n}}t}$ gives

$$
\left| B _ {n} \right| \propto \left| \frac {\tau}{\omega_ {n} n ^ {2}} \right| \propto \frac {1}{n ^ {3}}. \tag {10}
$$

The energy flux F in the gravitational wave scales as $|R_{\mathbf{x}0 \times 0} / \omega_{\mathbf{n}}|^{2} \propto 1 / \mathfrak{n}^{2}$ , so

$$
s e n s i t i v i t y \propto \frac {\left| B _ {n} \right| ^ {2}}{1 / n ^ {2}} \propto \frac {1}{n ^ {4}}. \tag {11}
$$

Solution 18.13. Inside the material we have

$$
\mathbf {T} _ {\mathbf {G W}, \mu} ^ {0 \mu} = \mathbf {T} _ {\mathbf {G W}, \mathbf {i}} ^ {0 \mathbf {i}} = \mathbf {F} ^ {0}
$$

where $\mathbf{F}^0$ is the rate, per unit volume, at which external forces are doing work on the gravitational waves. Isolate a chunk of the cement of cross section A and put a Gaussian "pill box" around it. From Gauss' theorem in three dimensions we have

$$
\int \mathrm {T} _ {\mathrm {G W}} ^ {0 \mathrm {i}} \mathrm {d} \Sigma_ {\mathrm {i}} = \int \mathrm {T} _ {, \mathrm {i}} ^ {0 \mathrm {i}} \mathrm {d V o l}.
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \left. \right.\left( \right.\mathrm {T} _ {\mathrm {G W}} ^ {0 \mathrm {x}} \left. \right| _ {\text {o u t}} - \mathrm {T} _ {\mathrm {G W}} ^ {0 \mathrm {x}} \left. \right| _ {\text {i n}}\left. \right) \mathrm {A} = \int \mathrm {F} ^ {0} \mathrm {d} \text {V o l}. \\ = \text {r a t e} \\ = - \text {r a t e} \\ \end{array}
$$

and hence:

(rate at which momentum is absorbed per unit area per unit time) = (rate at which energy is absorbed per unit area per unit time).

Solution 18.14. Take the wave to be propagating in the $z$ -direction and the molecular displacement to be in the $x$ -direction. If $\xi$ is the displacement of the molecule, the equation of geodesic deviation gives us a dynamical equation for the molecule's motion. In a complex representation, the equation is the real part of

$$
\ddot {\xi} + \Gamma \dot {\xi} + \omega_ {0} ^ {2} \xi = - R _ {0 x 0} ^ {x} X _ {0} = \frac {1}{2} \ddot {h} _ {x x} X _ {0}
$$

or

$$
\xi (- \omega^ {2} - \mathrm {i} \omega \Gamma + \omega_ {0} ^ {2}) = - \frac {\omega^ {2}}{2} \mathrm {h} _ {\bf x x} X _ {0} = - \frac {\omega^ {2}}{2} A _ {+} X _ {0} \mathrm {e} ^ {- \mathrm {i} \omega t}.
$$

Here $\omega$ is the frequency of the wave, $\Gamma \equiv (\text{damping time})^{-1}$ , and $\mathbf{X}_0$ is the distance in the $x$ -direction of the equilibrium position of the molecule from the center of mass. We can easily solve for $\xi$ ,

$$
\xi = \frac {1}{2} \omega^ {2} \mathrm {A} _ {+} \mathrm {X} _ {0} \mathrm {e} ^ {- \mathrm {i} \omega \mathrm {t}} [ (\omega^ {2} - \omega_ {0} ^ {2}) - \mathrm {i} \omega \Gamma ] / \Delta
$$

$$
\Delta \equiv (\omega^ {2} - \omega_ {0} ^ {2}) ^ {2} + \omega^ {2} \Gamma^ {2}
$$

and for $\dot{\xi}$

$$
\dot {\xi} = - \frac {1}{2} \omega^ {3} \mathrm {A} _ {+} \mathrm {X} _ {0} \mathrm {e} ^ {- \mathrm {i} \omega \mathrm {t}} [ \omega \Gamma + \mathrm {i} (\omega^ {2} - \omega_ {0} ^ {2}) ] / \Delta .
$$

From this we can easily evaluate the rate at which the molecule of mass m absorbs energy:

$$
\begin{array}{l} <   \dot {\mathbf {x}} \mathbf {F} ^ {\mathbf {x}} > = \frac {1}{2} \operatorname {R e} \left\{\left(\dot {\xi}\right) \left(\mathbf {F} ^ {\mathbf {x}}\right) ^ {*} \right\} \\ = \frac {1}{2} \operatorname {R e} \left\{(\dot {\zeta}) \left(- \frac {1}{2} m \omega^ {2} A _ {+} ^ {*} X _ {0} e ^ {i \omega t}\right) \right\} \\ = \frac {\mathrm {m} \omega^ {6}}{8} \Gamma | A _ {+} | ^ {2} X _ {0} ^ {2}. \\ \end{array}
$$

To find the momentum absorption we must recall that there is a longitudinal geodesic deviation, though it is smaller by a factor of order $\nu / c$

$$
\begin{array}{l} F ^ {z} = - m R ^ {z} _ {x x 0} X _ {0} \dot {\xi} = \frac {m}{2} \ddot {h} _ {x x} X _ {0} \dot {\xi} \\ = - \frac {1}{2} m \omega^ {2} X _ {0} R e \left\{A _ {+} e ^ {- i \omega t} \right\} R e \left\{\dot {\xi} \right\}. \\ \end{array}
$$

The time averaged force is then

$$
\begin{array}{l} <   F ^ {z} > = \frac {1}{4} m \omega^ {6} X _ {0} ^ {2} \Gamma <   R e (A _ {+} e ^ {- i \omega t}) \cdot R e (A _ {+} e ^ {- i \omega t}) > \\ = \frac {1}{8} m \omega^ {6} \Gamma | A _ {+} | ^ {2} X _ {0} ^ {2} \\ \end{array}
$$

which demonstrates on a microscopic basis the reason for the result in Problem 18.13.

Solution 18.15. Consider two particles a distance $d$ apart. Their relative velocity fluctuates under the influence of the wave by an amount $\Delta v \approx h\omega d$ . If the two particles collide during this cycle of the wave, their distance must have been of order $d \sim v / \omega$ , so $\Delta v \sim hv$ . On the average then, particles collide with energies fractionally larger by $(\Delta v / v)^2$ than they would in the absence of the wave. Since the collision randomizes their direction of motion, this energy is not subtracted out half a cycle later when the wave is reversed. Another effect also adds of order $(\Delta v / v)^2$ fractional energy per collision: the fact that particles moving toward each other at $v + \Delta v$ have a greater chance of colliding than do particles with $v - \Delta v$ . We conclude that at temperature $T$ every collision is, on the average, increased in energy by order $(\Delta v / v)^2 kT \sim h^2 kT$ . The number of collisions per volume per time is $n_0 v / \ell \sim (n_0 / \ell) (kT / m)^{\frac{1}{2}}$ , where $n_0$ is the number density and $m$ the mass of the particles. Thus the internal energy absorbed per volume per time is approximately

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathrm {E} _ {\text {i n t}}}{\mathrm {d} t} \sim \frac {\mathrm {h} ^ {2}}{\mathrm {n} _ {0} ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {m} ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \ell} \left(\mathrm {n} _ {0} \mathrm {k T}\right) ^ {\frac {3}{2}} \sim \frac {\mathrm {h} ^ {2}}{\mathrm {n} _ {0} ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {m} ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \ell} \mathrm {E} _ {\text {i n t}} ^ {\frac {3}{2}}. \tag {1}
$$

This is integrated to give

$$
E _ {i n t} ^ {\frac {1}{2}} (t) \sim \frac {t _ {0} E _ {i n t} ^ {\frac {1}{2}} (t = 0)}{t _ {0} - t} \tag {2}
$$

where $t_0 \sim (\ell / h^2)(m / kT)^{\frac{1}{2}}$ . Thus, $E_{\mathrm{int}} \to \infty$ in a finite time $t_0$ ; of course this does not happen: our Newtonian treatment breaks down at a time $t' \sim t_0$ when the gas becomes relativistic.

The "front" of the wave loses energy at the rate given by Equation (1). Since its internal energy density is of order $\omega^2\mathrm{h}^2\mathrm{c}^2/\mathrm{G}$ , it is damped in a distance

$$
\begin{array}{l} \ell_ {\text {d a m p i n g}} \sim c \tau_ {\text {d a m p i n g}} \sim \frac {\omega^ {2} h ^ {2} c ^ {3} / G}{h ^ {2} \left(n _ {0} k T\right) ^ {\frac {3}{2}} / \left(n _ {0} ^ {\frac {1}{2}} m ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \ell\right)} \\ \sim \ell \left(\frac {c}{v _ {\text {t h e r m a l}}}\right) ^ {3} \left(\frac {\omega^ {2}}{G m n _ {0}}\right). \\ \end{array}
$$

(The first term in parenthesis approaches unity for a relativistic gas; the second approaches unity for a wave at the “gravitational plasma frequency” of the gas.)

Solution 18.16. The characteristic quadrupole gravitational-wave power of a physical process is $(\mathrm{G} / \mathrm{c}^{5})|\ddot{\mathrm{Q}}|^{2}\sim (\mathrm{G} / \mathrm{c}^{5})\mathrm{M}^{2}\mathrm{v}^{4} / \mathrm{T}^{2}$ where M, v, T are relevant masses, velocities and times for the process. This can be conveniently written as

$$
\mathrm {P} _ {\mathrm {G W}} \sim \frac {\mathrm {G}}{\mathrm {c} ^ {5}} \left(\frac {\mathrm {M v} ^ {2}}{\mathrm {T}}\right) ^ {2} \sim \frac {\mathrm {P} ^ {2}}{3 \times 1 0 ^ {5 9} \mathrm {e r g s / s e c .}}.
$$

Here $\mathrm{P} \sim \mathrm{Mv}^2 / \mathrm{T}$ represents the characteristic "power" of internal energy flows.

For an explosion of energy E, characteristic time $\pmb{\tau}$ the "internal power flow" is $\mathbf{E} / \tau$ . If we assume that this internal power flow couples efficiently to gravitational waves, then in $c = G = 1$ units

$$
\mathrm {P} _ {\mathrm {G W}} \sim (\mathrm {E} / \tau) ^ {2}
$$

and

$$
\mathrm {E} _ {\mathrm {G W}} \sim \mathrm {P} _ {\mathrm {G W}} \tau \sim \mathrm {E} ^ {2} / \tau .
$$

The typical graviton emerging from this explosion will have energy $\hbar \omega \sim \hbar / \tau$ so the number of gravitons emerging will be of order

$$
N \sim \frac {E ^ {2} / \tau}{\hbar / \tau} = \frac {E ^ {2}}{\hbar} \sim \left(\frac {E}{1 0 ^ {1 6} \mathrm {e r g s}}\right) ^ {2}.
$$

Solution 18.17. The mass motions relevant to gravitational wave generation are the collisions of electrons with lattice points, photon scattering, etc. Since the thermal velocities of the electrons greatly exceed their drift velocity, the energy involved in a collision is of order kT. According to the analysis of Problem 18.16, the gravitational wave power generated by one electron should be $(\mathrm{kT} / \tau)^2$ where $\tau$ is the mean time between collisions of the electron with the lattice. The total energy, in gravitational waves, emitted during the bulb lifetime must then be

$$
\mathrm {E} _ {\mathrm {G W}} \sim \mathrm {N} \left(\frac {\mathrm {k T}}{\tau}\right) ^ {2} \mathrm {T} _ {\mathrm {L}} / (1 0 ^ {5 9} \mathrm {e r g s} / \mathrm {s e c .})
$$

$\mathbf{N} =$ number of conduction electrons in filament

$$
\mathrm {T} _ {\mathrm {L}} = \text {b u l b l i f e t i m e} \sim 4 \times 1 0 ^ {6} \text {s e c o n d s}.
$$

The typical graviton generated should have a frequency of roughly $r^{-1}$ so that $n$ , the number of gravitons is of order

$$
n \sim \frac {E _ {G W} ^ {\tau}}{\bar {n}} \sim \frac {N}{r \bar {n}} \frac {(k T) ^ {2} T _ {L}}{1 0 ^ {5 9} \text {e r g s / s e c .}}.
$$

For the light bulb kT must be on the order of an electron volt, since the thermal emission is primarily in the visible, and rough values of N and $\tau$ are $10^{17}$ electrons and $10^{-13}$ seconds. For these values the number of gravitons emitted during the bulb's lifetime is of order $10^{-19}$ .

To estimate the number of gravitons given out by the lightbulb when it is dropped, we assume that the energy of the falling light bulb is converted to kinetic energy of shattering; and we calculate the resulting production of gravitational waves according to the formula in Problem 18.16. If the light bulb weighs 20 grams and is dropped from a height of 1 meter, it gains $\sim 10^6$ ergs. Let us assume that the shattering is characterized by a time of .1 sec, so that the total power in shattering is $10^7$ ergs/sec. If $10\%$ of this is relevant to quadrupole oscillations, then

$$
\mathrm {P} _ {\mathrm {G W}} \sim \frac {(1 0 ^ {6} \mathrm {e r g s / s e c}) ^ {2}}{1 0 ^ {5 9} \mathrm {e r g s / s e c}} \approx 1 0 ^ {- 4 7} \mathrm {e r g s / s e c}
$$

$$
E _ {G W} \sim P _ {G W} \times . 1 \sec \approx 1 0 ^ {- 4 8} \text {e r g s}.
$$

The number of $\pi \omega \sim \pi / .1$ sec $\sim 10^{-26}$ erg gravitons is then on the order of $10^{-22}$ .

Solution 18.18. To linearized order in the gravitational field, the interaction Hamiltonian with a system whose stress-energy tensor is $\mathbf{T}_{\mu \nu}$ is

$$
\mathrm {H} = (8 \pi \mathrm {G}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {T} _ {\mu \nu} \mathrm {h} ^ {\mu \nu}. \tag {1}
$$

We shall use units with $c = \overline{n} = 1$ , so $G^{\frac{1}{2}} = 1.616 \times 10^{-33}$ cm. For the electron in the hydrogen atom,

$$
T _ {\mu \nu} = m _ {e} u _ {\mu} u _ {\nu} = \frac {p _ {\mu} p _ {\nu}}{m _ {e}}; \tag {2}
$$

we work in the transverse-traceless gauge for $\mathfrak{h}^{\mu \nu}$ , so

$$
\mathrm {h} _ {0 \mu} = 0, \quad \mathrm {h} _ {, j} ^ {\mathrm {i j}} = 0 \tag {3}
$$

and thus

$$
\mathrm {H} = (8 \pi \mathrm {G}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \frac {\mathrm {p} _ {\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {p} _ {\mathrm {j}} \mathrm {h} ^ {\mathrm {i j}}}{\mathrm {m} _ {\mathrm {e}}} \tag {4}
$$

Since a graviton has spin 2, the decay must be to an s state. The transition matrix element from a 3d state to a 1s state with the emission of a graviton with wave-vector $\underline{\mathbf{k}}$ and polarization $\lambda$ (for right-hand circular polarization $\lambda = 1$ and for left-hand circular polarization $\lambda = -1$ ) is

$$
\mathrm {T} = <   1 \mathrm {s}; \underline {{\mathrm {k}}}, \lambda | \mathrm {H} | 3 \mathrm {d}; 0 >. \tag {5}
$$

We now expand

$$
h ^ {i j} = \frac {1}{(2 \pi) ^ {\frac {3}{2}}} \int \frac {d ^ {3} k}{(2 \omega) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \sum_ {\lambda} \left(e ^ {- i k \cdot r} e _ {k, \lambda} ^ {i j} a _ {k, \lambda} + e ^ {i k \cdot r} e _ {k, \lambda} ^ {i j *} a _ {k, \lambda} ^ {\dagger}\right). \tag {6}
$$

Here $\omega$ is the frequency of the wave and $e^{ij}$ the polarization tensor. The creation and annihilation operators satisfy the equal-time commutation relations

$$
\left[ \mathbf {a} _ {\mathbf {k}, \lambda}, \mathbf {a} _ {\mathbf {k} ^ {\prime}, \lambda^ {\prime}} ^ {\dagger} \right] = \delta^ {3} (\underline {{\mathbf {k}}} - \underline {{\mathbf {k}}} ^ {\prime}) \delta_ {\lambda \lambda^ {\prime}}
$$

and so

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {a} _ {\mathbf {k}, \lambda} | 0 > = 0 \\ <   \underline {{\mathrm {k}}}, \lambda | a _ {\mathbf {k} ^ {\prime}, \lambda^ {\prime}} ^ {\dagger} | 0 > = \delta^ {3} \left(\underline {{\mathrm {k}}} - \underline {{\mathrm {k}}} ^ {\prime}\right) \delta_ {\lambda \lambda^ {\prime}}. \tag {7} \\ \end{array}
$$

Using Equations (4), (6), and (7) in Equation (5), we get

$$
T = \frac {(8 \pi G) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{m _ {e}} \frac {1}{(2 \pi) ^ {\frac {3}{2}}} \frac {1}{(2 \omega) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} <   1 s | e ^ {i k \cdot r} p _ {i} p _ {j} e ^ {i j ^ {*}} | 3 d >. \tag {8}
$$

Since $\mathrm{kr} \sim (1\mathrm{eV}) \times 10^{-8}\mathrm{cm} \sim 10^{-4}$ , we can make the approximation of putting $\mathbf{e}^{\mathbf{i}\mathbf{k} \cdot \mathbf{r}} = 1$ (cf. the "dipole" approximation for electromagnetic transitions). The transition rate is

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {d} \Gamma = 2 \pi | \mathrm {T} | ^ {2} \times (\text {d e n s i t y o f f i n a l s t a t e s f o r g r a v i t o n}) \\ = 2 \pi | \mathbf {T} | ^ {2} \omega^ {2} d \Omega \tag {9} \\ \end{array}
$$

where $\mathbf{d}\Omega$ is the solid angle into which the graviton is emitted. Hence

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \Gamma}{\mathrm {d} \Omega} = \frac {\mathrm {G} \omega}{\pi \mathrm {m} _ {\mathrm {e}} ^ {2}} | <   1 \mathrm {s} | \mathrm {p} _ {\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {p} _ {\mathrm {j}} \mathrm {e} _ {\mathrm {k}, \lambda} ^ {\mathrm {i j} ^ {*}} | 3 \mathrm {d} > | ^ {2}. \tag {10}
$$

If we use the explicit form of the wave functions, the matrix element in this equation can be evaluated. Since we do not observe the spins of the initial or final states of the atom, we will sum over the final spin states and average over the initial spin states. This is most easily done by introducing the pure spin-2 operator

$$
Q _ {i j} = p _ {i} p _ {j} - \frac {1}{3} \delta_ {i j} p ^ {2}. \tag {11}
$$

Since $\mathbf{e}_{\mathbf{i}}^{\mathbf{i}} = 0$ , we can replace $\mathbf{p}_{\mathbf{i}} \mathbf{p}_{\mathbf{j}}$ by $Q_{ij}$ in Equation (10). The spherical components of $Q_{ij}$ are

$$
\begin{array}{l} Q _ {2} \equiv \frac {1}{2} \left(Q _ {\mathbf {x x}} - Q _ {\mathbf {y y}} + 2 \mathrm {i} Q _ {\mathbf {x y}}\right) \\ Q _ {1} \equiv Q _ {z x} + i Q _ {z y} \\ Q _ {0} \equiv \left(\frac {2}{3}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \left(Q _ {z z} - \frac {1}{2} Q _ {x x} - \frac {1}{2} Q _ {y y}\right) \tag {12} \\ Q _ {- 1} \equiv Q _ {z x} - i Q _ {z y} \\ Q _ {- 2} \equiv \frac {1}{2} \left(Q _ {x x} - Q _ {y y} - 2 i Q _ {x y}\right). \\ \end{array}
$$

Since $\mathbf{e}_{\lambda}^{\mathrm{ij}}$ has x-y components (for $\lambda = \pm 1$ ):

$$
2 ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \left[ \begin{array}{c c} 1 & \pm \mathbf {i} \\ \pm \mathbf {i} & - 1 \end{array} \right]
$$

we find

$$
Q _ {i j} e _ {\lambda} ^ {i j *} = 2 ^ {\frac {1}{2}} Q _ {- 2 \lambda}. \tag {13}
$$

Since $\mathbf{Q}_{\sigma}$ has spin two, the Wigner-Eckart theorem says

$$
<   \mathrm {j} _ {\mathrm {j}} \mathrm {m} _ {\mathrm {j}} | \mathrm {Q} _ {\sigma} | \mathrm {j} _ {\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {m} _ {\mathrm {i}} > = <   \mathrm {j} _ {\mathrm {j}} \| \mathrm {Q} \| \mathrm {j} _ {\mathrm {i}} > <   2 \sigma \mathrm {j} _ {\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {m} _ {\mathrm {i}} | \mathrm {j} _ {\mathrm {j}} \mathrm {m} _ {\mathrm {j}} > \tag {14}
$$

where the "reduced" matrix is defined as

$$
<   j _ {j} \| Q \| j _ {i} > = \sum_ {m _ {i} m _ {j} \sigma} \frac {1}{2 j _ {j} + 1} <   2 \sigma j _ {i} m _ {i} | j _ {j} m _ {j} > <   j _ {j} m _ {j} | Q _ {\sigma} | j _ {i} m _ {i} > \tag {15}
$$

and where $< \mathrm{jm} j_{\mathrm{i}} m_{\mathrm{i}} | j_{\mathrm{j}} m_{\mathrm{j}} >$ denotes a Clebsch-Gordon coefficient. Thus Equations (13) and (14) (with the n quantum number added) give

$$
\left| <   n _ {j} j _ {j} m _ {j} \right| Q _ {i j} e ^ {i j ^ {*}} \left| n _ {i} j _ {i} m _ {i} > \right| ^ {2} = 2 \left| <   n _ {j} j _ {j} \| Q \| n _ {i} j _ {i} > \right| ^ {2} <   2 - 2 \lambda \left. j _ {i} m _ {i} \right| j _ {j} m _ {j} > \left| ^ {2} \right..
$$

Now we sum over $\mathfrak{m}_{\mathbf{j}}$ and average over $\mathfrak{m}_{\mathbf{i}}$ , using

$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {1}{2 \mathrm {j} _ {\mathrm {i}} + 1} \sum_ {\mathrm {m} _ {\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {m} _ {\mathrm {j}}} | <   2 - 2 \lambda \mathrm {j} _ {\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {m} _ {\mathrm {i}} | \mathrm {j} _ {\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {m} _ {\mathrm {j}} > | ^ {2} = \frac {1}{2 \mathrm {j} _ {\mathrm {i}} + 1} \sum_ {\mathrm {m} _ {\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {m} _ {\mathrm {j}}} \frac {(2 \mathrm {j} _ {\mathrm {j}} + 1)}{5} | <   \mathrm {j} _ {\mathrm {j}} - \mathrm {m} _ {\mathrm {j}} \mathrm {j} _ {\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {m} _ {\mathrm {i}} | 2 - 2 \lambda > | ^ {2} \\ = \frac {1}{5} \frac {2 j _ {j} + 1}{2 j _ {i} + 1} \\ \end{array}
$$

and thus

$$
\frac {d \Gamma}{d \Omega} = \frac {G \omega}{\pi m _ {e} ^ {2}} \frac {2}{5} \frac {2 j _ {j} + 1}{2 j _ {i} + 1} \left| <   n _ {j} j _ {j} \| Q \| n _ {i} j _ {i} > \right| ^ {2}. \tag {16}
$$

This is independent of the polarization of the graviton and of the angle of emission. The total rate is obtained by multiplying by 2 (for the two polarization states) and by $4\pi$ (solid angle), giving

$$
\Gamma = \frac {\mathrm {G} \omega}{\mathrm {m} _ {\mathrm {e}} ^ {2}} \frac {1 6}{5} \frac {2 \mathrm {j} _ {\mathrm {j}} + 1}{2 \mathrm {j} _ {\mathrm {i}} + 1} | <   \mathrm {n} _ {\mathrm {j}} \mathrm {j} _ {\mathrm {j}} \| \mathrm {Q} \| \mathrm {n} _ {\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {j} _ {\mathrm {i}} > | ^ {2}. \tag {17}
$$

The reduced matrix element can be evaluated from Equation (14) and any convenient choice of $\mathfrak{m}_{\mathbf{i}}$ and $\mathfrak{m}_{\mathbf{j}}$ . For example,

$$
<   1 \mathbf {s} \| Q \| 3 d > = \frac {<   1 0 0 | Q _ {0} | 3 2 0 >}{<   2 0 2 0 | 0 0 >}. \tag {18}
$$

The Clebsch-Gordon coefficient in Equation (18) is unity. From Equations (11) and (12),

$$
Q _ {0} = \left(\frac {3}{2}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \left(p _ {z} p _ {z} - \frac {1}{3} p ^ {2}\right).
$$

The $p^2$ term does not contribute to the matrix element in Equation (18) because it cannot couple $j = 0$ to $j = 2$ . Thus

$$
<   \mathbf {1 s} \| \mathrm {Q} \| 3 \mathrm {d} > = \left(\frac {3}{2}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} <   1 0 0 | _ {\mathrm {p} _ {z} \mathrm {p} _ {z}} | 3 2 0 >. \tag {19}
$$

In general one would introduce a complete set of states to evaluate the matrix element in Equation (19) as a sum of terms of the form $< 100|\mathfrak{p}_z|\mathfrak{n}> <   \mathfrak{n}|\mathfrak{p}_z|320>$ . Since the $|100\rangle$ wave function is so simple, however, we can use

$$
\mathbf {p} _ {z} \rightarrow - \mathrm {i} \partial / \partial z
$$

so

$$
\begin{array}{l} p _ {z} ^ {2} \rightarrow - \partial^ {2} / \partial z ^ {2} = - \left(\cos \theta \frac {\partial}{\partial r} - \frac {\sin \theta}{r} \frac {\partial}{\partial \theta}\right)\left(\cos \theta \frac {\partial}{\partial r} - \frac {\sin \theta}{r} \frac {\partial}{\partial \theta}\right) \\ = - \left(\cos^ {2} \theta \frac {\partial^ {2}}{\partial r ^ {2}} + \frac {\sin^ {2} \theta}{r} \frac {\partial}{\partial r}\right) \\ \end{array}
$$

when acting on the spherically-symmetric $|100\rangle$ state. Thus

$$
\begin{array}{l} <   3 2 0 | p _ {z} ^ {2} | 1 0 0 > = - \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} r ^ {2} d r \int_ {0} ^ {\pi} \sin \theta d \theta \int_ {0} ^ {2 \pi} d \phi \left(\frac {5}{1 6 \pi}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \frac {(3 \cos^ {2} \theta - 1)}{a ^ {\frac {3}{2}}} \frac {2 (3 0) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{9 5 5} \left(\frac {r}{a}\right) ^ {3} \\ \times \mathrm {e} ^ {- \mathrm {r} / 3 \mathrm {a}} \left(\cos^ {2} \theta \frac {\partial^ {2}}{\partial \mathrm {r} ^ {2}} + \frac {\sin^ {2} \theta}{\mathrm {r}} \frac {\partial}{\partial \mathrm {r}}\right) \frac {1}{(4 \pi) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \frac {2}{\mathrm {a} ^ {\frac {3}{2}}} \mathrm {e} ^ {- \mathrm {r} / \mathrm {a}} \\ = \frac {- 6 ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{1 9 1 a ^ {6}} \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} d r r ^ {5} e ^ {- \frac {4 r}{3 a}} \int_ {0} ^ {\pi} \sin \theta d \theta (3 \cos^ {2} \theta - 1) \left(\frac {\cos^ {2} \theta}{a ^ {2}} - \frac {\sin^ {2} \theta}{r a}\right) \\ = \frac {6 ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{1 9 1 a ^ {6}} \frac {8}{1 5} \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} d r e ^ {- \frac {4 r}{3 a}} \left(\frac {r ^ {5}}{a ^ {2}} + \frac {r ^ {4}}{a}\right) \\ = - \frac {1}{a ^ {2}} \frac {6 ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \times 8 \times 2 4 3 \times 5 7}{1 9 1 \times 1 5 \times 2 \times 2 5 6} \\ = \frac {- . 1 9}{a ^ {2}} \\ \end{array}
$$

where $\mathfrak{a} = (\mathfrak{m}_{\mathbf{e}}\alpha)^{-1}$ is the Bohr radius and $\alpha = 1 / 137$ is the fine structure constant.

$$
\begin{array}{l} \Gamma = \frac {G \omega}{m _ {\mathrm {e}} ^ {2}} \cdot \frac {1 6}{5} \cdot \frac {1}{5} \cdot \frac {3}{2} \cdot \frac {(. 1 9) ^ {2}}{a ^ {4}} \\ = . 3 6 \mathrm {G m} _ {\mathrm {e}} ^ {2} \omega a ^ {4}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Now $\mathbf{G} \mathbf{m}_{\mathbf{e}}^{2} = 1.75 \times 10^{-45}$ and $\omega = 12 \mathrm{eV}$ for $3 \mathrm{p} \rightarrow 1 \mathrm{s}$ . Thus the lifetime is $\Gamma^{-1} = 1.9 \times 10^{38}$ sec.

Solution 18.19. A star, and its thermal graviton flux are spherically symmetric only in the time averaged sense. Since a star's interior is composed of subatomic particles it is clear that, at a given time the star may be spherically symmetric to an astronomer, but to a mathematician, with his much more stringent requirements for symmetry, the star has a fine grain (atomic scale) graininess which makes all symmetry theorems inapplicable. It is, of course, the time dependence of these fine scale

particle inhomogeneities which generate gravitational (and electromagnetic!) radiation.

The multipolarity of the flux is related to the scale of angular asymmetries of the flux. (This should not be confused with the fact that the generation of the waves is locally quadrupole, nor with the fact that the time averaged flux is monopole - i.e. isotropic.) For a star like our Sun most of the graviton flux will be generated in the high temperature $(10^{7}\circ \mathrm{K})$ , dense $(50\mathrm{gm} / \mathrm{cm}^3)$ core. If we take as a characteristic radius of this core one quarter of the sun's radius $\sim 2\times 10^{10}\mathrm{cm}$ and the interparticle distance of $10^{-8}\mathrm{cm}$ implied by the density, we see that the angular scale of asymmetry is $\sim 10^{-18}$ radians. The spherical harmonic expansion of graininess of $10^{-18}$ radians requires $\ell$ values of order $10^{18}$ . In this fine-grain sense then, the flux has characteristically a $2^{10^{18}}$ -pole distribution.

Solution 18.20. In a nearly inertial coordinate system centered at the center of mass, the effect of the Riemann tensor, according to the equation of geodesic deviation, is to accelerate free particles in the following way

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {j}}}{\mathrm {d t} ^ {2}} = - \mathrm {R} _ {\mathrm {j 0 k 0}} \mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {k}} .
$$

We have here ignored the velocity dependent "magnetic" type terms which are smaller by a factor $\mathrm{v} / \mathrm{c}$ . (See solution to Problem 18.14.) This equation makes it clear that the Riemann tensor has a longitudinal effect if and only if one of the spatial indices of $\mathbf{R}_{\mathrm{j0k0}}$ is $z$ , the direction of wave propagation. It follows that $\Psi_{2}, \Psi_{3}, \overline{\Psi}_{3}$ have longitudinal effects but that $\Psi_{4}, \overline{\Psi}_{4}, \Phi_{22}$ are purely transverse.

To investigate the spin of the waves, transform from $x, y$ coordinates to new coordinates $x', y'$ rotated about the $z$ -axis in the positive sense by an angle $\phi$ . Under such a transformation the transformation for the Riemann components is, for instance

$$
R _ {x ^ {\prime} 0 ^ {\prime} z ^ {\prime} 0 ^ {\prime}} = R _ {x 0 z 0} \cos \phi + R _ {y 0 z 0} \sin \phi
$$

$$
\mathbf {R} _ {\mathbf {x} ^ {\prime} \mathbf {0} ^ {\prime} \mathbf {x} ^ {\prime} \mathbf {0} ^ {\prime}} = \mathbf {R} _ {\mathbf {x} 0 \mathbf {x} 0} \cos^ {2} \phi + \mathbf {R} _ {\mathbf {y} 0 \mathbf {y} 0} \sin^ {2} \phi + 2 \sin \phi \cos \phi \mathbf {R} _ {\mathbf {x} 0 \mathbf {y} 0}.
$$

From the form of the transformation for the Riemann components, the transformation laws for the symbols defined in the problem are easily calculated:

$$
\Psi_ {2 ^ {\prime}} \equiv - \frac {1}{6} R _ {z ^ {\prime} 0 ^ {\prime} z ^ {\prime} 0 ^ {\prime}} = - \frac {1}{6} R _ {z 0 z 0} = \Psi_ {2}
$$

$$
\Psi_ {3 ^ {\prime}} = e ^ {i \phi} \Psi_ {3}
$$

$$
\bar {\Psi} _ {3 ^ {\prime}} = e ^ {- i \phi} \Psi_ {3}
$$

$$
\Psi_ {4 ^ {\prime}} = e ^ {2 i \phi} \Psi_ {4}, \quad \overline {{\Psi}} _ {4 ^ {\prime}} = e ^ {- 2 i \phi} \overline {{\Psi}} _ {4}
$$

$$
\Phi_ {2 ^ {\prime} 2 ^ {\prime}} = \Phi_ {2 2}.
$$

Since $\Psi_{2}$ and $\Phi_{22}$ are unaffected by rotation about $z$ , the waves are scalar (spin 0) waves. For $\Psi_{3}$ and $\overline{\Psi}_{3}$ , a rotation of $180^{\circ}$ returns the wave to the same state of polarization (e.g. pure real), so these symbols must correspond to spin 1 waves. The symbols $\Psi_{4}$ and $\overline{\Psi}_{4}$ need only be rotated by $90^{\circ}$ to be in the same polarization state so they represent spin 2 waves. These symbols in fact correspond to the circular polarization states of the waves of general relativity.

Solution 18.21. In the figure are the six polarization modes of a weak, plane, null gravitational wave permitted in the generic metric theory of gravity. The figure shows the displacement that each mode induces on a sphere of test particles. The wave propagates in the $+z$ -direction (arrow at upper right) and has time dependence $\cos(\omega t)$ . Solid line, snapshot at $\omega t = 0$ , the broken line, one at $\omega t = \pi$ . There is no displacement perpendicular to the plane of the figure. The above modes are obtained by using the equation of geodesic deviation

![](images/ec3963e04392702dc8f62ea7739aafdf85e5e54cca83dba6f92caeeafe0e91ee.jpg)

![](images/80bdd4f08f84473f33d62c2a65c4dd6bfb6c45947a76ed4048e193d8190c0a1a.jpg)

![](images/c9d33d295300b9ef58e2f30341a722ef7dd57821f61e5a9ed390a16fe00028d2.jpg)

![](images/d66b025949da6b6f948acca28980cd787c4d28613bac317ebbc0619c47d3c6f8.jpg)

![](images/7a59d1965077520522f8196fb218616493763e7be8dee78ca7175915ea538375.jpg)

![](images/b1431e54aff95090256fdc8991f692adb22c9062c324cbc4c86e0266c2838d92.jpg)

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} ^ {2} \mathrm {x} ^ {\mathrm {k}}}{\mathrm {d t} ^ {2}} \approx \mathrm {x} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \mathrm {R} _ {0 \mathrm {j 0}} ^ {\mathrm {k}}.
$$

Solution 18.22. The function $H$ is determined by the vacuum field equations $R_{\alpha \beta} = 0$ . We first compute the Christoffel symbols from the Lagrangian for geodesics (see Problem 7.25)

$$
\mathbf {L} = \dot {\mathbf {x}} ^ {2} + \dot {\mathbf {y}} ^ {2} - \dot {\mathbf {u}} \dot {\mathbf {v}} + 2 \mathrm {H} \dot {\mathbf {u}} ^ {2}.
$$

From the Euler-Lagrange equation

$$
\frac {d}{d s} \left(\frac {\partial L}{\partial \dot {\mathbf {x}}}\right) - \frac {\partial L}{\partial \mathbf {x}} = 0
$$

we get

$$
\ddot {\mathbf {x}} + \mathrm {H} _ {, \mathbf {x}} \dot {\mathbf {u}} ^ {2} = 0
$$

and therefore $\Gamma_{uu}^{\mathbf{x}} = H_{,x}$ . Similarly $\ddot{y} + H_{,y} \dot{u}^{2} = 0$ implies $\Gamma_{uu}^{\mathbf{y}} = H_{,y}$ and $u = 0$ implies $\Gamma_{\alpha \beta}^{\mathbf{u}} = 0$ . From

$$
\ddot {\mathrm {v}} - 2 \mathrm {H} _ {, \mathrm {u}} \dot {\mathrm {u}} ^ {2} - 4 \mathrm {H} _ {, \mathrm {x}} \dot {\mathrm {x}} \dot {\mathrm {u}} - 4 \mathrm {H} _ {, \mathrm {y}} \dot {\mathrm {y}} \dot {\mathrm {u}} = 0
$$

we get

$$
\Gamma_ {u u} ^ {v} = - 2 H _ {, u} \quad \Gamma_ {x u} ^ {v} = - 2 H _ {, x} \quad \Gamma_ {y u} ^ {v} = - 2 H _ {, y}.
$$

All other $\Gamma$ 's are zero. In the expression (MTW, Equation 8.51b)

$$
\mathrm {R} _ {\alpha \beta} = \Gamma^ {\mu} _ {\alpha \beta , \mu} - (\log (- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}) _ {, \alpha \beta} + (\log (- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}) _ {, \mu} \Gamma^ {\mu} _ {\alpha \beta} - \Gamma^ {\mu} _ {\sigma \beta} \Gamma^ {\sigma} _ {\alpha \mu}
$$

only the first term contributes (note that $(-g)^{\frac{1}{2}} = 1$ ). We find the only nonzero term to be

$$
\mathrm {R} _ {\mathrm {u u}} = \mathrm {H} _ {, \mathrm {x x}} + \mathrm {H} _ {, \mathrm {y y}}.
$$

Any harmonic function in $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{y}$ will therefore satisfy the field equations. For a plane wave, $\mathbf{H}$ is a quadratic function in $\mathbf{x}$ and $\mathbf{y}$ , so that (exercise!) the Riemann tensor is a function of $\mathbf{u}$ alone and has no singularities in the $\mathbf{x} - \mathbf{y}$ plane.

# CHAPTER 19: SOLUTIONS

Solution 19.1. The relevant Newtonian equations are

$$
\nabla^ {2} \Phi = 4 \pi G \rho \quad (\text {g r a v i t a t i o n a l p o t e n t i a l}) \tag {1}
$$

$$
\partial \rho / \partial t + \nabla \cdot (\rho \underline {{v}}) = 0 \quad (\text {c o n t i n u i t y e q u a t i o n}) \tag {2}
$$

$$
\frac {\partial \underline {{v}}}{\partial t} + (\underline {{v}} \cdot \nabla) \underline {{v}} = - \frac {\nabla p}{\rho} - \nabla \Phi \quad \text {(e q u a t i o n o f m o t i o n)}. \tag {3}
$$

If the universe is static and homogeneous, then $\underline{\mathbf{v}} = 0$ , and $\rho$ and $\mathfrak{p}$ are constant in position and time. The solution of Equation (1) is then

$$
\Phi = \frac {2}{3} \pi G \rho (\underline {{r}} \cdot \underline {{r}}) + \underline {{C}} \cdot \underline {{r}} + K
$$

where $\underline{\mathbf{C}}$ and $\mathbf{K}$ are arbitrary constants of integration and $\underline{\mathbf{r}}$ is the position vector from the (arbitrary) origin. Equation (2) is identically zero. Equation (3), however, is contradictory: the left side vanishes since $\underline{\mathbf{v}} = 0$ ; on the right, $\nabla p$ vanishes by homogeneity, but $\nabla \Phi$ is not identically zero for any choice of $\underline{\mathbf{C}}$ , $\mathbf{K}$ . Thus, there is no solution to the equations.

Solution 19.2. Since the spacetime is isotropic everywhere, it is in particular spherically symmetric around some nonsingular observer. But by Birkhoff's theorem (Problem 16.3) the only vacuum spherical solutions are the Schwarzschild metrics, and the only one which is regular at the origin - where our observer is - is the $\mathbf{M} = 0$ case, i.e. flat Minkowski space.

Solution 19.3. By Liouville's theorem the number density of photons in phase space $\mathbf{I}_{\nu} / \nu^3$ is a constant along a light ray, and (see Problem 5.10)

it is also a Lorentz invariant. Using $(\nu_{\text{emitted}} / \nu_{\text{observed}}) = 1 + z$ , We have then

$$
\begin{array}{l} \text {F l u x o b s e r v e d} = \Omega \int I _ {\nu} ^ {\mathrm {o b s}} \mathrm {d} \nu_ {\mathrm {o b s}} = \Omega \int \left(\frac {I _ {\nu} ^ {\mathrm {o b s}}}{\nu_ {\mathrm {o b s}} ^ {3}}\right) \nu_ {\mathrm {o b s}} ^ {3} \mathrm {d} \nu_ {\mathrm {o b s}} \\ = \Omega \int \frac {\mathrm {I} _ {\nu} ^ {\mathrm {e m i t}}}{\nu_ {\mathrm {e m i t}} ^ {3}} \frac {\nu_ {\mathrm {e m i t}} ^ {3} \mathrm {d} \nu_ {\mathrm {e m i t}}}{(1 + z) ^ {4}} = \frac {\Omega}{(1 + z) ^ {4}} \int \mathrm {I} _ {\nu} ^ {\mathrm {e m i t}} \mathrm {d} \nu_ {\mathrm {e m i t}} \\ = \frac {\Omega}{(1 + z) ^ {4}} \frac {1}{\pi} \sigma \mathrm {T} ^ {4} \\ \end{array}
$$

where $\sigma$ is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant. Note that since a redshifted black body spectrum is also a black body spectrum, the flux and spectrum observed is just that which would come from a nearby stationary object of solid angle $\Omega$ and temperature $T / (1 + z)$ .

In all the above it doesn't matter whether the redshift is due to doppler motion, gravitational redshift, or cosmological redshift, because Liouville's theorem holds in general.

Solution 19.4. Homogeneity implies that the scalar curvature $\mathbf{R}$ of the hypersurface must be constant. $\mathbf{R}$ can be found by specializing the formulas of Problem 9.20, or from the formula

$$
R _ {i j} = \Gamma^ {k} _ {i j, k} - (\log | g | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}) _ {, i j} + \Gamma^ {k} _ {i j} (\log | g | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}) _ {, k} - \Gamma^ {k} _ {i m} \Gamma^ {m} _ {j k}
$$

where $|g|^{\frac{1}{2}} = a^3 \mathrm{fr}^2 \sin \theta$ and the $\Gamma$ 's are found from the Lagrangian for geodesics or as in Problem 7.6. This gives

$$
\begin{array}{l} R _ {\theta \theta} = \Gamma^ {r} _ {\theta \theta , r} - (\log (\sin \theta)), _ {\theta \theta} + \Gamma^ {r} _ {\theta \theta} (\log f r ^ {2}), _ {r} - \Gamma^ {\phi} _ {\theta \phi} \Gamma^ {\phi} _ {\theta \phi} - 2 \Gamma^ {\theta} _ {\theta r} \Gamma^ {r} _ {\theta \theta} \\ = - \left(\operatorname {r f} ^ {- 2}\right) _ {, \mathrm {r}} + \operatorname {c o s e c} ^ {2} \theta - \mathrm {r} ^ {- 1} \mathrm {f} ^ {- 3} \left(\mathrm {r} ^ {2} \mathrm {f}\right) _ {, \mathrm {r}} - \cot^ {2} \theta + 2 \mathrm {f} ^ {- 2} \\ = \left[ r ^ {2} \left(1 - f ^ {- 2}\right) \right] _ {, r} (2 r) ^ {- 1}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Since

$$
\mathbf {R} _ {\widehat {\theta} \widehat {\theta}} = \mathbf {R} _ {\theta \theta} (\mathbf {a} ^ {2} \mathbf {r} ^ {2}) ^ {- 1},
$$

and

$$
R _ {\hat {r} \hat {r}} = R _ {\hat {\phi} \hat {\phi}} = R _ {\hat {\theta} \hat {\theta}}
$$

by isotropy, we get

$$
\mathrm {R} = \mathrm {R} _ {\hat {\mathrm {r}} \hat {\mathrm {r}}} + \mathrm {R} \hat {\theta} \hat {\theta} + \mathrm {R} \hat {\phi} \hat {\phi} = 3 \left[ \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \left(1 - \mathrm {f} ^ {- 2}\right) \right], _ {\mathrm {r}} \left(2 \mathrm {a} ^ {2} \mathrm {r} ^ {3}\right) ^ {- 1} \equiv \mathrm {A}
$$

that is

$$
\mathbf {r} ^ {2} \left(1 - \mathrm {f} ^ {- 2}\right) = \mathbf {B r} ^ {4} + \mathbf {C}.
$$

Since $\mathbf{f} \to 1$ as $\mathbf{r} \to 0$ , we have $\mathbf{C} = 0$ . Thus

$$
f ^ {2} = \left(1 - B r ^ {2}\right) ^ {- 1}.
$$

If $\mathbf{B} \neq 0$ , we can scale $\mathbf{r}$ by letting $\mathbf{r}' = |\mathbf{B}|^{\frac{1}{2}}\mathbf{r}$ . The metric then takes the prescribed form with radial coordinate $\mathbf{r}'$ .

Solution 19.5.

(a) Let

$$
\mathbf {r} = \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} \sin \chi & \quad \mathrm {k} = + 1 \\ \chi & \quad \mathrm {k} = 0 \\ \sinh \chi & \quad \mathrm {k} = - 1 \end{array} \right.
$$

then

$$
\mathrm {d} \mathbf {r} = \left\{ \begin{array}{l} \cos \chi \quad \mathrm {d} \chi \\ \mathrm {d} \chi \\ \cosh \chi \quad \mathrm {d} \chi \end{array} \right.
$$

and

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathrm {r} ^ {2}}{1 - \mathrm {k r} ^ {2}} = \left\{ \begin{array}{l} \mathrm {d} \chi^ {2} \\ \mathrm {d} \chi^ {2} \\ \mathrm {d} \chi^ {2} \end{array} \right.
$$

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {r} ^ {2}}{1 - \mathrm {k r} ^ {2}} + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \mathrm {d} \Omega^ {2} = \mathrm {d} \chi^ {2} + \Sigma^ {2} (\chi) \mathrm {d} \Omega^ {2},
$$

where

$$
\Sigma^ {2} (\chi) = \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} \sin^ {2} \chi , & k = + 1 \\ \chi^ {2}, & k = 0 \\ \sinh^ {2} \chi , & k = - 1. \end{array} \right.
$$

(b) Let $\mathrm{dt} = \mathbb{R}(\eta)\mathrm{d}\eta$ define a transformation from the variable $t$ to the variable $\eta$ . Then

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - \mathrm {d t} ^ {2} + \mathrm {R} ^ {2} (\mathrm {t}) (\mathrm {d} \chi^ {2} + \Sigma^ {2} \mathrm {d} \Omega^ {2}) \\ = \mathbf {R} ^ {2} (\eta) (- \mathrm {d} \eta^ {2} + \mathrm {d} \chi^ {2} + \Sigma^ {2} \mathrm {d} \Omega^ {2}). \\ \end{array}
$$

Solution 19.6. From the Robertson-Walker metric (in "trigonometric" form), the metric for the spacelike 3-surfaces is

$$
\mathrm {d} \sigma^ {2} = \mathrm {a} ^ {2} \left[ \mathrm {d} \chi^ {2} + \sin^ {2} \chi \left(\mathrm {d} \theta^ {2} + \sin^ {2} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2}\right) \right].
$$

By analogy with the metric for 2-spheres, it is easy to guess that this metric can be represented as a 3-sphere embedded in a Euclidean 4-space with cartesian coordinates $W$ , $X$ , $Y$ , $Z$ . In particular, if we define

$$
\begin{array}{l} W = a \cos x \\ Z = a \sin \chi \cos \theta \\ \mathbf {X} = \mathbf {a} \sin \chi \sin \theta \cos \phi \\ \mathbf {Y} = \mathbf {a} \sin \chi \sin \theta \sin \phi \\ \end{array}
$$

(which satisfy $\mathbf{W}^2 +\mathbf{Z}^2 +\mathbf{X}^2 +\mathbf{Y}^2 = \mathbf{A}^2)$ , the metric becomes

$$
\mathrm {d} \sigma^ {2} = \mathrm {d} W ^ {2} + \mathrm {d} Z ^ {2} + \mathrm {d} X ^ {2} + \mathrm {d} Y ^ {2}.
$$

In Euclidean 4-space one can find two planes (e.g. X-Y and W-Z) which intersect only at the origin $(a = 0)$ . Thus, a rotation of the X-Y plane changes all X and Y coordinates except the origin and leaves all W-Z coordinates fixed; and vice versa for W-Z rotations. Therefore, a combination of both rotations leaves no points fixed except the

origin and (for nonzero a) takes all points of the 3-sphere into different points.

Solution 19.7. In some local proper frame suppose the bullet passes by with a proper velocity $\mathbf{V}$ . When it has moved a proper distance $\mathbf{dr}$ farther, it passes a cosmological observer whose velocity relative to the first proper frame is

$$
\delta \mathbf {V} = \mathbf {H} d \mathbf {r} = \mathbf {H} \mathbf {V} d t = \frac {\mathbf {R}}{\mathbf {R}} \mathbf {V} d t = \mathbf {V} \frac {d \mathbf {R}}{R}.
$$

This observer sees it moving (according to the velocity addition formula) at velocity

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {V} ^ {\prime} = \frac {\mathbf {V} - \delta \mathbf {V}}{1 - \mathbf {V} \delta \mathbf {V}} = \mathbf {V} - (1 - \mathbf {V} ^ {2}) \delta \mathbf {V} + \mathcal {O} (\delta \mathbf {V}) ^ {2} \\ = V - \left(1 - V ^ {2}\right) V \frac {d R}{R} + \mathcal {O} (\delta V) ^ {2}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Thus, $\mathrm{dV} / \mathrm{dR} = -(1 - V^2)V / R$ which is integrable, giving $\gamma \mathbf{V}\equiv (1 - V^{2})^{-\frac{1}{2}}\mathbf{V}$ $= \text{constant} /\mathbb{R} = \text{constant}\times (1 + z)$ . So $\mathbf{V}_1$ and $\mathbf{V}_2$ are related by $\gamma_2\mathbf{V}_2 / \gamma_1\mathbf{V}_1 = (1 + z)^{-1}$ . For a particle with nonzero rest mass, this result says that the relativistic momentum redshifts away as one power of $(1 + z)$ as the universe expands. For a photon, in the limit $\mathbf{V}\rightarrow 1$ , $\gamma \mathbf{V}\to \mathbf{h}\nu$ and we obtain the usual redshift factor. (Note that we have not taken into account the slowing down of the bullet due to the gravitational attraction of the cosmological matter at rest in the proper frame. The reader can verify that this is a higher order effect, with $\mathrm{dV}\propto (\mathrm{dr})^2$ , so it does not affect the differential equation for $\mathrm{dV} / \mathrm{dR}$ .

Solution 19.8. Start with the $\eta, \chi$ form of the metric

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = \mathrm {R} ^ {2} (\eta) \left[ - \mathrm {d} \eta^ {2} + \mathrm {d} \chi^ {2} + \Sigma^ {2} (\chi) \mathrm {d} \Omega^ {2} \right] \tag {1}
$$

(3rd form in Problem 19.5), where $\Sigma(\chi) = \sinh \chi$ , $\chi$ , $\sin \chi$ for $k = -1, 0, +1$ . Since conformal transformations preserve light cones, a natural first step is to go to null coordinates, e.g.

$$
u = \frac {1}{2} (\eta + \chi) \tag {2}
$$

$$
v = \frac {1}{2} (\eta - \chi)
$$

The metric now becomes

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = \mathrm {R} ^ {2} (\mathrm {u} + \mathrm {v}) [ - 4 \mathrm {d u d v} + \Sigma^ {2} (\mathrm {u} - \mathrm {v}) \mathrm {d} \Omega^ {2} ]. \tag {3}
$$

We now try a transformation which preserves the “‘nullness’” of the coordinates, and also preserves the symmetry between u and v:

$$
\begin{array}{l} a = \mathrm {g} (\mathrm {u}) \quad \mathrm {u} = \mathrm {f} (\alpha) \tag {4} \\ \beta = \mathbf {g} (\mathbf {v}) \quad \mathbf {v} = \mathbf {f} (\beta) \\ \end{array}
$$

where $g$ is the inverse function to $f$ . With this transformation, Equation (3) becomes

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = \mathrm {R} ^ {2} (\mathrm {u} + \mathrm {v}) \left[ - 4 \mathrm {f} ^ {\prime} (\alpha) \mathrm {f} ^ {\prime} (\beta) \mathrm {d} \alpha \mathrm {d} \beta + \Sigma^ {2} (\mathrm {u} - \mathrm {v}) \mathrm {d} \Omega^ {2} \right] \\ = \mathrm {R} ^ {2} (\mathrm {u} + \mathrm {v}) \mathrm {f} ^ {\prime} (\alpha) \mathrm {f} ^ {\prime} (\beta) \left[ - 4 \mathrm {d} \alpha \mathrm {d} \beta + \frac {\sum^ {2} (\mathrm {u} - \mathrm {v})}{\mathrm {f} ^ {\prime} (\alpha) \mathrm {f} ^ {\prime} (\beta)} \mathrm {d} \Omega^ {2} \right]. \tag {5} \\ \end{array}
$$

If the metric is to be conformally flat, the term in brackets must have the flat space form $[-4\mathrm{d}\alpha \mathrm{d}\beta + (\alpha - \beta)^2\mathrm{d}\Omega^2]$ . Using $\mathbf{f}'(\alpha) = \frac{\mathrm{d}\mathbf{u}}{\mathrm{d}\alpha} = \left(\frac{\mathrm{d}\alpha}{\mathrm{d}\mathbf{u}}\right)^{-1} = [\mathbf{g}'(\mathbf{u})]^{-1}$ , we get the condition

$$
\mathbf {g} ^ {\prime} (\mathbf {u}) \mathbf {g} ^ {\prime} (\mathbf {v}) [ \boldsymbol {\Sigma} (\mathbf {u} - \mathbf {v}) ] ^ {2} = [ \mathbf {g} (\mathbf {u}) - \mathbf {g} (\mathbf {v}) ] ^ {2}. \tag {6}
$$

For the case $k = 0$ , we have $\Sigma(u - v) = u - v$ , so $g(x) = x$ is a solution by inspection. For the other cases Equation (6) can be solved by first finding the solution for $v$ close to $u$ ; that is, for $v = u + \epsilon$ . Expanding Equation (6) in a Taylor series gives

$$
\begin{array}{l} \left(\mathrm {g} ^ {\prime}\right) ^ {2} \left[ 1 + \epsilon \mathrm {g} ^ {\prime \prime} / \mathrm {g} ^ {\prime} + \epsilon^ {2} \mathrm {g} ^ {\prime \prime \prime} / (2 \mathrm {g} ^ {\prime}) \dots \right] \epsilon^ {2} \left(1 - k \epsilon^ {2} / 6 \dots\right) ^ {2} \\ = \epsilon^ {2} (\mathrm {g} ^ {\prime}) ^ {2} \left[ 1 + \epsilon \mathrm {g} ^ {\prime \prime} / (2 \mathrm {g} ^ {\prime}) + \epsilon^ {2} \mathrm {g} ^ {\prime \prime \prime} / (6 \mathrm {g} ^ {\prime}) \dots \right] ^ {2}, \\ \end{array}
$$

so that with $\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{g}'$ , $\mathbf{q} = \mathbf{p}'$ and $\mathbf{k} = +1$ , we must solve

$$
2 q d q / d p - 4 p = 3 q ^ {2} / p.
$$

This Bernoulli differential equation gives

$$
q = p (A p - 4) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}, \quad A = c o n s t a n t,
$$

and integrating twice we get

$$
\begin{array}{l} u + B = \tan^ {- 1} (A p / 4 - 1) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \\ \mathbf {g} (\mathfrak {u}) = \mathbf {C} \tan (\mathfrak {u} + \mathbf {B}) + \mathbf {D}. \\ \end{array}
$$

We can now verify by substitution that this is in fact the general solution to Equation (6). Without loss of generality we can take $\mathbf{g} = \tan u$ . For $k = -1$ , we can similarly find $\mathbf{g} = \tanh u$ . With these solutions, Equation (6) turns into the trigonometric identities

$$
\sec u \sec v \sin (u - v) = \tan u - \tan v
$$

sech u sech v sinh (u-v) = tanh u - tanh v

and the metric of Equation (5) becomes

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = \frac {\mathrm {R} ^ {2} \left[ \tan^ {- 1} (\alpha) + \tan^ {- 1} (\beta) \right]}{\left(1 + \alpha^ {2}\right) \left(1 + \beta^ {2}\right)} \{- 4 \mathrm {d} \alpha \mathrm {d} \beta + (\alpha - \beta) ^ {2} \mathrm {d} \Omega^ {2} \}
$$

or

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = \frac {\mathrm {R} ^ {2} \left[ \tanh  ^ {- 1} (\alpha) + \tanh  ^ {- 1} (\beta) \right]}{\left(1 - \alpha^ {2}\right) \left(1 - \beta^ {2}\right)} \{- 4 \mathrm {d} \alpha \mathrm {d} \beta + (\alpha - \beta) ^ {2} \mathrm {d} \Omega^ {2} \}
$$

which is manifestly conformally flat. A conformally flat metric has zero Weyl tensor, so the Riemann tensor is only composed of the Ricci tensor and scalar curvature (see the introduction to Chapter 9).

$$
\mathsf {R} _ {\gamma \delta} ^ {\alpha \beta} = 2 \delta_ {[ \gamma} ^ {[ \alpha} \mathsf {R} _ {\delta ]} ^ {\beta ]} - \frac {1}{3} \delta_ {[ \gamma} ^ {[ \alpha} \delta_ {\delta ]} ^ {\beta ]} \mathsf {R}. \tag {8}
$$

With this the Einstein equation

$$
\mathrm {R} _ {\beta} ^ {\alpha} = 8 \pi \left(\mathrm {T} _ {\beta} ^ {\alpha} - \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {g} _ {\beta} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {T}\right)
$$

and the stress energy tensor

$$
\mathbf {T} _ {\beta} ^ {\alpha} = (\rho + \mathrm {p}) \mathbf {u} ^ {\alpha} \mathbf {u} _ {\beta} + \mathrm {p g} _ {\beta} ^ {\alpha}
$$

give $\mathbf{R}^{\alpha \beta}_{\gamma \delta}$ in terms of $\mathbf{g}_{\mu \nu},\mathfrak{u}^{\mu},\rho ,\mathfrak{p}$

Solution 19.9. If $D$ is the physical size of an object, and $\delta$ is its angle subtended, $d_A \equiv D / \delta$ . From the figure, and the Robertson-Walker metric, we see that $D = R(t)r_1\delta$ , so $d_A = r_1R(t_1)$ . If the object is moving transversely at a proper velocity $V$ and with an apparent angular motion

![](images/7daeecf98632c84374f02e5c441af77c74ee7ffdbf8525c58ae5cd766797240f.jpg)

$\mathrm{d}\delta /\mathrm{d}t$ , then $\mathbf{d}_{\mathbf{M}}\equiv \mathbf{V} / (\mathbf{d}\delta /\mathbf{d}t)$ . Let $t^\prime$ denote time measured at the emission of photons. Since $\mathrm{V} = \mathrm{d}(\mathrm{R}(t_1)\mathrm{r}_1\delta) / \mathrm{dt}'$ , and since $\mathrm{dt}' / \mathrm{dt} = \mathrm{R}(t_1) / \mathrm{R}_0$ because of the cosmological redshift, and noting that $\mathbb{R}(t_1)$ can be considered constant since its change induces no transverse motion, we get $\mathbf{d}_{\mathbf{M}} = \mathbf{R}_{0}\mathbf{r}_{1}$ . If the object has an intrinsic luminosity L, and we receive a flux $\ell$ , then $\mathtt{d_L}\equiv (\mathtt{L} / 4\pi \ell)^{\frac{1}{2}}$ . In a time dt' it emits an energy Ldt'. This energy is redshifted to the present by a factor $\mathbb{R}(t_1) / \mathbb{R}_0$ , and is now distributed over a sphere of proper area $4\pi (\mathfrak{r}_1\mathbb{R}_0)^2$ (see figure). Thus, $\ell = (\mathrm{Ldt'}\mathrm{R} / \mathrm{R}_0)(4\pi \mathrm{r}_1\mathrm{R}_0)^{-2} / \mathrm{dt}$ and $\mathtt{d}_{\mathtt{L}} = \mathtt{R}_{0}^{2}\mathtt{r}_{1} / \mathtt{R}(\mathtt{t}_{1})$ . Using $\mathbb{R}_0 / \mathbb{R}(\mathfrak{t}_1) =$ $1 + z$ , we have now obtained $(1 + z)^2\mathtt{d}_{\mathtt{A}} = (1 + z)\mathtt{d}_{\mathtt{M}} = \mathtt{d}_{\mathtt{L}}$

Solution 19.10. From the solution to Problem 19.9 we have $\mathsf{d}_{\mathbf{L}} = \mathsf{R}_0^2\mathsf{r}_1 / \mathsf{R}(\mathsf{t}_1)$ where $\mathbf{r}_1$ is the radial coordinate of the object and $\mathsf{R}(\mathsf{t}_1)$ is the scale factor at the time $\mathbf{t}_1$ at which the light was emitted. Since $\mathbf{H}_0 \equiv \dot{\mathbf{R}} / \mathbf{R}$ and $\mathbf{q}_0 \equiv -\ddot{\mathbf{R}}\mathbf{R} / \dot{\mathbf{R}}^2$ , the first terms in the power series expansion of $\mathbf{R}$ are

$$
\mathrm {R} (t) = \mathrm {R} _ {0} \left(1 + \mathrm {H} _ {0} \left(t - t _ {0}\right) - \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {q} _ {0} \mathrm {H} _ {0} ^ {2} \left(t - t _ {0}\right) ^ {2} + \dots\right). \tag {1}
$$

We can eliminate the factor $\mathbb{R}(t_1)$ from $d_L$ using $\mathbb{R}_0 / \mathbb{R}(t_1) = 1 + z$ , but we still need to find expressions for $\mathbb{R}_0$ and for $r_1$ . Setting $ds^2 = 0$ to get the path of light rays in the Robertson-Walker metric gives

$$
\int_ {t _ {1}} ^ {t _ {0}} \frac {\mathrm {d} t}{R (t)} = \int_ {0} ^ {r _ {1}} \frac {\mathrm {d} r}{(1 - \mathrm {k r} ^ {2}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \tag {2}
$$

Integrating Equation (2) to the lowest 2 orders in $(t_0 - t_1)$ and $r_1$ by means of Equation (1) gives

$$
r _ {1} = \frac {1}{R _ {0}} \left[ \left(t _ {0} - t _ {1}\right) + \frac {1}{2} H _ {0} \left(t _ {0} - t _ {1}\right) ^ {2} + \dots \right] \tag {3}
$$

and inverting Equation (1) to get $t - t_0$ in terms of $1 + z = R_0 / R(t_1)$

$$
\mathrm {g i v e s} \quad \mathrm {R} _ {0} \mathrm {r} _ {1} = \frac {1}{\mathrm {H} _ {0}} \left[ z - \frac {1}{2} (1 + \mathrm {q} _ {0}) z ^ {2} + \dots \right]
$$

so that

$$
\mathrm {d} _ {\mathbf {L}} = (1 + z) \mathrm {R} _ {0} \mathrm {r} _ {1} = \frac {1}{\mathrm {H} _ {0}} \left[ z + \frac {1}{2} \left(1 - \mathrm {q} _ {0}\right) z ^ {2} + \dots \right]
$$

or equivalently

$$
\ell = \frac {\mathrm {L}}{4 \pi \mathrm {d} _ {\mathrm {L}} ^ {2}} = \frac {\mathrm {L H} _ {0} ^ {2}}{4 \pi z ^ {2}} [ 1 + (\mathrm {q} _ {0} - 1) z + \dots ].
$$

Solution 19.11.

(a) Let $\mathfrak{n}(\mathfrak{t}_1)$ be the number of sources per unit volume at time $\mathfrak{t}_1$ . The volume element is

$$
\left| ^ {(3)} \mathbf {g} \right| ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d r} _ {1} \mathrm {d} \theta_ {1} \mathrm {d} \phi_ {1} = \mathrm {R} ^ {3} (\mathrm {t} _ {1}) (1 - \mathrm {k r} _ {1} ^ {2}) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {r} _ {1} ^ {2} \mathrm {d r} _ {1} \sin \theta_ {1} \mathrm {d} \theta_ {1} \mathrm {d} \phi_ {1}.
$$

Thus the number of sources between $\mathbf{r}_1$ and $\mathbf{r}_1 + \mathrm{d}\mathbf{r}_1$ at time $t_1$ is

$$
\mathrm {d} \mathbf {N} = 4 \pi \mathrm {R} ^ {3} \left(\mathrm {t} _ {1}\right) \left(1 - \mathrm {k r} _ {1} ^ {2}\right) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {r} _ {1} ^ {2} \mathrm {n} \left(\mathrm {t} _ {1}\right) \mathrm {d r} _ {1}.
$$

The quantities $\mathbf{r}_1$ and $\mathbf{t}_1$ are related by the equation for null rays propagating in the Robertson-Walker metric (Equation (2) in the solution of Problem 19.10) i.e. $\mathbf{r}_1 = \mathbf{r}(\mathbf{t}_1)$ where

$$
\mathrm {d r} _ {1} = (1 - \mathrm {k r} _ {1} ^ {2}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d t} _ {1} / \mathrm {R} (t _ {1}).
$$

We have, therefore

$$
d N = 4 \pi R ^ {2} \left(t _ {1}\right) r ^ {2} \left(t _ {1}\right) n \left(t _ {1}\right) | d t _ {1} |
$$

and

$$
\mathrm {N} (z) = \int_ {\mathrm {t} _ {z}} ^ {\mathrm {t} _ {0}} 4 \pi \mathrm {R} ^ {2} \left(\mathrm {t} _ {1}\right) \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \left(\mathrm {t} _ {1}\right) \mathrm {n} \left(\mathrm {t} _ {1}\right) \mathrm {d t} _ {1}
$$

where $t_{z}$ , the cosmological time corresponding to redshift $z$ , is defined implicitly by

$$
\frac {\mathrm {R} \left(\mathrm {t} _ {z}\right)}{\overline {{\mathrm {R} \left(\mathrm {t} _ {0}\right)}}} = \frac {1}{1 + z}. \tag {1}
$$

Since the number density of sources is conserved, we have

$$
\mathrm {n} (t) \mathrm {R} ^ {3} (t) = \text {c o n s t a n t}.
$$

(This follows from $(\mathrm{nu}^{\mu})_{;\mu} = 0)$ and hence

$$
\mathbf {N} (\mathbf {z}) = 4 \pi \mathfrak {n} (\mathfrak {t} _ {0}) \mathsf {R} ^ {3} (\mathfrak {t} _ {0}) \int_ {\mathfrak {t} _ {z}} ^ {\mathfrak {t} _ {0}} \mathsf {R} ^ {- 1} (\mathfrak {t} _ {1}) \mathsf {r} ^ {2} (\mathfrak {t} _ {1}) \mathsf {d t} _ {1}.
$$

If $z$ is small, $t_0 \approx t_z$ , and we can use the expansions

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathsf {R} (\mathsf {t} _ {1}) = \mathsf {R} (\mathsf {t} _ {0}) [ \mathbf {1} - \mathbf {H} _ {0} (\mathsf {t} _ {0} - \mathsf {t} _ {1}) + \dots ] \\ \mathrm {r} \left(t _ {1}\right) = \frac {t _ {0} - t _ {1}}{\mathrm {R} \left(t _ {0}\right)} \left[ 1 + \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {H} _ {0} \left(t _ {0} - t _ {1}\right) + \dots \right] \tag {2} \\ \end{array}
$$

(Solution 19.10, Equations (1) and (3)). Thus for small $z$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {N} (z) = 4 \pi \mathrm {n} \left(t _ {0}\right) \int_ {t _ {z}} ^ {t _ {0}} \left(t _ {0} - t _ {1}\right) ^ {2} [ 1 + 2 \mathrm {H} _ {0} \left(t _ {0} - t _ {1}\right) + \dots ] d t _ {1} \\ = \frac {4 \pi}{3} \mathrm {n} (t _ {0}) (t _ {0} - t _ {z}) ^ {3} \left[ 1 + \frac {3}{2} \mathrm {H} _ {0} (t _ {0} - t _ {z}) + \dots \right]. \\ \end{array}
$$

To evaluate $t_0 - t_z$ to $\mathcal{O}(z^2)$ from Equation (1) we need the next term in Equation (2):

$$
\mathsf {R} (\mathsf {t} _ {z}) = \mathsf {R} (\mathsf {t} _ {0}) \left[ 1 - \mathsf {H} _ {0} (\mathsf {t} _ {0} - \mathsf {t} _ {z}) - \frac {1}{2} \mathsf {q} _ {0} \mathsf {H} _ {0} ^ {2} (\mathsf {t} _ {0} - \mathsf {t} _ {z}) ^ {2} + \dots \right] .
$$

This gives

$$
\mathrm {H} _ {0} (\mathrm {t} _ {0} - \mathrm {t} _ {z}) = z \left[ 1 - z \left(1 + \frac {\mathrm {q} _ {0}}{2}\right) + \dots \right]
$$

and so, finally,

$$
\mathrm {N} (z) = \frac {4 \pi}{3} \frac {\mathrm {n} \left(\mathrm {t} _ {0}\right)}{\mathrm {H} _ {0} ^ {3}} z ^ {3} \left[ 1 - \frac {3}{2} z \left(1 + \mathrm {q} _ {0}\right) + \dots \right].
$$

Note that the field equations did not enter to this order in $\mathbf{z}$ .

(b) The flux received at the Earth is given by

$$
S = \frac {L R ^ {2} \left(t _ {1}\right)}{4 \pi r _ {1} ^ {2} R ^ {4} \left(t _ {0}\right)} \tag {3}
$$

(cf. Problem 19.9, where S was called $\ell$ ). Thus, as in part (a),

$$
N (S) = \int_ {t _ {s}} ^ {t _ {0}} 4 \pi R ^ {2} \left(t _ {1}\right) r ^ {2} \left(t _ {1}\right) n \left(t _ {1}\right) d t _ {1} \tag {4}
$$

where, from Equation (3),

$$
\frac {\mathrm {r} ^ {2} \left(\mathrm {t} _ {\mathrm {s}}\right)}{\mathrm {R} ^ {2} \left(\mathrm {t} _ {\mathrm {s}}\right)} = \frac {\mathrm {L}}{4 \pi \mathrm {S R} ^ {4} \left(\mathrm {t} _ {0}\right)}. \tag {5}
$$

When $S$ is large, $t_0 - t_s$ is small and expanding the integral in Equation (4) as in part (a) gives

$$
\mathbf {N} (\mathbf {S}) = \frac {4 \pi}{3} \left. \mathfrak {n} (\mathfrak {t} _ {0}) (\mathfrak {t} _ {0} - \mathfrak {t} _ {\mathrm {s}}) ^ {3} \left[ 1 + \frac {3}{2} \mathbf {H} _ {0} (\mathfrak {t} _ {0} - \mathfrak {t} _ {\mathrm {s}}) + \dots \right] \right..
$$

Inserting the expansions for $\mathbf{r}(t_{\mathbf{s}})$ and $\mathbb{R}(t_{\mathbf{s}})$ in Equation (5) gives

$$
\mathbf {t} _ {0} - \mathbf {t} _ {\mathrm {s}} = \left(\frac {\mathrm {L}}{4 \pi \mathrm {S}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \left[ 1 - \frac {3}{2} \mathrm {H} _ {0} \left(\frac {\mathrm {L}}{4 \pi \mathrm {S}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} + \dots \right]
$$

and so

$$
\mathrm {N (S)} = \frac {4 \pi}{3} \left. \mathrm {n (t _ {0})} \left(\frac {\mathrm {L}}{4 \pi \mathrm {S}}\right) ^ {\frac {3}{2}} \left[ 1 - 3 \mathrm {H _ {0}} \left(\frac {\mathrm {L}}{4 \pi \mathrm {S}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} + \dots \right] \right..
$$

Solution 19.12. Choose a new radial coordinate $\chi$ , defined by $\mathrm{d}\chi^2 = \mathrm{d}\mathbf{r}^2 / (1 - \mathbf{kr}^2)$ . Then for a radial photon $\mathrm{d}\theta = \mathrm{d}\phi = 0$ and the metric coefficients are independent of $\chi$ along the ray. Therefore $\chi$ is an ignorable coordinate, and $\mathfrak{p}_{\chi} =$ constant along the ray (see Problem 7.13). Raising this index with the metric gives

$$
\mathbf {p} ^ {\mathcal {X}} = \mathbf {C} / \mathbf {R} ^ {2} (t)
$$

where $\mathbf{C}$ is some constant. But $p^{\chi} = d\chi /d\lambda$ , so

$$
\mathrm {d} \lambda = \mathrm {C} ^ {- 1} \mathrm {R} ^ {2} (\mathrm {t}) \mathrm {d} \chi = \mathrm {C} ^ {- 1} \frac {\mathrm {R} ^ {2} (\mathrm {t}) \mathrm {d r}}{\left(1 - \mathrm {k r} ^ {2}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}
$$

Note that since $\mathbf{R}$ is a function of $t$ it is therefore a function of $r$ along a past null ray.

Solution 19.13. In the comoving orthonormal frame we have $\mathbf{T}_{\hat{\theta}}^{\hat{0}} = -\rho$ , and $\mathbf{T}_{\hat{\mathbf{r}}}^{\hat{\mathbf{r}}} = \mathbf{T}_{\hat{\phi}}^{\hat{\phi}} = \mathbf{T}_{\hat{\theta}}^{\hat{\theta}} = \mathbf{p}$ . The trace-reversed stress energy tensor $\overline{\mathbf{T}}$ thus has components $\overline{\mathbf{T}}_{\hat{\theta}}^{\hat{0}} = -\frac{1}{2} (\rho + 3\mathbf{p})$ , and $\overline{\mathbf{T}}_{\hat{\mathbf{t}}}^{\hat{\mathbf{i}}} = \frac{1}{2} (\rho - \mathbf{p})$ . We equate this to $1/8\pi G$ times the Ricci tensor. The Ricci tensor can be calculated e.g. from the results of Problem 9.20 (general spherically symmetric metric). The components are:

$$
R _ {\hat {0}} ^ {\hat {0}} = 3 \ddot {R} / R
$$

$$
R ^ {\hat {1}} _ {\hat {1}} = \frac {1}{R ^ {2}} (R \ddot {R} + 2 \dot {R} ^ {2} + 2 k).
$$

The equations in the problem now follow immediately. If, equivalently, the Einstein tensor $\mathbf{G}_{\mu \nu}$ (trace reverse Ricci tensor) had been equated to $8\pi \mathbf{T}_{\mu \nu}$ , two linear combinations of the equations would have been obtained.

Solution 19.14. Simply eliminating $\overline{\mathbf{R}}$ from the simultaneous equations in Problem 19.13 gives the first equation here. The second follows from this, from the identity $\frac{1}{2} \mathrm{d}[(\dot{\mathbf{R}})^2] / \mathrm{d}\mathbf{R} = \ddot{\mathbf{R}}$ , and from Equation (1) of Problem 19.13:

$$
\frac {1}{2} \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} \mathrm {R}} \left(\frac {8 \pi \mathrm {G}}{3} \rho \mathrm {R} ^ {2}\right) = \frac {1}{2} \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} \mathrm {R}} (\dot {\mathrm {R}}) ^ {2} = \ddot {\mathrm {R}} = - \frac {4}{3} \pi \mathrm {G} (\rho + 3 \mathrm {p}) \mathrm {R}
$$

$$
\frac {d}{d R} (\rho R ^ {2}) = - (\rho + 3 p) R
$$

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} R} \left(\rho R ^ {3}\right) = - 3 p R ^ {2}.
$$

Solution 19.15. Equation (1) follows immediately from the definition $\mathbf{H} \equiv \dot{\mathbf{R}} / \mathbf{R}$ and the first-order Friedmann equation (Problem 19.14)

$$
\dot {\mathbf {R}} ^ {2} + \mathbf {k} = \frac {8 \pi \mathbf {G}}{3} \rho \mathbf {R} ^ {2}.
$$

If we now take $\mathrm{d} / \mathrm{d}\mathbf{R}$ on this equation, use the identity $\frac{1}{2}\mathrm{d}[(\dot{\mathbf{R}})^2] / \mathrm{d}\mathbf{R} = \ddot{\mathbf{R}}$ , the other first order equation $\mathrm{d}(\rho \mathbf{R}^3) / \mathrm{d}\mathbf{R} = -3\mathbf{p}\mathbf{R}^2$ and the definition $\mathbf{q} \equiv -\ddot{\mathbf{R}}\mathbf{R} / \dot{\mathbf{R}}^{2}$ , we get Equation (2). If $\rho >> \mathfrak{p}$ then the left side of Equation (2) is negligible compared with the right side, and Equation (3) results. Equation (4) is immediate upon substituting Equation (3) into Equation (1). If $\mathfrak{p} = 1 / 3\rho$ , then eliminating $\rho$ from Equations (1) and (2) gives Equation (5), while eliminating the $\mathbf{k} / \mathbf{R}^2$ term gives Equation (6).

Solution 19.16. Using $\mathbf{T}_0^0 = -\rho$ , and $\mathbf{T}_{\mathbf{j}}^{\mathbf{j}}$ (no sum) = $\mathbf{p}$ , and $\mathbf{p}_{,\mathbf{j}} = 0$ (homogeneity) the $\mu = \mathbf{j}$ component is

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = \mathrm {T} _ {\mathrm {j}, \nu} ^ {\mathrm {j}} = \mathrm {T} _ {\mathrm {j}, \nu} ^ {\mathrm {v}} - \mathrm {T} _ {\alpha} ^ {\nu} \mathrm {T} _ {\mathrm {j} \nu} ^ {\alpha} + \mathrm {T} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {T} _ {\alpha \nu} ^ {\nu} \\ = p, j + \rho \Gamma_ {j 0} ^ {0} - p \Gamma_ {j k} ^ {k} + p \Gamma_ {j \nu} ^ {\nu} = (\rho + p) \Gamma_ {j 0} ^ {0} = 0 \\ \end{array}
$$

which is a tautology. The $\mu = 0$ component gives

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = \mathrm {T} _ {0; \nu} ^ {\nu} = \mathrm {T} _ {0, \nu} ^ {\nu} - \mathrm {T} _ {a} ^ {\nu} \Gamma_ {0 \nu} ^ {a} + \mathrm {T} _ {0} ^ {a} \Gamma_ {a \nu} ^ {\nu} \\ = - \frac {\mathrm {d} \rho}{\mathrm {d} t} + \rho \Gamma_ {0 0} ^ {0} - (\rho + p) \left(\log | g ^ {\frac {1}{2}}\right), _ {0} \\ \end{array}
$$

where $\Gamma_{\alpha \beta}^{\alpha} = (\log |g|^{\frac{1}{2}}),\beta$ has been used (see Problem 7.7). Thus

$$
\mathrm {d} \rho / \mathrm {d t} = - \frac {(\rho + \mathrm {p})}{\mathrm {R} ^ {3}} \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d t}} (\mathrm {R} ^ {3})
$$

and

$$
\mathrm {d} (\rho \mathrm {R} ^ {3}) = - 3 \mathrm {p R} ^ {2} \mathrm {d R}
$$

which we recognize as one of the first-order Friedmann equations (see Problem 19.14).

Solution 19.17. With $\mathfrak{p} = \rho = 0$ , and $\mathbf{k} = -1$ , the first-order Friedmann equation

$$
\dot {\mathbf {R}} ^ {2} + \mathbf {k} = \frac {8 \pi \mathbf {G}}{3} \rho \mathbf {R} ^ {2}
$$

becomes $\mathbf{R} = 1$ which implies $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{t}$ and thus the metric given is correct. Since the metric is spherically symmetric, the curvature radial coordinate is

$$
\mathbf {r} = \mathbf {t} \sinh \chi .
$$

It is not hard to guess a time coordinate

$$
\mathrm {T} = \mathrm {t} \cosh \chi
$$

and then to compute the transformed metric

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - \mathrm {d T} ^ {2} + \mathrm {d r} ^ {2} + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} (\mathrm {d} \theta^ {2} + \sin^ {2} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2})
$$

which is empty Minkowski space.

Solution 19.18.

(a) When matter dominates, we ignore pressure so that mass-energy density decreases in proportion to the increase in volume of the universe, and

$$
\rho = \rho_ {0} \left(\frac {\mathrm {R} _ {0}}{\overline {{\mathrm {R}}}}\right) ^ {3}. \tag {1}
$$

Define a new time coordinate ("development angle") by $\mathrm{d}\eta = \mathrm{d}t / \mathbf{R}$ . Then the Friedmann equation becomes

$$
\left(\frac {\dot {\mathrm {R}}}{\mathrm {R}}\right) ^ {2} = \left(\frac {\mathrm {d R} / \mathrm {d} \eta}{\mathrm {R} ^ {2}}\right) ^ {2} = \frac {8 \pi \mathrm {G}}{3} \rho_ {0} \left(\frac {\mathrm {R} _ {0}}{\mathrm {R}}\right) ^ {3} - \frac {\mathrm {k}}{\mathrm {R} ^ {2}} \tag {2}
$$

or

$$
R ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \frac {\mathrm {d} R}{\mathrm {d} \eta} = 2 \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} \eta} R ^ {\frac {1}{2}} = \left(\frac {8 \pi G}{3} \rho_ {0} R _ {0} ^ {3} - k R\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}
$$

which integrates to give

$$
\frac {1}{2} \eta = \int_ {0} ^ {\mathrm {R} ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \frac {\mathrm {d R} ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{\left(\frac {8 \pi}{3} \rho_ {0} \mathrm {R} _ {0} ^ {3} - \mathrm {k R}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} = \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} \sin^ {- 1} & \frac {\mathrm {R} ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{\left(\frac {8}{3} \pi \mathrm {G} \rho_ {0} \mathrm {R} _ {0} ^ {3}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \quad \mathrm {k} = + 1 \\ & \frac {\mathrm {R} ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{\left(\frac {8 \pi \mathrm {G}}{3} \rho_ {0} \mathrm {R} _ {0} ^ {3}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \quad \mathrm {k} = 0 \\ \sinh^ {- 1} & \frac {\mathrm {R} ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{\left(\frac {8 \pi \mathrm {G}}{3} \rho_ {0} \mathrm {R} _ {0} ^ {3}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \quad \mathrm {k} = - 1 \end{array} \right. \tag {3}
$$

From Problem 19.15 we know

$$
\mathrm {q} _ {0} = \frac {4 \pi \mathrm {G}}{3} \frac {\rho_ {0}}{\mathrm {H} _ {0} ^ {2}} \tag {4}
$$

and

$$
R _ {0} ^ {2} = \frac {k}{\left(2 q _ {0} - 1\right) H _ {0} ^ {2}} \quad (k = \pm 1). \tag {5}
$$

We see that since the left side is positive, $\mathbf{k} = \operatorname{sign}(2\mathbf{q}_0 - 1)$ . Thus in Equation (3)

$$
\frac {8 \pi}{3} \rho_ {0} \mathrm {R} _ {0} ^ {3} = \frac {2 \mathrm {q} _ {0}}{\mathrm {H} _ {0} | (2 \mathrm {q} _ {0} - 1) | ^ {\frac {3}{2}}} \quad (\mathrm {k} = \pm 1).
$$

Inverting Equation (3) gives

$$
\mathbf {R} = \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} \frac {\mathrm {q} _ {0}}{\mathrm {H} _ {0} (2 \mathrm {q} _ {0} - 1) ^ {\frac {3}{2}}} (1 - \cos \eta) & \quad \mathrm {k} = 1 \\ \frac {1}{4} \mathrm {H} _ {0} ^ {2} \mathrm {R} _ {0} ^ {3} \eta^ {2} & \quad \mathrm {k} = 0 \\ \frac {\mathrm {q} _ {0}}{\mathrm {H} _ {0} (1 - 2 \mathrm {q} _ {0}) ^ {\frac {3}{2}}} (\cosh \eta - 1) & \quad \mathrm {k} = - 1. \end{array} \right.
$$

Now integrating $\mathrm{dt} = \mathrm{Rd}\eta$ gives

$$
t = \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} \frac {q _ {0}}{H _ {0} \left(2 q _ {0} - 1\right) ^ {\frac {3}{2}}} (\eta - \sin \eta) & k = 1 \\ \frac {1}{1 2} H _ {0} ^ {2} R _ {0} ^ {3} \eta^ {3} & k = 0 \\ \frac {q _ {0}}{H _ {0} \left(1 - 2 q _ {0}\right) ^ {\frac {3}{2}}} (\sinh \eta - \eta) & k = - 1. \end{array} \right.
$$

The fact that $\mathbf{R}_0$ cannot be eliminated from the $k = 0$ case merely reflects the fact that the universe here has an arbitrary scaling and its geometry looks the same at all times. The value of $\mathbf{R}_0$ will not enter the computation of any physically measurable quantity.

(b) When radiation dominates, the mass-energy in a given comoving volume is not constant. There is an extra decrease in density from the redshift of photons, so

$$
\rho = \rho_ {0} \left(\frac {\mathbf {R} _ {0}}{\mathbf {R}}\right) ^ {4}.
$$

The analog of Equation (2) is

$$
\left(\dot {\frac {\mathrm {R}}{\mathrm {R}}}\right) ^ {2} = \left(\frac {\mathrm {d R / d \eta}}{\mathrm {R} ^ {2}}\right) ^ {2} = \frac {8 \pi \mathrm {G}}{3} \rho_ {0} \left(\frac {\mathrm {R} _ {0}}{\mathrm {R}}\right) ^ {4} - \frac {\mathrm {k}}{\mathrm {R} ^ {2}}
$$

or

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathrm {R}}{\left(\frac {8}{3} \pi \mathrm {G} \rho_ {0} \mathrm {R} _ {0} ^ {4} - \mathrm {k R} ^ {2}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} = \mathrm {d} \eta
$$

which has solutions:

$$
R = \left(\frac {8 \pi}{3} G \rho_ {0} R _ {0} ^ {4}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \times \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} \sin \eta & k = 1 \\ \eta & k = 0 \\ \sinh \eta & k = - 1. \end{array} \right. \tag {6}
$$

Instead of Equation (4), we now get (Problem 19.15)

$$
\mathbf {q} _ {0} = \frac {8 \pi \mathrm {G}}{3} \frac {\rho_ {0}}{\mathrm {H} _ {0} ^ {2}},
$$

and instead of Equation (5), we have

$$
R _ {0} ^ {2} = \frac {k}{\left(q _ {0} - 1\right) H _ {0} ^ {2}} \quad (k = \pm 1).
$$

Equation (6) therefore becomes

$$
\frac {8 \pi}{3} G \rho_ {0} R _ {0} ^ {4} = \left\{ \begin{array}{c c} \frac {q _ {0}}{(q _ {0} - 1) ^ {2} H _ {0} ^ {2}} & k = \pm 1 \\ H _ {0} ^ {2} R _ {0} ^ {4} & k = 0 \end{array} \right.
$$

and integrating for t gives

$$
t = \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} \frac {1}{H _ {0}} \left[ \frac {q _ {0} ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{q _ {0} - 1} \right] & (1 - \cos \eta) \\ \frac {1}{2} H _ {0} R _ {0} ^ {2} \eta^ {2} & \\ \frac {1}{H _ {0}} \left[ \frac {q _ {0} ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{1 - q _ {0}} \right] & (\cosh \eta - 1) \end{array} \quad k = 1 \right.
$$

Solution 19.19. After the bullet has reached nonrelativistic velocity, we have (from Problem 19.7)

$$
\frac {\text {c o n s t a n t}}{R} = \frac {d r _ {P}}{d t} = \frac {d r _ {P}}{d r} \frac {d r}{d R} \frac {d R}{d t} = \frac {d r}{d R} \dot {R} R \tag {1}
$$

where $\mathbf{r}$ is the Robertson-Walker coordinate, related to proper separations $(\mathbf{r}_{\mathbf{p}} \equiv$ proper separation) by $\mathrm{dr}_{\mathbf{p}} = \mathbf{R} \mathrm{d}\mathbf{r}$ (hence the last equality above). For a $k = 0$ Friedmann universe $\mathbf{R} \propto t^{\frac{2}{3}}$ so $\dot{\mathbf{R}} \propto \mathbf{R}^{-\frac{1}{2}}$ and Equation (1) gives us $\mathrm{dr} / \mathrm{dR} \propto \mathbf{R}^{-\frac{3}{2}}$ and hence

$$
r = A + B / R ^ {\frac {1}{2}}.
$$

As $t \to \infty$ , the $r_{\text{bullet}}$ approaches $r = A$ . The proper distance between the $r = A$ observer and the bullet is $R\Delta r = BR^{\frac{1}{2}}$ . Thus the proper distance becomes infinite even though the velocities (according to Equation (1)) approach each other.

If $k = -1$ , then $R$ becomes constant at large times and Equation (1) gives $\mathrm{dr} / \mathrm{dR} \propto \mathrm{R}^{-2}$ so that

$$
\mathrm {r} = \mathrm {A} + \mathrm {B} / \mathrm {R}.
$$

As $t \to \infty$ the bullets coordinate $r$ approaches A, but the proper distance to the $r = A$ observer $R\Delta r$ approaches B, a constant.

Solution 19.20. Measuring time in terms of development angle $\eta$ defined by $\mathrm{d}\eta = \mathrm{d}t / \mathbb{R}(t)$ , the Friedmann metric for a radial $(\mathrm{d}\theta = \mathrm{d}\phi = 0)$ photon becomes

$$
