0 = \mathrm {d} s ^ {2} = \mathbb {R} ^ {2} (\eta) (- \mathrm {d} \eta^ {2} + \mathrm {d} \chi^ {2})
$$

where $\mathrm{d}\chi^2 = \mathrm{d}\mathbf{r}^2 /(1 - \mathbf{r}^2)$ is the "trigonometric" radial coordinate on the 3-sphere (see Problem 19.5). From Problem 19.18 we see that the universe lives an interval $\Delta \eta = 2\pi$ (time between two zeros of $\mathbb{R}$ ), so in this time a photon can propagate $\Delta \chi = 2\pi$ , i.e. exactly once around the universe.

Solution 19.21. For a $\mathbf{k} = 0$ Friedmann universe we have

$$
\mathrm {R (t) / R _ {0}} = \left(\frac {3}{2} \mathrm {H _ {0} t}\right) ^ {\frac {2}{3}}.
$$

The energy emitted per source per unit proper time is L. This is redshifted by a factor $\mathbb{R}(\mathfrak{t}) / \mathbb{R}_0$ , so the total energy per star which is now

present is

$$
\tilde {\mathcal {E}} = \int_ {0} ^ {t _ {0}} L \frac {R (t)}{R _ {0}} d t = \frac {3}{5} L \left(\frac {3}{2} H _ {0}\right) ^ {\frac {2}{3}} t _ {0} ^ {5 / 3}
$$

or $\mathfrak{S} = 2\mathrm{L} / 5\mathrm{H}_0$ since $t_0 = 2 / 3\mathrm{H}_0$ . The energy per volume, u, now is thus $\frac{2}{5}\mathrm{H}_0^{-1}\mathrm{Ln}$ . Since the energy fills the universe isotropically, it corresponds to a flux

$$
\mathrm {\bf B} = \frac {\mathrm {\bf c}}{4 \pi} \mathrm {\bf u} = \frac {\mathrm {\bf c}}{1 0 \pi} \mathrm {\bf H} _ {0} ^ {- 1} \mathrm {\bf L n}.
$$

[By comparison, a Newtonian universe where the stars “‘turned on’” a time $t_0 = \left(\frac{3}{2} H_0\right)^{-1}$ ago would have

$$
\mathfrak {E} = \int_ {0} ^ {t _ {0}} L d t = \frac {2}{3} H _ {0} ^ {- 1} L
$$

and

$$
\mathrm {\bf B} = \frac {\mathrm {c}}{6 \pi} \left. \mathrm {\bf H} _ {0} ^ {- 1} \mathrm {\bf L n .} \right]
$$

Solution 19.22. From the solution of Problem 19.18, if $\mathbf{q}_0 > 0.5$ we must have a $\mathbf{k} = +1$ cosmology which expands according to

$$
R (t) / R _ {0} = \frac {q _ {0}}{2 q _ {0} - 1} (1 - \cos \eta). \tag {1}
$$

Setting $\mathbf{R}(t) = \mathbf{R}_0$ , so that $\eta = \eta_0$ , gives a formula which relates $\eta_0$ to $\mathbf{q}_0$ at the present epoch. The epoch is arbitrary, however, so it also relates $\eta$ and $\mathbf{q}$ at any epoch. Putting $\mathbf{q} = 0.5002$ gives $1 - \cos \eta = .0008$ at $z = 1500$ . Substituting this back into Equation (1) and putting $\mathbf{R}_0 / \mathbf{R}(t) = 1 + z = 1501$ , we can solve for $\mathbf{q}_0$ , getting

$$
\mathrm {q} _ {0} = [ 2 - (1 + z) (1 - \cos \eta) ] ^ {- 1} = 1. 2 5.
$$

If $\mathbf{q}_0 < 0.5$ , the $\mathbf{k} = -1$ analogy of Equation (1) is

$$
R (t) / R _ {0} = \frac {q _ {0}}{1 - 2 q _ {0}} (\cosh \eta - 1) \tag {2}
$$

so $\mathbf{q} = .4998$ gives $\cosh \eta - 1 = .0008$ at $z = 1500$ and

$$
\mathbf {q} _ {0} = [ 2 + (1 + z) (\cosh \eta - 1) ] ^ {- 1} = 0. 3 1 2.
$$

Solution 19.23.

(a) At the present epoch $t = t_0$ , the spacelike 3-surface has the metric

$$
\mathrm {d} \sigma^ {2} = \mathrm {R} _ {0} ^ {2} \left(\frac {\mathrm {d r} ^ {2}}{1 - \mathrm {r} ^ {2}} + \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \left(\mathrm {d} \theta^ {2} + \sin^ {2} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2}\right)\right).
$$

Noting that $0 \leq r \leq 1$ covers half the 3-sphere, we have

![](images/75f31c61ffa1e0775e15597cdca8dc0bb5a644a4268f1ac8f2828e3c0d882e5a.jpg)

$$
\operatorname {V} = \int_ {\mathrm {3 - s p h e r e}} \left| ^ {(3)} \mathrm {g} \right| ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d} ^ {3} \mathrm {x} = 2 \int_ {0} ^ {1} \mathrm {d r} \int_ {- 1} ^ {1} \mathrm {d} (\cos \theta) \int_ {0} ^ {2 \pi} \mathrm {d} \phi \frac {\mathrm {R} _ {0} ^ {3} \mathrm {r} ^ {2}}{\left(1 - \mathrm {r} ^ {2}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} = 2 \pi^ {2} \mathrm {R} _ {0} ^ {3}.
$$

For a matter dominated universe (see Problem 19.15)

$$
R _ {0} = H _ {0} ^ {- 1} \left(2 q _ {0} - 1\right) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \tag {1}
$$

so

$$
V = 2 \pi^ {2} \left(2 q _ {0} - 1\right) ^ {- \frac {3}{2}} H _ {0} ^ {- 3}. \tag {2}
$$

(b) Our past light cone sweeps outward a proper radial distance $c \, dt$ in a time $dt$ (see figure). From the metric, the proper area of a 2-sphere is $4\pi r^2 R^2$ , where $r$ and $R$ are both functions of $t$ along the light cone. Thus, the visible volume is $(c = 1 \, \text{now})$

$$
\mathbf {V} = \int_ {0} ^ {\mathrm {t} _ {0}} \mathrm {d t} 4 \pi \mathrm {r} ^ {2} (\mathrm {t}) \mathbf {R} ^ {2} (\mathrm {t}). \tag {3}
$$

The differential equation for $\mathbf{r}(t)$ follows from its definition as a null cone:

$$
- \mathrm {d t} = \frac {\mathrm {R} (\mathrm {t})}{\left(1 - \mathrm {r} ^ {2}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \mathrm {d r}, \tag {4}
$$

and $\mathbf{r} = 0$ at $t = t_0$ . To solve Equation (4) and evaluate the integral in

Equation (3), we use the solution for $\mathbf{R}(t)$ parameterized by "development angle" $\eta$ (see the solution to Problem 19.18).

$$
R = A (1 - \cos \eta), \quad t = A (\eta - \sin \eta) \tag {5}
$$

where

$$
A = \frac {q _ {0}}{\left(2 q _ {0} - 1\right) ^ {\frac {3}{2}} H _ {0}} \tag {6}
$$

and the current value of $\eta$ is

$$
\eta_ {0} = \cos^ {- 1} \left(\frac {1 - q _ {0}}{q _ {0}}\right). \tag {7}
$$

Equation (4) now becomes $\mathrm{d}(\sin^{-1}\mathbf{r}) = \mathrm{d}\eta$ so that

$$
r = \sin (\eta_ {0} - \eta) \tag {8}
$$

and Equation (3) becomes

$$
\mathbf {V} = \int_ {0} ^ {\eta_ {0}} \mathbf {A} (1 - \cos \eta) \mathrm {d} \eta 4 \pi \sin^ {2} (\eta_ {0} - \eta) \mathbf {A} ^ {2} (1 - \cos \eta) ^ {2} .
$$

This straightforward integral gives

$$
V = 4 \pi A ^ {3} \left(\frac {6 1}{8 0} \sin 2 \eta_ {0} + \frac {5}{4} \eta_ {0} - \frac {5}{2} \sin \eta_ {0} - \frac {3}{8} \eta_ {0} \cos 2 \eta_ {0} + \frac {1}{3 0} \sin 3 \eta_ {0}\right)
$$

which can be reduced with Equations (6) and (7) to a function of $\mathbf{q}_0$ and $\mathbf{H}_0$ . (c) The farthest coordinate radius that we see is, according to Equation (8),

$$
r _ {0} = \sin (\eta_ {0}) = (2 q _ {0} - 1) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} / q _ {0}
$$

where Equation (7) has been used. The volume out to this coordinate radius at the present epoch is (analogously to part (a))

$$
\begin{array}{l} V = 4 \pi \int_ {0} ^ {\mathrm {r} _ {0}} \frac {R _ {0} ^ {3} \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \mathrm {d r}}{\left(1 - \mathrm {r} ^ {2}\right) _ {-} ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} = 2 \pi R _ {0} ^ {3} \left[ \sin^ {- 1} \mathrm {r} _ {0} - \mathrm {r} _ {0} \left(1 - \mathrm {r} _ {0} ^ {2}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \right] \\ = \frac {2 \pi}{\mathrm {H} _ {0} ^ {3} \left(2 \mathrm {q} _ {0} - 1\right) ^ {\frac {3}{2}}} \left[ \cos^ {- 1} \left(\frac {1 - \mathrm {q} _ {0}}{\mathrm {q} _ {0}}\right) - \frac {\left(1 - \mathrm {q} _ {0}\right) \left(2 \mathrm {q} _ {0} - 1\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{\mathrm {q} _ {0} ^ {2}} \right]. \\ \end{array}
$$

Solution 19.24.

![](images/23b04c5eb40bcdf283d55f484c6874171dae3b8408c586be8ae9fa7c7731c348.jpg)

From the figure and the Robertson-Walker line element, $\ell = [\mathfrak{r}_1\mathsf{R}(\mathfrak{t}_1)]\delta$ . We can express $\mathsf{R}(\mathfrak{t}_1)$ as $\mathsf{R}_0 / (1 + z)$ ; the hard part is now to compute $\mathbf{r}_1$ : Use the $\eta, \chi$ form of the metric from Problem 19.5. Then $\mathbf{r}_1 = \Sigma (\chi_1)$ . The path of a photon is $0 = \mathbb{R}^2 (\mathrm{d}\chi^2 - \mathrm{d}\eta^2)$ , so $\chi_1 = \eta (\mathfrak{t}_0) - \eta (\mathfrak{t}_1)$ , and using the formula for $\sin (\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{B})$ , $\sinh (\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{B})$ we have:

$$
\mathbf {r} _ {1} = \boldsymbol {\Sigma} \left(\eta_ {0}\right) \mathbf {C} \left(\eta_ {1}\right) - \boldsymbol {\Sigma} \left(\eta_ {1}\right) \mathbf {C} \left(\eta_ {0}\right). \tag {1}
$$

[Here C means cos, 1, or cosh for $k = 1$ , 0, -1.] Now we need the formula relating $\eta$ of an epoch to its redshift and the current value of q (Problem 19.18):

$$
1 - \mathrm {C} (\eta) = \frac {2 \mathrm {q} _ {0} - 1}{\mathrm {q} _ {0}} \frac {1}{1 + z}
$$

$$
C (\eta) = \frac {q _ {0} z - q _ {0} + 1}{q _ {0} (1 + z)}.
$$

(This is true for all three values of k.) With this result we have

$$
\Sigma (\eta) = \frac {\left| 2 q _ {0} - 1 \right| ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \left(2 q _ {0} z + 1\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{q _ {0} (1 + z)}
$$

$$
\mathbf {r _ {1}} = \frac {\left| 2 \mathbf {q} _ {0} - 1 \right| ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{\mathbf {q} _ {0} ^ {2} (1 + z)} \left[ 1 - \mathbf {q} _ {0} + \mathbf {q} _ {0} z - (1 - \mathbf {q} _ {0}) (1 + 2 \mathbf {q} _ {0} z) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \right].
$$

So the apparent angle is

$$
\delta = \ell / r _ {1} R (t _ {1}) = \ell (1 + z) / r _ {1} R _ {0},
$$

and when we use Equation (3) of Problem 9.15 to eliminate $\mathbf{R}_0$ we get, finally,

$$
\delta = \frac {\ell \mathrm {H} _ {0} (1 + z) ^ {2} \mathrm {q} _ {0} ^ {2}}{\left[ 1 - \mathrm {q} _ {0} + \mathrm {q} _ {0} z - (1 - \mathrm {q} _ {0}) (1 + 2 \mathrm {q} _ {0} z) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \right]}.
$$

Solution 19.25. We work with units which make $c = k = 1$ . The density of matter ( $m_p =$ average baryon mass) is $\rho_{\text{matt}} = m_p n$ . The density of radiation is $\rho_{\text{rad}} = K a T^4$ , where $a$ is the radiation constant $= (8\pi^5) / (15 h^3)$ and $K$ is 1 (considering photons only) or $1 + \frac{7}{4} \left( \frac{4}{11} \right)^{4/3} = 1.454$ (considering photons and neutrinos - see Weinberg, p. 537). Equating these give

$$
1 = \frac {\rho_ {\mathrm {r a d}}}{\rho_ {\mathrm {m a t t}}} = \frac {\mathrm {K a T} ^ {4}}{\mathrm {n m} _ {\mathrm {p}}} \tag {1}
$$

The Saha equation for the fractional ionization $\mathbf{x}$ of hydrogen is

$$
\frac {\mathrm {x} ^ {2}}{1 - \mathrm {x}} = \frac {(2 \pi \mathrm {m _ {e} k T}) ^ {\frac {3}{2}} \exp \left(- \frac {1}{2} \alpha^ {2} \mathrm {m _ {e} / k T}\right)}{\mathrm {n h} ^ {3}}
$$

$(\mathfrak{m}_{\mathbf{e}} = \text{electron mass}, a = 1 / 137)$ . If recombination is defined by the left side of this equation being about unity, Equation (1) gives us

$$
\exp \left(\frac {1}{2} a ^ {2} m _ {\mathrm {e}} / k T\right) = \frac {1 5 (2 \pi m _ {\mathrm {e}} T) ^ {\frac {3}{2}} m _ {\mathrm {p}}}{8 \pi^ {5} K T ^ {4}}.
$$

Obviously the exponential sets the scale of the solution, $\mathbf{T} \sim \alpha^2 \mathfrak{m}_{\mathbf{e}}$ ; guessing a coefficient, computing the right side, and iterating once gives

more accurately: $\mathbf{T} \approx \frac{1}{80} \alpha^2 \mathfrak{m}_{\mathbf{e}}$ (cf. Problem 19.26). So, finally, when matter dominance and recombination occur at the same temperature $\mathbf{T}$ , we get

$$
\begin{array}{l} \sigma = \frac {4}{3} \frac {\mathrm {a T} ^ {3}}{\mathrm {n}} = \frac {4}{3} \frac {\mathrm {m _ {p}}}{\mathrm {T K}} \left(\frac {\mathrm {K a T} ^ {4}}{\mathrm {m _ {p}} \mathrm {n}}\right) \\ = \frac {4}{3} \frac {\mathrm {m} _ {\mathrm {p}}}{\mathrm {K} \left(\frac {1}{8 0} \alpha^ {2} \mathrm {m} _ {\mathrm {e}}\right)} = \frac {3 2 0}{3 \mathrm {K}} (1 3 7) ^ {2} (1 8 3 6) \\ \approx 4 \times 1 0 ^ {9}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Solution 19.26. The Saha equation for the equilibrium fractional ionization of hydrogen is (units with $k = c = 1$ )

$$
\frac {\mathrm {x} ^ {2}}{1 - \mathrm {x}} = \frac {(2 \pi \mathrm {m} _ {\mathrm {e}} \mathrm {T}) ^ {\frac {3}{2}} \mathrm {e} ^ {- \mathrm {B} / \mathrm {T}}}{\mathrm {n h} ^ {3}} \tag {1}
$$

where B is the ionization energy $= \frac{1}{2} a^2 m_e (a = 1/137)$ , and n is the number density of protons plus atoms. The value of n is related to $\sigma$ , the entropy per baryon by

$$
\sigma = \frac {4}{3} \frac {\mathsf {a T} ^ {3}}{\mathsf {n}}
$$

so if we take $x = 1/2$ and use $a = 8\pi^5 / 15h^3$ , Equation (1) becomes

$$
\frac {2 ^ {\frac {1}{2}} 4 \pi^ {7 / 2}}{4 5} \left. \frac {1}{\sigma} \left(\frac {\mathrm {T}}{\mathrm {m _ {e}}}\right) ^ {\frac {3}{2}} \right. = \exp \left[ - \frac {\mathrm {m _ {e}} / \mathrm {T}}{2 (1 3 7) ^ {2}} \right].
$$

This is easily solved by iteration. For example, iterating twice:

$$
\frac {\mathrm {m} _ {\mathrm {e}}}{\mathrm {T}} = 2 (1 3 7) ^ {2} \left(\log \frac {\sigma}{6 . 9 0 8} + \frac {3}{2} \log \frac {\mathrm {m} _ {\mathrm {e}}}{\mathrm {T}}\right)
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {T} \approx \frac {\mathrm {m _ {e}}}{2 (1 3 7) ^ {2}} \left[ \log \frac {\sigma}{6 . 9 0 8} + \frac {3}{2} \left(1 0. 5 3 + \log \log \frac {\sigma}{6 . 9 0 8}\right) \right] ^ {- 1} \\ = \left\{ \begin{array}{l} 4 3 3 0 ^ {0} \mathrm {K} \text {f o r} \sigma = 1 0 ^ {8} \\ 4 0 5 0 ^ {0} \mathrm {K} \text {f o r} \sigma = 1 0 ^ {9} \end{array} . \right. \\ \end{array}
$$

Solution 19.27. From Problem 19.18, near the singularity in the radiation-dominated regime,

$$
R = \left(\frac {8 \pi}{3} \rho_ {0} R _ {0} ^ {4}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \eta , \quad t = \left(\frac {8 \pi}{3} \rho_ {0} R _ {0} ^ {4}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \frac {\eta^ {2}}{2}. \tag {1}
$$

(This equation holds for any value of $\mathbf{k}$ in the limit $\eta \to 0$ .) Thus we have

$$
t ^ {2} = \frac {3 R ^ {4}}{3 2 \pi \rho_ {0} R _ {0} ^ {4}} = \frac {3}{3 2 \pi \rho} \tag {2}
$$

where we have used $\rho \propto \mathbb{R}^{-4}$ for the radiation-dominated era. The photon energy-density is

$$
\rho_ {\gamma} = \frac {8 \pi}{\mathrm {h} ^ {3}} \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} \frac {\mathrm {q} ^ {3} \mathrm {d q}}{\exp (\mathrm {q} / \mathrm {k T}) - 1} = \mathrm {a T} ^ {4}
$$

where $a$ is the Stefan-Boltzmann constant $8\pi^5 k^4 /15h^3$ . For highly relativistic electrons and positrons (which obey Fermi-Dirac Statistics)

$$
\rho_ {\mathrm {e +}} = \rho_ {\mathrm {e -}} = \frac {8 \pi}{\mathrm {h} ^ {3}} \int_ {0} ^ {\infty} \frac {\mathrm {q} ^ {3} \mathrm {d q}}{\exp (\mathrm {q / k T}) + 1} = \frac {7}{8} \mathrm {a T} ^ {4}.
$$

Thus the total mass-energy density is

$$
\rho = \rho_ {\gamma} + \rho_ {e +} + \rho_ {e -} = \frac {1 1}{4} a T ^ {4}
$$

and so

$$
T = \left(\frac {4}{1 1} \cdot \frac {3}{3 2 \pi a}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{4}} t ^ {- \frac {1}{2}}.
$$

If neutrinos are included and we assume they have zero chemical potential (cf. Weinberg, Section 15.6), then since they have only one spin state,

$$
\begin{array}{l} \rho_ {\nu_ {\mathbf {e}}} = \rho_ {\overline {{\nu}} _ {\mathbf {e}}} = \rho_ {\nu_ {\mu}} = \rho_ {\overline {{\nu}} _ {\mu}} = \frac {7}{1 6} a T ^ {4} \\ \rho = \frac {1 1}{4} a T ^ {4} + \frac {7}{4} a T ^ {4} = \frac {9}{2} a T ^ {4} \\ T = \left(\frac {2}{9} \cdot \frac {3}{3 2 \pi a}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{4}} t ^ {- \frac {1}{2}}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Solution 19.28. For relativistic particles, $\mathbf{E} \approx \mathbf{p}$ , the equilibrium density at a temperature $\mathbf{T}$ is (see the solution to Problem 19.27):

$$
\rho (T) = \int E n (p) d p = \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} a T ^ {4} & \text {f o r p h o t o n s} \\ \frac {7}{1 6} a T ^ {4} & \text {f o r e a c h n e u t r i n o s p e c i e s} \\ \frac {7}{8} a T ^ {4} & \text {f o r e a c h e l e c t r o n o r m u o n s p e c i e s .} \end{array} \right.
$$

Here a is Stefan's constant $= (8\pi^{5}\mathbf{k}^{4}) / (15\mathbf{h}^{3})$ . For this problem, then, we have

$$
\rho (\mathrm {T}) = \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} \left(1 + 4 \cdot \frac {7}{1 6} + 4 \cdot \frac {7}{8}\right) \mathrm {a T} ^ {4} & \mathrm {a t R _ {1}} \\ \left(1 + 4 \cdot \frac {7}{1 6} + 2 \cdot \frac {7}{8}\right) \mathrm {a T} ^ {4} & \mathrm {a t R _ {2}} \end{array} \right\} \equiv \mathrm {K a T} ^ {4},
$$

$$
\mathrm {K} _ {1} = 2 5 / 4, \quad \mathrm {K} _ {2} = 1 8 / 4.
$$

We now use the fact that the expansion of the universe is isentropic. The entropy per volume of such a relativistic gas is calculated from the first law of thermodynamics to be

$$
\frac {\mathrm {S}}{\mathrm {V}} = \frac {4}{3} \mathrm {K a T} ^ {3}.
$$

Since $V$ , the volume, varies as the expansion factor cubed, we have

$$
\text {c o n s t a n t} = \mathrm {S} \propto \frac {4}{3} \mathrm {K a} (\mathrm {R T}) ^ {3},
$$

giving for isentropic expansion

$$
\mathrm {K} _ {1} \mathrm {R} _ {1} ^ {3} \mathrm {T} _ {1} ^ {3} = \mathrm {K} _ {2} \mathrm {R} _ {2} ^ {3} \mathrm {T} _ {2} ^ {3}
$$

and

$$
\mathrm {T} _ {2} = \mathrm {T} _ {1} \left(\frac {\mathrm {R} _ {1}}{\mathrm {R} _ {2}}\right) \left(\frac {2 5}{1 8}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{3}}.
$$

Physically, this result says that in annihilating, the muons dump their energy (actually entropy) into the remaining particles, and increase their

temperature by a factor $(25 / 18)^{\frac{1}{3}}$ over the expected first-power decrease of T with R.

Solution 19.29. The standard model produces helium and deuterium by (roughly speaking) (1) freezing out a nonequilibrium neutron/proton ratio when the weak interactions for $n \to p$ become slower then the timescale for the expansion to drop the temperature, (2) making deuterium by $n + p \to d + y$ while most free neutrons have not yet decayed, and (3) using up almost all $d$ by $d + d \to He^3$ or $H^3 + \text{etc.}$ , $He^3$ or $H^3 + d \to He^4 + \text{etc.}$ .

We can thus answer the problem:

(i) There is no change in freezing out ratio, but higher baryon densities at any given temperature give more “cooking,” therefore more complete transformation to $\mathbf{He}^4$ : more $\mathbf{He}^4$ , less d.

(ii) The $\mathfrak{n} \to \mathfrak{p}$ reactions is slower, so more frozen out neutrons; other rates not changed, so: more He<sup>4</sup>, more d.

(iii) The reaction $\mathfrak{n} + \nu \leftrightarrow \mathfrak{p} + \mathfrak{e}^{-}$ is driven to the right by the degeneracy (Fermi) energy of the neutrinos; fewer neutrons give less $\mathrm{He}^4$ , less $\mathbf{d}$ .

(iv) This is similar to (iii) but for $\nu + p \leftrightarrow n + e^{+}$ , giving more He<sup>4</sup>, more d.

(v) The expansion time is related to density by

$$
t = \left(\frac {3}{3 2 \pi G \rho}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} + c o n s t a n t.
$$

Increased G thus means smaller times spent in each density stage. This gives a higher $n/p$ ratio. The small change in G does not substantially affect the fact that the neutrons are almost all consumed to d, since the decay time is still long, so: more d, more He<sup>4</sup>.

Solution 19.30. The cosmological constant is equivalent to a perfect fluid with effective density and pressure

$$
\rho_ {\mathrm {e f f}} = (8 \pi) ^ {- 1} \Lambda , \qquad \mathrm {p} _ {\mathrm {e f f}} = - (8 \pi) ^ {- 1} \Lambda .
$$

The dynamical equations of Problem 19.14 become

$$
\dot {\mathbf {R}} ^ {2} = \frac {1}{3} \Lambda \mathbf {R} ^ {2}
$$

with solution

$$
\mathbf {R} = \mathbf {R} _ {0} \mathrm {e} ^ {\mathrm {t} / \mathbf {T} _ {0}}, \qquad \mathbf {T} _ {0} ^ {- 1} = \frac {1}{3} \boldsymbol {\Lambda}.
$$

The metric is then

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - \mathrm {d t} ^ {2} + R _ {0} ^ {2} \mathrm {e} ^ {2 t / T _ {0}} (\mathrm {d r} ^ {2} + r ^ {2} \mathrm {d} \Omega^ {2}) .
$$

To transform this to a manifestly static form, introduce a "curvature coordinate"

$$
\mathbf {r} ^ {\prime} = \mathbf {R} _ {0} \mathbf {e} ^ {\mathrm {t / T} _ {0}} \mathbf {r}
$$

so that the metric becomes

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - \mathrm {d t} ^ {2} + \left(\mathrm {d r} ^ {\prime} - \mathrm {r} ^ {\prime} \mathrm {d t} / \mathrm {T} _ {0}\right) ^ {2} + \mathrm {r} ^ {\prime 2} \mathrm {d} \Omega^ {2}.
$$

Now eliminate $\mathbf{g}_{\mathbf{r}^{\prime}\mathbf{t}}$ by the transformation

$$
\mathbf {t} = \mathbf {t} ^ {\prime} + \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {\nabla} T _ {0} \log \left(\frac {\mathbf {r} ^ {\prime 2}}{T _ {0} ^ {2}} - 1\right)
$$

and find

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - \left(1 - \frac {\mathrm {r} ^ {\prime 2}}{\mathrm {T} _ {0} ^ {2}}\right) \mathrm {d t} ^ {\prime 2} + \left(1 - \frac {\mathrm {r} ^ {\prime 2}}{\mathrm {T} _ {0} ^ {2}}\right) ^ {- 1} \mathrm {d r} ^ {\prime 2} + \mathrm {r} ^ {\prime 2} \mathrm {d} \Omega^ {2}. \tag {2}
$$

The interpretation of Equation (1) as a nonstatic cosmological model is based on the assumption that the coordinates are comoving with the galaxies, the density of which is negligible in the dynamical equations.

Solution 19.31. The complete set of equations for isotropic, homogeneous universes is (see Problems 19.13 or 19.14)

$$
\left(\frac {\dot {\mathrm {R}}}{\dot {\mathrm {R}}}\right) ^ {2} = \frac {- \mathrm {k}}{\mathrm {R} ^ {2}} + \frac {8 \pi \rho}{3} \tag {1a}
$$

$$
2 \ddot {\frac {R}{R}} = - \left(\dot {\frac {R}{R}}\right) ^ {2} - \frac {k}{R ^ {2}} - 8 \pi p. \tag {1b}
$$

Since the universe contains only dust stress-energy, $\rho_0$ , and "vacuum polarization" stress-energy $\Lambda$ , we have

$$
\rho = \rho_ {0} + \Lambda / 8 \pi \tag {2a}
$$

$$
p = - \Lambda / 8 \pi . \tag {2b}
$$

Since all time derivatives of $\mathbf{R}$ higher than the second may be expressed as linear combinations of $\dot{\mathbf{R}}$ and $\ddot{\mathbf{R}}$ by Equations (1), the necessary and sufficient condition for a static solution is

$$
\dot {\mathbf {R}} = \ddot {\mathbf {R}} = 0. \tag {3}
$$

With the conditions of Equations (3) and (2), Equations (1) become

$$
\mathrm {k} / \mathrm {R} ^ {2} = (8 \pi \rho_ {0} + \Lambda) / 3 \tag {4a}
$$

$$
\mathrm {k} / \mathrm {R} ^ {2} = \Lambda \tag {4b}
$$

which imply

$$
\rho_ {0} = \Lambda / 4 \pi \tag {5a}
$$

$$
\mathbf {R} = \Lambda^ {- \frac {1}{2}}. \tag {5b}
$$

Since $\rho_0 > 0$ , Equation (4b) now implies $k = +1$ . To investigate the stability of the "Einstein universe," first combine Equations (1) to

give

$$
\frac {2 \ddot {\mathrm {R}}}{\mathrm {R}} = - \frac {8 \pi}{3} \rho - 8 \pi \mathrm {p} = - \frac {8 \pi}{3} \rho_ {0} + \frac {2}{3} \Lambda \tag {6}
$$

and let

$$
\mathbf {R} = \boldsymbol {\Lambda} ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} + \delta \mathbf {R} \tag {7a}
$$

$$
\rho_ {0} = \frac {\Lambda}{4 \pi} + \delta \rho_ {0} \tag {7b}
$$

Equation (6), to order $\delta \mathbf{R}$ , now becomes

$$
2 \Lambda^ {\frac {1}{2}} (\delta \ddot {\mathbf {R}}) = - \frac {8 \pi}{3} \delta \rho_ {0}. \tag {8}
$$

Now, since the total amount of dust is conserved, the dust stress-energy obeys

$$
\rho_ {0} \mathbf {R} ^ {3} = \text {c o n s t a n t}
$$

$$
\frac {\delta \rho_ {0}}{\rho_ {0}} = - 3 \frac {\delta R}{R}. \tag {9}
$$

Equation (8) then gives

$$
\left(\delta \ddot {\mathbf {R}}\right) - \Lambda \delta \mathbf {R} = 0, \tag {10}
$$

i.e. the perturbation $\delta R$ grows exponentially in time. Hence, the universe is unstable.

Solution 19.32. The line element of the "Einstein universe" $(\mathbf{k} = +1)$ is (see Problem 19.5)

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = \mathbf {R} ^ {2} \left[ \mathrm {d} \chi^ {2} + \sin^ {2} \chi (\mathrm {d} \theta^ {2} + \sin^ {2} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2}) \right] \tag {1}
$$

with

$$
0 \leq \chi \leq \pi , \quad 0 \leq \theta \leq \pi , \quad 0 \leq \phi \leq 2 \pi .
$$

The volume V is therefore

$$
V = \int_ {0} ^ {\pi} 4 \pi R ^ {3} \sin^ {2} \chi d \chi = 2 \pi^ {2} R ^ {3}. \tag {2}
$$

From Problem 19.31, we have the result, for this universe, that

$$
R = \left(4 \pi \rho_ {0}\right) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \tag {3}
$$

where $\rho_0$ is the matter (dust) energy density. Substitution of Equation (3) into Equation (2) yields

$$
V = 2 \pi^ {2} \left(\frac {c ^ {2}}{4 \pi G \rho_ {0}}\right) ^ {\frac {3}{2}} \tag {4}
$$

(where we have put in the factors of c and G).

Solution 19.33. Let $\Lambda_{\mathbf{E}}$ be the Einstein value of the cosmological constant so that the Einstein universe has $\rho_0 = \Lambda_{\mathbf{E}} / 4\pi$ , and $\mathbf{R} = \Lambda_{\mathbf{E}}^{-\frac{1}{2}}$ (see Problem 9.31). All dust cosmologies, even with nonzero $\Lambda$ , have $\rho \mathbf{R}^3 =$ constant. Write this as

$$
\rho \mathbf {R} ^ {3} = \frac {1 + \epsilon}{4 \pi \Lambda^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \tag {1}
$$

This is equivalent to writing $\Lambda = \Lambda_{\mathbf{E}}(1 + \epsilon)^2$ . The remaining dynamical equation of Problem 19.14 becomes

$$
\dot {\mathbf {R}} ^ {2} = \mathrm {V} (\mathrm {R}) \equiv \frac {\Lambda \mathrm {R} ^ {2}}{3} - 1 + \frac {2 (1 + \epsilon)}{3 \Lambda^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {R}}. \tag {2}
$$

For small $R$ , $\dot{R}^2 \sim 1 / R$ , i.e. $R \sim t^{\frac{2}{3}}$ . The expansion reaches a minimum when $\dot{R}$ is a minimum. Setting $dV / dR$ equal to zero, we find that this occurs when

$$
R = R _ {m} = (1 + \epsilon) ^ {\frac {1}{3}} \Lambda^ {- \frac {1}{2}}. \tag {3}
$$

When $\mathbf{R}$ is close to $\mathbf{R}_{\mathfrak{m}}$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \dot {\mathrm {R}} ^ {2} \approx \mathrm {V} (\mathrm {R} _ {\mathrm {m}}) + \frac {1}{2} (\mathrm {R} - \mathrm {R} _ {\mathrm {m}}) ^ {2} \mathrm {V} ^ {\prime \prime} (\mathrm {R} _ {\mathrm {m}}) + \dots \\ = (1 + \epsilon) ^ {\frac {2}{3}} - 1 + \left[ \Lambda^ {\frac {1}{2}} R - (1 + \epsilon) ^ {\frac {1}{3}} \right] ^ {2}. \\ \end{array}
$$

From a table of integrals, we find

$$
R = \frac {(1 + \epsilon) ^ {\frac {1}{3}}}{\Lambda^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \left[ 1 + \left\{1 - (1 + \epsilon) ^ {- \frac {2}{3}} \right\} ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \sinh \Lambda^ {\frac {1}{2}} \left(t - t _ {m}\right) \right] \tag {4}
$$

where $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{R}_{\mathbf{m}}$ at $t = t_{\mathbf{m}}$ . For small $\epsilon$ , this becomes

$$
\textsf {R} \approx \mathsf {R} _ {\mathrm {m}} \left[ 1 + \frac {\epsilon}{3} \sinh \Lambda^ {\frac {1}{2}} \left(t - t _ {\mathrm {m}}\right) \right].
$$

Thus $\mathbf{R}$ remains approximately equal to $\mathbf{R}_{\mathfrak{m}}$ until

$$
\epsilon \sinh \Lambda^ {\frac {1}{2}} \left(t - t _ {\mathrm {m}}\right) \approx 1
$$

i.e. for a time

$$
\left(t - t _ {\mathrm {m}}\right) \approx \Lambda^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \log \left(\frac {1}{\epsilon}\right).
$$

Hence if $\epsilon$ is made sufficiently small, the time can be made arbitrarily long. Eventually $\mathbf{R}$ continues to expand, and asymptotically

$$
\mathbf {H} ^ {2} = \frac {\dot {\mathbf {R}} ^ {2}}{\mathbf {R} ^ {2}} \rightarrow \frac {1}{3} \boldsymbol {\Lambda}
$$

and we get the de Sitter universe of Problem 19.30.

If quasar formation occurred at $z = 2$ , then $R = R_{m}$ at $z = 2$ i.e.

$$
R _ {0} = R _ {m} (1 + z) = 3 R _ {m} = 3 \Lambda^ {- \frac {1}{2}}.
$$

If this is so (with $\epsilon \ll 1$ ), then the density of matter now must be:

$$
\rho_ {0} = (4 \pi \mathrm {R} _ {0} ^ {3} \Lambda^ {\frac {1}{2}}) ^ {- 1} = (1 0 8 \pi) ^ {- 1} \Lambda
$$

$$
\mathbf {H} _ {0} = \left(\dot {\frac {\mathbf {R}}{\mathbf {R}}}\right) _ {0} = \left(\frac {1}{3} \boldsymbol {\Lambda} - \frac {1}{\mathsf {R} _ {0} ^ {2}} + \frac {2}{3 \boldsymbol {\Lambda} ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathsf {R} _ {0} ^ {3}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} = \frac {(2 0 \boldsymbol {\Lambda}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{9}.
$$

Taking $\mathrm{H}_0 = 10^{-28}\mathrm{cm}^{-1}$ gives $\Lambda = 4\times 10^{-56}\mathrm{cm}^{-2}$ , i.e. $\rho_0 = 1.2\times$ $10^{-58}\mathrm{cm}^{-2} = 1.6\times 10^{-30}\mathrm{gm / cm}^3$ .

Solution 19.34. The effective $\rho_{\mathbf{c}}$ density corresponding to $\Lambda$ is

$$
\rho_ {c} = \frac {\Lambda}{8 \pi} = \frac {1 0 ^ {- 5 7} c m ^ {- 2}}{8 \pi} \approx 4 \times 1 0 ^ {- 3 1} g m / c m ^ {3}.
$$

Typical densities in the solar system are

$$
\rho_ {\text {b o d y}} \sim 1 \mathrm {g m} / \mathrm {c m} ^ {3}
$$

$$
\rho_ {\text {s o l a r s y s t e m}} \sim 1 0 ^ {3 3} \mathrm {g m} / (1 0 ^ {1 5} \mathrm {c m}) ^ {3} \sim 1 0 ^ {- 1 2} \mathrm {g m} / \mathrm {c m} ^ {3}.
$$

Thus, the effect of $\Lambda$ in the solar system, in order of magnitude is

$$
\frac {\rho_ {c}}{\rho_ {\text {s o l a r s y s t e m}}} \sim 1 0 ^ {- 1 9}.
$$

Solution 19.35. The Friedmann equations (Problem 19.15) are

$$
\ddot {\mathbf {R}} = - \frac {4}{3} \pi \mathrm {G} (\rho + 3 \mathrm {p}) \mathbf {R}
$$

$$
\ddot {\mathbf {R}} \ddot {\mathbf {R}} + 2 \mathbf {R} + 2 \mathbf {k} = 4 \pi \mathbf {G} (\rho - \mathbf {p}) \mathbf {R} ^ {2}.
$$

For a static solution, all time derivatives must vanish, so we get two conditions, $\rho + 3p = 0$ and $\rho = 3k / 8\pi GR^2$ , which have no solution for a fluid with positive energy density and pressure (i.e. any known fluid).

Solutions 19.36 and 19.37. "Easy" cases: Since the metric is time invariant and reversible, $\mathbf{g}_{0\mathbf{i}} = 0$ and it has the form

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - \mathrm {g} _ {0 0} (\mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {i}}) \mathrm {d t} ^ {2} + \mathrm {g} _ {\mathrm {i j}} (\mathbf {x} ^ {\mathrm {k}}) \mathrm {d x} ^ {\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {d x} ^ {\mathrm {j}}.
$$

If it is homogeneous (Problem 19.37), clocks must tick at the same rate everywhere, so $\mathbf{g}_{00} =$ constant; if it is inhomogeneous but pressureless (Problem 19.36), it is possible to rescale the time coordinate locally to make $\mathbf{g}_{00} =$ constant. (This was shown in Problem 16.25.) The standard formula for $\Gamma_{\mu \nu \sigma}$ now shows by inspection that the only nonvanishing components are $\Gamma_{ijk}$ . (Any 0 component is either a time derivative of a component $\mathbf{g}_{0j}$ or a derivative of $\mathbf{g}_{00}$ .) Now $\mathbf{R}_{00}$ can be computed from the standard formula involving the $\Gamma$ 's; since every term contains a zero index on a $\Gamma$ , it follows that $\mathbf{R}_{00} = 0$ and hence

$$
\mathbf {T} _ {\hat {0}} ^ {\hat {0}} - \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {T} _ {\hat {\alpha}} ^ {\hat {a}} = 0
$$

and hence $\rho + 3\mathfrak{p} = 0$ . But this is impossible for a pressureless fluid, or for a physically reasonable perfect fluid (with positive $\mathfrak{p}$ and $\rho$ ).

"Harder" cases: Let $\pmb{\xi}$ be the time-symmetry Killing vector and $\mathbf{u}$ be the 4-velocity of the fluid. Since $\mathbf{u} \propto \pmb{\xi}$ (Problem 13.9) and $\pmb{\xi}$ is hypersurface-orthogonal, $\mathbf{u}$ is hypersurface-orthogonal and hence $\omega_{\alpha \beta} = 0$ . In the Raychaudhuri equation (Problem 14.10)

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \theta}{\mathrm {d} r} \propto \frac {\partial \theta}{\partial t} = 0
$$

where $\pmb {\xi} = \partial /\partial t$ . Thus the Raychaudhuri equation becomes

$$
0 = \mathrm {a} _ {; \alpha} ^ {\alpha} - 2 \sigma^ {2} - \frac {1}{3} \theta^ {2} - 8 \pi \left(\mathrm {T} _ {\alpha \beta} - \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \beta} \mathrm {T}\right) \mathrm {u} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {u} ^ {\beta}. \tag {1}
$$

For $p = 0$ (Problem 19.36) we have $a = 0$ from the Euler equation (Problem 14.3) and Equation (1) becomes

$$
0 = - 2 \sigma^ {2} - \frac {1}{3} \theta^ {2} - 4 \pi \rho
$$

which has no solution for positive $\rho$ .

When there is pressure, then in general a is nonzero; in fact,

$$
\mathbf {a} = \nabla \log | \boldsymbol {\xi} \cdot \boldsymbol {\xi} | ^ {\frac {1}{2}}.
$$

If we assume homogeneity (Problem 19.37), then $\mathbf{a} = 0$ and

$$
0 = - 2 \sigma^ {2} - \frac {1}{3} \theta^ {2} - 4 \pi (\rho + 3 p),
$$

which again has no solution for $\rho + 3\mathfrak{p} > 0$ .

# Solution 19.38.

(a) A galaxy moves with $\mathbf{x}^{\mathbf{i}} =$ constant implying that $\mathrm{dx}^{\mathrm{i}} = 0$ and hence that $\mathrm{ds}^2 = -\mathrm{dr}^2$ .

(b) For $\mathrm{d}\tau = 0$ , $\mathrm{ds}^2 = \mathbf{g}_{ij} \mathrm{dx}^i \mathrm{dx}^j$ .

(c) If $\mathbf{g}_{0i}$ and $\mathbf{g}_{ij}$ are independent of $\mathbf{x}^i$ , then on constant $r$ hypersurfaces, nothing depends upon $\mathbf{x}^i$ ; the space is homogeneous. On the other hand, a bad choice of coordinates and hence a seemingly distorted spacelike hypersurface may cause the metric functions of a homogeneous space to depend upon position.

(d) In Problem 14.9 we showed that if the spatial connecting vector between any two nearby geodesics is of constant length, then $\sigma_{\alpha \beta} = \theta = 0$ for the geodesic field. If $g_{0i}$ and $g_{ij}$ are independent of $\tau$ , then the connecting vector will have constant length.

(e) Since $\mathbf{u}^0 = +1$ , $\mathbf{u}^i = 0$ , we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} u _ {i; j} = - u ^ {\gamma} \Gamma_ {\gamma i j} = - \Gamma_ {0 i j} = - \frac {1}{2} \left(g _ {i 0, j} + g _ {0 j, i} - g _ {i j, 0}\right) \tag {1} \\ = \frac {1}{2} g _ {i j, 0} = \frac {1}{2} f \bar {g} _ {i j}. \\ \end{array}
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \theta \equiv u ^ {a}; a = | g | ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \left(| g | ^ {\frac {1}{2}} u ^ {a}\right), a = f ^ {- \frac {3}{2}} \left(f ^ {\frac {3}{2}}\right), 0 \tag {2} \\ = \frac {3}{2} \frac {\mathrm {f}}{\mathrm {f}}. \\ \end{array}
$$

Now, from Equations (1), (2), and the formula (see Problem 5.18)

$$
\sigma_ {\alpha ; \beta} = \frac {1}{2} \left(\mathrm {u} _ {\alpha ; \mu} \mathrm {P} ^ {\mu} _ {\beta} + \mathrm {u} _ {\beta ; \mu} \mathrm {P} ^ {\mu} _ {\alpha}\right) - \frac {1}{3} \theta \mathrm {P} _ {\alpha} \beta \tag {3}
$$

we arrive at

$$
\sigma_ {i j} = \frac {1}{2} \left(u _ {i; j} + u _ {j; i}\right) - \frac {1}{3} \theta g _ {i j} = \frac {1}{2} \dot {f} \bar {g} _ {i j} - \frac {1}{2} \dot {f} \bar {g} _ {i j} = 0. \tag {4}
$$

$$
a ^ {\alpha} = u ^ {\alpha}; \beta u ^ {\beta} = u ^ {\alpha}; 0 = \Gamma^ {\alpha} _ {0 0} \tag {f}
$$

$$
\mathrm {a} _ {\alpha} = \Gamma_ {\alpha 0 0} = \frac {1}{2} \left(\mathrm {g} _ {\alpha 0, 0} + \mathrm {g} _ {\alpha 0, 0} - \mathrm {g} _ {0 0, \alpha}\right) = \mathrm {g} _ {0 \alpha , 0}.
$$

If $\mathbf{a}_{\alpha} = 0$ , then $\mathbf{g}_{0\alpha,0} = 0$ and galaxies fall on geodesics if and only if $\mathbf{g}_{0\mathbf{i},0} = 0$ .

(g) From Problem 7.23 we showed that the necessary condition for a family of hypersurfaces to be orthogonal to $\mathbf{u}$ is $\omega_{\alpha \beta} = 0$ . Since $\mathbf{u} = \partial/\partial \tau$ and the hypersurfaces of constant $\tau$ are spanned by $\partial/\partial \mathbf{x}^{\mathbf{i}}$ , then $\omega_{\alpha \beta} \neq 0$ implies that $(\partial/\partial t) \cdot (\partial/\partial \mathbf{x}^{\mathbf{i}}) \neq 0$ and hence that $\mathbf{g}_{0\mathbf{i}} \neq 0$ . If $\omega_{\alpha \beta,0} \neq 0$ , then $\mathbf{g}_{\alpha \beta,0} \neq 0$ and from part (f), this means that the galaxies do not fall on geodesics.

Solution 19.39. From Solution 14.9 we know that the spatial connecting vector, $\xi$ , between two nearby world lines obeys

$$
\nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \boldsymbol {\xi} = \nabla_ {\boldsymbol {\xi}} \mathbf {u} + (\boldsymbol {\xi} \cdot \mathbf {a}) \mathbf {u}. \tag {1}
$$

Putting $\pmb{\xi} \equiv \mathbf{R}\mathfrak{n}$ in Equation (1) gives

$$
\mathrm {R n} ^ {\alpha}; \beta \mathrm {u} ^ {\beta} + \mathrm {n} ^ {\alpha} \dot {\mathrm {R}} = \mathrm {R n} ^ {\beta} \mathrm {u} ^ {\alpha}; \beta + \mathrm {R u} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {n} ^ {\gamma} \mathrm {a} _ {\gamma} \tag {2}
$$

where $\mathbf{R} \equiv \mathrm{d}\mathbf{R} / \mathrm{d}t = \mathfrak{u}^{\alpha}\mathbf{R}_{,\alpha}$ . If we now dot $\mathfrak{n}^{\alpha}$ into Equation (2) and use the decomposition equation for $\mathfrak{u}_{\alpha};\beta$ (see Problem 5.18)

$$
\mathfrak {u} _ {\alpha ; \beta} = \omega_ {\alpha \beta} + \sigma_ {\alpha \beta} + \frac {1}{3} \theta (\mathfrak {g} _ {\alpha \beta} + \mathfrak {u} _ {\alpha} \mathfrak {u} _ {\beta}) - \mathfrak {a} _ {\alpha} \mathfrak {u} _ {\beta}
$$

we obtain

$$
\begin{array}{l} \dot {\mathbf {R}} = \mathbf {R} \mathrm {n} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {n} ^ {\beta} \left[ \omega_ {\alpha \beta} + \sigma_ {\alpha \beta} + \frac {1}{3} \theta (\mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \beta} + \mathrm {u} _ {\alpha} \mathrm {u} _ {\beta}) - \mathrm {a} _ {\alpha} \mathrm {u} _ {\beta} \right] \\ + \mathrm {R u} ^ {\alpha} \mathrm {n} ^ {\gamma} \mathrm {n} _ {\alpha} \mathrm {a} _ {\gamma}. \tag {3} \\ \end{array}
$$

Now the antisymmetry of $\omega_{\alpha \beta}$ and the fact that $\mathfrak{n}^{\alpha}$ is a spatial unit vector $(\mathfrak{n}^{\alpha}\mathfrak{u}_{\alpha} = 0)$ used in Equation (3) give us

$$
\dot {\frac {\mathbf {R}}{\mathbf {R}}} = \sigma_ {\alpha \beta} \mathfrak {n} ^ {\alpha} \mathfrak {n} ^ {\beta} + \frac {1}{3} \theta .
$$

Averaging $\sigma_{\alpha \beta} \mathfrak{n}^{\alpha} \mathfrak{n}^{\beta}$ over directions produces a term proportional to the trace of $\sigma_{\alpha \beta}$ . Since $\sigma_{\alpha \beta}$ is traceless, we are left with

$$
\left\langle \frac {\dot {\bf R}}{\dot {\bf R}} \right\rangle = \frac {1}{3} \theta .
$$

Solution 19.40. Since $\mathbf{u} = \mathbf{e}_0$ we have

$$
\mathrm {u} _ {\alpha ; \beta} = \mathrm {u} _ {\alpha , \beta} - \Gamma^ {\gamma} _ {\alpha \beta} \mathrm {u} _ {\gamma} = 0 + \Gamma^ {0} _ {\alpha \beta}
$$

so that

$$
\theta = \mathrm {u} _ {\alpha ; \beta} \mathrm {g} ^ {\alpha \beta} = \Gamma_ {\alpha \beta} ^ {0} \mathrm {g} ^ {\alpha \beta} = - (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} [ \mathrm {g} ^ {0 \alpha} (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} ], a \tag {1}
$$

(see part (f) of Problem 7.7). Hence for a Robertson-Walker cosmology

$$
\theta = (- \mathbf {g}) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} (- \mathbf {g}) _ {, 0} ^ {\frac {1}{2}} = 3 \dot {\mathbf {R}} / \mathbf {R}.
$$

The rotation is

$$
\omega_ {\alpha \beta} = \mathrm {P} _ {\beta} ^ {\gamma} \mathrm {u} _ {[ a; \gamma ]} = 0
$$

since $\Gamma_{\alpha \beta}^{0}$ is symmetric. For the shear we have

$$
\sigma_ {\alpha \beta} = \mathrm {P} _ {\beta} ^ {\gamma} \mathrm {u} _ {(\alpha ; \gamma)} - \frac {1}{3} \theta \mathrm {P} _ {\alpha \beta}
$$

$$
\sigma_ {0 a} = 0
$$

$$
\begin{array}{l} \sigma_ {i j} = u _ {(i, j)} - \frac {1}{3} \theta g _ {i j} \\ = - \frac {1}{2} \left(\mathrm {g} _ {0 \mathrm {i}, \mathrm {j}} + \mathrm {g} _ {0 \mathrm {j}, \mathrm {i}} - \mathrm {g} _ {\mathrm {i j}, 0}\right) - \frac {\dot {\mathrm {R}}}{\mathrm {R}} \mathrm {g} _ {\mathrm {i j}} \\ = \frac {1}{2} g _ {i j, 0} - \dot {R} / R g _ {i j} = 0 \\ \end{array}
$$

For the anisotropic metric, Equation (1) still holds, so

$$
\begin{array}{l} \theta = (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} (- \mathrm {g}) _ {, 0} ^ {\frac {1}{2}} = \dot {\mathrm {a}} + \dot {\mathrm {b}} + \dot {\mathrm {c}} \\ \omega_ {\alpha \beta} = 0 \\ \sigma_ {0 \alpha} = 0 \\ \sigma_ {i j} = \frac {1}{2} g _ {i j, 0} - \frac {1}{3} \theta g _ {i j} = \frac {1}{3} A g _ {i j} \\ \end{array}
$$

where

$$
A = \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} 2 \dot {a} - \dot {b} - \dot {c} & \text {f o r} \quad i = j = x \\ 2 \dot {b} - \dot {a} - \dot {c} & \text {f o r} \quad i = j = y \\ 2 \dot {c} - \dot {a} - \dot {b} & \text {f o r} \quad i = j = z. \end{array} \right.
$$

Solution 19.41. The quickest way to compute the Ricci tensor for this metric is to use the formulas of Problem 9.33. We have

$$
\mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {i j}} = - \frac {1}{2} \dot {\mathrm {g}} _ {\mathrm {i j}} \tag {1}
$$

$$
0 = \mathrm {R} _ {\mathrm {t}} ^ {\mathrm {t}} = \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {i j}} \mathrm {K} ^ {\mathrm {i j}} - \dot {\mathrm {K}} \tag {2}
$$

$$
0 = \mathrm {R} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {t}} = \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {j}, \mathrm {i}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} - \mathrm {K}, \mathrm {j} \tag {3}
$$

$$
0 = \mathrm {R} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} = \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {K} - \dot {\mathrm {K}} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} \tag {4}
$$

where a dot denotes $\mathrm{d} / \mathrm{dt}$ . We have set $^{(3)}\mathbb{R}_{ij}$ to zero in Equation (4) because the 3-geometry is flat, and we have replaced covariant derivatives by partial derivatives in Equation (3).

Contracting Equation (4) gives $\mathbf{K} = \mathbf{K}^2$ and hence

$$
\mathrm {K} = - 1 / \mathrm {t} \tag {5}
$$

with a suitable choice of the constant of integration. Substituting Equation (5) back into Equation (4) gives

$$
\dot {\mathbf {K}} _ {j} ^ {i} = - \mathbf {K} _ {j} ^ {i} / t
$$

and hence

$$
\mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} = \mathrm {A} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} / \mathrm {t} \tag {6}
$$

where $\mathbf{A}_{\mathbf{j}}^{\mathbf{i}}$ is a constant matrix. We now choose the coordinates so that $\mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{j}}^{\mathbf{i}}$ is diagonal at some time $t = t_0$ . Then by Equation (6) $\mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{j}}^{\mathbf{i}}$ remains diagonal and can be written in the form

$$
\mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} = - \delta_ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {P} _ {\mathrm {j}} / \mathrm {t} \tag {7}
$$

where $\mathbf{P_j} =$ constant. For this equation to be consistent with Equation (5) we must have

$$
\sum_ {i} P _ {i} = 1. \tag {8}
$$

Equation (3) is automatically satisfied, Equation (2) implies

$$
\sum_ {i} P _ {i} ^ {2} = 1 \tag {9}
$$

and Equation (1) becomes

$$
\dot {\mathbf {g}} _ {i j} = - 2 \mathbf {g} _ {i m} \mathbf {K} _ {j} ^ {m} = 2 \mathbf {g} _ {i j} \mathbf {P} _ {j} / t
$$

and implies that

$$
\mathbf {g} _ {\mathrm {i j}} = \delta_ {\mathrm {i j}} \mathbf {t} ^ {2 \mathbf {P} _ {\mathrm {i}}}
$$

Thus the final form of the metric ("Kasner metric") is

$$
d s ^ {2} = - d t ^ {2} + t ^ {2 P _ {1}} d x ^ {2} + t ^ {2 P _ {2}} d y ^ {2} + t ^ {2 P _ {3}} d z ^ {2} \tag {10}
$$

subject to the constraints of Equations (8) and (9).

The volume of this universe is proportional to

$$
\left[ ^ {(3)} \mathbf {g} _ {\mathbf {i j}} \right] ^ {\frac {1}{2}} = \left(t ^ {2 \sum \mathbf {P} _ {\mathbf {i}}}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} = t
$$

which can be contrasted with the $t^{\frac{3}{2}}$ behavior of the radiation-dominated Friedmann model.

A convenient way of representing the constraints of Equations (8) and (9) is to draw a circle of radius 2/3 centered at $y = 1/3$ and to inscribe an equilateral triangle. The y-coordinates of the vertices of the triangle will satisfy Equations (8) and (9). From this we see that, except for the special case $(1,0,0)$ which can easily be shown to represent flat space, two of the P's lie between 0 and 1 and one P lies between 0 and -1/3. Thus space must contract along one axis while expanding along the other two.

# CHAPTER 20: SOLUTIONS

Solution 20.1. Note that there are no “real” (i.e. tidal) gravitational forces; the light goes in a straight line in any inertial frame, but in the accelerated frame of the tube it seems to bend.

Consider two inertial frames - one comoving with the tube at the instant the light enters it - the other comoving with the tube at the instant the light reaches the end. Since the length of the tube is seen in both frames as $\ell$ and since $\theta$ is always small, observers in either frame see it take $\ell / c$ for the light to cross the tube. At this time their relative velocity is $\beta = g \ell / c^2$ . (An observer in the second frame sees the first frame moving upward with velocity $\beta$ .) The direction angles of the photons path are related by (see Problem 1.8 and figure on p. 561):

$$
\cos \psi = \sin \theta \approx \theta = \frac {\cos \psi^ {\prime} + \beta}{1 + \beta \cos \psi^ {\prime}} = \beta .
$$

Therefore

$$
\theta = \mathrm {g} \ell / \mathrm {c} ^ {2}, \quad \mathrm {g} / \mathrm {c} ^ {2} = 1 0 ^ {- 1 6} \mathrm {m} ^ {- 1},
$$

so, if the tube is 10 meters long $\theta = 10^{-15}$ radians, and the total displacement $\frac{1}{2} (\mathrm{g / c}^2)\ell^2\approx 10^{-14}$ meters. This is very small compared to the wave length of light; it is difficult to imagine interferometric (or any other) techniques capable of detecting this. When lasers are used for alignment (as in the Stanford linear accelerator) they are meant to correct for the curvature of the surface of the earth - a much larger effect, obviously, since a light ray at the earth's surface is not bent so strongly as to be pulled into a circular orbit.

![](images/87274c657751d1a728e1412f32542d99f9a7dab6f5c3e5dd39770e5b9f380f82.jpg)

![](images/af0856a4d34f9ecb9508a549c10369d1638237efd3e577d7cea118957bfe5d03.jpg)

Solution 20.2.

![](images/d749c9c96299741b90e7b93e8de4bf9c23fe6970905d288bd55cf8535e6abc33.jpg)

![](images/6a05f796f8a9362c29b8cff8e0fda96789f65fcc6b265c6b2257267d47fe1385.jpg)

![](images/e2dd41a074dd107e23273027cc1527c0ecc6cf3684a3b2ec09c009bde25c2ac0.jpg)

Consider a sequence of freely falling observers, each occupying a local inertial frame of length $\mathrm{d}\ell$ . Let the $\mathfrak{n}^{\text{th}}$ freely falling observer $S_{\mathbf{n}}$ be momentarily at rest relative to the sun when the photon hits the left side of his frame and let him see the photon move at an angle $\theta$ relative to its inertial direction (see figure). When the photon leaves his frame and enters $S_{\mathbf{n + 1}}$ , which is now momentarily at rest, he is falling with speed $\beta = \gcd \ell = \frac{\mathbf{GM}}{\mathbf{R}^2}$ in the $\phi$ direction. This photon enters frame $S_{\mathbf{n + 1}}$ moving in the direction $\theta + \mathrm{d}\theta$ : the transformation of photon angles (see Problem 1.8) is

$$
\cos \psi = \frac {\cos \bar {\psi} - \beta}{1 - \beta \cos \bar {\psi}} = \cos \bar {\psi} - \beta \sin^ {2} \bar {\psi} + \mathcal {O} (\beta^ {2})
$$

so that

$$
\cos \psi - \cos \bar {\psi} = - \beta \sin^ {2} \bar {\psi}
$$

and

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {d} \theta = \beta \sin \psi \approx \beta \sin \phi \\ = \left(\frac {\mathrm {G M}}{\mathrm {R} _ {0} ^ {2} + \ell^ {2}}\right) \frac {\mathrm {R} _ {0}}{\left(\mathrm {R} _ {0} ^ {2} + \ell^ {2}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \mathrm {d} \ell . \tag {1} \\ \end{array}
$$

Thus the total deflection, $\delta$ is

$$
\delta = \int d \theta = G M R _ {0} \int_ {- \infty} ^ {\infty} \frac {d \ell}{\left(R _ {0} ^ {2} + \ell^ {2}\right) ^ {\frac {3}{2}}} = \frac {2 G M}{R _ {0}} \tag {2}
$$

which is one half the observed value!

In performing the calculation we assumed that a sequence of rulers was placed along the line $\mathbf{y} = \mathbb{R}_0$ parallel to the line $\mathbf{y} = 0$ , with the $(\mathfrak{n} + 1)^{\mathrm{th}}$ ruler locally parallel to the $\mathfrak{n}^{\mathrm{th}}$ ruler. It was the deflection with respect to these rulers that we calculated. But we did not correct for the fact that spacetime around the sun is curved (tidal forces!); in fact, a locally parallel sequence of rulers forms a curved line with respect to the $\mathbf{y} = 0$ line and it is with respect to this curved line that we actually calculated the photon deflection. Thus the real deflection, as measured by a distant observer, must include both effects: the deflection of the photon with respect to a set of locally parallel rulers, and the bending of the line (with respect to $\mathbf{y} = 0$ ) of the sequence of rulers.

Solution 20.3. We may consider purely equatorial motion with no loss of generality. In the gravitational field of the sun, the metric may then be approximated by

$$
d s ^ {2} \approx - \left(1 - \frac {2 M}{r}\right) d t ^ {2} + \left(1 + \frac {2 M}{r}\right) \left(d r ^ {2} + r ^ {2} d \phi^ {2}\right). \tag {1}
$$

Null geodesics for this metric have the form

$$
\frac {\mathrm {b}}{\mathrm {r}} = \sin \phi + \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}} (1 - \cos \phi) \tag {2}
$$

![](images/0e1a3ed941bf65ccbb19fdc99de86879b95d10c69cbf5a6b2c0330ac4fb8b481.jpg)

The angle $\alpha$ between the sun and the star (see figure), as measured by an astronomer on earth, satisfies

$$
\begin{array}{l} \tan \alpha = \tan (\pi - \phi_ {\mathbf {E}} + \delta a) \approx - \tan \phi_ {\mathbf {E}} + \frac {\delta a}{\cos^ {2} \phi_ {\mathbf {E}}} \\ = \frac {\mathrm {u} \hat {\phi}}{\mathrm {u} ^ {\hat {\mathrm {r}}}} = \left[ \frac {\left(1 + \frac {\mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) \mathrm {r} \mathrm {d} \phi / \mathrm {d} \lambda}{\left(1 + \frac {\mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) \mathrm {d r} / \mathrm {d} \lambda} \right] _ {\mathrm {E}} = \left[ \mathrm {r} \frac {\mathrm {d} \phi}{\mathrm {d r}} \right] _ {\mathrm {E}} \tag {3} \\ \end{array}
$$

where $\mathbf{u}^{\hat{\phi}}$ and $\mathbf{u}^{\hat{\mathbf{r}}}$ are the orthonormal components of the photon's 4-velocity, and we have taken advantage of the isotropic form of the metric in Equation (1) to define angles. If Equation (2) is now used, Equation (3) becomes

$$
\begin{array}{l} \tan \phi_ {\mathrm {E}} - \frac {\delta a}{\cos^ {2} \phi_ {\mathrm {E}}} = \frac {\sin \phi_ {\mathrm {E}} \cdot \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {b}} (1 - \cos \phi_ {\mathrm {E}})}{\cos \phi_ {\mathrm {E}} + \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {b}} \sin \phi_ {\mathrm {E}}} \\ \approx \tan \phi_ {\mathrm {E}} - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {b}} \frac {(1 - \cos \phi_ {\mathrm {E}})}{\cos^ {2} \phi_ {\mathrm {E}}} \tag {4} \\ \end{array}
$$

and hence

$$
\delta \alpha \cong \frac {2 M}{b} (1 + \cos \alpha) \approx \frac {2 M}{R _ {E}} \left(\frac {1 + \cos \alpha}{1 - \cos \alpha}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}. \tag {5}
$$

It is $\delta \alpha$ which is the deflection angle measured by an astronomer at earth. [See also MTW, Section 40.3, and references given therein.]

Solution 20.4. Since we wish to compute only the lowest order deflection due to $\underline{\mathbf{J}}$ , we can look for terms linear in $\underline{\mathbf{J}}$ and set $\mathbf{M} = \mathbf{0}$ in the line element. The total first order deflection will then be the sum of the usual term linear in $\mathbf{M}$ and a term linear in $\underline{\mathbf{J}}$ . Since the metric (for our purposes) is (see Problem 17.1),

$$
d s ^ {2} = - d t ^ {2} - \frac {4 J}{r} \sin^ {2} \theta d t d \phi + d r ^ {2} + r ^ {2} d \Omega^ {2} \tag {1}
$$

(J points along z-axis) the equations of motion may be deduced from the variational principle (Problem 7.25)

$$
\delta \int \left(- \dot {t} ^ {2} - \frac {4 J}{r} \sin^ {2} \theta \dot {t} \dot {\phi} + r ^ {2} + r ^ {2} \dot {\theta} ^ {2} + r ^ {2} \sin^ {2} \theta \dot {\phi} ^ {2}\right) d \lambda = 0 \tag {2}
$$

where $\dot{\mathbf{t}}\equiv \mathrm{dt} / \mathrm{d}\lambda$ etc., and $\lambda$ is affine parameter. The Euler equations for Equation (2), to first order in J, are

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} \left(2 \dot {\mathrm {t}} + \frac {4 \mathrm {J}}{\mathrm {r}} \sin^ {2} \theta \dot {\phi}\right) = 0 \tag {3a}
$$

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} \left(2 \dot {\phi} r ^ {2} \sin^ {2} \theta - \frac {4 J}{r} \sin^ {2} \theta \dot {t}\right) = 0, \tag {3b}
$$

which imply that

$$
\mathbf {P} _ {0} \equiv \dot {\mathbf {t}} + \frac {2 \mathrm {J}}{\mathrm {r}} \sin^ {2} \theta \dot {\phi} \tag {3c}
$$

$$
\mathrm {P} _ {\phi} \equiv \dot {\phi} \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \sin^ {2} \theta - \frac {2 \mathrm {J}}{\mathrm {r}} \sin^ {2} \theta \mathrm {P} _ {0} \tag {3d}
$$

are constants, and

$$
\ddot {\mathbf {r}} = \mathbf {r} \dot {\theta} ^ {2} + \mathbf {r} \sin^ {2} \theta \dot {\phi} ^ {2} + \frac {4 \mathrm {J}}{\mathbf {r} ^ {2}} \sin^ {2} \theta \dot {\phi} \mathbf {P} _ {0} \tag {3e}
$$

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} \left(\mathrm {r} ^ {2} \dot {\theta}\right) = \mathrm {P} _ {\phi} ^ {2} \cos \theta / \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \sin^ {3} \theta . \tag {3f}
$$

From Equation (3f), one may deduce a third constant of the motion (in addition to $\mathbf{P}_0$ and $\mathbf{P}_{\phi}$ ):

$$
\mathrm {P} _ {\theta} ^ {2} + \frac {\mathrm {P} _ {\phi} ^ {2}}{\sin^ {2} \theta} \equiv \mathrm {L} ^ {2} = \text {c o n s t a n t} \left(\mathrm {P} _ {\theta} \equiv \mathrm {r} ^ {2} \dot {\theta}\right). \tag {4}
$$

We will now decompose the bending into three contributions, each defined by the relationship (see figure) of the ray trajectory (a straight line to lowest order) to $\underline{\mathbf{J}}$ . (Since we are working only to linear order, the total bending must be the sum of such contributions.)

In both cases (a) and (b), the angular momentum of the light beam about the sun's center has zero projection onto $\underline{\mathbf{J}}$ , in case (c) it has unit projection.

![](images/54e1db66f01d14255c115da06c9ac5b546b0b21f0d4de5f9d04a1760e6a2790f.jpg)  
(a)

![](images/52f14c085bec1695d08c6df3d4a062ffc32f77851ff4e68dc3e21de2f9531e2e.jpg)  
(b)

![](images/3c7423abf58a23eab8f481d1fcfb871cd16babcad5b7edf1e488201d8c11609b.jpg)  
(c)

First we consider cases (a) and (b): In both cases (a) and (b) $\mathbf{P}_{\phi} = 0$ (since $\mathbf{P}_{\phi}$ is a constant of the motion). We wish to calculate $\mathbf{r}(\theta)$ . From Equation (3d) we have

$$
\dot {\phi} = \frac {2 \mathrm {J P} _ {0}}{\mathrm {r} ^ {3}} = \mathcal {O} (\mathrm {J}) \tag {5a}
$$

and from Equation (4)

$$
\mathbf {P} _ {\theta} = \mathbf {r} ^ {2} \dot {\theta} = \mathbf {L} = \text {c o n s t a n t}. \tag {5b}
$$

Equations (3e) and (5a), to $\mathcal{O}(\mathbf{J})$ , now give us

$$
\ddot {\mathbf {r}} = \mathbf {r} \dot {\theta} ^ {2}, \tag {5c}
$$

but this result and Equation (5b) guarantee straight-line motion! Thus in cases (a) and (b) $J$ makes no contribution to the bending of light.

Now we consider case (c): In case (c), we may set $\theta = \pi / 2$ , and $\dot{\theta} = 0$ (Equation (3f) guarantees that $\ddot{\theta}$ will always then be 0) and solve our equations accordingly. Using

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} = \frac {\mathrm {d} \phi}{\mathrm {d} \lambda} \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} \phi} = \left(\frac {\mathbf {P} _ {\phi}}{\mathbf {r} ^ {2}} + \frac {2 \mathbf {J P} _ {0}}{\mathbf {r} ^ {3}}\right) \frac {\mathrm {d}}{\mathrm {d} \phi},
$$

and defining $\mathfrak{u} \equiv 1 / \mathfrak{r}$ , $\ell \equiv \mathrm{P}_0 / \mathrm{P}_{\phi}$ , we may put Equation (3e) in the form

$$
u ^ {\prime \prime} + u = - 6 u ^ {2} J \ell - 2 J \ell u ^ {\prime 2} - 4 J \ell u u ^ {\prime \prime} \tag {6}
$$

where $\mathbf{u}' \equiv \mathrm{du} / \mathrm{d}\phi$ . We will solve Equation (6) by perturbation techniques; we define

$$
u = u _ {0} + u _ {1} \tag {7}
$$

with $\mathbf{u_1} = \mathcal{O}(\mathbf{J})$ . To lowest order, the equation is $\mathbf{u}_0^{\prime \prime} + \mathbf{u}_0 = 0$ which implies

$$
u _ {0} = \frac {1}{b} \cos \phi \tag {8}
$$

where $\mathbf{b}$ is the impact parameter and where we have chosen our coordinate system such that the ray moves parallel to the $y$ -axis. For the second order solution, $u_{1}$ must satisfy the equation

$$
\mathrm {u} _ {1} ^ {\prime \prime} + \mathrm {u} _ {1} = - \frac {2 \mathrm {J} \ell}{\mathrm {b} ^ {2}} \left(\sin^ {2} \phi + \cos^ {2} \phi\right) = - \frac {2 \mathrm {J} \ell}{\mathrm {b} ^ {2}} \tag {9a}
$$

or

$$
u _ {1} = - \frac {2 J \ell}{b ^ {2}}. \tag {9b}
$$

Thus, from Equations (7), (8), (9b), the solution for $\mathbf{u}$ is

$$
u \approx \frac {1}{b} \cos \phi - \frac {2 J \ell}{b ^ {2}}. \tag {10}
$$

The bending at each asymptote, $a$ , is found by setting $u = 0$ ( $r = \infty$ ). At one asymptote $\phi \approx \pi / 2 + a$ so that $\cos \phi \approx -a$ and

$$
a \approx - \frac {2 J \ell}{b}. \tag {11}
$$

There is an equal contribution at the other asymptote, so the total bending is

$$
\delta \phi = 2 a = - \frac {4 J}{b ^ {2}} \tag {12}
$$

(where we have used the fact that $\ell = \mathbf{P}_0 / \mathbf{P}_{\phi} \approx \mathfrak{b}^{-1}$ ).

From this result, and the results from cases (a) and (b), we may write the deflection of light by $\mathbf{J}$ as

$$
\delta \phi = - \frac {4 J \cdot n}{b ^ {2}} \tag {13}
$$

where $\mathbf{n}$ is a unit vector in the direction of the angular momentum of the ray about the Sun's center.

Note that the dimensionless small parameter in which we have been expanding is

$$
\left(\frac {\mathrm {J}}{\mathrm {b} ^ {2}}\right) _ {\text {s u n}} = \frac {1 . 7 \times 1 0 ^ {4 8} \mathrm {g m - c m} ^ {2} / \sec}{(7 \times 1 0 ^ {1 0} \mathrm {c m}) ^ {2}} \approx 1 0 ^ {- 1 2} \approx 2 \times 1 0 ^ {- 7} \operatorname {a r c s e c}.
$$

Solution 20.5. The index of refraction of a plasma is $n = (1 - \nu_{\mathfrak{p}}^2 /\nu^2)^{\frac{1}{2}}$ where $\nu_{\mathfrak{p}}$ is the plasma frequency $\nu_{\mathfrak{p}}^{2}\equiv \mathtt{n}_{e}e^{2} / \pi \mathtt{m}_{e}$ . The well-known equation describing the path of a ray through an inhomogeneous medium is [see, e.g. B. Rossi, Optics (Addison-Wesley, 1957), p. 54]

$$
\mathrm {d} (\mathrm {n m}) / \mathrm {d} \ell = \underline {{\nabla}} \mathrm {n} \tag {1}
$$

where $\underline{\mathfrak{m}}$ is a unit vector tangent to the ray and $\ell$ is the distance along the ray. Since the deflection angle we are computing is small, we can correctly find the first-order change in $\underline{\mathfrak{m}}$ by integrating Equation (1) along the unperturbed trajectory $\mathbf{y} = \mathbf{b}, -\infty < \mathbf{x} < +\infty$ . From the formula given for $\mathfrak{n}_{\mathbf{e}}$ we have

$$
n \approx 1 - \frac {1}{2} \frac {\nu p ^ {2}}{\nu^ {2}} \approx 1 -. 0 1 0 1 \left(\frac {r}{R _ {\odot}}\right) ^ {- 6. 5} \left(\frac {\nu}{1 0 ^ {9} H z}\right) ^ {- 2}
$$

or

$$
\underset {\sim} {\nabla} \mathrm {n} \approx \frac {. 0 6 5 7}{R _ {\odot}} \left(\frac {\mathrm {r}}{R _ {\odot}}\right) ^ {- 7. 5} \left(\frac {\nu}{1 0 ^ {9} \mathrm {H z}}\right) ^ {- 2} \hat {\mathrm {r}}. \tag {2}
$$

Thus, using $n = 1$ at $\pm \infty$ and $r = (x^2 + y^2)^{\frac{1}{2}}$ ,

$$
\left. \underline {{\mathfrak {m}}} \right| _ {+ \infty} - \underline {{\mathfrak {m}}} \left| _ {- \infty} \approx \frac {0 6 5 7}{R _ {\odot}} \left(\frac {\nu}{1 0 ^ {9} H z}\right) ^ {- 2} \int_ {- \infty} ^ {\infty} \left(\frac {r}{R _ {\odot}}\right) ^ {- 7. 5} \left[ \hat {x} \left(\frac {x}{r}\right) + \hat {y} \left(\frac {y}{r}\right) \right] d x. \right. \tag {3}
$$

The term in $\hat{\mathbf{x}}$ vanishes by symmetry in $\pm \mathbf{x}$ , while the $\hat{\mathbf{y}}$ term can be written as $2\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}$ . Since $\underline{\mathfrak{m}}$ is a unit vector, for small angles $(\underline{\mathfrak{m}}|_{\infty} - \underline{\mathfrak{m}}|_{-0}) \cdot \hat{\mathbf{y}}$ is the scattering angle we seek. Defining

$$
\eta \equiv \mathrm {b} / \mathrm {R} _ {\odot}, \quad \mathrm {z} \equiv \mathrm {r} / \mathrm {b}
$$

and using

$$
\mathbf {y} = \mathbf {b}, \quad \mathbf {x} ^ {2} = \mathbf {b} ^ {2} \left(\mathbf {z} ^ {2} - 1\right), \quad \mathrm {d} \mathbf {x} = \mathbf {b} \mathbf {z} \left(\mathbf {z} ^ {2} - 1\right) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d} \mathbf {z},
$$

we get

$$
\theta_ {\text {c o r o n a l}} \approx . 1 3 1 \left(\frac {\nu}{1 0 ^ {9} \mathrm {H z}}\right) ^ {- 2} \eta^ {- 6. 5} \int_ {1} ^ {\infty} z ^ {- 7. 5} \left(z ^ {2} - 1\right) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} d z. \tag {4}
$$

On putting $t = 1 / z^2$ , we recognize the integral in Equation (4) as a beta function, $\frac{1}{2} B(3.75, .5) = .158$ . Thus our final expression for the coronal deflection is

$$
\theta_ {\text {c o r o n a l}} \approx . 0 2 1 \left(\frac {\nu}{1 0 ^ {9} \mathrm {H z}}\right) ^ {- 2} \eta^ {- 6. 5}. \tag {5}
$$

The general relativistic deflection, $\theta_{\mathbf{GR}}$ , is

$$
\theta_ {\mathbf {G R}} \approx \frac {8 . 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 6}}{\eta} \tag {6}
$$

(see e.g. Problem 15.6). The two deflections are equal when

$$
\frac {8 . 5 \times 1 0 ^ {- 6}}{\eta} \approx . 0 2 1 \left(\frac {\nu}{1 0 ^ {9} \mathrm {H z}}\right) ^ {- 2} \eta^ {- 6. 5}
$$

or

$$
\eta \equiv \frac {b}{R _ {\odot}} \approx 4. 1 \left(\frac {\nu}{1 0 ^ {9} \mathrm {H z}}\right) ^ {- . 3 6}.
$$

For smaller impact parameters $b$ , the coronal deflection dominates.

Solution 20.6.

![](images/cfcd445977a3298f088f160ffed50eff63e726be547fd3e1fd8d4c93f04bd5e1.jpg)

b and h are measured in units of 4mm.

The relation of $\phi$ and $\theta$ (see figure) is given by Snell's law

$$
\mathbf {n} = \phi / \theta . \tag {1}
$$

The refraction required to simulate the sun's gravitational bending is

$$
\delta \equiv \phi - \theta = 1. 7 5 ^ {\prime \prime} / b. \tag {2}
$$

From Equations (1) and (2) $\theta$ must be

$$
\theta = 1. 7 5 ^ {\prime \prime} / b (n - 1) = 1. 6 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5} / b. \tag {3}
$$

From the geometry of the figure $\theta = -\mathrm{dh} / \mathrm{db}$ , so Equation (3) gives

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d h}}{\mathrm {d b}} = - \frac {1 . 6 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5}}{\mathrm {b}},
$$

$$
h = h _ {0} - 1. 6 \times 1 0 ^ {- 5} \log (b).
$$

Solution 20.7. In the Schwarzschild metric, in curvature coordinates, the equation for noncircular orbits may be put into the form (see e.g. solution to Problem 15.9)

$$
u ^ {\prime \prime} + u = \frac {M}{\tilde {L} ^ {2}} + 3 M u ^ {2} \equiv a + b u ^ {2} \tag {1}
$$

where $\mathbf{u} \equiv 1 / \mathbf{r}$ , $\mathbf{u}'' \equiv \mathrm{d}^2\mathbf{u} / \mathrm{d}\phi^2$ , and $\tilde{\mathbf{L}} \equiv$ angular momentum per unit mass of the orbiting particle (= constant). The solution to the lowest order, the linear portion of Equation (1), is

$$
u _ {0} = a + A \cos \phi \tag {2}
$$

where $A$ is a constant. The second order solution to Equation (1), $u_1$ , then satisfies the equation

$$
\begin{array}{l} u _ {1} ^ {\prime \prime} + u _ {1} \approx b u _ {0} ^ {2} = b (a ^ {2} + 2 a A \cos \phi + A ^ {2} \cos^ {2} \phi) \\ = \mathrm {b a} ^ {2} + \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {b A} ^ {2} + 2 \mathrm {a b A} \cos \phi + \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {b A} ^ {2} \cos 2 \phi . \tag {3} \\ \end{array}
$$

The inhomogeneous solution to Equation (3) is

$$
u _ {1} = b \left(a ^ {2} + \frac {1}{2} A ^ {2}\right) + a b A \phi \sin \phi - (b A ^ {2} / 6) \cos 2 \phi \tag {4}
$$

and

$$
u = \frac {1}{r} \approx u _ {0} + u _ {1}. \tag {5}
$$

The second term in $\mathbf{u}_1$ causes the nonperiodicity; we may put it into more useful form by use of the identity

$$
\epsilon \phi \sin \phi = \cos (\phi - \epsilon \phi) - \cos \phi + \mathcal {O} \left(\epsilon^ {2}\right). \tag {6}
$$

Since ab is a small quantity according to Equation (1), Equation (6) may be used to put Equation (4) into the form

$$
u _ {1} \approx b \left(a ^ {2} + \frac {1}{2} A ^ {2}\right) + A \cos (\phi - a b \phi) - A \cos \phi - (b A ^ {2} / 6) \cos 2 \phi \tag {7}
$$

and the perihelion shift, per revolution, is

$$
\delta \phi = 2 \pi \mathbf {a b} = 6 \pi \mathrm {M} ^ {2} / \tilde {\mathrm {L}} ^ {2}. \tag {8}
$$

We may express $\tilde{\mathbf{L}}$ in terms of the semimajor axis $a$ and eccentricity $e$ of the orbit using standard classical mechanics

$$
\tilde {\mathbf {L}} ^ {2} = \operatorname {M a} \left(1 - e ^ {2}\right). \tag {9}
$$

Equation (8) then becomes

$$
\delta \phi = \frac {6 \pi \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {a} \left(1 - \mathrm {e} ^ {2}\right)}, \tag {10}
$$

which in the limit of small e agrees with the solution of Problem 15.7.

Solution 20.8.

(a) Yes. First note that particle mass is constant:

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {m} ^ {2}}{\mathrm {d} \tau} = - \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {p} \cdot \mathbf {p}}{\mathrm {d} \tau} = - 2 \mathbf {p} \cdot \frac {\mathrm {d} \mathbf {p}}{\mathrm {d} \tau} = 0.
$$

Since $\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{m}\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{m}$ is constant, a factor of $\mathbf{m}$ can be taken out of each term in the force equation leaving

$$
\mathrm {d} \mathbf {u} ^ {\mu} / \mathrm {d} \tau = - (\eta^ {\mu \nu} \Phi_ {, \nu} + \mathbf {u} ^ {a} \Phi_ {, a} \mathbf {u} ^ {\mu})
$$

and showing that particle motion is independent of mass.

(b) Yes. Near the earth the earth is flat. Take $\mathbf{z}$ to be in the vertical direction, then we have $\Phi = \Phi(\mathbf{z})$ and the “source equation” for $\Phi$ is $\partial^2 \Phi / \partial z^2 = 0$ . Above the earth's surface then, the potential is

$$
\Phi = a z + b.
$$

The constant $a$ is determined by dropping a massive particle from rest and setting its initial acceleration to $g$ . With $u^0 = 1$ , $u^j = 0$ in the force equation, we have

$$
- \mathrm {m g} = \mathrm {d p} ^ {z} / \mathrm {d r} = - \mathrm {m} \Phi , z = - \mathrm {m a}
$$

and hence $\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{g}$

Now consider a photon moving vertically upward. It loses energy

$$
\mathrm {d p} ^ {0} = - \mathrm {p} ^ {\alpha} \Phi_ {, \alpha} \mathrm {d x} ^ {0} = - \mathrm {p} ^ {z} \Phi_ {, z} \mathrm {d t}
$$

in time $\mathbf{dt} = \mathbf{dz}$ . Since $\mathbf{p}^z = \mathbf{p}^0$ for a photon, the energy loss is then

$$
\mathrm {d p} ^ {0} = - \mathrm {p} ^ {0} \mathrm {g d z}.
$$

For an earth-based experiment $\mathbf{g}\Delta z << 1$ and

$$
\Delta \mathbf {p} ^ {0} / \mathbf {p} ^ {0} = \Delta \nu / \nu = - \mathbf {g} \Delta z
$$

in agreement with the Pound-Rebka experiment.

(c) No. For a photon $p\beta \mathrm{d}x^{\beta} = 0$ so the path of a photon is governed by

$$
\mathrm {d p} ^ {\mu} = - \mathrm {p} ^ {\alpha} \Phi_ {, \alpha} \mathrm {d x} ^ {\mu} = - \mathrm {d x} ^ {\alpha} \Phi_ {, \alpha} \mathrm {p} ^ {\mu}.
$$

Since dp is proportional to p, the photon must move in a straight line; there is no bending.

[Note: Also in disagreement with observations, this theory predicts a regression of the perihelion of a planet's motion, of magnitude

$$
\delta = 2 \pi \mathrm {M} _ {\odot} ^ {2} / \mathrm {J} ^ {2}
$$

where $\delta =$ shift per orbit, $\mathbf{J} =$ angular momentum of planet per unit mass. For nearly circular orbits, this becomes

$$
\delta = 2 \pi \mathrm {M} _ {\odot} / \mathrm {r}
$$

which is $\approx 13^{\prime \prime}$ /century for Mercury's orbit.]

Solution 20.9. Any standardized clock can be calibrated against a clock at infinity (which measures time). Then the ratio of the ticking rate of

a clock at $(\mathbf{r},\theta)$ , 4-velocity $\mathbf{u}$ , to the rate of a clock at infinity is

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \tau}{\mathrm {d} t} = \frac {1}{u ^ {0} (r , \theta)}. \tag {1}
$$

In Solution 16.19, we showed that $\mathbf{u}^0 =$ constant on the surface of a rigidly rotating, equilibrium configuration of a perfect fluid. Thus

$$
\mathrm {d} \tau / \mathrm {d} t = \text {c o n s t a n t}
$$

and all clocks on the surface of the earth have the same ticking rate! (The doppler shift effect exactly cancels the redshift effect.)

# CHAPTER 21: SOLUTIONS

# Solution 21.1.

(i) One way of finding the formula for the derivative of a determinant is from the identity

$$
\det A = e ^ {\operatorname {T r} \left\{\log A \right\}}
$$

valid for any nonsingular matrix A. (Here logA is the inverse function of $\mathbf{e}^{\mathbf{A}}$ , where the exponential is defined as a power series.) Thus we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} \delta (\det A) = e ^ {\operatorname {T r} \left\{\log A \right\}} \delta (\operatorname {T r} \left\{\log A \right\}) \\ = (\det A) \operatorname {T r} (A ^ {- 1} \delta A) \\ \end{array}
$$

and for $\mathbf{g} \equiv \det(\mathbf{g}_{\alpha \beta})$

$$
\delta \mathbf {g} = \textbf {g} \mathbf {\Delta g} ^ {\alpha \beta} \delta \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha} \beta
$$

where we have used the symmetry of $\mathfrak{g}_{\alpha \beta}$ . The desired result follows immediately:

$$
\begin{array}{l} \delta (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} = - \frac {1}{2} (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \delta \mathrm {g} \\ = \frac {1}{2} (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {g} ^ {\alpha \beta} \delta \mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \beta}. \\ \end{array}
$$

(ii) From the definition of $\mathbf{g}^{\alpha \beta}$ ,

$$
\mathbf {g} ^ {\alpha \beta} \mathbf {g} _ {\beta \gamma} = \delta_ {\gamma} ^ {\alpha},
$$

we have

$$
\delta \mathbf {g} ^ {\alpha \beta} \mathbf {g} _ {\beta \gamma} + \mathbf {g} ^ {\alpha \beta} \delta \mathbf {g} _ {\beta \gamma} = 0
$$

Multiplication by $\mathbf{g}^{\gamma \delta}$ gives

$$
\delta \mathbf {g} ^ {\alpha \beta} \delta_ {\beta} ^ {\delta} + \mathbf {g} ^ {\gamma \delta} \mathbf {g} ^ {\alpha \beta} \delta \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha} \beta = 0
$$

and hence the answer:

$$
\delta \mathbf {g} ^ {\alpha \delta} = - \mathbf {g} ^ {\gamma \delta} \mathbf {g} ^ {\alpha \beta} \delta \mathbf {g} _ {\beta \gamma}.
$$

Solution 21.2. The variation in $\mathbf{L}$ due to changes in $\Phi^{\mathbf{A}}$ is given by

$$
(- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \delta \mathrm {L} \left(\Phi^ {\mathbf {A}}, \Phi_ {, \mu^ {\prime}} ^ {\mathbf {A}}, \mathbf {g} _ {\mu \nu}\right) = \left(\frac {\partial \mathrm {L}}{\partial \Phi^ {\mathbf {A}}} \delta \Phi^ {\mathbf {A}} + \frac {\partial \mathrm {L}}{\partial \Phi_ {, \mu} ^ {\mathbf {A}}} \delta \Phi_ {, \mu} ^ {\mathbf {A}}\right) (- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}. \tag {1}
$$

The operation of varying $\Phi^{\mathbf{A}}$ commutes with partial differentiation

$$
\delta (\Phi_ {, \mu} ^ {\mathbf {A}}) = (\delta \Phi^ {\mathbf {A}}) _ {, \mu}
$$

so Equation (1) becomes

$$
(- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \delta \mathbf {L} = (- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \left. \frac {\partial \mathbf {L}}{\partial \Phi^ {\mathbf {A}}} \delta \Phi^ {\mathbf {A}} + \left(\frac {\partial \mathbf {L} (- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{\partial \Phi_ {, \mu} ^ {\mathbf {A}}} \delta \Phi^ {\mathbf {A}}\right) _ {, \mu} - \frac {\partial}{\partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\mu}} \left(\frac {\partial \mathbf {L} (- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{\partial \Phi_ {, \mu} ^ {\mathbf {A}}}\right) \delta \Phi^ {\mathbf {A}} \right..
$$

The second term is a perfect divergence, which gives a surface term in the integral $\delta S$ , and hence is zero since $\delta \Phi^{\mathbf{A}}$ is taken to vanish on the surface. Thus

$$
0 = \frac {\delta L}{\delta \Phi^ {A}} = \frac {\partial L}{\partial \Phi^ {A}} - \frac {\partial}{\partial x ^ {\mu}} \left(\frac {\partial L (- g) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{\partial \Phi_ {, \mu} ^ {A}}\right) \frac {1}{(- g) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}
$$

Solution 21.3. Since $\mathbf{L}(-\mathbf{g})^{\frac{1}{2}}\mathbf{d}^{4}\mathbf{x}$ is a scalar, it is unchanged under the infinitesimal coordinate transformation

$$
\overline {{\mathbf {x}}} ^ {\mu} = \mathbf {x} ^ {\mu} + \xi^ {\mu}.
$$

By renaming the dummy variable of integration, $\mathsf{d}^4\overline{\mathbf{x}}$ can be changed back to $\mathsf{d}^4\mathbf{x}$ . The net change in $\mathbf{L}(-\mathbf{g})^{\frac{1}{2}}$ is therefore the Lie derivative (see Problem 8.17), so that

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = \delta S = \int \mathcal {L} _ {\xi} (L (- g) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}) d ^ {4} x \\ = \int \left[ \frac {\delta \underline {{\mathrm {L}}}}{\delta \Phi^ {\mathrm {A}}} \left(\mathcal {Q} _ {\xi} \Phi^ {\mathrm {A}}\right) (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} + \frac {\delta (\mathrm {L} (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}})}{\delta \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \nu}} \mathcal {Q} _ {\xi} \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \nu} \right] d ^ {4} x. \tag {1} \\ \end{array}
$$

The first term in the integrand vanishes by the equation of motion (Problem

21.2), thus

$$
0 = \frac {1}{2} \int \mathrm {T} ^ {\mu \nu} \mathcal {L} _ {\pmb {\xi}} \mathbf {g} _ {\mu \nu} (- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d} ^ {4} \mathbf {x} .
$$

Since

$$
\mathcal {L} _ {\xi} \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \nu} = \xi_ {\mu ; \nu} + \xi_ {\nu ; \mu}
$$

we get

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = \int \mathrm {T} ^ {\mu \nu} \xi_ {\mu ; \nu} (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d} ^ {4} \mathrm {x} \tag {2} \\ = \int \left(\mathrm {T} ^ {\mu \nu} \xi_ {\mu}\right) _ {; \nu} (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d} ^ {4} \mathrm {x} - \int \mathrm {T} _ {; \nu} ^ {\mu \nu} \xi_ {\mu} (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d} ^ {4} \mathrm {x}. \\ \end{array}
$$

The first term is

$$
\int \left(\mathrm {T} ^ {\mu \nu} \xi_ {\mu}\right) _ {; \nu} (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d} ^ {4} \mathrm {x} = \int \left((- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {T} ^ {\mu \nu} \xi_ {\mu}\right) _ {, \nu} \mathrm {d} ^ {4} \mathrm {x}
$$

and gives a surface integral which vanishes. Since $\xi_{\mu}$ is arbitrary,

Equation (2) then gives

$$
\mathrm {T} _ {; \nu} ^ {\mu \nu} = 0.
$$

Solution 21.4.

(a) Under the independent variations

$$
\mathsf {g} _ {\mu \nu} \to \mathsf {g} _ {\mu \nu} + \delta \mathsf {g} _ {\mu \nu}
$$

$$
\Gamma_ {\mu \nu} ^ {a} \to \Gamma_ {\mu \nu} ^ {a} + \delta \Gamma_ {\mu \nu} ^ {a}
$$

we have

$$
\delta \left((- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {R}\right) = (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {g} ^ {\mu \nu} \delta \mathrm {R} _ {\mu \nu} + \mathrm {R} \delta (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} + (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {R} _ {\mu \nu} \delta \mathrm {g} ^ {\mu \nu}. \tag {1}
$$

Since

$$
\mathrm {R} _ {\mu \nu} = \Gamma_ {\mu \nu , a} ^ {a} - \Gamma_ {\mu a, \nu} ^ {a} + \Gamma_ {a \sigma} ^ {a} \Gamma_ {\mu \nu} ^ {\sigma} - \Gamma_ {\nu \sigma} ^ {a} \Gamma_ {\mu a} ^ {\sigma}, \tag {2}
$$

the first term on the right-hand side of Equation (1) becomes

$$
\begin{array}{l} (- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {g} ^ {\mu \nu} \delta \mathsf {R} _ {\mu \nu} = ((- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {g} ^ {\mu \nu} \delta \Gamma_ {\mu \nu} ^ {a}), _ {a} - ((- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {g} ^ {\mu \nu} \delta \Gamma_ {\mu a} ^ {a}), _ {\nu} \\ - \left((- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {g} ^ {\mu \nu}\right) _ {, \alpha} \delta \Gamma_ {\mu \nu} ^ {\alpha} + \left((- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {g} ^ {\mu \nu}\right) _ {, \nu} \delta \Gamma_ {\mu \alpha} ^ {\alpha} \tag {3} \\ + (- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {g} ^ {\mu \nu} (\delta \Gamma_ {\alpha \sigma} ^ {\alpha} \Gamma_ {\mu \nu} ^ {\sigma} + \Gamma_ {\alpha \sigma} ^ {\alpha} \delta \Gamma_ {\mu \nu} ^ {\sigma} - \delta \Gamma_ {\nu \sigma} ^ {\alpha} \Gamma_ {\mu \alpha} ^ {\sigma} - \Gamma_ {\nu \sigma} ^ {\alpha} \delta \Gamma_ {\mu \alpha} ^ {\sigma}). \\ \end{array}
$$

The first two terms on the right-hand side of Equation (3) are divergences, which therefore do not contribute to $\delta S$ . (The integral of a divergence can be converted to a surface integral and $\delta \Gamma = 0$ on the surface of the region of integration.) By appropriate relabeling of dummy indices, Equation (3) can be written

$$
(- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {g} ^ {\mu \nu} \delta \mathbf {R} _ {\mu \nu} = (- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \left(\mathbf {A} _ {\sigma} ^ {\mu \nu} + \delta_ {\sigma} ^ {\nu} \mathbf {B} ^ {\mu}\right) \delta \Gamma_ {\mu \nu} ^ {\sigma} \tag {4}
$$

where

$$
\mathrm {A} _ {\sigma} ^ {\mu \nu} \equiv \mathrm {g} ^ {\mu \nu} \mathrm {T} _ {\alpha \sigma} ^ {\alpha} - (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \left[ (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {g} ^ {\mu \nu} \right] _ {, \sigma} - \mathrm {g} ^ {\alpha \nu} \Gamma_ {\alpha \sigma} ^ {\mu} - \mathrm {g} ^ {\alpha \mu} \Gamma_ {\alpha \sigma} ^ {\nu} \qquad \qquad (5)
$$

$$
\mathbf {B} ^ {\mu} \equiv (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \left[ (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {g} ^ {\mu \beta} \right] _ {, \beta} + \mathrm {g} ^ {\alpha \beta} \Gamma_ {\alpha \beta} ^ {\mu}. \tag {6}
$$

By Problem 21.1 we have

$$
\delta [ (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} ] = - \frac {1}{2} (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \nu} \delta \mathrm {g} ^ {\mu \nu} \tag {7}
$$

so

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = \delta \mathsf {S} = (1 6 \pi) ^ {- 1} \int (- \mathsf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathsf {d} ^ {4} \mathbf {x} (\mathsf {A} _ {\sigma} ^ {\mu \nu} + \delta_ {\sigma} ^ {\nu} \mathsf {B} ^ {\mu}) \delta \Gamma_ {\mu \nu} ^ {\sigma} \\ + (1 6 \pi) ^ {- 1} \int (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {d} ^ {4} \mathbf {x} \Big (\mathrm {R} _ {\mu \nu} - \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {\nabla} \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \nu} \mathrm {R} \Big) \delta \mathbf {g} ^ {\mu \nu} \\ + \int \frac {\delta \left(\mathrm {L} _ {\text {m a t t e r}} (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}\right)}{\delta \mathrm {g} ^ {\mu \nu}} \delta \mathrm {g} ^ {\mu \nu} \mathrm {d} ^ {4} x. \tag {8} \\ \end{array}
$$

Since $\delta \Gamma_{\mu \nu}^{\sigma}$ is symmetric, the first term in Equation (8) gives

$$
\mathrm {A} _ {\sigma} ^ {\mu \nu} + \frac {1}{2} \delta_ {\sigma} ^ {\nu} \mathrm {B} ^ {\mu} + \frac {1}{2} \delta_ {\sigma} ^ {\mu} \mathrm {B} ^ {\nu} = 0. \tag {9}
$$

Contracting on $\sigma$ and $\nu$ in Equation (9) yields

$$
\mathrm {A} _ {\sigma} ^ {\mu \sigma} + 2 \mathrm {B} ^ {\mu} + \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {B} ^ {\mu} = 0. \tag {10}
$$

But contracting Equation (5) and comparing with Equation (6) shows

$$
\mathrm {A} _ {\sigma} ^ {\mu \sigma} = - \mathrm {B} ^ {\mu}. \tag {11}
$$

Equations (10) and (11) imply $\mathbf{B}^{\mu} = 0$ , so by Equation (9)

$$
\mathrm {A} _ {\sigma} ^ {\mu \nu} = 0. \tag {12}
$$

If the explicit expression in Equation (5) is used, Equation (12) can be written

$$
\mathrm {g} ^ {\mu \nu} \mathrm {C} _ {\sigma} = \mathrm {g} _ {, \sigma} ^ {\mu \nu} + \mathrm {g} ^ {a \nu} \Gamma_ {a \sigma} ^ {\mu} + \mathrm {g} ^ {a \mu} \Gamma_ {a \sigma} ^ {\nu} \tag {13}
$$

where

$$
\mathrm {C} _ {\sigma} \equiv (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} (- \mathrm {g}) _ {, \sigma} ^ {\frac {1}{2}} - \Gamma_ {a \sigma} ^ {a}. \tag {14}
$$

When we multiply Equation (13) by $\mathbf{g}_{\mu \nu}$ we find that

$$
\begin{array}{l} 4 \mathrm {C} _ {\sigma} = \mathrm {g} ^ {\mu \nu} _ {, \sigma} \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \nu} + 2 \Gamma_ {a \sigma} ^ {\alpha} \\ = - 2 (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} (- \mathrm {g}) _ {, \sigma} ^ {\frac {1}{2}} + 2 \Gamma_ {a \sigma} ^ {\alpha} \\ = - 2 \mathrm {C} _ {\sigma} \\ \end{array}
$$

(where Problem 21.1 has been used in the second line) and therefore that $\mathbf{C}_{\sigma} = 0$ . If we define

$$
\Gamma_ {\lambda \alpha \sigma} \equiv \mathrm {g} _ {\lambda \mu} \Gamma_ {\alpha \sigma} ^ {\mu} \tag {15}
$$

and if we now multiply Equation (13) by $\mathbf{g}_{\lambda \mu} \mathbf{g}_{\gamma \nu}$ , we get

$$
0 = - \mathrm {g} _ {\gamma \lambda , \sigma} + \Gamma_ {\lambda \gamma \sigma} + \Gamma_ {\gamma \lambda \sigma} \tag {16}
$$

and thus

$$
\frac {1}{2} \left(\mathrm {g} _ {\gamma \lambda , \sigma} + \mathrm {g} _ {\gamma \sigma , \lambda} - \mathrm {g} _ {\sigma \lambda , \gamma}\right) = \Gamma_ {\gamma \lambda \sigma} \tag {17}
$$

which is one of the required results.

We now return to Equation (8), and use Problem 21.3:

$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {\delta \left(\mathrm {L} _ {\text {m a t t e r}} (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}\right)}{\delta \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \nu}} \delta \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \nu} = \frac {\delta \left(\mathrm {L} _ {\text {m a t t e r}} (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}\right)}{\delta \mathrm {g} ^ {\mu \nu}} \delta \mathrm {g} ^ {\mu \nu} = \frac {1}{2} (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {T} ^ {\mu \nu} \delta \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \nu} \tag {18} \\ = - \frac {1}{2} (- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {T} _ {\mu \nu} \delta \mathbf {g} ^ {\mu \nu}. \\ \end{array}
$$

We conclude that

$$
(1 6 \pi) ^ {- 1} \left(\mathrm {R} _ {\mu \nu} - \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \nu} \mathrm {R}\right) - \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {T} _ {\mu \nu} = 0
$$

or

$$
\mathrm {G} _ {\mu \nu} = 8 \pi \mathrm {T} _ {\mu \nu}. \tag {19}
$$

(b) Although $\Gamma_{\mu \nu}^{a}$ (now taken to be the Christoffel symbols formed from $\mathfrak{g}_{\alpha \beta}$ ) is not a tensor, $\delta \Gamma_{\mu \nu}^{a}$ is a tensor (Problem 8.26). Thus we can simplify the calculation of $\delta \mathbb{R}_{\mu \nu}$ by working in a local inertial frame, where the $\Gamma$ 's vanish. In this frame, Equation (2) gives

$$
\delta \mathbf {R} _ {\mu \nu} = \delta \Gamma_ {\mu \nu , a} ^ {a} - \delta \Gamma_ {\mu a, \nu} ^ {a} = \delta \Gamma_ {\mu \nu ; a} ^ {a} - \delta \Gamma_ {\mu a; \nu} ^ {a}. \tag {20}
$$

Since this is a tensor equation, it is valid in any coordinate system. The $\Gamma$ 's are Christoffel symbols, so $g_{\mu \nu; a} = 0$ and

$$
\begin{array}{l} (- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {g} ^ {\mu \nu} \delta \mathsf {R} _ {\mu \nu} = (- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \left[ (\mathbf {g} ^ {\mu \nu} \delta \Gamma_ {\mu \nu} ^ {a}); a - (\delta \Gamma_ {\mu a} ^ {a} \mathbf {g} ^ {\mu \nu}); _ {\nu} \right] \\ = \left((- g) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} g ^ {\mu \nu} \delta \Gamma_ {\mu \nu} ^ {a}\right) _ {, a} - \left(\delta \Gamma_ {\mu a} ^ {a} g ^ {\mu \nu} (- g) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}\right), \\ \end{array}
$$

(where we have used Problem 7.7(g) in the second line). Since this is a perfect divergence, it does not contribute to $\delta S$ . The remaining terms in Equations (1) and (18) lead to Equation (19) as in part (a).

Solution 21.5. Taking the variation of the action

$$
S = - (8 \pi) ^ {- 1} \int (\Phi_ {; a} \Phi^ {; a} + m ^ {2} \Phi^ {2}) (- g) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} d ^ {4} x
$$

givessus

$$
0 = \frac {\delta \mathbf {L}}{\delta \Phi} = \left[ 2 \mathfrak {m} ^ {2} \Phi - 2 (- \mathfrak {g}) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} [ (- \mathfrak {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \Phi^ {, a} ] _ {, a} \right] (- \mathfrak {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}
$$

or

$$
\square \Phi - \mathrm {m} ^ {2} \Phi = 0. \tag {1}
$$

From Problem 21.3 the stress-energy is

$$
