\begin{array}{l} T _ {\mu \nu} \equiv - 2 (- g) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \frac {\delta [ (- g) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} L ]}{\delta g ^ {\mu \nu}} \\ = \frac {1}{4 \pi} (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {- \frac {1}{2}} \left[ \Phi_ {, \mu} \Phi_ {, \nu} (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} - \frac {1}{2} (\Phi_ {, a} \Phi^ {, a} + \mathrm {m} ^ {2} \Phi^ {2}) (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \nu} \right] \\ = \frac {1}{4 \pi} \left[ \Phi_ {, \mu} \Phi_ {, \nu} - \frac {1}{2} g _ {\mu \nu} \left(\Phi_ {, a} \Phi^ {, a} + m ^ {2} \Phi^ {2}\right) \right] \tag {2} \\ \end{array}
$$

and hence its divergence is

$$
\mathrm {T} _ {; \nu} ^ {\mu \nu} = \frac {1}{4 \pi} \left(\Phi_ {; \nu} ^ {, \mu} \Phi_ {; \nu} ^ {, \nu} + \Phi_ {; \nu} ^ {, \mu} \square \Phi - \Phi_ {, a} ^ {; \mu} \Phi_ {, a} ^ {, a} - \mathrm {m} ^ {2} \Phi \Phi_ {, a} ^ {, \mu}\right).
$$

For a scalar field $\Phi$ , we also have the relation

$$
\Phi_ {; \nu} ^ {\prime \mu} = \Phi_ {, \nu} ^ {i \mu}, \tag {4}
$$

thus Equation (3) becomes

$$
\mathrm {T} _ {; \nu} ^ {\mu \nu} = \frac {\Phi^ {\prime} \mu}{4 \pi} (\square \Phi - \mathrm {m} ^ {2} \Phi) \tag {5}
$$

which, by Equation (1), vanishes.

Solution 21.6. Varying the action integral gives us

$$
\begin{array}{l} \frac {\delta \left(\mathrm {L} [ - \mathrm {g} ] ^ {\frac {1}{2}}\right)}{\delta \mathrm {A} _ {\mu}} = - \frac {1}{4 \pi} \frac {\delta \left(\mathrm {A} _ {[ \mu ; \nu ]} \mathrm {A} ^ {[ \mu ; \nu ]} [ - \mathrm {g} ] ^ {\frac {1}{2}}\right)}{\delta \mathrm {A} _ {\mu}} = - \frac {1}{4 \pi} \frac {\delta \left(\mathrm {A} _ {[ \mu , \nu ]} \mathrm {A} ^ {[ \mu , \nu ]} [ - \mathrm {g} ] ^ {\frac {1}{2}}\right)}{\delta \mathrm {A} _ {\mu}} \\ = - \frac {1}{4 \pi} \cdot 2 \cdot 2 \cdot \frac {\partial}{\partial_ {\mathbf {x}} ^ {\mu}} \left(\mathrm {A} ^ {[ \mu , \nu ]} [ - \mathbf {g} ] ^ {\frac {1}{2}}\right) = - \frac {1}{2 \pi} \frac {\partial}{\partial_ {\mathbf {x}} ^ {\mu}} \left(\mathrm {F} ^ {\nu \mu} [ - \mathbf {g} ] ^ {\frac {1}{2}}\right) \\ = - \frac {\left[ - \mathrm {g} \right] ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{2 \pi} \mathrm {F} _ {; \mu} ^ {\nu \mu}, \tag {1} \\ \end{array}
$$

where we have used Problem 7.7 (i).

The prescription for the stress-energy gives

$$
\begin{array}{l} T _ {\alpha \beta} \equiv - 2 \frac {\delta L}{\delta g ^ {\alpha \beta}} + L g _ {\alpha \beta} = \frac {1}{8 \pi} \frac {\delta \left(F _ {\mu \nu} F _ {\sigma \tau} g ^ {\mu \sigma} g ^ {\nu \tau}\right)}{\delta g ^ {\alpha \beta}} - \frac {1}{1 6 \pi} F _ {\mu \nu} F ^ {\mu \nu} g _ {\alpha \beta} \tag {2} \\ = \frac {1}{4 \pi} F _ {\alpha} ^ {\nu} F _ {\beta \nu} - \frac {1}{1 6 \pi} F _ {\mu \nu} F ^ {\mu \nu} g _ {\alpha} \beta . \\ \end{array}
$$

Consider now the Lagrangian density

$$
\mathrm {L} = \left(- \frac {1}{1 6 \pi} \mathrm {F} _ {\mu \nu} \mathrm {F} ^ {\mu \nu} - \frac {1}{4 \pi} \mathrm {F} ^ {\mu \nu} \mathrm {A} _ {\mu ; \nu}\right) (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}. \tag {3}
$$

Variation of L with respect to $\mathbf{A}_{\mu}$ yields

$$
\mathbf {F} _ {; \nu} ^ {\mu \nu} = 0. \tag {4}
$$

Variation of L with respect to $\mathbf{F}^{\mu \nu}$ yields

$$
\mathbf {F} ^ {\mu \nu} = 2 \mathbf {A} ^ {[ \nu ; \mu ]}. \tag {5}
$$

Solution 21.7. Variation of the Lagrangian with respect to $\Phi$ yields

$$
(- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \left(\mathbf {R} + \frac {\omega}{\Phi^ {2}} \Phi_ {, a} \Phi^ {, a}\right) = - 2 \omega \frac {\partial}{\partial \mathbf {x} ^ {a}} \left(\frac {\Phi^ {, a} (- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}}{\Phi}\right)
$$

and thus

$$
2 \omega \Phi^ {- 1} \square \Phi - \omega \Phi^ {- 2} \Phi_ {, a} \Phi^ {, a} + R = 0. \tag {1}
$$

(We have used here

$$
\square \Phi \equiv \Phi_ {, a} ^ {; a} = \frac {1}{(- g) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}} \left. (\Phi^ {, a} (- g) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}) _ {, a} \cdot\right)
$$

Variation of L with respect to $\mathbf{g}_{\alpha \beta}$ involves three terms:

$$
\frac {\delta \left(- \omega \Phi , ^ {a} \Phi , ^ {b} \beta_ {g _ {a} \beta} \Phi^ {- 1} (- g) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}\right)}{\delta g _ {a \beta}} = - \frac {\omega}{\Phi} \left[ \Phi , ^ {a} \Phi , ^ {b} (\neg a) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} - \frac {1}{2} \Phi , ^ {a} \Phi , _ {\mu} g ^ {a \beta} (- g) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \right] \tag {2a}
$$

and

$$
\mathrm {(i i)} \qquad \delta (\Phi \mathsf {R} (- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}) = (- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \Phi \mathbf {G} ^ {\alpha \beta} \delta \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \beta} + \Phi (- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {g} ^ {\alpha \beta} \delta \mathsf {R} _ {\alpha \beta}, \qquad \mathrm {(2 b)}
$$

$$
(- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {g} ^ {a \beta} \delta \mathsf {R} _ {a \beta} = \frac {\partial}{\partial \mathbf {x} ^ {\gamma}} \left[ (- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} (\mathbf {g} ^ {a \beta} \delta \Gamma_ {a \beta} ^ {\gamma} - \mathbf {g} ^ {a \gamma} \delta \Gamma_ {a \tau} ^ {\tau}) \right] \qquad (2 c)
$$

(see Problem 21.4). Also, we have from the expression for the $\Gamma$ 's as derivatives of the g's

$$
\begin{array}{l} \delta \Gamma_ {\mu \nu} ^ {\lambda} = - \mathrm {g} ^ {\lambda \rho} \delta \mathrm {g} _ {\rho \sigma} \Gamma_ {\mu \nu} ^ {\sigma} + \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {g} ^ {\lambda \rho} \left[ \left(\delta \mathrm {g} _ {\rho \mu}\right), _ {\nu} + \left(\delta \mathrm {g} _ {\rho \nu}\right), _ {\mu} - \left(\delta \mathrm {g} _ {\mu \nu}\right), _ {\rho} \right] \tag {2d} \\ = \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {g} ^ {\lambda \rho} [ (\delta \mathbf {g} _ {\rho \mu}) _ {; \nu} + (\delta \mathbf {g} _ {\rho \nu}) _ {; \mu} - (\delta \mathbf {g} _ {\mu \nu}) _ {; \rho} ]. \\ \end{array}
$$

Using Equations (2c) and (2d), we can then write

$$
\Phi (- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathbf {g} ^ {\alpha \beta} \delta \mathbf {R} _ {\alpha \beta} \rightarrow \delta \mathbf {g} ^ {\alpha \beta} (- \mathbf {\Phi} _ {, \alpha ; \beta} + \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \beta} \square \Phi) (- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \qquad \qquad (2 \mathrm {e})
$$

where $\rightarrow$ denotes the operation of dropping all exact divergences (which vanish when integrated over). Combining Equations (2b) and (2e) we have

$$
\delta (\Phi \mathbf {R} (- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}) = (- \mathbf {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \delta \mathbf {g} ^ {\alpha \beta} [ - \Phi_ {, \alpha ; \beta} + \mathbf {g} _ {\alpha \beta} \square \Phi + \Phi \mathbf {G} _ {\alpha \beta} ]. (2 \mathrm {f})
$$

The remaining term needed is

$$
1 6 \pi \frac {\delta \left(\mathrm {L} _ {\text {m a t t e r}} (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}\right)}{\delta \mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \beta}} = - 8 \pi (- \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {T} ^ {\alpha \beta}. \tag {2g}
$$

From Equations (2a) and (2f), and (2g), we get the field equations obtained by varying $\mathbf{g}^{\alpha \beta}$ :

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {G} _ {\alpha \beta} + \Phi^ {- 1} \left(\mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \beta} \square \Phi - \Phi_ {, \alpha ; \beta}\right) - \omega \Phi^ {- 2} \left(\Phi_ {, \alpha} \Phi_ {, \beta} - \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {g} _ {\alpha \beta} \Phi_ {, \mu} \Phi^ {\prime , \mu}\right) \tag {3} \\ - 8 \pi \Phi^ {- 1} T _ {\alpha} \beta = 0. \\ \end{array}
$$

Solution 21.8. For simplicity choose coordinates (Gaussian normal coordinates; see Problem 8.25) in which the metric takes the form $\mathrm{ds}^2 = \mathrm{dn}^2 + (^3)\mathbf{g}_{ij}\mathrm{dx}^i\mathrm{dx}^j$ . The coordinates $\mathbf{x}^i (\mathbf{i} = 1,2,3)$ span the surface layer which is taken to be at $\mathfrak{n} = 0$ . From the initial value equations we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} G _ {j} ^ {i} = ^ {(3)} G _ {j} ^ {i} + \left(K _ {j} ^ {i} - \delta_ {j} ^ {i} \operatorname {T r} K\right) _ {, n} - (\operatorname {T r} K) K _ {j} ^ {i} + \frac {1}{2} \delta_ {j} ^ {i} (\operatorname {T r} K) ^ {2} \\ + \frac {1}{2} \delta_ {j} ^ {i} \operatorname {T r} \left(\mathrm {K} ^ {2}\right) = 8 \pi \mathrm {T} _ {j} ^ {i}. \\ \end{array}
$$

We now integrate over $\mathfrak{n}$ from $-\epsilon$ to $+\epsilon$ , using the fact that $\mathfrak{n}$ represents proper distance perpendicular to the 3-surface, and we let $\epsilon \rightarrow 0$ . Since the intrinsic geometry of the 3-surface is well defined, the only term that survives this limiting integration is the term involving the derivatives of the extrinsic curvature, so for [K] the discontinuity in $\mathbf{K}$ , we find

$$
\left[ \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} \right] - \delta_ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} \operatorname {T r} [ \mathrm {K} ] = 8 \pi \mathrm {S} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i}}
$$

or

$$
\operatorname {T r} [ \mathrm {K} ] = - 4 \pi \mathrm {S} _ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} = - 4 \pi^ {(3)} \mathrm {g} _ {\mathrm {i j}} \mathrm {S} ^ {\mathrm {i j}}
$$

and

$$
\left[ \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} \right] = 8 \pi \left(\mathrm {S} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} - \frac {1}{2} \delta_ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {S} _ {\mathrm {k}} ^ {\mathrm {k}}\right).
$$

Solution 21.9. From the initial value equations, we have

$$
G _ {i} ^ {n} = - \left\{K _ {i} ^ {m} | _ {m} - (T r K) _ {, i} \right\} = - \left\{K _ {i} ^ {m} - \delta_ {i} ^ {m} T r (K) \right\} | _ {m} = 8 \pi T _ {i} ^ {n}.
$$

The discontinuity of this equation across the 3-surface is

$$
\left. \left\{\left[ K _ {i} ^ {m} \right] - \delta_ {i} ^ {m} T r [ K ] \right\} \right| _ {m} = - 8 \pi \left[ T _ {i} ^ {n} \right].
$$

By combining this with our result from Problem 21.8, we have then

$$
S _ {i} ^ {m} \left| _ {m} = - \left[ T _ {i} ^ {n} \right] \right..
$$

Solution 21.10. The surface stress energy of the thin shell of dust is $S^{\alpha \beta} = \sigma u^{\alpha}u^{\beta}$ . From the solution to Problem 21.8, we have

$$
\left[ \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} \right] = 8 \pi \left\{\sigma \mathrm {u} ^ {\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {u} _ {\mathrm {j}} - \frac {1}{2} \delta_ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} (- \sigma) \right\}
$$

or

$$
\left[ \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {i j}} \right] = 8 \pi \sigma \left\{\mathrm {u} _ {\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {u} _ {\mathrm {j}} + \frac {1}{2} ^ {(3)} \mathrm {g} _ {\mathrm {i j}} \right\}. \tag {1}
$$

Since there is no stress-energy outside the shell $\left[\mathbf{T}_{\mathbf{i}}^{\mathbf{n}}\right] = 0$ and the equation of motion (see Problem 21.9) gives us

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = S _ {i} ^ {m} \left| _ {m} = \sigma_ {, m} u ^ {m} u _ {i} + \sigma u ^ {m} \right| _ {m} u _ {i} + \sigma u _ {i} \left| _ {m} u ^ {m} \right. \\ = \frac {\mathrm {d} \sigma}{\mathrm {d} r} u _ {i} + \sigma u _ {i} u ^ {m} | _ {m} + \sigma u _ {i} | _ {m} u ^ {m} \tag {2} \\ \end{array}
$$

By contracting this with $\mathbf{u}$ we verify the second relation

$$
\frac {\mathrm {d} \sigma}{\mathrm {d} r} + \sigma \left. u ^ {m} \right| _ {m} = 0. \tag {3}
$$

Comparison with Equation (2) shows that $\mathbf{u}_{\mathbf{i}}|_{\mathbf{m}}\mathbf{u}^{\mathbf{m}} = 0$ . Thus

$$
\mathbf {a} = \nabla_ {\mathbf {u}} \mathbf {u} = u ^ {i} \nabla_ {i} \left(u ^ {j} e _ {j}\right) = u ^ {i} \left(u ^ {j} \mid_ {i} e _ {j} + u ^ {j} K _ {i j} n\right) = u ^ {i} u ^ {j} K _ {i j} n. \tag {4}
$$

(We have used Equation (2) of Solution 9.32 to get the second-last equality.) The third relation now follows from Equations (1) and (4):

$$
\mathbf {a} ^ {+} - \mathbf {a} ^ {-} = \mathbf {u} ^ {\mathrm {i}} \mathbf {u} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \left[ \mathbf {K} _ {\mathrm {i j}} \right] \mathbf {n} = 4 \pi \sigma \mathbf {n}.
$$

To verify the fourth relation we need to show

$$
\left(\mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {i j}} ^ {+} + \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {i j}} ^ {-}\right) \mathrm {u} ^ {\mathrm {i}} \mathrm {u} ^ {\mathrm {j}} = 0.
$$

We do this by using the initial value equation $\left[\mathbf{G}_{\mathbf{n}}^{\mathbf{n}}\right] = 8\pi \left[\mathbf{T}_{\mathbf{n}}^{\mathbf{n}}\right] = 0$ . Since (3)R is continuous, this gives us

$$
\begin{array}{l} 0 = \left[ \operatorname {T r} \left(\mathrm {K} ^ {2}\right) - \left(\operatorname {T r} \mathrm {K}\right) ^ {2} \right] \\ = K _ {j} ^ {i +} K _ {i} ^ {j +} - K _ {j} ^ {i -} K _ {i} ^ {j -} - \left(K _ {i} ^ {i +}\right) ^ {2} + \left(K _ {i} ^ {i -}\right) ^ {2} \\ = \left(\mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i} +} + \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i} -}\right) \left\{\mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {\mathrm {j} +} - \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i} -} - ^ {(3)} \mathrm {g} ^ {\mathrm {i j}} \left(\mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {a}} ^ {\mathrm {a} +} - \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {a}} ^ {\mathrm {a} -}\right) \right\} \\ = \left(\mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i} +} + \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {i} -}\right) \left\{\left[ \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \right] - \delta_ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \operatorname {T r} [ \mathrm {K} ] \right\}, \\ \end{array}
$$

where we have used the easily verified fact that $\mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{j}}^{\mathbf{i} + }$ and $\mathbf{K}_{\mathbf{j}}^{\mathbf{i} - }$ commute. Since

$$
\left[ \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \right] = 8 \pi \sigma \left(\mathrm {u} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \mathrm {u} _ {\mathrm {i}} + \frac {1}{2} ^ {(3)} \delta_ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {\mathrm {j}}\right).
$$

it follows that

$$
\left[ K _ {i} ^ {i} \right] = 4 \pi \sigma
$$

and that

$$
\left[ \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \right] - \delta_ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \left[ \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {\mathrm {i}} \right] = 8 \pi \sigma \left(\mathrm {u} ^ {\mathrm {j}} \mathrm {u} _ {\mathrm {i}}\right)
$$

and therefore finally

$$
\left(\mathbf {K} _ {\mathbf {j}} ^ {\mathbf {i} +} + \mathbf {K} _ {\mathbf {j}} ^ {\mathbf {i} -}\right) \mathbf {u} ^ {\mathbf {j}} \mathbf {u} _ {\mathbf {i}} = 0.
$$

Solution 21.11. If $\tau$ is proper time measured by an observer at rest in the dust, then the motion of the shell is specified by $\mathbf{r}(\tau)$ , where $\mathbf{r}$ is the radial coordinate of the shell in either the interior or exterior metric. The metric of the shell can be specified by

$$
\mathrm {d s} ^ {2} = - \mathrm {d} \tau^ {2} + \mathrm {R} ^ {2} (\tau) \left(\mathrm {d} \theta^ {2} + \sin^ {2} \theta \mathrm {d} \phi^ {2}\right).
$$

Here $4\pi \mathbf{R}^2 =$ the surface area of the shell at $\pmb{\tau}$ , and thus $\mathbb{R}(\pmb {\tau}) = \mathbb{r}(\pmb {\tau})$

From the second relation in Problem 21.10 we have that

$$
0 = \frac {\mathrm {d} \sigma}{\mathrm {d} \tau} + \sigma \mathrm {u} ^ {\mathrm {i}} _ {\mathrm {i}} = (\sigma \mathrm {u} ^ {\mathrm {i}}) _ {\mathrm {i}} = (\sigma (^ {(3)} \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \mathrm {u} ^ {\mathrm {i}}) _ {\mathrm {, i}} / (^ {(3)} \mathrm {g}) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}.
$$

But $\mathbf{u}^{\tau} = 1$ and $^{(3)}\mathbf{g} = \mathbb{R}^{4}(\tau)$ so that $(\sigma \mathbb{R}^2)_\tau = 0$ . This implies that $4\pi \mathbb{R}^2\sigma = \mu$ , which we identify with the rest mass of the shell, is a constant.

To find the equation of motion we use the junction condition from Problem 21.10

$$
\left[ \mathrm {K} _ {\theta \theta} \right] = 8 \pi \sigma \left(\mathrm {u} _ {\theta} \mathrm {u} _ {\theta} + \frac {1}{2} ^ {(3)} \mathrm {g} _ {\theta \theta}\right) = 4 \pi \sigma^ {(3)} \mathrm {g} _ {\theta \theta} = \mu .
$$

Evaluating $\mathbf{K}_{\theta \theta}$ we have

$$
\mathrm {K} _ {\theta \theta} = - \mathrm {n} _ {\theta ; \theta} = \mathrm {n} _ {\alpha} \Gamma_ {\theta \theta} ^ {\alpha} = - \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {n} ^ {\mathrm {r}} \mathrm {g} _ {\theta \theta , \mathrm {r}} = - \mathrm {r} \mathrm {n} ^ {\mathrm {r}}
$$

and

$$
\left[ \mathrm {K} _ {\theta \theta} \right] = - \mathrm {r} \left(\mathrm {n} ^ {\mathrm {r} +} - \mathrm {n} ^ {\mathrm {r} -}\right) = \mu
$$

where $\mathfrak{n}^{r+}$ and $\mathfrak{n}^{r-}$ are the radial components of the normal evaluated in the exterior and the interior geometry.

By using $\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{n} = 0$ and $\mathbf{n} \cdot \mathbf{n} = -\mathbf{u} \cdot \mathbf{u} = 1$ , we can evaluate the components of $\mathbf{u}$ and $\mathbf{n}$ . Exterior to the shell we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} 1 = \left(1 - \frac {2 M}{r}\right) \left(u ^ {t}\right) ^ {2} - \left(u ^ {r}\right) ^ {2} / \left(1 - \frac {2 M}{r}\right) \\ 0 = n _ {r} u ^ {r} + n _ {t} u ^ {t} \\ 1 = - \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) ^ {- 1} \left(\mathrm {n} _ {\mathrm {t}}\right) ^ {2} + \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{\mathrm {r}}\right) \left(\mathrm {n} _ {\mathrm {r}}\right) ^ {2}, \\ \end{array}
$$

and by eliminating $\mathbf{u}^{\mathrm{t}}, \mathbf{n}_{\mathrm{t}}$ we find

$$
n _ {r} ^ {+} = \left[ \frac {1 + (u ^ {r}) ^ {2} / \left(1 - \frac {2 M}{r}\right)}{\left(1 - \frac {2 M}{r}\right)} \right] ^ {\frac {1}{2}}.
$$

On the shell $\mathbf{r} = \mathbf{R}(r)$ and $\mathbf{u}^{\mathrm{r}} = \mathrm{d}\mathbf{R} / \mathrm{d}r\equiv \dot{\mathbf{R}}$ so the contravariant component of $\mathbf{n}$ , exterior to the shell is

$$
\mathbf {n} ^ {\mathbf {r} +} = \left(1 - \frac {2 \mathbf {M}}{\mathbf {R}} + \dot {\mathbf {R}} ^ {2}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}.
$$

The calculation for $\mathfrak{n}^{\mathbf{r} - }$ is identical, and we get the same answer as above with M set to zero. Thus we have

$$
\mu = - \mathsf {R} (\mathsf {n} ^ {\mathsf {r} +} - \mathsf {n} ^ {\mathsf {r} -}) = - \mathsf {R} \bigg \{\left[ 1 - \frac {2 \mathsf {M}}{\mathsf {R}} + \dot {\mathsf {R}} ^ {2} \right] ^ {\frac {1}{2}} - \left[ 1 + \dot {\mathsf {R}} ^ {2} \right] ^ {\frac {1}{2}} \bigg \}
$$

which can be solved for $\mathbf{M}$ to give the equation of motion

$$
\mathrm {M} = \mu \left(1 + \dot {\mathrm {R}} ^ {2}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} - \mu^ {2} / 2 \mathrm {R}.
$$

If $\mathbf{R} = 0$ at $\mathbf{R} = \infty$ (i.e. infall from rest at $\infty$ ), then $\mathbf{M} = \mu$ and

$$
\dot {\mathbf {R}} = - \frac {\mu}{2 \mathbf {R}} \left(1 + \frac {4 \mathbf {R}}{\mu}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}}.
$$

This can easily be integrated to give

$$
\frac {4}{\mu} \tau = - \frac {1}{3} \left(1 + \frac {4 \mathrm {R}}{\mu}\right) ^ {\frac {3}{2}} + \left(1 + \frac {4 \mathrm {R}}{\mu}\right) ^ {\frac {1}{2}} - \frac {2}{3},
$$

where we have taken $\tau = 0$ to correspond to $\mathbf{R} = 0$ .

Solution 21.12. To find an instantaneous metric, we need only to consider the metric and field equations on a spacelike Cauchy surface $S$ , the surface of time symmetry. Six of the ten Einstein field equations determine the metric off $S$ ; these we do not need to worry about. The remaining four equations are initial-value equations and can be written in the form (see Equations (6) and (7) of Problem 9.33)

$$
\frac {1}{2} ^ {(3)} \mathrm {R} - \frac {1}{2} \left(\mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {\mathrm {i}}\right) ^ {2} + \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {K} _ {\ell} ^ {\mathrm {m}} \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {m}} ^ {\ell} = 0 \tag {1a}
$$

$$
\mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {i}} ^ {\mathrm {m}} | _ {\mathrm {m}} - \mathrm {K} _ {\mathrm {m}} ^ {\mathrm {m}} | _ {\mathrm {i}} = 0, \tag {1b}
$$

where ${}^{(3)}\mathbf{R}$ is the curvature scalar on $\mathbf{S}$ , $\mathbf{K}_{\mathfrak{m}}^{\mathbf{i}}$ is the extrinsic curvature, a slash represents covariant differentiation with respect to the three geometry ${}^{(3)}\mathbf{g}_{ij}$ on $\mathbf{S}$ , and where we have used the vacuum initial value equations.

At a moment of time symmetry the (timelike) normal to S, from which $\mathbf{K}_{ij}$ is computed, goes into minus itself under time reversal. Thus $\mathbf{K}_{ij} = -\mathbf{K}_{ij}$ and hence the extrinsic curvature of S must vanish. For $\mathbf{K}_{ij} = 0$ on S, Equation (1b) is trivially satisfied and Equation (1a) takes the form

$$
\left(^ {3}\right) \mathrm {R} = 0. \tag {2}
$$

As a solution to Equation (2), we begin with the ansatz

$$
\mathrm {g} _ {\mathrm {i j}} = \Phi^ {4} \eta_ {\mathrm {i j}} \tag {3}
$$

and see if there is a $\Phi$ which satisfies Equation (2):

$$
\left. ^ {(3)} \mathbf {R} = \mathbf {g} ^ {\mathrm {i j}} \left(\Gamma_ {\mathrm {i m}, \mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {m}} - \Gamma_ {\mathrm {i j}, \mathrm {m}} ^ {\mathrm {m}} + \Gamma_ {\mathrm {l i}} ^ {\mathrm {m}} \Gamma_ {\mathrm {j m}} ^ {\ell} - \Gamma_ {\mathrm {m l}} ^ {\ell} \Gamma_ {\mathrm {i j}} ^ {\mathrm {m}}\right). \right. \tag {4}
$$

With the ansatz of Equation (3) we have, for the Christoffel symbols

$$
\Gamma^ {\mathrm {m}} _ {\ell_ {\mathrm {i}}} = 2 \Phi^ {- 1} \left(\Phi_ {, \mathrm {i}} \delta^ {\mathrm {m}} _ {\mathrm {i}} + \Phi_ {, \ell} \delta^ {\mathrm {m}} _ {\mathrm {i}} - \Phi^ {\mathrm {, m}} \eta_ {\ell_ {\mathrm {i}}}\right), \tag {5a}
$$

$$
\mathrm {g} ^ {\mathrm {i j}} \Gamma_ {\mathrm {i m}, \mathrm {j}} ^ {\mathrm {m}} = - 6 \Phi^ {- 6} (\nabla \Phi) ^ {2} + 6 \Phi^ {- 5} \nabla^ {2} \Phi \tag {5b}
$$

$$
\mathrm {g} ^ {\mathrm {i j}} \Gamma_ {\mathrm {i j}, \mathrm {m}} ^ {\mathrm {m}} = 2 \Phi^ {- 6} (\nabla \Phi) ^ {2} - 2 \Phi^ {- 5} \nabla^ {2} \Phi \tag {5c}
$$

$$
\mathrm {g} ^ {\mathrm {i j}} \Gamma_ {\ell \mathrm {i}} ^ {\mathrm {m}} \Gamma_ {\mathrm {j m}} ^ {\ell} = - 4 \Phi^ {- 6} (\nabla \Phi) ^ {2} \tag {5d}
$$

$$
\mathrm {g} ^ {\mathrm {i j}} \Gamma_ {\mathrm {m} \ell} ^ {\ell} \Gamma_ {\mathrm {i j}} ^ {\mathrm {m}} = - 1 2 \Phi^ {- 6} (\nabla \Phi) ^ {2} \tag {5e}
$$

where we have used the symbols

$$
(\nabla \Phi) ^ {2} \equiv \Phi_ {, i} \Phi_ {, j} \eta^ {i j} \tag {5f}
$$

$$
\nabla^ {2} \Phi \equiv \Phi_ {, i, j} \eta^ {i j}. \tag {5g}
$$

Combining Equations (4)-(5), we see that Equation (2) reduces to

$$
\left(^ {(3)} \mathbf {R} = 0 = 8 \Phi^ {- 5} \nabla^ {2} \Phi , \right. \tag {6}
$$

which is satisfied by any solution of Laplace's equation. One such solution is

$$
\Phi = 1 + \sum_ {i} \frac {M _ {i}}{2 r _ {i}} \tag {7}
$$

leading to a metric

$$
\mathbf {g} _ {\mathbf {i j}} = \left(1 + \sum_ {\mathbf {i}} \frac {\mathbf {M} _ {\mathbf {i}}}{2 \mathbf {r} _ {\mathbf {i}}}\right) ^ {4} \eta_ {\mathbf {i j}}. \tag {8}
$$

The lowest order term in $\mathbf{M} / \mathbf{r}$ for the metric in Equation (8) is

$$
\mathbf {g} _ {\mathbf {i j}} \approx \left(1 + \sum_ {\mathbf {i}} \frac {2 \mathrm {M} _ {\mathbf {i}}}{\mathrm {r} _ {\mathbf {i}}}\right) \boldsymbol {\eta} _ {\mathbf {i j}}
$$

which is the “Post-Newtonian” approximation to the spatial part of a metric generated by point masses $\mathbf{M}_{\mathrm{i}}$ located at positions $\mathbf{r}_{\mathrm{i}}$ . Thus the metric in Equation (8) is, in fact, the instantaneous spatial metric for point masses at arbitrary positions at a moment of time symmetry.

Solution 21.13. Begin by noticing that the square of $\mathbf{U}^{\alpha}$ , which is a Lorentz invariant, is just the determinant of $\mathbf{U}^{\mathbf{A}\mathbf{A}'}$ . The analog of the Minkowski metric will then be a set of matrices which serve to raise and lower indices in constructing the determinant of $\mathbf{U}^{\mathbf{A}\mathbf{A}'}$ :

$$
\delta_ {D} ^ {C} \det  (U ^ {A B}) = U ^ {F C} U ^ {E G} \varepsilon_ {F E} \varepsilon_ {D G}
$$

where

$$
\varepsilon_ {\textbf {F E}} = \left( \begin{array}{c c} 0 & 1 \\ - 1 & 0 \end{array} \right) \qquad \delta_ {\textbf {D}} ^ {\textbf {C}} = \left( \begin{array}{c c} 1 & 0 \\ 0 & 1 \end{array} \right).
$$

Evidently, a pair of $\varepsilon$ matrices plays the role of the Minkowski metric.

To find the analog of a Lorentz transformation, denote such a transformation by

$$
\mathrm {U} ^ {\mathrm {F} ^ {\prime} \mathrm {C} ^ {\prime}} = \mathrm {L} _ {\mathrm {F C}} ^ {\mathrm {F} ^ {\prime} \mathrm {C} ^ {\prime}} \mathrm {U} ^ {\mathrm {F C}}.
$$

Then, invariance of the dot product of two vectors $\mathbf{U},\mathbf{V}$ , requires

$$
U ^ {A B} V ^ {C D} \varepsilon_ {A C} \varepsilon_ {B D} = L _ {E F} ^ {E ^ {\prime} F ^ {\prime}} U ^ {E F} L _ {G H} ^ {G ^ {\prime} H ^ {\prime}} V ^ {G H} \varepsilon_ {E ^ {\prime} G ^ {\prime}} \varepsilon_ {F ^ {\prime} H ^ {\prime}}.
$$

Thus we must have

$$
\varepsilon_ {\mathbf {E G}} \varepsilon_ {\mathbf {F H}} = L _ {\mathbf {E F}} ^ {\mathbf {E} ^ {\prime} \mathbf {F} ^ {\prime}} L _ {\mathbf {G H}} ^ {\mathbf {G} ^ {\prime} \mathbf {A} ^ {\prime}} \varepsilon_ {\mathbf {E} ^ {\prime} \mathbf {G} ^ {\prime}} \varepsilon_ {\mathbf {F} ^ {\prime} \mathbf {H} ^ {\prime}}.
$$

This implies that

$$
\delta_ {\mathbf {E}} ^ {\mathbf {M}} \delta_ {\mathbf {H}} ^ {\mathbf {K}} = \mathrm {L} _ {\mathbf {E F}} ^ {\mathbf {E} ^ {\prime} \mathbf {F} ^ {\prime}} \mathrm {L} _ {\mathbf {G H}} ^ {\mathbf {G} ^ {\prime} \mathbf {A} ^ {\prime}} \varepsilon_ {\mathbf {E} ^ {\prime} \mathbf {G} ^ {\prime}} \varepsilon_ {\mathbf {F} ^ {\prime} \mathbf {H} ^ {\prime}} \varepsilon^ {\mathbf {G M}} \varepsilon^ {\mathbf {F K}}
$$

and hence that

$$
\det  \left(L _ {E F} ^ {E ^ {\prime} F ^ {\prime}}\right) = 1.
$$

Thus the Lorentz transformation matrices must be unimodular (of unit determinant).

Solution 21.14.

(a) The result follows from the fact that the indices B, C and D range over only two values and hence cannot all be different.   
(b) Writing out the result of part (a) and using the antisymmetry of

$$
\begin{array}{l} \varepsilon_ {\mathbf {A B}} \mathrm {g i v e s} \\ \varepsilon_ {\mathbf {A B}} \varepsilon_ {\mathbf {C D}} + \varepsilon_ {\mathbf {A C}} \varepsilon_ {\mathbf {D B}} + \varepsilon_ {\mathbf {A D}} \varepsilon_ {\mathbf {B C}} = 0. \\ \end{array}
$$

Contracting now with $\xi^{\mathbf{CD}}$ gives (watch out for the signs!)

$$
- \varepsilon_ {\mathbf {A B}} \xi_ {\mathbf {C}} ^ {\mathbf {C}} + \xi_ {\mathbf {A B}} - \xi_ {\mathbf {B A}} = 0.
$$

Hence we have

$$
\xi_ {\mathbf {A B}} = \xi_ {\mathbf {(A B)}} + \xi_ {\mathbf {[ A B ]}} = \xi_ {\mathbf {(A B)}} + \frac {1}{2} \varepsilon_ {\mathbf {A B}} \xi_ {\mathbf {C}} ^ {\mathbf {C}} .
$$

Solution 21.15. Since $\varepsilon_{\mathbf{ab}}^{\mathbf{cd}} = \mathrm{i}(\delta_{\mathbf{A}}^{\mathbf{C}}\delta_{\mathbf{B}}^{\mathbf{D}}\delta_{\mathbf{B}}^{\mathbf{C}'}\delta_{\mathbf{A}}^{\mathbf{D}'} - \delta_{\mathbf{B}}^{\mathbf{C}}\delta_{\mathbf{A}}^{\mathbf{D}}\delta_{\mathbf{A}}^{\mathbf{C}'}\delta_{\mathbf{B}}^{\mathbf{D}'})$ (see e.g. F. A. E. Pirani, Lectures on General Relativity, p. 315) and ${}^{*}\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{ab}} = \frac{\mathrm{i}}{2}\varepsilon_{\mathrm{ab}}^{\mathrm{cd}}\mathrm{T}_{\mathrm{cd}}$ the result follows immediately.

Solution 21.16. Since primed and unprimed indices commute, we have

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathbf {T} _ {\mathrm {(a b)}} = \frac {1}{2} \left(\mathbf {T} _ {\mathbf {A B A ^ {\prime} B ^ {\prime}}} + \mathbf {T} _ {\mathbf {B A B ^ {\prime} A ^ {\prime}}}\right) \\ = \frac {1}{4} \left(\mathrm {T _ {A B A ^ {'} B ^ {'}} - T _ {B A A ^ {'} B ^ {'}} + T _ {B A B ^ {'} A ^ {'}} - T _ {A B B ^ {'} A ^ {'}}}\right) \\ + \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {\Delta T _ {(a b)}} + \frac {1}{4} \left(\mathrm {\Delta T _ {B A A ^ {'} B ^ {'}}} + \mathrm {\Delta T _ {A B B ^ {'} A ^ {'}}}\right) . \\ \end{array}
$$

With the result of Problem 21.14, this can be written as

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {T} _ {\left(\mathrm {a b}\right)} = 2 \mathrm {T} _ {\left[ \mathrm {A B} \right] \left[ \mathrm {A} ^ {\prime} \mathrm {B} ^ {\prime} \right]} + \frac {1}{2} \left(\mathrm {T} _ {\mathrm {B A A} ^ {\prime} \mathrm {B} ^ {\prime}} + \mathrm {T} _ {\mathrm {A B B} ^ {\prime} \mathrm {A} ^ {\prime}}\right) \\ = \frac {1}{2} \varepsilon_ {\mathbf {A B}} \varepsilon_ {\mathbf {A ^ {'} B ^ {'}}} \mathrm {T _ {C}} _ {\mathbf {C ^ {'}}} ^ {\mathbf {C ^ {'}}} + \frac {1}{2} \left(\mathrm {T _ {B A A ^ {'} B ^ {'}}} + \mathrm {T _ {A B B ^ {'} A ^ {'}}}\right). \\ \end{array}
$$

Finally, using the result of Problem 21.15, we get

$$
\begin{array}{l} \mathrm {T} _ {\mathrm {B A A ^ {'} B ^ {'}}} = \mathrm {T} _ {(\mathrm {a b})} - \frac {1}{2} \varepsilon_ {\mathrm {A B}} \varepsilon_ {\mathrm {A ^ {'} B ^ {'}}} \mathrm {T} _ {\mathrm {C}} ^ {\mathrm {C}} \mathrm {C} ^ {\prime} + * \mathrm {T} _ {\mathrm {a b}} \\ \mathbf {\tau} = \mathbf {\tau} _ {(\mathbf {a b})} - \frac {1}{2} \mathbf {\tau} _ {\mathbf {g} _ {\mathbf {a b}}} \mathbf {\tau} _ {\mathbf {c}} ^ {\mathbf {c}} + \mathbf {i} * \mathbf {T} _ {\mathbf {a b}}. \\ \end{array}
$$
